[F22B 37/263] Valves with water separators -- F22B 37/263 covers valves that incorporate internal water-separator elements to remove condensate from steam flow within boiler systems. These devices combine flow control with separation functions to protect downstream components and improve efficiency in steam generation plants.
[F22B 37/265] Apparatus for washing and purifying steam -- F22B 37/265 refers to devices that wash, filter or otherwise purify steam within a boiler system, removing suspended solids, oil, or condensate before the steam is utilized. Such apparatus may include washing chambers, scrubbers, or other treatment units integrated into steam-generation equipment to maintain steam quality.
[F22B 37/266] Separator reheaters -- F22B 37/266 covers separator reheaters, devices installed in steam-generation systems to reheat steam that has been cleaned of water droplets by a separator. By raising the temperature of the partially condensed steam, the reheaters improve thermal efficiency and protect downstream equipment from moisture damage. This sub-class falls under component parts or details of steam boilers within mechanical engineering.
[F22B 37/268] specially adapted for steam generators of nuclear power plants -- F22B 37/268 concerns component parts or details of steam boilers that are specially adapted for the steam generators of nuclear power plants, emphasizing designs that cope with the high-pressure, high-temperature water and the radiation environment inherent to nuclear generation.
[F22B 37/28] involving reversal of direction of flow -- F22B 37/28 covers aspects of steam-boiler design where the direction of fluid flow is intentionally reversed, such as in the arrangement of tubes, baffles or passages to improve heat transfer, control pressure drops or facilitate cleaning. It applies to component parts of steam generators that employ this flow-reversal technique.
[F22B 37/283] specially adapted for boiler drums -- F22B 37/283 covers components that are specially adapted for boiler drums, such as reinforcements, supports, or heating elements designed to improve drum performance and durability in steam-generating equipment.
[F22B 37/286] specially adapted for steam generators of nuclear power plants -- F22B 37/286 relates to components and details of steam boilers that are specifically designed for the steam generators of nuclear power plants, addressing material choices, geometry and safety features required to endure radiation, high pressure and the specialised water-chemistry conditions of nuclear operation.
[F22B 37/30] using impingement against baffle separators -- F22B 37/30 covers steam-boiler components in which a fluid jet is directed to strike baffle plates or separators, using impingement to improve heat-transfer efficiency, remove deposits, or promote mixing within the boiler. The technique is applied to enhance performance of steam-generation systems.
[F22B 37/303] specially adapted for boiler drums -- The F22B 37/303 class covers components that are specifically designed or modified for use with boiler drums, including fittings, supports, and accessories that facilitate drum operation, connection, or maintenance. It applies to steam-generator boiler designs where the drum's structural or functional requirements demand specialised parts.
[F22B 37/306] specially adapted for steam generators of nuclear power plants -- F22B 37/306 concerns components and design details that are specifically modified for steam generators in nuclear power plants, addressing the unique radiation, temperature and safety requirements of nuclear steam production.
[F22B 37/32] using centrifugal force -- F22B 37/32 covers steam boiler designs that employ centrifugal force to move or separate fluids, such as using rotating drums or impellers to enhance water-steam separation, improve circulation, or drive feedwater pumps within the boiler system.
[F22B 37/322] specially adapted for boiler drums -- This subclass covers features or devices that are specifically designed or modified for use with boiler drums, such as fittings, supports, or internal arrangements that improve drum operation or integration within a steam boiler system. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering, steam generation, methods of steam generation, and component parts of steam boilers.
[F22B 37/325] using a revolving element -- F22B 37/325 covers steam-boiler components that employ a rotating element to aid steam generation. It includes devices such as rotating drums, revolving burners or rotary heat-exchange surfaces that move relative to the fluid to improve heat transfer, mixing or combustion. The classification is part of methods of steam generation within mechanical engineering.
[F22B 37/327] specially adapted for steam generators of nuclear power plants -- F22B 37/327 covers components or design features of steam boilers that are specifically adapted for use in nuclear power plant steam generators. It includes modifications to withstand radiation, high-pressure conditions, and to integrate with reactor systems.
[F22B 37/34] Adaptations of boilers for promoting water circulation -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Auxiliary devices for promoting water circulation -> F22D7/00
[F22B 37/36] Arrangements for sheathing or casing boilers -- F22B 37/36 covers the design and construction of outer protective structures for steam boilers, including casings, cladding and insulation that enclose the boiler's pressure vessel and its components. The code addresses how these sheathing arrangements are integrated to secure the boiler, control heat loss and protect against external damage.
[F22B 37/365] Casings of metal sheets, e.g. expansion plates, expansible joints -- This subclass covers casings made from metal sheets that are used to contain or support expanding components such as expansion plates and expansible joints in steam-generating equipment. It pertains to structural elements that accommodate thermal expansion in boilers.
[F22B 37/38] Determining or indicating operating conditions in steam boilers, e.g. monitoring direction or rate of water flow through water tubes -- F22B 37/38 covers devices and methods for determining or indicating operating conditions within steam boilers, such as monitoring the direction and rate of water flow through boiler water tubes. It falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation, and methods of steam generation for component parts or details of steam boilers.
[F22B 37/40] Arrangements of partition walls in flues of steam boilers, e.g. built-up from baffles -- F22B 37/40 covers the design and positioning of partition walls inside the flues of steam boilers, usually formed from baffles, to direct gas flow, enhance heat transfer and control combustion dynamics in steam-generation systems.
[F22B 37/42] Applications, arrangements or dispositions of alarm or automatic safety devices -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Safety devices for feed-water heaters -> F22D1/14 - Emergency feed-water supply -> F22D11/003
[F22B 37/421] Arrangements for detecting leaks -- F22B 37/421 covers specific arrangements used to detect leaks in steam-generation equipment, focusing on component parts or details of steam boilers. The classification falls under methods of steam generation within mechanical engineering.
[F22B 37/423] Valves for testing steam generators -- F22B 37/423 covers valves specifically designed for testing steam generators, including devices for pressure, leak-down or blow-down tests that verify boiler performance and integrity. These valves are classified under component parts of steam boilers within mechanical engineering and steam-generation methods.
[F22B 37/425] Feed-water supply alarm devices using floats -- F22B 37/425 covers devices that use floating elements to monitor the feed-water level in steam boilers and provide an alarm when the water falls below a safe threshold. These float-activated warning systems are classified under mechanical engineering, steam generation methods and component parts of steam boilers.
[F22B 37/426] Feed-water supply alarm devices using electric signals -- F22B 37/426 covers alarm devices for steam-boiler feed-water systems that employ electrical signals to detect abnormal conditions such as loss of flow, low pressure or inadequate water level and to trigger warnings or shutdowns. These devices are part of the control and safety mechanisms that protect boilers from damage caused by insufficient feed water.
[F22B 37/428] Feed-water supply alarm devices using dilatation of solids or liquids -- F22B 37/428 covers alarm devices for the feed-water supply of steam boilers that operate by detecting expansion of a solid or liquid element. When the element dilates due to loss of water pressure or flow, the device signals a fault to prevent boiler damage.
[F22B 37/44] of safety valves -- F22B 37/44 pertains to safety valves for steam boilers, covering devices that relieve excess pressure to prevent over-pressure accidents and the design, operation, and integration of these valves within boiler components.
[F22B 37/443] Safety devices extinguishing the fire -- F22B 37/443 covers safety mechanisms installed on steam-boiler components that automatically suppress or extinguish a fire within the boiler system. These devices are designed to prevent overheating, explosion or damage by detecting flame or excessive temperature and intervening with water, steam, or other extinguishing agents. The class falls under mechanical engineering - steam generation - methods of steam generation - component parts or details of steam boilers.
[F22B 37/446] Safety devices responsive to overpressure -- F22B 37/446 covers safety devices that react to excessive pressure in steam boilers. These include pressure-relief valves, rupture discs and other mechanisms that open, vent or otherwise protect the boiler when an over-pressure condition is sensed, preventing damage or explosion.
[F22B 37/46] responsive to low or high water level, e.g. for checking, suppressing or extinguishing combustion in boilers -- This subclass relates to devices or control methods that sense low or high water levels in a steam boiler and automatically act to check, suppress or extinguish the combustion. It includes level-detecting sensors, level-controlled shut-off valves and other safety mechanisms that protect the boiler from water-level excursions.
[F22B 37/47] responsive to abnormal temperature, e.g. actuated by fusible plugs -- F22B 37/47 covers safety features of steam-boiler components that react to abnormal temperature conditions, such as fusible plugs that melt or other actuators that open to relieve pressure when the boiler overheats.
[F22B 37/475] Safety devices with fusible plugs -- F22B 37/475 covers safety devices that incorporate fusible plugs for steam boilers, where a metal plug melts at a predetermined temperature to open a passage and relieve excess pressure, protecting the boiler from rupture. These devices are designed to act autonomously without external control, providing a fail-safe for overheating conditions.
[F22B 37/48] Devices or arrangements for removing water, minerals or sludge from boilers  -- F22B 37/48 covers devices or arrangements that extract water, mineral deposits or sludge from steam boilers, such as separators, skimmers and cleaning systems installed in boiler components. It pertains to the mechanical-engineering field of steam-generation methods and focuses on boiler parts that maintain fluid purity and heat-transfer efficiency.
[F22B 37/483] specially adapted for nuclear steam generators -- The CPC subclass F22B 37/483 covers components or detailed features of steam boilers that are specially adapted for use in nuclear steam generators, addressing design modifications required for nuclear-powered steam production.
[F22B 37/486] Devices for removing water, minerals or sludge from boilers  F22B37/483 ,  F22B37/50 ,  F22B37/52  and  F22B37/54  take precedence -- F22B 37/486 classifies devices installed in steam boilers that remove condensate, mineral deposits or sludge to maintain efficient operation. It falls under component parts of steam generation systems and follows more specific groups such as F22B 37/483, 37/50, 37/52 and 37/54.
[F22B 37/50] for draining or expelling water -- The CPC class F22B 37/50 covers features of steam boilers that are designed to drain or expel water, such as condensate-removal systems, drainage pipes, blow-down devices and related mechanisms that ensure efficient steam generation by preventing water accumulation in the boiler.
[F22B 37/52] Washing-out devices -- Washing-out devices are mechanisms installed in steam boilers to remove scale, sediment and corrosion deposits from boiler surfaces and tubes, typically by spraying water, steam or chemicals while the boiler is in operation or during shutdown. They help maintain heat-transfer efficiency, prevent overheating and extend boiler life.
[F22B 37/54] De-sludging or blow-down devices -- F22B 37/54 covers devices that remove accumulated sludge or discharge excess water from steam boilers, commonly called de-sludging or blow-down apparatus. These systems typically incorporate valves, pipes, or pumps to control the release of boiler contents and maintain water quality and pressure during operation.
[F22B 37/545] Valves specially adapted therefor -- F22B 37/545 covers valves that are specially designed for use in steam-generation equipment, particularly boiler systems. The entry includes valves adapted to withstand the high pressures and temperatures of steam, as well as those engineered for specific boiler functions such as safety release, flow control or steam distribution.
[F22B 37/56] Boiler cleaning control devices, e.g. for ascertaining proper duration of boiler blow-down -- Devices that monitor and regulate cleaning processes in steam boilers, particularly systems that determine the appropriate length of blow-down to remove scale and impurities. They typically include sensors, timing mechanisms and control logic to ensure efficient boiler operation and prevent damage.
[F22B 37/565] Blow-down control, e.g. for ascertaining proper duration of boiler blow-down -- The F22B 37/565 classification covers devices and methods for controlling boiler blow-down, especially systems that determine and regulate the proper duration of blow-down to maintain optimal boiler performance and prevent scaling. It falls under component parts or details of steam boilers within the broader field of methods of steam generation in mechanical engineering.
[F22B 37/58] Removing tubes from headers or drums -- F22B 37/58 relates to the removal of tubes from boiler headers or drums, detailing methods and apparatus used to extract tubes during maintenance or repair. It falls under the CPC group for component parts of steam boilers within mechanical engineering.
[F22B 37/60] specially adapted for steam boilers of instantaneous or flash type -- F22B 37/60 covers components or details that are specially adapted for instantaneous or flash-type steam boilers, such as burners, heat exchangers, feedwater devices or control elements designed to generate steam rapidly from water without a conventional boiler water-level. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation, and methods of steam generation.
[F22B 37/62] specially adapted for steam boilers of forced-flow type -- The classification covers inventions that provide special adaptations for steam boilers operating with forced flow of water or steam, such as enhanced burners, feedwater distributors, or control devices designed to improve efficiency, safety, or reliability in forced-flow boiler systems.
[F22B 37/64] Mounting of, or supporting arrangements for, tube units -- The subclass F22B 37/64 deals with the ways tube bundles are mounted or supported inside steam-generation boilers, including brackets, frames, saddles and other structural arrangements that secure the tubes during operation. It falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation and boiler component design.
[F22B 37/645] involving upper vertically-disposed water tubes and lower horizontally- or helically disposed water tubes -- F22B 37/645 relates to steam-boiler designs that use water tubes positioned vertically in the upper part of the boiler and horizontally or helically in the lower part, enhancing water circulation and heat transfer within the vessel.
[F22B 37/66] involving vertically-disposed water tubes -- The subclass F22B 37/66 refers to steam-generator designs that incorporate water tubes positioned vertically within the boiler. It covers the arrangement, construction and related details of these vertically-disposed tubes as component parts of steam boilers, forming part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for methods of steam generation.
[F22B 37/68] involving horizontally-disposed water tubes -- This subgroup deals with steam generators whose water-tube bundles are arranged horizontally, covering the design, arrangement and construction details of such horizontally-disposed tubes within boiler systems. It includes components, mounting and related features specific to this tube orientation.
[F22B 37/70] Arrangements for distributing water into water tubes -- This subclass concerns designs that supply water to the water tubes of a steam boiler, including manifolds, headers, distributors and related piping that ensure uniform water flow and proper filling.
[F22B 37/72] involving injection devices -- F22B 37/72 relates to methods of steam generation that incorporate injection devices, covering the design and operation of apparatus that inject water, fuel or additives into a boiler or steam circuit to improve efficiency, control, or output. It falls under component parts or details of steam boilers within the broader categories of mechanical engineering and steam generation.
[F22B 37/74] Throttling arrangements for tubes or sets of tubes -- F22B 37/74 covers throttling arrangements applied to individual tubes or groups of tubes used in steam-generation equipment. It pertains to the design and placement of flow-restricting devices that control the passage of steam or water within boiler tube bundles, affecting pressure and heat-transfer performance.
[F22B 37/76] Adaptations or mounting of devices for observing existence or direction of fluid flow -- F22B 37/76 covers adaptations or mounting arrangements for devices that detect the presence or direction of fluid flow in steam-boiler components, such as flow meters, sight glasses or pressure indicators, focusing on how these observation tools are integrated within the boiler system.
[F22B 37/78] Adaptations or mounting of level indicators -- F22B 37/78 covers the ways level indicators are adapted, installed or mounted within steam-boiler components, addressing the positioning, supports, brackets and associated mechanisms that enable the devices to monitor water or steam levels accurately.
[F22B 5/00] Kinds of steam boilers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Steam boilers containing a closed vessel designed to withstand internal pressure for generating steam. The drum is contacted externally by flue gases. This place also covers old documents (state of the art, which basis is not up to date, for example basic techniques used at the beginning of the 19th century) with big rotating drums, documents with auxiliary water tubes outside the boiler body and components and accessories of the drum.
[F22B 5/005] with rotating drums -- The subclass F22B 5/005 covers steam boilers that incorporate rotating drums as part of their design, used to improve heat transfer and water circulation within the boiler. This arrangement falls under kinds of steam boilers, methods of steam generation, and steam-generation engineering, and is employed in various industrial and power-plant applications to enhance efficiency and control of steam production.
[F22B 5/02] with auxiliary water tubes outside the boiler body -- F22B 5/02 covers steam boilers that incorporate auxiliary water tubes positioned outside the main boiler shell. These external tubes are used to increase heating surface, improve water circulation, or facilitate the removal of scale, and are typical of water-tube boiler designs where the tubes are not contained within the pressure vessel. This subclass falls under methods of steam generation within mechanical engineering.
[F22B 5/04] Component parts thereof -- F22B 5/04 covers the individual components of steam boilers, such as fire-tubes, water-tubes, drums, hearths, burners and auxiliary fittings that make up the boiler assembly. It addresses the design, arrangement and function of these parts within various boiler types.
[F22B 7/00] Steam boilers of furnace-tube type, i.e. the combustion of fuel being performed inside one or more furnace tubes built-in in the boiler body -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Boilers in which combustion of fuel is performed inside one or more furnace-tubes running through a sealed container of water. Combustion heat is transferred through the walls of the tubes by thermal conduction, thereby heating water and creating steam. This place also covers steam boilers of furnace-tube type with auxiliary water tubes inside the furnace tube and outside the boiler body, with auxiliary fire tubes and component parts of the boiler, for example walling of flues.
[F22B 7/02] without auxiliary water tubes -- This CPC subclass covers furnace-tube steam boilers where combustion takes place inside one or more tubes that form the boiler body and no auxiliary water tubes are employed for heat transfer.
[F22B 7/04] with auxiliary water tubes
[F22B 7/06] inside the furnace tube in transverse arrangements -- F22B 7/06 covers furnace-tube steam boilers in which the combustion chamber is formed by tubes positioned transversely across the boiler, so that the fuel burns inside these tubes rather than in a separate furnace space. The classification applies to designs where the tube arrangement runs across the flow direction, affecting heat transfer and boiler construction.
[F22B 7/08] inside the furnace tube in longitudinal arrangement -- This subclass concerns furnace-tube steam boilers in which the fuel is combusted inside one or more tubes that run longitudinally through the boiler body, allowing heat to be transferred directly to the surrounding water for steam generation.
[F22B 7/10] outside the boiler body -- F22B 7/10 classifies features of furnace-tube steam boilers in which the combustion zone or related parts are situated outside the boiler vessel, including external furnace tubes, burners or heat-exchange elements. It concerns methods of steam generation that use this external arrangement to heat the water within the boiler.
[F22B 7/12] with auxiliary fire tubes -- F22B 7/12 relates to furnace-tube steam boilers that incorporate auxiliary fire tubes alongside the main furnace tubes. The auxiliary tubes serve to increase heating surface and improve heat distribution within the boiler.
[F22B 7/14] with both auxiliary water tubes and auxiliary fire tubes -- F22B 7/14 relates to furnace-tube steam boilers that incorporate both auxiliary water tubes, which increase heating surface, and auxiliary fire tubes, which provide additional combustion space within the boiler. This arrangement enhances heat transfer efficiency and allows flexible fuel burning.
[F22B 7/16] Component parts thereof -- F22B 7/16 pertains to the individual component parts and sub-assemblies of furnace-tube steam boilers, where fuel combustion takes place inside one or more tubes built into the boiler body, including items such as furnace tubes, fireboxes, and associated fittings.
[F22B 7/18] Walling of flues -- F22B 7/18 concerns the construction of flue walls inside furnace-tube steam boilers, covering the design, materials and techniques used to line or reinforce the interior passages where hot gases travel, ensuring structural integrity, heat resistance and efficient heat transfer. This subcategory falls under methods of steam generation for furnace-tube type boilers.
[F22B 7/20] Furnace tubes -- F22B 7/20 covers furnace tubes, which are structural components integrated into the boiler body where fuel combustion takes place to generate steam. These tubes form a furnace-tube type boiler, allowing heat transfer from the flame to the surrounding water.
[F22B 9/00] Steam boilers of fire-tube type, i.e. the flue gas from a combustion chamber outside the boiler body flowing through tubes built-in in the boiler body -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Boilers in which hot flue gases from a combustion chamber outside the boiler body, said body being a sealed container, are channelled through tubes built-in the boiler body that are surrounded by the fluid to be heated. Heat of the gases is transferred through the walls of the tubes by thermal conduction, heating the water and creating steam. This place also covers arrangements of the fire tubes and the boiler as well as components of the boiler itself.
[F22B 9/02] the boiler body being disposed upright, e.g. above the combustion chamber -- This subclass covers fire-tube steam boilers in which the boiler shell is arranged vertically, typically positioned above the combustion chamber so that flue gases pass upward through tubes within the upright body; it belongs to the mechanical-engineering methods of steam generation group.
[F22B 9/04] the fire tubes being in upright arrangement -- F22B 9/04 relates to fire-tube steam boilers in which the flue-gas tubes are arranged vertically within the boiler shell, the combustion chamber being outside the vessel. The upright tube configuration is a specific subclass of fire-tube boiler designs used for generating steam.
[F22B 9/06] Arrangement of header boxes providing for return diversion of flue gas flow -- F22B 9/06 relates to fire-tube steam boilers and specifies arrangements of header boxes that divert flue gases back into the boiler, enabling recirculation of hot gases for more uniform heating and improved thermal efficiency.
[F22B 9/08] the fire tubes being in horizontal arrangement -- The subclass F22B 9/08 concerns fire-tube steam boilers in which the tubes carrying the flue gases are arranged horizontally inside the boiler shell. It falls under methods of steam generation within the fire-tube type boiler category.
[F22B 9/10] the boiler body being disposed substantially horizontally, e.g. at the side of the combustion chamber -- F22B 9/10 refers to fire-tube steam boilers whose boiler body is arranged substantially horizontally, for example positioned at the side of the combustion chamber, rather than vertically. This subclass groups designs where the boiler shell's orientation is a key feature of the steam-generation method.
[F22B 9/12] the fire tubes being in substantially-horizontal arrangement -- F22B 9/12 covers fire-tube steam boilers in which the combustion gases pass through tubes that are positioned substantially horizontally within the boiler shell, a layout used to improve heat transfer and space efficiency in fire-tube type steam generation.
[F22B 9/14] Arrangement of header boxes providing for return diversion of flue gas flow -- F22B 9/14 deals with fire-tube steam boilers where the header boxes are arranged to divert the flue gas back through the boiler. The configuration redirects combustion gases after they have passed the tubes, enhancing heat utilisation and allowing control of flow patterns within the system.
[F22B 9/16] the boiler body containing fire tubes disposed crosswise in inclined upward arrangement -- F22B 9/16 covers fire-tube steam boilers whose boiler shell contains fire tubes arranged crosswise in an inclined upward pattern. The configuration places the tubes diagonally upward across the boiler body to promote water circulation and efficient heat transfer.
[F22B 9/18] Component parts thereof -- F22B 9/18 covers the individual component parts of fire-tube steam boilers, where combustion gases flow through tubes built into the boiler shell. It includes items such as the tubes themselves, tube sheets, headers, drums, and related fittings that form the internal flow path and support structure of the boiler.
[F22D] PREHEATING, OR ACCUMULATING PREHEATED, FEED-WATER FOR STEAM GENERATION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This subclass covers general aspects of, or methods for, preheating or accumulating feed water for steam generation. All devices in a power plant or a steam boiler for heating water in liquid form, like economisers or water preheaters and accumulating water for preheating purposes, controlling and regulating the water level in said water accumulators, feed-water supply like automatic water feeding and controlling methods are covered. Furthermore safety feed water supply and arrangements of feed water pumps are also covered.  **Glossary:** - Economiser: heat exchanger for heating feed water normally beyond the boiling point improving the boiler's efficiency
[F22D 1/00] Feed-water heaters, i.e. economisers or like preheaters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General aspects and constructional features of devices or heat exchangers for heating water normally beyond the boiling point in order to improve the boiler's efficiency arranged in a flow path up stream of an evaporator. This group covers also economisers and preheaters with water tube arranged in the boiler furnace, in fire tubes or in flue ways, with water tubes arranged outside the boiler furnace and heated for example in a separate heat exchanger. Furthermore also water and air preheating systems, combinations of exhaust-steam and smoke-gas water preheater and preheaters incorporating thermal de-aeration are covered.  **Glossary:** - Economiser: feed water heater
[F22D 1/003] Feed-water heater systems  F22D1/325 ,  F22D1/36  and  F22D1/40  take precedence -- F22D 1/003 covers feed-water heater systems used in steam-generating plants, including economisers and similar preheaters that raise the temperature of feed water before it enters the boiler. The classification includes arrangements, flow paths and control of such heaters, with more detailed sub-classes F22D1/325, F22D1/36 and F22D1/40 taking precedence for particular configurations.
[F22D 1/006] with heating tubes  F22D1/24  takes precedence -- This CPC group covers feed-water heaters that incorporate heating tubes within the heater to transfer heat from exhaust steam or other sources to the feed water, improving efficiency in steam-generation systems.
[F22D 1/02] with water tubes arranged in the boiler furnaces, fire tubes or flue ways
[F22D 1/04] the tubes having plain outer surfaces, e.g. in vertical arrangement -- F22D 1/04 covers feed-water heater tubes whose outer surfaces are plain, without fins or projections, and are often positioned vertically to promote efficient heat transfer when pre-heating boiler feed water in steam-generation systems. This sub-group falls under the broader class for steam-generation feed-water preheating devices such as economisers.
[F22D 1/06] in horizontal arrangement -- The subclass F22D 1/06 covers feed-water heating systems, such as economisers, that are configured with a horizontal arrangement of the heating elements or flow paths. This layout places the water and heat-transfer surfaces side-by-side to enhance heat exchange in steam-generation boilers.
[F22D 1/08] the tubes having fins, ribs, gills, corrugations, or the like on their outer surfaces, e.g. in vertical arrangement -- This subclass covers feed-water heaters in which the heat-transfer tubes are externally provided with fins, ribs, gills, corrugations or similar features, often arranged vertically, to increase heat-transfer efficiency.
[F22D 1/10] in horizontal arrangement -- F22D 1/10 covers feed-water heaters, such as economisers, that are arranged horizontally within a steam-generation system. The classification applies to devices that preheat or accumulate feed water before it enters the boiler, emphasizing a horizontal layout of the heating elements.
[F22D 11/00] Feed-water supply not provided for in other main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All other aspects of feed water supply, which are not covered by the other main groups, like for example emergency feed water supply, arrangements of feed water cleaning, when the water is cleaned between condenser and boiler and arrangements of feed water pumps
[F22D 11/003] Emergency feed-water supply -- F22D 11/003 - Emergency feed-water supply covers systems and methods for providing a temporary or backup source of preheated water to boilers when the normal feed-water supply is unavailable or fails. It includes devices such as auxiliary pumps, storage tanks, and automatic switching arrangements that ensure continuous steam generation.
[F22D 11/006] Arrangements of feedwater cleaning with a boiler -- F22D 11/006 covers arrangements for cleaning boiler feedwater, such as filtration, deaeration or chemical treatment integrated with the boiler, to remove impurities before the water is used for steam generation. It falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation, preheating or accumulating preheated feed-water, and specifically addresses feed-water supply not provided for in other main groups.
[F22D 11/02] Arrangements of feed-water pumps -- F22D 11/02 covers the layout and interconnection of feed-water pumps used to supply preheated or accumulated feed-water to steam-generation boilers when the supply is not addressed in other main groups. It includes the positioning, number, and coupling of pumps within a plant, as well as related piping and control arrangements designed to ensure reliable delivery of feed-water to the boiler.
[F22D 11/04] with means to eliminate steam formation -- F22D 11/04 pertains to feed-water systems for steam generators that incorporate features to prevent flash steam formation. It covers arrangements such as mixers, pressure-controlled injection or heat-exchange devices that keep the preheated water from boiling before it reaches the boiler, thereby improving efficiency and safety.
[F22D 11/06] for returning condensate to boiler -- The F22D 11/06 group addresses arrangements that return condensate to the steam boiler as feed water. It includes devices or processes that recycle condensate directly into the boiler, improving thermal efficiency without separate pre-heating stages.
[F22D 1/12] Control devices, e.g. for regulating steam temperature -- This subclass covers devices that control and maintain the temperature of steam in feed-water heaters such as economisers by adjusting flow or heat transfer. They are used to regulate steam temperature during preheating of feed water in mechanical engineering steam generation systems.
[F22D 1/14] Safety or venting devices -- Safety or venting devices for feed-water heaters are mechanisms that protect the heater from over-pressure, excess temperature, or water-hammer by automatically releasing steam, gas, or liquid. They include pressure relief valves, rupture discs, and vent fittings that discharge when preset limits are exceeded, ensuring safe operation of economisers and similar preheaters in steam-generation cycles.
[F22D 1/16] with water tubes arranged otherwise than in the boiler furnace, fire tubes, or flue ways -- This subclass deals with feed-water heaters whose water tubes are placed outside the boiler furnace, fire-tube bank or flue-way, such as side-mounted or externally arranged tubes used to preheat or store boiler feed water.
[F22D 1/18] and heated indirectly -- F22D 1/18 covers feed-water heaters that are heated indirectly, such as economisers that use waste heat from flue gases or exhaust steam to raise the temperature of water before it enters a boiler. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically steam generation and the preheating or accumulation of feed-water for steam production. It addresses designs where the heating medium does not contact the water directly.
[F22D 1/20] and directly connected to boilers -- The F22D 1/20 subclass covers feed-water heaters that are directly attached to boilers, including economisers or similar devices that preheat or store feed water for steam generation. It pertains to mechanical-engineering solutions for improving boiler efficiency by heating incoming water before it enters the steam-producing system.
[F22D 1/22] and provided for rotary movements -- F22D 1/22 covers feed-water heaters, such as economisers, that are mounted on a rotating assembly and move together with the turbine or other rotating parts. The provision for rotary movement allows the heater to follow the motion of the drive shaft, simplifying connections and improving reliability.
[F22D 1/24] with fire tubes or flue ways traversing feed-water vessels -- This subclass covers feed-water heaters in which fire tubes or flue ways pass through the water-containing vessel, allowing combustion gases to directly transfer heat to the water before it enters a boiler.
[F22D 1/26] with means, other than tubes, to separate water and heating medium, e.g. bulk heaters without internal flues or tubes, jacketted smoke-boxes or flue or flues -- F22D 1/26 covers feed-water heaters that separate water from the heating medium without using tubes, for example bulk-type heaters, jacketted smoke-boxes or similar arrangements where the heating gas flows around a chamber rather than through internal tubes.
[F22D 1/28] for direct heat transfer, e.g. by mixing water and steam -- F22D 1/28 covers feed-water heating arrangements that achieve temperature rise by direct heat transfer, such as mixing water with steam without an intermediate heat-exchange surface. It is a subcategory of steam-generation preheating systems that prepare feed-water before boiler entry.
[F22D 1/30] with stages, steps, baffles, dishes, circular troughs, or other means to cause interrupted or cascading fall of water -- This subclass concerns feed-water heaters that employ internal stages, steps, baffles, dishes, circular troughs or similar devices to create an interrupted or cascading fall of water, thereby improving heat transfer before the water enters the boiler.
[F22D 1/32] arranged to be heated by steam, e.g. bled from turbines -- F22D 1/32 covers feed-water heaters that receive heat directly from steam, typically steam bled off turbines, to raise the temperature of water before it enters the boiler. It is a subclass of steam-generation preheating arrangements.
[F22D 1/325] Schematic arrangements or control devices therefor -- F22D 1/325 covers the schematic layout and control devices for feed-water heating systems in steam-generation plants, including economisers and similar preheaters. It addresses how the components are arranged and managed to regulate the preheating of feed water before it enters the boiler.
[F22D 1/34] and returning condensate to boiler with main feed supply -- The subclass addresses arrangements that recycle condensate from the boiler back into the main feedwater line, combining a feed-water heater or economiser with the primary feed supply to improve heat recovery and overall steam-generation efficiency.
[F22D 1/36] Water and air preheating systems -- F22D 1/36 covers systems that preheat feed-water or air before it enters steam-generation equipment, such as economisers and similar feed-water heaters. The classification is used for mechanical-engineering inventions that improve the efficiency of steam plants by raising the temperature of water or combustion air prior to its use.
[F22D 1/38] Constructional features of water and air preheating systems -- F22D 1/38 concerns the design and structural elements of systems that preheat water and air for steam generation. It addresses the arrangement of feed-water heaters, economisers and similar components that recover heat from exhaust gases or steam to improve thermal efficiency.
[F22D 1/40] Combinations of exhaust-steam and smoke-gas preheaters -- This CPC subclass covers systems that integrate exhaust-steam recuperators with smoke-gas heat exchangers to preheat feed-water in steam-generating plants. It includes combined economisers that recover heat from both turbine exhaust and combustion gases.
[F22D 1/42] specially adapted for locomotives -- F22D 1/42 covers feed-water heating devices that are specially designed for use on locomotives. These include economisers or similar preheaters that recover waste heat from engine exhaust or other sources to raise the temperature of water before it enters the boiler, improving the thermal efficiency of railway steam power plants.
[F22D 1/44] Smoke-gas preheaters -- F22D 1/44 covers smoke-gas preheaters, a type of feed-water heater that uses exhaust gases from combustion to raise the temperature of water before it enters a boiler, improving overall plant efficiency and reducing fuel consumption. These devices are employed in steam-generation systems such as power plants and industrial boilers.
[F22D 1/46] Exhaust-steam preheaters -- F22D 1/46 pertains to exhaust-steam preheaters, which use steam taken from turbine exhaust to raise the temperature of feed-water before it enters the boiler. This practice improves the thermodynamic efficiency of steam-generation systems by recovering waste heat.
[F22D 1/48] Details
[F22D 1/50] incorporating thermal de-aeration of feed-water -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - De-aeration produced in the course of direct heat transfer -> F22D1/28
[F22D 3/00] Accumulators for preheated water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accumulators and storage vessels for water which is heated in an economiser arranged within the combustion chamber or combined with steam accumulators and thereby heated by steam or connected directly to boilers by water conduits. Water accumulators with preheated water specially adapted for locomotives and control devices are also covered by this group.
[F22D 3/02] arranged within combustion chambers -- F22D 3/02 covers devices that are positioned inside combustion chambers to store or circulate pre-heated feed-water for steam generation. The classification applies to accumulators that help improve boiler efficiency by using heat already present in the combustion zone.
[F22D 3/04] combined with steam accumulators -- The CPC subclass F22D 3/04 covers installations in which a steam-generation system is combined with a water-preheat accumulator, allowing feed water to be stored at elevated temperature to improve boiler efficiency. It includes integrated designs that merge steam boilers with thermal storage units for pre-heated water.
[F22D 3/06] directly connected to boilers -- The CPC subclass F22D 3/06 covers accumulators that store preheated feed-water and are installed directly on a boiler, allowing heat recovered from the boiler to be reused in the steam-generation cycle and improving overall boiler efficiency.
[F22D 3/08] specially adapted for locomotives -- A locomotive-specific accumulator for pre-heated feed water stores thermal energy between cycles to reduce heat loss and improve steam-generation efficiency. The device is designed to integrate with the locomotive's boiler system, providing ready-available hot water for rapid steam production.
[F22D 3/10] Control devices -- F22D 3/10 covers control devices used with accumulators that store pre-heated feed-water for steam generators, including valves, regulators, sensors and automatic systems that manage temperature, pressure and flow of the stored water to optimise steam production.
[F22D 5/00] Controlling water feed or water level -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices and methods for controlling the supply of feed water and the feed water level using intermediate compartments, pivoting buckets, receptacles outside of the boiler and devices with float actuated valves, responsive to thermal expansion and contraction, with electric switches. Also, regulators with varying the speed or delivery pressure of feed pumps and automatic feed-control systems are covered by said group.
[F22D 5/02] with an intermediate compartment from which the water is fed by gravity after mechanically moving the compartment, the movement being controlled according to water level -- A system for controlling water feed or water level in steam generation uses an intermediate compartment that is mechanically moved and then feeds water by gravity, the movement being regulated according to the water level. This arrangement preheats or accumulates feed water before it enters the boiler.
[F22D 5/04] with pivoting buckets -- F22D 5/04 covers devices that control the feed-water supply or level in steam-generation systems by using pivoting buckets, which tilt to discharge or retain water as needed during preheating or accumulation of feed water. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation, and the preheating or accumulating of feed-water for steam production.
[F22D 5/06] with receptacles external to, but in free communication with, the boilers and adapted to move up and down in accordance with change in water level -- F22D 5/06 concerns systems for controlling water feed or water level in steam generators by using receptacles placed outside the boiler yet in free communication with it, which move up and down as the water level changes.
[F22D 5/08] with float-actuated valves -- F22D 5/08 covers systems that regulate the feed water or water level in steam-generation plants using valves that are operated by a float. The float senses the water height and mechanically opens or closes the valve to maintain the desired level, providing simple, reliable control without electronic sensors.
[F22D 5/10] and with pistons or membranes unitary with the feed inlet valves -- F22D 5/10 covers steam-generation systems in which the feed-water preheating or accumulation unit incorporates pistons or flexible membranes that are integral with the feed inlet valves, allowing direct control of water feed rate or level within the boiler's feed-water circuit.
[F22D 5/12] and with dipping tubes -- The class F22D 5/12 pertains to steam-generator feed-water control devices that employ dipping tubes to sense water level and regulate the water feed. It includes arrangements where one or more tubes extend into the water reservoir, providing a direct mechanical or hydraulic indication of level for automatic or manual feed control.
[F22D 5/14] responsive to thermal expansion and contraction, e.g. of solid elements -- The F22D 5/14 subclass groups steam-generator feed-water control systems that exploit the thermal expansion or contraction of solid parts to sense temperature changes and automatically adjust water feed or level. It includes mechanisms where solid elements deform with heat to drive valves or pumps.
[F22D 5/16] of fluids
[F22D 5/18] for varying the speed or delivery pressure of feed pumps -- This subclass covers systems that adjust the speed or output pressure of feed pumps delivering feed-water to a steam generator, enabling precise control of water flow and level during preheating or accumulation stages.
[F22D 5/20] without floats -- F22D 5/20 covers control of water feed or water-level in steam-generation feed-water preheating or accumulators using devices that operate without floats. It includes methods and apparatuses that employ alternative sensing techniques-such as pressure, capacitance, ultrasonic or electronic sensors-to maintain the desired water level.
[F22D 5/22] with floats -- F22D 5/22 covers steam-generation systems that regulate the feed-water supply or level by means of float devices, such as float-operated valves, float-controlled pumps or level-sensing mechanisms. The floats detect water level changes and actuate the feed control to maintain the desired pre-heat or reservoir conditions.
[F22D 5/24] with electric switches -- The F22D 5/24 subclass covers steam-generation systems that control feed-water supply or level by means of electric switches, encompassing devices that start, stop or adjust water flow using electrically actuated switching mechanisms. It is situated under mechanical engineering, steam generation, and preheating or accumulating preheated feed-water for steam generation.
[F22D 5/26] Automatic feed-control systems -- F22D 5/26 relates to automatic feed-control systems for steam generators that regulate the supply of pre-heated or accumulated feed-water. The technology monitors water level or temperature and adjusts flow rates to maintain optimal conditions for steam production.
[F22D 5/28] responsive to amount of steam withdrawn -- The classification covers control systems that adjust the feed-water supply or water level in a boiler based on the amount of steam being withdrawn. It includes sensors, controllers and mechanisms that vary preheating or accumulation of feed water to match steam demand.
[F22D 5/30] responsive to both water level and amount of steam withdrawn or steam pressure -- The F22D 5/30 entry pertains to control devices or methods that regulate the feed-water supply to a boiler based on the combined measurement of water level and the amount of steam withdrawn or its pressure. It is used to maintain optimal water-level conditions while responding to varying steam demand in steam-generation systems.
[F22D 5/32] influencing the speed or delivery pressure of the feed pumps -- The subclass F22D 5/32 concerns mechanisms or control strategies that modify the speed or delivery pressure of feed pumps supplying preheated water to steam-generating systems. It includes devices that regulate pump performance to maintain desired water flow or pressure levels.
[F22D 5/34] Applications of valves -- This subclass covers the use of valves to regulate or control the flow of preheated feed-water in steam-generating systems, including maintaining water level and feed rates. It includes valve types and operating methods applied to feed-water preheating circuits.
[F22D 5/36] for feeding a number of steam boilers designed for different ranges of temperature and pressure -- F22D 5/36 pertains to systems or methods that distribute feed-water to multiple steam boilers that operate at different temperature and pressure ranges, ensuring each boiler receives the appropriate amount and conditions of water for efficient steam generation. It addresses the control of water feed and level across diverse boiler units within a single plant.
[F22D 7/00] Auxiliary devices for promoting water circulation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices in for promoting or enhancing water circulation like saddles, deflecting plates, Venturi arrangements, injectors for water or steam, rotary devices like for example pumps, said devices also specially adapted for locomotive boilers. Control devises of said devices are also covered by said group.
[F22D 7/02] Saddles or like directing plates fitted to furnace tubes -- F22D 7/02 covers saddles or similar directing plates fitted to furnace tubes to guide water circulation in feed-water preheating or accumulation systems for steam generation. These auxiliary devices help direct and control water flow around the tubes, improving heat transfer and overall system efficiency.
[F22D 7/04] Injectors for water or steam -- F22D 7/04 covers injectors used to introduce water or steam into a boiler feed-water circuit, aiding circulation and mixing of the fluid. These auxiliary devices employ motive steam to draw and push water, improving feed-water preheating and overall steam-generation efficiency.
[F22D 7/06] Rotary devices, e.g. propellers -- F22D 7/06 covers rotary water-circulating apparatus such as propellers used as auxiliary devices to promote the movement of feed-water in preheating or accumulation systems for steam generation. The classification includes any rotating members that create flow or turbulence to improve heat transfer before the water enters the boiler.
[F22D 7/08] Arrangements of pumps, e.g. outside the boilers -- The subclass deals with configurations of pumps that supply feed-water to steam-generation systems, particularly when the pumps are positioned outside the boiler itself. It covers the layout of piping, drive placement and associated auxiliary components that enable effective water circulation and preheating in mechanical engineering applications.
[F22D 7/10] within the boilers -- The code F22D 7/10 designates internal boiler components that facilitate the movement of water, such as circulators, pumps or baffles, to enhance pre-heating or accumulation of feed-water in steam-generation systems. These auxiliary devices improve heat-transfer efficiency and help maintain uniform temperature distribution within the boiler.
[F22D 7/12] Control devices -- F22D 7/12 covers control devices that form part of auxiliary equipment used to promote water circulation in steam-generation systems, particularly for preheating, storing or feeding water to the boiler. These devices regulate flow, temperature or pressure to ensure efficient feed-water handling.
[F22D 7/14] specially adapted for locomotive boilers -- F22D 7/14 pertains to auxiliary devices that enhance water circulation and are specially adapted for locomotive boilers, including systems for preheating or accumulating feed-water used in steam generation.
[F22G] SUPERHEATING OF STEAM -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This subclass covers general aspects of, or methods for, generating superheated steam. Methods of steam superheating characterised by the form of heating method and by location, arrangement and disposition, constructional features of steam superheaters, control systems for controlling superheat temperature and all component parts or details of steam superheaters are covered. Thereby this subclass is limited in only methods of, or apparatus for, the generation of superheated steam for heating or power purposes.  **Glossary:** - desuperheater: reduction of superheated steam temperature - attemperator: reduction of superheated steam temperature by bringing superheated steam into direct contact with water or steam or mixtures thereof - steam conditioners: reduction of superheated steam to the needed temperature
[F22G 1/00] Steam superheating characterised by heating method -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Superheaters and methods of generation of superheated steam characterised by the heating method, such as the heat being supplied by steam, by hot flue gases from a furnace or a steam boiler, by radiation, by chemical radiation or by a separate heat source independent from heat supply of the steam boiler, including electrically heated superheaters. Furthermore methods of creating superheated steam by throttling such as reducing the pressure or direct superheaters such as devices and methods for mixing steam with furnace gases or other combustion products are classified in this main group
[F22G 1/005] the heat being supplied by steam -- F22G 1/005 refers to steam superheating where the heat source for raising the steam temperature is steam itself, typically through regeneration or reheating cycles within the same system. It is a subcategory of mechanical engineering dealing with steam generation and the specific heating method employed.
[F22G 1/02] with heat supply by hot flue gases from the furnace of the steam boiler -- F22G 1/02 covers steam superheaters that obtain heat from the hot flue gases exiting the furnace of a steam boiler. The classification applies to designs where the flue-gas stream directly supplies thermal energy to the superheating elements, improving efficiency by using waste heat.
[F22G 1/04] by diverting flow or hot flue gases to separate superheaters operating in reheating cycle, e.g. for reheating steam between a high-pressure turbine stage and an intermediate turbine stage -- This CPC subclass F22G 1/04 covers steam-superheating techniques that divert the flow of hot gases to separate superheaters operating in a reheating cycle, such as reheating steam between a high-pressure turbine stage and an intermediate-pressure turbine stage.
[F22G 1/06] with heat supply predominantly by radiation -- F22G 1/06 covers steam-superheating methods where the heat is supplied mainly by radiation, such as infrared or other radiant sources, rather than convection or conduction. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically steam generation and superheating, and classifies patents that use radiative heating to raise steam temperature.
[F22G 1/08] from heated brickwork or the like -- The subclass covers steam superheating that is accomplished by routing the steam over or through heated brickwork, firebrick chambers, refractory linings or similar solid heat-retaining structures, where the brickwork itself serves as the primary source of additional heat.
[F22G 1/10] with provision for superheating by throttling -- The CPC subclass F22G 1/10 relates to steam generators that achieve superheating by throttling, i.e., by reducing pressure through a valve or nozzle to raise the steam temperature. It falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation and the superheating of steam categories.
[F22G 1/12] by mixing steam with furnace gases or other combustion products -- F22G 1/12 covers the technique of superheating steam by directly mixing it with furnace gases or other combustion products, allowing heat transfer from the hot gases to the steam. This method is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically within steam generation and the superheating of steam, and is distinguished from other heating methods by the use of mixed combustion gases.
[F22G 1/14] using heat generated by chemical reactions -- This subclass covers methods where the heat source for superheating steam comes from exothermic chemical reactions, such as combustion of fuel or catalytic processes, rather than conventional heating elements. It includes systems that directly transfer reaction heat to the steam stream to raise its temperature above saturation.
[F22G 1/16] by using a separate heat source independent from heat supply of the steam boiler, e.g. by electricity, by auxiliary combustion of fuel oil -- F22G 1/16 relates to steam superheating performed with a heat source that is independent of the boiler's own heat supply, such as electrical heating or auxiliary combustion of fuel oil.
[F22G 1/165] by electricity -- The F22G 1/165 subclass denotes steam superheating achieved by electricity, encompassing methods that use resistive, induction, microwave or other electrically driven heating elements to raise steam temperature above saturation. It is a subcategory of steam-superheating characterised by heating method within mechanical engineering.
[F22G 3/00] Steam superheaters characterised by constructional features -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional features and details of component parts of steam superheaters such as steam tube arrangements, superheater drain arrangements, steam tubes with steam flowing in opposite direction in one pipe, annular steam tubes, steam superheaters with heating tubes, headers and collectors of superheaters. Furthermore, arrangements for the protection of superheater elements and connecting or sealing of superheater tubes are covered.
[F22G 3/001] Steam tube arrangements not dependent of location -- F22G 3/001 covers steam superheater designs in which the arrangement of tubes is defined independently of their exact position within the boiler. It includes generic tube patterns, spacing and interconnections that apply regardless of where the tubes are located, focusing on constructional features rather than specific layout locations.
[F22G 3/002] with helical steam tubes -- F22G 3/002 pertains to steam superheaters that incorporate helical steam tubes. These tubes are arranged in a helical shape to increase heat transfer and compactness within the superheater unit, falling under the mechanical engineering domain of steam generation and superheating.
[F22G 3/003] Superheater drain arrangements -- The entry concerns the design of drainage systems for steam superheaters, detailing arrangements that remove condensate or moisture from the superheating elements. It includes various conduit configurations, collection chambers and routing methods that maintain heat transfer efficiency while preventing water accumulation.
[F22G 3/004] Steam tubes with steam flowing in opposite directions in one pipe, e.g. Field tubes  F22G3/005  takes precedence -- The classification F22G 3/004 covers steam-superheater tubes in which the same pipe carries steam flowing in opposite directions, a construction commonly known as a field tube. This arrangement enables more uniform heating, reduces temperature gradients along the tube wall, and improves overall heat-transfer efficiency in superheating applications.
[F22G 3/005] Annular steam tubes, i.e. the steam being heated between concentric tubes with the heating fluid flowing in inner and around outer tube -- F22G 3/005 pertains to steam superheaters that heat steam in the annular space between concentric tubes, with the heating medium flowing inside the inner tube and around the outer tube. This constructional feature is used in mechanical-engineering applications for superheating steam.
[F22G 3/006] Steam superheaters with heating tubes  F22G3/005  takes precedence -- F22G 3/006 defines steam superheaters that use heating tubes as the principal heating elements, distinguishing them from other constructional variants. The subclass falls under steam superheaters characterised by constructional features and is subordinate to F22G3/005, which takes precedence.
[F22G 3/007] Headers; Collectors, e.g. for mixing -- This CPC subclass F22G 3/007 concerns the design of headers and collectors used in steam superheaters, devices that route and combine multiple steam or fluid streams within a boiler system. It addresses constructional features that enable efficient mixing, distribution, and flow control of superheated steam.
[F22G 3/008] Protection of superheater elements, e.g. cooling superheater tubes during starting-up periods, water tube screens -- F22G 3/008 covers measures for protecting superheater elements in steam-generation systems, such as cooling superheater tubes during start-up or using water-tube screens to prevent overheating and damage. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Steam Generation → Superheating of Steam, focusing on constructional features that enhance durability and safety of superheater components.
[F22G 3/009] Connecting or sealing of superheater or reheater tubes with collectors or distributors -- F22G 3/009 deals with the ways superheater or reheater tubes are connected to or sealed with their collectors or distributors, emphasizing constructional features that provide leak-tight, mechanically robust joints for reliable steam superheating. It is situated in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under steam generation and superheating of steam.
[F22G 5/00] Controlling superheat temperature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and devices for controlling superheat steam by regulating flue gas flow, by circulating flue gases, by displacing superheater sections, by attemperating the superheated steam (i.e. spraying water into steam), by indirectly cooling or heating the superheated steam in auxiliary enclosed heat-exchangers, by by-passing steam around superheater sections. Furthermore, applications of combustion-control devices and combined control procedures for controlling superheat temperature are covered.
[F22G 5/02] Applications of combustion-control devices, e.g. tangential-firing burners, tilting burners -- The entry covers the use of combustion-control devices, such as tangential-firing burners or tilting burners, to regulate the temperature of superheated steam during the superheating process. It pertains to methods that adjust fuel delivery or flame orientation to achieve precise superheat control.
[F22G 5/04] by regulating flue gas flow, e.g. by proportioning or diverting -- F22G 5/04 pertains to methods for controlling the temperature of superheated steam by adjusting the flow of flue gases, for example through proportioning, diverting or otherwise regulating the combustion gas stream.
[F22G 5/06] by recirculating flue gases -- F22G 5/06 covers techniques for regulating the temperature of superheated steam in boilers by redirecting part of the flue gas back into the heating zone. Recirculating flue gases allows finer control of superheat, improves thermal efficiency and reduces temperature overshoot.
[F22G 5/08] preventing furnace gas backflow through recirculating fan -- The F22G 5/08 subclass covers methods or devices that stop exhaust gases from flowing backward into a furnace through a recirculating fan used in steam superheaters. It pertains to controlling superheat temperature by ensuring the fan only moves gases in the intended direction, improving efficiency and safety of steam-generation systems.
[F22G 5/10] by displacing superheater sections -- F22G 5/10 relates to the control of superheat temperature in steam generators by moving or repositioning superheater sections. This method, within the mechanical engineering field of steam generation and superheating, allows fine adjustment of heat transfer to maintain desired steam conditions.
[F22G 5/12] by attemperating the superheated steam, e.g. by injected water sprays -- F22G 5/12 pertains to controlling the temperature of superheated steam by attemperating it, typically using injected water sprays or other moisture-adding techniques to reduce superheat in steam generation systems.
[F22G 5/123] Water injection apparatus -- A water-injection apparatus used in steam-generation systems to control the temperature of superheated steam by spraying water into the flow, thereby moderating superheat levels, improving efficiency and protecting downstream equipment.
[F22G 5/126] in combination with steam-pressure reducing valves -- F22G 5/126 covers arrangements where the control of superheated steam temperature is achieved together with steam-pressure reducing valves, allowing simultaneous regulation of temperature and pressure in a steam generation system.
[F22G 5/14] by live steam -- F22G 5/14 covers methods or apparatus that control the temperature of superheated steam by using live (saturated) steam as the heating or regulating medium, typically to maintain desired superheat levels in boilers or turbines. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation and the superheating of steam.
[F22G 5/16] by indirectly cooling or heating the superheated steam in auxiliary enclosed heat-exchanger -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for regulating the temperature of superheated steam by passing it through an auxiliary enclosed heat-exchanger that cools or heats the steam indirectly, rather than direct contact methods. It is located under Mechanical Engineering → Steam Generation → Superheating of Steam, focusing on control of superheat temperature.
[F22G 5/18] by by-passing steam around superheater sections -- F22G 5/18 relates to methods for controlling the temperature of superheated steam by diverting part of the steam flow around one or more sections of a superheater, thereby adjusting the amount of heat added to the steam. This technique is used in steam-generation systems to fine-tune superheat levels and improve efficiency.
[F22G 5/20] by combined controlling procedures -- The F22G 5/20 class covers techniques that regulate the temperature of superheated steam using a combination of control actions, such as coordinated adjustments of heat-transfer rates, flow, and pressure. It includes integrated control loops or hybrid strategies that work together to maintain a set superheat level in steam-generation systems.
[F22G 7/00] Steam superheaters characterised by location, arrangement, or disposition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Steam superheaters, which are characterised by their location, arrangement or disposition, like superheaters being located in locomotive boilers, in fire tubes, in jackets around fire tubes, in furnace tubes, in fire boxes, in smoke boxes, in flues or in water-tube boilers.
[F22G 7/005] for locomotive boilers  F22G7/065 ,  F22G7/105  take precedence
[F22G 7/02] in fire tubes -- F22G 7/02 classifies steam superheaters that are incorporated within fire tubes of a boiler, detailing the location, arrangement or disposition of the heating surfaces inside the tube that carries combustion gases. It applies to designs where the tube itself serves both as a conduit for flame gases and as the superheating element, enhancing efficiency in steam generation.
[F22G 7/04] in jackets around fire tubes -- F22G 7/04 covers steam superheaters in which the superheating elements are formed as jackets that surround the fire tubes of a boiler. The design uses the space between the outer jacket and the fire tube to transfer heat directly to the steam, providing a compact arrangement that integrates heating and tube protection. This sub-class falls under steam-generation technologies focused on superheating by location and disposition.
[F22G 7/06] in furnace tubes -- F22G 7/06 covers steam superheaters that are installed inside the tubes of a furnace, focusing on their position, layout and grouping within the boiler's heating zone. It relates to designs where the superheating elements are placed directly in the combustion gas path of the furnace tubes.
[F22G 7/065] for locomotive boilers -- F22G 7/065 covers steam superheaters in locomotive boilers, focusing on the position, layout or orientation of the superheating elements within the boiler. It falls under mechanical engineering, steam generation and superheating of steam.
[F22G 7/08] in fire-boxes -- This subclass concerns steam superheaters that are installed inside the fire-box of a boiler. It covers arrangements where the superheating coils or tubes are positioned within the combustion chamber, affecting heat transfer and maintenance.
[F22G 7/10] in smoke-boxes -- F22G 7/10 covers steam superheater components that are positioned inside the smoke-box of a boiler, detailing how their location, arrangement or disposition interacts with furnace gases. It is used to classify designs where the superheating elements occupy the space between the fire tube bank and the boiler outlet.
[F22G 7/105] for locomotive boilers -- F22G 7/105 covers steam superheaters designed specifically for locomotive boilers, focusing on the placement, arrangement or disposition of the superheating elements within the boiler system. This subclass falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, steam generation and superheating of steam.
[F22G 7/12] in flues -- The F22G 7/12 entry covers steam superheaters that are installed directly in the flues of a boiler, where the hot combustion gases pass. It relates to the specific location, arrangement or disposition of the superheating elements within these gas-flow passages, a subset of steam-generation technology in mechanical engineering.
[F22G 7/14] in water-tube boilers, e.g. between banks of water tubes -- This subclass covers steam superheaters installed in water-tube boilers that are placed between rows or banks of water tubes. The arrangement uses the space between tube banks to heat steam, improving heat transfer without requiring a separate superheater chamber.
[F22G 7/145] of inclined type, i.e. the water-tube sets being inclined with respect to the horizontal plane -- F22G 7/145 classifies steam superheaters whose water-tube bundles are arranged at an angle to the horizontal, i.e., inclined-type superheaters, within the mechanical-engineering field of steam generation and superheating.
[F23] COMBUSTION APPARATUS -- F23 encompasses devices and installations that produce heat or power by burning fuels, such as furnaces, boilers, incinerators, burners and related components. It also includes control and emission-reduction systems that manage the combustion process in industrial and domestic applications.
[F23B] METHODS OR APPARATUS FOR COMBUSTION USING ONLY SOLID FUEL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General function-oriented aspects of methods and apparatus for combustion of solid fuel wherein the main body of fuel is either  and wherein the combustion does not involve liquid or gaseous fuels, or pulverulent fuel burned in suspension.  The solid fuel can be in the form of lumps or pieces, e.g. coal, briquettes or firewood, or pulverulent or granulated, e.g. coal powder, wood pellets or wood chips.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion of fuels that are solid at room temperatures, but burned in melted form, e.g. candle wax -> C11C5/00 , F23C , F23D - Combustion of solid fuel suspended in a stream of air, e.g. combustion in fluidised beds or combustion of pulverised fuel using burners where the fuel is transported into the combustion chamber by an air stream -> F23C , F23D1/00 - Combustion of solid fuel suspended in a liquid, e.g. combustion of coal-water slurry -> F23C , F23D - Combustion of both solid fuel and fluent fuel, simultaneously or alternately -> F23C1/00 , F23D17/00  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The term "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Ash: means any solid combustion residues, for example remaining in the fuel bed or suspended in the flue gases - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame - Combustion: means the direct combination of oxygen gas, e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of the apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Firebridge: a low wall separating the fuel bed from adjacent flue gas passages in apparatus for combustion of solid fuel, for example in reverberatory furnaces or fire-tube boilers - Flue gases: any gaseous products of combustion - Grate: a perforated surface, e.g. a grid, which supports or delimits a bed of burning fuel and serves to supply primary air
[F23B 1/00] Combustion apparatus using only lump fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus using only lump fuel.
[F23B 10/00] Combustion apparatus characterised by the combination of two or more combustion chambers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus characterized by the combination of two or more combustion chambers.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion methods including secondary combustion in the same combustion chamber -> F23B90/04
[F23B 10/02] including separate secondary combustion chambers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus including two or more combustion chambers in which one of the combustion chambers works as a secondary combustion chamber.  Combustion apparatus including two or more physically separated combustion zones, e.g. by a perforated partition wall in a single enclosure in which one of the combustion zones works as a secondary combustion chamber.
[F23B 1/02] for indirect heating of a medium in a vessel, e.g. for boiling water  steam generation  F22 -- F23B 1/02 classifies combustion devices that heat a liquid or gas indirectly within a closed vessel, typically by burning solid lump fuel to generate steam or hot water. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus that use only solid fuel, and covers indirect heating arrangements such as boilers for steam generation.
[F23B 1/04] External furnaces, i.e. with furnace in front of the vessel -- F23B 1/04 pertains to external furnaces positioned in front of a vessel that burn only solid lump fuel, such as coal or biomass, to heat the vessel's contents. This sub-class falls under mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus designed for solid-fuel use.
[F23B 1/06] for heating water-tube boilers, e.g. Tenbrink flue furnaces -- The F23B 1/06 subclass covers combustion devices that burn only solid lump fuels to generate heat for water-tube boilers, exemplified by Tenbrink-type flue furnaces. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus using solid fuel, and addresses the design and operation of such heating systems.
[F23B 1/08] Internal furnaces, i.e. with furnaces inside the vessel -- The subclass F23B 1/08 covers internal furnaces in which the combustion chamber is located inside the same vessel that contains the material to be heated, usually using lump solid fuels such as coal or coke. It includes designs where the heating zone is integrated within the vessel rather than being external.
[F23B 1/10] for heating locomotive boilers -- The F23B 1/10 subclass covers combustion devices and methods that heat locomotive boilers by burning solid lump fuel such as coal. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus that use only solid fuel, and addresses the design, operation and control of such boiler-heating systems.
[F23B 1/12] with a plurality of combustion chambers -- F23B 1/12 covers combustion apparatus that uses only solid lump fuel and incorporates a plurality of combustion chambers, allowing the fuel to be burned in separate zones for improved control and efficiency. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within combustion apparatus designed for solid-fuel burning.
[F23B 1/16] the combustion apparatus being modified according to the form of grate or other fuel support  for incinerators  F23G5/002 -- F23B 1/16 refers to combustion apparatus in which the grate or other fuel support is specifically modified for incinerators that burn lump solid fuel. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 1/165] using roller grate -- F23B 1/165 refers to combustion apparatus that uses only solid lump fuel and incorporates a roller grate for feeding and supporting the fuel bed. The roller grate typically consists of cylindrical or drum-like elements that rotate to convey the solid fuel through the combustion zone, promoting uniform burning and efficient heat transfer. This subclass falls under methods or apparatus for combustion using solid fuel within mechanical engineering.
[F23B 1/18] using inclined grate -- F23B 1/18 is a subclass of mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus that deals specifically with devices using an inclined grate to feed and burn lump solid fuels. The classification covers furnaces, boilers or incinerators where the fuel slides down the grate by gravity while combustion occurs, allowing continuous combustion of solid fuel without additional handling mechanisms.
[F23B 1/20] using step-type grate -- A step-type grate is a combustion-air distribution device used in solid-fuel burners where the fuel is presented as lump fuel. The grate's stepped design creates a series of horizontal platforms that allow fuel to descend gradually while maintaining a controlled air flow across each level, promoting steady combustion and efficient heat release. This arrangement falls under CPC code F23B 1/20 for combustion apparatus using only solid fuel.
