[F23B 1/22] using travelling grate -- F23B 1/22 covers combustion apparatus that employs a travelling grate to feed and burn solid lump fuel, such as in furnaces or boilers. The classification pertains to methods or devices that enable continuous movement of the grate for efficient solid-fuel combustion.
[F23B 1/24] using rotating grate -- This subclass relates to combustion apparatus that feed solid lump fuel via a rotating grate, allowing continuous fuel feeding and automatic ash removal. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 1/26] using imperforate fuel supports -- F23B 1/26 designates combustion apparatus that use only solid lump fuel placed on an imperforate (solid, non-perforated) fuel support. The subclass covers devices in which the fuel support has no openings for gas flow, focusing on methods or equipment that burn solid fuel exclusively.
[F23B 1/28] using ridge-type grate, e.g. for combustion of peat, sawdust, or pulverulent fuel  combustion of peat, sawdust  F23G7/10
[F23B 1/30] characterised by the form of combustion chamber -- The subgroup F23B 1/30 covers combustion devices that burn solid lump fuel and are distinguished by the shape or geometry of the combustion chamber, such as cylindrical, annular or other configurations that influence flame stability, mixing and heat-transfer characteristics. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 1/32] rotating -- The F23B 1/32 subclass defines rotating combustion devices that burn solid lump fuel, such as rotary kilns or drum burners, where the rotation aids fuel handling, heat distribution, or combustion efficiency. It falls within the broader category of solid-fuel combustion apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F23B 1/34] annular -- The F23B 1/34 subclass covers combustion devices that use only solid, lump fuel in an annular configuration. It includes apparatus where the fuel burns within a ring-shaped chamber or where the flame front travels around a circular path, providing uniform heat distribution for heating or process applications. This design is used mainly in mechanical engineering for solid-fuel boilers and furnaces.
[F23B 1/36] shaft-type -- The F23B 1/36 classification covers shaft-type combustion apparatus that burn solid lump fuel. In these devices the fuel is introduced into a rotating shaft where it moves longitudinally while being heated and ignited, providing a continuous solid-fuel fire for heating or power generation. This arrangement is used in boilers and furnaces where compact, mechanically driven fuel handling is required.
[F23B 1/38] for combustion of peat, sawdust, or pulverulent fuel on a grate or other fuel support  combustion of peat, sawdust  F23G7/10 -- The subclass F23B 1/38 covers combustion devices that burn solid fuels such as peat, sawdust or finely divided fuels on a grate or other fuel support, typically in boilers or heating equipment. It is part of the broader group dealing with methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 20/00] Combustion apparatus specially adapted for portability or transportability -- F23B 20/00 covers combustion devices designed to be easily moved or carried, such as portable stoves, mobile burners, and transportable heating units that operate on solid fuels. The classification targets apparatus whose construction, mounting or accessories facilitate relocation or use in temporary settings.
[F23B 2101/00] Indexing scheme related to adaptation of combustion apparatus to boilers -- The F23B 2101/00 subgroup defines indexing schemes that relate to adapting solid-fuel combustion apparatus for use with boilers. It covers classification structures, coding methods and reference systems that organise design features, operating parameters and integration aspects of such combustion-boiler adaptations.
[F23B 2103/00] Adaptation of combustion apparatus for placement in or against an opening of a boiler, e.g. for replacing an oil burner -- The code covers modifications that allow a solid-fuel combustion device to be installed directly into or against a boiler opening, typically to substitute an existing oil burner. It includes structural and sealing adaptations that enable the new apparatus to integrate with the boiler housing while maintaining safe operation.
[F23B 2103/02] for producing an essentially horizontal flame -- The F23B 2103/02 subclass covers combustion apparatus adapted for installation in or against a boiler opening, such as a retrofit replacing an oil burner, that operates on solid fuel and generates an essentially horizontal flame. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for solid-fuel combustion.
[F23B 2700/00] Combustion apparatus for solid fuel -- F23B 2700/00 covers devices and systems that burn solid fuels, including furnaces, stoves, boilers and incinerators designed to generate heat or power exclusively from materials such as coal, wood, charcoal or pellets. The classification addresses the mechanical features of the combustion chamber, feed mechanisms, air supply and exhaust arrangements that enable efficient solid-fuel combustion.
[F23B 2700/003] adapted for use in water-tube boilers -- F23B 2700/003 pertains to solid-fuel combustion equipment that has been modified for incorporation into water-tube boilers, encompassing burners, grates or related components adapted to the flow and heat-transfer characteristics of this boiler type.
[F23B 2700/004] adapted for use in Tenbrink boilers -- F23B 2700/004 designates combustion apparatus for solid fuel that has been modified for integration into Tenbrink boilers, a specific type of solid-fuel boiler system. The subclass is part of the broader group covering methods or apparatus for solid-fuel combustion within mechanical engineering.
[F23B 2700/005] adapted for use in locomotives -- F23B 2700/005 denotes a combustion apparatus designed for solid fuels that is specifically adapted for use in locomotives. It falls within the mechanical engineering subclass for combustion apparatus employing only solid fuel, targeting railway propulsion systems.
[F23B 2700/006] Details of locomotive combustion apparatus -- F23B 2700/006 classifies the specific components and design features of locomotive combustion apparatus that burn solid fuel. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel, and covers details such as fireboxes, grates and fuel-feeding mechanisms.
[F23B 2700/007] with pressurised combustion chambers -- This CPC subclass covers devices that burn solid fuel in chambers that are kept at a pressure above atmospheric, such as pressurised furnaces, boilers or stoves. It includes features that maintain the pressure to improve combustion efficiency or control emissions.
[F23B 2700/008] with interchangeable combustion chambers -- The code identifies solid-fuel combustion devices that feature interchangeable combustion chambers, enabling the user to swap chambers for different fuels or performance requirements. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 2700/009] adapted for use in various steam boilers -- The code F23B 2700/009 covers solid-fuel combustion devices that are specifically adapted for use in a range of steam-boiler configurations. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel, describing designs that can be integrated into different boiler types.
[F23B 2700/01] adapted for boilers built up from sections -- This subclass covers combustion devices using only solid fuel that are specially designed or modified to fit boilers constructed from separate sections or modules. It includes features that enable integration of the firebox or furnace into a sectional-boiler structure, facilitating assembly, maintenance or scaling.
[F23B 2700/011] with fuel shaft for steam boilers -- This subclass covers solid-fuel combustion apparatus that employ a fuel shaft to deliver solid material into steam boilers, focusing on the mechanical arrangement for feeding fuels such as coal, wood or biomass into the furnace.
[F23B 2700/012] with predrying in fuel supply area -- A combustion apparatus for solid fuel that incorporates a predrying stage in the fuel-supply area, drying the solid fuel before it enters the combustion zone to improve efficiency and reduce emissions, classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, solid-fuel combustion methods.
[F23B 2700/013] for use in baking ovens or cooking vessels -- The F23B 2700/013 subclass covers combustion devices that use solid fuel and are intended specifically for baking ovens or cooking vessels. These apparatuses are designed to generate heat for culinary heating processes, distinguishing them from broader solid-fuel combustion equipment used in other industrial or heating contexts.
[F23B 2700/014] for use in reverberatory furnaces -- The CPC subclass F23B 2700/014 pertains to apparatus used in reverberatory furnaces that burn solid fuel, focusing on designs that reflect and distribute heat within the furnace, often applied in metalworking or glass production.
[F23B 2700/018] with fume afterburning by staged combustion -- The CPC subclass F23B 2700/018 concerns solid-fuel combustion devices that incorporate fume afterburning through staged combustion, where exhaust gases are passed into a secondary combustion zone to complete oxidation and lower pollutant emissions.
[F23B 2700/022] with various types of fume afterburners -- F23B 2700/022 covers solid-fuel combustion devices that incorporate one or more fume afterburners of various designs, used to further burn residual gases from the primary flame and reduce emissions. The subclass pertains to the arrangement, operation or integration of these afterburning stages within the overall apparatus.
[F23B 2700/023] with various arrangements not otherwise provided for -- F23B 2700/023 covers solid-fuel combustion devices that use miscellaneous or non-standard configurations not classified elsewhere. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus for solid fuel, and captures innovative or unusual arrangements of burners, chambers, or fuel-feeding systems.
[F23B 2700/037] Burners for solid or solidified fuel, e.g. metaldehyde blocks -- F23B 2700/037 relates to burners specifically engineered for solid or solidified fuels such as metaldehyde blocks, used in combustion apparatus that operate only on solid fuel. The classification addresses designs that improve fuel feeding, ignition, and combustion efficiency while controlling emissions.
[F23B 2900/00] Special features of, or arrangements for combustion apparatus using solid fuels -- F23B 2900/00 covers special features and arrangements of combustion apparatus that operate on solid fuels. It includes designs for fuel feeding, storage, ignition and control systems that improve efficiency, safety or adaptability of solid-fuel burners, furnaces and boilers.
[F23B 2900/00001] Combustion chambers with integrated fuel hopper -- The entry defines designs for combustion chambers that incorporate an internal fuel hopper, enabling solid fuel to be stored and delivered directly within the chamber. This integrated arrangement streamlines fuel feeding, improves combustion control, and eliminates separate external hopper connections.
[F23B 2900/00003] Combustion devices specially adapted for burning metal fuels, e.g. Al or Mg -- F23B 2900/00003 covers combustion devices designed specifically to burn solid metal fuels, such as aluminium or magnesium, including their special features or arrangements. It falls under methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel within the broader categories of mechanical engineering and combustion apparatus.
[F23B 2900/00004] Means for generating pulsating combustion of solid fuel -- This subclass covers devices that create a pulsating or oscillating combustion process when burning solid fuels, such as wood, coal or solid waste. It includes mechanisms like resonant chambers, pulsating burners, acoustic drivers or flow-modulating structures that repeatedly ignite or extinguish the flame to generate periodic pressure or temperature fluctuations, improving combustion efficiency, emissions control or heat transfer.
[F23B 2900/00005] Means for applying acoustical energy to flame -- F23B 2900/00005 covers devices that direct acoustic energy onto a flame, such as speakers or ultrasonic transducers positioned near solid-fuel burners, to modify combustion characteristics and improve flame stability or efficiency.
[F23B 2900/00006] Means for applying electricity to flame, e.g. an electric field -- F23B 2900/00006 covers devices that apply electricity to a flame, such as using an electric field to influence combustion, within solid-fuel combustion apparatus. It pertains to methods or arrangements that enhance or control flame behavior by electrically charged means, typically integrated into mechanical engineering combustion systems.
[F23B 2900/99001] Retrofitting or converting solid fuel stoves to gas or liquid fuels -- This CPC subclass covers technologies for modifying existing solid-fuel stoves so they can operate on gas or liquid fuels. It includes designs, kits or methods that retrofit burners, fuel feeds and control systems to enable alternative fuel use while retaining the original stove structure. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and special arrangements for solid-fuel combustion.
[F23B 3/00] Combustion apparatus which is portable or removable with respect to the boiler or other apparatus which is heated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus which is portable or removable with respect to the boiler or other apparatus which is heated.
[F23B 30/00] Combustion apparatus with driven means for agitating the burning fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus using e.g. rotary, vibrating or travelling grates, reciprocating bars or pushers, rocking bars, screw conveyors or any other means located inside the combustion chamber for agitating or advancing the fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion grates or details of grates per se -> F23H
[F23B 30/02] with movable, e.g. vibratable, fuel-supporting surfaces -- The code F23B 30/02 covers combustion devices that employ movable or vibratable surfaces to support solid fuel, providing a driven means to agitate the burning material and improve flame stability and efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and solid-fuel combustion methods.
[F23B 30/04] with fuel-supporting surfaces that are rotatable around a horizontal or inclined axis and support the fuel on their inside, e.g. cylindrical grates -- The code designates solid-fuel combustion devices that employ driven, fuel-supporting surfaces rotating around a horizontal or inclined axis, such as cylindrical grates that turn to agitate and hold the burning material.
[F23B 30/06] with fuel supporting surfaces that are specially adapted for advancing fuel through the combustion zone -- F23B 30/06 refers to combustion devices that incorporate fuel-supporting surfaces specially shaped to advance solid fuel through the combustion zone, often using driven means to agitate the fuel. It is a subclass of methods or apparatus for solid-fuel combustion within mechanical engineering.
[F23B 30/08] with fuel-supporting surfaces that move through the combustion zone, e.g. with chain grates -- F23B 30/08 covers combustion devices in which the surfaces that support solid fuel move through the burning zone, for example chain-grate systems that continuously stir or transport the burning material.
[F23B 30/10] with fuel-supporting surfaces having fuel advancing elements that are movable, but remain essentially in the same place, e.g. with rollers or reciprocating grate bars -- A subclass of solid-fuel combustion devices that incorporates fuel-supporting surfaces equipped with movable elements such as rollers or reciprocating grate bars, which advance the fuel while remaining essentially fixed in place. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 40/00] Combustion apparatus with driven means for feeding fuel into the combustion chamber -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus using e.g. throwing devices, reciprocating pushers, screw conveyors or other driven means located outside the combustion chamber for supplying fuel directly into the combustion chamber.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion apparatus wherein the driven means, located outside the combustion chamber, advance the fuel only up to a location above the burning bed, from where it proceeds by gravity, e.g. on a slide or chute -> F23B50/12
[F23B 40/02] the fuel being fed by scattering over the fuel-supporting surface -- F23B 40/02 covers solid-fuel combustion apparatus in which the fuel is introduced by scattering it over the surface that supports the combustion zone, such as a grate or moving bed, rather than by pre-forming a continuous feed or using a chute. This method facilitates even distribution and efficient burning of loose solid particles.
[F23B 40/04] the fuel being fed from below through an opening in the fuel-supporting surface -- The subgroup pertains to solid-fuel combustion devices that introduce fuel from below by passing it through an opening in the fuel-supporting surface, usually employing a mechanically driven feeding mechanism.
[F23B 40/06] the fuel being fed along the fuel-supporting surface -- F23B 40/06 covers combustion devices in which solid fuel is introduced to the chamber by moving it along a surface that supports the fuel, such as belts, rollers or sliding plates. The driven feeding means transport the fuel continuously or intermittently across the support, ensuring controlled delivery into the combustion zone. This subclass is used for solid-fuel burners where the fuel-supporting surface plays a key role in the feeding process.
[F23B 40/08] into pot- or trough-shaped grates -- F23B 40/08 relates to combustion devices that employ driven mechanisms to deliver solid fuel into a pot- or trough-shaped grate, the grate shape guiding fuel distribution and supporting efficient burning in stoves, furnaces or similar apparatus.
[F23B 5/00] Combustion apparatus with arrangements for burning uncombusted material from primary combustion  combustion apparatus characterised by the combination of two or more combustion chambers  F23C6/00 ; the primary combustion being pulverulent fuel  F23C9/003 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus with arrangements for burning uncombusted material from primary combustion.
[F23B 50/00] Combustion apparatus in which the fuel is fed into or through the combustion zone by gravity, e.g. from a fuel storage situated above the combustion zone -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus using gravity as sole means for feeding the fuel into the combustion zone.
[F23B 50/02] the fuel forming a column, stack or thick layer with the combustion zone at its bottom -- A combustion apparatus in which solid fuel is supplied by gravity from a storage above the fire, forming a vertical column, stack or thick layer with the combustion zone situated at the bottom. This falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Methods or Apparatus for Combustion Using Only Solid Fuel, CPC code F23B 50/02.
[F23B 50/04] the movement of combustion air and flue gases being substantially transverse to the movement of the fuel -- This subclass covers solid-fuel combustion devices where the fuel is introduced by gravity from a storage above the firebox, and the primary airflow and exhaust gases flow essentially across, rather than along, the path of the falling fuel. The transverse movement of air and flue gases improves mixing and combustion efficiency. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, solid-fuel methods.
[F23B 50/06] the flue gases being removed downwards through one or more openings in the fuel-supporting surface -- This subclass pertains to solid-fuel combustion apparatus where gravity feeds the fuel and the flue gases are removed downward through one or more openings in the surface that supports the fuel.
[F23B 50/08] with fuel-deflecting bodies forming free combustion spaces inside the fuel layer -- F23B 50/08 covers combustion devices that feed solid fuel by gravity from a storage above the fire zone, employing internal fuel-deflecting bodies that create open spaces within the fuel layer to improve air flow and combustion efficiency.
[F23B 50/10] with the combustion zone at the bottom of fuel-filled conduits ending at the surface of a fuel bed -- A subclass of solid-fuel combustion devices in which gravity feeds fuel from a storage above the combustion zone, with the flame zone situated at the bottom of fuel-filled conduits that terminate at the surface of a fuel bed. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 50/12] the fuel being fed to the combustion zone by free fall or by sliding along inclined surfaces, e.g. from a conveyor terminating above the fuel bed -- This classification covers solid-fuel combustion devices where the fuel is delivered to the combustion zone solely by gravity, such as dropping from a storage container above the fire or sliding down inclined surfaces like a conveyor. The feed method relies on free fall or slope-driven movement rather than mechanical forcing. It applies to apparatuses designed for solid fuel combustion using these gravity-based feeding techniques.
[F23B 5/02] in main combustion chamber -- The code F23B 5/02 pertains to combustion devices in which the treatment of unburned material-such as secondary burning, reheating, or oxidation-takes place inside the main combustion chamber rather than in a separate secondary chamber, typically in systems that use solid or pulverised fuels and may have multiple chambers.
[F23B 5/025] recirculating uncombusted solids to combustion chamber -- This subclass addresses combustion devices that recycle solid particles that have not fully burned in a primary chamber back into a secondary combustion chamber, typically when the primary fuel is a pulverised solid. It includes arrangements with two or more chambers aimed at improving fuel utilization and reducing emissions.
[F23B 5/04] in separate combustion chamber; on separate grate -- F23B 5/04 covers solid-fuel combustion devices where material that escapes primary burning is routed to an additional chamber for secondary combustion, the secondary zone being provided with its own grate or support surface. The arrangement uses two or more chambers to improve fuel utilisation and reduce emissions.
[F23B 60/00] Combustion apparatus in which the fuel burns essentially without moving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus wherein the fuel is loaded on a grate or on a support on which it rests till complete exhaustion without further move or agitation.
[F23B 60/02] with combustion air supplied through a grate -- The subclass F23B 60/02 covers solid-fuel combustion devices in which the fuel remains essentially stationary while combustion air is delivered through a grate, allowing the air to pass upward or sideways to the burning material. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and solid-fuel combustion methods.
[F23B 7/00] Combustion techniques; Other solid-fuel combustion apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion techniques; Other solid-fuel combustion apparatus.
[F23B 70/00] Combustion apparatus characterised by means returning solid combustion residues to the combustion chamber -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus wherein solid combustion residues, e.g. ashes or fly-ashes are returned to the chamber for complete burning.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fluidised bed combustors for solid fuels. -> F23C10/002
[F23B 7/002] characterised by gas flow arrangements -- The subclass F23B 7/002 covers solid-fuel combustion devices whose distinguishing feature is the arrangement of gas flow paths, including the design of inlet, outlet and circulation channels that control the supply of air or combustion gases to improve efficiency, flame stability and pollutant reduction.
[F23B 7/005] with downdraught through fuel bed and grate -- F23B 7/005 covers solid-fuel combustion devices that use a downdraught passing through the fuel bed and grate to improve airflow and ash removal. It is a sub-class of methods or apparatus for combustion using only solid fuel within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F23B 7/007] with fluegas recirculation to combustion chamber -- This subclass pertains to solid-fuel combustion systems that include a mechanism for recirculating flue gases back into the combustion chamber, typically to enhance fuel efficiency, temperature control, or emission reduction. It covers apparatuses and methods where the recirculated gases mix with incoming fuel or air before combustion.
[F23B 80/00] Combustion apparatus characterised by means creating a distinct flow path for flue gases or for non-combusted gases given off by the fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus having either means guiding the flue gas along specific path, e.g. for increasing heat transfer to the heat recovery means or creating longer permanence time of the gases in the combustion chamber, or for flue gas recirculation.
[F23B 80/02] by means for returning flue gases to the combustion chamber or to the combustion zone -- A combustion apparatus that includes a system for returning flue gases to the combustion chamber or zone, thereby recycling heat and unburned gases to improve efficiency and reduce emissions. This arrangement is classified under CPC F23B 80/02 within mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, solid-fuel combustion methods.
[F23B 80/04] by means for guiding the flow of flue gases, e.g. baffles -- The code F23B 80/04 covers solid-fuel combustion devices that incorporate elements such as baffles or other guides to create a defined flow path for flue gases or other non-combusted gases. These flow-directing means improve draft control, heat distribution, or emission reduction by shaping the gas movement within the apparatus.
[F23B 90/00] Combustion methods not related to a particular type of apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for starting the combustion, for staged combustion, i.e. a first combustion in a oxidant deficient atmosphere followed by secondary combustion in oxidant rich atmosphere, or for catalytic combustion.
[F23B 90/02] Start-up techniques -- F23B 90/02 relates to start-up techniques for solid-fuel combustion systems, covering methods used to initiate and stabilize the burning process independent of any particular apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods for combustion using only solid fuel.
[F23B 90/04] including secondary combustion -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion apparatus including separate secondary combustion chambers -> F23B10/02
[F23B 90/06] the primary combustion being a gasification or pyrolysis in a reductive atmosphere -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Among combustion methods including secondary combustion, those involving gasification or pyrolysis in a reductive atmosphere in the primary zone, and, involving combustion in an oxidative atmosphere in the secondary zone.
[F23B 90/08] in the presence of catalytic material -- This subclass covers solid-fuel combustion processes that employ a catalyst to promote or modify the reaction, such as charcoal or coal burning with catalytic additives, without specifying a particular device. It is used for methods of igniting, sustaining or controlling solid fuel flames in the presence of catalytic material.
[F23B 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F23B 99/00 is a residual group in the CPC for solid-fuel combustion devices that are not covered by any other subclass subgroup. It serves as a catch-all for inventions involving burners, furnaces, or related apparatus where the specific technical features fall outside the defined categories. The provision ensures that novel solid-fuel combustion concepts can still be classified.
[F23C] METHODS OR APPARATUS FOR COMBUSTION USING FLUID FUEL OR SOLID FUEL SUSPENDED IN AIR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General function-oriented aspects of methods and apparatus for combustion of  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Burners -> F23D  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel, e.g. together with the fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The expression "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame. A burner includes means for feeding air that are arranged in immediate connection with a fuel feeding conduit, for example concentric with it. - Combustion: a heat-producing sequence of chemical reactions between a burnable substance and molecular oxygen, e.g. in air in most cases generating light in the form of flames or a glow - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of an apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel
[F23C 1/00] Combustion apparatus specially adapted for combustion of two or more kinds of fuel simultaneously or alternately, at least one kind of fuel being either a fluid fuel or a solid fuel suspended in air -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion apparatus characterised by the combination of two or more -> F23C6/00 - Pilot flame igniters -> F23Q9/00
[F23C 10/00] Fluidised bed combustion apparatus -- F23C 10/00 covers apparatus for fluidised-bed combustion, where solid or fluid fuel is suspended in a stream of air that fluidises a bed of particulate material. The device typically includes means for feeding fuel, maintaining the fluidised bed, igniting and controlling combustion, and extracting heat for power generation or industrial processes.
[F23C 10/002] for pulverulent solid fuel  F23C10/005  -  F23C10/32  take precedence
[F23C 10/005] comprising two or more beds -- The subclass F23C 10/005 defines fluidised-bed combustion apparatus that incorporates two or more distinct beds within a single unit, enabling staged combustion, improved heat management, or fuel-type flexibility. It falls under mechanical engineering classifications for combustion devices using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 10/007] comprising a rotating bed -- This subclass covers fluidised-bed combustion devices that incorporate a rotating bed to improve mixing and heat transfer. The rotating bed maintains uniform fluidisation of fuel particles and ash, enhancing combustion efficiency and reducing emissions.
[F23C 10/01] in a fluidised bed of catalytic particles -- F23C 10/01 relates to fluidised-bed combustion apparatus that incorporates catalytic particles within the bed to promote or accelerate the combustion of solid or fluid fuels suspended in air. The presence of the catalyst improves reaction rates, enables lower operating temperatures and can reduce emissions in these combustion systems.
[F23C 10/02] with means specially adapted for achieving or promoting a circulating movement of particles within the bed or for a recirculation of particles entrained from the bed -- The code F23C 10/02 covers fluidised-bed combustion devices that include features designed to create or enhance the circulation of particles within the bed, or to recirculate particles that have been entrained from the bed. It is a subcategory of methods or apparatuses for combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, falling under the broader field of mechanical engineering combustion apparatus.
[F23C 10/04] the particles being circulated to a section, e.g. a heat-exchange section or a return duct, at least partially shielded from the combustion zone, before being reintroduced into the combustion zone -- The entry covers fluidised-bed combustion devices in which solid or fluid-fuel particles are routed from the main combustion zone to a heat-exchange section or a return duct that is at least partially insulated from the flame, and then recirculated back into the combustion area.
[F23C 10/06] the circulating movement being promoted by inducing differing degrees of fluidisation in different parts of the bed -- This subclass covers fluidised-bed combustion apparatus in which the circulation of the bed material is encouraged by creating zones with different degrees of fluidisation, causing a directed movement of particles across the bed. The approach uses varying fluidisation intensity in separate regions to promote the circulating flow.
[F23C 10/08] characterised by the arrangement of separation apparatus, e.g. cyclones, for separating particles from the flue gases -- F23C 10/08 covers fluidised-bed combustion equipment in which the design includes separation devices, such as cyclones, positioned to remove solid particles from the flue gases. The classification is used for patents describing the arrangement of these cyclonic separators within the combustion system.
[F23C 10/10] the separation apparatus being located outside the combustion chamber -- The code F23C 10/10 covers fluidised-bed combustion devices in which the apparatus for separating solid particles from the flue gas is positioned external to the combustion chamber. It pertains to systems that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air and require downstream removal of unburned material or ash before the gas leaves the plant.
[F23C 10/12] the particles being circulated exclusively within the combustion zone -- The subclass F23C 10/12 describes fluidised-bed combustion devices where the solid particles acting as fuel are recirculated only within the combustion zone, without external transport. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 10/14] the circulating movement being promoted by inducing differing degrees of fluidisation in different parts of the bed -- The code F23C 10/14 covers fluidised-bed combustion equipment in which a circulating flow is created by applying different fluidisation intensities to separate zones of the bed. By varying the degree of fluidisation, the upper region is more fluidised than the lower, generating upward and downward currents that improve mixing, heat transfer and solid-fuel handling.
[F23C 10/16] specially adapted for operation at superatmospheric pressures, e.g. by the arrangement of the combustion chamber and its auxiliary systems inside a pressure vessel -- This subclass relates to fluidised-bed combustion apparatus that are designed to operate at pressures above atmospheric, typically by placing the combustion chamber and its auxiliary systems inside a pressure-rated vessel. Such designs enable higher combustion efficiency and fuel flexibility under super-pressurized conditions.
[F23C 10/18] Details -- The F23C 10/18 subclass covers specific structural and functional details of fluidised-bed combustion apparatus, such as the design of the bed, fuel feed mechanisms, air distribution, and heat-exchange elements used when burning fluid or solid fuels suspended in air. It focuses on the engineering features that enable controlled, efficient combustion in a fluidised medium.
[F23C 1/02] lump and liquid fuel -- F23C 1/02 covers combustion devices designed to burn a mixture of solid lump fuel and liquid fuel, either simultaneously or in sequence, within the same apparatus. The classification addresses arrangements where the solid fuel is suspended in air and the liquid fuel is introduced as a fluid, enabling combined combustion for improved efficiency or flexibility.
[F23C 10/20] Inlets for fluidisation air, e.g. grids -- F23C 10/20 pertains to inlet devices that supply the fluidisation air in fluidised-bed combustion systems, including grids, perforated plates or other structures designed to distribute air uniformly across the bed.
[F23C 10/22] Fuel feeders specially adapted for fluidised bed combustion apparatus -- Fuel feeders specially adapted for fluidised-bed combustion apparatus comprise devices that introduce solid or pulverised fuel into a circulating bed of air and particles, ensuring uniform distribution and stable combustion. Typical designs include screw feeders, rotary valves or pneumatic distributors engineered to withstand high temperatures and to regulate feed rates precisely.
[F23C 10/24] Devices for removal of material from the bed -- The F23C 10/24 class covers equipment used in fluidised-bed combustion systems to extract solid material from the combustion bed, such as ash, unburned particles or spent fuel. Typical devices include conveyor belts, screw extractors, pneumatic lifts or rotary valves that discharge material while maintaining bed fluidisation and overall process stability.
[F23C 10/26] combined with devices for partial reintroduction of material into the bed, e.g. after separation of agglomerated parts
[F23C 10/28] Control devices specially adapted for fluidised bed, combustion apparatus -- Control devices designed specifically for fluidised-bed combustion systems, including mechanisms that regulate air or gas flow, fuel feeding, temperature, pressure or mixing within the bed to optimise combustion efficiency and stability. These devices may incorporate sensors, actuators or feedback loops tailored to the unique behaviour of fluidised-bed reactors.
[F23C 10/30] for controlling the level of the bed or the amount of material in the bed -- F23C 10/30 defines apparatus and control systems that regulate the level or amount of material in a fluidised-bed combustion chamber for fluid or solid fuel combustion. The subclass includes mechanisms for adjusting bed height, feed rate, or material distribution to maintain optimal combustion conditions.
[F23C 10/32] by controlling the rate of recirculation of particles separated from the flue gases -- This subclass covers fluidised-bed combustion apparatus that includes a system for separating particles from the flue gases and adjusting the rate at which those particles are recirculated, thereby controlling combustion temperature, efficiency and emissions. It falls within the broader category of methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 1/04] lump and gaseous fuel -- The F23C 1/04 class covers combustion devices designed to burn two or more fuels together or in succession, where at least one fuel is a fluid or a solid suspended in air. It specifically includes apparatus that can handle both lump (solid) fuel and gaseous fuel in the same system.
[F23C 1/06] lump and pulverulent fuel -- The code F23C 1/06 covers combustion devices designed to burn two or more fuels at the same time or in sequence, where at least one fuel is a fluid or a solid fuel dispersed in air, such as lump and pulverulent fuels. These apparatuses are used in mechanical engineering for mixed-fuel combustion applications.
[F23C 1/08] liquid and gaseous fuel -- The subclass classifies combustion devices built to burn two or more fuels either simultaneously or alternately, with at least one fuel being a liquid or gaseous fuel (or a solid fuel suspended in air). It covers apparatuses that can switch between or mix these fuels during operation.
[F23C 1/10] liquid and pulverulent fuel -- F23C 1/10 covers combustion devices designed to burn a mixture of liquid fuel and pulverised solid fuel, or to switch between these fuel types. The apparatus handles fuels that are introduced as a fluid or as fine particles suspended in air, enabling simultaneous or alternating use of the two forms.
[F23C 1/12] gaseous and pulverulent fuel -- F23C 1/12 covers combustion devices designed to burn a mixture of gas and finely divided solid fuel, such as powdered coal, either together or in succession. The classification applies to apparatus that can handle at least one fluid fuel or a solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 13/00] Apparatus in which combustion takes place in the presence of catalytic material -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion in a fluidised bed of catalytic particles -> F23C10/01 - Radiant gas burners using catalysis for flameless combustion -> F23D14/18
[F23C 13/02] characterised by arrangements for starting the operation, e.g. for heating the catalytic material to operating temperature -- F23C 13/02 relates to combustion devices that incorporate a catalyst and specifies features for initiating operation, such as mechanisms that pre-heat the catalytic material to its required temperature. The code applies to apparatuses where starting arrangements ensure the catalyst reaches effective operating conditions before normal combustion proceeds.
[F23C 13/04] characterised by arrangements of two or more catalytic elements in series connection -- The entry covers combustion devices that employ catalytic material and specifically arrange two or more catalytic elements in a series configuration. This series setup allows the fuel-air mixture to pass sequentially over multiple catalysts, enhancing reaction control and efficiency in fluid-fuel or solid-fuel-in-air combustors.
[F23C 13/06] in which non-catalytic combustion takes place in addition to catalytic combustion, e.g. downstream of a catalytic element -- This subclass refers to combustion apparatus in which, after the fuel passes over a catalytic element, further non-catalytic combustion occurs downstream of the catalyst, providing additional flame stabilization or heat release in devices that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 13/08] characterised by the catalytic material -- This subclass covers combustion devices that employ a catalytic material to enhance or accelerate the burning of fluid or solid fuels dispersed in air, such as catalytic burners or heated catalysts. These arrangements are used in heating, power generation, or emission-control applications.
[F23C 15/00] Apparatus in which combustion takes place in pulses influenced by acoustic resonance in a gas mass -- An apparatus that creates pulsed combustion by exploiting acoustic resonance within a gas volume, where pressure waves enhance flame stability and efficiency, is used in burners, engines or turbines to improve fuel utilization and reduce emissions.
[F23C 2200/00] Combustion techniques for fluent fuel -- F23C 2200/00 covers methods and apparatus for combusting fluid fuels, including techniques for fuel injection, atomisation, air-fuel mixing, flame stabilization and control within burners or combustion chambers.
[F23C 2201/00] Staged combustion -- F23C 2201/00 pertains to staged combustion, a technique that introduces fuel and oxidiser in separate zones so that the flame develops progressively, allowing better temperature control and lower emissions. It is applied in boilers, furnaces and gas-turbine combustors that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 2201/10] Furnace staging -- F23C 2201/10 (Furnace staging) pertains to methods and apparatus that introduce fuel into a furnace in multiple, separate zones to control temperature, improve efficiency and lower emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, and addresses staged combustion techniques for better heat distribution and pollutant reduction.
[F23C 2201/101] in vertical direction, e.g. alternating lean and rich zones -- This subclass refers to staged-combustion arrangements in which the fuel-air mixture is stratified vertically, forming alternating lean and rich zones along the height of the combustor. Such vertical zoning is used to enhance efficiency, control emissions, and improve flame stability in burners that suspend fluid or solid fuel particles in air.
[F23C 2201/102] in horizontal direction -- A staged-combustion arrangement in which the combustion zone propagates primarily in a horizontal direction, applied to devices that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air. It falls under mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus, specifically methods or apparatus for combustion using suspended fuels.
[F23C 2201/20] Burner staging -- F23C 2201/20 - Burner staging relates to combustion devices that divide the flame or fuel-air mixture into two or more distinct zones, typically a primary zone followed by one or more secondary zones, to control temperature, improve efficiency and reduce emissions. The classification covers methods and apparatuses for arranging burners or injectors to achieve staged combustion in boilers, furnaces, or similar equipment.
[F23C 2201/30] Staged fuel supply -- The code F23C 2201/30 covers arrangements for delivering fuel to a combustion device in successive portions rather than continuously. Staged fuel supply methods are used in burners or furnaces to control the air-fuel mix, improve combustion stability, reduce emissions and enhance thermal efficiency in systems that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 2201/301] with different fuels in stages -- The F23C 2201/301 subclass covers staged-combustion systems that introduce two or more different fuels at separate points in the combustion process, allowing each fuel to burn under distinct conditions for improved efficiency, emissions control, or temperature management. This includes designs where a primary fuel is ignited first and one or more secondary fuels are added later in the combustion chamber.
[F23C 2201/40] Intermediate treatments between stages -- F23C 2201/40 covers technologies that treat the combustion gases or fuel between successive stages of a staged combustion process, typically to improve efficiency, reduce emissions, or control temperature. It applies to systems using fluid or solid fuels suspended in air where intermediate chambers, mixers, or reactors are inserted between the primary and secondary combustion zones.
[F23C 2201/401] Cooling -- This CPC subclass deals with cooling systems for staged-combustion apparatus that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air, covering methods and devices for removing heat from the combustion zone using water, air, or other cooling media.
[F23C 2202/00] Fluegas recirculation -- F23C 2202/00 - Fluegas recirculation covers methods and apparatus designed to redirect a portion of exhaust gases back into the combustion chamber to improve efficiency, reduce emissions, and control temperature. It is used in various combustion systems where fuel is burned in air, such as boilers, furnaces, and internal combustion engines.
[F23C 2202/10] Premixing fluegas with fuel and combustion air -- F23C 2202/10 covers the premixing of flue gas with fuel and combustion air in combustion systems, a method that improves mixture uniformity and helps control emissions in burners and furnaces that recycle exhaust gases.
[F23C 2202/20] Premixing fluegas with fuel -- The code F23C 2202/20 covers devices or methods that premix recirculated flue gas with incoming fuel before combustion, improving flame stability, lowering nitrogen-oxide emissions and enhancing overall thermal efficiency in burners, furnaces and similar combustion systems.
[F23C 2202/30] Premixing fluegas with combustion air -- F23C 2202/30 covers combustion systems in which flue gases are mixed with fresh combustion air before ignition, a form of flue-gas recirculation used to control temperature, improve fuel efficiency and lower pollutant formation in burners and furnaces. It applies to methods and apparatus that deliberately premix recycled exhaust with the incoming oxidizer.
[F23C 2202/40] Inducing local whirls around flame
[F23C 2202/50] Control of recirculation rate -- F23C 2202/50 concerns the control of flue-gas recirculation rate in combustion equipment that burns fluid or solid fuels suspended in air, covering systems and methods that adjust the amount of exhaust gas mixed back into the combustion zone to regulate temperature, efficiency and emissions.
[F23C 2203/00] Flame cooling methods otherwise than by staging or recirculation -- F23C 2203/00 covers techniques for reducing flame temperature in combustion devices that do not rely on staging the flame or recirculating combustion gases. Methods may involve direct cooling with liquids, sprays, heat-absorbing surfaces, or other means that lower flame temperature while maintaining combustion.
[F23C 2203/10] using heat exchanger -- It covers combustion systems that cool the flame by passing flame gases through a heat exchanger, rather than by staging or recirculation techniques. The method applies to fluid or solid fuels suspended in air, typically in burners or furnaces.
[F23C 2203/20] using heat absorbing device in flame -- F23C 2203/20 relates to combustion apparatuses in which a heat-absorbing device is placed directly in the flame to cool it, rather than using staging or recirculation. The classification applies to methods or equipment that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air and employ this flame-cooling technique to control temperature, emissions, or stability.
[F23C 2203/30] Injection of tempering fluids -- The subclass addresses flame-cooling techniques that use injection of tempering fluids, such as water, steam or cooling gases, directly into the combustion zone, excluding methods based on staging or recirculation. It applies to combustion apparatus where fluid or solid fuel is suspended in air and seeks to control temperature, emissions or stability by diluting or absorbing heat in the flame.
[F23C 2205/00] Pulsating combustion -- Pulsating combustion refers to a cyclic, intermittent form of burning where the flame or reaction zone repeatedly ignites, extinguishes, and reignites, creating pressure and temperature oscillations. This mode is used in engines and burners to enhance mixing of fuel and air, improve combustion stability, and increase thermal efficiency.
[F23C 2205/10] with pulsating fuel supply -- The subclass F23C 2205/10 covers combustion systems that deliver fluid or solid-in-air fuel in a pulsating or intermittent manner. It relates to methods and apparatus where the fuel flow is modulated to create pressure or flame oscillations, improving mixing, stability or efficiency. Typical applications include burners, engines and boilers that employ a pulsating fuel supply.
[F23C 2205/20] with pulsating oxidant supply -- F23C 2205/20 covers combustion devices or processes in which the supply of oxidant is intermittently varied, creating pulsating flow to improve flame stability or efficiency. It applies to systems using fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air, such as burners or furnaces that modulate air or oxygen delivery.
[F23C 2206/00] Fluidised bed combustion -- Fluidised bed combustion uses a stream of air or gas to keep solid particles, such as sand or ash, in suspension, creating a fluid-like bed in which powdered solid fuel or liquid droplets are burned. The turbulent mixing promotes uniform temperature, high combustion efficiency and lower pollutant formation, and the technology is applied in power plants and industrial furnaces.
[F23C 2206/10] Circulating fluidised bed -- The CPC subclass F23C 2206/10 covers circulating fluidised bed systems, a type of combustion apparatus where solid fuel particles are suspended in an upward gas stream that circulates the bed material through a riser and a separator, enabling uniform temperature distribution and efficient fuel burning with reduced emissions.
[F23C 2206/101] Entrained or fast fluidised bed -- The F23C 2206/101 subclass covers combustion systems that employ an entrained or fast fluidised bed, where solid fuel particles are suspended in a rapid stream of upward-flowing air or gas to promote efficient burning. It pertains to designs that achieve high heat transfer and fuel burnout by keeping the bed particles in constant motion.
[F23C 2206/102] Control of recirculation rate -- The entry relates to devices or methods that regulate the rate at which combustion gases or particles are recirculated in a fluidised-bed combustor, covering control of airflow, mixing or fuel feed to keep the combustion process stable and efficient.
[F23C 2206/103] Cooling recirculating particles -- The F23C 2206/103 subclass covers cooling of particles that are recirculated in a fluidised-bed combustion system, such as methods for lowering the temperature of ash or fuel particles before they re-enter the combustion zone.
[F23C 2700/00] Special arrangements for combustion apparatus using fluent fuel -- F23C 2700/00 covers special arrangements for combustion apparatus that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, including burners, injectors and related control devices. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 2700/02] Combustion apparatus using liquid fuel -- F23C 2700/02 covers combustion devices that utilize liquid fuel, such as burners, boilers and furnaces, where the fuel is introduced and ignited in a controlled manner. It includes special arrangements of the fuel delivery, atomisation and flame stabilization within the apparatus.
[F23C 2700/023] without pre-vaporising means -- F23C 2700/023 covers combustion apparatus that burns fluid or solid fuel suspended in air without using any pre-vaporising equipment. The classification applies to special arrangements where the fuel is introduced directly into the combustion zone, eliminating the need for prior vaporisation. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 2700/026] with pre-vaporising means -- This subgroup covers combustion devices that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air and incorporate a pre-vaporising system to atomise the fuel before ignition. The pre-vaporising means improve mixing and stability of the flame, typically by heating or pressurising the fuel prior to entry into the combustion chamber.
[F23C 2700/04] Combustion apparatus using gaseous fuel -- F23C 2700/04 covers combustion devices specially configured to burn gaseous fuels, such as burners, furnaces or boilers that mix fuel gas with air for efficient ignition and flame control. The classification includes any arrangement that directly supplies, mixes or regulates a gas-phase fuel within the combustion chamber.
[F23C 2700/043] for surface combustion -- F23C 2700/043 classifies surface-combustion devices where a fluid or finely divided solid fuel is burned over a solid surface, such as burners, ovens or furnaces that spread the flame to heat the surface itself. It includes special arrangements that direct the fuel-air mixture onto the surface to achieve efficient heat transfer, and falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid or suspended solid fuels.
[F23C 2700/046] generating heat by heating radiant bodies -- This subclass covers systems that produce heat by heating a radiant body, which then emits thermal energy to a surrounding space or process. It includes arrangements where the radiant element is heated as part of combustion apparatus using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, and addresses special configurations for such radiant-body heating.
[F23C 2700/06] Combustion apparatus using pulverized fuel -- This CPC entry covers devices that burn pulverized fuel, such as coal or biomass, in a combustion chamber where the fuel is introduced as a fine solid into a stream of air. It includes designs for burners, furnaces, and boilers that handle fluidized or spray-injected particulate fuel. The code is used for patents relating to such apparatus.
[F23C 2700/063] Arrangements for igniting, flame-guiding, air supply in -- This subclass covers specific configurations in combustion devices that involve the ignition system, mechanisms that direct the flame, and methods of supplying air to support burning of liquid or solid fuel particles suspended in air. It includes designs such as pilot igniters, flame holders, swirlers and air-mixing ducts that improve flame stability and combustion efficiency.
[F23C 2700/066] Other special arrangements -- F23C 2700/066 covers miscellaneous special arrangements of combustion devices that utilize fluid or atomized solid fuels suspended in air, where the specific configuration does not fall under any more detailed sub-group. It includes unique burner designs, fuel-air mixing techniques, or auxiliary features that improve performance but are not otherwise classified.
[F23C 2900/00] Special features of, or arrangements for combustion apparatus using fluid fuels or solid fuels suspended in air -- F23C 2900/00 covers special design features and configurations of combustion devices that burn fluid fuels or solid fuels introduced as a suspension in air, such as atomizers, burners, and related components. It includes arrangements that affect fuel-air mixing, ignition, flame stabilization and heat transfer within these apparatuses. This subgroup falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 2900/01001] Co-combustion of biomass with coal -- F23C 2900/01001 covers technologies that combine biomass and coal in a single combustion process, where the solid fuels are introduced into an air stream for burning. It pertains to methods or apparatus designed for co-combustion of these fuels in devices such as furnaces, boilers or industrial burners.
[F23C 2900/03001] Miniaturized combustion devices using fluid fuels -- This subclass covers small-scale combustion units that burn fluid fuels suspended in air, typically for portable or low-power applications; it includes designs that minimize size while maintaining efficient fuel-air mixing and flame stability.
[F23C 2900/03002] Combustion apparatus adapted for incorporating a fuel reforming device -- The CPC subclass F23C 2900/03002 covers combustion apparatus that incorporates a fuel reforming device, such as a reformer integrated into burners or boilers to convert fuel before combustion. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, and addresses special features or arrangements for such systems.
[F23C 2900/03003] Annular combustion chambers -- F23C 2900/03003 covers annular combustion chambers, a design where the combustion space forms a ring surrounding a central axis and is used for burning fluid fuels or solid fuels suspended in air. These chambers promote uniform heat distribution and efficient fuel-air mixing.
[F23C 2900/03004] Tubular combustion chambers with swirling fuel/air flow -- The CPC code F23C 2900/03004 relates to tubular combustion chambers that employ a swirling flow of fuel and air, improving mixing and flame stability. It applies to designs where fluid or solid fuels are suspended in air within a tube-shaped chamber and includes specific arrangements that generate the swirl.
[F23C 2900/03005] Burners with an internal combustion chamber, e.g. for obtaining an increased heat release, a high speed jet flame or being used for starting the combustion -- F23C 2900/03005 covers burners that incorporate an internal combustion chamber to boost heat release, generate a high-speed jet flame, or serve as an ignition source for the main combustion process. The design is applied to apparatus where fluid or solid fuels are suspended in air, enhancing efficiency or enabling rapid flame initiation.
[F23C 2900/03006] Reverse flow combustion chambers -- F23C 2900/03006 classifies reverse-flow combustion chambers, a type of combustion apparatus that directs the flow of fuel-air mixture opposite to its initial direction to enhance mixing, flame stability and heat-transfer efficiency. The entry falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, and covers special features or arrangements of such reverse-flow designs.
[F23C 2900/03007] Sealed combustion chambers with balanced flue -- The entry covers sealed combustion chambers that incorporate a balanced flue to distribute exhaust gases evenly, improving stability and efficiency in devices where fluid or solid fuels are suspended in air. It addresses design features that maintain chamber pressure while ensuring uniform flow through the flue, suitable for various combustion apparatuses.
[F23C 2900/03008] Spherical or bulb-shaped combustion chambers -- The code F23C 2900/03008 covers combustion chambers that are spherical or bulb-shaped and are employed in apparatus where fluid fuel or solid fuel is suspended in air. Such chamber shapes promote uniform fuel-air mixing, stable flame propagation and efficient heat transfer in combustion devices.
[F23C 2900/03009] Elongated tube-shaped combustion chambers -- The CPC subclass F23C 2900/03009 covers combustion devices that use fluid or solid fuels suspended in air and feature an elongated tube-shaped combustion chamber. It applies to designs where the chamber's lengthwise geometry is a key element of the combustion process, typically in burners, furnaces or engines.
[F23C 2900/05081] Disposition of burners relative to each other creating specific heat patterns -- This subclass covers configurations where multiple burners are arranged relative to one another to generate defined heat patterns, such as zonal or directional heating, in combustion devices that use fluid or solid fuels suspended in air. It includes designs that control burner spacing, orientation and sequencing to achieve targeted temperature distributions.
[F23C 2900/05082] Disposition of radial jet burners in relation to an impingement surface, e.g. a heat transfer surface, to obtain flame re-attachment combustion -- This CPC subclass covers the arrangement of radial jet burners relative to an impingement surface such as a heat-transfer plate, focusing on positioning that promotes flame re-attachment during combustion of fluid or solid fuels suspended in air.
[F23C 2900/06041] Staged supply of oxidant -- This subclass addresses combustion devices that introduce the oxidant in multiple stages to control flame development and improve efficiency, especially for burners using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air. It covers arrangements for staged oxidant supply that optimize mixing, reduce emissions, and facilitate stable combustion.
[F23C 2900/06042] Annular arrangement of burners in a furnace, e.g. in a gas turbine, operated in alternate lean-rich mode -- The code refers to a combustion system in which burners are placed in an annular configuration around a furnace, such as in a gas turbine, and are operated alternately in lean and rich modes to control the combustion process.
[F23C 2900/06043] Burner staging, i.e. radially stratified flame core burners -- This subclass relates to burner staging methods that create a radially stratified flame core in combustion devices using fluid or solid fuels suspended in air, allowing control of temperature and emissions through layered flame zones.
[F23C 2900/07001] Air swirling vanes incorporating fuel injectors -- This subclass defines combustion apparatus in which air swirling vanes are integrated with fuel injectors to promote thorough mixing of fluid or solid fuel particles suspended in air, improving flame stability and efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using fuels suspended in air.
[F23C 2900/07002] Premix burners with air inlet slots obtained between offset curved wall surfaces, e.g. double cone burners -- This subclass covers premixed burners that draw air through slots formed between offset curved wall surfaces such as double-cone walls, allowing the fuel-air mixture to be introduced and ignited within the combustion zone. The arrangement enhances mixing, flame stability and control of the combustion process.
[F23C 2900/07021] Details of lances -- F23C 2900/07021 relates to the design and arrangement of lances in combustion equipment that burns fluid or solid fuels suspended in air, focusing on how the lance delivers fuel and shapes the flame. It includes specifications such as lance geometry, material selection, and injection mechanisms intended to optimise combustion performance.
[F23C 2900/07022] Delaying secondary air introduction into the flame by using a shield or gas curtain -- In mechanical engineering this subclass covers combustion apparatus where secondary air is delayed by employing a shield or a gas curtain around the flame, regulating airflow to improve combustion stability or emissions when using fluid or solid fuels suspended in air.
[F23C 2900/09001] Cooling flue gas before returning them to flame or combustion chamber -- The code F23C 2900/09001 designates features that cool flue gases before they are recirculated to the flame or combustion chamber, typically using heat-exchanger or cooling-circuit arrangements. This recirculation of cooled gases enhances combustion efficiency and temperature control in burners that employ fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 2900/09002] Specific devices inducing or forcing flue gas recirculation -- The entry classifies devices that intentionally induce or force the recirculation of flue gases within combustion systems that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air, such as ducts, fans, or venturi-type arrangements that redirect exhaust gases back into the combustion zone to improve mixing, temperature control, or emissions reduction.
[F23C 2900/10001] Use of special materials for the fluidized bed -- This CPC subclass (F23C 2900/10001) covers the use of particular materials-such as refractory linings, catalysts, heat-resistant alloys or specialised inserts-in fluidised-bed combustion devices that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air. It addresses material choices aimed at enhancing efficiency, durability, temperature control or emission reduction.
[F23C 2900/10002] Treatment devices for the fluidizing gas, e.g. cooling, filtering -- F23C 2900/10002 covers components that condition the gas used to fluidize solid or liquid fuel in combustion devices, such as coolers, filters or scrubbers that remove heat, particulates or contaminants before the gas enters the combustion zone.
[F23C 2900/10003] Fluidized beds with expanding freeboard, i.e. cross-section increasing upwardly -- This subclass covers combustion apparatus where the fluidized-bed freeboard expands upward, giving the chamber a larger cross-section at the top to improve gas flow and particle disengagement. It applies to mechanical-engineering devices that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air within a fluidized-bed reactor.
[F23C 2900/10004] Adding inert bed material to maintain proper fluidized bed inventory -- The code F23C 2900/10004 designates methods for adding inert material into a fluidized-bed combustion system to keep the solid inventory at the desired level. By introducing non-combustible particles, the bed remains properly fluidized, supporting stable combustion of fluid or suspended solid fuels.
[F23C 2900/10005] Arrangement comprising two or more beds in separate enclosures -- This subclass relates to combustion devices that use two or more distinct reaction zones, each housed in its own enclosure, to burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air. The arrangement enables staged-combustion control, improving temperature management and reducing emissions.
[F23C 2900/10006] Pressurized fluidized bed combustors -- Pressurized fluidized-bed combustors are devices that burn liquid or solid fuels suspended in a high-pressure gas stream, creating a fluidized mixture of fuel particles for efficient, uniform combustion and heat recovery. The pressure improves reaction rates, allows compact designs, and eases pollutant control.
[F23C 2900/10007] Spouted fluidized bed combustors -- Spouted fluidized bed combustors are combustion devices that suspend solid or fluid fuel particles in an upward air stream, creating a spouted, fluidized bed that enhances mixing and heat transfer. The design uses a high-velocity air jet to lift and circulate the fuel, allowing efficient combustion of fuels suspended in air while reducing emissions.
[F23C 2900/10008] Special arrangements of return flow seal valve in fluidized bed combustors -- This subgroup covers inventions that provide a special arrangement of a return-flow seal valve in fluidized-bed combustors, aimed at controlling gas recirculation and preventing leakage during combustion of fluid or solid fuels suspended in air. It falls under methods or apparatus for combustion using fuel suspended in air within the broader mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F23C 2900/13001] Details of catalytic combustors -- F23C 2900/13001 covers the specific design and functional details of catalytic combustors used in devices that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air. It includes arrangements of catalyst bodies, inlet and outlet configurations, temperature control features, and methods for integrating catalysts into combustion chambers to enhance efficiency and emissions control.
[F23C 2900/13002] Catalytic combustion followed by a homogeneous combustion phase or stabilizing a homogeneous combustion phase -- F23C 2900/13002 covers combustion designs that first use a catalyst to ignite a fuel-air mixture and then allow the flame to continue in a homogeneous combustion stage, or that incorporate features to stabilize that homogeneous phase. It applies to apparatus and methods for burning fluid or solid fuels suspended in air.
[F23C 2900/99001] Cold flame combustion or flameless oxidation processes -- The subclass F23C 2900/99001 covers combustion techniques that generate a cold flame or flameless oxidation, where the fuel-air mixture burns at temperatures below the visible flame zone. These processes are used to achieve low-temperature combustion, reduce emissions and enable controlled heating of materials.
[F23C 2900/99003] Combustion techniques using laser or light beams as ignition, stabilization or combustion enhancing means -- The code designates methods and devices that employ laser or other light beams to initiate, sustain or improve combustion of fluid or solid fuels dispersed in air, covering ignition, flame-stabilisation and combustion-enhancement techniques using directed radiation.
[F23C 2900/99004] Combustion process using petroleum coke or any other fuel with a very low content in volatile matters -- This CPC subclass (F23C 2900/99004) covers combustion processes in which petroleum coke or other fuels containing very little volatile material are burned, typically by suspending the solid fuel in air within a combustion apparatus. It addresses the special features and arrangements required for efficient burning of such low-volatile fuels.
[F23C 2900/99005] Combustion techniques using plasma gas -- The subclass F23C 2900/99005 refers to combustion methods that use a plasma gas to ignite or sustain the burning of liquid or solid fuels suspended in air, covering designs such as plasma torches, arcs or ionised-gas streams employed in burners, engines or reactors as heat sources or catalytic enhancers.
[F23C 2900/99006] Arrangements for starting combustion -- The class F23C 2900/99006 covers arrangements for initiating combustion in devices that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, such as ignition devices, pilot flames, or other startup systems that promote flame establishment.
[F23C 2900/99008] Unmixed combustion, i.e. without direct mixing of oxygen gas and fuel, but using the oxygen from a metal oxide, e.g. FeO -- F23C 2900/99008 covers unmixed combustion processes where the fuel is not directly mixed with oxygen gas but obtains oxygen from a metal oxide such as iron oxide. The method applies to fluid or solid fuels suspended in air and is classified under combustion apparatus using suspended fuels.
[F23C 2900/99009] Combustion process using vegetable derived fuels, e.g. from rapes -- F23C 2900/99009 is a CPC subclass within mechanical engineering for combustion apparatus that uses fluid or solid fuels suspended in air, specifically covering processes that burn vegetable-derived fuels such as rapeseed oil. It classifies inventions where renewable bio-fuels are atomised or dispersed in the combustion chamber, providing a distinct category for bio-fuel combustion technologies.
[F23C 2900/9901] Combustion process using hydrogen, hydrogen peroxide water or brown gas as fuel
[F23C 2900/99011] Combustion process using synthetic gas as a fuel, i.e. a mixture of CO and H -- The subclass defines combustion processes that employ synthetic gas-a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen-as the fuel, applied in devices where fluid or solid fuels are suspended in air for burning. It is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid or solid fuels suspended in air.
[F23C 3/00] Combustion apparatus characterised by the shape of the combustion chamber -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion apparatus wherein the shape or the location of the combustion chamber is of particular relevance, e.g. radiant tubes, cyclonic combustors and submerged combustors  Illustrative example: EP2299175, US547981, US2900930, WO2010/090453
[F23C 3/002] the chamber having an elongated tubular form, e.g. for a radiant tube -- F23C 3/002 covers combustion devices whose chamber is formed as an elongated tube, such as a radiant tube, to provide a long, narrow path for the flame and heat transfer. The tubular shape improves radiant heating efficiency and allows compact arrangement of the combustion system. This subclass falls under methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 3/004] the chamber being arranged for submerged combustion  F23C3/002  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion apparatus wherein the chamber having an elongated tubular form, e.g. for a radiant tube -> F23C3/002
[F23C 3/006] the chamber being arranged for cyclonic combustion  for waste  F23G5/32
[F23C 3/008] for pulverulent fuel -- F23C 3/008 covers combustion chambers specially shaped for burning pulverulent (powdered) fuels, detailing how chamber geometry aids mixing, flame stabilization, and efficient fuel use. The subclass includes designs where the chamber form is adapted to handle solid particles suspended in air.
[F23C 5/00] Disposition of burners with respect to the combustion chamber or to one another -- This subclass covers the spatial arrangement of burners within a combustion chamber or the relative positioning of multiple burners to each other. It includes designs that define burner placement to optimize flame shape, heat distribution and efficiency, and the geometric relationship between burners and chamber walls.
[F23C 5/02] Structural details of mounting -- This subclass addresses the structural details of mounting burners in combustion apparatus that use fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, covering how burners are positioned or attached relative to the combustion chamber or to each other, including brackets, supports and other mounting arrangements.
[F23C 5/06] Provision for adjustment of burner position during operation -- This subclass covers features that allow the position of a burner to be altered while the device is running, enabling fine-tuning of flame shape, heat distribution, or fuel-air mixing. Such adjustments may be made manually or automatically relative to the combustion chamber or neighboring burners.
[F23C 5/08] Disposition of burners -- This subclass covers the spatial arrangement and positioning of individual burners within a combustion chamber or relative to each other, including patterns, spacing, orientation, and mounting configurations that affect flame propagation and heat distribution. It is used to classify inventions concerning burner layout for efficient combustion of fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 5/10] to obtain a flame ring -- F23C 5/10 covers arrangements of burners within a combustion chamber or relative to each other that are designed to produce a circular flame, known as a flame ring. This sub-class is part of the mechanical-engineering group for combustion apparatus using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 5/12] for pulverulent fuel -- This CPC subclass concerns burners that use pulverulent (powdered) fuel and defines how such burners are positioned with respect to the combustion chamber or to each other, covering arrangements for solid-fuel combustion in air.
[F23C 5/14] to obtain a single flame of concentrated or substantially planar form, e.g. pencil or sheet flame -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Disposition of burners to obtain rotating flames, i.e. flames moving helically or spirally -> F23C5/32
[F23C 5/24] to obtain a loop flame -- The F23C 5/24 entry covers configurations where burners are positioned so that the flame circulates in a loop, either by their placement relative to the combustion chamber or to other burners. This arrangement creates a recirculating, or "loop," flame that can improve mixing and stability in combustion processes.
[F23C 5/28] to obtain flames in opposing directions, e.g. impacting flames -- F23C 5/28 covers burner arrangements that generate flames in opposite directions, such as configurations that cause flames to impact each other, to control or improve combustion within a chamber.
[F23C 5/32] to obtain rotating flames, i.e. flames moving helically or spirally -- F23C 5/32 covers arrangements of burners or combustion chambers designed to generate rotating flames that move helically or spirally. The subclass includes configurations that create a swirl in the flame by specific positioning or geometry of the burners.
[F23C 6/00] Combustion apparatus characterised by the combination of two or more combustion chambers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Consuming smoke or fumes in separate combustion apparatus -> F23G7/06
[F23C 6/02] in parallel arrangement -- A combustion apparatus in which two or more combustion chambers are placed side by side and operate simultaneously, each receiving fuel and air in a parallel configuration. The parallel arrangement may share common inlet or exhaust ducts or have separate ones, and is intended to increase capacity, improve heat distribution, or enhance overall efficiency.
