[F23C 6/04] in series connection -- F23C 6/04 designates combustion devices that employ two or more combustion chambers linked in series, so the output of one chamber serves as the inlet for the next. This arrangement is used to improve fuel utilization, temperature control and emissions in systems that burn fluid or solid fuels suspended in air.
[F23C 6/042] with fuel supply in stages  for staged combustion  F23C6/047 -- A combustion device that incorporates two or more chambers and delivers its fuel in separate stages to achieve staged combustion, controlling the ignition and burn sequence for improved efficiency or emission reduction.
[F23C 6/045] with staged combustion in a single enclosure -- This subgroup covers combustion devices that employ staged combustion within one enclosure, using two or more chambers arranged so that fuel or air is introduced sequentially to control temperature and emissions. The design enables efficient burning of fluid or solid fuels suspended in air while confining the entire staged process to a single housing.
[F23C 6/047] with fuel supply in stages -- F23C 6/047 classifies combustion devices that introduce their fuel in multiple stages, allowing staged injection or delivery of fluid or solid fuel into the combustion chamber to improve control, efficiency or emissions.
[F23C 7/00] Combustion apparatus characterised by arrangements for air supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aspects of the air supply related to the introduction of the combustion air into the combustion chamber in proximity of the burner, e.g. passages, swirling vanes, flow controlling means, limited to apparatus of methods for fluid fuels only.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Inlets for fluidisation air -> F23C10/20 - Baffles or shields with air supply passages -> F23M9/04
[F23C 7/002] the air being submitted to a rotary or spinning motion  cyclonic combustion chamber  F23C3/006 -- F23C 7/002 covers combustion apparatus in which the air supplied to the burner is set into a rotary or spinning motion, typically using a cyclonic chamber to create a vortex. This arrangement enhances mixing of air with fluid or solid fuel particles and promotes more uniform, efficient combustion. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and methods or apparatus for combustion using fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 7/004] using vanes -- F23C 7/004 relates to combustion devices that employ vanes to guide, swirl or mix the incoming air, thereby enhancing the combustion of fluid fuel or solid-fuel particles suspended in air. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → methods or apparatus for combustion using fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air, specifically covering arrangements for air supply that use vane structures.
[F23C 7/006] adjustable -- F23C 7/006 covers combustion devices that incorporate adjustable air-supply arrangements, such as ducts, louvers or valves that can be varied to control the amount and direction of airflow to the fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 7/008] Flow control devices  F23C7/006  takes precedence -- F23C 7/008 covers flow-control devices used in combustion apparatus that burn fluid or solid fuel suspended in air, specifically components that regulate the air supply to the combustion zone. It includes valves, nozzles, regulators and similar elements designed to adjust air flow for optimal combustion.
[F23C 7/02] Disposition of air supply not passing through burner -- F23C 7/02 classifies combustion devices in which the air required for burning is delivered to the combustion zone without being routed through the burner itself, such as systems that supply air via external ducts or separate inlets. This arrangement is used for fluid-fuel or solid-fuel suspensions in air, falling under mechanical engineering combustion apparatus.
[F23C 7/04] to obtain maximum heat transfer to wall of combustion chamber -- F23C 7/04 covers combustion devices that arrange the air supply so the flame's heat is transferred as efficiently as possible to the chamber wall, often using ducts, swirler vanes or preheated air streams to promote uniform wall heating and improve thermal efficiency.
[F23C 7/06] for heating the incoming air -- This subgroup covers combustion devices in which the air supplied to the burner is preheated, typically by passing it through heat exchangers, recirculated flue gases, or other waste-heat recovery elements, to raise flame temperature, improve fuel efficiency and control emissions.
[F23C 7/08] indirectly by a secondary fluid other than the combustion products -- F23C 7/08 covers combustion devices in which the air needed for burning is delivered indirectly through a secondary fluid that does not become part of the combustion products, such as water, steam, or another inert medium. This arrangement separates the air supply from the flame zone to improve mixing, temperature control, or emission reduction.
[F23C 9/00] Combustion apparatus characterised by arrangements for returning combustion products or flue gases to the combustion chamber -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fluidised beds with means specially adapted for achieving or promoting a circulating movement of particles within the bed or for a recirculation of particles entrained from the bed -> F23C10/02 - Combined with devices for partial reintroduction of material into the bed, e.g. after separation of agglomerated parts -> F23C10/26 - Fluidised bed incinerators with means specially adapted for achieving or promoting a circulating movement of particles within the bed or for a recirculation of particles entrained from the bed -> F23G5/30
[F23C 9/003] for pulverulent fuel  for fluidized bed  F23C10/02
[F23C 9/006] the recirculation taking place in the combustion chamber -- F23C 9/006 refers to combustion devices that deliberately recirculate part of the exhaust gases or combustion products back into the combustion chamber. The recirculation can improve fuel efficiency, reduce emissions, or stabilize flame conditions in burners that use fluid or solid fuels suspended in air. This subclass falls under methods and apparatus for combustion using such fuels.
[F23C 9/06] for completing combustion -- F23C 9/06 pertains to combustion devices that recycle flue gases or combustion products back into the combustion chamber to achieve more complete combustion of fluid or solid fuel suspended in air. The arrangement enhances fuel efficiency and reduces emissions by promoting thorough oxidation of the fuel.
[F23C 9/08] for reducing temperature in combustion chamber, e.g. for protecting walls of combustion chamber -- The F23C 9/08 subclass covers combustion apparatuses that employ devices for returning exhaust gases or flue gases to the combustion chamber in order to lower the chamber temperature, typically to protect the chamber walls or other components from overheating. This includes methods and structures that redirect hot gases back into the fire zone as part of temperature-control strategies.
[F23C 99/00] Subject-matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F23C 99/00 is a residual group within the subclass for combustion apparatus that use fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air. It covers any device, method or component related to such combustion that is not specifically defined in the other groups of the subclass. The entry serves as a catch-all category for unanticipated or miscellaneous inventions in this area.
[F23C 99/001] Applying electric means or magnetism to combustion  for combustion engines  F02B51/04 ,  F02M27/04 -- The subclass F23C 99/001 concerns techniques that employ electrical or magnetic energy to affect combustion in engines, such as electrically assisted ignition, flame-holding, or magnetic manipulation of the fuel-air mixture, where the effect is not covered by any other more specific group in the combustion-using-fluid-or-solid-fuel-suspended-in-air hierarchy.
[F23C 99/003] Combustion process using sound or vibrations  for combustion engines  F02B51/06 ,  F02M27/08 ; liquid fuel burners using ultrasonic means for spraying the fuel  F23D11/34 -- The CPC subclass F23C 99/003 covers combustion processes that employ acoustic or vibrational energy, such as using sound waves to ignite or improve combustion in engines and burners. It includes methods like ultrasonic fuel atomisation for liquid fuels and the use of vibration to enhance fuel-air mixing. This subclass is placed under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and combustion using fluid or solid fuel suspended in air.
[F23C 99/005] Suspension-type burning, i.e. fuel particles carried along with a gas flow while burning  fluidized-bed combustion apparatus  F23C10/00 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fluidised bed apparatus -> F23C10/00
[F23C 99/006] Flameless combustion stabilised within a bed of porous heat-resistant material  F23C13/00  takes precedence; gas burners with radiant combustion on a porous surface  F23D14/16 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus in which combustion takes place in the presence of catalytic material -> F23C13/00 - Radiant gas burners with combustion on or in a porous body -> F23D14/16 - Radiant gas burner with a catalyst flameless combustion -> F23D14/18
[F23C 99/008] Combustion methods wherein flame cooling techniques other than fuel or air staging or fume recirculation are used -- This subclass covers combustion processes in which the flame is cooled by means other than fuel or air staging or by recirculating exhaust gases, such as external cooling sprays, heat-absorbing surfaces, or inert gas dilution, applied to fluid-fuel or solid-fuel burners.
[F23D] BURNERS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Burners, i.e. devices by which fluid fuel, or solid fuel suspended in air, is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame, for example, burners in which:  **Glossary:** - air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame. A burner includes means for feeding air that are arranged in immediate connection with a fuel feeding conduit, for example, concentric with it. - combustion: a heat-producing sequence of chemical reactions between a burnable substance and molecular oxygen, e.g. in air, in most cases generating light in the form of flames or a glow - combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - combustion space: the zone downstream of the fuel (and, where applicable, air) outlet where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - fuel: any combustible material that can be burned, regardless of whether the main purpose of burning it is for releasing energy therefrom or for disposing of it or rendering it less harmful - pilot flame: a small flame that is lit or kept alight in order to provide ignition to a more powerful burner - primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - retention flame: a small flame that is kept alight in order to maintain the uninterrupted operation of a more powerful burner - secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The expression "secondary air" covers "tertiary air", etc. - torch: a burner fired with fuel gas and oxygen and specially adapted to apply heat to a workpiece, for example, for use in welding, cutting or brazing
[F23D 1/00] Burners for combustion of pulverulent fuel -- Burners for combustion of pulverulent fuel are devices that atomize or disperse finely ground solid fuels, mixing them with air to achieve efficient, rapid ignition and flame stability. They are used in industrial heating, power generation and material processing where powdered coal, coke or biomass serves as the primary energy source.
[F23D 1/005] burning a mixture of pulverulent fuel delivered as a slurry, i.e. comprising a carrying liquid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Burners specially adapted for combusting a fine mixture of solid fuel paricles and a liquid, e.g. a slurry of pulverized coal in water.
[F23D 1/02] Vortex burners, e.g. for cyclone-type combustion apparatus -- F23D 1/02 covers vortex burners employed in cyclone-type combustion apparatus, where a swirling gas flow creates a vortex that mixes and ignites pulverised fuel. The design enhances flame stability, improves combustion efficiency and reduces emissions, and is used in industrial furnaces, boilers and similar burners that rely on rotational flow for fuel combustion.
[F23D 1/04] Burners producing cylindrical flames without centrifugal action -- Burners that create a steady cylindrical flame by directing the fuel-air mixture without inducing any rotation or centrifugal forces, resulting in a straight-column fire pattern suitable for uniform heating or process applications.
[F23D 1/06] Burners producing sheet flames -- F23D 1/06 pertains to burners that create sheet-like flames for burning pulverulent fuels, providing a broad, uniform flame front commonly used in industrial heating and material processing applications.
[F23D 11/00] Burners using a direct spraying action of liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion space -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of the subject matter classified in this place:  1. Liquid fuel is sprayed into the combustion space by fluid pressure  2. Liquid fuel is entrained into the combustion space by a stream of gaseous medium, for example, air or steam  3. Liquid fuel is vaporised within a conduit and released in gaseous form into the combustion space
[F23D 11/001] Spraying nozzle combined with forced draft fan in one unit -- F23D 11/001 covers burners that integrate a spraying nozzle with a forced-draft fan in a single unit, allowing direct injection of liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion space for enhanced mixing and flame stability. It lies within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of combustion apparatus and burners.
[F23D 11/002] Spraying nozzle arranged within furnace openings -- The classification refers to burners that introduce liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion zone by means of a spray action, and it specifically covers the arrangement of a spraying nozzle positioned within furnace openings to deliver fuel and stabilize the flame.
[F23D 11/004] for producing radiant heat -- F23D 11/004 covers burners that generate radiant heat by directly spraying liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion space. The atomisation promotes efficient combustion and a strong radiant output for heating applications.
[F23D 11/005] with combinations of different spraying or vaporising means -- F23D 11/005 covers burners that inject liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion zone and incorporate multiple spraying or vaporising techniques in combination, such as mixing atomising nozzles with steam or air-assisted sprays to improve fuel distribution and flame stability. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and burners that use a direct spraying action.
[F23D 11/007] combination of means covered by sub-groups  F23D11/10  and  F23D11/24 -- F23D 11/007 concerns burners that use a direct spray of liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion space and combine the features defined in sub-group 10 with those of sub-group 24.
[F23D 11/008] combination of means covered by sub-groups  F23D5/00  and  F23D11/00 -- F23D 11/008 covers burners that combine the direct spraying of liquid droplets or vaporised fuel into the combustion space with additional features defined in sub-group F23D5/00, such as auxiliary air supply or flame-stabilising means. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners that employ a direct spray injection action.
[F23D 11/02] the combustion space being a chamber substantially at atmospheric pressure -- The code F23D 11/02 covers burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion zone, where the combustion space is a chamber operating at substantially atmospheric pressure. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the burner sub-category.
[F23D 11/04] the spraying action being obtained by centrifugal action -- F23D 11/04 covers burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion zone by means of a centrifugal spraying process. The spraying is generated by rotating the liquid source so that centrifugal force ejects the droplets, providing direct atomisation for combustion.
[F23D 11/06] using a horizontal shaft -- F23D 11/06 covers burners that inject liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion zone by means of a horizontal shaft, typically driving a spray nozzle or atomiser to create a direct-spray flame. The classification applies to mechanical arrangements where the shaft orientation is essentially horizontal, enabling controlled fuel delivery and mixing within the burner.
[F23D 11/08] using a vertical shaft -- The subclass F23D 11/08 defines burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion space by means of a direct spray using a vertical shaft. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under combustion apparatus and burners.
[F23D 11/10] the spraying being induced by a gaseous medium, e.g. water vapour -- This CPC subclass F23D 11/10 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burners, and it covers burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion space by a direct spraying action, where the spray is generated by a gaseous medium such as water vapour.
[F23D 11/101] medium and fuel meeting before the burner outlet -- F23D 11/101 relates to burners where the combustion medium and fuel are combined before leaving the burner, usually by directly spraying liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion space. This pre-mixing arrangement promotes more uniform combustion, enhancing stability, efficiency and emissions control.
[F23D 11/102] in an internal mixing chamber -- This subclass covers burners that introduce liquid fuel as a spray of droplets or vapor directly into the combustion space within an internal mixing chamber, allowing the fuel to mix with air before ignition. It is part of the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners, and addresses designs that rely on direct liquid injection for efficient combustion.
[F23D 11/103] with means creating a swirl inside the mixing chamber -- F23D 11/103 covers burners that introduce liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion zone and employ a device that generates a swirling motion in the mixing chamber to improve fuel-air mixing. The swirl enhances combustion efficiency and stability for spray-type burners.
[F23D 11/104] intersecting at a sharp angle, e.g. Y-jet atomiser -- F23D 11/104 covers burners that introduce liquid fuel or vapor by directly spraying droplets into the combustion space using intersecting jets that meet at a sharp angle, exemplified by Y-jet atomisers. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of combustion apparatus and burners, specifying this particular atomising technique.
[F23D 11/105] at least one of the fluids being submitted to a swirling motion -- F23D 11/105 concerns burners that introduce liquid or vaporised fuels into the combustion chamber while imparting a swirling motion to at least one of the fluids, improving mixing and flame stability. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners using direct spraying of liquids.
[F23D 11/106] medium and fuel meeting at the burner outlet -- The classification F23D 11/106 concerns burners in which the fuel and the combustion medium are combined at the burner outlet, usually by directly spraying liquid droplets or vaporised fuel into the combustion space.
[F23D 11/107] at least one of both being subjected to a swirling motion -- F23D 11/107 refers to burners that introduce liquid droplets or vapour directly into the combustion chamber and impose a swirling motion on the spray, the flame, or both. The swirl enhances mixing of fuel and air, improving combustion efficiency and stability.
[F23D 11/108] medium and fuel intersecting downstream of the burner outlet -- F23D 11/108 covers burners in which the liquid fuel or its vapor is sprayed, and the mixing of the fuel with the combustion medium takes place after the burner outlet, so that the medium and fuel intersect downstream of the discharge point. This downstream mixing approach is used to improve flame stability, control emissions, or accommodate variable fuel properties.
[F23D 11/12] characterised by the shape or arrangement of the outlets from the nozzle -- F23D 11/12 covers burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid into the combustion chamber by direct spraying and are defined by the particular shape or spatial arrangement of the nozzle outlets, rather than by the spraying mechanism itself.
[F23D 11/14] with a single outlet, e.g. slit
[F23D 11/16] in which an emulsion of water and fuel is sprayed -- This subclass covers burners in which a mixture of water and fuel is atomised and injected directly into the combustion chamber. The emulsion is sprayed as liquid droplets or vapor, providing combined combustion and cooling effects. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners (F23D 11/16).
[F23D 11/18] the gaseous medium being water vapour generated at the nozzle -- F23D 11/18 covers burners that deliver a gaseous medium consisting of water vapour produced at the nozzle, where the vapour is generated by atomising or vaporising a liquid directly as it exits the nozzle into the combustion chamber. This sub-class deals with direct-spray combustion devices that use water-based media to enhance flame stability or reduce emissions.
[F23D 11/20] the water vapour being superheated -- This subclass covers burners that inject liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion space and then superheat the resulting water vapour above its saturation temperature. It is classified under CPC code F23D 11/20 within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion apparatus and burners.
[F23D 11/22] the gaseous medium being vaporised fuel, e.g. for a soldering lamp -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spraying of liquid fuel droplets being induced by vaporised fuel.
[F23D 11/24] by pressurisation of the fuel before a nozzle through which it is sprayed by a substantial pressure reduction into a space -- The subclass F23D 11/24 refers to burners that first pressurise the fuel and then discharge it through a nozzle where a large pressure reduction creates a spray of liquid droplets or vapor into the combustion chamber. This direct-spraying action is used to form a fine fuel cloud for efficient combustion.
[F23D 11/26] with provision for varying the rate at which the fuel is sprayed -- F23D 11/26 covers burners that introduce liquid fuel or vapor as a fine spray directly into the combustion chamber and include a feature allowing the spray rate to be adjusted during operation, enabling control of flame characteristics and efficiency.
[F23D 11/28] with flow-back of fuel at the burner, e.g. using by-pass -- This subclass covers burners that include a provision for fuel flow-back to the burner, such as a bypass arrangement, which helps recirculate unburned liquid droplets or vapor for improved combustion efficiency and flame stability. It belongs to the group of direct-spray liquid-fuel burners.
[F23D 11/30] with return feed of uncombusted sprayed fuel to reservoir -- This subclass covers burners that inject liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion space and include a mechanism that returns any unburned sprayed fuel back to a reservoir for reuse.
[F23D 11/32] by electrostatic means -- A subclass of burners that introduce liquid droplets or vapour directly into the combustion space and employ electrostatic charging to control and atomise the spray, falling under combustion apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F23D 11/34] by ultrasonic means -- The subclass F23D 11/34 covers burners that introduce liquid fuel or vaporised liquid into the combustion space by means of an ultrasonic spray. Ultrasonic transducers generate high-frequency vibrations that break the liquid into fine droplets, creating a direct spray for more efficient mixing and combustion. This method is used in applications where precise fuel atomisation is required.
[F23D 11/345] with vibrating atomiser surfaces -- This subclass covers burners that employ a vibrating atomiser surface to spray liquid or vaporised fuel directly into the combustion zone, enhancing atomisation and mixing. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners.
[F23D 11/36] Details
[F23D 11/38] Nozzles -- F23D 11/38 covers nozzles used in burners that inject liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion zone. These devices atomise fuel for better mixing and flame stability and are employed in heating, industrial and power applications.
[F23D 11/383] with swirl means -- F23D 11/383 covers burners that introduce liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion chamber and incorporate swirl devices to improve fuel-air mixing. The swirl means create a rotating flow that promotes efficient combustion and reduces emissions.
[F23D 11/386] Nozzle cleaning -- F23D 11/386 covers methods and devices for cleaning the spray nozzles of burners that introduce liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion zone. The focus is on removing deposits that can impair spray performance and combustion efficiency, typically by employing cleaning agents, mechanical actions, or reverse-flow sprays. This ensures consistent fuel atomisation and reliable operation of liquid-fuel burners.
[F23D 11/40] Mixing tubes -- F23D 11/40 covers mixing tubes employed in burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion zone by spraying. These tubes blend the fuel with air or oxidiser to create a combustible mixture, enhancing atomisation and flame stability in combustion apparatus.
[F23D 11/402] Mixing chambers downstream of the nozzle -- F23D 11/402 pertains to burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporized liquid by direct spray into the combustion space and include a mixing chamber located downstream of the nozzle to improve fuel-air mixing before ignition.
[F23D 11/404] Flame tubes  not forming part of the burner  F23M9/06
[F23D 11/406] Flame stabilising means, e.g. flame holders -- F23D 11/406 relates to flame-stabilising devices, such as flame holders, that are installed in burners which inject liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion space. These components help maintain a consistent flame, improving combustion stability and efficiency.
[F23D 11/408] Flow influencing devices in the air tube -- F23D 11/408 covers devices that modify the airflow within the air tube of burners that inject liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion zone, such as mixers, swirlers or restrictors that shape the gas-liquid flow for improved mixing and flame stability.
[F23D 11/42] Starting devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liquid fuel burners characterised by the means for starting the combustion, e.g. fixing, locating or assembling the ignition device to the burner  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Igniting -> F23Q
[F23D 11/44] Preheating devices -- The F23D 11/44 classification covers preheating devices employed in burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion zone by a spraying action. These devices raise the temperature of the fuel or surrounding air before ignition to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions, and stabilize flame characteristics. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and burners.
[F23D 11/441] Vaporising devices incorporated with burners -- F23D 11/441 covers burners that incorporate a vaporising device which introduces liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion chamber. The spray action provides fuel atomisation for more efficient combustion and is used in various heating and industrial processes.
[F23D 11/443] heated by the main burner flame -- The code F23D 11/443 refers to burners that heat a spray of liquid droplets or vaporised liquid by the main burner flame within the combustion chamber. It covers combustion apparatus where the primary flame directly provides thermal energy to the sprayed fuel, typical of direct-spray burners used in industrial heating and process applications.
[F23D 11/445] the flame and the vaporiser not coming into direct contact -- F23D 11/445 covers burners in which the liquid or vapor is atomised and introduced into the combustion zone while the vapouriser and the flame are physically separated, preventing direct contact between them. This arrangement allows the spray to be heated or ignited without the vapouriser being exposed to flame.
[F23D 11/446] heated by an auxiliary flame -- F23D 11/446 designates burners that introduce liquid droplets or vapor directly into the combustion zone and are heated by an auxiliary flame, a subclass within mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus. It covers devices where a secondary flame supplies the initial heat to atomize the fuel before main combustion.
[F23D 11/448] heated by electrical means -- This subclass covers burners that introduce liquid droplets or vaporised liquid directly into the combustion space and obtain the necessary heating or ignition from electrical energy rather than a pilot flame, using electric heating elements to start and sustain combustion.
[F23D 11/46] Devices on the vaporiser for controlling the feeding of the fuel -- The code covers devices mounted on a fuel-vaporiser that regulate the amount of fuel introduced into a direct-spray burner, such as metering valves, flow regulators or control chambers, to ensure precise fuel feeding and stable combustion.
[F23D 14/00] Burners for combustion of a gas, e.g. of a gas stored under pressure as a liquid -- F23D 14/00 pertains to burners designed to combust gases, including those supplied from pressurised liquid storage such as propane or butane. The classification covers the structure, operation and control of such burners, addressing fuel delivery, ignition and flame stabilisation mechanisms used in heating, cooking or industrial processes.
[F23D 14/02] Premix gas burners, i.e. in which gaseous fuel is mixed with combustion air upstream of the combustion zone -- Premix gas burners are devices in which the gaseous fuel is combined with combustion air before entering the flame zone, allowing controlled ignition and efficient combustion of gases stored under pressure, typically used in heating, cooking, or industrial processes.
[F23D 14/04] induction type, e.g. Bunsen burner -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This subgroup covers:  Atmospheric burners referring to burners that are purely induction type burners where air is drawn into the burner without any help of blowers or fans for the injection of fuel draws air into the burner. A Bunsen burner is an example of such a burner.  Aerated burners referring to burners where a fan or blower is used, in addition to the induction effect of fuel injection, to supply air to the burner. The fan can be forced draft or induced draft type.
[F23D 14/045] with a plurality of burner bars assembled together, e.g. in a grid-like arrangement -- F23D 14/045 covers burners designed for the combustion of gaseous fuels, especially those stored under pressure as a liquid, where multiple burner bars are combined into a single unit, often in a grid-like configuration, to provide a distributed flame front and improved heat distribution.
[F23D 14/06] with radial outlets at the burner head -- Burners for gas combustion, especially where the gas is stored under pressure as a liquid, featuring a burner head that discharges the flame through radial outlets. The design directs the flame outward from the head for even heat distribution.
[F23D 14/065] with injector axis inclined to the burner head axis -- A subclass of burners for gas combustion-particularly gases stored under pressure as liquids-where the fuel-injector axis is set at an angle to the axis of the burner head, allowing inclined injection into the flame zone.
[F23D 14/08] with axial outlets at the burner head -- The subclass F23D 14/08 covers burners whose combustion gas is discharged through axial outlets located at the burner head, a design used when igniting gases stored under pressure as liquids. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners.
[F23D 14/085] with injector axis inclined to the burner head axis -- This subclass addresses burners in which the fuel injector is mounted at an angle to the burner-head axis, a configuration that improves fuel-air mixing and flame control for gases stored under pressure as liquids. The inclined injector arrangement can enhance flame stability, shape the combustion zone, and allow more efficient ignition in combustion apparatus.
[F23D 14/10] with elongated tubular burner head -- Burners classified under F23D 14/10 are designed for the combustion of a pressurised-liquid gas that is vapourised at the point of use. The entry specifically covers devices that incorporate an elongated tubular burner head to direct and mix the gas-air flame, improving stability and heat distribution in applications such as heating, soldering or industrial processing.
[F23D 14/105] with injector axis parallel to the burner head axis -- F23D 14/105 covers burners that combust pressurised liquid-derived gases where the fuel injector is aligned parallel to the burner head axis. This arrangement provides a straight flow of fuel into the flame zone, typical for domestic and industrial gas heating systems.
[F23D 14/12] Radiant burners -- Radiant burners are combustion devices that burn gaseous fuel, often supplied as a liquid under pressure, and emit heat primarily by radiation rather than convection. They belong to the mechanical-engineering class for combustion apparatus and are used where direct radiant heating is required.
[F23D 14/125] heating a wall surface to incandescence -- F23D 14/125 covers burners designed to heat a wall or surface until it glows, i.e. reaches incandescence, typically by combusting a pressurised liquid-gas fuel. The classification is used for devices where the radiant heat from the glowing surface is the primary output, such as infrared heating panels, radiant furnaces and specialized industrial heating elements.
[F23D 14/126] cooperating with refractory wall surfaces -- This subgroup covers burners whose combustion zone is in direct contact with or closely adjacent to refractory wall surfaces, using the wall to reflect heat, protect the burner, or improve flame stability, typically for gas stored under pressure as a liquid.
[F23D 14/14] using screens or perforated plates -- A sub-group of gas-fuel burners that employ screens or perforated plates to shape the flame, protect the burner tip, filter particulates, and enhance mixing or stability of the fuel-air mixture. These devices are used where a gas stored under pressure as a liquid is combusted, and they fall under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners.
[F23D 14/145] combustion being stabilised at a screen or a perforated plate -- F23D 14/145 refers to burners in which the flame is stabilized by a screen or perforated plate, commonly used for gas-fuel combustion where the gas may be stored under pressure as a liquid. The classification addresses the design features that control flame attachment and spread over the screening element.
[F23D 14/147] with perforated plates as radiation intensifying means -- A subclass of burners that combust gases, especially those supplied from pressurised liquid storage, and employ perforated plates to intensify radiant heat transfer during combustion. It is situated under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → burners in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F23D 14/148] with grids, e.g. strips or rods, as radiation intensifying means -- F23D 14/148 defines burners for gas combustion that employ grids, strips or rods as radiation-intensifying means, improving heat transfer by increasing the emitting surface within the flame. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners, covering devices that use such metallic structures to enhance radiative output.
[F23D 14/149] with wires, threads or gauzes as radiation intensifying means -- This subclass concerns burners that combust gases-often from pressurized liquefied storage-and employ wires, threads or gauze structures to intensify the radiated heat output.
[F23D 14/151] with radiation intensifying means other than screens or perforated plates -- Burners designed for gas combustion, especially where the gas is stored under pressure as a liquid, that employ radiation-intensifying devices other than screens or perforated plates-such as reflectors, lenses or other concentrating elements-to enhance heat output. This subclass groups such radiant-enhancement techniques within the broader burner category.
[F23D 14/16] using permeable blocks -- F23D 14/16 relates to burners that use permeable blocks to combust gas, including gases stored under pressure as liquids. The design channels fuel through a porous block where it mixes with air before ignition, enhancing flame stability and heat distribution.
[F23D 14/18] using catalysis for flameless combustion -- The CPC code F23D 14/18 covers burners for gas combustion that employ a catalytic process to achieve flameless, low-temperature combustion, typically using pressurised liquefied gas as fuel. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners.
[F23D 14/181] with carbon containing radiating surface -- Burners for gas combustion, particularly those using pressurised liquid fuel, that employ a carbon-containing surface to radiate heat. The carbon material serves as an infrared emitter, enhancing thermal output while the gas flame provides primary combustion. This design combines convective and radiative heating in a single burner unit.
[F23D 14/20] Non-premix gas burners, i.e. in which gaseous fuel is mixed with combustion air on arrival at the combustion zone -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Torches, e.g. for brazing or heating -> F23D14/38
[F23D 14/22] with separate air and gas feed ducts, e.g. with ducts running parallel or crossing each other -- The CPC subclass F23D 14/22 pertains to burners used for combusting a gas, such as a liquefied gas stored under pressure, that are designed with distinct ducts for air and fuel delivery, the ducts being arranged parallel to one another or intersecting in crossing configurations.
[F23D 14/24] at least one of the fluids being submitted to a swirling motion -- The CPC class F23D 14/24 pertains to burners used for gas combustion in which at least one of the supplied fluids is caused to follow a swirling or vortex motion before ignition. This swirl improves mixing and flame stability, and the classification falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners.
[F23D 14/26] with provision for a retention flame -- This subclass defines gas burners that incorporate a retention flame, a small continuously burning pilot used to reignite the main flame if it goes out, typically in devices where the fuel is stored under pressure as a liquid. It applies to combustion apparatus in the mechanical-engineering field.
[F23D 14/28] in association with a gaseous fuel source, e.g. acetylene generator, or a container for liquefied gas -- F23D 14/28 covers burners that are integrated with a gaseous fuel source, such as an acetylene generator or a container of liquefied gas, to supply fuel directly to the combustion device. The classification applies to systems where the fuel source and burner are combined for more efficient or compact operation.
[F23D 14/30] Inverted burners, e.g. for illumination -- This CPC subclass (F23D 14/30) covers inverted burners, where the combustion chamber is positioned above the flame outlet so the flame points downward, a design commonly used for illumination purposes. The entry applies to burners that burn gases stored under pressure as liquids.
[F23D 14/32] using a mixture of gaseous fuel and pure oxygen or oxygen-enriched air -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Torches, e.g. for cutting, brazing, welding or heating -> F23D14/38
[F23D 14/34] Burners specially adapted for use with means for pressurising the gaseous fuel or the combustion air -- Burners specially adapted for use with pressurised gaseous fuel or pressurised combustion air are covered by this subclass. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners, focusing on devices that integrate or cooperate with compressors, pumps or other pressure-boosting equipment to deliver fuel or air at elevated pressures for efficient flame control.
[F23D 14/36] in which the compressor and burner form a single unit -- F23D 14/36 relates to burners in which the gas compressor and the combustion burner are combined into a single integrated unit, typically for use with gases stored under pressure as liquids. The classification covers devices where compression and ignition functions are mechanically merged to simplify fuel handling and improve efficiency.
[F23D 14/38] Torches, e.g. for brazing or heating -- The subclass concerns torches that serve as burners for gas-fuel combustion, such as devices used in brazing, heating or similar processes where a pressurised gas is released as a liquid and ignited. It is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and burners.
[F23D 14/40] for welding -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Torches specially adapted for use under water -> F23D14/44
[F23D 14/42] for cutting -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Torches specially adapted for use under water -> F23D14/44
[F23D 14/44] for use under water -- F23D 14/44 covers burners that ignite a gas stored under pressure as a liquid and are specifically designed for operation underwater. These devices are used in submerged combustion apparatus such as underwater welding torches or marine heating systems, where the flame must be sustained despite the surrounding water pressure.

[F23D 14/465] for torches  F23D14/52  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Nozzles for torches -> F23D14/52
[F23D 14/48] Nozzles -- F23D 14/48 relates to nozzles employed in burners that combust gases stored under pressure as liquids, such as liquefied petroleum gas. The classification covers designs that shape, atomize, or otherwise direct the fuel flow to achieve efficient, controlled combustion within the burner system.
[F23D 14/50] Cleaning devices therefor -- F23D 14/50 covers cleaning devices for burners that combust a gas stored under pressure as a liquid, used in mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus. It includes mechanisms and systems designed to remove deposits, soot or other residues from the burner interior to maintain efficient flame performance.
[F23D 14/52] for torches -- F23D 14/52 covers burners designed for torches used to ignite and sustain combustion of a gas, often supplied as a pressurised liquid. These devices typically direct the flame for applications such as cutting, welding or heating.
[F23D 14/54] for cutting or welding metal -- F23D 14/54 refers to gas-fuel burners specifically designed for metal cutting or welding operations. This subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners, covering devices that combust pressurised liquid-stored gas to produce the high-temperature flame required for cutting or joining metal workpieces.
[F23D 14/56] for spreading the flame over an area, e.g. for desurfacing of solid material, for surface hardening or for heating workpieces -- F23D 14/56 classifies burners used to spread a flame across a surface, such as for desurfacing solid material, surface hardening, or heating workpieces. These devices are intended for gaseous fuels, often stored under pressure as liquids, and fall under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners.
[F23D 14/58] characterised by the shape or arrangement of the outlet or outlets from the nozzle, e.g. of annular configuration -- F23D 14/58 denotes burners for gas combustion whose nozzle outlet shape or arrangement is specifically defined, such as an annular configuration, influencing the flame pattern. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → burners and addresses devices using gas stored under pressure as a liquid.
[F23D 14/583] of elongated shape, e.g. slits -- The subclass F23D 14/583 includes burners that are formed with an elongated geometry, such as slits, and are designed for the combustion of gaseous fuels, particularly those supplied from pressure-liquid storage.
[F23D 14/586] formed by a set of sheets, strips, ribbons or the like -- A burner in which the combustion zone is created by arranging sheets, strips, ribbons or similar elongated components to form a porous or layered structure that allows the gas-fuel to pass through and burn, often used for gases stored under pressure as liquids. This configuration provides a large surface area for flame stabilization and heat transfer while maintaining a compact, modular design.
[F23D 14/60] Devices for simultaneous control of gas and combustion air -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices operating on both combustion air and fuel gas for controlling air to fuel ratio
[F23D 14/62] Mixing devices -- F23D 14/62 belongs to the CPC group for burners that combust a gas, including fuel stored under pressure as a liquid, and it specifically covers mixing devices employed in such burners to blend fuel and air or other oxidising agents before ignition.
[F23D 14/64] with injectors -- F23D 14/64 relates to burners that combust gases, especially those drawn from pressurised liquid storage, and that employ injector devices to atomise or mix the fuel with air for efficient combustion. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burners in the CPC system.
[F23D 14/66] Preheating the combustion air or gas -- The code F23D 14/66 covers burners used for the combustion of a gas, including liquefied gases stored under pressure, in which the combustion air or the fuel gas is heated before ignition. This preheating improves combustion efficiency and stability. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of combustion apparatus and burners.
[F23D 14/68] Treating the combustion air or gas, e.g. by filtering, or moistening -- F23D 14/68 relates to methods for conditioning the combustion air or fuel gas before it enters a burner, including processes such as filtering, moistening or other treatments that enhance combustion efficiency and stability.
[F23D 14/70] Baffles or like flow-disturbing devices -- F23D 14/70 covers baffles and other devices that disturb the flow of gases within burners used for combustion of pressurised gas stored as a liquid. These flow-disturbing components are intended to improve mixing, flame stability, or heat transfer in mechanical engineering combustion apparatus.
[F23D 14/72] Safety devices, e.g. operative in case of failure of gas supply -- The CPC class F23D 14/72 covers safety devices for gas-fuel burners that activate when the gas supply fails, such as shut-off valves, flame-detectors or pressure-release mechanisms designed to prevent uncontrolled combustion. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners.
[F23D 14/725] Protection against flame failure by using flame detection devices  pilot flame igniters with interlock with main fuel supply  F23Q9/08 -- The classification F23D 14/725 covers safety systems for gas-fuel burners that prevent flame loss by employing flame-detection sensors, pilot-flame igniters, and interlocks that automatically shut off the main fuel supply when the flame extinguishes. It applies to burners using pressurised liquid-to-gas fuel and relates to the broader CPC subgroup F23Q9/08 on flame-failure protection devices.
[F23D 14/74] Preventing flame lift-off -- F23D 14/74 refers to measures that keep the flame anchored to the burner outlet in gas-combustion burners, preventing flame lift-off and improving stability and safety, especially for burners using gas stored under pressure as a liquid.
[F23D 14/76] Protecting flame and burner parts -- The class covers protective measures for flame and burner components, such as liners, shields or casings that safeguard parts exposed to high-temperature combustion gases. It includes devices preventing overheating, corrosion or mechanical damage in burners used for gas-fuel combustion, especially where the fuel is stored under pressure as a liquid.
[F23D 14/78] Cooling burner parts -- F23D 14/78 classifies cooling features applied to components of burners used for gas combustion, such as those employing pressurized liquid fuel. It covers designs, methods, and devices that provide heat-removal from burner parts to maintain performance and safety.
[F23D 14/80] Selection of a non-toxic gas -- F23D 14/80 classifies burners that operate on non-toxic gases, covering the choice of gases stored under pressure as liquids for safe combustion. It includes criteria for selecting gases that pose minimal health or environmental hazards while providing suitable flame characteristics.
[F23D 14/82] Preventing flashback or blowback -- F23D 14/82 concerns safety measures for gas burners that stop flashback or blowback of the flame into the fuel supply. It includes flame arrestors, back-pressure devices and other design features that keep the combustion zone isolated from the inlet and prevent hazardous reverse propagation.
[F23D 14/825] using valves -- The CPC subclass F23D 14/825 covers burners that employ valves to regulate the flow of a pressurised gas stored as a liquid for combustion. It pertains to mechanical-engineering devices where valve control is integral to the burner's operation.

[F23D 17/00] Other burners -- F23D 17/00, classified under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners, covers burners that do not fit into more specific sub-categories. It includes a variety of designs, fuel sources and operating principles that differ from standard gas, oil or solid-fuel burners, encompassing innovative or specialized combustion devices.
[F23D 17/002] gaseous or liquid fuel -- F23D 17/002 covers burners that operate on gaseous or liquid fuels, including designs for heating, drying, or combustion processes where the fuel is supplied in gas or liquid form. It pertains to non-specific burner types that do not fall under more specialized sub-categories.
[F23D 17/005] gaseous or pulverulent fuel -- F23D 17/005 covers burners that operate on gaseous or pulverulent fuels, including designs and methods for delivering and combusting such fuels in various applications. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners, other burners.
[F23D 17/007] liquid or pulverulent fuel -- This subclass covers burners that use liquid or finely divided (pulverulent) fuels, such as oil, gas-derived liquids or powdered solid fuels. It includes the design of fuel-feeding arrangements and combustion methods specific to these fuel types within combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2200/00] Indexing codes associated with burners -- F23D 2200/00 is a Cooperative Patent Classification subgroup that falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and burners. It covers the use of indexing codes to categorize and identify different types of burners and their features. The entry aids in systematic searching and classification of burner-related inventions.
[F23D 2201/00] Burners adapted for particulate solid or pulverulent fuels -- A burner designed to operate with solid particles or pulverised fuels such as coal dust, wood chips or powdered metals, featuring adaptations that ensure reliable fuel delivery, stable combustion and safe handling of the particulate material. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners, coded F23D 2201/00.
[F23D 2201/10] Nozzle tips -- The classification F23D 2201/10 covers nozzle tips for burners that are designed to operate with particulate solid or pulverulent fuels. It pertains to the geometry and features of the tip that shape the fuel-air stream, control spray or jet characteristics, and enhance combustion efficiency in solid-fuel burners.
[F23D 2201/101] tiltable -- This subclass covers burners that are designed to operate on solid or powdered fuels and can be tilted to adjust the direction of the flame or fuel feed. The tiltable feature allows the burner to be angled relative to its mounting surface for improved combustion control.
[F23D 2201/20] Fuel flow guiding devices -- F23D 2201/20 relates to devices that direct the flow of solid or pulverised fuel into burners, providing uniform distribution and stable combustion. These fuel-flow guiding elements include nozzles, distributors or channelled plates specifically designed for particulate fuels.
[F23D 2201/30] Wear protection -- The F23D 2201/30 subclass deals with wear-protection solutions for burners that operate on solid or pulverised fuels, covering protective linings, coatings and design features intended to minimise erosion, abrasion and material loss in the combustion zone. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burners.
[F23D 2202/00] Liquid fuel burners -- F23D 2202/00 covers burners that use liquid fuels such as oil, gasoline or kerosene to produce a controlled flame for heating, drying or propulsion. It includes designs for atomising, mixing and igniting the fuel-air mixture, and mechanisms for regulating flame size and intensity in industrial or domestic applications.
[F23D 2203/00] Gaseous fuel burners -- F23D 2203/00 classifies burners that utilise gaseous fuels such as natural gas, LPG or hydrogen to generate a flame for heating, cooking or industrial processes. It covers the design, configuration and operation of the burner head, fuel-air mixing, ignition and flame control mechanisms.
[F23D 2203/002] Radiant burner mixing tubes -- The CPC subclass F23D 2203/002 covers mixing tubes incorporated into radiant burners that use gaseous fuel. It includes tube designs that blend fuel and combustion air before the radiant heating zone to achieve uniform flame and efficient heat transfer. The classification applies to burners where the mixing tube is a key element of the combustion system.
[F23D 2203/005] Radiant burner heads -- Radiant burner heads are components of gaseous-fuel burners that generate heat primarily by infrared radiation rather than convection. They are designed with surfaces that become hot and emit radiant energy to heat a target area, and are used in various combustion devices such as furnaces, ovens and industrial heating systems.
[F23D 2203/007] Mixing tubes, air supply regulation -- F23D 2203/007 covers mixing tubes used in gaseous fuel burners to blend fuel and air before combustion, and mechanisms that regulate the air supply to achieve proper mixture and flame stability. The classification applies to devices that integrate these functions within burner assemblies.
[F23D 2203/10] Flame diffusing means -- F23D 2203/10 designates flame-diffusing means used in gaseous-fuel burners, which are devices that spread, shape or modify the flame to improve combustion stability, heat distribution and emission control. These components belong to the broader class of combustion apparatus within mechanical engineering.
[F23D 2203/101] characterised by surface shape -- F23D 2203/101 covers gaseous-fuel burners that are defined primarily by the shape of their burning surface. The surface geometry-such as curvature, pattern or texture-is the distinguishing feature influencing flame formation, heat distribution or combustion efficiency within the broader class of mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2203/1012] tubular -- The F23D 2203/1012 entry covers tubular burners that use gaseous fuel, where the flame is produced within a tube or passageway to provide a controlled, directed flame. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners and specifically gaseous fuel burners. It characterises burners whose primary construction is a hollow tube that channels the fuel-air mixture for combustion.
[F23D 2203/1015] spherical -- The code F23D 2203/1015 designates spherical burners that use gaseous fuel, a subset of burners within the combustion apparatus group of mechanical engineering. These devices feature a spherical combustion chamber to promote a uniform flame and efficient mixing of gas and air.
[F23D 2203/1017] curved -- The CPC subclass F23D 2203/1017 pertains to gaseous-fuel burners whose flame-producing elements or combustion chamber have a curved geometry. It includes designs where the curvature is used to shape the flame, fit confined spaces or improve heat distribution in combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2203/102] using perforated plates -- This subgroup pertains to gaseous fuel burners that incorporate perforated plates to shape, mix, or distribute the flame or combustion gases. The plates may be used to control airflow, improve mixing, or protect components, enhancing combustion efficiency and stability. The classification captures designs where perforated elements are an essential part of the burner geometry.
[F23D 2203/1023] with specific free passage areas -- F23D 2203/1023 classifies gaseous fuel burners featuring designated free passage areas that permit unrestricted flow of fuel or flame, such as vent channels or openings engineered to enhance mixing, combustion stability, or heat distribution. These passages are shaped or positioned to control the movement of gases while avoiding obstruction, and the subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners.
[F23D 2203/1026] with slotshaped openings -- This subclass covers gaseous-fuel burners that incorporate slot-shaped openings in the combustion zone. The slots are used to direct flame, mix fuel and air, or shape the heat output, and are a defining feature of the burner design in patent disclosures.
[F23D 2203/103] using screens -- The subclass F23D 2203/103 covers gaseous-fuel burners that employ screens within the combustion zone. Screens, such as metal meshes or perforated plates, are used to shape the flame, protect the burner core, filter particulates, and enhance stability or prevent flashback.
[F23D 2203/104] Grids, e.g. honeycomb grids -- F23D 2203/104 covers the use of grids, including honeycomb-type structures, within gaseous fuel burners. The classification addresses how these perforated or cellular grids are incorporated into the burner design to influence flame stability, heat distribution, or emissions control.
[F23D 2203/105] Porous plates -- F23D 2203/105 covers porous plates used in gaseous fuel burners to create a stable flame front and enhance heat exchange. The plates, typically ceramic or metal, let fuel pass through a fine matrix where it ignites, producing a low-emission, uniform flame suitable for heating, drying or combustion control.
[F23D 2203/1055] with a specific void range -- F23D 2203/1055 classifies gaseous-fuel burners that feature a specifically defined void range, such as the spacing or open area within the burner geometry used to influence flame stability and heat distribution. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → burners.
[F23D 2203/106] Assemblies of different layers -- F23D 2203/106 covers burner constructions in which two or more functional layers are combined into an integrated assembly, such as a premixing layer, a flame stabilisation layer and a heat-transfer layer, configured for gaseous fuel combustion.
[F23D 2203/107] coated with catalysts -- The F23D 2203/107 subgroup covers gaseous-fuel burners whose combustion surfaces are treated with a catalyst coating. It includes designs where the catalyst layer is applied to the burner to promote or accelerate flame initiation and stabilize combustion, typically to improve efficiency, lower emissions, or enable operation at lower temperatures.
[F23D 2203/108] with stacked sheets or strips forming the outlets -- This subclass covers gaseous-fuel burners whose outlet is formed by a series of stacked sheets or strips, creating a layered passage for the flame. The design is intended to control flow and heat distribution in combustion devices.
[F23D 2204/00] Burners adapted for simultaneous or alternative combustion having more than one fuel supply -- The subclass covers burners that are constructed to accept two or more fuel supplies so that they can burn the fuels either at the same time or alternately, providing flexibility in heating or process applications and improving overall efficiency. It includes designs where separate fuel lines, mixing chambers or control mechanisms enable the simultaneous or selective combustion of different combustible media.
[F23D 2204/10] gaseous and liquid fuel -- This CPC subclass covers burners that can operate with both gas and liquid fuels, either simultaneously or alternately, allowing multiple fuel supplies for flexible combustion. It sits within mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burners, and addresses designs and methods for using gaseous and liquid fuels in a single burner.
[F23D 2204/20] gaseous and pulverulent fuel -- This subclass covers burners designed to work with both gaseous and pulverulent fuels, enabling simultaneous or alternative combustion using more than one fuel supply. It includes configurations that can mix or switch between the two fuel types for greater efficiency or operational flexibility.
[F23D 2204/30] liquid and pulverulent fuel -- Burners that can combust both liquid and pulverulent (powdered) fuels, either simultaneously or alternately, using multiple fuel supply lines. Such devices provide flexibility to switch or combine fuel types for improved efficiency or adaptability.
[F23D 2205/00] Assemblies of two or more burners, irrespective of fuel type -- Assemblies of two or more burners, independent of the type of fuel used, are classified under this code. [F23D 2206/0021] Gas burners for use in furnaces of the reverberatory, muffle or crucible type -- Gas burners adapted for heating reverberatory, muffle or crucible furnaces, employing a gaseous fuel to generate a controlled flame that provides uniform temperature distribution within these specialized thermal processing vessels.

[F23D 2208/00] Control devices associated with burners -- F23D 2208/00 covers devices that monitor and regulate burner operation, such as flame-sensing sensors, fuel-air mix controllers, ignition timers, safety shut-off mechanisms and electronic or pneumatic regulators that adjust flame characteristics to maintain stable combustion. These control systems are used in a wide range of heating, process-heat and power-generation burners.
[F23D 2208/005] Controlling air supply in radiant gas burners -- The entry F23D 2208/005 concerns devices that regulate the air flow to radiant gas burners used in combustion apparatus. [F23D 2208/10] Sensing devices -- The CPC subclass F23D 2208/10 refers to sensing devices employed in burner control systems, including sensors that detect temperature, flame presence, pressure, gas composition or other combustion parameters to enable safe, efficient regulation of burners.
[F23D 2209/00] Safety arrangements -- The CPC class F23D 2209/00 relates to safety arrangements for burners used in combustion apparatus. It covers protective devices, monitoring systems and control measures that prevent fire hazards, flame-out, over-pressure or accidental ignition, ensuring safe operation of heating or industrial burners.
[F23D 2209/10] Flame flashback -- F23D 2209/10 covers safety arrangements that prevent flame flashback in burners, where a flame moves upstream toward the fuel source and may cause hazardous conditions. It includes devices or methods such as flashback arrestors, pressure differentials, or flame-holding structures that stop the flame from propagating back into the combustion chamber.
[F23D 2209/20] Flame lift-off / stability -- F23D 2209/20 pertains to safety arrangements for burners that manage flame lift-off and stability, describing features that prevent the flame from separating from the burner surface and ensure consistent combustion performance.
[F23D 2209/30] Purging -- F23D 2209/30 covers safety measures that purge burners or their fuel supply systems of combustible gases or vapors before ignition or after shutdown. It includes devices, procedures and control systems that clear residual fuel, air or flue gases to prevent flashback, explosion or uncontrolled combustion. The classification is used for inventions related to purging mechanisms in burner safety arrangements.
[F23D 2210/00] Noise abatement -- F23D 2210/00 pertains to measures for reducing the acoustic emissions of burners used in combustion devices. It includes designs such as sound-absorbing liners, mufflers, flame-stabilising geometries and other modifications that lower noise levels during operation. The code is applied in patents addressing noise abatement in mechanical engineering combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2210/101] using noise dampening material -- F23D 2210/101 concerns the application of noise-dampening material in burners of combustion apparatus to reduce acoustic emissions during operation. It is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus, burners, with a focus on noise abatement.
[F23D 2211/00] Thermal dilatation prevention or compensation -- F23D 2211/00 relates to devices or methods that prevent or compensate for thermal expansion in burners used in combustion apparatus. It includes structures, supports, or control mechanisms that maintain dimensional stability during heating to avoid distortion, leakage or performance loss. The classification falls under Mechanical Engineering, Combustion Apparatus, Burners.
[F23D 2212/00] Burner material specifications
[F23D 2212/005] Radiant gas burners made of specific materials, e.g. rare earths -- This subclass pertains to radiant gas burners whose design incorporates particular materials, such as rare-earth elements, to meet defined performance, durability, or corrosion-resistance specifications.
[F23D 2212/10] ceramic -- F23D 2212/10 designates specifications for ceramic materials employed in burners, covering composition, properties, and performance criteria relevant to combustion apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners, focusing on material requirements for durability, heat resistance, and safety.
[F23D 2212/101] Foam, e.g. reticulated -- A reticulated foam is a lightweight, porous polymer material whose open-cell structure gives a high surface area while allowing gas flow, making it suitable for burner components that require efficient flame spread and heat transfer. Its low density and thermal resistance help control combustion temperatures and reduce material wear in burner applications.
[F23D 2212/103] Fibres -- F23D 2212/103 defines the material specifications for fibres employed in burners, detailing required composition, dimensions, mechanical strength and thermal resistance to ensure reliable performance in combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2212/105] Particles -- The subclass F23D 2212/105 addresses the specification and use of particles in burner construction, covering the selection, composition, size, shape and distribution of refractory or catalytic particles and other solid additives that influence combustion performance and durability. It relates to material-level design of burners.
[F23D 2212/20] metallic -- F23D 2212/20 defines the material specifications for burners that use metallic components, covering the selection, composition, thermal and corrosion-resistance properties, and manufacturing tolerances required for metal burner parts. This subgroup refines the broader burner material categories by focusing on metals suitable for combustion-heat applications.
[F23D 2212/201] Fibres -- The code F23D 2212/201 refers to the specification of fibre materials used in burners, covering the selection, treatment and performance of fibrous components that affect combustion stability, heat resistance and corrosion protection. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burner material specifications within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F23D 2212/203] Particles -- The CPC subclass F23D 2212/203 covers the specification of particles used in burner construction and operation. It relates to material characteristics such as size, composition and distribution that influence the performance, efficiency and emissions of combustion burners. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners and burner material specifications.
[F23D 2213/00] Burner manufacture specifications -- The CPC subclass F23D 2213/00 covers technical specifications governing the manufacture of burners, including required materials, dimensional tolerances, construction methods, performance criteria such as flame stability and emissions, and testing procedures to ensure compliance with safety and efficiency standards. It guides designers and producers of combustion devices.
[F23D 2214/00] Cooling -- F23D 2214/00 covers cooling methods and devices for burners, including arrangements that remove heat from combustion chambers, flame holders or other burner components to prevent overheating and maintain performance. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Burners.
[F23D 23/00] Assemblies of two or more burners -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gas burner with provisions for a retention flame -> F23D14/26
[F23D 2900/00] Special features of, or arrangements for burners using fluid fuels or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas -- This subclass covers burners that burn liquid or solid fuels delivered as a spray or suspension in a carrier gas, emphasizing design details such as fuel injection, mixing chambers, flame stabilization, and auxiliary devices that improve combustion efficiency or control emissions.
[F23D 2900/00001] local catalytic coatings applied to burner surfaces -- Local catalytic coatings applied to burner surfaces are thin layers of catalyst deposited on parts of a burner that use fluid or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas. The coatings promote more complete combustion, improve flame stability and lower pollutant emissions, especially in precision heating or industrial processes.
[F23D 2900/00002] Cleaning burner parts, e.g. burner tips -- This CPC subclass covers methods for cleaning burner components such as burner tips in combustion apparatus. It includes techniques for removing deposits or residues from parts that handle fluid or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners.
[F23D 2900/00003] Fuel or fuel-air mixtures flow distribution devices upstream of the outlet -- Devices for distributing fuel or fuel-air mixtures before they exit a burner, covering arrangements that handle fluid fuels or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas, used to achieve uniform flow and proper mixing upstream of the outlet in combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2900/00004] Burners specially adapted for generating high luminous flames, e.g. yellow for fuel-rich mixtures -- F23D 2900/00004 covers burners specially adapted to produce high-luminous yellow flames by operating fuel-rich mixtures, typically used when a bright visual effect is required. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners, and addresses arrangements where fluid or solid fuels are dispersed in a carrier gas to enhance luminosity.
[F23D 2900/00006] Liquid fuel burners using pure oxygen or oxygen-enriched air as oxidant -- F23D 2900/00006 covers burners that combust liquid fuels with pure oxygen or oxygen-enriched air as the oxidising medium. The code is used for designs where the fuel is delivered in a carrier gas and the combustion system is tailored for high-oxygen environments.
[F23D 2900/00008] Burner assemblies with diffusion and premix modes, i.e. dual mode burners -- The subclass F23D 2900/00008 covers burner assemblies that are capable of operating in both diffusion and premixed combustion modes, enabling dual-mode performance for fluid or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under combustion apparatus and burners.
[F23D 2900/00011] Burner with means for propagating the flames along a wall surface -- A burner designed to move its flame along a wall surface, typically using a fluid or solid fuel mixed with a carrier gas, enabling controlled combustion over a planar area. This arrangement is classified under the Cooperative Patent Classification for combustion apparatus, specifically within burners that employ such flame-propagation mechanisms.
[F23D 2900/00012] Liquid or gas fuel burners with flames spread over a flat surface, either premix or non-premix type, e.g. "Flächenbrenner" -- F23D 2900/00012 covers burners that spread a flame across a flat surface using liquid or gas fuels, either premixed or non-premixed, commonly referred to as flat-surface burners. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners, and focuses on arrangements where the fuel is delivered in a carrier gas.
[F23D 2900/00013] with means for spreading the flame in a fan or fishtail shape over a melting bath -- The code F23D 2900/00013 designates burners that employ a mechanism to fan- or shape the flame into a fishtail pattern across a melting bath, typically used in metal melting or similar processes. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burner categories, covering fluid or solid fuel combustion with a carrier gas.
[F23D 2900/00014] Pilot burners specially adapted for ignition of main burners in furnaces or gas turbines -- The code F23D 2900/00014 covers pilot burners designed to ignite the main burners in furnaces or gas turbines. [F23D 2900/00015] Pilot burners specially adapted for low load or transient conditions, e.g. for increasing stability -- F23D 2900/00015 covers pilot burners specially adapted for low-load or transient conditions, such as when increased stability is required. [F23D 2900/00016] Preventing or reducing deposit build-up on burner parts, e.g. from carbon -- This CPC subgroup concerns features or arrangements for burners that use fluid or solid fuels carried in a gas stream, specifically aimed at preventing or reducing the formation of deposits such as carbon on burner components. It covers designs that mitigate fouling and maintain efficient combustion.

[F23D 2900/00018] Means for protecting parts of the burner, e.g. ceramic lining outside of the flame tube -- This subclass pertains to protective devices for burners, such as ceramic linings applied to the outside of a flame tube, that shield burner components from high temperatures, erosion or chemical attack in combustion apparatus using fluid or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas.
[F23D 2900/00019] Outlet manufactured from knitted fibres -- A burner outlet constructed from knitted fibres, designed to form the exit point for fluid or solid fuels carried in a gas stream, falls under this subclass. [F23D 2900/01001] Pulverised solid fuel burner with means for swirling the fuel-air mixture -- This CPC subclass (F23D 2900/01001) covers burners that use pulverised solid fuels, where the fuel particles are mixed with air and the mixture is forced to swirl before combustion. The swirling arrangement improves flame stability and heat transfer for combustion apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F23D 2900/03081] Catalytic wick burners -- Catalytic wick burners are combustion devices that use a wick saturated with liquid or solid fuel, which is vaporized by a carrier gas and ignited on a catalytic surface to sustain flame without external ignition. The catalyst lowers the ignition temperature and enables stable burning of low-energy fuels, offering efficient heat output and reduced emissions.
[F23D 2900/03082] Wick made of specific material, e.g. ceramic -- This subclass covers burners that use a wick made of a particular material, such as ceramic, to support fluid or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas. The focus is on the wick composition and its effect on combustion performance.
[F23D 2900/05001] Burner using gel type fuel -- A gel-type fuel burner is a combustion device that uses a semi-solid or gelled fuel mixture, often suspended in a carrier gas, to generate a controlled flame. [F23D 2900/05002] Use of porous members to convert liquid fuel into vapor -- F23D 2900/05002 covers burners that employ porous components to atomize or vaporise liquid fuel before combustion. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burners, and addresses special arrangements where a carrier gas suspends the fuel within a porous matrix.
[F23D 2900/11001] Impinging-jet injectors or jet impinging on a surface -- This CPC subclass covers burners that use impinging jets of fluid or solid fuels carried in a gas, where the jet strikes a surface or another jet to improve combustion. [F23D 2900/11101] Pulverising gas flow impinging on fuel from pre-filming surface, e.g. lip atomizers -- This subclass covers burners where a high-velocity gas stream strikes a fuel film on a pre-filming surface, breaking the fuel into droplets for combustion, as in lip-type atomizers. [F23D 2900/31001] Wick burners without flame spreaders or burner hood -- This subclass covers wick-type burners that operate without any flame-spreading devices or protective hoods.
