[F23D 2900/31002] Wick burners with flame spreaders or burner hood -- A wick burner incorporating a flame spreader or a burner hood is designed to distribute the flame evenly and protect the flame front when burning fluid or solid fuels carried by a gas. This arrangement improves combustion stability, heat distribution, and reduces emissions in combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2900/31003] Inverted wick burners -- Inverted wick burners place the wick below the combustion zone so fuel is drawn upward by capillary action, allowing stable flames with liquid fuels or solid particles carried in a gas stream. This design is classified in CPC F23D 2900/31003, a subgroup for special features of burners that use fluids or solids suspended in a carrier gas.
[F23D 2900/31004] Wick burners using alcohol as a fuel -- The subclass F23D 2900/31004 pertains to burners in which a wick supplies alcohol fuel that is vaporised and burned, typically with a carrier gas, to provide a controlled flame for heating or cooking applications. It covers the design and arrangement of such wick-fed alcohol burners.
[F23D 2900/31005] Wick burners using oil as a fuel -- This subclass covers wick burners that use oil as the fuel, drawing the oil through a wick to produce a flame, often with a carrier gas to aid combustion. [F23D 2900/31006] Details of blue flame wick burners -- This class covers the design and operation of blue-flame wick burners that burn fluid fuels or solid fuel particles suspended in a carrier gas. [F23D 2900/31008] Blue flame burners with flame spreader or burner hood without a bead at the wick carrying tube -- The subclass defines blue-flame burners that incorporate a flame spreader or hood but omit a bead at the wick-carrying tube, used for burning fluid or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas. [F23D 2900/31013] Wick adjusting devices engaging the tube carrying the wick -- Wick adjusting devices engaging the tube carrying the wick are mechanisms that modify the position, tension or exposure of a wick inside a burner tube, allowing precise control of fuel delivery and flame shape in burners that use fluid or solid fuels carried by a gas.

[F23D 2900/31016] Burners in which the gas produced in the wick is not burned instantaneously -- This CPC subclass F23D 2900/31016 covers burners that use a wick to generate combustible gas which is not ignited immediately, allowing the gas to accumulate or mix before combustion. Such designs are typically used in lamps or heating devices where controlled, delayed flame initiation improves efficiency or safety.
[F23D 2900/31017] Burners using carburetted gas -- Burners that employ carburetted gas-a mixture of gaseous fuel and liquid in a carrier-are covered, describing designs where this fuel is burned in combustion apparatus. [F23D 2900/31018] Nozzles and cleaning devices therefor -- The entry covers nozzle designs and associated cleaning mechanisms for burners that operate with fluid fuels or solid fuels carried in a gaseous stream, focusing on features that improve fuel-air mixing, spray quality, and resistance to clogging, as well as methods for removing deposits and restoring nozzle performance.
[F23D 2900/31019] Mixing tubes combined with burner heads -- The code refers to burner designs in which mixing tubes are integrated with the burner head to premix fluid or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas before combustion. This arrangement enhances fuel-air homogeneity, stabilises the flame and can improve overall efficiency of combustion apparatus.
[F23D 2900/3102] Preheating devices -- F23D 2900/3102 refers to devices that preheat the fuel-air mixture or carrier gas in burners that operate with fluid fuels or solid fuels suspended in a carrier gas. The class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and burners, focusing on special features and arrangements for such fluid-fuel burners.

[F23D 3/04] with flame spreaders -- The F23D 3/04 class covers burners that combust a liquid fuel and incorporate flame spreaders to distribute the flame over a larger surface area, improving combustion stability and heat distribution. [F23D 3/06] Inverted wick burners, e.g. for illumination -- This subclass covers inverted wick burners used for illumination, where a wick is positioned upside down to draw fuel by capillary action from a liquid reservoir and burn it to produce light. [F23D 3/08] characterised by shape, construction, or material, of wick -- This CPC subgroup pertains to burners whose wick's shape, construction, or material is a defining characteristic of the device. [F23D 3/10] Blue-flame burners -- F23D 3/10 designates burners that generate a blue flame by combusting liquid fuels, typically using a premixed vapor-air mixture for clean, high-temperature heating. [F23D 3/12] with flame spreaders -- The subclass F23D 3/12 covers burners that combust liquid fuels and incorporate flame spreaders to distribute the flame across a broader surface, enhancing heat uniformity and reducing localized overheating. It applies to devices such as furnaces, ovens and industrial heating units where controlled liquid-fuel combustion with spread-out flames is required.
[F23D 3/14] with mixing of air and fuel vapour in a chamber before the flame -- The subclass F23D 3/14 relates to burners that premix air and fuel vapour in a chamber before ignition. It covers combustion devices for liquid fuels where the air-fuel mixture is formed in a separate mixing space prior to the flame, improving combustion stability and efficiency.
[F23D 3/16] using candles -- F23D 3/16 covers burners that use candles as the combustion source for burning liquid fuels. It includes designs where a candle flame initiates or sustains liquid combustion and related control or safety features specific to candle-based systems.
[F23D 3/18] Details of wick burners -- F23D 3/18 relates to wick burners, which are combustion devices that draw liquid fuel through a wick to sustain a flame for heating or lighting. The classification covers design details such as wick material, geometry, capillary action, and mechanisms that regulate fuel supply and flame characteristics.
[F23D 3/20] Flame spreaders -- F23D 3/20 covers flame spreaders used in burners that combust liquid fuels. These devices distribute the flame over a larger area to improve heat uniformity and control combustion characteristics. They are part of combustion apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F23D 3/22] Devices for mixing evaporated fuel with air -- Devices that blend evaporated fuel with air to form a uniform combustible mixture before ignition in liquid-fuel burners, often employing swirling, atomising or venturi principles to improve mixing efficiency and flame stability.
[F23D 3/24] Carriers for wicks -- F23D 3/24 relates to components that hold or support wicks in burners using liquid fuel, such as oil-lamp or heater wicks. It covers the design, materials and arrangement of wick carriers that ensure stable fuel delivery and flame formation, often focusing on capillary action and heat resistance. These carriers may be integral to the burner body or provided as removable inserts.
[F23D 3/26] Safety devices thereon -- The subclass F23D 3/26 deals with safety mechanisms incorporated in burners that combust liquid fuels, covering devices that detect flame failure, regulate or cut off fuel flow, and otherwise protect against hazardous operation of liquid-fuel burners.
[F23D 3/28] Wick-adjusting devices -- Wick-adjusting devices are mechanisms incorporated in liquid-fuel burners to modify the position or tension of the wick that supplies fuel to the flame. By changing the wick's exposure to the liquid, they regulate fuel flow, flame size and combustion stability.
[F23D 3/30] directly engaging with the wick -- F23D 3/30 covers burners in which the liquid fuel is fed to the flame by direct contact with a wick, as found in oil lamps and similar wick-fed devices. The wick itself supplies the combustible liquid to the combustion zone, providing a simple means of regulating fuel flow and flame size. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners, and specifically combustion of a liquid.
[F23D 3/32] engaging with a tube carrying the wick -- The subclass F23D 3/32 covers burners that combust liquid fuel using a wick drawn through a tube, focusing on features that attach, secure or otherwise engage with that tube. It includes designs for holding the tube in place, aligning it with the combustion zone, or ensuring reliable fuel delivery within the burner assembly.
[F23D 3/34] Wick stop devices -- Wick stop devices are mechanisms that interrupt the fuel supply to the wick of a liquid-fuel burner, preventing further combustion when the flame is extinguished or when safety is required. They are used in burners that operate by drawing liquid fuel through a wick and are classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners, and combustion of a liquid.
[F23D 3/36] Devices for trimming wicks -- F23D 3/36 classifies mechanisms used to cut or shape the wick of a liquid-fuel burner, ensuring proper flame size and stability. It includes manual or automated devices that adjust wick length to control fuel flow and combustion efficiency.
[F23D 3/38] Devices for replacement of wicks -- F23D 3/38 covers devices that replace the wick in liquid-fuel burners, enabling automatic or manual replenishment of the wick to maintain combustion. These mechanisms are used in lamps, heaters and similar appliances to improve reliability and control of fuel feed.
[F23D 3/40] the capillary action taking place in one or more rigid porous bodies -- This subgroup covers the capillary action that moves liquid fuel through one or more rigid porous bodies in burners, describing how wicking or fluid transport within solid porous structures supplies fuel to the combustion zone.
[F23D 5/00] Burners in which liquid fuel evaporates in the combustion space, with or without chemical conversion of evaporated fuel -- This CPC subclass F23D 5/00 covers burners in which liquid fuel is introduced into the combustion chamber and allowed to evaporate before burning, optionally undergoing chemical transformation of the vapor. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners and is used for technologies such as fuel-spray burners, oil-fired furnaces and similar devices where evaporation occurs within the flame zone.
[F23D 5/02] the liquid forming a pool, e.g. bowl-type evaporators, dish-type evaporators -- This subclass covers burners in which the liquid fuel is held as a pool inside the combustion chamber, such as bowl-type or dish-type evaporators, allowing the fuel to evaporate before it ignites. It pertains to combustion apparatus where the pool-forming design influences fuel evaporation and flame formation.
[F23D 5/04] Pot-type evaporators, i.e. using a partially-enclosed combustion space -- This subclass covers burners that employ a pot-type evaporator, in which liquid fuel is heated inside a partially enclosed combustion space before vaporising and burning. The design allows controlled fuel evaporation and mixing, improving stability and efficiency in heating and industrial applications.
[F23D 5/045] with forced draft -- Burners that evaporate liquid fuel within the combustion space and rely on a forced draft, meaning airflow is supplied by a fan or blower to maintain combustion conditions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners.
[F23D 5/06] the liquid forming a film on one or more plane or convex surfaces -- This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and burners where liquid fuel evaporates in the combustion space. The code F23D 5/06 covers burners in which the liquid forms a film on one or more planar or convex surfaces before vaporising.
[F23D 5/08] on cascaded surfaces -- F23D 5/08 covers burners that evaporate liquid fuel within the combustion space using cascaded surfaces, where the fuel spreads over a series of stepped or inclined plates to promote rapid vaporisation before ignition. This arrangement improves fuel-air mixing and flame stability and may be employed with or without catalytic conversion of the vaporised fuel.
[F23D 5/10] on grids -- It covers burners that use a grid, such as a perforated plate or mesh, as the surface on which liquid fuel is spread, atomized or evaporated in the combustion space. The grid serves to support flame, distribute fuel and improve heat transfer while the fuel may undergo chemical conversion before burning.
[F23D 5/12] Details
[F23D 5/123] Inserts promoting evaporation -- F23D 5/123 covers inserts placed in liquid-fuel burners that accelerate the evaporation of the fuel within the combustion zone. These devices may be porous bodies, heated elements or structures that increase surface area, facilitating faster fuel vaporisation and a more stable flame, thereby improving combustion efficiency.
[F23D 5/126] Catalytic elements -- This CPC group covers burners in which liquid fuel is evaporated in the combustion zone and the flame or combustion process is assisted by catalytic elements, which may be placed in the combustion space or in a separate zone, with or without chemical conversion of the evaporated fuel.
[F23D 5/14] Maintaining predetermined amount of fuel in evaporator -- F23D 5/14 covers burners where liquid fuel is evaporated in the combustion zone and the system provides a way to keep a set amount of fuel in the evaporator, ensuring a steady supply of fuel vapour for consistent combustion.
[F23D 5/16] Safety devices -- F23D 5/16 covers safety devices for burners in which liquid fuel evaporates in the combustion space, including mechanisms that prevent unsafe conditions such as flameout, over-pressure or fuel leakage.
[F23D 5/18] Preheating devices -- F23D 5/18 relates to preheating devices employed in burners where liquid fuel evaporates within the combustion space, with or without subsequent chemical conversion. These devices raise the temperature of the fuel, combustion chamber, or surrounding air to improve ignition and combustion stability.
[F23D 7/00] Burners in which drops of liquid fuel impinge on a surface -- The entry F23D 7/00 covers burners in which a stream of liquid fuel is broken into droplets that strike a surface, where the impact aids atomisation, ignition or flame stabilization. It lies within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion apparatus, specifically the subclass for burners. This classification is used by the EPO and USPTO to index patents dealing with impinging-drop burner designs.
[F23D 9/00] Burners in which a stream of liquid fuel impinges intermittently on a hot surface -- Burners that deliver a liquid fuel in a pulsed jet onto a pre-heated surface, causing the fuel to spread, vaporize and ignite on contact. The intermittent impingement creates localized flame zones and is used for precise heat output control in various combustion systems.
[F23D 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F23D 99/00 is a residual subclass for burners within the combustion-apparatus group whose technical features are not covered by any other defined subclass. It captures novel or unconventional burner designs that do not fit existing categories, providing a catch-all slot for subject matter not otherwise specified.
[F23D 99/002] Burners specially adapted for specific applications -- F23D 99/002 designates burners that are specially adapted for particular uses and are not covered by any other subgroup of the burner subclass. These devices incorporate unique structural or functional features tailored to specific industrial, residential or aerospace applications, such as low-NOx emission configurations, high-temperature tolerance or specialized fuel handling.
[F23D 99/004] for use in particular heating operations -- F23D 99/004 covers burners that are specially adapted for particular heating operations, such as custom-designed units for furnaces, ovens, or other process equipment where the combustion system is optimized for a specific application.
[F23D 99/005] for heating liquids, e.g. for vaporising or concentrating -- F23D 99/005 designates burner arrangements specifically intended to heat liquids, such as systems used for vaporising or concentrating them, where the application is not covered by any other group within the burners subclass of combustion apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, burners.
[F23G] CREMATION FURNACES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus specially adapted for combustion of the following substances:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Disposal of solid waste -> B09B - Reclamation of contaminated soil -> B09C - Gas turbine plants -> F02C - Generating steam -> F22B - Generating combustion products of high temperature or high pressure -> F23R - Domestic stoves or ranges for local heating or cooking -> F24B , F24C - Apparatus for heat treatment of materials or articles -> F27B  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The expression "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspende in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame. A burner includes means for feeding air that are arranged in immediate connection with a fuel feeding conduit, for example concentric with it. - Combustion: the direct combination of oxygen gas, e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of a combustion apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Fuel: any combustible material that can be burned, regardless of whether the main purpose of burning it is for releasing energy therefrom or for disposing of it or rendering it less harmful - Waste: an undesired material that can be consumed by combustion for the primary purpose of disposing of it or rendering it less harmful, and not solely for releasing energy therefrom
[F23G 1/00] Furnaces for cremation of human or animal carcasses -- F23G 1/00 designates furnaces specifically designed for the cremation of human or animal remains. These devices belong to the combustion apparatus subgroup of mechanical engineering and are used to reduce bodies to ash through high-temperature combustion, often in funeral or forensic contexts.
[F23G 2200/00] Waste incineration -- F23G 2200/00 is a CPC subclass under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Cremation Furnaces that pertains to waste incineration technologies. It covers designs, methods and equipment for burning solid refuse or other waste streams in furnaces similar to those used for cremation.
[F23G 2201/00] Pretreatment -- F23G 2201/00 refers to pretreatment procedures and equipment used before cremation, such as removal of fluids, cleaning, or chemical conditioning of the material to enhance combustion efficiency.
[F23G 2201/10] Drying by heat -- The F23G 2201/10 subclass covers methods and apparatuses that remove moisture from materials using heat generated within cremation furnaces. It includes designs where hot gases or surfaces directly dry the load before or during combustion, improving efficiency and reducing emissions. This falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2201/101] using indirect heat transfer -- This CPC subclass pertains to cremation furnaces that employ indirect heat transfer, meaning the heat source is separated from the material being incinerated. It includes designs where hot gases, heated surfaces, or heat-exchanger elements convey thermal energy to the body without direct flame contact, improving control and reducing emissions.
[F23G 2201/20] Dewatering by mechanical means -- F23G 2201/20 covers mechanical dewatering techniques applied to material before it enters a cremation furnace, such as pressing, centrifuging or squeezing to remove moisture and improve combustion efficiency. This pretreatment step is part of the broader category of combustion apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F23G 2201/30] Pyrolysing -- The F23G 2201/30 subclass covers the pretreatment step of pyrolysing material within cremation furnace systems. It relates to methods that thermally decompose organic waste by heating it in the absence of oxygen before or during the main combustion stage, improving fuel efficiency and reducing emissions. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and cremation furnace technologies.
[F23G 2201/301] Treating pyrogases -- The entry F23G 2201/301 pertains to pretreatment processes for the gases produced in cremation furnaces. It covers methods and equipment that clean, cool, filter or otherwise treat pyrogases before they are released to the environment, aiming to reduce emissions of pollutants and improve compliance with air-quality regulations.
[F23G 2201/302] Treating pyrosolids -- The classification F23G 2201/302 covers processes for treating pyrosolids generated in cremation furnaces, focusing on pretreatment steps that separate, clean, or otherwise handle solid combustion residues before disposal or further use. It addresses methods that improve handling, reduce environmental impact, and prepare the material for downstream processing.
[F23G 2201/303] Burning pyrogases -- The subclass F23G 2201/303 covers the burning of pyrogases produced in cremation furnaces. It includes methods and devices for combusting these gases as a pretreatment step to reduce emissions and recover energy.
[F23G 2201/304] Burning pyrosolids -- The F23G 2201/304 entry covers the pretreatment step of burning pyrosolids within cremation furnaces, a subcategory of combustion apparatus used in mechanical engineering. It details processes for incinerating solid carbon-based residues as part of waste-to-energy or disposal operations.
[F23G 2201/40] Gasification -- F23G 2201/40 covers gasification processes used in the pretreatment stage of cremation furnaces, wherein solid material is thermally converted into combustible gases before combustion. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces and pretreatment, and addresses methods and equipment that facilitate this gas-producing step.
[F23G 2201/50] Devolatilising -- Devolatilising is a pretreatment step applied in cremation furnaces where volatile organic compounds and moisture are removed from the material before the main combustion stage. The process reduces emissions, improves fuel efficiency and helps achieve complete ash conversion in the furnace.
[F23G 2201/60] Separating -- F23G 2201/60 relates to methods and devices for separating materials during the pretreatment stage of cremation furnaces, such as removing ash, bone fragments, or other debris before or during combustion. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, and pretreatment.
[F23G 2201/601] different calorific values
[F23G 2201/602] different sizes -- This CPC subclass covers pretreatment operations in cremation furnaces that handle material of different sizes, including sorting, size reduction or segregation before combustion. It addresses methods and devices that adjust the furnace feed based on particle or object dimensions.
[F23G 2201/603] recyclable material -- F23G 2201/603 covers recyclable material employed in the pretreatment stage of cremation furnaces, a subset of combustion apparatus within mechanical engineering. It addresses the handling, preparation and use of waste or by-products that can be recovered or reused during the cremation process.
[F23G 2201/70] Blending -- The F23G 2201/70 subclass concerns blending operations used in the pretreatment of material that will be introduced into a cremation furnace. It covers methods for mixing or homogenising feedstock to improve combustion performance, efficiency or emissions control in cremation equipment.
[F23G 2201/701] with additives -- The CPC subclass F23G 2201/701 covers pretreatment steps applied to cremation furnaces that involve the use of additives. It pertains to mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus where chemicals or other substances are introduced to modify the fuel, improve combustion efficiency, or aid in the removal of residues before the actual cremation process.
[F23G 2201/702] with other waste -- The CPC subclass F23G 2201/702 covers cremation furnaces that include pretreatment steps for handling other waste together with the primary material. It relates to mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus designed to process mixed waste streams during cremation.
[F23G 2201/80] Shredding -- F23G 2201/80 describes shredding methods or devices used as a pretreatment step in cremation furnaces, where solid material such as remains or waste is broken into smaller pieces before incineration. It falls under the mechanical engineering subclass for combustion apparatus and specifically addresses pretreatment processes for cremation.
[F23G 2201/90] Cooling -- F23G 2201/90 covers cooling techniques applied to cremation furnaces during pretreatment, including methods to lower the temperature of the furnace or its components after combustion, such as using water, air flow or heat exchangers. These measures help protect equipment, reduce emissions and prepare the furnace for subsequent operations.
[F23G 2202/00] Combustion -- F23G 2202/00 - Combustion covers the methods and devices for generating and controlling flame and heat within cremation furnaces, including fuel feeding, flame shaping, temperature regulation and exhaust-gas handling to achieve efficient body incineration.
[F23G 2202/10] in two or more stages -- The subclass refers to cremation furnaces whose combustion is carried out in two or more distinct stages, such as separate heating zones or sequential temperature phases. This staged approach can improve thermal efficiency, control emissions, or provide better material handling during the cremation process.
[F23G 2202/101] with controlled oxidant supply -- This subgroup of the CPC pertains to cremation furnaces that incorporate a means of regulating the oxidant feed to the fire. The controlled supply may be achieved with valves, pressure regulators, or electronic feedback that adjusts oxygen flow, allowing precise temperature control and reduced emissions. It is used to improve efficiency and safety in combustion processes for body cremation.
[F23G 2202/102] with supplementary heating -- The classification F23G 2202/102 covers cremation furnaces that incorporate supplementary heating, meaning additional heat sources are used alongside the primary combustion to improve temperature control, reduce processing time, or ensure complete combustion of the material being cremated.
[F23G 2202/103] in separate chambers -- F23G 2202/103 designates cremation furnaces that use separate chambers for combustion, isolating the fire or heat source from the primary chamber holding the remains. The arrangement allows distinct control of temperature and airflow, improving efficiency and reducing emissions in cremation processes.
[F23G 2202/104] with ash melting stage -- The CPC subclass F23G 2202/104 covers cremation furnaces that incorporate an ash-melting stage. It addresses mechanical-engineering designs where the combustion process is followed by a phase that melts the resulting ash, improving residue handling and overall efficiency. This subclass falls under combustion apparatus within the broader mechanical-engineering category.
[F23G 2202/105] with waste supply in stages -- This CPC subclass covers cremation furnaces that introduce the material to be incinerated in multiple, sequential batches rather than all at once. Staged waste supply allows better temperature control, reduces emissions, and improves fuel efficiency during the cremation process. The code is used for designs where the feed mechanism supplies waste in distinct steps.
[F23G 2202/106] with recirculation of unburned solid or gaseous matter into combustion chamber -- The CPC subclass F23G 2202/106 pertains to cremation furnace designs in which unburned solid or gaseous matter is fed back into the combustion chamber, enhancing fuel utilization and reducing emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and specifically the combustion aspect of cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2202/20] to temperatures melting waste -- F23G 2202/20 pertains to cremation furnace technology designed to raise waste to its melting temperature for disposal or material recovery. The classification includes heating systems, temperature control, and furnace structures used in waste-melting processes.
[F23G 2202/30] in a pressurised chamber -- This subclass defines cremation furnaces that burn material in a sealed, pressurised chamber, using the internal pressure to enhance combustion efficiency and control emissions. The design typically incorporates pressure-tight vessels and regulated airflow to achieve higher temperatures and more complete oxidation during the cremation process.
[F23G 2202/40] in a pulsed combustion chamber -- The code covers the design and operation of pulsed-combustion chambers employed in cremation furnaces. It addresses the periodic injection of fuel or oxidizer to create short, high-intensity combustion bursts that improve thermal efficiency and control emissions within the furnace.
[F23G 2202/50] in a matrix bed combustion chamber -- The code F23G 2202/50 covers combustion that takes place within a matrix-bed chamber, where a solid matrix (such as refractory bricks or packed particles) provides a structured bed for the fuel to burn. It relates to the design and operation of cremation furnaces and similar devices that use the matrix to improve heat transfer, control flame spread and maintain stable temperatures during the combustion process.
[F23G 2202/60] in a catalytic combustion chamber -- F23G 2202/60 refers to combustion in a catalytic combustion chamber. It falls under cremation furnaces and combustion apparatus. This code is part of mechanical engineering.
[F23G 2202/70] with application of specific energy -- F23G 2202/70 defines cremation furnaces in which the combustion process is driven by a specific form of energy, such as electricity, gas, or another designated source. The classification covers furnace designs and operating methods that apply that particular energy to achieve the cremation function.
[F23G 2202/701] Electrical fields -- F23G 2202/701 covers the application of electrical fields in cremation furnaces, detailing how electric fields are employed to influence combustion processes, flame stability, or material treatment within these specialized combustion apparatuses. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the combustion apparatus subgroup for cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2202/703] Acoustic energy -- F23G 2202/703 pertains to the application of acoustic energy in cremation furnaces, such as using sound waves to assist fuel combustion, heat distribution, or emissions control. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and specifically cremation furnace technology within the CPC.
[F23G 2203/00] Furnace arrangements -- The CPC subclass F23G 2203/00 falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → cremation furnaces and concerns the specific arrangement of a cremation furnace, including the layout of heating elements, fuel and air supply channels, temperature-control devices and structural components that together define the furnace's overall configuration.
[F23G 2203/10] Stoker grate furnace -- A stoker grate furnace is a cremation furnace in which solid fuel is supplied onto a moving grate that combusts under regulated airflow, delivering consistent high temperature for the incineration of remains. The grate arrangement allows continuous feeding and removal of ash, improving efficiency and heat distribution within the furnace.
[F23G 2203/101] with stepped or inclined grate -- F23G 2203/101 covers furnace arrangements for cremation furnaces that incorporate a stepped or inclined grate. The grate design allows ash and residues to move downward by gravity, improving combustion efficiency and facilitating removal of material during the cremation process. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2203/103] with roller grate -- F23G 2203/103 covers cremation furnace arrangements that incorporate a roller grate, a moving grate system that transports material through the combustion zone. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering designs where the roller grate is integral to the furnace's operation.
[F23G 2203/105] with endless chain or travelling grate -- The subclass F23G 2203/105 pertains to furnace arrangements in cremation furnaces that use an endless chain or travelling grate to convey material through the combustion zone, enabling continuous movement and more uniform heat distribution.
[F23G 2203/107] with vibrating grate -- The code F23G 2203/107 designates cremation furnace arrangements that incorporate a vibrating grate to convey solid material, such as ash or remains, through the combustion zone. The vibrating grate improves material handling, promotes uniform heating and enhances fuel efficiency.
[F23G 2203/20] Rotary drum furnace -- F23G 2203/20 covers rotary drum furnaces, a cremation furnace arrangement in which a cylindrical drum rotates while heated, continuously mixing the material to ensure uniform combustion and efficient heat transfer.
[F23G 2203/201] using oscillating movement -- The subclass F23G 2203/201 covers cremation furnace arrangements that incorporate an oscillating movement of the furnace or its components, such as the chamber, hearth or feed mechanism, to enhance the combustion process, improve heat distribution, or facilitate the handling of cremated material. This oscillation may be mechanical, pneumatic or otherwise driven to achieve periodic motion within the furnace system.
[F23G 2203/202] rotating around substantially vertical axis -- F23G 2203/202 covers furnace arrangements in which the combustion chamber or the whole furnace rotates about a substantially vertical axis, a design used in cremation ovens to enhance mixing of gases and heat distribution by means of vertical-axis rotation.
[F23G 2203/203] with conically shaped drum -- This CPC subclass addresses cremation furnaces whose arrangement includes a conically-shaped drum that holds and moves the material through the combustion chamber, enhancing material flow and heat distribution. The design is intended to improve the efficiency and completeness of the incineration process.
[F23G 2203/204] having non-circular inner cross-section -- The subclass F23G 2203/204 pertains to cremation furnace designs where the chamber's interior cross-section is not circular, covering arrangements such as rectangular, elliptical or other polygonal shapes that influence heat distribution, material flow and structural support within the furnace.
[F23G 2203/205] with water-cooled wall -- This subclass covers cremation furnaces that incorporate a water-cooled wall to manage heat and protect the furnace structure. The water-cooled wall is designed to remove excess heat, improve safety, and enhance durability in combustion apparatus used for cremation.
[F23G 2203/206] with charging ports in the sidewall -- This CPC subgroup concerns cremation furnace arrangements that provide one or more charging ports built into the sidewall of the furnace, enabling lateral introduction of material rather than top- or bottom-loading.
[F23G 2203/207] with air supply ports in the sidewall -- F23G 2203/207 classifies cremation furnace arrangements that incorporate air supply ports positioned in the sidewall of the furnace, a specific feature of combustion apparatus within mechanical engineering.
[F23G 2203/208] with interior agitating members -- F23G 2203/208 pertains to cremation furnace arrangements that include interior agitating members, such as rods, paddles or other moving elements, to stir the material during combustion, thereby enhancing heat distribution and facilitating ash removal.
[F23G 2203/209] with variable inclination of rotation axis -- F23G 2203/209 covers cremation furnace designs in which the rotation axis can be adjusted to different inclinations during operation. The variable inclination allows control of fuel exposure, temperature distribution and material handling inside the furnace. It falls under furnace arrangements within combustion apparatus of mechanical engineering.
[F23G 2203/21] with variable speed of rotation -- F23G 2203/21 pertains to cremation furnace arrangements in which the rotating element can be operated at variable speeds, allowing adjustment of the burning process within combustion apparatus.
[F23G 2203/211] Arrangement of a plurality of drums -- The entry covers furnace arrangements in cremation equipment where several drums are positioned together to achieve uniform heating, material handling or flow control. It defines the spatial configuration, support and interconnection of multiple rotating drums within the combustion apparatus.
[F23G 2203/212] Sealing arrangements between rotary and stationary parts -- Sealing arrangements between rotary and stationary parts in cremation furnaces provide a barrier that prevents the escape of gases, fumes and heat while allowing relative motion. They typically employ flexible gaskets, lip seals, or labyrinth designs that maintain integrity under high temperatures and corrosive environments, ensuring safe and efficient furnace operation.
[F23G 2203/30] Cyclonic combustion furnace -- A cyclonic combustion furnace is a type of cremation furnace that uses a swirling airflow to enhance fuel mixing and heat transfer, allowing efficient and uniform burning of materials. The cyclonic motion creates a vortex that stabilizes the flame and reduces emissions, and the design is applied in mechanical engineering for high-temperature waste or body incineration.
[F23G 2203/40] Stationary bed furnace -- F23G 2203/40 identifies a stationary-bed furnace used in cremation apparatus, where the material rests on a fixed bed while being incinerated in a combustion chamber. The classification resides under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, and furnace arrangements.
[F23G 2203/401] with support for a grate or perforated plate -- F23G 2203/401 classifies furnace arrangements in cremation apparatus that incorporate a support for a grate or perforated plate, allowing material to be positioned above the combustion zone for efficient burning. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2203/403] with substantial cylindrical combustion chamber -- A cremation furnace arrangement with a substantially cylindrical combustion chamber, classified under mechanical engineering for combustion apparatus. The cylindrical design promotes even heat distribution and efficient fuel use in the cremation process.
[F23G 2203/50] Fluidised bed furnace -- F23G 2203/50 designates fluidised bed furnace arrangements used in cremation apparatus, where a bed of solid particles is aerated to suspend the material, enabling efficient combustion of remains.
[F23G 2203/501] with external recirculation of entrained bed material -- The subclass F23G 2203/501 covers cremation furnaces in which solid particles or bed material that become entrained in the combustion gases are removed from the furnace and returned to the combustion zone by an external recirculation system, improving efficiency and temperature control.
[F23G 2203/502] with recirculation of bed material inside combustion chamber -- The entry covers furnace arrangements for cremation furnaces that incorporate recirculation of the bed material within the combustion chamber, a design that helps maintain temperature uniformity and improve fuel efficiency by re-introducing spent material for further combustion.
[F23G 2203/503] with two or more fluidised beds -- This subclass covers cremation furnace designs that employ two or more fluidised beds to improve heat distribution and fuel-combustion efficiency, falling under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and furnace arrangements for cremation.
[F23G 2203/504] with essentially horizontal flow of bed material -- The subclass F23G 2203/504 relates to cremation furnace arrangements where the particulate or solid bed material flows essentially horizontally through the combustion zone, enabling uniform heat transfer and efficient handling of the material during the cremation process.
[F23G 2203/505] with fluidised bed rotated as a whole -- F23G 2203/505 designates cremation furnace arrangements in which the fluidised bed is rotated as a single unit. The rotating bed improves mixing of the waste material and combustion gases, facilitating uniform incineration and efficient ash removal. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2203/60] Mobile furnace -- A mobile furnace is a transportable heating unit designed to perform combustion processes such as cremation while being relocatable or mounted on a vehicle. It typically incorporates compact furnace arrangements that allow operation at required temperatures without permanent site installation.
[F23G 2203/601] carried by a vehicle -- The CPC subclass F23G 2203/601 concerns arrangements of cremation furnaces that are mounted on or integrated into a vehicle, allowing the furnace to be transported and operated while on-board. It falls under mechanical engineering and combustion apparatus categories, covering mobile cremation units for field or emergency use.
[F23G 2203/70] Modular furnace -- A modular furnace is a cremation furnace built from interchangeable sections that can be assembled in various configurations to suit different capacities and site constraints. Its design facilitates easier installation, maintenance, and relocation while ensuring the controlled combustion of cadaveric material within a sealed chamber.
[F23G 2203/80] Furnaces with other means for moving the waste through the combustion zone -- The code designates cremation furnaces that use alternative mechanisms - such as conveyors, screw feeds or pneumatic systems - to move waste through the combustion zone rather than traditional loading methods. It belongs to the mechanical engineering hierarchy under combustion apparatus and cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2203/801] using conveyors -- This subclass covers the use of conveyor systems to transport bodies, ash, or other materials within cremation furnace installations. It includes designs such as belt, chain, or screw conveyors integrated into the furnace layout for efficient loading, unloading, or moving of residues. The focus is on mechanical handling methods employed in cremation apparatus.
[F23G 2203/8013] Screw conveyors -- F23G 2203/8013 covers screw conveyors employed in cremation furnace arrangements, facilitating the transport of ash or other solid residues within mechanical engineering combustion apparatus. The classification pertains to the specific design and integration of helical screw elements for moving material in firebox or exhaust systems.
[F23G 2203/8016] Belt conveyors -- Belt conveyors in this subclass are employed within cremation furnace arrangements to move material such as ash, remains, or fuel through or between furnace sections, facilitating continuous processing and handling in combustion apparatus.
[F23G 2203/803] Rams or pushers -- Rams or pushers are mechanical devices installed in cremation furnaces to move, stir, or spread cremated material within the chamber. They help ensure uniform combustion and efficient processing of the remains by applying force or pressure to the material.
[F23G 2203/805] using a rotating hearth -- The subclass deals with cremation furnace arrangements that employ a rotating-hearth to improve heat uniformity, allow continuous loading and discharge of material, and enhance combustion efficiency. It includes designs where the hearth turns about a vertical axis or is driven by mechanical means to move the load through the fire.
[F23G 2204/00] Supplementary heating arrangements -- F23G 2204/00 relates to supplementary heating arrangements for cremation furnaces. It includes devices or systems that add heat beyond the main combustion zone, such as secondary burners, after-burners, or auxiliary heaters, to improve temperature control, fuel use, or processing speed.
[F23G 2204/10] using auxiliary fuel -- F23G 2204/10 pertains to cremation furnaces that incorporate a supplementary heating arrangement by burning an auxiliary fuel to raise or sustain the furnace temperature. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and specifically addresses the use of additional fuel sources in cremation processes.
[F23G 2204/101] solid fuel -- The code F23G 2204/101 designates solid fuels such as coal, wood or biomass that are employed as supplementary heating in cremation furnaces, augmenting the primary heat source to achieve the required temperature for cremation. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and specifically cremation furnace technology.
[F23G 2204/103] gaseous or liquid fuel -- This CPC subgroup covers cremation furnace designs that use an auxiliary heating system powered by gaseous or liquid fuels. It includes burners, fuel delivery and control mechanisms that supplement the primary heat source to achieve required temperatures for cremation.
[F23G 2204/20] using electric energy -- The code F23G 2204/20 covers the use of electric energy as a supplementary heating method in cremation furnaces, which are specialized combustion apparatuses within the broader field of mechanical engineering. This includes any electric-driven heating elements or power supplies that augment the primary heat source for the furnace.
[F23G 2204/201] Plasma -- The CPC subclass F23G 2204/201 covers the use of plasma as a supplementary heating method in cremation furnaces. It pertains to devices that generate plasma arcs or jets to raise temperature and improve combustion efficiency during the cremation process. The classification is part of the mechanical engineering section for combustion apparatus.
[F23G 2204/202] Laser -- The code covers the use of laser devices as an auxiliary heat source in cremation furnaces. It includes methods where laser radiation supplements conventional combustion to improve temperature control, fuel efficiency or residue reduction.
[F23G 2204/203] Microwave -- The entry covers the use of microwave energy as an auxiliary heating method in cremation furnaces, providing additional heat to support the combustion process.
[F23G 2204/204] Induction -- F23G 2204/204 pertains to the use of induction heating as a supplementary heating arrangement in cremation furnaces. It covers devices that generate heat by electromagnetic induction to boost the temperature of the primary combustion zone, improving fuel efficiency and reducing emissions in the cremation process.
[F23G 2205/00] Waste feed arrangements -- F23G 2205/00 covers the design and operation of waste-feed systems for cremation furnaces, including mechanisms for introducing bodies, containers or other refuse into the combustion chamber, methods of controlling feed rate, positioning, and securing material to ensure efficient, safe incineration.
[F23G 2205/10] using ram or pusher -- F23G 2205/10 covers waste-feed arrangements for cremation furnaces that use a ram or pusher to move material into the combustion chamber. It classifies mechanical systems employing linear or reciprocating motion to load waste for incineration.
[F23G 2205/101] sequentially operated -- F23G 2205/101 refers to cremation-furnace waste-feed arrangements that are sequentially operated, meaning the feeding devices introduce material into the furnace one after another rather than simultaneously. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2205/12] using conveyors -- F23G 2205/12 is a subclass of the Cremation Furnaces group that deals specifically with waste-feed arrangements employing conveyors to transport material into the furnace, a feature used in the design and operation of combustion apparatus within mechanical engineering.
[F23G 2205/121] Screw conveyor -- F23G 2205/121 designates screw conveyors that feed waste into cremation furnaces. These devices move solid waste along a helical screw within a tube, providing controlled delivery of material for combustion. The class is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and cremation furnace waste-feed arrangements.
[F23G 2205/122] Belt conveyor -- F23G 2205/122 designates belt-conveyor systems employed in the waste-feed arrangements of cremation furnaces, a subcategory of combustion apparatus within mechanical engineering. It covers conveyors that move solid residues, ash or other feedstock into, through, or out of the furnace during operation.
[F23G 2205/123] Roller conveyor -- F23G 2205/123 designates a roller conveyor used to transport waste material within cremation furnace feed systems. The conveyor comprises a series of rollers that support and move solid waste toward the furnace combustion zone, facilitating continuous feeding and handling. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, waste feed arrangements.
[F23G 2205/124] Chain conveyor -- A chain conveyor is a mechanical device that transports solid waste on a continuous loop of linked plates into a cremation furnace as part of the waste-feed arrangement. It is driven by a motor and guides material from the loading point to the combustion zone, ensuring steady feed for efficient cremation.
[F23G 2205/125] Vibrating conveyor -- A vibrating conveyor is a mechanical feeding device that moves bulk waste by rapid oscillatory motion, guiding it along a set path toward a cremation furnace. It provides continuous, controlled delivery of ash or other feedstock into the combustion chamber, and is classified under CPC F23G 2205/125 within mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, waste feed arrangements.
[F23G 2205/14] using hopper or bin -- The entry covers cremation furnace designs that introduce waste material through a hopper or bin, providing a controlled and continuous feed into the combustion chamber. It includes features for dispensing, level control and shielding the furnace from irregular loading.
[F23G 2205/16] using chute -- The subcategory defines the use of a chute to introduce waste material into a cremation furnace, detailing the chute's design, positioning and operation for delivering feed to the combustion zone. It falls under waste-feed arrangements for cremation furnaces within the mechanical-engineering classification for combustion apparatus.
[F23G 2205/18] using airlock systems -- F23G 2205/18 concerns waste-feed arrangements for cremation furnaces that use airlock systems to control the introduction of material while limiting oxygen ingress, improving combustion efficiency and safety. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2205/20] using airblast or pneumatic feeding -- This subclass deals with cremation furnace waste-feed arrangements that use an airblast or pneumatic system to transport material into the combustion chamber. The air-blast stream entrains solid or particulate waste, providing continuous, hands-free feeding and better feed uniformity.
[F23G 2206/00] Waste heat recuperation -- F23G 2206/00 pertains to waste-heat recuperation systems that capture heat from the exhaust of cremation furnaces and reuse it for other purposes, improving overall energy efficiency of the combustion apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within the category of combustion apparatus and cremation furnace technologies.
[F23G 2206/10] reintroducing the heat in the same process, e.g. for predrying -- The code F23G 2206/10 designates technologies that capture heat produced in a cremation furnace and feed it back into the same process, for example to pre-dry the material before combustion. It is a subcategory of waste-heat recuperation within cremation furnace devices under mechanical engineering.
[F23G 2206/20] using the heat in association with another installation
[F23G 2206/201] with an industrial furnace -- Cremation furnaces that integrate an industrial furnace for waste-heat recuperation, capturing heat generated during cremation and re-using it to power auxiliary processes or preheat combustion air.
[F23G 2206/202] with an internal combustion engine -- The F23G 2206/202 class covers cremation furnaces that incorporate waste-heat recuperation using an internal combustion engine. It belongs to the mechanical engineering section, specifically the combustion apparatus subgroup for cremation furnaces, and details designs where the engine drives a heat-exchange system to improve energy efficiency.
[F23G 2206/203] with a power/heat generating installation -- This subclass designates cremation furnace designs that integrate a power or heat-generating unit to recover waste heat from the combustion process, allowing the furnace to supply usable energy or heat elsewhere. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, waste-heat recuperation.
[F23G 2207/00] Control -- The F23G 2207/00 entry pertains to control systems for cremation furnaces, covering devices and methods that regulate temperature, combustion air, fuel feed and other operating parameters. [F23G 2209/00] Specific waste -- F23G 2209/00 refers to cremation furnaces for specific waste. This code is used for classification purposes in mechanical engineering. It falls under combustion apparatus and cremation furnaces.

[F23G 2209/142] Halogen gases, e.g. silane -- The code F23G 2209/142 classifies technologies for managing halogen gases, such as silane, as a specific waste stream in cremation furnace operations. It covers handling, treatment or disposal methods for these gases within combustion apparatuses.

[F23G 2209/28] Plastics or rubber like materials
[F23G 2209/281] Tyres -- The CPC subclass F23G 2209/281 covers the use of tyres as a specific waste feedstock in cremation furnaces, detailing how tyre material is introduced, combusted, or otherwise processed within these combustion apparatuses under the broader mechanical-engineering domain.
[F23G 2209/30] Solid combustion residues, e.g. bottom or flyash -- The CPC subclass F23G 2209/30 covers solid combustion residues generated in cremation furnaces, such as bottom ash and fly ash produced during the incineration of human remains. [F23G 2900/00] Special features of, or arrangements for incinerators -- F23G 2900/00 classifies special features or arrangements for incinerators, including feeding mechanisms, temperature control, exhaust treatment, and structural adaptations that enhance performance, safety or efficiency of combustion apparatus used in cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2900/00001] Exhaust gas recirculation -- This CPC subclass covers systems that redirect a portion of exhaust gases back into the combustion chamber of a cremation furnace or incinerator to improve temperature control, reduce emissions, and enhance fuel efficiency. It pertains to mechanical engineering designs for exhaust gas recirculation in combustion apparatus.
[F23G 2900/50001] Combination of two or more furnaces -- F23G 2900/50001 covers arrangements that combine two or more cremation furnaces into a single system, enabling simultaneous or sequential operation for improved efficiency or capacity. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, and addresses special features of incinerators.
[F23G 2900/50002] Burning with downwards directed draft through the waste mass -- The CPC subclass F23G 2900/50002 refers to cremation furnaces that burn waste with a draft directed downwards through the material. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and special arrangements for incinerators, describing a specific airflow design that improves combustion efficiency.
[F23G 2900/50003] Waste oxidation, pyrolysis or gasification in water under supercritical conditions -- The code F23G 2900/50003 classifies processes that treat waste by oxidation, pyrolysis or gasification in water at supercritical temperature and pressure, a method used for highly efficient incineration or cremation. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, and addresses special features or arrangements for incinerators.
[F23G 2900/50004] Furnace with inclined hearth -- A furnace with an inclined hearth is a combustion apparatus designed for cremation or other high-temperature processes where the floor slopes downward to facilitate the movement of ash and residue toward an extraction point, improving material flow and cleaning efficiency.
[F23G 2900/50005] Waste in combustion chamber supported on bed made of special materials -- The classification covers cremation furnaces in which waste placed in the combustion chamber rests on a bed formed from specially selected materials that support the waste, improve heat distribution, and resist corrosion or degradation during incineration. It applies to arrangements that employ such engineered beds to enhance the efficiency and durability of the combustion process.
[F23G 2900/50006] Combustion chamber walls reflecting radiant energy within the chamber -- The subclass addresses combustion chambers whose interior surfaces are designed to reflect radiant energy back into the fire zone, enhancing heat utilization. It is applied primarily to cremation furnaces and other incinerators where efficient thermal transfer is required.
[F23G 2900/50007] Co-combustion of two or more kinds of waste, separately fed into the furnace -- The CPC entry F23G 2900/50007 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Cremation Furnaces, covering special features or arrangements for incinerators. It relates to the co-combustion of two or more kinds of waste that are fed separately into a furnace. This subclass details methods for handling mixed waste streams in cremation or incineration installations.
[F23G 2900/50008] Combustion of waste suspended or lifted by upward gas flows
[F23G 2900/50009] Furnace with progressive waste movements in vertical or steeply inclined direction -- A cremation furnace that transports waste progressively in a vertical or steeply inclined path, enabling continuous feeding and combustion. This design is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and cremation furnaces for incinerators. It corresponds to CPC entry F23G 2900/50009.
[F23G 2900/50201] Waste pyrolysis, gasification or cracking by indirect heat transfer -- The code F23G 2900/50201 designates a feature of cremation-type combustion apparatus that uses indirect heat transfer to pyrolyse, gasify or crack waste materials. It covers systems where heat is supplied to the waste without direct flame contact, enabling controlled thermal conversion for energy recovery or disposal.
[F23G 2900/50202] Waste pyrolysis, gasification or cracking in presence of catalysts -- A subclass of mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus covering cremation-furnace type incinerators, this code addresses waste treatment methods that employ catalysts to pyrolyze, gasify or crack material. It specifies catalytic processes for converting solid waste into gases or smaller molecules within high-temperature furnace environments.
[F23G 2900/50203] Waste pyrolysis, gasification or cracking in a mechanically fluidised bed, e.g. obtained by a centrifugal force -- This code covers waste treatment by pyrolysis, gasification or cracking carried out in a mechanically fluidised bed, for example one generated by centrifugal force, as part of incinerator or cremation furnace systems. The method mechanically agitates the waste to enhance thermal conversion into gases, oils or char.
[F23G 2900/50204] Waste pre-treatment by pyrolysis, gasification or cracking -- The code F23G 2900/50204 covers methods for pre-treating waste in incinerator-type combustion systems using thermal conversion processes such as pyrolysis, gasification or cracking to break down material before final combustion. It pertains to cremation furnace designs or related incinerator arrangements that incorporate these waste-reduction stages as part of their operation.
[F23G 2900/50205] Waste pre-treatment by pyrolysis, gasification or cracking followed by condensation of gas into combustible oil or fat -- The code denotes a waste-pre-treatment method in which material is first subjected to pyrolysis, gasification or cracking, and the resulting gases are then cooled and condensed to form a combustible oil or fat. It applies to incinerator-related equipment that converts waste into liquid fuel.
[F23G 2900/50206] Pelletising waste before combustion -- The CPC subclass F23G 2900/50206 covers methods and apparatus for pelletising waste prior to its combustion in incinerators, focusing on the preparation of fuel particles that improve handling and burn efficiency. It is positioned under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and cremation furnaces.
[F23G 2900/50207] Thermoforming of plastic waste materials before combustion -- The entry covers a method that heats plastic waste and reshapes it-typically into sheets or other uniform forms-before it is introduced into a cremation furnace or other incinerator, improving combustion efficiency and handling of the material.
[F23G 2900/50208] Biologic treatment before burning, e.g. biogas generation -- The code F23G 2900/50208 refers to combustion apparatus that incorporate a prior biological treatment step, such as anaerobic digestion, to convert waste into biogas before incineration. This arrangement uses the generated gas as fuel for the furnace, improving energy efficiency and reducing emissions.
[F23G 2900/50209] Compacting waste before burning -- The code refers to devices that compact waste before it is burned in cremation or incineration furnaces, enhancing handling and fuel efficiency. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for combustion apparatus and addresses special features or arrangements for incinerators.
[F23G 2900/50211] Evaporating, e.g. liquid waste before burning -- The CPC subclass F23G 2900/50211 pertains to techniques that evaporate or pre-dry liquid waste before it is introduced into a combustion chamber of a cremation furnace. It covers arrangements for removing moisture from waste to improve incineration efficiency and reduce emissions.
[F23G 2900/50212] Extruding waste before combustion -- F23G 2900/50212 relates to devices or processes that extrude solid waste to form a continuous strand or shape before it is introduced into a combustion or cremation furnace. The extrusion step improves handling, feeding and combustion efficiency of the waste. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and cremation furnaces for special incinerator arrangements.
[F23G 2900/50213] Preheating processes other than drying or pyrolysis -- This classification concerns methods used in cremation or incineration equipment to raise the temperature of waste or bodies before the main combustion stage, excluding drying or pyrolysis steps. It covers auxiliary heating arrangements such as pre-heaters, heat exchangers or auxiliary burners that supply sensible heat to the load prior to ignition. It is part of the CPC subgroup for special features of incinerators.
[F23G 2900/50214] Separating non combustible matters -- The F23G 2900/50214 subclass deals with features of cremation or incinerator systems that separate non-combustible materials from the fuel or waste, such as ash removal, screening or sorting mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, cremation furnaces, and specifically addresses special arrangements for handling non-combustible residues.
[F23G 2900/50401] Drying waste by mixing with drying chemicals, e.g. with CaO -- This subclass covers techniques for removing moisture from waste within cremation or incineration furnaces by adding chemical drying agents such as calcium oxide. The method involves mixing the waste with the drying chemical to accelerate dehydration before or during combustion. It is applied to improve the efficiency of incinerators handling wet waste.
[F23G 2900/508] Providing additional energy for combustion, e.g. by using supplementary heating -- F23G 2900/508 concerns techniques for supplying extra energy to a combustion process, such as using auxiliary burners, external heat sources or supplementary heating systems, to raise the temperature or improve efficiency of cremation furnaces and other incinerators.
[F23G 2900/50801] using the heat from externally heated bodies, e.g. steel balls -- The CPC subclass F23G 2900/50801 covers cremation furnaces that utilize heat transferred from externally heated bodies, such as steel balls, to assist the incineration process. It addresses arrangements that recover thermal energy from these heated media and apply it to the combustion chamber, improving efficiency of the cremation apparatus.
[F23G 2900/50802] using solid propellant -- The CPC class F23G 2900/50802 covers methods or apparatus that employ solid propellant in cremation furnaces or other incinerators, situated within the mechanical-engineering combustion-apparatus hierarchy. It addresses special features or arrangements for using solid fuel in such devices.
[F23G 2900/50803] using solar energy
[F23G 2900/50804] using thermit or other compositions of metal oxides as auxiliary fuel -- The classification F23G 2900/50804 covers cremation furnaces that employ thermit or other metal-oxide mixtures as an auxiliary fuel. It pertains to special features or arrangements for incinerators within the broader category of combustion apparatus in mechanical engineering, specifying the use of these exothermic chemical reactions to supplement the primary heat source.
[F23G 2900/51001] using arc discharge electrodes to provide heat -- The CPC entry F23G 2900/51001 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and specifically cremation furnaces, and concerns special features or arrangements for incinerators. It covers the use of arc-discharge electrodes as a heat source in such furnaces, providing an electrically generated plasma or arc to achieve the high temperatures required for cremation.
[F23G 2900/52001] Rotary drums with co-current flows of waste and gas -- The entry refers to rotary drum incinerators in which waste and gas travel together in the same direction through the drum, a design used for efficient combustion in cremation furnaces. This configuration falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy for combustion apparatus and specifically addresses special features of incinerator arrangements.
[F23G 2900/52002] Rotary drum furnaces with counter-current flows of waste and gas -- A rotary drum furnace in which solid waste travels through the drum in the opposite direction to the circulating combustion gases, allowing efficient heat exchange and thorough incineration; such counter-current designs are applied to cremation and other high-temperature waste-destruction processes.
[F23G 2900/52003] Rotary drum furnaces with foramenous drum walls, e.g. grate drums -- Rotary drum furnaces with porous drum walls, also known as grate drums, are used in cremation equipment to improve combustion by letting gases pass through the rotating drum. The design enhances heat distribution and ash removal in combustion apparatus.
[F23G 2900/53801] Multi-hearth furnaces with vertical axis -- The code F23G 2900/53801 identifies multi-hearth furnaces that rotate around a vertical axis and are used for high-temperature incineration such as cremation. The design features a series of stacked hearths through which material descends, allowing continuous combustion and efficient heat transfer.
[F23G 2900/54001] Hearths or supports movable into and from the furnace, e.g. by a conveyor -- The CPC entry F23G 2900/54001 covers movable hearths or supporting structures that can be transferred into and out of a cremation furnace, typically using a conveyor system, enabling easy loading and unloading of the combustion chamber. It pertains to mechanical engineering solutions for handling incinerator components.
