[F23H] GRATES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All details of combustion grates, including all relevant accessories and peripheral devices such as driving mechanisms or cooling arrangements for example, independently from the type of solid fuel which is supported and burned on the grate.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Inlets for fluidisation air for fluidised bed combustion apparatus -> F23C10/20  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The term "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Ash: means any solid combustion residues, for example remaining in the fuel bed or suspended in the flue gases - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame - Combustion: means the direct combination of oxygen gas, e.g. in air and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of the apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Flue gases: any gaseous products of combustion - Grate: a perforated surface, e.g. a grid which supports or delimits a bed of burning fuel and serves to supply primary air
[F23H 1/00] Grates with solid bars -- F23H 1/00 classifies grates that are made of solid bars for use in combustion apparatus such as furnaces or boilers. The code covers grate designs that support solid fuel while permitting airflow and ash removal, and it resides within the mechanical engineering hierarchy for combustion apparatus.
[F23H 1/02] having provision for air supply or air preheating, e.g. air-supply or blast fittings which form a part of the grate structure or serve as supports -- F23H 1/02 covers grates equipped with built-in air-supply or air-preheating features, such as blast fittings that are integral to the grate framework or serve as supporting elements, enabling controlled airflow to the combustion zone.
[F23H 1/04] having a variable burning surface -- F23H 1/04 refers to solid-bar grates used in combustion apparatus where the burning surface can be altered, typically by moving or reshaping the bars to vary the area exposed to flame, allowing control of heat output.
[F23H 1/06] having bars at different levels -- This subclass covers grates that consist of solid-bars positioned at varying heights within the grate structure, allowing staggered airflow or fuel distribution in combustion devices. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, grates.
[F23H 1/08] Vertical grates -- F23H 1/08 relates to vertical grates made of solid bars that are used in combustion apparatus such as furnaces or boilers. The upright, bar-type construction provides a fixed support for fuel or ash while allowing airflow through the grate.
[F23H 11/00] Travelling-grates -- Travelling-grates are movable grate assemblies used in combustion apparatus to transport solid fuel through a furnace, providing continuous combustion and ash removal. The CPC code F23H 11/00 classifies devices where the grate moves longitudinally or transversely to feed material.
[F23H 11/02] with the bars disposed on transverse bearers -- The code F23H 11/02 classifies travelling-grates in combustion apparatus whose bars are mounted on transverse bearers, meaning the grate bars are supported by crosswise beams that span the grate width. It is used to identify designs where the bar arrangement is fixed to these transverse supports for improved stability and heat distribution.
[F23H 11/04] with the bars pivoted at one side -- This CPC subclass covers travelling-grate systems in which each bar is hinged or pivoted on one side, allowing the grate to move or fold as the bars advance through the combustion zone. It applies to mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus such as furnace or boiler grates.
[F23H 11/06] with the bars movable relatively to one another -- The CPC subclass F23H 11/06 covers travelling-grate combustion devices whose individual bars can shift relative to one another. Such movable-bar arrangements are employed to regulate fuel distribution, ash discharge and airflow through the grate, improving combustion control and efficiency.
[F23H 11/08] with several individually-movable grate surfaces -- F23H 11/08 covers combustion apparatus where the grate consists of several individually movable surfaces that can be adjusted or displaced relative to each other, allowing independent control of fuel flow or airflow across the grate. Such travelling-grate designs are used in furnaces, boilers and incinerators to improve combustion efficiency and handle varied fuel loads.
[F23H 11/10] with special provision for supply of air from below and for controlling air supply -- This subgroup deals with travelling-grate combustion units that include a provision for supplying air from below the grate and for controlling that airflow, enabling better fuel combustion and emission control.
[F23H 11/12] inclined travelling grates -- Inclined travelling grates are movable grate surfaces positioned on a slope that convey solid fuel or ash through a combustion chamber, allowing continuous feeding and discharge while maintaining controlled combustion conditions. The classification covers the grate structure, drive mechanisms, support arrangements and methods for removal of residue on such inclined, traveling platforms.
[F23H 11/14] serving as auxiliary grates -- The F23H 11/14 subclass covers travelling-grate combustion devices that incorporate additional, secondary grates used to support or supplement the primary moving grate. These auxiliary grates may be employed to enhance fuel distribution, improve ash removal, or provide extra structural support within the grate system.
[F23H 11/16] for multi-layer stoking -- F23H 11/16 covers travelling-grate burners in which the fuel is supplied to a grate composed of several stacked layers, allowing simultaneous combustion on multiple levels. It pertains to mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus where multi-layer stoking improves heat distribution and efficiency. This subclass refines the broader category of grates used in furnaces, stoves and similar devices.
[F23H 11/18] Details
[F23H 11/20] Driving-means -- The CPC entry F23H 11/20 covers the driving means for travelling-grates used in combustion apparatus. It includes mechanisms such as rollers, belts, chains, gears, motors, hydraulic or pneumatic drives that move the grate to transport solid fuel through a furnace or boiler. This classification falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Grates → Travelling-grates.
[F23H 11/22] Moving fuel along grate -- The CPC subclass F23H 11/22 covers mechanisms that move solid fuel along a travelling grate within a combustion apparatus. It pertains to the transport of fuel across the grate surface to ensure uniform burning and efficient heat release.
[F23H 11/24] Removal of ashes -- F23H 11/24 covers methods and devices for removing ash from travelling-grate combustion systems. It relates to mechanisms that convey, discharge, or otherwise extract ash buildup from the moving grate surface to maintain efficient operation. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, grates.
[F23H 11/26] by dumping -- F23H 11/26 covers travelling-grates that operate by a dumping action, where the grate sections tilt or shift to release ash, residue or fuel material from the combustion chamber. This mechanism is used in stoves, boilers or incinerators to facilitate continuous removal of spent grate material without stopping operation.
[F23H 11/28] Replaceable burning-surface -- F23H 11/28 pertains to replaceable burning surfaces incorporated in travelling-grates for combustion apparatus. It covers designs that allow the grate's heat-exposed lining or plates to be removed and renewed, facilitating maintenance, extending service life and improving efficiency.
[F23H 13/00] Grates not covered by any of groups -- F23H 13/00 designates grates used in combustion apparatus that do not fall within any of the more specific grate groups. These grates support solid fuel, allow combustion gases to pass, and are employed in furnaces, boilers or incinerators where a generic grate configuration is required.
[F23H 13/02] Basket grates, e.g. with shaking arrangement -- F23H 13/02 covers basket-type grates used in combustion apparatus, typically featuring a shaking or vibrating mechanism to aid the discharge of ash and unburned residues. These grates consist of a perforated container that holds fuel while allowing airflow, and the motion assists in maintaining efficient combustion and easy cleaning. It is classified under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Grates.
[F23H 13/04] Telescoping grates -- Telescoping grates are adjustable grate assemblies that can expand or contract to vary the grate opening, allowing control of fuel feed and airflow in combustion devices. They are used where grates need to accommodate different fuel sizes or adjust combustion rates.
[F23H 13/06] Dumping grates -- F23H 13/06 refers to dumping grates, a type of grate used in combustion apparatus that can be tilted, shifted or otherwise moved to discharge ash, slag or unburned material. The category covers any such grate that does not fall under more specific groups within the grates subclass.
[F23H 13/08] Grates specially adapted for gas generators and also applicable to furnaces -- Grates specially adapted for gas generators and also applicable to furnaces are grate designs optimized for the intense heat and airflow conditions of gas-generating units, such as circulating fluidized beds, while also being suitable for conventional furnace heating. They incorporate specific material, shape or perforation features that enhance combustion efficiency and durability compared with standard grates.
[F23H 15/00] Cleaning arrangements for grates -- The F23H 15/00 subgroup covers devices, systems and methods designed to clean grates in combustion equipment such as boilers, furnaces or burners. It includes mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic or other arrangements that remove ash, slag or other deposits to maintain efficient heat transfer and operation.
[F23H 17/00] Details of grates -- The F23H 17/00 subclass pertains to the specific design and construction details of grates used in combustion apparatus. It includes features such as grate material, shape, spacing, support structure, adjustability, and cleaning or maintenance mechanisms that affect fuel support and airflow.
[F23H 17/02] End fittings on bars -- F23H 17/02 pertains to end fittings on grate bars in combustion apparatus. It includes components such as brackets, clamps or seals that attach to the bar ends to secure, adjust or seal them within the grate structure.
[F23H 17/04] of travelling-grates -- F23H 17/04 covers travelling or moving grates used in combustion apparatus. The entry pertains to grates that advance longitudinally or reciprocally to transport solid fuel through a furnace, enhancing heat distribution and allowing continuous feeding. It includes designs, drive mechanisms and arrangements for such movable grate systems.
[F23H 17/06] Provision for vertical adjustment of grate -- F23H 17/06 covers devices that allow the grate in a combustion system to be raised or lowered to control fuel flow and air exposure. The vertical adjustment mechanism may be manual or automatic and is used to optimise combustion efficiency and heat output.
[F23H 17/08] Bearers -- The subclass F23H 17/08 covers the structural elements that support or hold a grate in a combustion apparatus, such as frames, brackets, support plates or other bearing components that secure the grate in position during operation.
[F23H 17/10] Dead plates -- Dead plates are fixed, non-moving components integrated into grates of combustion apparatus to support the grate structure, seal off sections, or protect the frame from direct fire exposure. They provide a durable surface that does not participate in material handling or air flow, helping to control combustion zones and improve durability of the grate system.
[F23H 17/12] Fire-bars -- F23H 17/12 - Fire-bars are metal grates placed in combustion chambers such as boilers, furnaces or stoves to support fuel while allowing air and combustion gases to flow through, thereby promoting efficient burning and heat transfer.
[F23H 2700/00] Grates characterised by special features or applications -- F23H 2700/00 covers grates that incorporate distinctive design elements or are intended for particular uses within combustion equipment. These grates may feature adjustable geometry, enhanced slag handling, or materials suited to extreme temperatures, and are used in furnaces, boilers and incinerators to improve fuel burning efficiency.
[F23H 2700/001] Grates specially adapted for steam boilers -- This subclass covers grates that are specially adapted for use in steam boilers, detailing designs and features that optimise combustion and heat transfer for steam generation. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the broader category of grates.
[F23H 2700/002] Inclined grates with longitudinally movable gratebars -- F23H 2700/002 designates inclined grates whose gratebars can be moved longitudinally, allowing the opening to be adjusted while the grate remains sloped. This feature is used in combustion apparatus to control airflow, improve fuel burning efficiency and facilitate ash removal.
[F23H 2700/003] Stepped grates with rotatable or slidable gratebars -- A stepped grate for combustion apparatus where each grate bar can be rotated or slid to adjust spacing or position, allowing controlled fuel flow and ash removal. The design improves combustion efficiency and facilitates cleaning by enabling selective movement of individual bars within the grate structure.
[F23H 2700/004] Rotary grates with horizontal axis -- A rotary grate whose axle runs horizontally, used in combustion apparatus to convey solid fuel across a burning zone. This type of grate is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, grates with special features or applications.
[F23H 2700/005] Rotary grates with vertical axis -- F23H 2700/005 refers to rotary grates that turn around a vertical axis, employed in combustion equipment to convey solid fuel or waste for burning. This configuration provides continuous feeding and uniform distribution, enhancing combustion efficiency and reducing residue buildup.
[F23H 2700/006] Grates of basket-type -- F23H 2700/006 identifies basket-type grates, which are removable frames or containers that hold solid fuel in combustion apparatus, allowing easy loading and cleaning while supporting airflow. These grates are a specific subclass of grates with special features used in mechanical engineering.
[F23H 2700/007] Basket grates with shaking arrangement -- Basket grates with shaking arrangement are grate structures used in combustion apparatus where the grate consists of a basket that can be periodically rocked or vibrated to promote fuel movement and ash removal. The shaking mechanism helps maintain efficient burning and reduces clinker formation. This feature is classified under CPC F23H 2700/007.
[F23H 2700/008] Mobile grates -- Mobile grates are movable grating structures used in combustion apparatus to support solid fuel or waste while permitting airflow. Their design allows relocation within a furnace or boiler for cleaning, maintenance, and adjustment of the combustion zone.
[F23H 2700/009] Grates specially adapted for incinerators -- Grates specially adapted for incinerators are designed to support the efficient combustion of waste in high-temperature incineration systems. Their construction and geometry allow steady material flow, promote thorough burning, and withstand corrosive gases and intense heat, making them essential components of combustion apparatus used in waste-to-energy and hazardous-material treatment plants.
[F23H 2900/00] Special features of combustion grates -- F23H 2900/00 covers inventions that incorporate special features of combustion grates used in heating or industrial burners. The classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and grates. It includes designs that improve heat distribution, grate adjustability, cleaning, or fuel handling.
[F23H 2900/03021] Liquid cooled grates -- Liquid-cooled grates are combustion grates equipped with a circulating liquid system that removes heat from the grate surface, stabilising temperature, protecting the grate material and reducing emissions. This design is used in boilers, furnaces or incinerators where precise thermal control and prolonged grate life are required.
[F23H 2900/09041] Vibrating grates for solid fuels -- A vibrating grate for solid fuels is a combustion grate that incorporates a mechanical vibration mechanism to enhance fuel feeding, distribution and combustion efficiency. The vibration reduces clogging and improves ash removal, allowing more uniform burning in boilers or stoves.
[F23H 2900/17001] Specific materials therefor -- F23H 2900/17001 covers patents that disclose the particular materials used in special-feature combustion grates, a detailed subcategory of grates employed in combustion apparatus within the broader field of mechanical engineering.
[F23H 2900/17002] Detachable or removable worn-out parts -- A detachable or removable worn-out part is a component of a combustion grate that can be taken out for replacement when it degrades, allowing maintenance without dismantling the entire grate assembly. This feature is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, grates, special features of combustion grates.
[F23H 3/00] Grates with hollow bars -- F23H 3/00 covers grates that use hollow bars in combustion apparatus such as boilers or furnaces. The hollow bars permit gases or air to flow through the grate, enhancing combustion efficiency, enabling cooling or cleaning, and improving fuel burn control.
[F23H 3/02] internally cooled -- A grate of the hollow-bar type that incorporates internal passages for circulating coolant, cooling the grate from within during combustion. The internal cooling controls grate temperature, enhances durability and efficiency, and helps maintain consistent combustion conditions.
[F23H 3/04] externally cooled, e.g. with water, steam or air -- F23H 3/04 classifies grates that have hollow bars and are cooled from the outside, typically by circulating water, steam or air. The cooling removes heat from the grate surface, improving temperature control and protecting the grate material during combustion processes. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and grates with hollow bars.
[F23H 5/00] Double grates -- A double grate is a pair of parallel grates positioned one behind the other within a combustion apparatus to improve fuel feeding, air distribution and ash removal. It is used in furnaces, boilers or stoves to enhance burning efficiency and maintain stable fire conditions.
[F23H 7/00] Inclined grates -- Inclined grates are sloped support structures in combustion apparatus that hold fuel or waste while allowing it to slide down by gravity as it burns. Their angle facilitates efficient air flow, fuel feeding, and ash removal, improving firebox performance in boilers, furnaces and similar devices.
[F23H 7/02] with fixed bars -- This CPC subclass covers inclined grates that incorporate permanently mounted bars. The fixed bars support solid fuel while the tilt facilitates drainage and airflow for efficient combustion and are commonly used in furnace, boiler or incinerator designs.
[F23H 7/04] in parallel disposition -- F23H 7/04 covers inclined grates in combustion devices that are arranged side-by-side in a parallel disposition, meaning multiple grate sections run parallel to each other rather than staggered or intersecting. This configuration facilitates uniform fuel loading and ash removal across the grate surface.
[F23H 7/06] with movable bars disposed parallel to direction of fuel feeding -- This subclass pertains to inclined grates used in combustion apparatus that feature movable bars aligned parallel to the direction of fuel feeding, enabling adjustment of grate spacing and facilitating controlled fuel flow across the grate surface. It is applied where bar movement is essential for regulating combustion and ash removal.
[F23H 7/08] reciprocating along their axes -- F23H 7/08 designates inclined grates in combustion apparatus that are capable of reciprocating along their own axes. The subgroup includes mechanisms where the grate slides or oscillates back-and-forth to aid fuel feeding, combustion control, or ash removal.
[F23H 7/10] rocking about their axes -- F23H 7/10 covers inclined grates for combustion apparatus that are designed to rock about their longitudinal or transverse axes during operation. The rocking motion helps distribute fuel, control airflow and improve combustion efficiency while reducing the risk of blockages on the grate surface.
[F23H 7/12] with movable bars disposed transversely to direction of fuel feeding -- This subclass pertains to inclined grates in combustion apparatus that feature movable bars arranged transversely to the direction of fuel feeding, enabling adjustment of fuel passage and combustion control.
[F23H 7/14] reciprocating along their axis -- F23H 7/14 concerns inclined grates that move back-and-forth along their own longitudinal axis. The classification covers mechanisms for reciprocating the grate to feed, discharge or stir solid fuel within combustion chambers, typically in boilers or furnaces.
[F23H 7/16] rocking about their axes -- Inclined grates that rotate or swing about their own axes within combustion apparatus are classified under CPC code F23H 7/16. This subclass covers designs where the grate motion assists fuel feeding, ash removal or airflow regulation in mechanical-engineering fire-box systems.
[F23H 7/18] reciprocating in an upward direction -- F23H 7/18 covers inclined grates in combustion apparatus that are driven to move reciprocally upward, typically to discharge ash or fuel residues. The upward motion helps clear the grate surface and maintain efficient burning.
[F23H 9/00] Revolving-grates -- Revolving-grates are rotating grate assemblies used in combustion apparatus to continuously turn fuel or waste material, promoting even burning and preventing blockage. They are commonly employed in incinerators, boilers and similar equipment to improve heat distribution and combustion efficiency.
[F23H 9/02] Revolving cylindrical grates -- F23H 9/02 covers cylindrical grates that rotate, used in combustion devices to continuously present fresh material to the flame and discharge ash. The design typically involves a drum or barrel with perforations that turn by gravity, airflow or mechanical drive, improving fuel burning efficiency and cleaning.
[F23H 9/04] Grates rocked as a whole -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All grates somehow moved as a whole, thus including also vibrating grates.
[F23H 9/06] the bars being rocked about axes transverse to their lengths -- This subclass concerns revolving grates whose bars are rocked about axes that run across the bars rather than along them, allowing a rocking motion to vary fuel exposure. It applies to grate mechanisms used in combustion apparatus.
[F23H 9/08] the bars being rocked about their longitudinal axes -- This subgroup deals with grate designs in which each bar is made to oscillate or rock about its own longitudinal axis while the grate rotates, a motion that aids fuel mixing, ash discharge and heat transfer within combustion chambers.
[F23H 9/10] and modified to move fuel along the grate -- F23H 9/10 covers revolving-grates that are specially modified so the fuel is conveyed along the grate surface while it rotates. The feature typically involves mechanisms that advance the material longitudinally as part of a combustion apparatus.
[F23H 9/12] the bars being vertically movable in a plane -- F23H 9/12 covers revolving grates whose individual bars can be moved vertically while remaining in the same plane, allowing adjustments of grate spacing or cleaning during combustion. The feature is used in burners, boilers or incinerators to control fuel flow and heat transfer.
[F23J] REMOVAL OR TREATMENT OF COMBUSTION PRODUCTS OR COMBUSTION RESIDUES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion apparatus for burning smoke or fumes, e.g. exhaust gases -> F23G7/06  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The term "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Ash: means any solid combustion residues, for example remaining in the fuel bed or suspended in the flue gases - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame - Combustion: means the direct combination of oxygen gas, e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of the apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Flue gases: any gaseous products of combustion - Grate: a perforated surface, e.g. a grid, which supports or delimits a bed of burning fuel and serves to supply primary air
[F23J 1/00] Removing ash, clinker, or slag from combustion chambers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing solid particulate material, e.g. fly ash, from flue gases -> F23J15/022 - Devices for removal of material from the bed of fluidised bed combustion apparatus -> F23C10/24
[F23J 1/02] Apparatus for removing ash, clinker, or slag from ash-pits, e.g. by employing trucks or conveyors, by employing suction devices -- F23J 1/02 concerns equipment used in combustion systems to extract ash, clinker or slag from ash-pits. It includes transport solutions such as trucks, belt or screw conveyors and suction devices that move the solid residues out of the pit for disposal or further processing.
[F23J 1/04] Hand tools, e.g. rakes, prickers, tongs -- This subclass covers manually operated devices for extracting solid combustion residues such as ash, clinker or slag from burners and chambers, including rakes, prickers, tongs and similar hand tools used to reach, lift, or push out the material.
[F23J 1/06] Mechanically-operated devices, e.g. clinker pushers -- The subclass F23J 1/06 covers mechanically-operated devices for extracting ash, clinker or slag from combustion chambers, such as pushers that move clinker out of furnaces during operation.
[F23J 1/08] Liquid slag removal -- F23J 1/08 deals with techniques for extracting liquid slag from the chambers of combustion devices such as furnaces or boilers. It is part of mechanical engineering, under combustion apparatus and the treatment of combustion residues, and addresses the removal of ash, clinker or slag that solidifies in the combustion zone.
[F23J 11/00] Devices for conducting smoke or fumes, e.g. flues -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Chimneys -> E04H12/28 - Removing cooking fumes from domestic stoves or ranges -> F24C15/20 - Fume conduits of furnaces, kilns, ovens, or retorts -> F27D17/302
[F23J 11/02] for conducting smoke or fumes originating from various locations to the outside, e.g. in locomotive sheds, in garages -- The F23J 11/02 entry covers devices that channel smoke or fumes generated at various points to the external environment, such as ventilation systems used in locomotive sheds, garages, and similar facilities. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the removal or treatment of combustion products, specifically for conducting smoke or fumes.
[F23J 11/04] in locomotives -- F23J 11/04 covers flues, ducts or other devices for channeling smoke or fumes generated by combustion engines in locomotives. The classification is part of mechanical engineering, focusing on removal or treatment of combustion products. It applies to designs that direct exhaust gases away from crew compartments or improve emission control in railway vehicles.
[F23J 11/06] for conducting smoke horizontally -- The F23J 11/06 subclass pertains to devices that convey smoke or fumes in a horizontal direction, such as side-mounted flues, ducts or channels used in combustion apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering topics dealing with the removal or treatment of combustion products.
[F23J 11/08] for portable apparatus -- F23J 11/08 covers portable devices that conduct smoke or fumes away from combustion sources, such as handheld or mobile flues and similar apparatuses used to transport combustion gases for removal or treatment in temporary or movable settings.
[F23J 11/10] for tents -- F23J 11/10 covers smoke- or fume-conducting devices specifically designed for tents, such as flues, vents or chimneys that direct combustion gases away from the shelter. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and the broader category of removal or treatment of combustion products.
[F23J 11/12] Smoke conduit systems for factories or large buildings -- F23J 11/12 covers smoke conduit systems designed for factories or large buildings, including the networks of flues, ducts and related components that channel and remove smoke, fumes or combustion gases from industrial or commercial premises to improve safety and air quality.
[F23J 13/00] Fittings for chimneys or flues -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Side-supporting means for chimney stacks -> E04H12/20 - Ladders permanently attached to chimneys -> E06C9/00 - Draught-inducing apparatus associated with chimneys or flues -> F23L17/00 - Tops for chimneys, terminals for flues -> F23L17/02
[F23J 13/02] Linings -- The CPC code F23J 13/02 relates to linings that are incorporated inside chimneys or flues to protect the surrounding structure from high temperatures, corrosion, and deposition of combustion residues. It covers refractory, insulating or protective inner layers and related methods for applying such linings in combustion-product treatment systems.
[F23J 13/025] composed of concentric elements, e.g. double walled  terminals for balanced flue arrangements  F23L17/04 -- This subclass concerns fittings for chimneys or flues that are built from concentric or double-walled elements, such as terminals designed to provide a balanced flue flow. It includes devices where an inner wall carries the flue gases while an outer wall offers insulation or structural support, improving thermal efficiency and reducing condensation. The entry is part of the mechanical-engineering group dealing with removal or treatment of combustion products.
[F23J 13/04] Joints -- F23J 13/04 relates to the design and construction of joints used in fittings for chimneys or flues, which are components of systems that remove or treat combustion products and residues in mechanical engineering applications.
[F23J 13/06] Mouths -- Mouths are the terminal openings or connections on chimneys, flues or related combustion apparatus that allow the discharge of gases, smoke, or vapour to the atmosphere, often incorporating sealing or protective elements to control flow and prevent ingress of external substances.
[F23J 13/08] Doors or covers specially adapted for smoke-boxes, flues, or chimneys -- The CPC entry F23J 13/08 covers specially adapted doors or covers used in smoke-boxes, flues or chimneys of combustion apparatus. These components are designed to control the flow of gases, protect the chimney structure and facilitate maintenance, often featuring sealing or insulating features.
[F23J 15/00] Arrangements of devices for treating smoke or fumes -- F23J 15/00 covers the design and configuration of systems that treat smoke or fumes generated by combustion processes. It includes the spatial arrangement, integration and interconnection of filters, scrubbers, catalytic converters, condensers or other devices aimed at cleaning exhaust gases before release. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the removal or treatment of combustion products or residues.
[F23J 15/003] for supplying chemicals to fumes, e.g. using injection devices -- This CPC subclass covers systems that inject chemicals into smoke or fumes to treat or neutralise combustion products, typically using injection devices integrated into the exhaust flow. It is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the removal or treatment of combustion residues.
[F23J 15/006] Layout of treatment plant -- This subgroup covers the overall spatial organization of facilities that treat smoke or fumes from combustion processes, including the arrangement of ducts, reactors, filters, and auxiliary equipment. It addresses plant-scale layout considerations such as flow paths, equipment positioning, and integration of treatment stages within a single installation.
[F23J 15/02] of purifiers, e.g. for removing noxious material -- The CPC subclass F23J 15/02 covers arrangements of purifying devices integrated into combustion systems to remove noxious material from smoke or fumes, such as filters, scrubbers or other treatment units. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → removal or treatment of combustion products or residues.
[F23J 15/022] for removing solid particulate material from the gasflow -- F23J 15/022 covers arrangements of devices that remove solid particulate matter from the gas flow emerging from combustion processes. It includes filters, cyclones, electrostatic precipitators and other systems that separate dust, ash or soot from exhaust gases to treat smoke or fumes.
[F23J 15/025] using filters -- F23J 15/025 covers the use of filters in arrangements of devices that treat smoke or fumes from combustion processes. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and removal or treatment of combustion products or residues.
[F23J 15/027] using cyclone separators -- This CPC subclass concerns arrangements of devices that treat smoke or fumes by employing cyclone separators, which use a swirling gas flow to separate particulate matter from combustion emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and removal or treatment of combustion products or residues.
[F23J 15/04] using washing fluids -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Scrubbers for removing solids only -> F23J15/022
[F23J 15/06] of coolers -- The subclass F23J 15/06 relates to coolers that are part of arrangements for treating smoke or fumes generated by combustion. It includes heat-exchange devices, water-spray or air-cooling systems and other means of lowering the temperature of combustion gases within smoke-treatment installations.
[F23J 15/08] of heaters
[F23J 2211/00] Flue gas duct systems -- F23J 2211/00 covers flue gas duct systems used to transport combustion gases from boilers or furnaces to treatment or discharge points, including design, construction, and arrangement of ducts, elbows, and manifolds. The classification relates to mechanical engineering of combustion apparatus focusing on removal or treatment of combustion products.
[F23J 2211/10] Balanced flues (combining air supply and flue gas exhaust) -- F23J 2211/10 relates to balanced flues that integrate the supply of combustion air with the exhaust of flue gases in a single duct system, enhancing efficiency and minimizing heat loss. This configuration allows simultaneous intake and discharge, and is commonly employed in boilers, furnaces and similar combustion apparatus to streamline the removal or treatment of combustion products.
[F23J 2211/101] with coaxial duct arrangement -- F23J 2211/101 covers flue-gas duct systems that employ a coaxial arrangement, in which one duct is positioned inside another to convey the gas stream while the surrounding duct provides insulation, secondary flow or protective shelter. This design is used in combustion equipment to improve heat recovery, reduce heat loss and protect downstream components.
[F23J 2211/20] Common flues for several combustion devices -- This class covers ductwork that serves as a shared exhaust pathway for two or more separate combustion units, such as boilers, furnaces or incinerators, allowing their flue gases to be combined and vented through a single flue. It includes design features that manage pressure, temperature and corrosion in shared ducts.
[F23J 2211/30] Chimney or flue associated with building ventilation system -- The classification covers chimneys or flues that are integrated with a building's ventilation system, allowing exhaust gases from combustion to be discharged while also serving the building's air-handling requirements. It relates to mechanical-engineering flue-gas duct systems that combine removal of combustion products with building ventilation functions.
[F23J 2211/40] Chimney with internal flue pipe system -- The CPC entry F23J 2211/40 pertains to chimneys that contain an internal flue-pipe system for conveying flue gases within a single duct, facilitating the removal or treatment of combustion products and residues. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and flue-gas duct systems.
[F23J 2213/00] Chimneys or flues -- The CPC subclass F23J 2213/00, titled "Chimneys or flues," relates to the design and construction of passages that convey exhaust gases from combustion apparatuses to the atmosphere or to treatment units. It includes chimney stacks, flues, ducts and associated sealing, support and material features used for the safe removal of combustion products.
[F23J 2213/10] Linings -- Linings for chimneys or flues are protective or functional layers applied to the interior surfaces of ducts that convey combustion gases, intended to resist corrosion, heat and residue buildup, and to improve the durability and performance of the exhaust system.
[F23J 2213/101] Fastening means therefor -- This subclass pertains to devices that attach or secure elements of chimneys or flues used for removing or treating combustion gases and residues, such as brackets, clamps, fastening plates and similar mounting means. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the treatment of combustion products, and addresses the fastening methods employed in such exhaust systems.
[F23J 2213/20] Joints -- F23J 2213/20 pertains to joints used in chimneys or flues for the removal or treatment of combustion products or residues, covering the connections that join sections of the flue and provide airtight, heat-resistant sealing.
[F23J 2213/201] between stack and branch pipes -- The CPC subclass F23J 2213/201 relates to chimneys or flues and specifically to the portion that connects the main stack with its branch pipes, addressing the handling or treatment of combustion gases in that intermediate section.
[F23J 2213/202] between duct or stack sections -- F23J 2213/202 covers devices or arrangements installed between sections of a chimney or flue to manage the flow of gases or solids produced by combustion. It includes dampers, seals, transition pieces, or other components that facilitate cleaning, temperature regulation, or prevent leakage between stack sections.
[F23J 2213/203] between stack/duct and combustion apparatus -- The subclass covers the interface elements that join a chimney or flue to a combustion device, such as transition fittings, connectors or sealings that guide combustion gases from the stack into the furnace. It includes designs that manage flow continuity and minimise leakage at this junction.
[F23J 2213/204] Sealing arrangements -- F23J 2213/204 covers sealing arrangements used in chimneys or flues to prevent leakage of gases, smoke or condensate during removal or treatment of combustion products, including gaskets, packing and other sealing devices that accommodate thermal expansion and vibrations.
[F23J 2213/30] Specific materials -- The subclass F23J 2213/30 covers the use of particular construction materials for chimneys or flues that convey combustion gases or residues. It relates to selecting alloys, ceramics, composites or specialized coatings that provide resistance to heat, corrosion, abrasion or deposit buildup in combustion-product removal systems.
[F23J 2213/301] flexible -- The class F23J 2213/301 relates to flexible chimney or flue elements that convey combustion gases or residues, such as bendable ducts, articulated sections or other deformable structures designed to retain sealing and heat-resistant performance while accommodating movement or alignment.
[F23J 2213/302] plastic -- F23J 2213/302 relates to chimneys or flues where the primary material is plastic. The subclass addresses the use of polymeric construction for conveying gases or residues from combustion processes, focusing on the design, installation and protection of plastic ducts in such systems.
[F23J 2213/303] metallic -- F23J 2213/303 covers metallic elements used in the construction or lining of chimneys and flues for the removal or treatment of combustion gases and residues. It includes metal pipes, ducts and other structural parts designed to withstand high-temperature conditions and corrosion in mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus.

[F23J 2213/305] glass -- F23J 2213/305 classifies glass elements used in chimneys or flues that form part of systems for removing or treating combustion products or residues. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the subcategory dealing with treatment of combustion by-products.
[F23J 2213/40] Heat insulation fittings -- F23J 2213/40 covers heat-insulating fittings used in chimneys or flues of combustion apparatus, aimed at reducing heat loss and protecting surrounding structures. [F23J 2217/60] using settling/precipitation chambers -- The entry describes the use of settling or precipitation chambers in combustion apparatus to separate solid particles and residues from exhaust gases. [F23J 2219/00] Treatment devices -- F23J 2219/00 covers devices that treat or process the products and residues formed by combustion, such as flue gases, ash, or soot, to remove pollutants, recover energy, or facilitate disposal. [F23J 2219/10] Catalytic reduction devices -- Catalytic reduction devices are installations that employ a catalyst to convert harmful combustion gases, such as nitrogen oxides, carbon monoxide or unburned hydrocarbons, into less polluting substances like nitrogen, carbon dioxide and water. They are typically integrated into exhaust or flue-gas treatment systems of furnaces, boilers and power plants to meet emission regulations and improve overall combustion product cleanliness.

These are apparatuses that lower pollutant concentrations by physical means such as scrubbing, absorption, dilution, cooling or filtering, without employing a chemical catalyst. They belong to the broader group of treatment devices within mechanical engineering combustion apparatus.

These techniques fall under treatment devices for removing pollutants from flue gases or ash.
[F23J 2219/60] Sorption with dry devices, e.g. beds -- This subclass covers devices that remove or treat combustion gases or residues by passing them through dry sorbent media such as fixed beds. It includes sorption processes that use solid absorbents without liquid carriers, typically for cleaning flue gases or capturing particulates. The classification is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and treatment of combustion products.
[F23J 2219/70] Condensing contaminants with coolers -- The subclass covers devices that cool combustion gases so that contaminants condense and can be removed, typically using water-cooled or air-cooled condensers incorporated in exhaust treatment systems. It applies to equipment designed to recover or treat condensable pollutants from combustion residues.
[F23J 2219/80] Quenching -- Quenching covers methods and devices for rapidly cooling combustion gases, flames, or solid residues after a combustion process, typically by spraying water, steam, air or other cooling media to lower temperature, condense vapors, or solidify molten material. The technique is used to control emissions, prevent re-ignition and facilitate handling of the treated products.

[F23J 2900/13023] Ducts with non-circular cross-section -- A duct having a non-circular cross-section is used in specialized systems that transport or clean combustion gases and residues. [F23J 2900/15001] Irradiating fumes with electron or light beams, e.g. UV, for oxidizing or dissociating SOx and NOx -- The entry F23J 2900/15001 addresses methods that use electron beams or light sources such as ultraviolet radiation to treat combustion gases, specifically to oxidise or dissociate sulfur oxides and nitrogen oxides. [F23J 2900/15002] Cleaning fumes by letting them to pass a molten metal bath -- This CPC subclass covers a treatment step in which combustion gases are directed over or through a bath of molten metal that absorbs or reacts with pollutants, thereby purifying the fumes. [F23J 2900/15003] Supplying fumes with ozone -- The code F23J 2900/15003 covers devices or processes that introduce ozone into combustion gases as a special arrangement for conducting or purifying fumes, aimed at oxidising pollutants, improving odor control or enhancing overall treatment of combustion products. [F23J 2900/15021] Preventing emission of hexavalent chromium at the chimney outlet -- F23J 2900/15021 covers devices or methods that prevent the release of hexavalent chromium from the exhaust of combustion plants, typically by treating chimney gases before they are emitted. [F23J 2900/15022] Electrochemical cell filters -- The CPC entry F23J 2900/15022 covers electrochemical cell filters used to treat or purify combustion fumes and residues. [F23J 2900/15023] Magnetic filters -- Magnetic filters are devices installed in combustion exhaust systems to capture ferromagnetic particles and residues from flue gases using a magnetic field. [F23J 2900/15024] Photocatalytic filters -- Photocatalytic filters are specialized components installed in combustion exhaust systems to purify fumes by using light-activated catalytic surfaces that break down pollutants such as NOx, CO and volatile organics into harmless gases, thereby enhancing emission control in industrial or vehicular applications.
[F23J 2900/15025] Cyclone walls forming heat exchangers -- This subclass relates to cyclone walls that are employed as heat exchangers for treating combustion fumes, combining particle separation with heat recovery. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → removal or treatment of combustion products or residues, specifically special arrangements for conducting or purifying combustion gases.
[F23J 2900/15026] Cyclone separators with horizontal axis -- A cyclone separator with a horizontal axis is a device that uses centrifugal force to separate solid particles or liquids from combustion gases by forcing the gas stream to spiral around a horizontal cylindrical or conical chamber, allowing heavier particles to settle while the cleaned gas exits.
[F23J 2900/15027] Preventing emission of CO -- This CPC subclass covers technologies that limit carbon monoxide release from combustion processes. It is placed under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the treatment of combustion gases, focusing on special arrangements for conducting or purifying fumes to prevent CO emissions.
[F23J 2900/15041] Means for absorbing SOx using seawater -- The CPC entry F23J 2900/15041 covers devices that remove sulfur oxides from combustion gases by contacting the fumes with seawater, such as scrubbers or absorption chambers that dissolve SOx in the water. [F23J 2900/15061] Deep cooling or freezing of flue gas rich of CO -- The sub-class F23J 2900/15061 deals with methods that cool or freeze flue gases that contain a high proportion of carbon monoxide in order to aid their purification or removal. It includes techniques for rapid temperature reduction that cause condensation of contaminants or enable subsequent treatment steps in combustion exhaust systems.
[F23J 2900/15081] Reheating of flue gases -- The code F23J 2900/15081 covers methods and apparatuses that reheat flue gases after they have been treated or purified. It pertains to special arrangements within combustion exhaust systems that raise gas temperature to improve energy efficiency or facilitate further processing. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and treatment of combustion residues.
[F23J 3/00] Removing solid residues from passages or chambers beyond the fire, e.g. from flues by soot blowers -- The classification F23J 3/00 covers methods and devices for removing solid combustion residues such as soot from passages or chambers after the fire, typically using soot blowers, and falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and removal or treatment of combustion products.
[F23J 3/02] Cleaning furnace tubes -- F23J 3/02 covers techniques and apparatus for cleaning furnace tubes, specifically the removal of solid combustion residues such as soot from the interior of tubes or passages downstream of the fire. Methods include the use of soot blowers, pneumatic or mechanical cleaning devices, and other means to keep heat-exchange surfaces clear.
[F23J 3/023] cleaning the fireside of watertubes in boilers -- F23J 3/023 covers techniques and devices for cleaning the fireside of water-tube boilers, focusing on the removal of solid residues such as soot, scale or ash from passages and chambers located beyond the combustion zone, often by means of soot blowers or comparable cleaning tools.
[F23J 3/026] cleaning the chimneys -- F23J 3/026 relates to cleaning chimneys by removing solid residues, such as soot, from flues and passages downstream of the fire, typically using soot-blowing devices.
[F23J 3/04] Traps -- Traps are devices for removing solid residues such as soot or ash from flues, passages or chambers downstream of the fire in combustion apparatus, often employing soot-blowing or similar cleaning methods.
[F23J 3/06] Systems for accumulating residues from different parts of furnace plant -- F23J 3/06 relates to systems that gather solid combustion residues from various locations within a furnace plant into a common collection unit, employing conveyors, hoppers or similar means to facilitate removal and downstream handling.
[F23J 7/00] Arrangement of devices for supplying chemicals to fire -- It covers configurations of systems that introduce chemicals such as additives, extinguishing agents, or fuel enhancers into a flame or combustion chamber, including piping, injectors, distribution networks and control mechanisms for precise dosing. The subclass is applied to inventions that improve flame stability, emissions control or safety by optimizing the arrangement of chemical-supply devices.
[F23J 9/00] Preventing premature solidification of molten combustion residues
[F23J 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F23J 99/00 is a catch-all CPC subgroup under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Removal or Treatment of Combustion Products or Residues, covering any device, method or material related to treating combustion by-products that does not fit into any of the more specific groups of this subclass.
[F23K] FEEDING FUEL TO COMBUSTION APPARATUS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General arrangements for preparing and supplying fuel to a combustion apparatus, including accessories and peripheral devices such as for example pumps, pulverisers, pre-heaters or vaporisers, arrangements for adding chemicals to fuels, subdivided according to the type of the fuel which is burned.  Details of devices specially adapted to feed or prepare fuels.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fuel feeders specially adapted for fluidised bed combustion apparatus -> F23C10/22  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The term "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Ash: means any solid combustion residues, for example remaining in the fuel bed or suspended in the flue gases - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame - Combustion: means the direct combination of oxygen gas,e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of the apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Flue gases: any gaseous products of combustion - Grate: a perforated surface, e.g. a grid, which supports or delimits a bed of burning fuel and serves to supply primary air
[F23K 1/00] Preparation of lump or pulverulent fuel in readiness for delivery to combustion apparatus -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for processing solid or powdered fuels so they can be supplied to burners, including grinding, crushing, pelletizing, drying, and mixing steps that ready the material for combustion. It belongs to mechanical engineering and focuses on preparing lump or pulverulent fuel for delivery to combustion apparatus.
[F23K 1/02] Mixing solid fuel with a liquid, e.g. preparing slurries -- This subclass covers methods of preparing fuel slurries by mixing solid combustible material with a liquid carrier to enable efficient feeding into combustion apparatus. It includes processes that form homogeneous solid-liquid mixtures for delivery to burners, furnaces or other heat generators.
[F23K 1/04] Heating fuel prior to delivery to combustion apparatus -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for heating fuel before it is supplied to a combustion device. It includes pre-heating of solid, liquid or gaseous fuels using burners, heat exchangers, ovens or other energy sources to improve ignition, reduce emissions and enhance efficiency.
[F23K 2200/00] Pretreatment and supply of solid fuel -- The CPC entry F23K 2200/00 covers technologies for preparing and delivering solid fuels to combustion devices, including processes such as drying, crushing, briquetting, and storage, as well as mechanical feeding, conveyance and control systems that ensure a consistent fuel supply to burners. It is used to classify inventions that improve the handling, pretreatment and supply of solid fuels like coal, wood or biomass in combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2201/00] Pretreatment of solid fuel -- The F23K 2201/00 subclass covers techniques for preparing solid fuels before they are introduced into a combustion device. It includes processes such as drying, size reduction, grinding, crushing, briquetting, agglomeration, and pre-heating that improve handling, storage, or combustion performance of the fuel. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and feeding fuel to combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2201/10] Pulverizing -- The CPC class F23K 2201/10 covers the pulverizing of solid fuels as a pretreatment step before they are fed to combustion apparatus. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy, specifically within combustion-apparatus feed-system technologies, and addresses methods for reducing solid fuel size to improve handling, flow and combustion efficiency.
[F23K 2201/1003] Processes to make pulverulent fuels -- The subclass F23K 2201/1003 covers methods for converting solid fuels into fine, powder-like forms for use in combustion devices. It includes processes such as grinding, pulverising, drying and other pretreatment steps that produce a pulverulent fuel suitable for feeding into burners or other combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2201/1006] Mills adapted for use with furnaces -- F23K 2201/1006 covers mills that are specifically designed or modified for integration with furnaces, used to pretreat solid fuels before they are fed into combustion apparatus. These mills perform grinding, crushing or size reduction to improve fuel handling and combustion efficiency.
[F23K 2201/101] to a specific particle size -- This CPC subclass covers the pretreatment of solid fuel by reducing it to a defined particle size before feeding it to a combustion apparatus. It includes processes, devices, or control measures that ensure the fuel particles meet a specific size range to improve combustion efficiency or handling.
[F23K 2201/103] with hot gas supply -- The subclass F23K 2201/103 refers to combustion devices that feed solid fuel which has been pretreated and incorporate a hot-gas supply to assist ignition, improve fuel heating, or sustain the combustion process.
[F23K 2201/20] Drying -- In the CPC, code F23K 2201/20 belongs to the mechanical-engineering section, specifically to combustion apparatus and the feeding-fuel subcategory. It addresses the pretreatment of solid fuels by drying, describing processes or equipment that remove moisture from coal, biomass or similar materials before they are introduced into a burner. This improves combustion efficiency and reduces emissions.
[F23K 2201/30] Separating -- The F23K 2201/30 subclass covers processes that separate unwanted components from solid fuel before it is introduced into a combustion device, including mechanical, physical or chemical methods for removing ash, moisture, slag or inert particles to enhance fuel quality and combustion efficiency.
[F23K 2201/50] Blending -- The entry covers methods for mixing solid fuel with other substances or fuel types prior to introduction into a combustion device. Blending seeks to improve fuel homogeneity, moisture content, reactivity and overall combustion efficiency by mechanical mixing, pelletising or coating processes.
[F23K 2201/501] with other fuels or combustible waste -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, feeding fuel to combustion apparatus, specifically pretreatment of solid fuel. Code F23K 2201/501 covers methods for preparing solid fuel that will be used together with other fuels or combustible waste before it is fed to the combustion device.
[F23K 2201/503] with non-combustible liquids to prepare slurries -- The code refers to pretreating solid fuel by mixing it with non-combustible liquids to form a slurry before it is fed to a combustion device. This method improves handling, transport and feeding characteristics of the fuel, and is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and fuel-feeding processes.
[F23K 2201/505] with additives -- The entry covers pretreatment of solid fuel by incorporating additives before it is introduced into a combustion apparatus, aiming to improve fuel handling, combustion efficiency or emissions. It is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and feeding fuel to such apparatus.
[F23K 2203/00] Feeding arrangements -- The F23K 2203/00 feeding arrangements class covers devices and methods that supply fuel to combustion apparatus, including pipes, pumps, nozzles, mixers and control systems that regulate the flow, pressure or timing of fuel delivery to burners, engines or furnaces.
[F23K 2203/002] Feeding devices for steam boilers, e.g. in locomotives -- This CPC subclass covers devices that supply fuel to steam boilers, such as those used in locomotives, and includes mechanisms for delivering solid, liquid or gaseous fuel into the boiler's combustion chamber. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → feeding fuel to combustion apparatus, with the code F23K 2203/002.
[F23K 2203/004] Feeding devices with reciprocating members -- The class covers reciprocating feeding mechanisms that deliver fuel to a combustion device. It includes pistons, oscillating plungers and similar moving members that draw, compress and discharge fuel or air into burners, engines or furnaces. These devices are used to regulate fuel flow in various combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2203/006] Fuel distribution and transport systems for pulverulent fuel -- This subclass addresses the design and operation of systems that convey, distribute, and deliver pulverised fuels such as coal dust or powdered biomass to burners or combustion chambers. It includes pipelines, ducts, conveyors, blowers, valves and related control devices that ensure uniform fuel flow and proper dosing within a combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2203/008] Feeding devices for pulverulent fuel -- The CPC code F23K 2203/008 designates feeding devices for pulverulent fuel used in combustion apparatus. It includes mechanisms that transport, meter and introduce powdered fuel into burners or furnaces, covering systems such as conveyors, feeders and atomisers.
[F23K 2203/10] Supply line fittings -- Supply line fittings are components that connect, join or control the pipes and tubes delivering fuel to a combustion apparatus. They include couplings, connectors, valves and related hardware that ensure a secure, leak-tight path for fuel from storage or supply systems to burners or engines. This subgroup covers the design and arrangement of such fittings within the feeding arrangement of fuel-fed combustion devices.
[F23K 2203/101] Wear protection devices -- F23K 2203/101 covers devices that protect the components of fuel-feeding systems in combustion apparatus from wear, such as liners, coatings, shields or sacrificial elements that reduce abrasion, corrosion or erosion of valves, injectors, pumps and related parts during fuel delivery. It includes any protective arrangement designed to extend the service life of feeding mechanisms.
[F23K 2203/102] Flashback safety, e.g. inertizing devices -- The CPC subclass F23K 2203/102 covers safety measures that prevent flashback in fuel-feeding systems of combustion devices, typically by using inertizing or flashback-arresting mechanisms. It includes devices that introduce inert gas or other means to stop flame propagation back toward the fuel source, protecting equipment and operators.
[F23K 2203/103] Storage devices -- F23K 2203/103 refers to storage devices used in feeding fuel to combustion apparatus, covering containers such as tanks, bunkers, or reservoirs that hold solid, liquid or gaseous fuels before they are supplied to burners, ovens, furnaces or engines. The classification includes designs for fuel preservation, level monitoring and safety features that enable controlled delivery to the combustion system.
[F23K 2203/104] Metering devices -- The F23K 2203/104 subclass covers devices that measure and control the quantity of fuel supplied to combustion systems. It includes flow meters, injectors and other metering mechanisms that ensure precise fuel delivery to burners, boilers or engines, supporting efficient combustion and emission control.
[F23K 2203/105] Flow splitting devices to feed a plurality of burners -- F23K 2203/105 covers flow-splitting devices that distribute a single fuel or air stream to several burners in a combustion apparatus. These arrangements ensure each burner receives the required portion of the flow, improving combustion control and efficiency. The class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and feeding fuel to combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2203/20] Feeding/conveying devices -- F23K 2203/20 designates feeding or conveying devices that supply fuel to combustion apparatus, including pumps, feeders, conveyors and related mechanisms that regulate the delivery of solid, liquid or gaseous fuel to burners or engines. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and the feeding arrangements portion of the CPC.
[F23K 2203/201] using pneumatic means -- The subclass F23K 2203/201 covers feeding arrangements that supply fuel to a combustion apparatus by means of pneumatic devices such as blowers, compressors or pressurised air streams. It includes systems that use air pressure to convey solid, liquid or gaseous fuel from a source to the burner.
[F23K 2203/202] using screws -- The CPC subclass F23K 2203/202 relates to fuel-feeding arrangements that employ screws to convey solid fuel into combustion equipment such as furnaces, boilers or burners. It covers the design, operation and integration of screw feed mechanisms for continuous or intermittent fuel delivery.
[F23K 2203/203] using pistons or rams -- F23K 2203/203 classifies feeding arrangements that employ pistons or rams to introduce fuel into a combustion apparatus. The method typically involves reciprocating motion to meter and deliver fuel under controlled pressure for efficient combustion.
[F23K 2300/00] Pretreatment and supply of liquid fuel -- The subclass covers processes and equipment for conditioning liquid fuels before they are delivered to a burner or engine, such as filtration, heating, emulsifying, de-watering and metering, and the means of transporting the treated fuel to the combustion chamber. It includes pumps, pipelines, valves and control systems that ensure reliable, regulated supply of pre-treated liquid fuel to combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2300/10] Pretreatment -- The F23K 2300/10 subclass defines methods and devices for pretreating liquid fuel before it is supplied to a combustion apparatus. It includes processes such as filtration, heating, emulsifying, de-watering, or chemical conditioning aimed at improving fuel quality, stability, or combustibility for engines, boilers, or burners.
[F23K 2300/101] Application of magnetism or electricity -- This subclass covers methods that employ magnetic or electrical fields to treat liquid fuel before it is supplied to a combustion apparatus, such as demagnetisation, charge-separation or electrostatic filtration, in order to improve fuel quality, handling or combustion performance.
[F23K 2300/103] Mixing with other fluids -- The subclass F23K 2300/103 covers techniques and equipment for mixing liquid fuel with other fluids as a pretreatment step before supplying it to a combustion apparatus, including blending, emulsifying or adding additives to improve combustion performance.
[F23K 2300/20] Supply line arrangements -- The classification relates to the design and configuration of lines that transport liquid fuel to combustion devices, covering pipes, hoses, manifolds, pumps and associated components. It includes arrangements for pretreatment steps such as filtration, heating, metering or mixing before the fuel reaches the burner or engine, and addresses how these supply lines are integrated within the overall fuel-feeding system.
[F23K 2300/201] Pumps -- F23K 2300/201 identifies pumps that are employed to pretreat and deliver liquid fuel to combustion apparatus. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the feeding of fuel to burners, and includes devices for fuel pressurisation, metering and transfer prior to combustion.
[F23K 2300/202] Filtering -- The entry F23K 2300/202 relates to filtering of liquid fuel as part of its pretreatment and supply to a combustion apparatus. It covers methods and devices that remove solids, contaminants or water from fuels such as diesel, gasoline or biomass liquids before they are injected or sprayed into burners, furnaces, boilers or internal-combustion engines.
[F23K 2300/203] Purging -- The entry covers methods and devices for removing residual liquid fuel, air or gases from fuel lines, injectors, tanks or combustion chambers in order to prevent unwanted ignition, corrosion or malfunction before start-up or after shutdown. It includes purging of pipelines, filters and related components in engines, boilers, burners and other combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2300/204] Preheating -- The F23K 2300/204 preheating class covers techniques and apparatus for heating liquid fuel before it is delivered to a combustion system. It includes devices that raise fuel temperature to improve atomisation, flow, or ignition characteristics, and may be integrated with fuel pumps, filters, or heat exchangers within the fuel-supply line. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the feeding of fuel to combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2300/205] Vaporising -- The F23K 2300/205 subclass pertains to vaporising techniques used in the pretreatment and supply of liquid fuel to combustion apparatus. It covers devices and processes that convert liquid fuel into a vapor-rich form-such as pre-heating, atomising or evaporating systems-before the fuel is fed to the burner for combustion. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the feeding of fuel to combustion devices.
[F23K 2300/206] Control devices -- F23K 2300/206 covers control devices employed in the pretreatment and supply of liquid fuel to combustion apparatus. It includes regulators, pumps, valves, sensors and other mechanisms that manage fuel flow, pressure, temperature or composition before the fuel reaches the burner. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion apparatus → Feeding fuel to combustion apparatus → Pretreatment and supply of liquid fuel.
[F23K 2400/00] Pretreatment and supply of gaseous fuel -- The class F23K 2400/00 covers technologies for conditioning and delivering gaseous fuels to combustion devices, including drying, filtering, pressure regulation, mixing and metering of gases such as natural gas, LPG or hydrogen before they reach burners, injectors or engines. It addresses apparatus and processes that ensure the fuel is suitably prepared and supplied for efficient and reliable combustion.
[F23K 2400/10] Pretreatment -- F23K 2400/10 covers methods and devices for pretreating gaseous fuels before they are supplied to combustion apparatus, including removal of contaminants, drying, and conditioning to improve combustion efficiency and reduce emissions.
[F23K 2400/20] Supply line arrangements -- The entry F23K 2400/20 addresses the design and configuration of supply lines that transport gaseous fuel from storage, pretreatment or regulation units to burners or other combustion components. It includes the routing, manifold layouts, valve and pressure-control placement, and safety features required for reliable fuel delivery.
[F23K 2400/201] Control devices -- The F23K 2400/201 subclass covers control devices used in the pretreatment and supply of gaseous fuel to combustion apparatus. It includes mechanisms that regulate pressure, flow, mixing or timing of gas before it enters burners or furnaces, ensuring safe and efficient fuel delivery.
[F23K 2900/00] Special features of, or arrangements for fuel supplies -- F23K 2900/00 relates to special features or configurations of fuel-supply systems for combustion apparatus, encompassing arrangements for storing, metering, delivering or regulating fuel to burners or engines, and any inventive means of controlling the fuel flow rather than the combustion process itself.
[F23K 2900/00001] Treating the fuel, either liquid or gaseous, with sound waves to enhance fuel properties -- The entry covers methods that apply acoustic energy to liquid or gaseous fuel in order to modify its physical or chemical characteristics before combustion. Sound waves are used to atomize, mix, heat, or otherwise enhance fuel properties, improving combustion efficiency or reducing emissions. This technique applies to fuel-supply arrangements in combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2900/00002] Treating the fuel, either liquid or gaseous, with far-infrared radiations [FIR] to enhance fuel properties -- The code F23K 2900/00002 designates methods of treating liquid or gaseous fuel by exposing it to far-infrared radiation before it is supplied to a combustion device. The treatment seeks to modify physical or chemical properties to improve combustion efficiency, emissions or fuel stability. It is located in the CPC hierarchy under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and fuel feeding.
[F23K 2900/01001] Cleaning solid fuel before combustion to achieve reduced emissions -- This subclass covers methods or devices that clean solid fuels-such as coal, biomass or charcoal-before they are introduced into a burner in order to lower emissions. Techniques may include washing, sieving, de-ashing or other treatments that remove contaminants, moisture or oversized particles, thereby improving combustion efficiency and reducing pollutants.
[F23K 2900/01002] Treating solid fuel with electromagnetic fields before combustion -- This CPC subclass covers methods or apparatuses that apply electromagnetic fields to solid fuels such as coal, biomass or waste before they are fed into a combustion chamber, aiming to alter ignition characteristics, combustion efficiency or emissions. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, feeding fuel to combustion apparatus, with special features for fuel supply arrangements.
[F23K 2900/01003] Pulverizing solid fuel in vacuum or a reduced pressure environment -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for grinding solid fuel while operating under vacuum or reduced pressure, aimed at improving fuel handling in combustion systems. It includes equipment that creates low-pressure environments to achieve efficient pulverisation of solid fuels.
[F23K 2900/01041] Heating by using exhaust gas heat -- This CPC subclass covers arrangements that capture heat from the exhaust gases of a combustion apparatus and apply it to preheat fuel or related components, improving overall efficiency of mechanical-engineering systems. It is a special feature for fuel supply in combustion equipment.
[F23K 2900/03001] Airlock sections in solid fuel supply lines -- The code F23K 2900/03001 refers to devices that incorporate airlock sections within solid fuel supply lines to prevent backflow of air or gases while allowing continuous fuel movement. These airlock sections are used in combustion apparatus to maintain pressure differentials and improve fuel feeding efficiency.
[F23K 2900/05001] Control or safety devices in gaseous or liquid fuel supply lines -- This CPC entry covers devices that monitor, regulate or protect the flow of gaseous or liquid fuels in supply lines to combustion systems, such as pressure regulators, shut-off valves, flow sensors and emergency-stop mechanisms. It groups safety and control features for fuel delivery arrangements.
[F23K 2900/05002] Valves for gaseous fuel supply lines -- The CPC code F23K 2900/05002 covers valves used in gaseous fuel supply lines for combustion apparatus. It falls under the mechanical engineering group for feeding fuel to combustion apparatus and addresses special features or arrangements for fuel supplies.
[F23K 2900/05003] Non-continuous fluid fuel supply -- The CPC code F23K 2900/05003 pertains to arrangements that supply fluid fuel to a combustion apparatus in a non-continuous manner, such as intermittent injectors, metering valves or timed pumps that deliver fuel only when required, rather than a steady flow. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fuel-feeding systems for combustion devices.
[F23K 2900/05004] Mixing two or more fluid fuels -- This subclass covers devices or methods for combining two or more liquid or gaseous fuels before they are delivered to a combustion chamber, aiming to achieve desired burn characteristics or emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fuel-feeding arrangements for combustion apparatus.
[F23K 2900/05081] Treating the fuel with catalyst to enhance combustion -- This CPC subclass covers methods of treating fuel with a catalyst before it is supplied to a combustion apparatus in order to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions, or enhance flame stability. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → feeding fuel to combustion apparatus → special features of, or arrangements for fuel supplies.
[F23K 2900/05082] Removing gaseous substances from liquid fuel line, e.g. oxygen -- This CPC subclass addresses methods or devices that remove gaseous contaminants such as oxygen from a liquid-fuel line before the fuel reaches the combustion chamber, thereby enhancing fuel stability and engine performance. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and fuel-feeding arrangements.
[F23K 2900/05083] Separating watery fractions from liquid fuel -- The subclass describes devices or processes that remove water or other aqueous fractions from liquid fuel before it is supplied to a combustion apparatus. It includes separators, coalescers, centrifuges and similar arrangements designed to purify fuel by extracting watery components, thereby improving fuel quality and combustion performance.
[F23K 2900/05084] Creating a combustible foam of liquid fuels and air -- This subclass covers methods for producing a combustible foam by mixing liquid fuel with air to create a fuel-air mixture that can be delivered to a combustion device. It relates to fuel feeding arrangements that generate a stable foam for improved atomization, combustion stability or safety.
[F23K 2900/05141] Control or safety devices in liquid fuel supply line -- This CPC subclass covers devices that monitor, regulate or protect the liquid-fuel line feeding a combustion apparatus, such as shut-off valves, pressure regulators and safety interlocks designed to prevent over-pressure, leakage or unintended fuel flow.
[F23K 2900/05142] Special materials for liquid fuel supply lines -- This classification covers inventions that focus on the selection or use of particular materials for liquid fuel supply lines in combustion systems. It includes solutions that improve durability, corrosion resistance, temperature handling or safety of the fuel conduits.
[F23K 3/00] Feeding or distributing of lump or pulverulent fuel to combustion apparatus -- F23K 3/00 pertains to the mechanisms and methods used to supply solid or powdered fuels to burners, furnaces and other combustion devices. It includes apparatus such as conveyors, hoppers, metering feeders and distribution systems that regulate the flow, positioning and quantity of lump or pulverulent fuel to achieve efficient and controlled combustion.
[F23K 3/02] Pneumatic feeding arrangements, i.e. by air blast -- F23K 3/02 addresses pneumatic fuel feeding arrangements that use an air blast to transport lump or powdered fuel into a combustion apparatus. These systems convey the fuel by a stream of air, enabling controlled distribution and improved mixing with the combustion air, often applied in boilers, furnaces and similar heating devices.
[F23K 3/04] for locomotive boiler furnaces -- F23K 3/04 covers methods and devices for supplying solid or powdered fuel to the furnace of a locomotive boiler.
