It includes feed mechanisms, distribution arrangements and related components that ensure fuel reaches the combustion zone in railway steam engines.
[F23K 3/06] for shaft-type furnaces -- F23K 3/06 relates to feeding or distributing lump or pulverulent fuel specifically in shaft-type furnaces. It includes apparatus and methods that introduce solid or powdered fuel into the furnace chamber for combustion, covering feed mechanisms, distribution systems and associated controls.
[F23K 3/08] for furnaces having movable grate bars -- This subgroup covers the supply and distribution of solid fuels to furnaces that use movable grate bars to regulate fuel positioning and combustion, applicable to both lump and pulverulent fuels.
[F23K 3/10] Under-feed arrangements -- Under-feed arrangements are methods that introduce lump or pulverulent fuel from below the combustion chamber, allowing the material to rise into the fire or furnace. This upward feeding helps maintain stable combustion and can improve fuel distribution while minimizing spillage.
[F23K 3/12] feeding by piston -- F23K 3/12 covers methods and devices that deliver solid or powdered fuel to a combustion chamber using a piston mechanism. The piston pressurizes and pushes the fuel into the burner, enabling precise metering and continuous feed. This technique is used in engines and furnaces where controlled fuel supply is required.
[F23K 3/14] feeding by screw -- F23K 3/14 covers screw-type devices that convey lump or powdered fuel into a combustion chamber. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the feeding of fuel, specifying screw feeding mechanisms for solid fuels.
[F23K 3/16] Over-feed arrangements -- Over-feed arrangements are mechanisms that deliberately deliver a surplus of solid or powdered fuel into a furnace or boiler, typically to improve ignition, maintain stable combustion, or handle variations in load. They form part of the feeding or distributing of lump or pulverulent fuel within combustion apparatus.
[F23K 3/18] Spreader stokers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeding devices wherein the fuel is fed by scattering it over the fuel-supporting surface.
[F23K 3/20] with moving hoppers -- F23K 3/20 covers methods and devices that feed lump or powdered fuel to a combustion system by means of hoppers that move, typically sliding, tilting or rotating, to discharge fuel into the burner. This sub-class relates to mechanical arrangements that transport fuel from storage to the combustion chamber using the motion of the hopper itself.
[F23K 3/22] Controlling thickness of fuel bed -- The F23K 3/22 subclass covers methods and apparatus for regulating the thickness of a solid or powdered fuel layer in a combustion device. It addresses control strategies that ensure a consistent bed depth to promote stable burning, efficient heat release, and prevent uneven combustion or flameout.
[F23K 5/00] Feeding or distributing other fuel to combustion apparatus -- F23K 5/00 covers systems and methods for supplying or distributing fuels other than conventional liquids or gases to combustion devices, such as solid, powdered, or alternative energy carriers. It includes apparatus for handling, metering, and delivering these fuels to burners, furnaces, or engines, focusing on the feeding mechanisms rather than the combustion process itself. This subclass lies within mechanical engineering, under combustion apparatus and fuel feeding categories.
[F23K 5/002] Gaseous fuel -- The code F23K 5/002 covers methods and apparatus for supplying gaseous fuels such as natural gas, LPG or hydrogen to burners, furnaces, engines or other combustion devices, including pipelines, regulators, mixers and injection systems that control flow, pressure and safety during fuel delivery.
[F23K 5/005] from a central source to a plurality of burners -- The subclass F23K 5/005 covers arrangements that convey fuel from a single central source to several burners, such as manifolds, distribution pipes and associated valves that split the flow to multiple combustion zones. It addresses the design and operation of the distribution network that ensures each burner receives the appropriate fuel quantity.
[F23K 5/007] Details
[F23K 5/02] Liquid fuel -- F23K 5/02 is a CPC subclass covering the feeding or distribution of liquid fuel to combustion apparatus. It includes systems and methods for delivering liquid fuels such as gasoline, diesel or kerosene to burners, engines or boilers. The code falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Feeding Fuel to Combustion Apparatus.
[F23K 5/04] Feeding or distributing systems using pumps -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Feeding or distributing systems using pumps from a central source to a plurality of burners -> F23K5/06
[F23K 5/06] from a central source to a plurality of burners -- F23K 5/06 addresses systems that convey fuel from a single central supply to multiple burners in a combustion apparatus, encompassing manifolds, distribution piping and associated control arrangements that ensure each burner receives the required fuel quantity.
[F23K 5/08] Preparation of fuel -- F23K 5/08 covers methods and apparatus for preparing fuel before it is introduced into a combustion device, including blending, drying, heating or chemically treating the fuel to improve combustion performance. It is part of the hierarchy mechanical engineering - combustion apparatus - feeding fuel to combustion apparatus.
[F23K 5/10] Mixing with other fluids -- The F23K 5/10 subclass addresses systems or methods that blend additional liquids or gases with fuel as it is fed to a combustion device, such as mixing water, air or additives with gasoline or oil to enhance combustion characteristics. It covers mechanisms that combine these fluids during or immediately before delivery to the burner.
[F23K 5/12] Preparing emulsions -- This subclass covers processes for creating fuel-oil emulsions, where liquid fuel is mixed with water or other liquids to form a stable dispersion used for feeding combustion apparatus. It includes methods of emulsifying, stabilising, and conditioning the mixture before injection or delivery to burners or engines.
[F23K 5/14] Details thereof -- F23K 5/14 pertains to the detailed elements of systems that feed or distribute alternative fuels to combustion apparatuses. It includes specific components, configurations, and operational features for handling non-standard or secondary fuel streams within burners, boilers, or engines.
[F23K 5/142] Fuel pumps -- F23K 5/142 covers fuel pumps that feed or distribute alternative fuels to combustion devices such as engines, burners or boilers. It includes any pump-mechanical, electric or hydraulic-that moves liquid, gaseous or solid fuel from storage to the combustion chamber, regardless of the specific pump design.
[F23K 5/145] combined with fans -- F23K 5/145 defines a subclass of fuel-feeding or distributing systems for combustion apparatus that are combined with fans or blowers, enabling simultaneous delivery of fuel and airflow to improve mixing or combustion efficiency. It applies to integrated arrangements where the fan is part of the fuel-supply unit.
[F23K 5/147] Valves -- The subclass F23K 5/147 covers valves that control the supply or distribution of fuel to combustion devices. It includes fuel-feed, shut-off, pressure-regulating and metering valves used in burners, engines or furnaces for both liquid and gaseous fuels.
[F23K 5/16] Safety devices -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cleaning or purging devices, e.g. filters -> F23K5/18
[F23K 5/18] Cleaning or purging devices, e.g. filters -- F23K 5/18 covers cleaning or purging devices such as filters used for feeding or distributing other fuel to combustion apparatus. The classification relates to mechanisms that remove contaminants or purge fuel-lines in mechanical-engineering combustion systems.
[F23K 5/20] Preheating devices -- F23K 5/20 covers devices that raise the temperature of fuel before it is supplied to burners, ovens or other combustion equipment, improving ignition and efficiency. These preheating systems are used in a range of mechanical-engineering applications where fuel is fed or distributed to combustion apparatus.
[F23K 5/22] Vaporising devices -- Vaporising devices are components that convert liquid or solid fuel into a gaseous form before it enters a combustion chamber, ensuring proper mixing with air for efficient burning. Typical examples include carburetors, fuel-atomisers and injector nozzles, which are used to feed or distribute fuel in engines, burners and similar apparatus.
[F23L] SUPPLYING AIR OR NON-COMBUSTIBLE LIQUIDS OR GASES TO COMBUSTION APPARATUS IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Supply of air or non-combustible gases or liquids to combustion apparatus in general, for example:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Air-supply arrangements for fluent fuels -> F23C - Firebridges for delivery of air or steam to combustion apparatus -> F23M3/04 - Baffles or deflectors for air or combustion products with air-supply passages -> F23M9/04 - Dampers and throat restrictors for open fire-places -> F24 - Air inlet valves for open fire fronts -> F24  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The term "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Ash: means any solid combustion residues, for example remaining in the fuel bed or suspended in the flue gases - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame - Combustion: a heat-producing sequence of chemical reactions between a burnable substance and molecular oxygen, e.g. in air, in most cases generating light in the form of flames or a glow - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of the apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Firebridge: a low wall separating the fuel bed from adjacent flue gas passages in apparatus for combustion of solid fuel, for example in reverberatory furnaces or fire-tube boilers - Flue gases: any gaseous products of combustion - Grate: a perforated surface, e.g. a grid, which supports or delimits a bed of burning fuel and serves to supply primary air
[F23L 1/00] Passages or apertures for delivering primary air for combustion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Passages or apertures for delivering primary air for combustion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Baffles or deflectors in air inlets -> F23M9/02
[F23L 1/02] by discharging the air below the fire -- F23L 1/02 pertains to designs where the primary combustion air is introduced through passages that discharge the air beneath the fire, ensuring direct delivery of air to the burning zone. This subcategory falls under the broader group for supplying air or non-combustible liquids or gases to combustion apparatus.
[F23L 11/00] Arrangements of valves or dampers after the fire -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements of valves or dampers after the fire.
[F23L 11/005] for closing the flue during interruption of burner function -- F23L 11/005 concerns devices that shut the flue of a combustion apparatus when the burner is interrupted, using valves or dampers positioned downstream of the fire. The arrangement isolates the exhaust path to prevent reverse flow and improve safety.
[F23L 11/02] for reducing draught by admission of air to flues -- F23L 11/02 covers arrangements of valves or dampers placed downstream of the fire that admit air into flues to lessen draft in combustion apparatus. Such devices regulate airflow in boilers, furnaces or similar systems to improve combustion stability and efficiency.
[F23L 13/00] Construction of valves or dampers for controlling air supply or draught -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Construction of valves or dampers for controlling air supply or draught.
[F23L 13/02] pivoted about a single axis but having not other movement -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Valves formed as linked slats each pivoted about an axis -> F23L13/08
[F23L 13/04] with axis perpendicular to face -- F23L 13/04 covers valves or dampers whose operating axis is oriented perpendicular to the valve face, used to regulate the supply of air or non-combustible gases to combustion equipment. The classification applies to such components in fuel-air control systems.
[F23L 13/06] slidable only -- F23L 13/06 classifies valves or dampers used to regulate air, non-combustible liquids or gases supplied to combustion equipment, where the controlling element moves only by sliding action. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and general air-supply control.
[F23L 13/08] operating as a roller blind -- The code F23L 13/08 covers devices that regulate the flow of air or other non-combustible gases to a combustion chamber by using a roller-blind type valve or damper. Such mechanisms act as adjustable shutters that can be rolled up or down to control draught and supply.
[F23L 13/10] having a compound movement involving both sliding and pivoting -- F23L 13/10 covers valves or dampers used to regulate air or inert fluid flow to combustion devices that operate by a combined sliding and pivoting motion. The compound movement allows precise control of draught while maintaining a compact mechanism.
[F23L 15/00] Heating of air supplied for combustion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heating of air supplied for combustion.
[F23L 15/02] Arrangements of regenerators -- The F23L 15/02 subclass covers the design and layout of regenerators that preheat air or other non-combustible gases before they enter a combustion device. It includes details of regenerator placement, flow-path arrangement and integration with heating systems for improved thermal efficiency.
[F23L 15/04] Arrangements of recuperators -- This subclass covers designs and configurations of heat exchangers that recover heat from exhaust gases to preheat combustion air or other fluids before they enter a burner. It includes placement, flow paths, and integration of recuperators in the combustion system to improve efficiency.
[F23L 15/045] using intermediate heat-transfer fluids -- The classification covers devices or methods that employ an intermediate heat-transfer fluid to raise the temperature of air before it enters a combustion chamber. It includes systems where the heated fluid absorbs energy from another source and transfers it to the combustion air, improving efficiency or control.
[F23L 17/00] Inducing draught -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inducing draught.
[F23L 17/005] using fans -- F23L 17/005 covers the use of fans to create draft in combustion apparatus, specifically for supplying air or non-combustible liquids or gases to the fire source. It addresses methods of inducing airflow to improve combustion efficiency.
[F23L 17/02] Tops for chimneys or ventilating shafts -- F23L 17/02 covers devices that form the uppermost part of a chimney or ventilating shaft, known as tops, which are designed to protect the opening and enhance the draft that draws air or non-combustible gases through the combustion system. These tops may incorporate weather shields, rain caps or aerodynamic shapes to improve flow and prevent ingress of precipitation.
[F23L 17/04] Balanced-flue arrangements, i.e. devices which combine air inlet to combustion unit with smoke outlet -- Balanced-flue arrangements are devices that combine the inlet of air or non-combustible gases with the outlet of combustion smoke in a single, coordinated passage. By linking the supply and exhaust flows, they maintain a balanced draft that improves combustion efficiency and reduces the risk of flame lift-off. This configuration is used in burners, furnaces and other combustion apparatus where precise control of airflow and exhaust is required.
[F23L 17/06] branched -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices that induce draught in combustion apparatus by supplying air or non-combustible liquids or gases through branched passages. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the category of combustion apparatus and the supply of gases to such apparatus, focusing on branched configurations.
[F23L 17/08] with coaxial cones or louvres -- The CPC subclass F23L 17/08 covers mechanisms that create draft in combustion equipment by using coaxial cones or louvres to direct airflow or non-combustible fluids, facilitating the supply of air or gases to the fire chamber. This falls under the broader groups for combustion apparatus and draft induction.
[F23L 17/10] wherein the top moves as a whole -- F23L 17/10 refers to draught-inducing devices whose entire top or lid moves as a single unit to create airflow, typically in furnaces, boilers or similar combustion equipment. The moving top functions to draw fresh air or gases into the combustion zone, enhancing combustion efficiency. This subclass falls under the broader category of supplying air or non-combustible liquids or gases to combustion apparatus.
[F23L 17/12] Devices for fastening the top or terminal to chimney, shaft, or flue -- The code F23L 17/12 covers devices used to secure the top or terminal end of a chimney, shaft or flue to the surrounding structure. These fastening mechanisms ensure a reliable seal and structural stability, preventing leakage of gases and maintaining the integrity of the combustion exhaust system.
[F23L 17/14] Draining devices -- F23L 17/14 covers draining devices used in combustion systems to remove liquids or condensate from air or gas supply lines, flues or ducts that are part of draught-inducing arrangements. These devices help maintain efficient airflow and prevent water accumulation in burners, boilers or other fuel-burning equipment.
[F23L 17/16] Induction apparatus, e.g. steam jet, acting on combustion products beyond the fire -- F23L 17/16 classifies induction devices that use a jet of steam or another fluid to create a draught in the combustion gases after the flame, thereby assisting the supply of air or non-combustible gases to the burner.
[F23L 2700/00] Installations for increasing draught in chimneys -- F23L 2700/00 covers devices and systems designed to improve the airflow or suction in chimney structures, such as draft boosters, fans, or ejectors that increase the natural draft to enhance combustion efficiency. These installations are used in heating, industrial or power generation settings to ensure reliable flue gas removal.
[F23L 2700/001] Installations for increasing draught in chimneys -- This subclass covers devices and systems that boost draft in chimney flues, such as fans, blowers or suction mechanisms, to improve airflow and combustion efficiency in boilers, furnaces and similar equipment. The installations may be mechanically or electrically powered and are used across various heating and industrial processes.
[F23L 2700/002] Specific draught control devices for locomotives -- F23L 2700/002 refers to draught control devices specially designed for locomotives, which regulate the supply of air or non-combustible gases to the firebox to optimise combustion and maintain proper chimney draft. These devices are part of installations that increase draught in combustion apparatus.
[F23L 2900/00] Special arrangements for supplying or treating air or oxidant for combustion -- The F23L 2900/00 class covers special arrangements for delivering or conditioning the air or oxidant used in combustion systems, such as mixers, humidifiers, heaters, filters, pressurizers or distribution networks that ensure proper supply and treatment of the combustion gas. It is applied to devices and methods that control the quality, temperature, pressure or flow of the oxidant before it reaches the furnace, boiler or engine.
[F23L 2900/00001] Treating oxidant before combustion, e.g. by adding a catalyst -- The CPC subclass F23L 2900/00001 covers methods and devices for conditioning the oxidant supplied to a combustion system, such as catalytic pretreatment of air before it enters the burner. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the supply of air or non-combustible gases.
[F23L 2900/05021] Gas turbine driven blowers for supplying combustion air or oxidant, i.e. turbochargers -- The entry covers gas-turbine-driven blowers that deliver combustion air or oxidant to a furnace, boiler or similar device, essentially functioning as turbochargers. It pertains to specialized arrangements for supplying or treating the gas flow that fuels combustion.
[F23L 2900/07001] Injecting synthetic air, i.e. a combustion supporting mixture made of pure oxygen and an inert gas, e.g. nitrogen or recycled fumes -- The entry covers devices that inject a synthetic air mixture-pure oxygen combined with an inert gas such as nitrogen or recycled exhaust-into a combustion system. This special arrangement supplies a controlled oxidant, improving combustion efficiency or emissions control.
[F23L 2900/07002] Injecting inert gas, other than steam or evaporated water, into the combustion chambers -- The classification covers methods or devices that introduce an inert gas, other than steam or evaporated water, into a combustion chamber to modify the flame or lower emissions. It belongs to the mechanical engineering group for special arrangements of supplying or treating air or oxidant for combustion.
[F23L 2900/07003] Controlling the inert gas supply -- This CPC subclass, within mechanical engineering and combustion apparatus, pertains to the supply of air or non-combustible gases to a burner. It addresses systems that monitor and adjust the flow of inert gases, such as nitrogen or argon, to control mixture composition, flame stability or emissions in combustion processes.
[F23L 2900/07004] Injecting liquid or solid materials releasing oxygen, e.g. perchlorate, nitrate, peroxide, and chlorate compounds, or appropriate mixtures thereof -- This CPC subclass deals with injecting liquid or solid oxygen-releasing materials such as perchlorate, nitrate, peroxide or chlorate compounds, or mixtures thereof, into a combustion apparatus to provide oxidant. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, supplying air or non-combustible liquids or gases, and special arrangements for supplying or treating air or oxidant. Entry code F23L 2900/07004.
[F23L 2900/07005] Injecting pure oxygen or oxygen enriched air -- This subclass deals with methods and devices for introducing pure oxygen or oxygen-enriched air into a combustion system to improve efficiency or emissions. It covers designs that inject the enriched oxidant directly into the furnace, burner or mixing zone, typically as a special air supply arrangement.
[F23L 2900/07006] Control of the oxygen supply -- This subclass deals with mechanisms that regulate the amount of oxygen or oxidant delivered to a combustion device. It includes sensors, valves, feedback loops and other control strategies designed to adjust airflow for optimal flame stability, efficiency or emissions reduction.
[F23L 2900/07007] using specific ranges of oxygen percentage -- F23L 2900/07007 defines inventions that adjust the oxygen concentration delivered to a combustion device within specified percentage ranges, using methods such as mixing, enrichment, or depletion to improve flame stability, efficiency or emissions. The subclass is part of mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the supply or treatment of air or non-combustible oxidant for combustion.
[F23L 2900/07008] Injection of water into the combustion chamber -- The code F23L 2900/07008 covers devices that introduce water directly into a combustion chamber to modify the flame, improve temperature control, reduce emissions, or enhance fuel efficiency. Such injection systems are a specific method of supplying a non-combustible liquid to a combustion apparatus, often used in boilers, furnaces, or engines to achieve better performance and lower pollutant formation.
[F23L 2900/07009] Injection of steam into the combustion chamber -- This CPC subclass covers devices or methods that inject steam into a combustion chamber to improve fuel efficiency, reduce emissions, or control temperature, typically used in boilers, furnaces, or gas turbines.
[F23L 2900/15021] using regenerative heat exchanger bodies with different layers of material -- The entry covers designs where a regenerative heat exchanger for a combustion system is built from multiple concentric or stacked layers of material, each layer having distinct thermal or structural properties to improve heat recovery and manage temperature gradients during air or oxidant supply. This arrangement enhances efficiency and durability of the combustion apparatus.
[F23L 2900/15022] using pre-purging regenerator beds -- The code covers methods that pre-purge the regenerator beds of a combustion system before they supply or treat the incoming air or oxidant. By removing residual gases or contaminants from the beds, the pre-purging improves the efficiency and stability of the combustion process during start-up or transitions.
[F23L 2900/15041] Preheating combustion air by recuperating heat from ashes -- The entry covers arrangements that raise the temperature of combustion air by recovering heat from ash, typically through heat-exchanger elements placed in the exhaust stream, thereby improving combustion efficiency and reducing fuel consumption.
[F23L 2900/15042] Preheating combustion air by auxiliary combustion, e.g. in a turbine -- This CPC subclass covers methods and arrangements that preheat the combustion air by burning additional fuel, often in a turbine, before it enters the main combustor. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the supply of air or non-combustible gases for combustion.
[F23L 2900/15043] Preheating combustion air by heat recovery means located in the chimney, e.g. for home heating devices -- The code F23L 2900/15043 designates arrangements that preheat the air or oxidant supplied to a combustion device by recovering heat from exhaust gases in the chimney, such as in domestic heating installations. This falls under the broader class for supplying or treating air for combustion apparatus.
[F23L 2900/15044] Preheating combustion air by heat recovery means using solar or other clean energy -- A method for supplying combustion air that uses recovered heat from solar energy or other clean energy sources to preheat the air before it enters the furnace or burner, improving efficiency and reducing fuel consumption. This approach applies to systems where air or oxidant is treated or conditioned prior to combustion.
[F23L 3/00] Arrangements of valves or dampers before the fire -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements of valves or dampers before the fire.
[F23L 5/00] Blast-producing apparatus before the fire -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Blast-producing apparatus before the fire.
[F23L 5/02] Arrangements of fans or blowers -- The CPC entry F23L 5/02 covers the layout and integration of fans or blowers that supply air or non-combustible gases to combustion devices, especially in blast-producing apparatus prior to ignition. It focuses on how these airflow units are arranged within the overall system to ensure proper delivery and pressure for efficient combustion.
[F23L 5/04] by induction of air for combustion, e.g. using steam jet -- The code F23L 5/04 covers methods of supplying combustion air by induced flow, for example using a steam jet to draw in air before ignition. It pertains to blast-producing devices that generate the air stream prior to the fire, applicable to burners, furnaces and similar combustion apparatus.
[F23L 7/00] Supplying non-combustible liquids or gases, other than air, to the fire, e.g. oxygen, steam -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Supplying non-combustible liquids or gases, other than air, to the fire, e.g. oxygen, steam.
[F23L 7/002] Supplying water -- F23L 7/002 covers devices or methods that deliver water-a non-combustible liquid-to a fire or combustion chamber, typically for cooling, steam generation, or controlling combustion. It resides within mechanical engineering, specifically the sub-category for supplying non-combustible liquids or gases other than air to combustion apparatus.
[F23L 7/005] Evaporated water; Steam -- The CPC subclass F23L 7/005 refers to the use of evaporated water or steam as a non-combustible fluid supplied to a combustion device. It covers methods of delivering steam to the fire to control temperature, improve combustion efficiency, or provide auxiliary heating.
[F23L 7/007] Supplying oxygen or oxygen-enriched air -- The subclass F23L 7/007 covers methods and devices for delivering oxygen or oxygen-enriched air to a combustion system, such as furnaces or burners, to enhance or control the fire. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the broader category of supplying non-combustible gases to combustion devices.
[F23L 9/00] Passages or apertures for delivering secondary air for completing combustion of fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Passages or apertures for delivering secondary air for completing combustion of fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Baffles or deflectors in air inlets -> F23M9/02
[F23L 9/02] by discharging the air above the fire -- The F23L 9/02 subclass covers arrangements that deliver secondary air to a combustion chamber by discharging the air above the fire, using passages or apertures that direct the flow upward. This technique improves combustion efficiency by completing fuel oxidation with non-combustible gases.
[F23L 9/04] by discharging the air beyond the fire, i.e. nearer the smoke outlet -- This subgroup covers designs where secondary air is introduced downstream of the primary flame, through passages or apertures that discharge air near the exhaust or smoke outlet to complete fuel combustion. It is used in burners and furnaces to improve efficiency and reduce emissions.
[F23L 9/06] by discharging the air into the fire bed -- F23L 9/06 relates to the delivery of secondary air directly into the fire bed of a combustion device. It covers passages, apertures or other means that discharge air onto the fuel bed to complete combustion, typically used in furnaces, boilers or similar apparatus.
[F23L 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass.
[F23M] CASINGS, LININGS, WALLS OR DOORS SPECIALLY ADAPTED FOR COMBUSTION CHAMBERS, e.g. FIREBRIDGES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The term "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Ash: means any solid combustion residues, for example remaining in the fuel bed or suspended in the flue gases - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame - Combustion: means the direct combination of oxygen gas, e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of the apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Firebridge: a low wall separating the fuel bed from adjacent flue gas passages in apparatus for combustion of solid fuel, for example in reverberatory furnaces or fire-tube boilers - Flue gases: any gaseous products of combustion - Grate: a perforated surface, e.g. a grid, which supports or delimits a bed of burning fuel and serves to supply primary air
[F23M 11/00] Safety arrangements -- The classification covers safety devices and measures incorporated in firebridges, casings, linings, walls or doors of combustion chambers to prevent hazardous conditions, including pressure relief, fire detection and protective shut-off mechanisms.
[F23M 11/02] Preventing emission of flames or hot gases, or admission of air, through working or charging apertures -- F23M 11/02 relates to safety measures that stop flames, hot gases or external air from passing through the working or charging openings of combustion-chamber casings, linings, walls or doors such as firebridges. It covers seals, shutters, cooling devices and similar arrangements that control aperture flow.
[F23M 11/04] Means for supervising combustion, e.g. windows -- F23M 11/04 covers devices that allow observation or monitoring of the combustion process, such as viewing windows or other transparent sections installed in casings, linings, walls or doors of combustion chambers. These features enable operators to supervise fire conditions while maintaining safety and structural integrity.
[F23M 11/042] Viewing ports of windows -- F23M 11/042 covers viewing ports or windows incorporated into casings, linings, walls or doors that are specially adapted for combustion chambers, such as firebridges. The classification addresses the design, mounting and sealing of these openings to maintain safety and containment while allowing observation of the combustion process.
[F23M 11/045] by observing the flame -- F23M 11/045 covers safety measures that rely on visual observation of the flame in combustion chambers, such as flame monitoring devices or operators checking flame characteristics to detect abnormal conditions.
[F23M 11/047] by observing the flue gas  controlling combustion using gas detectors  F23N5/003 -- This subclass covers safety systems that regulate a combustion process by continuously monitoring the composition of flue gases with gas detectors and adjusting fuel or air flow accordingly. It applies to devices that use real-time gas analysis to prevent overheating, incomplete combustion or hazardous emissions in boilers, furnaces and similar equipment.
[F23M 20/00] Details of combustion chambers, not otherwise provided for -- The F23M 20/00 subclass covers specific design features, materials and construction details of combustion chambers that are not covered elsewhere, such as liners, cooling systems, seals and door arrangements tailored to improve performance, durability or safety. It pertains to mechanical engineering of fire-bridges, casings and walls specially adapted for combustion apparatus.
[F23M 20/005] Noise absorbing means -- The subclass F23M 20/005 covers noise-absorbing means incorporated in casings, linings, walls or doors that are specially adapted for combustion chambers such as firebridges. It includes acoustic panels, liners, coatings and other structures designed to dampen sound generated during combustion, when the specific details are not covered by any other more specific subclass.
[F23M 2700/00] Constructional details of combustion chambers -- The CPC group F23M 2700/00 covers the structural design of combustion-chamber components, including firebridges, casings, linings and doors, detailing materials, shapes, mounting methods and other constructional features that influence the chamber's performance and durability. It applies to mechanical-engineering devices where precise configuration of these elements is critical for efficient combustion.
[F23M 2700/005] Structures of combustion chambers or smoke ducts -- F23M 2700/005 addresses the constructional details of combustion chambers and smoke ducts, including casings, linings, walls, doors and firebridges specially adapted for such chambers. It focuses on the structural design aspects of these components within mechanical engineering.
[F23M 2700/0053] Bricks for combustion chamber walls -- The F23M 2700/0053 entry covers bricks specifically designed for the walls, linings or doors of combustion chambers such as firebridges. It includes constructional details of these bricks, focusing on materials, dimensions and mounting methods that enable them to withstand high-temperature combustion environments.
[F23M 2700/0056] Bricks for water tube combustion chamber walls -- F23M 2700/0056 covers bricks used as lining material in water-tube combustion chamber walls. These bricks are designed to withstand high temperatures and corrosive environments within the chamber, providing structural support and thermal insulation.
[F23M 2700/007] Automatic fire extinguishing devices -- F23M 2700/007 covers automatic fire extinguishing devices that are built into the casings, linings, walls or doors of combustion chambers. The subclass defines constructional features that enable the system to detect a fire and suppress it without manual operation, improving safety of the chamber's enclosure.
[F23M 2700/008] Preventing outwards emission of flames or hot gases -- F23M 2700/008 covers features of combustion-chamber casings, linings, walls or doors that are designed to stop flames or hot gases from escaping outward. The classification includes designs such as firebridges, shields, or internal geometry that redirect or contain combustion products, enhancing safety and efficiency of burners, furnaces and similar equipment.
[F23M 2900/00] Special features of, or arrangements for combustion chambers -- This subclass covers special features and arrangements of combustion chambers, such as specially designed casings, linings, walls, doors and firebridges that enhance safety, heat resistance, or flow control.
[F23M 2900/05001] Preventing corrosion by using special lining materials or other techniques -- This CPC subclass covers methods or devices that protect combustion-chamber casings, linings, walls or doors from corrosion by using special lining materials or other anti-corrosion techniques. It is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, casings specially adapted for combustion chambers.
[F23M 2900/05002] Means for accommodate thermal expansion of the wall liner -- The entry F23M 2900/05002 covers devices or design features that allow the wall liner of a combustion-chamber casing, lining, wall or door to adjust for thermal expansion during operation, ensuring proper fit and preventing stress or leakage. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and special arrangements for combustion chambers.
[F23M 2900/05003] Details of manufacturing specially adapted for combustion chambers -- The code F23M 2900/05003 pertains to manufacturing methods and material treatments specifically devised for producing casings, linings, walls or doors that are adapted to combustion chambers such as firebridges. It covers special fabrication steps, surface preparations and assembly techniques that meet the unique thermal and mechanical demands of these components.
[F23M 2900/05004] Special materials for walls or lining -- The entry F23M 2900/05004 covers the use of specialized or advanced materials in the construction of walls, linings or doors that are designed specifically for combustion chambers, such as firebridges. It includes descriptions of material properties, coatings, composites or heat-resistant treatments intended to improve durability, thermal protection or corrosion resistance under high-temperature combustion conditions.
[F23M 2900/05005] Sealing means between wall tiles or panels -- The CPC code F23M 2900/05005 falls under Mechanical Engineering > Combustion Apparatus > Casings, Linings, Walls or Doors Specially Adapted for Combustion Chambers, focusing on special features or arrangements for combustion chambers. It specifically covers sealing means placed between wall tiles or panels to prevent gas leakage and improve the integrity of the combustion-chamber enclosure.
[F23M 2900/05021] Wall blocks adapted for burner openings -- F23M 2900/05021 covers wall blocks specifically shaped to occupy burner openings in combustion chambers, such as firebridges, forming part of casings, linings, walls or doors. The blocks provide a tailored interface between the fuel source and the chamber to control flow and protect surrounding structures.
[F23M 2900/09061] Moving baffles, e.g. rotating baffles, for creating vortices -- Moving or rotating baffles installed in the walls or linings of a combustion chamber to generate vortices that improve fuel-air mixing, flame stability and heat distribution. The feature is classified under mechanical engineering for special combustion-chamber arrangements.
[F23M 2900/09062] Tube-shaped baffles confining the flame -- The classification refers to tube-shaped baffles installed inside a combustion chamber to limit and guide the flame. These cylindrical members form part of the chamber's lining or wall, helping to confine the fire, improve flame control and protect surrounding structures from direct heat exposure.
[F23M 2900/11021] Means for avoiding accidental fires in rooms where the combustion device is located -- This subgroup covers safety features incorporated in casings, linings, walls or doors of combustion chambers that are designed to prevent accidental fires spreading in the room where a combustion device is installed, such as fire-blocking barriers, temperature-activated shut-off devices or protective seals.
[F23M 2900/11041] Means for observing or monitoring flames using photoelectric devices, e.g. phototransistors -- This subclass covers devices that detect flame characteristics by converting light emitted from a flame into electrical signals using photoelectric components such as phototransistors. It is employed for flame monitoring, safety and control in combustion chambers and related equipment.
[F23M 2900/13001] Energy recovery by fuel cells arranged in the combustion plant -- The F23M 2900/13001 classification covers combustion-chamber systems that integrate fuel-cell units to capture and convert residual heat or chemical energy into electrical power, enhancing overall plant efficiency by recovering energy that would otherwise be lost. This entry applies to designs where the fuel cells are incorporated as part of the combustion apparatus.
[F23M 2900/13002] Energy recovery by heat storage elements arranged in the combustion chamber -- The CPC entry F23M 2900/13002 relates to arrangements that place heat-storage elements inside a combustion chamber to capture and reuse thermal energy, improving the efficiency of the combustion process. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically casings, linings, walls or doors specially adapted for combustion chambers.
[F23M 2900/13003] Energy recovery by thermoelectric elements, e.g. by Peltier/Seebeck effect, arranged in the combustion plant -- F23M 2900/13003 covers the use of thermoelectric devices such as Peltier or Seebeck elements to recover waste heat from combustion chambers by converting it directly into electrical energy. The classification applies to casings, linings, walls or doors of combustion apparatus where these elements are incorporated as part of the chamber design.
[F23M 2900/13004] Energy recovery by thermo-photo-voltaic [TPV] elements arranged in the combustion plant -- The code F23M 2900/13004 refers to the use of thermo-photo-voltaic (TPV) elements within a combustion plant to capture waste heat from the chamber and convert it directly into electricity, thereby improving overall energy efficiency of the furnace or boiler system.
[F23M 3/00] Firebridges -- Firebridges are structural members that span across the interior of combustion chambers, linking opposing walls or casings to reinforce the furnace and maintain alignment under thermal stress. They also serve as conduits for heat distribution, helping to even temperature gradients and improve overall efficiency.
[F23M 3/02] modified for circulation of fluids, e.g. air, steam, water -- F23M 3/02 refers to firebridges-casings, linings, walls or doors for combustion chambers-that are specially adapted to permit the circulation of fluids such as air, steam or water, enhancing heat transfer or cooling within the apparatus.
[F23M 3/04] for delivery of gas, e.g. air, steam -- F23M 3/04 covers firebridges that are specially adapted for combustion chambers and are used to deliver gas such as air or steam to the combustion apparatus.
[F23M 3/06] into or towards fire -- This subgroup addresses components of firebridges that direct or channel the flame or hot gases into the combustion chamber, such as inlet sections or openings designed to direct fire toward the main burner.
[F23M 3/08] away from fire, e.g. towards smoke outlet -- This subclass covers firebridge components that are designed to channel gases or flames away from the combustion zone toward a smoke outlet, such as walls, linings or doors in a combustion chamber that direct flow outward rather than into the fire.
[F23M 3/10] transversely -- The CPC subclass F23M 3/10 covers firebridges situated transversely, i.e., across the width of a combustion chamber, used to protect walls or liners from heat and flame. It pertains to devices that extend horizontally between opposite walls to shield structural components in boilers, furnaces, or similar equipment.
[F23M 3/12] characterised by shape or construction -- The CPC subclass F23M 3/12 covers firebridges whose patent claims are defined primarily by their particular shape or construction features, distinguishing them from other firebridge designs based on geometry, form or structural arrangement. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and specifically casings or walls adapted for combustion chambers.
[F23M 3/14] with apertures for passage of combustion products -- The subclass F23M 3/14 covers firebridges-structural elements that connect or span combustion chambers-and specifically those provided with openings that allow combustion gases to flow through them. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and casings, linings, walls or doors specially adapted for combustion chambers.
[F23M 3/16] built-up in sections, e.g. using bars or blocks -- This classification covers firebridges that are assembled from multiple parts such as bars or blocks, forming a built-up structure. It applies to casings, linings, walls or doors designed for combustion chambers that are fabricated in sections rather than as a single piece.
[F23M 3/18] double -- F23M 3/18 designates firebridges that employ a double configuration, i.e., two parallel or concentric walls, liners or doors surrounding a combustion chamber. The dual layers provide enhanced heat insulation, structural strength and a sealed passage for gases, improving durability and safety of the combustion apparatus.
[F23M 3/20] comprising loose refractory material, wholly or in part -- F23M 3/20 designates firebridges - components such as casings, linings, walls or doors for combustion chambers - that are formed wholly or in part from loose refractory material. This subclass groups inventions where the refractory filler is not fixed, allowing it to settle or be replaced during service. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion apparatus.
[F23M 3/22] movable -- The F23M 3/22 entry covers firebridges that are movable, including adjustable or detachable casings, linings, walls, or doors used in combustion chambers. It relates to devices that can be repositioned to aid maintenance, control flame spread, or adapt to varying operating conditions.
[F23M 5/00] Casings -- F23M 5/00 covers casings, linings, walls or doors designed specifically for combustion chambers such as firebridges, including protective structures that contain or isolate the flame and heat. It is used for inventions relating to the construction, material selection and mounting of these components within burners, furnaces, boilers or similar devices.
[F23M 5/02] characterised by the shape of the bricks or blocks used -- F23M 5/02 covers casings, linings, walls or doors for combustion chambers that are distinguished by the shape of the bricks or blocks used, such as specially formed fire-bricks that define the enclosure's geometry.
[F23M 5/025] specially adapted for burner openings -- F23M 5/025 covers casings, liners or walls that are specially adapted to accommodate burner openings in combustion chambers. It includes designs that integrate firebridges, sealing arrangements or reinforced sections to ensure safe, efficient passage of flames and gases while maintaining structural integrity. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering for combustion apparatus, focusing on protection and access around burner ports.
[F23M 5/04] Supports for linings -- F23M 5/04 covers devices that support or retain linings, walls, casings or doors specially adapted for combustion chambers, such as fire-bridges. The subclass pertains to mechanical-engineering solutions that secure inner protective layers within combustion apparatuses. It is a narrow segment of the CPC hierarchy under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → casings, linings, walls or doors.
[F23M 5/06] Crowns or roofs for combustion chambers -- F23M 5/06 covers crowns or roofs for combustion chambers, the components that form the upper surface or closing cover of a firebox or furnace. These parts are designed to withstand high temperatures, protect surrounding structures, and facilitate heat transfer into the combustion space.
[F23M 5/08] Cooling thereof -- The subclass F23M 5/08 relates to cooling measures applied to casings, linings, walls or doors that are specially adapted for combustion chambers such as firebridges. It covers designs, materials or systems that dissipate heat from these components to protect them and maintain efficient operation.
[F23M 5/085] using air or other gas as the cooling medium -- F23M 5/085 pertains to casings, linings, walls or doors of combustion chambers that are cooled by a flow of air or other gases. It covers designs in which the cooling medium is directed over or through the casing, such as firebridges, to dissipate heat and protect the structure.
[F23M 7/00] Doors -- F23M 7/00 pertains to doors specially designed for combustion chambers, including firebridges and other sealing or access doors that separate or control the flow of gases within burners, boilers and similar apparatus. The classification covers structural features, mounting methods and sealing arrangements that enable safe, efficient operation of combustion devices.
[F23M 7/02] Frames therefor -- The classification F23M 7/02 deals with structural frames that support casings, linings, walls or doors specifically adapted for combustion chambers such as firebridges. It covers the design, construction and mounting of these frames within mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus.
[F23M 7/04] Cooling doors or door frames -- F23M 7/04 relates to cooling doors or door frames employed in combustion apparatus such as firebridges. These components are designed to control heat flow and protect surrounding structures by providing a cooled barrier for the combustion chamber.
[F23M 9/00] Baffles or deflectors for air or combustion products -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Baffles or deflectors for air or combustion products structurally associated with burners -> F23D
[F23M 9/003] in flue gas ducts -- F23M 9/003 covers baffles or deflectors installed in flue gas ducts of combustion apparatus to direct airflow or combustion gases. The class relates to casings, linings, walls or doors specially adapted for combustion chambers such as firebridges within mechanical engineering.
[F23M 9/006] Backflow diverters -- Backflow diverters are devices installed in combustion-chamber casings or firebridges that prevent reverse flow of gases, flames or combustion products. They act as baffles or deflectors to redirect airflow and exhaust, improving combustion control and safety within the chamber.
[F23M 9/02] in air inlets -- This subclass covers devices that form or shape air inlets for combustion chambers, including baffles, deflectors or specially adapted openings that guide airflow into the firebox. It is part of the mechanical-engineering group for combustion apparatus casings and linings.
[F23M 9/04] with air supply passages in the baffle or shield -- This subclass relates to baffles or deflectors fitted in combustion chambers that contain built-in passages for supplying air through the baffle or shield, improving air-fuel mixing or flame stability in devices such as firebridges.
[F23M 9/06] in fire-boxes -- F23M 9/06 pertains to baffles or deflectors that are positioned inside fire-boxes, which are casings or linings specially adapted for combustion chambers such as firebridges. The classification is applied to mechanical-engineering inventions that control the flow of air or combustion gases within the fire-box.
[F23M 9/08] Helical or twisted baffles or deflectors -- This CPC subclass covers baffles or deflectors that are shaped in a helix or twisted form and are mounted in casings, linings, walls or doors of combustion chambers to guide airflow or combustion gases. The design aims to improve mixing, heat transfer or reduce emissions within firebridges and related equipment.
[F23M 9/10] Baffles or deflectors formed as tubes, e.g. in water-tube boilers -- F23M 9/10 covers baffles or deflectors that are formed as tubular elements, such as those used in water-tube boilers to direct airflow or combustion gases. These components are part of casings, linings, walls or doors specially adapted for combustion chambers.
[F23N] REGULATING OR CONTROLLING COMBUSTION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means and methods for regulating or controlling combustion, including details of the accessories and components for carrying out said controls such as for example fuel valves, detectors, sensors, timers independently of the type of the fuel which is burned.  Safety controls.  Pneumatic, electronic, electrical or hydraulic circuits used for controlling or regulating combustion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control devices specially adapted for fluidised-bed combustion apparatus -> F23C10/28 - Condition reponsive controls for regulating combustion in domestic stoves with open fires for solid fuel -> F24B1/187  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The term "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Ash: means any solid combustion residues, for example remaining in the fuel bed or suspended in the flue gases - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame - Combustion: means the direct combination of oxygen gas, e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of the apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Flue gases: any gaseous products of combustion - Grate: a perforated surface, e.g. a grid, which supports or delimits a bed of burning fuel and serves to supply primary air - Firebridge: a low wall separating the fuel bed from adjacent flue gas passages in apparatus for combustion of solid fuel, for example in reverberatory furnaces or fire-tube boilers
[F23N 1/00] Regulating fuel supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Regulating fuel supply.
[F23N 1/002] using electronic means  F23N1/04  -  F23N1/10  take precedence
[F23N 1/005] using electrical or electromechanical means  F23N1/04  -  F23N1/10  take precedence -- F23N 1/005 covers the regulation of fuel supply in combustion apparatus by means of electrical or electromechanical devices, such as solenoid valves, sensors, or electronic controllers. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion, and is subordinate to the broader categories F23N 1/04-F23N 1/10.
[F23N 1/007] using mechanical means  F23N1/04  -  F23N1/10  take precedence -- F23N 1/007 covers combustion apparatus in which the fuel supply is regulated by mechanical means, such as throttles, valves, or mechanical governors, that override other control methods. It lies under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → regulating or controlling combustion → regulating fuel supply.
[F23N 1/02] conjointly with air supply -- F23N 1/02 covers combustion apparatus in which the fuel feed is coordinated with the air supply, enabling simultaneous control of both streams to optimise the combustion process. It applies to burners, boilers and similar devices where fuel and air are regulated together for stable and efficient operation.
[F23N 1/022] using electronic means -- F23N 1/022 pertains to combustion apparatus in which the fuel supply is regulated by electronic means. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the subgroup for controlling combustion, and addresses electronic control of fuel flow.
[F23N 1/025] using electrical or electromechanical means -- The subclass covers techniques for controlling the fuel supply to combustion devices through electrical or electromechanical means, including sensors, actuators, and electronic controllers that adjust flow rates or timing to maintain desired combustion conditions. This applies to systems such as engines, boilers, furnaces and other apparatus where precise fuel regulation is achieved by electronic or electromechanical intervention.
[F23N 1/027] using mechanical means -- F23N 1/027 covers mechanical arrangements for controlling the fuel flow to a combustion apparatus. It includes devices such as valves, pistons, cams or linkage systems that adjust the amount of fuel delivered in response to operating conditions, without reliance on electronic or chemical control methods.
[F23N 1/04] conjointly with air supply and with draught
[F23N 1/042] using electronic means -- This subgroup covers methods and devices that use electronic circuitry, sensors or digital control to adjust the fuel amount supplied to a combustion apparatus, allowing precise regulation of the combustion process. It includes electronic fuel injectors, microprocessor-based control units and related electronic monitoring systems.
[F23N 1/045] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 1/045 classifies methods for regulating the fuel supply of combustion apparatus by electrical or electromechanical means such as solenoid or motor-driven valves, electronic throttles or controller units. It covers inventions that adjust fuel flow to achieve desired combustion conditions.
[F23N 1/047] using mechanical means -- F23N 1/047 covers methods and devices that regulate the fuel supply to a combustion system by employing mechanical components such as valves, pistons, or linkages. The mechanical means adjust the amount or flow rate of fuel in response to control signals or operating conditions.
[F23N 1/06] conjointly with draught -- F23N 1/06 covers combustion systems in which the fuel-supply regulation is combined with draught control, linking fuel flow management to the adjustment of airflow or exhaust. It pertains to devices or methods that coordinate these two functions to maintain stable combustion conditions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 1/062] using electronic means -- F23N 1/062 covers electronic techniques for controlling the fuel flow to combustion devices. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the regulation of combustion, and addresses how electronic sensors, controllers or actuators adjust fuel-supply to improve efficiency, emissions or safety.
[F23N 1/065] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 1/065 covers methods and devices that regulate the fuel supply of combustion apparatus by using electrical or electromechanical mechanisms, such as sensors, actuators, and control circuitry to adjust fuel flow in response to operating conditions.
[F23N 1/067] using mechanical means -- This subclass covers devices that adjust the fuel flow to a combustion system by using mechanical components such as valves, pistons or levers, rather than electronic control. It applies to engines, furnaces and other burners where the fuel supply is modulated mechanically.
[F23N 1/08] conjointly with another medium, e.g. boiler water -- The F23N 1/08 subclass covers methods and devices for regulating fuel supply by mixing the fuel with a secondary medium, such as boiler water, to control combustion. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the regulation of combustion processes, and addresses combined-fuel-and-water delivery systems.
[F23N 1/082] using electronic means -- F23N 1/082 falls under Mechanical Engineering, Combustion Apparatus, Regulating or Controlling Combustion and covers the use of electronic devices to control the fuel feed to a combustion system. [F23N 1/085] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 1/085 covers methods and devices that regulate the fuel supply to a combustion apparatus by employing electrical or electromechanical means, such as sensors, actuators, or electronic controllers, to adjust flow rate, pressure, or mixture in response to operating conditions.
[F23N 1/087] using mechanical means -- F23N 1/087 covers the use of mechanical devices to control the fuel supply in combustion apparatus, such as valves, pumps or linkages that adjust flow rate. This subclass falls under regulation of combustion within mechanical engineering.
[F23N 1/10] and with air supply or draught -- F23N 1/10 pertains to combustion apparatus in which the fuel feed is coordinated with the air or draught supply to control the combustion process, covering mechanisms that adjust both fuel and airflow together for regulated burning.

[F23N 2005/165] with ultrasonic means -- This subgroup covers heating, steam-generation or hot-water equipment that incorporates ultrasonic transducers or other ultrasonic means, for example to improve heat transfer, promote cavitation, or assist cleaning within the system.
[F23N 2005/181] using detectors sensitive to rate of flow of air -- The CPC subclass F23N 2005/181 covers inventions that employ sensors responsive to the rate of airflow, such as hot-wire, thermal, or pressure-difference detectors, for monitoring, controlling or optimizing ventilation, climate-control or safety functions.
[F23N 2005/182] Air flow switch -- F23N 2005/182 covers devices that detect or control airflow using a switch mechanism, typically employed in ventilation, heating, or cooling systems to activate or deactivate equipment based on the presence or speed of air.
[F23N 2005/185] using detectors sensitive to rate of flow of fuel -- This subgroup of CPC relates to combustion engines where a detector that measures fuel flow rate is employed to control or monitor engine performance, fuel consumption, or emissions. It includes devices such as flow meters, sensors, and related control systems that respond to instantaneous fuel flow.
[F23N 2221/00] Pretreatment or prehandling -- F23N 2221/00 covers technologies used to prepare or handle a fuel or combustion medium before it enters a combustion apparatus. This includes pretreatment steps such as cleaning, drying, mixing, size reduction or removal of contaminants that improve ignition, efficiency or emissions control in mechanical engineering combustion systems.

It applies to mechanical-engineering solutions that move, feed or distribute substances before they enter the combustion zone.
[F23N 2221/04] Preheating liquid fuel -- The subclass F23N 2221/04 relates to systems and methods that raise the temperature of liquid fuel before it enters a combustion chamber, improving ignition, flame stability and overall efficiency. It includes heaters, heat exchangers or other pretreatment devices and the control arrangements used to deliver the warmed fuel to burners, boilers or similar combustion apparatus.
[F23N 2221/06] Preheating gaseous fuel -- The classification covers devices or processes that raise the temperature of a gas used as fuel before it enters a combustion chamber, improving ignition and efficiency. It includes heating elements, heat exchangers, or integrated systems that preheat natural gas, propane, hydrogen, or similar fuels as part of combustion control.
[F23N 2221/08] Preheating the air -- F23N 2221/08 covers methods and devices for preheating the air supplied to a furnace or burner, a step that raises combustion efficiency and can lower emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion, and the pretreatment or prehandling sub-category.
[F23N 2221/10] Analysing fuel properties, e.g. density, calorific -- The entry F23N 2221/10 covers techniques for determining fuel characteristics such as density and calorific value, which are used to assess and optimise combustion processes. It relates to the pretreatment or prehandling stage where fuel properties are measured before regulation or control of the combustion apparatus.
[F23N 2221/12] Recycling exhaust gases -- F23N 2221/12 covers devices and methods that treat or reuse exhaust gases from combustion processes, including the collection, cleaning, heat recovery, and recirculation of these gases back into the system. This sub-group falls under pretreatment or prehandling within the regulation of combustion in mechanical engineering.

It includes methods for analyzing sensor data such as temperature, pressure or flame-position signals and applying algorithms that adjust fuel flow, air supply or ignition timing to improve efficiency, safety or emissions. This subclass falls within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2223/02] Multiplex transmission -- F23N 2223/02 covers methods and devices that transmit several control signals simultaneously to a combustion system by means of multiplexing, allowing coordinated regulation of parameters such as fuel flow, temperature or pressure. The classification is used for signal-processing techniques that combine or separate multiple data streams in combustion control.

It includes devices and methods for storing, retrieving, and updating data that regulate or monitor combustion processes, such as temperature, pressure or fuel-flow information.
[F23N 2223/06] Sampling -- F23N 2223/06 covers the sampling of sensor signals used to monitor and regulate combustion processes. It includes methods for acquiring discrete measurements of temperature, pressure, or flame characteristics to enable real-time control of burners and other combustion equipment.
[F23N 2223/08] Microprocessor -- F23N 2223/08 refers to the use of microprocessors in the signal-processing functions that regulate or control combustion apparatus. This subclass lies within mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → regulating or controlling combustion → signal processing.
[F23N 2223/10] Correlation -- The F23N 2223/10 entry covers techniques that analyze and correlate signals from sensors in combustion systems to improve regulation and control of the flame. [F23N 2223/42] Function generator -- F23N 2223/42 concerns function generators that produce electrical signals for use in controlling or regulating combustion processes. [F23N 2225/22] heat losses
[F23N 2225/24] indicated in an amount of money -- F23N 2225/24 covers devices or methods that present combustion-related measurements as monetary values, such as indicating fuel cost, energy price or expense per unit of use, to help users monitor and control combustion costs in real time.
