[F23N 2227/00] Ignition or checking -- F23N 2227/00 deals with devices or methods for igniting or checking combustion processes, typically in furnaces or boilers. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and regulating or controlling combustion. The classification covers ignition sources, spark generators, and monitoring systems used to initiate or verify combustion.
[F23N 2227/02] Starting or ignition cycles -- The F23N 2227/02 class covers technologies that manage the start-up or ignition phases of combustion devices, including the timing, sequencing and control of ignition events. It relates to systems that regulate how a burner or engine initiates combustion to ensure reliable and safe operation.
[F23N 2227/04] Prepurge -- A prepurge is a preparatory stage before ignition in which the combustion chamber is flushed with a fresh or inert gas to remove residual gases, moisture, or unburned fuel. This clearing helps achieve reliable ignition, lower emissions and prevents flashback or explosion during the start-up of burners and other combustion devices.
[F23N 2227/06] Postpurge -- The entry covers mechanisms that carry out a post-purge after the main combustion phase, venting or flushing the combustion chamber to remove remaining gases, extinguish any residual flame and cool the system before shutdown. Such features are applied in burners, furnaces and other combustion devices to enhance safety, reduce emissions and prepare the apparatus for the next operating cycle.
[F23N 2227/08] Hold fire apparatus -- A hold fire apparatus is a component that temporarily prevents ignition in a combustion system, allowing the engine or furnace to be stopped or started safely. It monitors and controls the firing sequence, holding the fire until a command or safety condition is met, thereby protecting equipment and enhancing operational control.
[F23N 2227/10] Sequential burner running -- A sequential burner running is a control method in which individual burners are ignited one after another in a predetermined order rather than all at once, allowing the combustion system to maintain stable flame conditions, improve efficiency, and reduce emissions during start-up or load changes. This technique falls under the regulation of ignition and checking functions within combustion apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F23N 2227/12] Burner simulation or checking -- F23N 2227/12 covers methods and devices for simulating or testing the performance of burners, particularly focusing on ignition and preliminary checks. It includes computational models, laboratory rigs, and diagnostic procedures used to verify that a combustion apparatus ignites correctly and operates within desired parameters.
[F23N 2227/14] Flame simulation -- The code F23N 2227/14 covers methods and systems for modeling and visualizing flame behavior in combustion devices, including computational or experimental techniques that predict flame propagation, temperature distribution and emissions. It is applied to patents dealing with design, safety assessment or control of ignition systems in engines, burners and similar apparatus.
[F23N 2227/16] Checking components, e.g. electronic -- The code covers devices or methods that monitor and verify the proper functioning of ignition or checking systems in combustion apparatus, typically using electronic sensors, circuits or diagnostic components. It relates to electronic checking components that ensure reliable ignition performance.
[F23N 2227/18] Applying test signals, e.g. periodic -- The class covers techniques for introducing test signals, such as periodic pulses, into the ignition or monitoring circuits of combustion devices to verify correct firing, detect malfunctions, and calibrate performance. These signals are applied during maintenance or diagnostic procedures to ensure reliable operation.
[F23N 2227/20] Calibrating devices -- The CPC subclass F23N 2227/20 deals with the calibration of devices used for ignition or checking in combustion systems. It includes procedures and equipment for adjusting and verifying spark plugs, ignition sensors, flame detectors and related control units to ensure accurate performance.
[F23N 2227/22] Pilot burners -- Pilot burners are small auxiliary flames that provide a reliable source of ignition for the main combustion process in burners, furnaces or boilers and can also serve for flame monitoring or safety checks. They help ensure stable start-up and continuous operation of larger combustion apparatus.
[F23N 2227/24] the pilot burner not burning continuously -- This CPC subclass covers pilot-burners that do not burn continuously, addressing ignition or checking functions in combustion apparatus regulation. It is situated in the mechanical engineering hierarchy under combustion apparatus and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2227/26] comprising two or more distinct pilot burners -- This subclass covers combustion apparatus whose ignition system incorporates two or more separate pilot burners, each capable of lighting the main flame or providing a check function. The distinct pilot burners operate independently to enhance reliability and control of the combustion process.
[F23N 2227/28] Ignition circuits -- F23N 2227/28 relates to ignition circuits that generate the spark or signal required to start combustion in mechanical devices. It falls under combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion, specifically the ignition or checking function, and covers electrical or electronic circuit designs used for this purpose.
[F23N 2227/30] for pilot burners -- This CPC subclass covers devices and methods that provide ignition or monitoring functions for pilot burners, the small auxiliary burners used to start and sustain combustion in larger heating or combustion systems. It includes systems that detect pilot-flame presence, generate a spark or flame, and control the pilot's operation to ensure reliable and safe startup of the main combustion apparatus.
[F23N 2227/32] Igniting for a predetermined number of cycles -- This subclass covers methods or devices that ignite a combustion system for a set number of cycles, typically to control start-up or test sequences. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and ignition control.
[F23N 2227/34] Continuously applied ignition cycles -- F23N 2227/34 covers combustion devices in which the ignition is supplied continuously-applied rather than in discrete bursts, providing a steady flame or heating output. The classification applies to systems that maintain ignition throughout operation to regulate or control the combustion process.
[F23N 2227/36] Spark ignition, e.g. by means of a high voltage -- This CPC subgroup covers devices that generate a spark to ignite a combustible mixture, typically using a high-voltage discharge. It includes spark plugs, ignition coils and related control circuits used in internal-combustion engines or burners. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion, and specifically ignition or checking.
[F23N 2227/38] Electrical resistance ignition -- The entry F23N 2227/38 refers to ignition or checking methods that use an electrically heated resistive element to initiate combustion. It covers devices in which a current passes through a resistor to generate heat sufficient to ignite a fuel-air mixture, typically employed in burners and other combustion apparatus.
[F23N 2227/40] Catalytic ignition -- F23N 2227/40 - catalytic ignition describes systems that initiate combustion in mechanical engineering apparatus by using a catalyst to trigger the flame, providing a means of regulating or controlling the combustion process.
[F23N 2227/42] Ceramic glow ignition -- A ceramic glow ignition is a device that uses a ceramic heating element to generate a reliable, low-energy spark for initiating combustion in burners or engines. It provides rapid, consistent ignition and can withstand high temperatures and harsh environments, making it suited for controlled combustion systems such as industrial furnaces and vehicle engines.
[F23N 2229/00] Flame sensors -- F23N 2229/00 designates flame sensors, devices that detect the presence or characteristics of a flame to enable monitoring and control of combustion processes. These sensors are used in burners, furnaces and other combustion equipment to ensure safe, efficient operation and to trigger control actions such as fuel cut-off or ignition timing.
[F23N 2229/02] Pilot flame sensors -- F23N 2229/02 covers pilot flame sensors, which are devices that detect the presence or absence of a flame in a pilot burner and provide a signal to regulate or shut off fuel supply. These sensors are typically used in combustion apparatus to ensure safe and efficient operation by monitoring the pilot flame continuously.
[F23N 2229/04] sensitive to the colour of flames -- The CPC subclass F23N 2229/04 covers flame sensors that detect the colour of a flame. These sensors are employed in combustion apparatus to monitor and control the burning process by responding to variations in flame hue, allowing precise regulation of fuel and air supply.
[F23N 2229/06] with periodical shutters -- The F23N 2229/06 subclass relates to flame sensors that incorporate periodical shutters, which cyclically interrupt the optical path to detect changes in flame radiation and improve reliability. Such shutters modulate the sensor's exposure, enabling more accurate control of combustion processes.
[F23N 2229/08] detecting flame flicker -- This CPC subclass covers devices that monitor variations in a flame's intensity to detect flicker, used to assess combustion stability or control. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion, flame sensors.
[F23N 2229/10] comprising application of periodical fuel flow fluctuations -- The CPC subclass F23N 2229/10 covers flame sensors that incorporate periodic fuel-flow fluctuations as a means of detecting or monitoring the flame. The technique modulates the fuel supply at regular intervals and analyzes the resulting signal changes to assess combustion stability or presence. It is applied in combustion control systems for engines, boilers and industrial burners.
[F23N 2229/12] with flame rectification current detecting means -- The F23N 2229/12 subclass covers flame-sensor devices that determine flame presence or characteristics by measuring the rectified electrical current that flows through the flame, i.e., ion-current detection. These sensors are used to regulate or control combustion processes by providing a reliable flame-presence signal.
[F23N 2229/14] using two or more different types of flame sensor -- This subclass covers combustion control systems that employ two or more distinct flame-sensor types together, such as optical and ionization sensors, to improve detection reliability, response speed, or safety in flames monitoring. It includes configurations, combinations, and methods that integrate the different sensor technologies for coordinated operation.
[F23N 2229/16] using two or more of the same types of flame sensor -- F23N 2229/16 relates to the use of two or more flame sensors of the same type in a combustion system to detect, monitor or control the flame. It falls within the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2229/18] Flame sensor cooling means -- This subclass covers devices or methods that provide cooling for flame sensors used in combustion control systems. It includes coolant circulation, heat-sinking structures, and thermal protection materials designed to prevent sensor overheating and ensure reliable operation.
[F23N 2229/20] Camera viewing -- F23N 2229/20 covers camera-based flame detection systems used to monitor and control combustion processes. The classification includes devices that capture visual images of flames to assess combustion stability, detect flame failure, or adjust the fuel-air mixture. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion, and flame sensors.
[F23N 2229/22] the sensor's sensitivity being variable -- Variable-sensitivity flame sensors detect combustion by adjusting their response to changes in flame intensity, allowing accurate monitoring over a wide range of fire conditions. The sensor's output adapts to differing heat fluxes, improving control of burners and safety systems in engines and industrial furnaces.
[F23N 2231/00] Fail safe -- F23N 2231/00 - Fail safe covers devices or methods that ensure a combustion system shuts down or enters a safe mode when a fault or hazardous condition is detected. It includes automatic shutdown mechanisms, backup controls, and protective features that prevent uncontrolled combustion or explosion.
[F23N 2231/02] using electric energy accumulators -- F23N 2231/02 concerns the use of electric energy accumulators-batteries, supercapacitors or similar storage devices-to regulate or control combustion, providing a fail-safe power source for ignition, monitoring or safety functions in combustion apparatus.
[F23N 2231/04] for electrical power failures -- F23N 2231/04 covers systems that maintain safe control of combustion apparatus when the electrical supply is interrupted. It pertains to fail-safe mechanisms that automatically regulate or shut down the burner to prevent hazardous conditions.
[F23N 2231/06] for flame failures -- The CPC subclass F23N 2231/06 covers safety mechanisms that respond to loss of flame in combustion devices. It includes systems that detect flame interruption and automatically inhibit fuel supply or extinguish the fire to prevent uncontrolled operation. These fail-safe features are used in boilers, furnaces and other combustion apparatus.
[F23N 2231/08] for pilot flame failures -- This CPC subgroup covers safety measures that act when a pilot flame in a combustion system is lost. It includes devices or control strategies that automatically shut down the main flame, switch to a backup ignition source, or otherwise prevent unsafe operation after pilot-flame failure.
[F23N 2231/10] for component failures -- F23N 2231/10 covers safety features in combustion equipment that detect or respond to failures of individual components, ensuring the system can be shut down or operated in a protected mode. It relates to fail-safe mechanisms that regulate or control combustion when a part such as a valve, sensor or fuel line malfunctions.
[F23N 2231/12] for ignition failures -- F23N 2231/12 covers safety mechanisms that act when ignition fails in combustion equipment. It includes devices that automatically shut down, isolate fuel or otherwise control the combustion process to prevent hazardous operation of burners, furnaces or similar apparatus.
[F23N 2231/14] for earthquakes -- This CPC subclass F23N 2231/14 covers mechanisms that automatically shut down or isolate combustion equipment when an earthquake is detected, providing a fail-safe response to prevent uncontrolled fires or explosions. It is positioned under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2231/16] using melting materials or shape memory alloys -- F23N 2231/16 covers fail-safe systems for combustion apparatus that regulate or shut down the flame by employing materials that melt at a predetermined temperature or shape-memory alloys that change shape when heated, thereby interrupting fuel flow or combustion when safety limits are exceeded.
[F23N 2231/18] Detecting fluid leaks -- F23N 2231/18 covers systems and methods that detect fluid leaks in combustion apparatus as part of fail-safe measures for regulating or controlling combustion. The classification includes sensors, monitoring devices and signal-processing techniques that identify leaks of fuel, air or exhaust gases to maintain safe and efficient operation.
[F23N 2231/20] Warning devices -- A warning device for a combustion apparatus that operates as a fail-safe component, detecting abnormal conditions and providing an alert to prevent uncontrolled combustion. It is classified under CPC F23N 2231/20, within mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2231/22] using warning lamps -- F23N 2231/22 covers the use of warning lamps as part of a fail-safe system in combustion apparatus, providing visual alerts when abnormal or hazardous conditions are detected. The lamps help regulate or control combustion by signalling the need for corrective action or shutdown.

[F23N 2231/26] for clogging air inlet -- The CPC code F23N 2231/26 covers a fail-safe measure that deliberately clogs the air inlet of a combustion device to stop or control the flame in the event of a malfunction. [F23N 2231/28] preventing flash-back or blow-back -- The CPC subclass F23N 2231/28 deals with mechanisms that prevent flash-back or blow-back in combustion apparatus, ensuring safe operation by interrupting reverse flame propagation. [F23N 2233/08] with variable speed -- Ventilators that are employed to regulate or control combustion processes and whose operating speed can be varied. [F23N 2235/00] Valves, nozzles or pumps -- This CPC subgroup covers devices that regulate the flow of fuel, air or other working fluids within combustion apparatus, such as valves, nozzles and pumps used to start, stop, throttle or otherwise control the combustion process.

[F23N 2235/04] in stacks -- Placement of valves, nozzles or pumps within exhaust stacks to regulate or control the flow of combustion gases, typically to adjust pressure, temperature or composition of flue gases as they exit a combustion system. [F23N 2235/08] used with heat exchanges -- It covers valves, nozzles or pumps that are employed in combustion apparatus to regulate or control fluid flow when used with heat exchangers, directing heat transfer and maintaining system temperature.
[F23N 2235/10] power assisted, e.g. using electric motors -- F23N 2235/10 classifies combustion control components such as valves, nozzles or pumps that are powered by an auxiliary source, for example an electric motor, to assist their operation. Such assemblies may be arranged in series or parallel to control fuel, air or exhaust flow in burners, boilers or engines, improving response and safety.
[F23N 2235/20] Membrane valves -- Membrane valves are a type of valve used in combustion equipment to regulate flow of gases or fluids. They employ a flexible membrane that separates the operating medium from the actuator, providing sealed control without leakage. Typically applied where precise modulation of combustion air or fuel is required.
[F23N 2235/22] cooperating with magnets -- F23N 2235/22 covers combustion-engine components such as valves, nozzles or pumps whose operation is aided or controlled by magnetic elements, including magnetic actuators or fields that influence fuel, air or exhaust flow for regulating combustion. [F23N 2235/30] Pumps -- The CPC entry F23N 2235/30 covers pumps that are incorporated in combustion apparatus to regulate or control the combustion process. It relates to mechanical-engineering solutions such as valves, nozzles or pumps used for delivering fuel, air or combustion gases.
[F23N 2237/00] Controlling -- The CPC subclass F23N 2237/00 covers devices, methods and control systems that regulate combustion processes, such as adjusting fuel-air ratios, flame temperature or ignition timing to maintain optimal performance. It is situated under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Regulating or Controlling Combustion and pertains to any technology that monitors and modifies combustion parameters.
[F23N 2237/02] two or more burners -- The code F23N 2237/02 covers combustion devices that employ two or more burners for regulating or controlling the combustion process. [F23N 2237/04] at two or more different localities -- The subclass F23N 2237/04 covers systems or methods that regulate combustion simultaneously at two or more separate locations within a device, such as multiple burners, zones or chambers, allowing coordinated control of flame, temperature or fuel-air mixture across the different areas. This enables more precise management of the overall combustion process.
[F23N 2237/06] two predeterming temperatures, e.g. day-night -- The F23N 2237/06 class covers combustion-control devices that operate with two preset temperature limits, such as separate day and night settings, to modulate fuel flow or flame intensity. It pertains to systems that switch between these predetermined temperatures to maintain desired combustion conditions.
[F23N 2237/08] two or more different types of fuel simultaneously -- This subclass covers combustion devices that simultaneously utilize two or more distinct fuel types, including the mechanisms and control strategies for feeding, mixing, and regulating each fuel during operation. It addresses the technical features that enable coordinated combustion of heterogeneous fuels in a single system.
[F23N 2237/10] High or low fire -- F23N 2237/10 relates to systems that control the intensity of a fire, allowing the combustion process to be set to a high or low level. Such control is used in furnaces, boilers or other combustion apparatus to achieve a desired temperature or efficiency.
[F23N 2237/12] catalytic burners -- F23N 2237/12 covers catalytic burners, devices that use a catalyst to facilitate combustion of fuel, allowing stable flames at lower temperatures and improving efficiency. These burners fall under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the sub-category of regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2237/14] burners with gasification or vaporizer elements -- The code F23N 2237/14 covers burners that integrate gasification or vaporizer units to improve fuel conversion before combustion. Such devices are used to regulate or control the combustion process by producing a premixed or partially gasified fuel stream, enhancing efficiency and emissions control.
[F23N 2237/16] secondary air -- F23N 2237/16, titled secondary air, concerns the introduction of additional air into a combustion process to enhance mixing, control flame temperature and improve efficiency or emissions. It is applied in combustion apparatus as a means of regulating or controlling combustion by adjusting the amount or distribution of secondary airflow.
[F23N 2237/18] fluidized bed burners -- Fluidized-bed burners are combustion devices that suspend solid particles in a flowing gas, creating a fluidized medium that enhances mixing of fuel and air for efficient, uniform heat release. In the CPC subclass F23N 2237/18 they are addressed as a means of regulating or controlling combustion by adjusting parameters such as fluidization velocity, fuel feed rate, or burner geometry.
[F23N 2237/20] one or more bypass conduits -- The code F23N 2237/20 covers combustion devices that include one or more bypass conduits used to divert part of the flow for regulating or controlling the combustion process. Such bypass passages allow adjustment of fuel-air mixture, temperature or pressure to improve efficiency or emissions. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and the sub-category of regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2237/22] water injection -- Water injection is a method used in combustion apparatus to regulate flame temperature and reduce emissions by introducing a controlled amount of water into the combustion zone. The injected water absorbs heat, moderates the combustion process, and can improve fuel efficiency while limiting pollutants such as nitrogen oxides. This technique falls under the CPC classification F23N 2237/22 for controlling combustion.
[F23N 2237/24] height of burner -- This CPC subclass defines features related to the height of the burner in devices that regulate or control combustion. It is used to classify inventions that adjust burner position to influence flame characteristics and efficiency.
[F23N 2237/26] oxygen-air ratio -- F23N 2237/26 covers devices or methods for adjusting the oxygen-air mixture supplied to a combustion system to achieve a desired ratio. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the regulation of combustion, and addresses how the proportion of oxygen to air is controlled to influence flame characteristics, efficiency, and emissions.
[F23N 2237/28] oxygen as pure oxydant -- The CPC entry F23N 2237/28 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Regulating or Controlling Combustion → Controlling and covers devices or methods that use pure oxygen as the oxidant to regulate combustion processes, improving flame stability, temperature control or efficiency.
[F23N 2237/30] matrix burners -- Matrix burners are combustion devices that use a porous or structured matrix to mix fuel and air and regulate flame characteristics, enabling precise control of heat-output and emissions. They fall under CPC class F23N 2237/30, covering controlling mechanisms for combustion apparatus within mechanical engineering.
[F23N 2237/32] Nox -- This CPC entry covers technologies for monitoring and limiting nitrogen-oxide (NOx) emissions in combustion devices. [F23N 2239/00] Fuels -- F23N 2239/00 covers inventions relating to the type, composition or handling of fuels used in combustion apparatus where the focus is on regulating or controlling the combustion process, including methods for fuel selection, preparation, delivery or treatment to achieve desired performance or emissions.
[F23N 2239/02] Solid fuels -- F23N 2239/02 relates to solid fuels employed in combustion apparatus where the combustion process is regulated or controlled. [F23N 2241/10] Generating vapour -- F23N 2241/10 covers devices or methods that generate vapour as part of a combustion system, such as steam-producing boilers or vapor generators used to control or regulate the combustion process. [F23N 2241/11] Torches -- F23N 2241/11 covers torches used to direct a flame for heating, cutting or welding, where the device includes means for regulating or controlling the combustion process. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 2241/12] Stack-torches -- Stack-torches are devices mounted on the outlet of a combustion stack or chimney that ignite and burn residual gases and vapors in the exhaust stream. By providing a secondary flame they improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions of unburned hydrocarbons and carbon monoxide, and help regulate the temperature and flow of the stack gases.

[F23N 2241/16] Spectrometer burners -- F23N 2241/16 designates spectrometer burners, which combine a combustion source with an integrated spectroscopic system to analyse flame emissions. [F23N 2241/18] Incinerating apparatus -- F23N 2241/18 relates to incinerating apparatus, i.e., machines designed to thermally destroy waste or other materials by combustion. [F23N 2900/05181] Controlling air to fuel ratio by using a single differential pressure detector -- This subgroup covers systems that regulate the air-fuel mixture in combustion devices by monitoring a single differential pressure detector and using its signal to adjust fuel injection or air flow for optimal combustion.
[F23N 3/00] Regulating air supply or draught -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Regulating air supply or draught.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Regulating air supply conjointly with fuel supply -> F23N1/00
[F23N 3/002] using electronic means  F23N3/02  -  F23N3/08  take precedence -- The entry F23N 3/002 covers combustion apparatus where the air supply or draught is regulated by electronic means. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus and the control of combustion, and takes precedence over the broader F23N 3/02-F23N 3/08 categories.
[F23N 3/005] using electrical or electromechanical means  F23N3/02  -  F23N3/08  take precedence -- F23N 3/005 covers the use of electrical or electromechanical devices to adjust the air flow or draught in combustion equipment. [F23N 3/007] using mechanical means  F23N3/02  -  F23N3/08  take precedence
[F23N 3/02] Regulating draught by direct pressure operation of single valves or dampers -- This subclass covers systems that control the draft in combustion equipment by directly operating a single valve or damper with pressure, adjusting the air supply to maintain the desired airflow. It applies to furnaces, boilers and similar devices where a single-actuator mechanism regulates the draught.
[F23N 3/04] by operation of single valves or dampers by temperature sensitive elements -- F23N 3/04 covers combustion-air regulation that uses a single valve or damper operated by temperature-sensitive elements such as bimetallic strips or thermostatic devices. The system adjusts draught automatically in response to temperature changes to control the burning process.
[F23N 3/042] using electronic means -- F23N 3/042 pertains to the use of electronic devices for controlling the air supply or draught in combustion apparatus such as furnaces, boilers or burners. It includes electronic sensors, controllers and actuators that adjust airflow to achieve desired combustion conditions.
[F23N 3/045] using electrical or electromechanical means -- This subgroup includes methods and devices that regulate the air supply or draught to a combustion chamber by using electrical or electromechanical components such as solenoid valves, variable-speed fans, or actuators, providing precise control of airflow. It applies to furnaces, boilers, and internal-combustion engines to improve combustion efficiency and reduce emissions.
[F23N 3/047] using mechanical means -- The code F23N 3/047 covers the use of mechanical devices-such as dampers, valves, actuators or other moving parts-to regulate the supply of air or draught in combustion apparatus, thereby controlling the combustion process. It applies to systems that adjust airflow through purely mechanical means rather than electronic or pneumatic control.
[F23N 3/06] by conjoint operation of two or more valves or dampers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Regulating air supply or draught by power-assisted systems -> F23N3/08
[F23N 3/065] using mechanical means -- F23N 3/065 classifies inventions that control the air supply or draught in combustion apparatus through purely mechanical devices such as valves, levers, pistons or gears, rather than electronic or hydraulic means. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 3/08] by power-assisted systems -- This subclass covers combustion devices where the air supply or draft is adjusted by powered means such as electric, hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, motors or other power-assisted systems rather than manually. The control may be used to optimise flame stability, efficiency or emissions in furnaces, boilers and similar apparatus.
[F23N 3/082] using electronic means -- F23N 3/082 relates to combustion equipment where the air or draught is regulated by electronic means, such as sensors, processors or actuators that automatically adjust airflow to optimise flame stability, efficiency or emissions. It is used for electronic control of burners, furnaces and similar devices.
[F23N 3/085] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 3/085 covers the regulation of air supply or draught in combustion devices by means of electrical or electromechanical components. It includes the use of sensors, actuators, solenoid valves, variable-speed fans and electronic control units to adjust airflow, pressure or fuel-air mixture for optimal combustion. This subclass groups any system that relies on electric power or electromechanical motion to achieve the desired combustion control.
[F23N 3/087] using mechanical means -- F23N 3/087 covers the use of mechanical devices-such as dampers, valves, fans, or actuators-to adjust the air supply or draught in combustion apparatus, thereby regulating the burning process. It includes systems that physically vary airflow rates rather than relying on electronic or chemical control methods.
[F23N 5/00] Systems for controlling combustion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems for controlling combustion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Regulating fuel supply -> F23N1/00 - Regulating air supply or draught -> F23N3/00
[F23N 5/003] using detectors sensitive to combustion gas properties  F23N5/02 ,  F23N5/18  -  F23N5/26  take precedence -- The entry covers systems that control combustion by employing detectors that respond to the physical or chemical properties of the combustion gases, such as temperature, composition, or radiation. These sensors provide feedback to adjust fuel, air, or other parameters, enabling precise regulation of the combustion process.
[F23N 5/006] the detector being sensitive to oxygen -- F23N 5/006 covers combustion-control systems that incorporate detectors whose operation depends on measuring oxygen levels. It includes sensors, probes or monitoring units that detect oxygen concentration in the combustion zone and use that information to adjust fuel or air flow for optimal combustion. This subclass pertains to devices whose sensitivity to oxygen is the primary control principle.
[F23N 5/02] using devices responsive to thermal changes or to thermal expansion of a medium -- F23N 5/02 covers systems for controlling combustion that use devices responding to temperature changes or to the thermal expansion of a medium, such as thermostats, bimetallic strips or other heat-sensitive actuators, to adjust fuel, air or flame parameters and maintain desired operating conditions.
[F23N 5/022] using electronic means  F23N5/04  -  F23N5/14  take precedence -- F23N 5/022 covers systems for regulating combustion that employ electronic means, including electronic sensors, controllers and actuators that monitor and adjust fuel-air mixture, temperature or flame characteristics. It builds on the broader categories F23N5/04 to F23N5/14, which are taken as prior art.
[F23N 5/025] using electrical or electromechanical means  F23N5/04  -  F23N5/14  take precedence -- F23N 5/025 covers systems that regulate or control combustion by means of electrical or electromechanical devices, such as sensors, actuators, controllers and related circuitry used to adjust fuel flow, air supply or flame characteristics in burners, furnaces and similar apparatus.
[F23N 5/027] using mechanical means  F23N5/04  -  F23N5/14  take precedence -- F23N 5/027 covers mechanical devices used to regulate or control combustion processes, such as dampers, valves, actuators and other moving components that adjust fuel-air mix, flame position, temperature or exhaust flow. It falls under mechanical engineering - combustion apparatus - regulating or controlling combustion.
[F23N 5/04] using bimetallic elements -- The F23N 5/04 subclass covers combustion-control systems that employ bimetallic elements, such as strips or coils, to sense temperature changes and actuate valves, regulators or safety devices. These bimetallic components bend or deform with heat, providing a simple mechanical means of adjusting fuel flow, ignition timing or airflow to maintain desired combustion conditions.
[F23N 5/042] using electronic means -- This subclass covers combustion control systems that employ electronic devices such as sensors, processors and actuators to monitor and adjust fuel, air and temperature parameters, enabling automatic regulation of combustion processes. It includes electronic controllers, microprocessor-based systems and signal processing for optimizing efficiency and emissions.
[F23N 5/045] using electrical or electromechanical means -- The entry covers combustion-control systems that employ electrical or electromechanical techniques, such as sensors, actuators, electronic controllers or motor-driven devices, to monitor and adjust fuel-air mixtures, ignition timing, flame temperature or other parameters for efficient and safe operation of burners, furnaces, boilers and related equipment.
[F23N 5/047] using mechanical means -- F23N 5/047 covers systems that regulate or control combustion by mechanical means such as valves, actuators, or moving components that adjust fuel, air, or heat flow. It belongs to the mechanical engineering section under combustion apparatus and addresses mechanical approaches to maintaining desired combustion conditions.
[F23N 5/06] using bellows -- This CPC subclass covers the use of bellows to regulate or control combustion in mechanical devices. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion apparatus → regulating or controlling combustion and pertains to systems that adjust airflow or fuel delivery with bellows to maintain desired flame conditions.
[F23N 5/062] using electronic means -- F23N 5/062 covers combustion control systems that employ electronic devices such as sensors, microprocessors or digital controllers to monitor and adjust fuel, air or ignition parameters. It is situated in the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Apparatus → Regulating or Controlling Combustion and addresses electronic means for regulating combustion efficiency, emissions or safety.
[F23N 5/065] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 5/065 covers systems that regulate combustion by employing electrical or electromechanical techniques, such as sensors, actuators and electronic controllers, to adjust fuel flow, air supply or ignition timing. These methods replace or supplement purely mechanical control mechanisms to achieve precise and responsive combustion management.
[F23N 5/067] using mechanical means -- F23N 5/067 covers systems for controlling combustion that use mechanical means. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion. The classification includes devices such as mechanical actuators, linkages, or gear-driven valves to adjust fuel or air flow.
[F23N 5/08] using light-sensitive elements -- F23N 5/08 covers combustion control systems that employ light-sensitive elements such as photodiodes or photocells to detect flame characteristics and modulate fuel or air flow for efficient and safe operation. These devices use optical signals to regulate the combustion process in furnaces, boilers, burners and similar equipment.
[F23N 5/082] using electronic means -- F23N 5/082 relates to combustion-control systems that employ electronic devices such as sensors, microprocessors, and electronic actuators to monitor and adjust fuel, air-flow, temperature or other parameters. The classification covers electronic feedback loops, digital controllers and other electronic means used to regulate or optimise combustion processes.
[F23N 5/085] using electrical or electromechanical means -- The F23N 5/085 subclass covers systems that regulate or control combustion by employing electrical or electromechanical means, such as sensors, actuators, control circuits or drive mechanisms that adjust fuel flow, air supply, ignition timing or flame temperature.
[F23N 5/087] using mechanical means -- F23N 5/087 relates to combustion control systems that use mechanical devices-such as valves, actuators, linkages or gear mechanisms-to adjust fuel or air flow, flame size or temperature, providing regulation through physical movement rather than electronic or chemical means.
[F23N 5/10] using thermocouples -- F23N 5/10 refers to combustion-control systems that employ thermocouples to sense temperature and adjust fuel or air flow. The classification covers devices and methods that use the thermocouple's voltage output to regulate combustion conditions, ensuring efficient and safe operation of burners, furnaces or engines.
[F23N 5/102] using electronic means -- F23N 5/102 includes the use of electronic devices, sensors and control circuitry to regulate the operation of combustion apparatus, such as adjusting fuel-air ratios, temperature or ignition timing. The classification covers electronic monitoring, feedback loops and software-based strategies for precise combustion control.
[F23N 5/105] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 5/105 relates to methods and devices that regulate or control combustion by employing electrical or electromechanical techniques, such as sensors, actuators and control circuits, to adjust fuel flow, air supply or ignition timing. It is used in combustion apparatus where electronic or electromechanical means are applied to achieve stable and efficient operation.
[F23N 5/107] using mechanical means, e.g. safety valves -- F23N 5/107 covers mechanical devices that regulate or control combustion, such as safety valves and similar mechanical means. These devices are used to maintain desired combustion conditions in engines, furnaces or other combustion apparatus.
[F23N 5/12] using ionisation-sensitive elements, i.e. flame rods -- The F23N 5/12 subclass covers combustion-control systems that employ ionisation-sensitive devices such as flame rods to detect flame presence and adjust fuel or air flow. These ionisation sensors generate an electrical signal proportional to the combustion intensity, enabling precise regulation of the burn process. This technology is used in boilers, furnaces and other heating equipment to improve efficiency and safety.
[F23N 5/123] using electronic means -- F23N 5/123 covers systems that regulate or control combustion by employing electronic devices such as sensors, processors, and actuators. [F23N 5/126] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 5/126 covers systems that regulate combustion by using electrical or electromechanical means, such as sensors, controllers, actuators, or electronic circuits to adjust fuel flow, airflow, or flame characteristics. [F23N 5/14] using thermo-sensitive resistors -- This subclass pertains to combustion control systems that employ temperature-sensitive resistors, such as thermistors, to monitor temperature and adjust fuel or air flow for optimal burning. The resistors generate electrical signals that inform control circuits to regulate the combustion process.
[F23N 5/143] using electronic means -- F23N 5/143 covers systems that regulate or control combustion by employing electronic devices such as sensors, microprocessors or digital controllers. [F23N 5/146] using electrical or electromechanical means -- This subclass concerns combustion control systems that employ electrical or electromechanical techniques, such as electronic sensors, actuators, variable-frequency drives or feedback controllers, to adjust fuel flow, air-fuel ratio, flame temperature or exhaust recirculation for optimal combustion performance.
[F23N 5/16] using noise-sensitive detectors -- F23N 5/16 covers methods that employ noise-sensitive detectors, such as acoustic sensors, to monitor and adjust combustion processes. By detecting sound characteristics of flames or exhaust, the system can regulate fuel input, air flow, or ignition timing to maintain optimal combustion and reduce emissions.
[F23N 5/18] using detectors sensitive to rate of flow of air or fuel -- F23N 5/18 covers combustion control systems that employ detectors responsive to the flow rate of air or fuel, using the measured flow to adjust fuel delivery or air supply for optimal combustion. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion.

It includes electronic feedback loops, digital controllers and software-based regulation of burners, furnaces and engines.
[F23N 5/187] using electrical or electromechanical means -- F23N 5/187 refers to systems for controlling combustion that use electrical or electromechanical means. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, regulating or controlling combustion. These technologies employ sensors, actuators or electronic controllers to adjust fuel flow, air supply or ignition timing for efficient and safe combustion.
[F23N 5/188] using mechanical means -- The F23N 5/188 subclass covers systems that regulate or control combustion by means of mechanical devices such as valves, actuators, pistons, linkages or other moving parts, rather than electronic or chemical methods. It includes mechanical arrangements for adjusting fuel flow, air supply or flame position to maintain desired combustion conditions.
[F23N 5/20] with a time program acting through electrical means, e.g. using time-delay relays -- A combustion control system that operates according to a preset time program implemented by electrical components, such as time-delay relays, to regulate the combustion process. This falls under the CPC subclass for mechanical engineering, specifically combustion apparatus with timed electrical control.

[F23N 5/22] with a time program acting through mechanical means, e.g. using cams -- This subclass covers combustion control systems that employ a predetermined timing sequence implemented mechanically, for example cam-driven devices that adjust fuel or air supply. It relates to mechanisms that coordinate ignition or flame regulation without electronic or hydraulic actuation.
[F23N 5/24] Preventing development of abnormal or undesired conditions, i.e. safety arrangements -- This CPC subclass covers safety measures that prevent abnormal or undesired conditions in combustion systems, such as overheating, flame-out, pressure spikes or unstable operation. It includes devices, controls and procedures that detect and intervene to maintain safe, stable combustion in burners, furnaces, boilers or engines.

It relates specifically to methods that intervene physically in the combustion process rather than using electronic, hydraulic or chemical means.
[F23N 5/26] Details -- F23N 5/26 concerns the detailed components and features of systems that regulate or control combustion processes, such as sensors, actuators, and feedback mechanisms used to maintain optimal flame conditions. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the subclass for combustion apparatus and the broader group for regulating or controlling combustion. This code captures the technical specifics of how combustion is monitored and adjusted in various equipment.
[F23N 5/265] using electronic means -- This subclass covers combustion control systems that use electronic devices to monitor, regulate, or adjust the combustion process, such as sensors, microprocessors, and feedback loops. It includes electronic ignition, temperature sensors, and digital controllers employed to improve efficiency and lower emissions.
[F23Q] IGNITION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or methods specially adapted for providing the heat, spark or flame required to start combustion of a fuel. Such apparatus can itself be a combustion apparatus (e.g. a cigarette lighter or a pilot flame) that is provided with ignition arrangements of its own.  Mechanical igniters.  Lighter containing fuel e.g. for cigarettes.  Igniters using electrically-produced sparks.  Make-and break ignition.  Incandescent ignition.  Pilot flame igniters.  Arrangement of catalytic igniters.  Devices for effecting ignition from a remote location that is remote from the combustion apparatus to be ignited, e.g. for combustion apparatus that is difficult or dangerous to access.  Testing of ignition installations.  Extinguishing-devices, e.g. for blowing-out or snuffing candle flames.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Match receptacles or boxes -> A24F27/00 - Devices for igniting matches -> A24F29/00 - Hand fire extinguishers -> A62C11/00 , A62C25/00 - Chemical contact igniters, e.g. catalytic igniters -> C06C9/00 - Flints (composition, manufacture) -> C06C15/00 - Matches -> C06F5/00 - Fire-lighters -> C10L11/00 - Candles with incorporated means (e.g. impregnated wicks) for self-extinguishing -> C11C5/00 - Ignition arrangements for blasting -> F42D1/04  **Glossary:** - Retention flame: A small flame that is kept alight in order to maintain the uninterrupted operation of a more powerful burner. - Pilot flame: A small flame that is lit or kept alight in order to provide ignition to a more powerful burner. - Fire lighter: An element that either consists of combustible material or absorbs liquid fuel, and is intended for use in a fire-place or the like for simplifying the ignition of solid fuel.
[F23Q 1/00] Mechanical igniters -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lighters containing fuel -> F23Q2/00 - Matches; manufacture of matches -> C06F
[F23Q 1/02] using friction or shock effects -- This subclass covers devices that create a spark for ignition through friction or shock effects, such as flint-wheel or percussion igniters used in internal-combustion engines, stoves, fireworks and similar apparatus.
[F23Q 1/04] on a part moved by the fuel-controlling member, e.g. by a tap on a gas cooker -- F23Q 1/04 pertains to mechanical igniters that are operated by a component moved by the fuel-controlling member, such as the tap of a gas cooker. The classification covers ignition devices placed on parts whose position is adjusted by the fuel-control mechanism.
[F23Q 1/06] Portable igniters -- Portable igniters are handheld mechanical devices used to initiate combustion in engines, furnaces or other fire-producing equipment, typically employing a spark-or flame-generating mechanism that can be manually triggered. They are classified under CPC section F23Q for mechanical igniters within combustion apparatus.
[F23Q 11/00] Arrangement of catalytic igniters -- It covers the spatial configuration and placement of catalytic igniters within combustion devices, focusing on how igniters are integrated to initiate fuel burning. The classification is used for patents describing designs that improve ignition reliability, efficiency or control in engines, burners or other mechanical systems.
[F23Q 11/04] at the burner
[F23Q 11/06] remote from the burner, e.g. on the chimney of a lamp -- This subclass deals with combustion devices that place a catalytic igniter away from the main burner, for example mounted on a lamp's chimney, to start the flame. The remote arrangement provides reliable ignition while keeping the igniter isolated from direct combustion.
[F23Q 11/08] on a part moved by the fuel controlling member -- The F23Q 11/08 subclass covers the positioning of catalytic igniters on a component that is displaced by a fuel-controlling element, such as a valve or injection piston, within combustion devices. It deals with how the igniter's location is linked to the moving fuel-regulating part.
[F23Q 11/10] and moving out of the flame after ignition
[F23Q 13/00] Igniters not otherwise provided for -- F23Q 13/00 covers igniters that are not specifically classified elsewhere, comprising devices that generate a spark, flame, or heat to start combustion in engines, furnaces, or similar equipment. These may include electric, pyrotechnic, laser or chemical igniters used in internal combustion engines, gas turbines, burners and related systems where a specialized ignition source is required but not otherwise defined.
[F23Q 13/005] using light, e.g. sunlight or laser -- F23Q 13/005 covers igniters that create a spark by using light, such as concentrated sunlight or laser radiation, instead of electrical or chemical means. It falls under igniters not otherwise provided for in combustion apparatus within mechanical engineering.
[F23Q 13/02] using gas burners, e.g. gas pokers -- The F23Q 13/02 subclass covers igniters that employ gas-burner flames, such as handheld gas pokers, to start combustion in heating or cooking apparatus where no other igniter type is specified. It includes devices that generate a flame directly from a gas source for ignition purposes.
[F23Q 13/04] using portable burners, e.g. torches, fire pots -- The F23Q 13/04 class covers portable ignition devices such as torches or fire pots used to ignite a flame in situ. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, ignition, and igniters not otherwise provided for. The entry pertains to the use of handheld burners for starting fires or heating tasks.
[F23Q 2/00] Lighters containing fuel, e.g. for cigarettes -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lighters with incandescent ignition but without fuel -> F23Q7/00 , F23Q7/14 - Lighters using solar energy -> F23Q13/005
[F23Q 2007/002] with sensing means -- F23Q 2007/002 covers ventilation, heating or air-conditioning apparatus that incorporates sensors to monitor conditions such as temperature, pressure, smoke, gas or humidity and use the sensed data to control the device's operation, trigger alarms or adjust performance.
[F23Q 2007/004] Manufacturing or assembling methods -- F23Q 2007/004 is a CPC subclass that covers methods for manufacturing or assembling, focusing on processes and operational steps used to produce or put together articles, particularly in textiles and garments. It includes techniques for forming, joining, shaping, finishing and automation of component assembly, grouping patents that disclose such production procedures.
[F23Q 2007/005] pressure sensors -- F23Q 2007/005 covers pressure sensors, devices that convert fluid or gas pressure into measurable electrical signals. The classification includes transducers, strain-gauge, capacitive, piezoelectric and resonant types, as well as integrated modules and their applications in monitoring, control and safety systems.
[F23Q 2007/007] ion current sensors -- F23Q 2007/007 covers devices that detect or measure ion currents, often employed in electrochemical, plasma or related applications. The entry includes sensors that convert ion flow into electrical signals for monitoring, control or analysis purposes.
[F23Q 2007/008] temperature sensors -- This CPC group covers devices that sense temperature for monitoring or control, such as thermocouples, resistance temperature detectors, infrared and semiconductor sensors, and their associated signal-conditioning elements used in heating, ventilation, air-conditioning and fire-protection installations.
[F23Q 2/02] Lighters with liquid fuel -- This subclass covers lighter devices that employ liquid fuel as the combustible source, including designs for cigarette lighters where the fuel is stored in a liquid reservoir and delivered to a flame-producing component.
[F23Q 2/04] with cerium-iron alloy and wick -- This subclass covers lighters that use a cerium-iron alloy as the ignition material together with a wick to deliver fuel. The alloy acts as a pyrophoric component that ignites when exposed to air, and the wick transports liquid fuel to the flame. It applies to portable hand-held lighters for cigarettes and similar uses.
[F23Q 2/06] with friction wheel -- This class covers lighters that contain fuel, for example for cigarettes, and ignite by means of a friction wheel that generates a spark.
[F23Q 2/08] with ignition by spring action of the cover -- A lighter that holds its own fuel, such as a cigarette lighter, ignites when the cover is pressed, using a spring-loaded mechanism that drives the spark or flame source. This sub-class covers devices where the cover's motion directly triggers ignition, rather than a separate button or flint operation. It falls under mechanical engineering combustion apparatus for ignition.
[F23Q 2/10] with other friction member -- F23Q 2/10 covers fuel-containing lighters in which the ignition is produced by a friction element other than the usual striking wheel, such as an additional friction member or alternative surface that generates spark when rubbed. This subclass refines the broader category of combustion apparatus for cigarette lighters.
[F23Q 21/00] Devices for effecting ignition from a remote location -- F23Q 21/00 covers devices that enable ignition of a combustion apparatus from a distance, such as remote starter systems, wireless ignition controls, and trigger mechanisms that initiate flame or spark without direct manual contact. These devices may use electrical, pneumatic, or wireless signals to activate the igniter.
[F23Q 2/12] with cerium-iron alloy without wick -- The CPC subclass F23Q 2/12 pertains to disposable or refillable lighters that ignite using a cerium-iron alloy as the fuel source and do not employ a wick, covering devices such as cigarette lighters that rely on this alloy-based ignition system.
[F23Q 2/14] with cerium-iron alloy and torch ignited by striking or pushing -- This subclass covers fuel-containing lighters that ignite by striking or pushing a cerium-iron alloy to generate a spark for a torch flame, commonly used in cigarette lighters. The alloy acts as a pyrophoric material that ignites the fuel on demand.
[F23Q 2/16] Lighters with gaseous fuel, e.g. the gas being stored in liquid phase -- A lighter that uses a gaseous fuel, such as butane, which is stored in a liquid phase within the device. The classification applies to ignition devices in the mechanical-engineering field where the fuel changes from liquid to gas during use to produce a flame. This entry groups lighters designed for cigarettes and similar applications that rely on pressurised liquid fuel.
[F23Q 2/161] with friction wheel -- F23Q 2/161 classifies lighters that ignite fuel by means of a friction wheel, typically used for cigarettes. The device uses a rotating wheel and a flint to generate a spark that ignites the fuel, falling under mechanical engineering combustion apparatus ignition.
[F23Q 2/162] with non-adjustable gas flame  if electrically ignited  F23Q2/28 -- F23Q 2/162 covers lighters that contain a fuel source such as for cigarettes and produce a gas flame that cannot be adjusted, where the flame is generated by an electrical ignition mechanism. The classification applies to devices in which the flame size is fixed and the ignition is achieved electrically rather than mechanically. This entry falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and ignition.
[F23Q 2/163] Burners (gas valves) -- F23Q 2/163 covers burners that incorporate gas valves to control the flow of fuel in ignition devices such as lighters. The classification pertains to mechanical-engineering components that regulate combustible gas release for lighting or heating purposes and falls under the combustion apparatus and ignition sub-categories.
[F23Q 2/164] Arrangements for preventing undesired ignition -- F23Q 2/164 covers designs and safety mechanisms in fuel-filled lighters, such as cigarette lighters, that prevent accidental ignition. It includes features like child-resistant switches, flame-arresting valves, and control systems that inhibit ignition unless a specific action is performed.
[F23Q 2/165] with more than one flame -- A lighter that holds fuel for cigarettes and produces two or more flames simultaneously, classified under the mechanical-engineering subclass for combustion apparatus and ignition devices. This entry covers designs where multiple flames are generated to improve lighting performance or user convenience.
[F23Q 2/167] with adjustable flame -- F23Q 2/167 covers lighters that contain a fuel source for lighting cigarettes or similar, featuring a mechanism to vary the flame size or intensity. The classification is used for devices where the user can adjust the flame output to suit different lighting needs.
[F23Q 2/173] Valves therefor -- The CPC entry F23Q 2/173 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, ignition for lighters containing fuel such as cigarettes and specifies the valves that regulate fuel delivery. It includes design, placement and operation of valve components used to control the flow of lighter fluid.
[F23Q 2/18] Lighters with solid fuel -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers devices that generate a flame by burning solid fuel, typically used as hand-operated lighters for cigarettes or other applications. It includes designs where the solid fuel is stored in a reservoir or cartridge and ignited by a mechanical trigger.
[F23Q 2/20] with cerium-iron alloy and friction wheel -- A subcategory of ignition devices for fuel-containing lighters that employ a friction wheel made of a cerium-iron alloy to generate the spark for ignition.
[F23Q 2/22] with cerium-iron alloy and tinder -- This subclass covers fuel-containing lighters, such as cigarette lighters, that use a cerium-iron alloy together with a tinder material to start ignition. The alloy acts as a pyrophoric catalyst while the tinder provides the combustible medium for flame generation.
[F23Q 2/24] ignition pills or strips with inflammable parts -- F23Q 2/24 covers ignition pills or strips that incorporate a flammable component and are used in fuel-containing lighters such as those for cigarettes. These devices provide a ready source of ignition by releasing heat when activated, facilitating the lighting of combustible materials.
[F23Q 2/26] combined with liquid fuel lighters -- The subclass concerns lighters that use liquid fuel and integrate the ignition mechanism in a single device, typically for lighting cigarettes. It includes designs where the fuel reservoir and flame-producing system are combined, describing the construction and operation of liquid-fuel cigarette lighters.
[F23Q 2/28] Lighters characterised by electrical ignition of the fuel -- The classification covers lighters that use an electrical spark to ignite the fuel rather than a mechanical flame wheel. It includes devices where ignition is generated by a battery or capacitor powering a heating element or plasma, used for lighting cigarettes or other applications.
[F23Q 2/282] with incandescent ignition  lighters with incandescent ignition but without fuel  F23Q7/00 -- Lighters that generate ignition using an incandescent heating element yet contain no combustible fuel themselves, instead relying on an external fuel source for burning.
[F23Q 2/285] with spark ignition -- F23Q 2/285 covers lighters that contain a fuel source and ignite by a spark, such as those used for cigarettes. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and ignition, and relates to portable devices where a spark generator ignites the fuel for a flame.
[F23Q 2/287] piezoelectric -- The code F23Q 2/287 covers lighters that use a piezoelectric element to create a spark for igniting the fuel. It applies to lightweight, hand-held devices such as cigarette lighters where the mechanical deformation of a crystal generates the electrical discharge needed for ignition.
[F23Q 2/30] Lighters characterised by catalytic ignition of fuel -- The F23Q 2/30 class refers to lighters that ignite their fuel by means of a catalytic reaction rather than a spark or flame, a design commonly used for cigarette lighting. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and ignition categories, covering devices where a catalyst initiates fuel combustion.
[F23Q 23/00] Testing of ignition installations -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Testing of ignition for internal-combustion engines -> F02P17/00
[F23Q 23/02] Testing of ignition timing -- The F23Q 23/02 entry covers methods and apparatus for measuring or verifying the timing of ignition events in combustion systems, such as internal-combustion engines, including sensors, diagnostic procedures, and analysis techniques used to ensure the spark or flame occurs at the correct point in the cycle.
[F23Q 23/08] Testing of components -- F23Q 23/08 relates to methods and procedures for evaluating the performance, reliability and safety of ignition-system components such as spark plugs, igniters and related hardware. It includes laboratory or field testing to assess parameters like ignition timing, energy output, durability and conformity with regulatory standards, and is used for patents involving diagnostic and test apparatus for ignition installations.

[F23Q 2/46] Friction wheels -- F23Q 2/46 relates to friction wheels employed in lighters that contain fuel, such as cigarette lighters. [F23Q 7/00] Incandescent ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition by a hot glowing surface; Electrically heated igniters, e.g. glow plugs used for internal combustion engines; Igniters comprising a hot glowing surface or igniting solid fuel or fluid-fuel burners; Portable igniters comprising electric means to heat a hot glowing surface, e.g. cigarette igniters.  .
[F23Q 7/001] Glowing plugs for internal-combustion engines -- Incandescent ignition refers to glowing-plug systems that heat an electrode to a high temperature so that fuel-air mixtures in internal-combustion engines ignite upon contact, eliminating the need for a spark. [F23Q 7/02] for igniting solid fuel -- F23Q 7/02 covers devices that ignite solid fuel by means of an incandescent source. [F23Q 7/06] structurally associated with fluid-fuel burners -- F23Q 7/06 covers designs where the ignition mechanism is integrated with fluid-fuel burners, linking the incandescent ignition system directly to the burner structure. It addresses arrangements that ensure reliable ignition of liquid or gaseous fuels in heating or cooking equipment.
[F23Q 7/08] for evaporating and igniting liquid fuel, e.g. in hurricane lanterns -- The F23Q 7/08 entry covers devices that both evaporate and ignite liquid fuel, typically used in portable lighting such as hurricane lanterns. It falls under incandescent ignition within combustion apparatus in the mechanical engineering field.

It applies to burners where ignition timing is linked to the gas supply control.
[F23Q 7/14] Portable igniters -- The CPC subclass F23Q 7/14 covers portable igniters that use incandescent ignition for starting combustion processes. It applies to spark-plug-type igniters where the transformer is built into the unit.

[F23Q 7/24] Safety arrangements -- F23Q 7/24 designates safety arrangements for incandescent ignition devices used in combustion apparatus, covering protective housings, flame arrestors, fail-safe mechanisms and other features that prevent accidental ignition or fire spread.

The provision may involve automatic electrical or thermal triggers to re-ignite the combustion process.
[F23Q 9/00] Pilot flame igniters -- F23Q 9/00 covers pilot flame igniters used to initiate combustion in burners and heating systems. These devices generate a small, continuously burning flame that reliably lights the main fuel flow in furnaces, boilers, or gas turbines. The classification includes designs, control mechanisms, and mounting arrangements for such igniters.
[F23Q 9/02] without interlock with main fuel supply -- The F23Q 9/02 subclass covers pilot-flame igniters that function without any interlock linking them to the main fuel supply, meaning the pilot can remain lit independently of the primary fuel flow. It pertains to mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus where ignition devices are separate from the main fuel control system.
[F23Q 9/04] for upright burners, e.g. gas-cooker burners -- This CPC subclass F23Q 9/04 covers pilot flame igniters designed for upright burners such as those used in gas cookers. It pertains to ignition devices that provide a continuous pilot flame to initiate combustion in vertical burner configurations.
[F23Q 9/045] Structurally associated with a main-burner -- Pilot flame igniters that are built into or directly attached to the main burner, providing a flame source that initiates and sustains the primary combustion process. The classification covers designs where the igniter's structure is an integral part of the burner assembly, ensuring reliable start-up and stable operation of the combustion apparatus.
[F23Q 9/06] for inverted burners, e.g. gas lamps -- F23Q 9/06 pertains to ignition devices for inverted burners, where the flame issues upward, such as in gas-lamp applications. The subclass covers pilot-flame igniters specifically designed for these burner configurations.
[F23Q 9/08] with interlock with main fuel supply -- Pilot flame igniters equipped with a safety interlock that disconnects the main fuel supply when the pilot fails to ignite, preventing uncontrolled fuel flow and reducing fire risk in combustion devices. This mechanism ensures the main flame only activates after confirmation of a stable pilot flame, enhancing overall system safety.
[F23Q 9/10] to determine the sequence of supply of fuel to pilot and main burners -- This classification covers methods for sequencing the fuel feed to a pilot flame igniter and the main burner in a combustion device. It specifies how the pilot is supplied first or concurrently to ensure reliable ignition before the main flame is lit. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, ignition and pilot flame igniters.
[F23Q 9/12] to permit the supply to the main burner in dependence upon existence of pilot flame -- A device or control that enables fuel or gas to be supplied to a main burner only when a pilot flame is detected, ensuring safe ignition and preventing unlit fuel flow. This functionality is used in combustion apparatus to coordinate the main burner operation with the presence of a pilot flame.
[F23Q 9/14] using electric means, e.g. by light-sensitive elements -- This subclass covers pilot flame igniters that are activated by electric means, including devices that use light-sensitive components to trigger ignition. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus and ignition.
[F23R] GENERATING COMBUSTION PRODUCTS OF HIGH PRESSURE OR HIGH VELOCITY, e.g. GAS-TURBINE COMBUSTION CHAMBERS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion chambers that are specially adapted for generation of gaseous combustion products of high pressure or high velocity, and therefore suitable for use as a source of motive power rather than merely a source of heat. Examples of such special adaptations are:  Methods of combustion in combustion chambers that are specially adapted for generation of combustion products of high pressure or high velocity.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fluidised bed combustion chambers specially adapted for operation at super-atmospheric pressures -> F23C10/16  **Glossary:** - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The expression "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Burner: a device by which fluid fuel or solid fuel suspended in air is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame. A burner includes means for feeding air that are arranged in immediate connection with a fuel feeding conduit, for example concentric with it. - Combustion: the direct combination of oxygen gas, e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Combustion zone: the part of a combustion apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel - Flame tube: The portion of a combustion chamber downstream of the zone where fuel and primary air are mixed. - Fuel: any combustible material that can be burned, regardless of whether the main purpose of burning it is for releasing energy therefrom or for disposing of it or rendering it less harmful - Pilot flame: a small flame that is lit or kept alight in order to provide ignition to a more powerful burner - Retention flame: a small flame that is kept alight in order to maintain the uninterrupted operation of a more powerful burner - Torch: a burner fired with fuel gas and oxygen and specially adapted to apply heat to a workpiece, for example for use in welding, cutting or brazing
[F23R 2900/00] Special features of, or arrangements for continuous combustion chambers -- F23R 2900/00 covers special features and arrangements applied to continuous combustion chambers, such as those used in gas-turbine burners, including methods for fuel injection, flame stabilization, cooling, and flow shaping to achieve high-pressure or high-velocity combustion.
[F23R 2900/00001] Arrangements using bellows, e.g. to adjust volumes or reduce thermal stresses -- F23R 2900/00001 covers the use of bellows in continuous combustion chambers such as gas-turbine combustors to vary chamber volume or mitigate thermal stresses. The arrangement allows flexible expansion and contraction, improving durability under high-pressure or high-velocity operation.
[F23R 2900/00002] Gas turbine combustors adapted for fuels having low heating value [LHV] -- This subclass covers gas turbine combustors specially configured to operate on fuels with low heating value, addressing fuel injection, flame stabilization and cooling strategies that maintain efficient high-pressure, high-velocity combustion in continuous chambers.
[F23R 2900/00004] Preventing formation of deposits on surfaces of gas turbine components, e.g. coke deposits -- The F23R 2900/00004 entry covers methods or arrangements that inhibit deposit formation-such as coke-on the internal surfaces of gas-turbine combustion chambers and related components, helping to maintain efficient high-pressure or high-velocity combustion by keeping critical surfaces clean.

The classification addresses the use of plasma to ignite or sustain the flame and to improve combustion efficiency.
[F23R 2900/00009] Using plasma torches for igniting, stabilizing, or improving the combustion process -- The code F23R 2900/00009 covers the use of plasma torches to ignite, stabilize or otherwise enhance combustion in continuous high-pressure or high-velocity chambers, such as gas-turbine combustors, by providing plasma-assisted ignition, flame holding or efficiency-improving effects.
[F23R 2900/00012] Details of sealing devices -- This subclass covers specific design features and arrangements of seals used in continuously operating combustion chambers that generate high-pressure or high-velocity combustion products, such as those in gas-turbine burners. It includes details of sealing devices that prevent gas leakage, protect components, and maintain chamber integrity.
[F23R 2900/00013] Reducing thermo-acoustic vibrations by active means -- The code designates inventions that actively suppress thermo-acoustic vibrations in continuously operating combustion chambers, such as those used in gas-turbine burners. It covers control systems, sensors, actuators or other active means that detect and counteract pressure-wave oscillations to improve stability and efficiency. This falls under mechanical-engineering combustion apparatus that generate high-pressure or high-velocity combustion products.
[F23R 2900/00014] Reducing thermo-acoustic vibrations by passive means, e.g. by Helmholtz resonators -- F23R 2900/00014 relates to passive methods for reducing thermo-acoustic vibrations in continuous high-pressure or high-velocity combustion chambers, such as the incorporation of Helmholtz resonators. These techniques aim to improve the stability and efficiency of gas-turbine combustion devices.
[F23R 2900/00015] Trapped vortex combustion chambers -- F23R 2900/00015 refers to trapped vortex combustion chambers, a type of continuous combustion chamber that uses a swirling flow to confine the flame in a vortex, improving stability and efficiency in high-pressure or high-velocity gas-turbine applications.
[F23R 2900/00016] Retrofitting in general, e.g. to respect new regulations on pollution -- F23R 2900/00016 covers methods and devices for retrofitting continuous combustion chambers, such as gas-turbine chambers, to meet updated pollution regulations. It applies to modifications that improve emissions performance without redesigning the entire system.
[F23R 2900/00017] Assembling combustion chamber liners or subparts -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices for assembling liners or subparts of continuous high-pressure or high-velocity combustion chambers, such as those found in gas-turbine combustors. It includes arrangements for joining or mounting chamber liners, segments and related components within the combustion system.
[F23R 2900/00018] Manufacturing combustion chamber liners or subparts -- This subclass addresses processes for producing liners or individual sub-parts of continuous combustion chambers, especially those used in high-pressure or high-velocity gas-turbine combustors. It includes machining, forming, joining, coating and assembly techniques that create components that line or form the chamber interior.
[F23R 2900/00019] Repairing or maintaining combustion chamber liners or subparts -- F23R 2900/00019 covers methods and apparatuses for repairing or maintaining liners and other subcomponents of continuous combustion chambers used to generate high-pressure or high-velocity gases, such as those in gas-turbine engines.
[F23R 2900/03041] Effusion cooled combustion chamber walls or domes -- F23R 2900/03041 covers combustion chambers whose walls or domes are cooled by effusion, where a protective gas or fluid passes through porous material to remove heat and protect the structure during continuous high-pressure, high-velocity combustion, such as in gas-turbine burners.
[F23R 2900/03042] Film cooled combustion chamber walls or domes -- The CPC entry F23R 2900/03042 covers film-cooled walls or domes of continuous combustion chambers, where a thin protective gas film is applied to the interior surface to shield it from the extreme temperatures of the combustion products. This cooling method is common in gas-turbine combustors and other high-pressure, high-velocity combustion devices.
[F23R 2900/03043] Convection cooled combustion chamber walls with means for guiding the cooling air flow -- The CPC entry F23R 2900/03043 concerns combustion chambers in which the walls are cooled by convection, featuring specific arrangements that direct the airflow used for cooling.
