This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and high-pressure or high-velocity combustion product generation.
[F23R 2900/03044] Impingement cooled combustion chamber walls or subassemblies -- This classification covers combustion chambers where the walls or components are cooled by directed fluid jets that impinge on the surface to remove heat, typically used in high-pressure or high-velocity gas-turbine applications. It includes arrangements for continuous combustion chambers with impingement cooling of walls or subassemblies.
[F23R 2900/03045] Convection cooled combustion chamber walls provided with turbolators or means for creating turbulences to increase cooling -- The entry refers to combustion chambers, especially those in gas-turbine systems, whose walls are cooled by convection and fitted with turbolators or other turbulence-creating features to improve cooling efficiency.
[F23R 2900/03281] Intermittent fuel injection or supply with plunger pump or other means therefor -- The code F23R 2900/03281 covers systems that deliver fuel to a continuously operating combustion chamber in a non-steady, intermittent manner, using a plunger-type pump or comparable device. It applies to high-pressure or high-velocity combustion setups such as gas-turbine chambers where precise, pulsed fuel supply is required.
[F23R 2900/03282] High speed injection of air and/or fuel inducing internal recirculation -- The CPC entry F23R 2900/03282 pertains to continuous combustion chambers that generate high-pressure or high-velocity products, such as gas-turbine combustors. It specifically covers designs in which air and/or fuel are injected at high speed to create internal recirculation, enhancing mixing, flame stability and overall combustion efficiency.
[F23R 2900/03341] Sequential combustion chambers or burners -- The code F23R 2900/03341 covers sequential combustion chambers or burners that are arranged in series within a continuous combustion system, such as those used in high-pressure or high-velocity gas-turbine combustors, to stage fuel release and improve efficiency.
[F23R 2900/03342] Arrangement of silo-type combustion chambers -- The entry defines a configuration in which one or more silo-shaped combustion chambers are arranged in series or parallel to provide continuous combustion for high-pressure or high-velocity gas-turbine applications. The design focuses on the spatial layout, sealing and flow linking of the silos to maintain a stable flame and efficient fuel use.
[F23R 2900/03343] Pilot burners operating in premixed mode -- F23R 2900/03343 covers pilot burners that operate in a premixed mode, used within continuous combustion chambers such as those in gas-turbine systems. The code specifies the arrangement and features of these burners when generating high-pressure or high-velocity combustion products. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion apparatus, and high-pressure/high-velocity combustion chambers.
[F23R 3/00] Continuous combustion chambers using liquid or gaseous fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Continuous combustion chambers using liquid or gaseous fuel.

[F23R 3/06] Arrangement of apertures along the flame tube -- F23R 3/06 relates to the geometric layout of openings-such as holes, slots or perforations-situated along the flame tube of continuous combustion chambers that burn liquid or gaseous fuels. The arrangement is intended to influence flame stability, mixing, pressure drop and the high-pressure or high-velocity flow of combustion products.
[F23R 3/08] between annular flame tube sections, e.g. flame tubes with telescopic sections -- This subclass covers continuous combustion chambers that produce high-pressure or high-velocity gases, such as those used in gas-turbine combustors, where the annular flame tube is formed by telescopic or nested sections positioned between one another. It includes designs in which the flame tube consists of multiple concentric or staggered rings that can be assembled or adjusted relative to each other.

[F23R 3/12] inducing a vortex -- F23R 3/12 relates to techniques for creating a vortex within continuous combustion chambers that use liquid or gaseous fuel, typically in high-pressure or high-velocity applications such as gas-turbine combustors. The classification covers designs or methods that induce swirling flow to improve mixing, flame stability, or combustion efficiency. It is part of the broader group on combustion apparatus that generate combustion products at elevated pressure or velocity.
[F23R 3/14] by using swirl vanes -- The CPC subclass F23R 3/14 covers continuous combustion chambers, such as those in gas-turbine engines, that generate high-pressure or high-velocity products and employ swirl vanes to create a rotating flow of liquid or gaseous fuel for improved mixing and flame stability.
[F23R 3/16] with devices inside the flame tube or the combustion chamber to influence the air or gas flow -- The subclass F23R 3/16 pertains to continuous combustion chambers, such as gas-turbine combustors using liquid or gaseous fuel, that contain internal components - baffles, swirlers, injectors or similar devices - placed inside the flame tube or chamber to modify the flow of air or gas, improving mixing, stability or pressure development.
[F23R 3/18] Flame stabilising means, e.g. flame holders for after-burners of jet-propulsion plants -- F23R 3/18 covers devices that stabilise the flame in continuous high-pressure or high-velocity combustion chambers, such as flame holders used in after-burners of jet-propulsion engines. The classification pertains to mechanisms that maintain steady combustion of liquid or gaseous fuel under conditions typical of gas-turbine or similar high-speed applications.
[F23R 3/20] incorporating fuel injection means -- F23R 3/20 covers continuous combustion chambers-such as those in gas-turbine engines-that integrate fuel injection means directly into the chamber. The classification addresses the arrangement, design and operation of injectors supplying liquid or gaseous fuel to generate high-pressure or high-velocity combustion products.
[F23R 3/22] movable, e.g. to an inoperative position -- This subclass covers continuous high-pressure or high-velocity combustion chambers, such as those in gas turbines, that are designed to be moved to a non-operational position for safety, maintenance or shut-down purposes, typically using liquid or gaseous fuel.
[F23R 3/24] of the fluid-screen type -- This subclass covers continuous combustion chambers in which fuel and air are mixed through a fluid-screen, a design commonly used for high-pressure or high-velocity gas-turbine combustors. The fluid-screen arrangement provides stable flame anchoring and efficient combustion of liquid or gaseous fuels.
[F23R 3/26] Controlling the air flow -- This subclass covers devices and methods for regulating the flow of air into continuous combustion chambers that use liquid or gaseous fuel, such as gas-turbine burners. It includes air-mixing valves, ducts and control systems that adjust airflow to maintain stable combustion and achieve the required pressure or velocity of the exhaust gases.
[F23R 3/28] characterised by the fuel supply -- F23R 3/28 covers continuous combustion chambers, such as those used in gas-turbine engines, where the defining aspect is the design or arrangement of the fuel supply system. It includes any features that affect how liquid or gaseous fuel is delivered to the combustion zone, influencing pressure and velocity of the generated gases.
[F23R 3/283] Attaching or cooling of fuel injecting means including supports for fuel injectors, stems, or lances -- F23R 3/283 covers devices and methods for mounting or thermally managing fuel-injecting components in continuous high-pressure or high-velocity combustion chambers, such as those used in gas-turbine combustors. It includes supports, cooling passages, and other structures that secure injectors, stems or lances while protecting them from overheating.
[F23R 3/286] having fuel-air premixing devices  F23R3/30  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion chambers wherein the fuel supply means comprise fuel pre-vaporising devices -> F23R3/30
[F23R 3/30] comprising fuel prevapourising devices -- F23R 3/30 relates to continuous combustion chambers for gas-turbine or similar engines that include devices for prevapourising the fuel before it enters the combustion zone, improving mixing and combustion stability.
[F23R 3/32] being tubular -- F23R 3/32 covers tubular continuous combustion chambers in which liquid or gaseous fuel is burned to generate high-pressure or high-velocity products, as used in gas-turbine engines. The classification applies to tube-shaped chambers that operate continuously and supply combustion gases for propulsion or power generation.
[F23R 3/34] Feeding into different combustion zones -- F23R 3/34 covers arrangements for introducing fuel or oxidizer into separate zones of a continuous high-pressure combustion chamber, such as gas-turbine combustors, to create distinct combustion regions. The classification includes devices that split or stage the feed to control temperature, emission or flame stability.
[F23R 3/343] Pilot flames, i.e. fuel nozzles or injectors using only a very small proportion of the total fuel to insure continuous combustion  ignition in gas-turbine plants  F02C7/264 ; pilot flame igniters  F23Q9/00 -- F23R 3/343 covers pilot flames used in gas-turbine combustion chambers, where a small-flow fuel nozzle or injector supplies a minimal amount of fuel to maintain ignition and stable combustion. It also includes pilot flame igniters for continuous operation.
[F23R 3/346] for staged combustion -- F23R 3/346 pertains to staged combustion methods applied in continuous combustion chambers that use liquid or gaseous fuels, typically for gas-turbine applications. The classification covers designs where the combustion process is divided into separate zones or stages to control temperature, pressure and emissions, improving efficiency and stability of high-pressure or high-velocity exhaust streams.
[F23R 3/36] Supply of different fuels -- F23R 3/36 relates to arrangements for feeding multiple fuel types to continuous combustion chambers, such as those used in gas-turbine engines. The entry covers systems that can switch between or mix liquid and gaseous fuels to sustain high-pressure or high-velocity combustion.
[F23R 3/38] comprising rotary fuel injection means -- F23R 3/38 covers continuous combustion chambers, such as those in gas-turbine engines, that employ rotary fuel injection devices to introduce liquid or gaseous fuel into the high-pressure, high-velocity flow.
[F23R 3/40] characterised by the use of catalytic means -- F23R 3/40 relates to continuous combustion chambers, such as gas-turbine combustors, that employ catalytic means to promote the burning of liquid or gaseous fuels, enabling stable, high-pressure or high-velocity combustion products. The use of a catalyst improves ignition and flame stability while allowing efficient fuel conversion.
[F23R 3/42] characterised by the arrangement or form of the flame tubes or combustion chambers -- The subclass F23R 3/42 pertains to continuous combustion chambers, such as those used in gas-turbine combustors, where the inventive focus is on the geometric layout or shape of the flame tubes or individual combustion chambers. It addresses how their arrangement influences flame stability, pressure distribution and overall performance.
[F23R 3/425] Combustion chambers comprising a tangential or helicoidal arrangement of the flame tubes -- The class covers combustion chambers in which the flame tubes are positioned in a tangential or helicoidal pattern around the flow path, creating a swirling or spiral flow that enhances mixing, stabilizes the flame and allows high-pressure or high-velocity combustion, typical of gas-turbine combustors.
[F23R 3/44] Combustion chambers comprising a tubular flame tube within a tubular casing -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Multiple tubular flame tubes within a common casing -> F23R3/46 - Annular combustion chambers -> F23R3/50 - Reverse-flow combustion chambers -> F23R3/54
[F23R 3/46] Combustion chambers comprising an annular arrangement of flame tubes within a common annular casing or within individual casings -- The code covers designs where several flame tubes are placed in a circular ring, either inside one common annular casing or each in its own casing, forming an annular combustion zone for gas-turbine or similar high-pressure engines.
[F23R 3/48] Flame tube interconnectors, e.g. cross-over tubes -- F23R 3/48 covers flame-tube interconnectors, such as cross-over tubes that link individual flame tubes within a continuous combustion chamber. These devices allow combustion gases to pass between tubes, helping to equalise flow, stabilize the flame and improve performance in high-pressure or high-velocity combustors like gas-turbine chambers.
[F23R 3/50] Combustion chambers comprising an annular flame tube within an annular casing -- A combustion chamber design in which a tubular flame zone is formed as an annulus inside a surrounding annular casing, allowing gases to flow around the inner tube. This arrangement is used in continuous gas-turbine combustors to generate high-pressure, high-velocity combustion products from liquid or gaseous fuels.
[F23R 3/52] Toroidal combustion chambers -- F23R 3/52 covers toroidal combustion chambers, a form of continuous combustion chamber with a doughnut-shaped geometry used to generate high-pressure or high-velocity combustion products. The subclass applies to designs that burn liquid or gaseous fuel continuously, such as those found in gas-turbine combustion systems.
[F23R 3/54] Reverse-flow combustion chambers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion gases are reversed in the combustor.
[F23R 3/56] Combustion chambers having rotary flame tubes -- F23R 3/56 relates to combustion chambers that incorporate rotary flame tubes, where the tubes rotate within the chamber to sustain continuous combustion of liquid or gaseous fuels. The design is used to produce high-pressure or high-velocity combustion gases, typical of gas-turbine applications.
[F23R 3/58] Cyclone or vortex type combustion chambers -- F23R 3/58 covers combustion chambers that create a cyclonic or vortex flow to mix fuel and air, typically used in gas-turbine or other high-pressure, high-velocity systems. The design relies on swirling motion to enhance combustion efficiency and stabilize the flame, often employing liquid or gaseous fuels.
[F23R 3/60] Support structures -- F23R 3/60 relates to the support structures used inside continuous combustion chambers of gas-turbine or other high-pressure/high-velocity combustors, where liquid or gaseous fuels are burned. It covers ribs, liners, brackets, cooling passages and other internal elements that maintain chamber geometry, resist vibration and aid heat removal.
[F23R 5/00] Continuous combustion chambers using solid or pulverulent fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  High pressure or high velocity combustion chambers using solid, e.g. lumps of wood, or pulverulent fuel . e.g. pulverized coal or biomass.
[F23R 7/00] Intermittent or explosive combustion chambers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  High pressure or high velocity combustion chambers wherein the combustion is pulsating or resonating type, e.g. pulse detonation
[F24] HEATING -- F24 falls under the mechanical engineering section and addresses technologies for producing, distributing and regulating heat. It includes heating apparatus such as boilers, radiators, heat exchangers, space-heating systems and associated control devices for residential, commercial and industrial applications.
[F24B] DOMESTIC STOVES OR RANGES FOR SOLID FUELS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Domestic stoves or ranges for solid fuel. The solid fuel can be in the form of lumps or pieces, e.g. coal, briquettes or firewood, or pulverulent or granulated material, e.g. wood pellets.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For solid fuels in combination with gaseous fuels, liquid fuels or other kinds of energy supply -> F24C1/02  **Glossary:** - Combustion chamber: a chamber in which fuel is burned to establish a self-supporting fire or flame and which surrounds that fire or flame - Burner: a device by which fluent or pulverised fuel is passed to a combustion space where it burns to produce a self-supporting flame. A burner includes means for feeding air that are arranged in immediate connections with a fuel feeding conduit, for example concentric with it. - Air: a mixture of gases containing free oxygen and able to promote or support combustion - Primary air: air supplied to the burning fuel, e.g. together with the fuel, in order to liberate combustible gases - Secondary air: air supplied to the combustible gases liberated by the primary air in order to complete their combustion. The expression "secondary air" covers "tertiary air" etc. - Combustion: the direct combustion of oxygen gas, e.g. in air, and a burnable substance - Combustion zone: the part of an apparatus where the reaction takes place between air and fuel
[F24B 1/00] Stoves or ranges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Closed stoves and stoves with open fires, e.g. fireplaces. A fireplace comprising a door, optional with a window for a visual aspect, is considered as a stove with open fire.  Ranges for cooking with optionally a baking oven.
[F24B 1/003] on which a removable cooking element is arranged -- This classification (F24B 1/003) pertains to domestic stoves or ranges that use solid fuels and include a cooking element-such as a grill, hot plate or pan-that can be removed for cleaning, replacement or reconfiguration. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and the subcategory of solid-fuel domestic cooking appliances.
[F24B 1/006] incorporating a catalytic combustor -- F24B 1/006 covers domestic stoves or ranges that use solid fuels and incorporate a catalytic combustor to improve combustion efficiency and reduce emissions. The catalytic element promotes more complete fuel burning, leading to lower smoke and higher heat output in household heating appliances.
[F24B 1/02] Closed stoves -- Closed stoves are airtight heating or cooking appliances that use solid fuel and feature a sealed combustion chamber to limit airflow, improving efficiency and reducing emissions. They typically incorporate a door or hatch for fuel loading and may include heat-exchange surfaces to transfer heat to the surrounding environment or cooking vessels. This design distinguishes them from open-fire ranges within the domestic solid-fuel stove category.
[F24B 1/022] easily collapsible or easily removable -- F24B 1/022 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that are designed to be easily collapsible or removable, allowing quick disassembly for transport, cleaning, or storage. The feature is intended to simplify handling and maintenance of heating appliances in residential settings.
[F24B 1/024] for pulverulent fuels -- This subgroup of the CPC relates to domestic stoves or ranges that are designed to operate on pulverulent fuels such as powdered coal, peat, wood pellets or similar fine solid fuels, covering burners, fuel feeding mechanisms and combustion chambers adapted for the handling and combustion of such materials.
[F24B 1/026] with several combustion zones -- F24B 1/026 classifies domestic stoves or ranges that use solid fuels and are designed with several combustion zones, allowing the appliance to contain multiple separate heating chambers or stages within a single unit.
[F24B 1/028] with means for regulating combustion -- This subclass covers domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels and incorporate devices to adjust the combustion process, such as airflow controls, thermostatic regulators or automatic fuel-feeding mechanisms, aimed at improving heat output, efficiency and safety.
[F24B 1/04] built-up from glazed tiles -- F24B 1/04 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges where the cooking chamber is formed by joining glazed tiles into a built-up, heat-resistant lining. The tile construction provides a smooth, fire-proof surface that improves heat distribution and eases cleaning.
[F24B 1/06] Construction of tiles or bracing means therefor, e.g. shim liner -- F24B 1/06 covers the design and installation of tiles and their supporting or bracing structures in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges. It includes elements such as shims, liners or other means that secure, space or protect fire-box tiles, ensuring proper alignment and heat resistance.
[F24B 1/08] with fuel storage in a single undivided hopper within stove or range -- F24B 1/08 classifies domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate a single, undivided hopper for storing the fuel inside the appliance. The design integrates the fuel compartment directly into the stove body rather than using separate chambers.
[F24B 1/10] with combustion in horizontal direction -- The code F24B 1/10 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges where the combustion chamber is arranged so that the flame spreads horizontally rather than vertically. This classification applies to designs that direct the burn along a horizontal plane, often to improve heat distribution across the cooking surface or room. It is a subcategory of heating equipment for household use.
[F24B 1/14] with predistillation in the hopper -- F24B 1/14 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate a predistillation step in the hopper, where fuel is partially vaporised before reaching the combustion zone, enhancing fuel use efficiency and reducing smoke emissions.
[F24B 1/16] with fuel storage in multiple or divided hoppers within the stove or range -- The F24B 1/16 subclass refers to solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate fuel storage in two or more separate hoppers inside the appliance, allowing the fuel to be divided among distinct compartments for improved handling, combustion control or space utilization.
[F24B 1/18] Stoves with open fires, e.g. fireplaces -- The F24B 1/18 class groups heating appliances that integrate an open fire, such as stoves or ranges that function as fireplaces. It covers designs where combustion occurs in an exposed chamber and the heat is transferred directly to the cooking or heating area.
[F24B 1/1802] adapted for the use of both solid fuel and another type of fuel or energy supply   for stoves without open fires  F24C1/02 -- A dual-fuel domestic stove or range designed for solid fuel that can also operate on a second fuel or energy source, such as gas, electricity or liquid fuel, and that functions without an open fire, falls under CPC code F24B 1/1802. This entry covers the adaptation of stoves for combined-fuel use.
[F24B 1/1804] Reverse-draught fireplaces -- F24B 1/1804 defines reverse-draught fireplaces, heating devices that use a built-in flue or duct to create a pressure difference that pulls combustion gases upward, enhancing heat extraction and reducing smoke emission.
[F24B 1/1806] Mounting of closed stoves in a fireplace -- Mounting of closed stoves in a fireplace covers the methods and arrangements for securely installing sealed solid-fuel stoves within a fireplace cavity. It includes the use of brackets, supports, anchoring systems, clearance requirements and integration with the hearth or surrounding structure to ensure stable, safe operation.
[F24B 1/1808] Simulated fireplaces -- F24B 1/1808 covers simulated fireplaces, devices that create the visual effect of a fire for aesthetic or heating purposes without using real combustion, typically integrated into solid-fuel stoves or ranges.
[F24B 1/181] Free-standing fireplaces, e.g. for mobile homes -- F24B 1/181 covers free-standing fireplaces intended for installation in mobile homes or similar portable structures. These units provide heating without integrating into the building's fixed chimney system, often featuring compact, self-contained designs suitable for limited spaces.
[F24B 1/182] with additional provisions for cooking -- F24B 1/182 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate extra cooking features such as ovens, grills, or burners, allowing food preparation alongside heating. The classification identifies appliances designed to combine heating and cooking in a single unit.
[F24B 1/183] with additional provisions for heating water -- The classification F24B 1/183 covers solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate a built-in water-heating system, such as a kettle or boiler, allowing simultaneous cooking and heating of water.
[F24B 1/185] with air-handling means, heat exchange means, or additional provisions for convection heating -- F24B 1/185 pertains to solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate air-handling devices, heat-exchange components, or other provisions designed to create convection heating, thereby improving heat distribution within the oven or surrounding space.
[F24B 1/187] Condition responsive controls for regulating combustion -- F24B 1/187 covers condition-responsive control devices for solid-fuel domestic stoves and ranges that adjust combustion based on sensed parameters such as temperature, pressure, oxygen level or flame size. The classification includes sensors, thermostats, automatic dampers and electronic circuits that modify air-fuel ratios or ignition timing to maintain optimal burning conditions.
[F24B 1/1875] combined with extinguishing devices -- The CPC subclass F24B 1/1875 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that are equipped with fire-extinguishing devices, such as automatic shut-off, water, foam or powder discharge systems, or other mechanisms designed to suppress a flame or halt combustion. It applies to heating appliances where the extinguishing feature is integral to the stove or range construction.
[F24B 1/188] characterised by use of heat exchange means  -- F24B 1/188 covers solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate a heat-exchange system to capture and reuse part of the generated heat, for example to preheat water, air or other media. The classification applies when the appliance's design includes a dedicated heat-exchanger element that improves overall energy efficiency.
[F24B 1/1881] the heat exchange medium being either water or air -- The subclass F24B 1/1881 pertains to domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that employ a heat-exchange medium, specifically water or air, to transfer the generated heat for space heating or domestic hot-water supply. It covers designs where the combustion chamber is linked to a water-or-air circuit, allowing the furnace to serve as a central heating source.
[F24B 1/1883] the heat exchange medium being both water and air -- F24B 1/1883 covers domestic stoves or ranges that use solid fuels and employ both water and air as the heat-exchange medium, such as configurations where water circulates together with combustion gases to enhance heating efficiency.
[F24B 1/1885] the heat exchange medium being air only -- F24B 1/1885 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges where the only heat-exchange medium is air, meaning the appliance transfers heat directly to the surrounding atmosphere without using water, oil, or other fluids. It applies to designs that rely solely on heated air for cooking or space heating.
[F24B 1/1886] the heat exchanger comprising only tubular air ducts within the fire -- This subgroup covers solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges whose heat-exchanger consists solely of tubular air ducts positioned within the fire, using the tubes to transfer heat from the combustion zone to the cooking or heating air. The design eliminates separate metal plates or fins, relying on the ducted airflow for heat exchange.
[F24B 1/1888] with forced circulation -- F24B 1/1888 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate a forced-circulation system to move air or combustion gases through the appliance, enhancing heat transfer and fuel efficiency. The classification applies to designs where fans or blowers are used to distribute heat more evenly within the cooking chamber or to the surrounding room.
[F24B 1/189] characterised by air-handling means, i.e. of combustion-air, heated-air, or flue-gases, e.g. draught control dampers -- F24B 1/189 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate air-handling means, such as devices for controlling combustion-air, preheated air, or flue-gas flow, for example draft-control dampers.
[F24B 1/1895] flue-gas control dampers -- Flue-gas control dampers are devices installed in the exhaust system of solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges to regulate the flow of combustion gases, improve combustion efficiency, and reduce emissions.
[F24B 1/19] Supplying combustion-air -- The F24B 1/19 entry covers arrangements for delivering combustion-air to solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including air inlets, ducts, fans, and control mechanisms that regulate the supply of oxygen to the fire. It pertains to mechanical-engineering solutions aimed at improving combustion efficiency, safety, and temperature control in household heating appliances.
[F24B 1/1902] in combination with provisions for heating air only -- The subclass F24B 1/1902 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate elements designed only to heat air, without any cooking or food-preparation function. It applies to units where the heating provision is separate from or additional to the primary cooking apparatus.
[F24B 1/1905] in combination with provisions for heating water only -- The subclass F24B 1/1905 addresses solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate a water-heating function as the only additional provision. It covers designs where hot water is produced alongside cooking heat, without any other ancillary uses such as space heating.
[F24B 1/1907] in combination with provisions for heating both air and water -- F24B 1/1907 covers solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that include provisions for heating both the surrounding air and water, such as integrated boiler or water-circulation features.
[F24B 1/191] Component parts -- F24B 1/191 covers the individual component parts used in domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels such as wood, coal or peat. It includes elements like burners, grates, fireboxes, doors, hatches, and ventilation components that together form the functional structure of the appliance.
[F24B 1/1915] Means for removing ash -- This subclass covers mechanisms incorporated in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that facilitate ash removal, such as removable ash trays, ash pans, cleaning brushes, or automatic ejection devices designed to collect and discharge accumulated ash from the fire chamber.
[F24B 1/192] Doors -- This subgroup addresses doors used in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including their construction, sealing mechanisms, hinges, latches and safety features. It covers innovations aimed at improving heat retention, user access and fire protection.
[F24B 1/193] Grates -- F24B 1/193 relates to grates employed in domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels. It covers the design, placement and features of these supporting structures that hold fuel and allow airflow for efficient combustion and heat distribution.
[F24B 1/195] Fireboxes -- Fireboxes are the combustion chambers of domestic stoves or ranges that use solid fuels such as wood, coal or peat. They confine the fire, support the fuel, and channel heat toward the cooking surface or heating outlet while containing ash and gases.
[F24B 1/1952] Multiple fire-boxes -- The F24B 1/1952 subclass covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate two or more fire-boxes, allowing separate heating zones or simultaneous use of different fuels. It addresses the design, construction and arrangement of multiple combustion chambers within a single appliance for enhanced cooking or heating flexibility.
[F24B 1/1955] Hoods -- The CPC code F24B 1/1955 pertains to hoods used with domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels, specifying devices that capture, filter or direct smoke, fumes and heat away from the cooking area.
[F24B 1/1957] Heat reflectors -- F24B 1/1957 covers heat-reflector elements incorporated into domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges. These components are designed to redirect radiant energy toward the cooking chamber or heating surface, thereby enhancing thermal efficiency and reducing fuel consumption.
[F24B 1/197] Hearths -- Hearths are the fire-resistant floor or base of a solid-fuel stove or range that supports the fuel and flame and protects the surrounding structure. They typically consist of a refractory material or metal grate designed to contain and radiate heat efficiently while preventing damage to the appliance housing. This element is essential for safe, effective operation of domestic heating appliances that burn solid fuels.
[F24B 1/198] Surrounds-fronts -- F24B 1/198 covers the design and construction of surrounds and front components of solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including protective panels, doors and decorative front elements that enclose the combustion chamber.
[F24B 1/199] Fuel-handling equipment -- F24B 1/199 covers fuel-handling equipment used in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including devices that store, transport, feed or load solid fuels such as wood, coal or pellets into the combustion chamber. It encompasses feeders, conveyors, hoppers and related mechanisms that automate or facilitate fuel supply.
[F24B 1/20] Ranges -- The F24B 1/20 entry covers domestic ranges that operate on solid fuels, such as wood or coal, designed for heating and cooking within a household. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the heating subgroup for stoves and ranges using solid fuel.
[F24B 1/202] specially adapted for travelling -- F24B 1/202 covers solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that are specially adapted for travelling, including portable, lightweight or collapsible units designed for easy transport and use outside a fixed location.
[F24B 1/205] collapsible -- The code F24B 1/205 designates features of solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that can be collapsed or folded for compact storage, transport, or installation. It covers mechanisms such as hinged, telescopic, or detachable components that allow the cooking chamber, firebox, or supporting structures to be reduced in size.
[F24B 1/207] movable -- F24B 1/207 covers domestic stoves or ranges that use solid fuels and are designed to be movable, allowing them to be detached and repositioned within a residence. The subclass applies to portable or transportable heating and cooking units intended for household use, distinguishing them from permanently installed appliances.
[F24B 1/22] in which the baking oven is arranged above the fire-box -- F24B 1/22 covers domestic stoves or ranges that position the baking oven directly above the fire-box, allowing heat from the solid-fuel combustion chamber to rise into the oven for cooking. This arrangement is typical of traditional wood- or coal-fired kitchen appliances where the oven is integrated into the same enclosure as the cooking fire.
[F24B 1/24] with built-in masses for heat storage or heat insulation -- F24B 1/24 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate built-in thermal masses, such as bricks or stones, to store heat and improve insulation. The integrated mass absorbs heat during combustion and releases it gradually, enhancing energy efficiency and providing a steadier cooking temperature.
[F24B 1/26] Stoves with additional provisions for cooking -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering heating domestic stoves or ranges for solid fuels and covers stoves or ranges that incorporate extra cooking features beyond basic heating. It includes integrated ovens, grill plates or other cooking appliances built into a solid-fuel stove, designed to prepare food while providing heat.
[F24B 1/28] Combined installations of stoves or ranges, e.g. back-to-back stoves with a common fire-box -- This subclass covers designs where two or more solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges are installed together sharing a common fire-box, providing multiple cooking surfaces in a single unit. It includes back-to-back arrangements and other combined installations that integrate the fire source for the paired appliances.
[F24B 13/00] Details solely applicable to stoves or ranges burning solid fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of stoves, e.g. doors for closed stoves, arrangements for removal of ashes, arrangements or mountings of fire-grates or arrangements for feeding solid fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Component parts or accessories for stoves with open-fires -> F24B1/191
[F24B 13/002] Surrounds -- The code F24B 13/002 designates features that form the surrounding structure of domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels, such as the enclosure, casing or external housing that contains the combustion chamber and associated components. This classification is used to cover the protective and aesthetic elements surrounding the heating appliance.
[F24B 13/004] Doors specially adapted for stoves or ranges  doors for open fires  F24B1/192
[F24B 13/006] Arrangements for cleaning, e.g. soot removal; Ash removal -- F24B13/006 covers the design of cleaning systems for solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including mechanisms for removing soot, ash, and related residues from the combustion chamber or firebox. This includes ash pans, soot extractors, self-cleaning surfaces, and any arrangement that facilitates maintenance of the appliance.
[F24B 13/008] Ash containers -- The entry classifies devices that collect and hold ash generated by domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels. It includes containers, trays or removable bins intended to simplify ash removal and maintenance of the heating appliance, and applies only to solid-fuel heating equipment.
[F24B 13/02] Arrangement or mountings of fire-grate assemblies -- The CPC code F24B 13/02 covers the design and installation of fire-grate assemblies within domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels, focusing on how the grate is arranged, supported and mounted inside the heating chamber. It includes any structural features that position or secure the grate for effective combustion.
[F24B 13/04] Arrangements for feeding solid fuel, e.g. hoppers -- The entry covers designs of feeding mechanisms for solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, such as hoppers, conveyors or other devices that supply wood, coal or similar fuels to the combustion chamber. It relates to the arrangement and operation of these fuel-delivery systems within the stove or range.
[F24B 15/00] Implements for use in connection with stoves or ranges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices used in connection with stoves, e.g. scratchers, pokers, ash-sifters, coal boxes, shovels to remove ashes and grippers for manipulation of firewood.  Devices for starting a fire or stirring up the fire.
[F24B 15/002] Scratchers or pokers -- Scratchers or pokers are hand tools used to stir, turn, or break up food and ash in solid-fuel stoves or ranges. They are typically made of metal or heat-resistant material with a long handle to keep the user away from the fire. This implements category is classified under CPC code F24B 15/002.
[F24B 15/005] Igniting devices; Fire-igniting fans -- F24B 15/005 covers devices that initiate or assist combustion in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including electric igniters, spark-producing mechanisms and fans that blow air onto the fire to promote ignition.
[F24B 15/007] Ash-sifters -- F24B 15/007 classifies devices that are attached to or used with solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges for the purpose of separating ash from fuel residues. The implements, known as ash-sifters, typically consist of a perforated container or brush that allows ash to be removed while retaining unburned material. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stoves or ranges for solid fuels.
[F24B 15/02] for breaking coal -- F24B 15/02 covers implements designed for use with solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that break coal into smaller pieces, facilitating easier handling and more efficient combustion. These devices are part of the heating sector within mechanical engineering and are specifically intended for coal-fuel preparation.
[F24B 15/04] Coal hods -- In the CPC system, code F24B 15/04 falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic stoves or ranges for solid fuels, and implements used with stoves. It covers coal hods, containers designed to hold and transport coal for feeding solid-fuel cooking appliances.
[F24B 15/06] Shovels with ejectors -- F24B 15/06 refers to shovels equipped with ejector mechanisms designed for use with domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges. The tools combine a standard stove shovel with a built-in blower or suction device that expels ash or debris from the firebox, simplifying cleaning and enhancing safety.
[F24B 15/08] Shovels with sifters -- F24B 15/08 refers to shovels that incorporate a sifter screen for use with solid-fuel stoves or ranges. The tool combines a digging blade with a mesh to separate ash or debris from finer material while cleaning the heating appliance.
[F24B 15/10] Coal tongs -- Coal tongs are hand-held implements designed for safely handling and moving pieces of coal in domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges. They typically consist of long, insulated handles that grip the coal to prevent burns and facilitate loading or removal from the firebox.
[F24B 3/00] Heaters not covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heaters for burning solid fuel, e.g. brazier, fire-pits or fire basket.
[F24B 5/00] Combustion-air or flue-gas circulation in or around stoves or ranges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion-air or flue-gas circulation in or around stoves or ranges; The supply of combustion air in or around stoves. The air could be primary air supplied to the burning fuel and/or secondary air for completing combustion.  Circulation of flue gases in or around stoves or ranges in order to improve heat exchange.
[F24B 5/02] in or around stoves -- This subclass covers ways of moving combustion-air or flue-gas within or surrounding solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including ducts, fans, vents and other arrangements that affect draft, mixing or heat distribution.
[F24B 5/021] combustion-air circulation -- This CPC subclass deals with the arrangement and control of airflow used to supply combustion air to solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including methods for circulating air through the fire chamber, venting flue gases, and improving combustion efficiency.
[F24B 5/023] Supply of primary air for combustion -- F24B 5/023 covers devices and methods that provide the primary air needed for fuel combustion in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges. It includes arrangements for directing fresh air into the fire chamber or burners to ensure proper mixing and efficient burning. This subclass focuses on the supply of combustion-air rather than circulation of exhaust gases.
[F24B 5/025] Supply of secondary air for completing combustion of fuel -- The classification covers methods and apparatus that introduce secondary combustion air into solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges to complete fuel burn. It includes designs for circulating air or flue gases around the firebox, improving efficiency and reducing emissions by ensuring thorough oxidation of the fuel.
[F24B 5/026] Supply of primary and secondary air for combustion -- The classification covers methods and devices that deliver both primary and secondary combustion air to solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges, including ducts, fans, blowers or passive passages that direct air into the firebox and around the flame to enhance fuel burn and lower emissions.
[F24B 5/028] Arrangements combining combustion-air and flue-gas circulation -- The CPC entry F24B 5/028 covers designs for solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges in which the supply of combustion air and the removal of flue gases are integrated into a single circulation system. Such arrangements route fresh air to the fire chamber while simultaneously drawing exhaust gases through the same or connected passages, improving combustion efficiency, heat distribution and safety in household heating and cooking appliances.
[F24B 5/04] the air or gas passing downwards through the bottom of the stove of fire grate -- F24B 5/04 covers arrangements that direct combustion air or flue gases downward through the bottom of a solid-fuel domestic stove or range, typically via the fire grate. It addresses the design of air inlets, grate openings and flow paths that enhance mixing, combustion efficiency and heat transfer within the heating chamber.
[F24B 5/06] in or around ranges -- F24B 5/06 covers the circulation of combustion air or flue gases in or around domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges. The classification includes designs that direct airflow through the cooking chamber, chimney, or surrounding cavity to improve combustion efficiency or heat distribution.
[F24B 5/08] around the baking oven -- F24B 5/08 covers arrangements that direct combustion-air or flue-gas circulation around the baking oven of a solid-fuel domestic stove or range, aiming to improve heating uniformity or exhaust removal within the oven compartment. It pertains to airflow designs integrated with the oven structure.
[F24B 5/082] several ovens -- The F24B 5/082 classification relates to domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that incorporate several ovens, specifically addressing the circulation of combustion air or flue gases around those ovens to improve heating efficiency and control.
[F24B 5/085] the oven in arranged under the fire-box -- The F24B 5/085 subclass covers arrangements where the oven is positioned beneath the fire-box in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges. It relates specifically to designs that affect the circulation of combustion air or flue gases around the oven for improved heating efficiency.
[F24B 5/087] Regulable circulation -- F24B 5/087 relates to devices that allow the circulation of combustion air or flue gases in solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges to be adjusted, providing controllable flow rates for improved combustion efficiency or temperature regulation.
[F24B 7/00] Stoves, ranges or flue-gas ducts, with additional provisions for convection heating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Provisions for convection heating to improve the heat exchange between the stove or range and the surrounding. These provisions could be placed external, e.g. annular device surrounding a flue-gas duct or placed internal.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Stoves with open fires characterised by use of heat exchange means -> F24B1/188
[F24B 7/002] Ranges -- F24B 7/002 relates to domestic solid-fuel ranges that include extra elements for convection heating, such as built-in flue-gas ducts or specialized chambers designed to improve heat distribution within the appliance.
[F24B 7/005] Flue-gas ducts -- F24B 7/005 covers flue-gas ducts designed for domestic solid-fuel stoves and ranges that also provide convection heating, directing combustion gases from the firebox to the chimney while facilitating heat distribution into the cooking or living space. The ducts may be integral with the appliance or form part of a separate system that enhances overall heating efficiency.
[F24B 7/007] Stoves or ranges with additional provisions for heating both air and water -- This subclass includes domestic solid-fuel stoves and ranges that are equipped to heat both the surrounding air and water, typically by means of built-in water-heaters, heat exchangers or steam generators. The designs provide convection heating for the room while simultaneously supplying hot water for domestic use.
[F24B 7/02] with external air ducts -- This CPC subclass covers domestic solid-fuel stoves, ranges or flue-gas ducts that incorporate external air ducts to provide convection heating. It includes designs where air is drawn from or discharged to the outside through ducts separate from the main combustion chamber, improving heat distribution and efficiency.
[F24B 7/025] with forced circulation -- F24B 7/025 covers domestic stoves or ranges that burn solid fuels and incorporate a forced-circulation system, such as fans or blowers, to enhance convection heating and improve flue-gas flow. The code applies to designs that actively move air or gases to increase heating efficiency.
[F24B 7/04] with internal air ducts -- The F24B 7/04 subclass covers solid-fuel domestic stoves, ranges or flue-gas ducts that incorporate internal air ducts to create convection heating. The ducts channel hot gases through the appliance or surrounding space, enhancing heat distribution for more uniform cooking or room warming.
[F24B 7/045] with forced circulation -- This subclass covers solid-fuel domestic stoves, ranges or flue-gas ducts that incorporate a forced-circulation system to create convection heating, improving heat distribution within the appliance or its surroundings. It applies to designs where air movement is generated mechanically rather than by natural buoyancy.
[F24B 7/06] without air ducts -- This subclass covers solid-fuel domestic stoves or ranges that provide heating without the use of separate air-ducts, relying on natural convection or other internal airflow methods. It includes designs where flue-gas ducts may be present but no dedicated air-supply ducts are incorporated.
[F24B 9/00] Stoves, ranges or flue-gas ducts, with additional provisions for heating water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stoves or ranges comprising additional provisions for heating water. The water could be used as domestic water or for central heating.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Stoves with open fires with additional provisions for cooking -> F24B1/182 - Stoves with open fires for heating water -> F24B1/183
[F24B 9/003] Ranges -- F24B 9/003 covers domestic solid-fuel ranges that combine a cooking surface with water-heating capability, including the associated flue-gas ducts. These appliances are classified under mechanical-engineering heating for domestic stoves or ranges for solid fuels and provide both cooking and water-heating functions.
[F24B 9/006] flue-gas ducts -- This classification covers ducts, channels, or passageways that convey combustion gases from domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges to the outside or to a heat-exchange device. It includes designs for safe removal of flue gases, integration with water-heating systems, and related components such as exhaust pipes.
[F24B 9/02] in open containers, e.g. bain-marie -- F24B 9/02 covers domestic solid-fuel stoves or ranges that heat water using open vessels, such as a bain-marie or similar container placed within the cooking chamber. The invention relates to the arrangement of flue-gas ducts or heating elements to provide indirect, evenly distributed heat for water heating.
[F24B 9/04] in closed containers -- This subclass covers domestic solid-fuel stoves, ranges or flue-gas duct systems that heat water using a heating element placed inside a sealed container, isolating the water from direct exposure to the combustion chamber. The closed-container design improves safety and efficiency by containing the heating medium.
[F24C] DOMESTIC STOVES OR RANGES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Domestic stoves or ranges in which the fuel is not restricted to solid fuel, e.g. gaseous fuels, liquid fuels or electricity is used.  Details for such stoves or ranges.  Cooktops.  Devices for removing cooking fumes, e.g. extractor hoods.  Baking ovens with convection heat.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Domestic stoves or ranges exclusively for solid fuels -> F24B  **Glossary:** - Stove: appliance used for generating heat - Range, kitchen stove, cooking stove, cookstove or cooker: kitchen appliance designed to cook food. Such a device has both burners on the top (also known in American English as the cooktop and, in British English, the hob) and, often, an oven - Cooktop: has burners on the top and is usually installed into a countertop - Drop-in range: has both burners on the top and an oven and hangs from a cutout in the countertop (that is, it cannot be installed free-standing on its own)
[F24C 1/00] Stoves or ranges in which the fuel or energy supply is not restricted to solid fuel or to a type covered by a single one of the following groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stoves or ranges which are adapted for the use of two or more kinds of fuel or energy supply.  Stoves or ranges solely adapted for radiation heating.
[F24C 1/02] adapted for the use of two or more kinds of fuel or energy supply -- F24C 1/02 is a subclass of heating equipment for domestic stoves or ranges that are engineered to run on two or more different fuels or energy sources, rather than being limited to a single type such as solid fuel only. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stove categories.
[F24C 1/04] simultaneously -- F24C 1/04 covers domestic stoves or ranges designed to operate with more than one type of fuel or energy source at the same time, rather than being limited to a single fuel type such as solid fuel. The classification applies to heating appliances that can simultaneously draw on multiple energy supplies.
[F24C 1/06] by replacing parts, e.g. replacing burners by electric heaters -- F24C 1/06 covers domestic stoves or ranges that allow replacement of heating components, such as swapping traditional burners for electric heating elements, enabling the appliance to operate with different energy sources.
[F24C 1/08] solely adapted for radiation heating -- F24C 1/08 covers domestic stoves or ranges that are designed to operate with multiple fuel or energy types rather than a single specific fuel, and that heat solely by radiation rather than convection or other methods. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stove categories in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F24C 1/10] with reflectors -- The entry F24C 1/10 refers to domestic stoves or ranges that are not limited to a single fuel type and that incorporate reflectors to direct radiated heat toward the cooking zone, thereby improving heating efficiency and uniformity.
[F24C 11/00] Combinations of two or more stoves or ranges, e.g. each having a different kind of energy supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of two or more stoves or ranges, e.g. each having a different kind of energy supply.
[F24C 1/12] of circular shape -- This CPC subclass defines circular-shaped domestic stoves or ranges whose fuel or energy supply is not limited to solid fuel or to any single fuel type covered by other groups. It is positioned under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Domestic Stoves or Ranges.
[F24C 1/14] Radiation heating stoves and ranges, with additional provision for convection heating -- This subclass covers domestic cooking appliances that primarily heat by radiant energy while also providing supplemental convection heating, and which are not limited to a single type of solid fuel or energy source.
[F24C 1/16] with special adaptation for travelling, e.g. collapsible -- This subclass covers portable stoves or ranges designed for travel, featuring collapsible or otherwise adaptable structures, and does not limit the fuel type to solid fuels or a single fuel group. It falls under mechanical engineering heating domestic stoves or ranges.
[F24C 13/00] Stoves or ranges with additional provisions for heating water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stoves or range wherein residual heat is used for heating water.
[F24C 14/00] Stoves or ranges having self-cleaning provisions, e.g. continuous catalytic cleaning or electrostatic cleaning -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pyrolytic cooking ovens.  Stoves or ranges wherein a cleaning liquid is used.
[F24C 14/005] using a cleaning liquid -- This subgroup relates to domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate self-cleaning features and utilise a cleaning liquid, such as a detergent or solvent, to dissolve and remove cooking residues during the cleaning cycle. It covers designs where the liquid is applied automatically or manually as part of the stove's cleaning process.
[F24C 14/02] pyrolytic type -- Pyrolytic type stoves or ranges incorporate self-cleaning provisions such as continuous catalytic cleaning or electrostatic cleaning, using high-temperature oxidation to burn off food residues. This technology is classified under CPC code F24C 14/02 within the mechanical engineering heating domain for domestic stoves or ranges.
[F24C 14/025] for gaseous fuels -- This CPC subclass covers domestic stoves or ranges that operate on gaseous fuels and incorporate self-cleaning mechanisms, such as continuous catalytic or electrostatic cleaning, within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic cooking appliances.
[F24C 15/00] Details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of stoves or ranges such as: arrangements for circulation of cooling air to avoid excessive heat in the housing or door, illumination of oven cavities, doors specially adapted for stoves or ranges, shelves or racks used in cooking ovens.  Cooking hot plate using gas or electrical energy.  Devices for removing cooking fumes, either from a cooking cavity or from a cooking area, e.g. extractor hoods.  Arrangements of ducts for hot gases in or around baking ovens, e.g. convection baking ovens.
[F24C 15/001] arrangements for discharging combustion gases -- F24C 15/001 covers designs for venting or exhausting combustion gases from domestic stoves or ranges. It includes flues, chimneys, ducts and other mechanisms that safely transport smoke and gases away from the cooking area, preventing buildup and ensuring proper airflow.
[F24C 15/002] for stoves of the closed type -- This subclass covers closed-type stoves, where the cooking chamber is sealed during operation and combustion gases pass through a heat exchanger before being vented, improving efficiency and safety. It is situated in the Mechanical Engineering hierarchy under Heating and Domestic Stoves or Ranges.
[F24C 15/003] moisturising of air -- F24C 15/003 addresses techniques for adding moisture to the air inside domestic stoves or ranges. It includes steam injection, water reservoirs and humidification devices that improve cooking results or heat distribution. The entry falls under mechanical engineering - heating - domestic stoves or ranges.
[F24C 15/004] Windows not in a door -- F24C 15/004 covers heating appliances such as domestic stoves, ranges or ovens that incorporate a glazed opening or window that is separate from, and not formed as part of, the main door, allowing visual observation of the combustion chamber without opening the door.
[F24C 15/005] Coatings for ovens -- The CPC subclass F24C 15/005 falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic stoves or ranges and covers coatings applied to ovens, including protective, non-stick, heat-reflective or decorative layers that improve performance, durability, cleaning or energy efficiency.
[F24C 15/006] Arrangements for circulation of cooling air -- The CPC entry F24C 15/006 covers designs that direct cooling air through domestic stoves or ranges. It includes any arrangement of ducts, fans, vents or natural airflow paths intended to remove heat generated during cooking, thereby protecting components and improving temperature control.
[F24C 15/007] Removable panels -- A removable panel is a component of a domestic stove or range that can be taken off for cleaning, maintenance or replacement. The CPC subclass F24C 15/007 covers such panels within heating equipment for household cooking appliances.
[F24C 15/008] Illumination for oven cavities -- The code F24C 15/008 covers solutions that provide interior lighting inside the cooking chamber of domestic ovens and ranges, such as lamps, light-emitting diodes, illumination windows or associated control mechanisms. It includes the arrangement, activation and integration of these lighting elements to enhance visibility of the oven cavity during use.
[F24C 15/02] Doors specially adapted for stoves or ranges -- F24C 15/02 covers doors that are specifically designed for use on domestic stoves or ranges. These doors may incorporate heat-resistant materials, sealing features, or safety mechanisms to contain combustion gases and improve energy efficiency.
[F24C 15/021] sealings for doors or transparent panel -- This CPC subclass covers sealing solutions for doors or transparent panels of domestic stoves, ranges and similar heating appliances, intended to prevent heat loss, smoke or flame leakage and to improve safety and energy efficiency; it belongs to the broader door and window sealing category within heating devices.
[F24C 15/022] Latches -- F24C 15/022 relates to latch devices used on domestic stoves or ranges, such as mechanisms that secure doors or panels during cooking, cleaning or transport. It falls within the mechanical-engineering heating category that addresses household cooking appliances.
[F24C 15/023] Mounting of doors, e.g. hinges, counterbalancing -- F24C 15/023 covers the mounting of doors on domestic stoves and ranges, including hinge arrangements, pivot mechanisms and counterbalancing devices that assist door opening and closing. The classification addresses structural features that secure the door to the appliance while allowing controlled movement and alignment during operation.
[F24C 15/024] Handles -- F24C 15/024 covers the design and configuration of handles used on domestic stoves or ranges. It includes grips, knobs, levers or pull-out mechanisms that allow users to open doors, adjust settings or move components. The subclass addresses material, shape and attachment methods relevant to cooking appliances.
[F24C 15/025] with air curtain closures -- F24C 15/025 covers domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate an air-curtain closure, a device that creates a thin stream of air across the opening to limit heat loss when the door or lid is open, improving energy efficiency and safety in household cooking appliances.
[F24C 15/026] stowing of door in open position -- F24C 15/026 belongs to the mechanical-engineering heating group for domestic stoves or ranges and covers devices that hold a stove or range door in an open, stowed position, allowing hands-free operation while cooking. It relates to mechanisms that lock or support the door after it has been opened.
[F24C 15/027] located at bottom side of housing -- The CPC subclass F24C 15/027 covers domestic stoves or ranges in which the heating or burner unit is positioned on the lower side of the appliance housing, describing a bottom-side placement of the firebox, burner or heating plate.
[F24C 15/028] Stoves doors -- F24C 15/028 covers the design, construction and functional aspects of doors for domestic stoves or ranges, including door panels, hinges, handles, sealing mechanisms and materials used to improve heat containment and safety.
[F24C 15/04] with transparent panels -- F24C 15/04 covers domestic heating appliances such as stoves, ranges or ovens that incorporate transparent panels, typically glass doors or windows, to allow visual observation of the cooking space while the device operates. This sub-group refines the broader categories of mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stoves or ranges by specifying the presence of viewable panels.
[F24C 15/045] being dismountable, e.g. giving access for cleaning -- F24C 15/045 covers domestic stoves or ranges designed to be dismountable, providing convenient access for cleaning components such as burners, grates or interior surfaces. It is a subcategory of heating equipment within mechanical engineering.
[F24C 15/06] Ornamental features, e.g. grate fronts or surrounds -- The CPC class F24C 15/06 covers decorative elements applied to domestic stove or range components such as grill fronts, ornamental surrounds, and other aesthetic features that enhance appearance without affecting function. These features may be made from metal, glass, or other materials and are intended primarily for visual appeal.
[F24C 15/08] Foundations or supports plates -- F24C 15/08 covers plates that serve as foundations or supporting elements for domestic stoves and ranges. These plates provide a stable base, distribute the appliance's weight, and often incorporate mounting features that attach the unit to the floor or surrounding structure, facilitating safe installation and efficient heat transfer.
[F24C 15/083] Anti-tip arrangements -- F24C 15/083 refers to anti-tip arrangements for domestic stoves or ranges, covering devices that prevent the appliance from tipping over during use, such as stabilizing brackets, weighted bases, or locking mechanisms.
[F24C 15/086] Adjustable legs or pillars -- Adjustable legs or pillars are components that allow the height or level of a domestic stove or range to be modified for stable installation, alignment with countertops, or ergonomic use. They typically incorporate threaded, telescoping, or pivoting mechanisms to accommodate uneven floors or user preferences.
[F24C 15/10] Tops, e.g. hot plates -- F24C 15/10 covers removable or fixed heating surfaces positioned on the top of a stove or range, such as electric or gas hot plates used for cooking or warming. These tops serve as supplemental or primary heating elements separate from built-in burners.
[F24C 15/101] provisions for circulation of air -- F24C 15/101 covers the means for moving air within domestic stoves or ranges, such as fans, ducts, vents or circulation passages that distribute combustion gases or heat throughout the appliance. These provisions improve heat distribution, combustion efficiency and user safety by ensuring proper airflow.
[F24C 15/102] electrically heated -- This subgroup of the mechanical-engineering heating hierarchy covers domestic cooking appliances that generate heat directly from electrical resistance, such as electric stoves, ranges and ovens, and includes the design and arrangement of their electric heating elements.
[F24C 15/103] being movable or rotatable -- The CPC class F24C 15/103 covers domestic stoves and ranges that include components designed to move or rotate, such as swivel burners, turnable oven racks, rotatable grills, or pivoting doors, enabling adjustment of cooking position or access. It focuses on the mechanical features that allow these parts to change orientation during operation.
[F24C 15/104] Arrangements of connectors, grounding, supply wires -- The CPC subclass F24C 15/104 covers the design and layout of electrical connectors, grounding methods and supply wiring for domestic stoves or ranges. It includes the positioning of power plugs, internal wiring schemes, protective earth connections and arrangement of supply cables that ensure safe and reliable operation of heating appliances.
[F24C 15/105] Constructive details concerning the regulation of the temperature -- The subclass F24C 15/105 concerns the structural and design features of domestic stoves or ranges that are used to control temperature, including mechanisms, sensors, and other engineering elements that adjust heat output. It addresses how temperature-regulating components are integrated into kitchen heating appliances.
[F24C 15/106] electric circuits -- This CPC subclass deals with the electrical circuitry that powers and controls domestic cooking appliances such as stoves and ranges. It includes wiring diagrams, control switches, temperature regulators, and safety devices that manage the operation of heating elements. The subclass covers design and integration of power supplies, thermostats, and electronic control modules for household cooking equipment.
[F24C 15/107] Pan supports or grates therefor -- The code F24C 15/107 falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stoves or ranges. It covers devices that hold or support pans, including grates or other structures used to position cookware within a stove or range. These components aid in even heating and secure placement of pans during cooking.
[F24C 15/108] Mounting of hot plate on worktop -- The code F24C 15/108 covers arrangements for fixing a hot plate directly onto a kitchen worktop, including methods of attachment, reinforcement and integration with the surrounding surface. It pertains to domestic cooking appliances within the heating and stove subgroup of mechanical engineering.
[F24C 15/12] Side rests -- Side rests are structural components attached to domestic stoves or ranges that support cookware or utensils, positioned at the sides of the cooking surface. They provide a convenient resting place, improving safety and ergonomics.
[F24C 15/14] Spillage trays or grooves -- F24C 15/14 covers spillage trays, channels or grooves incorporated in domestic stoves or ranges to catch liquid or food overflow, protect burners and facilitate cleaning. The feature is part of heating equipment in the mechanical-engineering field.
[F24C 15/16] Shelves, racks or trays inside ovens -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group covers shelves, racks and related accessories like rotisserie spits, trays and supports to be used inside ovens.
[F24C 15/162] Co-operating with a door, e.g. operated by the door -- F24C 15/162 classifies stove or range designs in which the door is linked to other functions, such as automatically controlling heating, ventilation, safety or ignition when the door is opened or closed. It encompasses mechanisms that are activated by the door's movement, providing coordinated operation of the appliance.
[F24C 15/164] Rotisserie spits inside ovens -- The F24C 15/164 classification covers rotisserie spits that are integrated into ovens, including their mounting, drive mechanisms, and control systems for rotating food during cooking. It applies to domestic stoves or ranges where the rotisserie function is built into the oven cavity.
[F24C 15/166] with integrated heating means -- F24C 15/166 covers domestic stoves or ranges where the heating element is built into the appliance itself, such as ovens, grills, or hob burners that incorporate the heat source directly rather than using separate burners or external heaters. This subcategory pertains to designs that integrate the heating means within the cooking unit.
[F24C 15/168] with telescopic rail systems -- The subclass F24C 15/168 covers domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate telescopic rail systems, such as sliding oven or drawer mechanisms that extend and retract to facilitate loading and unloading. These features are classified under heating appliances in the mechanical engineering sector.
[F24C 15/18] Arrangement of compartments additional to cooking compartments, e.g. for warming or for storing utensils or fuel containers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangement of compartments additional to cooking compartments, e.g. for warming, for storing utensils or fuel containers; Arrangements of additional heating or cooking apparatus, e.g. grills, and additional cooking arrangements on stoves.
[F24C 15/20] Removing cooking fumes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for removing cooking fumes from cooking cavities or from cooking ranges, e.g. using an extractor hood.
[F24C 15/2007] from oven cavities -- F24C 15/2007 pertains to the design, construction or treatment of oven cavities in domestic stoves or ranges, covering aspects such as heating element arrangement, insulation and the geometry of the cooking space.
[F24C 15/2014] with means for oxidation of cooking fumes -- F24C 15/2014 covers domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate devices to oxidise cooking fumes, such as catalytic converters or thermal oxidisers, to reduce odours and pollutants. It falls under the mechanical-engineering heating group for household cooking appliances. This subclass specifies the integration of fume-treatment technology within the stove or range design.
[F24C 15/2021] Arrangement or mounting of control or safety systems -- The subclass F24C 15/2021 covers the design and placement of control or safety components in domestic stoves and ranges, including the mounting of thermostats, switches, flame-failure devices, and other protective systems within the appliance housing. It addresses how these elements are arranged to ensure reliable operation and user safety.
[F24C 15/2028] using an air curtain -- The subclass F24C 15/2028 covers domestic stoves or ranges that employ an air curtain, a thin stream of air directed across the opening to limit heat loss, prevent drafts, and improve combustion efficiency. It includes designs where the air curtain is generated by the appliance itself or by an auxiliary fan to create a barrier between the fire chamber and the surrounding environment.
[F24C 15/2035] Arrangement or mounting of filters  F24C15/2021  takes precedence; filters  per se   B01D46/00 -- This subclass covers the arrangement or mounting of filters in domestic stoves or ranges, describing how filter units are positioned, secured, or integrated within the heating appliance. It is a subset of mechanical-engineering heating for household cooking equipment, subordinate to F24C15/2021 and distinct from filter substances classified under B01D46/00.
[F24C 15/2042] Devices for removing cooking fumes structurally associated with a cooking range e.g. downdraft  F24C15/2007  takes precedence -- F24C 15/2042 covers devices that remove cooking fumes and are structurally integrated with a cooking range, for example downdraft ventilation systems. It is situated within the hierarchy mechanical engineering → heating → domestic stoves or ranges. This entry narrows the broader subclass F24C15/2007.
[F24C 15/205] with means for oxidation of cooking fumes -- F24C 15/205 relates to domestic stoves or ranges that include a means for oxidising cooking fumes, such as catalytic converters or high-temperature burners that convert pollutants into less harmful gases, thereby improving indoor air quality by reducing odorous or hazardous emissions from cooking.
[F24C 15/2057] using a cleaning liquid -- The code F24C 15/2057 covers methods for cleaning domestic stoves or ranges by applying a liquid cleaning agent, including how the liquid is distributed, acts on residues and is removed during or after cooking. It is a sub-category of mechanical engineering heating for household cooking appliances.
[F24C 15/2064] illumination for cooking hood -- F24C 15/2064 concerns lighting devices incorporated into cooking hoods to illuminate the cooking surface while the hood extracts smoke and steam. The code is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating equipment, specifically domestic stoves or ranges.
[F24C 15/2071] mounting of cooking hood -- F24C 15/2071 relates to the way a cooking hood is attached to a domestic stove or range. It covers methods, brackets, supports, and structural arrangements used to secure the hood for effective removal of vapors and smoke. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stoves or ranges.
[F24C 15/2078] movable -- This subclass pertains to domestic stoves or ranges whose heating unit or enclosure can be moved, including portable, slide-out or detachable cooking appliances and designs that allow repositioning of the stove within a kitchen or for outdoor use.
[F24C 15/2085] adjustable in height -- F24C 15/2085 covers domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate mechanisms allowing the cooking surface or components to be raised or lowered to suit user preference or ergonomic needs. The adjustability may involve sliding frames, telescoping supports, or hinged sections that modify the height of burners, grills, or oven doors.
[F24C 15/2092] extendable or pivotable -- F24C 15/2092 covers domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate extendable or pivotable components, such as oven doors, grates, or heating elements that can be moved outward or rotated to facilitate loading, unloading, or cleaning.
[F24C 15/22] Reflectors for radiation heaters -- F24C 15/22 covers reflectors used in radiation heaters, particularly in domestic stoves and ranges, that redirect emitted heat toward the cooking area or surrounding space. The classification includes designs, materials, and configurations of reflective surfaces that enhance energy efficiency and heating performance.
[F24C 15/24] Radiant bodies or panels for radiation heaters -- F24C 15/24 pertains to the design and use of radiant bodies or panels that serve as the heating elements in radiation heaters, such as those incorporated in domestic stoves and ranges. It covers the form, material and mounting of these components for efficient heat emission.
[F24C 15/26] Handles for carrying -- F24C 15/26 concerns handles intended for carrying domestic stoves or ranges. It falls under mechanical engineering heating equipment and addresses designs that facilitate safe, ergonomic movement of the appliance.
[F24C 15/28] Draught shields -- The CPC entry F24C 15/28 defines draught shields, components fitted in domestic stoves or ranges to control and direct airflow. By shaping the combustion chamber's draft, these shields improve fuel efficiency, reduce heat loss and help maintain a steady flame. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Domestic Stoves or Ranges.
[F24C 15/30] Arrangements for mounting stoves or ranges in particular locations -- F24C 15/30 covers designs and methods for installing domestic stoves or ranges in specific positions within a kitchen or building, addressing brackets, supports, recesses, and placement relative to countertops, walls, or ventilation. It includes configurations that facilitate secure mounting, ergonomic access, and compliance with safety standards.
[F24C 15/32] Arrangements of ducts for hot gases, e.g. in or around baking ovens -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Baking ovens comprising convection heating using forced circulation.  Baking ovens comprising air moisturising means, e.g. steam ovens.
[F24C 15/322] with forced circulation -- The subclass covers domestic cooking appliances such as stoves or ranges that incorporate a forced circulation system to move heat or combustion gases through the cooking chamber, improving temperature uniformity and efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stoves or ranges, and is identified by CPC code F24C 15/322.
[F24C 15/325] electrically-heated -- The CPC code F24C 15/325 classifies domestic stoves or ranges that are heated by electricity. It includes cooking appliances such as electric ovens, stovetops and ranges where the heat is generated by resistive or induction elements rather than gas or other fuels.
[F24C 15/327] with air moisturising -- This subgroup covers domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate a device for adding moisture to the heated air, such as a steam or water-vapour generator, to improve cooking or heating performance. It is a specialization of heating equipment within the mechanical-engineering classification for household appliances.
