[F24C 7/002] Stoves  F24C7/02  -  F24C7/10  take precedence -- The CPC subclass F24C 7/002 concerns electric heating elements and associated control devices used in domestic stoves and ranges, covering designs that provide cooking or baking functions powered by electricity. It falls under mechanical engineering → heating → domestic stoves or ranges and has priority over the broader subdivisions F24C 7/02 to F24C 7/10.
[F24C 7/004] simulating flames
[F24C 7/006] using electrically heated liquids -- The entry F24C 7/006 covers domestic stoves or ranges that heat liquids by means of electric energy, such as water, oil or other cooking fluids heated with electric elements. It includes designs where the liquid itself is the primary heating medium for cooking or warming food.
[F24C 7/008] using electrode heaters -- The subclass F24C 7/008 covers domestic electric stoves or ranges that generate heat by means of electrode heaters, i.e., resistive elements that act as electrodes within the cooking surface. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic stoves or ranges, and electric-energy-heated appliances.
[F24C 7/02] using microwaves -- This subclass concerns domestic cooking appliances that use microwave energy as the primary heating source in electric stoves or ranges, covering designs, control mechanisms and methods for generating and directing microwave radiation to cook or heat food.
[F24C 7/04] with heat radiated directly from the heating element -- F24C 7/04 covers electric domestic stoves or ranges in which the heating element radiates heat directly to the cookware or food without an intermediate medium such as air or water. It is a subclass of mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic stoves or ranges, specifying designs that use direct radiant heating from the electric element.
[F24C 7/043] Stoves -- F24C 7/043 covers domestic stoves or ranges that generate heat using electric energy. The code applies to appliances such as electric ovens, cooktops and combined stovetop-oven units designed for household cooking and heating.
[F24C 7/046] Ranges -- The CPC entry F24C 7/046 covers domestic cooking ranges that are powered by electricity. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Domestic Stoves or Ranges, specifying electric-energy heating rather than gas or solid fuels. This classification is used by the EPO and USPTO to index patents for electric kitchen ranges.
[F24C 7/06] Arrangement or mounting of electric heating elements -- This CPC subclass concerns the arrangement and mounting of electric heating elements in domestic stoves or ranges, covering how the elements are positioned, supported, or integrated within the appliance. It includes brackets, fixtures, and placement strategies that ensure effective heating and safe operation in electrically powered cooking equipment.
[F24C 7/062] on stoves -- F24C 7/062 covers domestic stoves or ranges that are heated by electric energy, describing the design and operation of electric heating elements, control systems, and safety features specific to household cooking appliances. It falls within the mechanical engineering hierarchy under heating and domestic stoves or ranges.
[F24C 7/065] with reflectors  reflectors  per se   F24C15/22 -- This subclass pertains to electric domestic stoves or ranges that incorporate reflective surfaces to direct or concentrate radiant heat, improving cooking efficiency or protecting surrounding components. The reflectors are treated as a distinct element of the heating appliance.
[F24C 7/067] on ranges -- The CPC subclass F24C 7/067 covers features and arrangements that are specifically applied to domestic ranges, i.e., kitchen cooking appliances that combine multiple burners or cooking zones. It includes structural or functional aspects of the range such as the layout of the cooking surface, integration with ovens, control panels and safety devices, and any innovations that affect how the range heats and cooks food. This subclass falls under heating for domestic stoves or ranges powered by electricity.
[F24C 7/08] Arrangement or mounting of control or safety devices -- F24C 7/08 relates to electric stoves or ranges and specifies the way control and safety devices such as thermostats, switches, timers or circuit breakers are arranged and mounted within the appliance. It covers the positioning, mounting methods and integration of these components with the heating elements or user interface to ensure proper operation and safety.
[F24C 7/081] on stoves -- The CPC subclass F24C 7/081 pertains to domestic electric stoves or ranges, detailing specific features or components that are mounted on the appliance. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating devices used in household cooking.
[F24C 7/082] on ranges, e.g. control panels, illumination -- This classification pertains to electric domestic stoves or ranges and includes aspects such as control panels and illumination systems for the cooking appliance. It falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy, heating, domestic stoves or ranges.
[F24C 7/083] on tops, hot plates -- The subclass F24C 7/083 covers domestic stoves or ranges that are powered by electricity and specifically addresses the design of hot plates mounted on the top surface of the appliance, including their heating elements, controls and safety features. It falls under the mechanical engineering group for heating equipment.
[F24C 7/085] on baking ovens -- F24C 7/085 covers baking ovens that are part of domestic stoves or ranges powered by electric energy, falling under the mechanical-engineering category for heating appliances. It addresses designs, components, and methods specific to electric-heated baking chambers used in household cooking equipment.
[F24C 7/086] touch control -- F24C 7/086 covers touch-control systems for electric domestic stoves or ranges, including capacitive, resistive or similar touch-sensitive interfaces that allow users to set temperature, power level or cooking programs by contact with a sensor surface. It pertains to the design and integration of such user-interface elements in electric cooking appliances.
[F24C 7/087] of electric circuits regulating heat -- F24C 7/087 relates to electric control circuits that regulate the heating of domestic electric stoves or ranges, covering thermostats, electronic controllers and associated circuitry used to maintain the desired cooking temperature.
[F24C 7/088] on stoves -- The CPC subclass F24C 7/088 covers domestic stoves or ranges that are heated by electric energy, focusing on the design, arrangement and control of electric heating elements within these appliances. It includes features such as electric burners, heating coils, thermostatic regulation and safety mechanisms specific to household cooking equipment.
[F24C 7/10] with special adaptation for travelling, e.g. collapsible -- This subclass covers electric stoves or ranges that are specifically designed for portable or travelling use, such as models that can be folded, collapsed, or otherwise compacted for transport. The adaptation focuses on structural features that allow the appliance to be disassembled or reduced in size without affecting its heating function.
[F24C 7/105] Stoves -- The entry F24C 7/105 covers electric domestic stoves, including kitchen ranges and countertop units that use electrical heating elements for cooking. It addresses the design, construction, control mechanisms and safety features of appliances that convert electric energy into heat for preparing food.
[F24C 9/00] Stoves or ranges heated by a single type of energy supply not covered by groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stoves and ranges using a single type of energy supply not covered by groups F24C3/00 - F24C7/00 or subclass F24B .  Stoves or ranges using the heat from an exothermal reaction not involving a supply of free oxygen gas.  Stoves or ranges using special gaseous fuel, e.g. acetylene and hydrogen.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Solar heat collectors for heating objects, e.g. solar cookers -> F24S20/30
[F24D] DOMESTIC- OR SPACE-HEATING SYSTEMS, e.g. CENTRAL HEATING SYSTEMS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems for domestic - or space heating, in which heat is generated at one location and transmitted by a circuit to a consumer at a different location, of the following kind:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Steam engine plants using steam or condensate extracted or exhausted from steam engine plants for heating purposes -> F01K17/02  **Glossary:** - Central heating system: a system in which heat is generated or stored at central sources inside a building and is distributed by means of a transfer fluid to the spaces or areas to be heated within the building - District heating system (less commonly called teleheating): a system for distributing heat generated in a distant centralized location outside a building and distributed by means of a transfer fluid to multiple buildings or facilities - Domestic hot water heating system: a system for heating tap water coming from the main water supply for domestic use other than space heating, e.g. cooking, cleaning, bathing
[F24D 1/00] Central Heating Systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Central heating systems comprising steam as heat transport medium and combinations thereof:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - District heating systems -> F24D10/00 - Central heating systems using heat accumulated in storage masses -> F24D11/00
[F24D 10/00] District heating systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  District heating systems with:  "District heating system" (less commonly called teleheating) is a system for distributing heat generated in a distant centralized location outside a building and distributed by means of a transfer fluid to multiple buildings or facilities. In the normal central heating systems, the heat source is located within the building.
[F24D 10/003] Domestic delivery stations having a heat exchanger -- Domestic delivery stations equipped with heat exchangers are units in district-heating networks that receive hot water or steam from the central plant and transfer its thermal energy to a secondary circuit serving individual buildings or neighborhoods. They regulate flow and temperature, allowing the primary network to operate at optimal conditions while providing safe, controllable heat to end users. This enables efficient distribution of heating for residential or commercial premises.
[F24D 10/006] Direct domestic delivery stations -- F24D 10/006 relates to components of district-heating networks that receive heat from a central plant and supply it directly to individual homes or apartments. It covers direct domestic delivery stations such as heat-exchange substations, pipe-side regulators and control units that transfer the medium to building-level distribution. These devices enable efficient, on-site provision of heating within centralized heating systems.
[F24D 1/005] in combination with systems for domestic water supply -- The CPC subclass F24D 1/005 covers central heating installations that are combined with domestic water-supply systems, such as boilers or heat-exchangers that provide space heating alongside hot-water distribution for household use. It addresses the integrated design and operation of these dual-purpose heating solutions.
[F24D 1/02] operating with live steam -- The CPC subclass F24D 1/02 covers central heating installations that use live steam as the heating medium, encompassing boilers, steam distribution networks, radiators and related controls designed to supply heat directly from steam generated on site. This falls under mechanical engineering, heating and domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 1/04] operating with exhaust steam -- This CPC subgroup covers central heating installations that use exhaust steam, typically recovered from industrial processes or power generation, as the heat source for distributing warmth to buildings. It includes systems that collect, condition and circulate the steam to radiators or other heat-emitters within domestic or space-heating networks.
[F24D 1/06] operating with superheated steam -- F24D 1/06 covers central heating systems that use superheated steam as the heat-transfer medium. It includes the generation, distribution and control of superheated steam for space heating, as well as related safety and efficiency measures specific to such systems.
[F24D 1/08] Feed-line arrangements, e.g. providing for heat-accumulator tanks, expansion tanks -- F24D 1/08 covers feed-line arrangements for central heating systems, such as the inclusion of heat-accumulator tanks or expansion tanks that manage fluid flow and pressure. These configurations ensure stable operation and accommodate thermal expansion in domestic or space-heating circuits.
[F24D 11/00] Central heating systems using heat accumulated in storage masses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Central heating systems using heat accumulated in storage masses, in particular:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Self-contained storage heating units -> F24D15/02
[F24D 11/001] district heating system -- District heating systems that convey heat generated at a central plant to a network of multiple end-users through insulated pipelines, often incorporating storage masses to accumulate heat for later distribution. The classification covers the design, control, and operation of such centralized heating networks for residential, commercial, or industrial purposes.
[F24D 11/002] water heating system -- The CPC subclass F24D 11/002 pertains to water heating systems that employ a storage mass to accumulate thermal energy for central heating. It includes arrangements such as heat-storage tanks, accumulator-based boilers and related controls that release stored heat to supply domestic or space heating.
[F24D 11/003] combined with solar energy -- Central heating systems that store thermal energy in masses such as water tanks, concrete or phase-change materials and incorporate solar energy as a supplemental source are covered by this class. The classification applies to designs where solar collectors feed heat into the storage or directly assist the heating circuit, enabling efficient use of both stored and solar heat.
[F24D 11/004] with conventional supplementary heat source -- F24D 11/004 covers central heating installations that store thermal energy in a mass-such as water tanks, concrete or brick-to provide space heating, and also incorporate a conventional auxiliary heat source (for example a boiler or furnace) to supplement the stored heat when needed. This allows continuous heating while reducing reliance on the primary source.
[F24D 11/005] with recuperation of waste heat -- F24D 11/005 pertains to central heating systems that incorporate a storage mass to accumulate heat and include mechanisms for recovering waste heat, thereby enhancing overall energy efficiency. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 11/006] air heating system -- F24D 11/006 covers central heating arrangements that warm air by drawing it through a heat-accumulating storage mass, such as a concrete slab or brick block, and then distributing the heated air throughout a building for space heating.
[F24D 11/007] combined with solar energy -- F24D 11/007 covers central heating installations that store thermal energy in mass such as water or stone and integrate solar collectors to supply heat, using the stored heat for domestic or space heating.
[F24D 11/008] with conventional supplementary heat source -- This subclass covers central heating installations that store thermal energy in masses such as water tanks or solid materials and supplement the stored heat with a conventional source like a boiler or furnace when additional heating is required.
[F24D 11/009] with recuperation of waste heat -- F24D 11/009 covers central heating installations that incorporate storage masses to capture and reuse waste heat. The system stores excess thermal energy during periods of low demand and releases it later to maintain comfortable temperatures, improving overall efficiency and reducing fuel consumption.
[F24D 11/02] using heat pumps -- F24D 11/02 covers central heating installations that employ heat-pump technology to extract thermal energy and store it in a mass-based heat-accumulator, supplying space heating for residential or commercial buildings. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic-or-space-heating systems.
[F24D 11/0207] district heating system -- The CPC subclass F24D 11/0207 covers district heating systems that distribute heat accumulated in thermal storage masses to a network of buildings. It relates to centralized heating infrastructure where stored thermal energy is supplied through a pipe network for space- or water heating, enabling efficient use of large-scale storage in residential or mixed-use districts.
[F24D 11/0214] water heating system -- The F24D 11/0214 subclass refers to water heating systems that store thermal energy in a mass-such as tanks, bricks or phase-change material-and release that heat for domestic central heating. It covers designs that accumulate heat during off-peak periods or from renewable sources and then use the stored energy to warm water and space in residential or similar settings.
[F24D 11/0221] combined with solar energy -- F24D 11/0221 covers central heating installations that store heat in thermal masses such as water tanks, bricks or other reservoirs and integrate solar-energy collectors to supplement or replace conventional fuel sources, allowing solar heat to be accumulated and later released for space heating.
[F24D 11/0228] combined with conventional heater -- The code refers to central heating installations that store thermal energy in a mass and supplement that stored heat with a conventional heater, such as a boiler, to satisfy heating demand. It covers systems where the stored heat is used in combination with a regular heating source.
[F24D 11/0235] with recuperation of waste energy -- This subclass relates to central heating installations that store accumulated heat in a thermal mass and include a system for recuperating waste energy to improve overall efficiency. It applies to domestic or space-heating systems where recovered heat is reused within the heating cycle.
[F24D 11/0242] contained in exhausted air -- F24D 11/0242 covers central heating installations that store thermal energy in a solid or liquid mass and recover heat from the air that is expelled from the system, using the exhausted air to transfer stored heat back into the heating circuit. This subclass relates to domestic or space-heating schemes where the exhaust stream acts as a source of reclaimed warmth.
[F24D 11/025] contained in waste water
[F24D 11/0257] air heating system -- F24D 11/0257 relates to air heating systems that are part of central heating installations using stored thermal mass to provide space heating. It covers devices and methods for heating and distributing air in domestic or other buildings where the heat source is accumulated in a mass before being conveyed to the occupied area.
[F24D 11/0264] combined with solar energy -- F24D 11/0264 relates to central heating installations that store heat in a thermal mass and are designed to be supplemented by solar energy as an additional heat source.
[F24D 11/0271] combined with conventional energy -- The entry covers central heating installations that store thermal energy in a mass and operate together with conventional energy supplies, such as gas or electricity, to provide domestic or space heating. It specifies how the stored heat is integrated with the primary fuel source to deliver a combined heating solution.
[F24D 11/0278] with recuperation of waste energy -- A central-heating system that stores heat in a thermal mass and includes a recuperator to capture and reuse waste energy, thereby increasing the overall efficiency of the heating cycle.
[F24D 11/0285] contained in exhausted air -- F24D 11/0285 relates to central heating installations that recuperate heat from the air leaving the system, using the exhaust stream as a source for stored-heat release. It addresses methods and devices that capture this contained heat to improve overall efficiency of domestic or space-heating networks.
[F24D 11/0292] contained in waste water
[F24D 12/00] Other central heating systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements of central heating systems in which a combination of more than one heat source is disclosed.  In particular, this class covers central heating systems without an storage mass recuperating waste heat source with a separate flue gas condenser.  The cases in which one of the heat sources is a heat pump are classified in other relevant subclasses.
[F24D 12/02] having more than one heat source -- F24D 12/02 covers central heating installations that combine two or more heat-generating devices such as boilers, heat pumps or renewable units to supply space heating. It includes arrangements where the sources operate simultaneously or can be alternated for redundancy or improved efficiency.
[F24D 13/00] Other domestic- or space-heating systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F24D 13/02] solely using resistance heating, e.g. underfloor heating -- F24D 13/02 covers domestic or space heating systems that generate heat solely by electrical resistance, such as underfloor heating. These installations rely on resistive elements without any auxiliary heat source and belong to the wider mechanical-engineering heating category.
[F24D 13/022] resistances incorporated in construction elements -- This subclass covers heating elements built into structural components such as walls, ceilings, floors or panels, where the resistance heating material is integrated during manufacture. It includes electric resistance heating incorporated directly into building elements for space-or-domestic heating.
[F24D 13/024] in walls, floors, ceilings -- This subgroup covers heating installations that are built into the structural components of a building, such as walls, floors or ceilings, providing radiant or convective heat directly from the construction elements. It includes methods and devices for embedding heating elements within these surfaces for domestic or space heating applications.
[F24D 13/026] in door, windows -- F24D 13/026 pertains to heating installations that integrate a heat source directly into doors or windows, such as radiant panels or embedded heating elements. The code applies to domestic or space-heating systems where the heating device is built into the door or window structure.
[F24D 13/028] Glass panels, e.g. mirrors, design radiators, etc. -- F24D 13/028 classifies heating installations that incorporate glass panels such as mirrors, decorative or design radiators, and other glazed surfaces that serve to radiate or distribute heat in domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 13/04] using electric heating of heat-transfer fluid in separate units of the system -- F24D 13/04 covers domestic or space-heating systems that employ electric elements to heat a circulating fluid in separate units, such as electric boilers or heat-pump auxiliaries, rather than heating the space directly. It is part of the broader class for mechanical engineering heating installations.
[F24D 15/00] Other domestic- or space-heating systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other domestic- or non-central space-heating systems, in particular:
[F24D 15/02] consisting of self-contained heating units, e.g. storage heaters -- The CPC entry F24D 15/02 covers heating installations that are made up of self-contained heating units, such as storage heaters, which operate independently and supply heat to a domestic or space-heating system. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating, specifically for other domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 15/04] using heat pumps -- Covers domestic or space heating systems that employ heat-pump technology to extract thermal energy from ambient sources and deliver it for building heating, including dedicated heat-pump units and integrated central-heating installations.
[F24D 17/00] Domestic hot-water supply systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Domestic hot-water supply systems using  Domestic hot-water supply systems comprising arrangements for preventing the occurrence or proliferation of microorganisms in the water  Domestic hot-water supply systems comprising recirculation systems.
[F24D 17/0005] using recuperation of waste heat  F24D17/0036  takes precedence -- F24D 17/0005 covers domestic hot-water supply systems that incorporate recuperation of waste heat, improving energy efficiency in residential heating. It is classified under mechanical engineering - heating - domestic or space-heating systems, and takes precedence over the related entry F24D 17/0036.
[F24D 17/001] with accumulation of heated water -- F24D 17/001 covers domestic heating or hot-water systems that incorporate a storage tank to accumulate heated water for later use. The subclass includes designs where a buffer or cylinder retains thermal energy, enabling steady supply to radiators, taps or mixed space-heating circuits and improving efficiency.
[F24D 17/0015] using solar energy  F24D17/0036  takes precedence -- F24D 17/0015 covers domestic hot-water supply systems that obtain heat primarily from solar energy, such as solar collectors or panels integrated with storage tanks to provide hot water for residential heating or domestic use. This subclass applies to designs where solar input is the main source for water heating.
[F24D 17/0021] with accumulation of the heated water -- This CPC subclass covers domestic hot-water supply systems that incorporate a storage or accumulation unit for the heated water, such as tanks or vessels that retain heat between heating cycles. It relates to heating installations used in homes or buildings, focusing on the water-storage aspect of central heating configurations.
[F24D 17/0026] with conventional heating means  F24D17/0036  takes precedence -- The subclass F24D 17/0026 covers domestic hot-water supply systems that rely on ordinary heating devices such as boilers, furnaces or heat pumps, rather than novel or renewable technologies. It applies when conventional heating means are integrated into the system and is superseded by the more specific subclass F24D 17/0036.
[F24D 17/0031] with accumulation of the heated water -- The code F24D 17/0031 covers domestic hot-water supply systems that incorporate a storage or accumulator for the heated water. It includes configurations such as buffer tanks, boiler-integrated tanks or separate hot-water reservoirs that retain heat for later use, improving energy efficiency and providing a steady supply of hot water.
[F24D 17/0036] with combination of different kinds of heating means -- F24D 17/0036 covers domestic hot-water supply systems that employ a combination of different heating methods, such as integrating a conventional boiler with solar collectors, heat pumps, or other auxiliary sources to provide water heating. The classification is used for inventions that blend multiple heating means within a single residential or space-heating installation.
[F24D 17/0042] recuperated waste heat and solar energy -- F24D 17/0042 covers technologies that recover waste heat and combine it with solar energy to supply domestic hot-water, typically as part of central heating or space-heating installations. It addresses systems that integrate heat-recovery units with solar collectors to improve overall energy efficiency.
[F24D 17/0047] with accumulation of the heated water -- The subgroup F24D 17/0047 covers domestic hot-water supply systems that include a storage or accumulation unit for the heated water, such as tanks or reservoirs, allowing on-demand delivery and improved energy use.
[F24D 17/0052] recuperated waste heat and conventional heating means -- This subgroup covers domestic heating and hot-water systems that recover waste heat from sources such as exhaust gases, industrial processes or other residual streams and combine it with conventional heating equipment like boilers or heat pumps, boosting overall energy efficiency.
[F24D 17/0057] with accumulation of the heated water -- F24D 17/0057 concerns domestic hot-water supply installations that include a storage or accumulator for the heated water. The classification covers systems where a tank or reservoir retains hot water for later use, often combined with central heating circuits and control devices to improve efficiency and availability.
[F24D 17/0063] solar energy and conventional heaters -- F24D 17/0063 classifies domestic hot-water supply systems that combine solar energy with conventional heating devices. It covers the integration of solar collectors, storage tanks, heat exchangers and control mechanisms with gas, oil or electric boilers to provide hot water for residential use. The entry addresses both system design and the interaction between solar and conventional heating components.
[F24D 17/0068] with accumulation of the heated water -- F24D 17/0068 pertains to domestic hot-water supply systems that incorporate a means of storing heated water, such as a buffer tank or accumulator, to improve energy efficiency and ensure a reserve of hot water.
[F24D 17/0073] Arrangements for preventing the occurrence or proliferation of microorganisms in the water -- This subcategory covers designs and measures incorporated into domestic hot-water supply installations to inhibit microbial growth in the circulating water, such as filtration, disinfection, temperature control, and material selection. It includes methods that prevent colonisation of pathogens and biofilm formation in central heating or space-heating systems.
[F24D 17/0078] Recirculation systems -- Recirculation systems are arrangements that continuously or periodically circulate hot water through domestic hot-water supply networks to maintain temperature and reduce waiting times at fixtures. They typically incorporate pumps, thermostatic controls, and dedicated loops or zone valves, and are used in residential, commercial, or institutional buildings to improve energy efficiency and user comfort.
[F24D 17/0084] Coaxial tubings -- F24D 17/0084 covers coaxial tubings employed in domestic hot-water supply and central-heating installations. The arrangement consists of an inner tube surrounded by an outer tube, allowing simultaneous flow of hot water and a surrounding medium for insulation, heat exchange, or condensation control, and is used to improve efficiency and space saving in residential heating systems.
[F24D 17/0089] Additional heating means, e.g. electric heated buffer tanks or electric continuous flow heaters, located close to the consumer, e.g. directly before the water taps in bathrooms, in domestic hot water lines -- F24D 17/0089 covers supplementary heating devices for domestic hot-water supply, such as electric buffer tanks or continuous-flow heaters installed near the point of use. The devices are positioned close to taps, for example in bathroom lines, to provide additional heating directly before consumption.
[F24D 17/0094] Recovering of cold water -- F24D 17/0094 covers techniques for extracting heat from cold water in domestic hot-water supply or central heating installations, allowing energy to be reclaimed before the water is heated for use. It includes heat exchangers, pre-heating units and control schemes that improve overall efficiency of heating systems.
[F24D 17/02] using heat pumps -- F24D 17/02 relates to domestic hot-water supply systems that use heat-pump technology to provide heating or hot water for residences. The entry covers configurations where a heat pump extracts heat from ambient sources and transfers it to the water circuit of a central heating or domestic hot-water system.
[F24D 18/00] Small-scale combined heat and power [CHP] generation systems specially adapted for domestic heating, space heating or domestic hot-water supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combined heat and power [CHP] generation systems, also known as cogeneration systems [CGS], which are specially adapted for heating of, or for supplying hot-water to, homes, offices and small buildings, i.e. systems providing both heat for heating fluids and electrical power.  A small-scale CHP system usually comprises the following units:  The small-scale CHP system can also optionally comprise:  **Glossary:** - CGS: Cogeneration system - CHP: Combined heat and power
[F24D 19/00] Details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details concerning the heating system and the central heating radiators, in particular:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Details of water or air heaters, e.g. casings, cover lids or ornamental panel for heaters (not central heating radiators) and arrangements of mountings or supports of heaters (not central heating radiators) -> F24H9/00 , F24H9/02 , F24H9/06 - Details of heat-exchange or heat-transfer apparatus, of general application -> F28F
[F24D 19/0002] Means for connecting central heating radiators to circulation pipes -- This CPC subclass F24D 19/0002 covers devices that join central heating radiators to the circulating pipework. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic or space heating systems. The entry relates to fittings, connectors or couplings designed for secure, leak-free attachment of radiators within central heating installations.
[F24D 19/0004] In a one pipe system -- F24D 19/0004 covers heating installations that use a single pipe to convey hot water from the source to radiators or convectors, typically employing flow-control or balancing devices to distribute heat in domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0007] Comprising regulation means -- F24D 19/0007 covers domestic or space-heating systems that incorporate regulation means, such as thermostats, controllers, valves or actuators, to adjust temperature, flow or pressure. The entry applies to central heating installations where feedback or set-point control is integrated to optimise performance and energy use.
[F24D 19/0009] In a two pipe system -- This subclass covers heating installations that use a separate supply and return pipe to distribute hot water or steam, typical of residential or building central-heating systems where the heated fluid travels in one line to radiators and returns via another line. It includes the arrangement, control and components specific to two-pipe circuits.
[F24D 19/0012] Comprising regulation means -- F24D 19/0012 covers domestic or space-heating installations that incorporate regulation means such as thermostats, control valves, or automated controllers, enabling the system to adjust temperature, flow or output to maintain a set heating condition. It applies to central heating or other building-level heating solutions that include these control components.
[F24D 19/0014] Connection means adaptable for one and two pipe systems -- F24D 19/0014 designates pipe-connection devices for heating installations that can be fitted to either a single-pipe or a dual-pipe layout, enabling flexible joining of components such as radiators, boilers or distribution manifolds within domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0017] Connections between supply and inlet or outlet of central heating radiators -- F24D 19/0017 covers connections between the supply and the inlet or outlet of central heating radiators, a subcategory of domestic or space-heating systems within the mechanical engineering heating field.
[F24D 19/0019] Means for adapting connections -- F24D 19/0019 covers devices and arrangements that adapt connections between heating-system components, such as adapters, couplings, reducers and transition fittings that enable linking of pipes, radiators, boilers or other elements with differing sizes, materials or standards.
[F24D 19/0021] Flexible tubes or hoses -- This subclass covers flexible tubes or hoses used in domestic or space-heating systems such as central heating, including their construction, materials, and connection methods. It relates to mechanical-engineering aspects of heating installations.
[F24D 19/0024] Connections for plate radiators -- This class relates to the various types of pipe and conduit joints used to attach plate radiators to a heating circuit, including flanged, welded, soldered or threaded connections and associated sealing or mounting hardware for domestic or building space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0026] Places of the inlet on the radiator -- The CPC subclass F24D 19/0026 concerns domestic or space-heating systems, specifically radiators, and defines the various locations where the inlet (supply connection) can be placed on the radiator. It includes positions such as top, bottom, side or intermediate points and addresses how inlet placement influences fluid flow and heat distribution.
[F24D 19/0029] on a top corner -- F24D 19/0029 covers heating arrangements in which the heating element or outlet is positioned on a top corner of a room or enclosure, such as radiators, convectors or vents mounted at the upper corner of a wall or ceiling. The classification applies to domestic or space-heating systems that use this corner placement to distribute heat within the space.
[F24D 19/0031] on the top in the middle -- F24D 19/0031 relates to heating apparatus positioned on the top in the middle of a room, such as centrally mounted ceiling radiators or panels that distribute heat evenly from a high central location. It falls under domestic- or space-heating systems in the mechanical-engineering classification.
[F24D 19/0034] on a bottom corner -- F24D 19/0034 designates features of heating devices that are positioned at a bottom corner of a space, such as registers, radiators or duct elements intended to deliver heat from the lowest part of a room or building. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic or space-heating systems, covering arrangements that exploit the bottom-corner location to improve heat distribution.
[F24D 19/0036] on the bottom in the middle -- This CPC subgroup covers heating installations where the primary heat source is positioned on the floor at the central region of a room or space. The arrangement is intended to distribute warmth evenly from a bottom-center location, typical of floor-mounted radiators or panel heaters.
[F24D 19/0039] Places of the outlet on the radiator -- This subclass covers the positioning of the outlet or discharge opening on radiators used in domestic or space-heating systems, including top, side, bottom or rear locations and related design features that affect fluid flow and heat distribution.
[F24D 19/0041] on the top in the middle -- F24D 19/0041 covers heating installations in which the heat-generating unit is positioned at the top of a room or enclosure, centrally located across the width. This arrangement is used to promote upward convection and even temperature distribution in domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0043] on the opposite top corner -- F24D 19/0043 covers heating installations in which a heat-emitting element, thermostat or control device is placed in the opposite top corner of a room or building space to promote even temperature distribution. The classification is used for designs of domestic or central heating systems that locate the heating component at that remote upper corner, such as radiators, convectors or sensors installed there to improve comfort and efficiency.
[F24D 19/0046] on the top on the same side -- The code covers domestic or space-heating installations where the heat-emitting components-such as radiators, convectors or heat exchangers-are mounted on the upper part of a wall or panel on the same side, directing heat upward into the occupied space.
[F24D 19/0048] on the bottom in the middle -- The CPC entry F24D 19/0048 covers heating installations placed at the bottom-center of a room or enclosure, such as floor-mounted radiators, panels or heat exchangers positioned centrally at floor level to provide uniform space heating. It is used for domestic or space-heating systems where the heat source is located in the middle of the lower surface to improve convection and comfort.
[F24D 19/0051] on the bottom on the opposite corner -- The sub-group F24D 19/0051 deals with heating installations in which the heat-emitting element is placed at the bottom of the opposite corner of a room or enclosure. It falls under domestic or space-heating systems and addresses the arrangement of radiators, panels or floor-mounted units to optimize heat distribution from that position.
[F24D 19/0053] on the bottom on the same side -- F24D 19/0053 concerns heating arrangements in which the heat-generating or heat-transfer elements are positioned at the bottom of a device on the same side, typically for domestic or space-heating systems such as central heating installations. It covers designs that place the heating source low and laterally aligned to improve convection or radiant heat distribution within the heated space.
[F24D 19/0056] Supplies from the central heating system -- F24D 19/0056 covers devices that draw heat, water, fuel or control signals from a central heating installation and distribute them to secondary circuits or appliances. It includes taps, pumps, valves and other ancillary units that obtain their energy or fluid directly from the main system.
[F24D 19/0058] coming out the floor -- F24D 19/0058 covers heating installations that discharge heated fluid or air through openings in the floor, such as floor registers, diffusers or grilles, providing direct space heating from a central system.
[F24D 19/006] Alongside the radiator -- F24D 19/006 pertains to heating installations where a radiator is positioned alongside another radiator or heating element within a domestic or space-heating system. The subclass addresses the arrangement, mounting, and connection details for side-by-side radiators, emphasizing space-saving layouts and combined heat distribution.
[F24D 19/0063] under the radiator -- F24D 19/0063 covers arrangements or devices placed beneath a radiator in domestic or space-heating systems, such as mounting brackets, heat-distribution plates or secondary heat exchangers that enhance heat transfer from the radiator to the surrounding area.
[F24D 19/0065] coming out the wall -- F24D 19/0065 covers heating installations where the heat-emitting component projects from a wall, such as wall-mounted radiators, surface‐mounted convectors or wall-through outlets that deliver warm air or water to a room. The classification addresses the design, mounting and connection of these wall-emerging heating elements in domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0068] alongside the radiator -- F24D 19/0068 covers devices or arrangements placed alongside a radiator in domestic or space-heating systems, such as mounts, brackets, or auxiliary components that support the radiator, facilitate its connection to the building structure, or improve heat distribution and accessibility.
[F24D 19/007] under the radiator -- F24D 19/007 relates to components or arrangements situated beneath a radiator in domestic or space-heating systems, such as pipework, valves, distribution fittings or mounting structures that support or supply the radiator and affect its operation. This sub-class covers the design and installation aspects of the space under the radiator within the broader heating-technology field.
[F24D 19/0073] Means for changing the flow of the fluid inside a radiator -- F24D 19/0073 pertains to devices that modify the coolant flow inside a radiator, including valves, bypasses or other flow-control mechanisms, to regulate temperature and improve the efficiency of domestic or space heating systems.
[F24D 19/0075] Valves for isolating the radiator from the system -- The code F24D 19/0075 designates valves used to disconnect a radiator from a heating circuit, typically in domestic or space-heating installations. These components enable maintenance or control of heat flow by isolating the radiator without affecting the rest of the system.
[F24D 19/0078] Plugs -- Plugs for domestic or space-heating installations, encompassing electrical or fluid connectors that join components of central-heating systems to provide power or medium flow within the apparatus.
[F24D 19/008] Details related to central heating radiators -- F24D 19/008 covers specific features and design details of radiators used in central heating systems, such as connection fittings, mounting arrangements, flow distribution elements and surface treatments that affect heat output and installation in residential or commercial heating installations.
[F24D 19/0082] Humidifiers for radiators -- F24D 19/0082 pertains to humidifying devices that are incorporated with radiators used in domestic or space-heating systems. The entry covers humidifiers mounted on or integrated into radiators to add moisture to indoor air while the heating system is operating.
[F24D 19/0085] Fresh air entries for air entering the room to be heated by the radiator -- The classification covers arrangements for introducing fresh outdoor air into a heated room where the air passes by a radiator to be warmed before distribution. It pertains to domestic or space-heating systems that incorporate dedicated fresh-air inlets integrated with radiator heating.
[F24D 19/0087] Fan arrangements for forced convection -- F24D 19/0087 covers fan arrangements used to create forced-air convection in heating installations such as domestic or space-heating systems, including central heating setups. It specifies the design, placement and operation of fans that move heated air through ducts, radiators or rooms to improve heat distribution. This entry falls under the CPC mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating technologies.
[F24D 19/009] Magnets, e.g. for attaching a cover -- F24D 19/009 covers the use of magnets in heating installations, specifically for securing covers, panels or other components of domestic or space-heating systems such as boilers, radiators or heat exchangers, facilitating easy attachment, removal and sealing of protective parts.
[F24D 19/0092] Devices for preventing or removing corrosion, slime or scale -- F24D 19/0092 pertains to devices incorporated in domestic or space-heating installations that prevent or remove corrosion, slime or scale. It includes systems such as chemical inhibitors, mechanical cleaning mechanisms, filtration or de-scaling units designed to protect heating equipment and maintain efficient operation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → heating → domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0095] Devices for preventing damage by freezing -- This subclass includes appliances, controls, or mechanisms that protect heating installations, pipes, or equipment from freezing damage, such as automatic drain valves, thermostatic shutdowns, insulation accessories, or freeze-protection circuits used in central heating or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0097] Casings or frame structures for hydraulic components -- F24D 19/0097 covers the design and construction of casings or frame structures that house hydraulic components in domestic or space heating systems such as boilers or heat exchangers. These protective enclosures provide mechanical support, pressure containment and protection against corrosion or external damage.
[F24D 19/02] Arrangement of mountings or supports for radiators -- The CPC subclass F24D 19/02 covers inventions relating to the way radiators are mounted or supported within heating installations. It includes designs of brackets, frames and other mounting arrangements that secure radiators to walls, floors or ceilings, ensuring stability and effective heat distribution. This falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic or space heating systems.
[F24D 19/0203] Types of supporting means -- F24D 19/0203 covers the different kinds of supporting structures used in domestic or space-heating installations, such as brackets, frames, mounts, pedestals and other fixings that secure boilers, radiators, heat exchangers, pipework or related components in place. It includes both permanent and adjustable supports designed to bear the weight, resist vibration and accommodate thermal expansion within heating systems.
[F24D 19/0206] Tube shaped supports inserted into a wall -- Tube-shaped supports that are inserted into a wall to provide a secure anchor point for heating components such as pipes, radiators or convectors. The supports typically consist of a cylindrical sleeve or bracket that is fixed in the wall structure, allowing the attached heating element to be mounted with proper alignment and stability. This element is used in domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 19/0209] Supporting means having bracket -- F24D 19/0209 covers brackets and other supporting structures used to mount or hold components of domestic or space-heating installations, such as radiators, boilers, pipework or heat exchangers. The classification applies to designs that secure these elements within central heating or similar systems, ensuring proper positioning and stability during operation.
[F24D 19/0213] Floor mounted supporting means -- The CPC subclass F24D 19/0213 covers devices that support and secure floor-mounted heating equipment, such as boilers, heat exchangers or radiators, providing stable mounting and load distribution. It includes brackets, frames and anchoring systems used in domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 19/0216] Supporting means having a rail -- This CPC subgroup lies within mechanical engineering, heating, domestic- or space-heating systems and concerns supporting means that incorporate a rail. It covers rail-type brackets, channels or frames that hold or guide pipes, ducts, radiators or other heating components, providing structural stability and alignment in central-heating installations.
[F24D 19/022] Constructional details of supporting means for radiators -- F24D 19/022 covers the design and construction of brackets, mounts, and other support structures that hold radiators in place within domestic or space-heating installations. It includes details on attachment methods, material choices, load-bearing features, and adjustments for pipe connections.
[F24D 19/0223] Distance pieces between the radiator and the wall -- F24D 19/0223 relates to devices that create a gap between a radiator and the surrounding wall, such as mounting brackets, spacers or insulating pads. These distance pieces facilitate installation, improve heat distribution, prevent direct contact with wall surfaces and can provide space for vapor barriers or piping. They are used in domestic or space heating systems to enhance performance and protect building structures.
[F24D 19/0226] Additional means supporting the process of mounting -- F24D 19/0226 pertains to auxiliary devices that facilitate the installation of heating-system components, such as brackets, clamps, guides or temporary supports used during the mounting of radiators, boilers, pipework or other domestic or space-heating equipment. These means aid alignment, stability and secure fixing while the main system is being assembled.
[F24D 19/023] Radiators having fixed suspension means for connecting the radiator to the support means -- The entry covers radiators that are equipped with a permanent, fixed suspension system for attaching the unit to its supporting structure in domestic or space-heating installations. It specifies the design of built-in brackets or mounts that eliminate separate hanging hardware, facilitating direct connection to walls, floors or pipework.
[F24D 19/0233] Templates for installing the radiator -- The entry covers tools and templates used to position and mount radiators during installation in domestic or space-heating systems, including alignment guides, mounting brackets and positioning devices that ensure correct placement and connection of radiators within a heating circuit.
[F24D 19/0236] Water tubes or pipes forming part of the supporting means -- F24D 19/0236 covers water tubes or pipes that are incorporated into the structural or supporting elements of heating installations, such as the frames, brackets or casings of domestic or space-heating systems. The claim is limited to the pipe's function as part of the mechanical support rather than as the primary heat-transfer medium. This subclass is used when the tubing contributes to the stability or mounting of the heating apparatus.
[F24D 19/024] Functioning details of supporting means for radiators -- F24D 19/024 relates to the functional aspects of the devices that support radiators in domestic or space-heating installations. It includes mechanisms for mounting, adjusting, securing or dampening vibrations of radiator brackets and other supporting structures. The class addresses how these means operate within central heating systems.
[F24D 19/0243] Means for moving the radiator horizontally to adjust the radiator position -- The F24D 19/0243 entry covers devices that enable a radiator to be slid or pivoted horizontally for position adjustment, such as tracks, rollers, brackets or other sliding mechanisms integrated into domestic or space-heating systems, allowing users to modify radiator placement to suit room layout or heating performance.
[F24D 19/0246] Means for moving the radiator vertically to adjust the radiator position -- F24D 19/0246 covers devices that allow a radiator to be moved vertically to set its height, such as adjustable brackets, telescoping supports or motorised actuators. It applies to mechanisms that enable up-down positioning of domestic or space heating radiators to optimise heat distribution or accommodate surrounding furnishings.
[F24D 19/025] Eccentric means for moving the radiator vertically -- This subclass covers arrangements where an eccentric device such as a cam or offset shaft is employed to raise or lower a radiator in a domestic or space-heating system. The mechanism provides vertical adjustment to control heat output or accommodate installation constraints. It is part of the broader group for heating appliances with movable components.
[F24D 19/0253] Adjusting a dimension, e.g. length, of the radiator support, e.g. telescopic rails -- This CPC subclass pertains to methods and devices that adjust the length of radiator supports, for example by using telescopic rails, to modify the dimension of heating elements in domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 19/0256] Radiators clamped by supporting means -- The subclass F24D 19/0256 covers radiators that are secured in place by clamps, brackets or other supporting devices, focusing on the means of attachment rather than the radiator's heat-transfer function. It applies to domestic or space-heating systems where clamping ensures stability and proper operation.
[F24D 19/0259] Radiators clamped by supporting means around a column or tube -- This subclass defines radiators that are attached to a heating column or tube by clamps that surround the pipe, providing a secure, non-welded mounting. The clamping apparatus supports the radiator and holds it in place within domestic or space-heating circuits, often simplifying installation and maintenance.
[F24D 19/0263] Radiators clamped by supporting means between two columns or tubes -- Radiators that are held in place by clamps or brackets situated between two adjacent columns or tubes, forming a supported assembly within a domestic or space heating system. This arrangement secures the heat-emitting elements while allowing efficient flow of heating fluid through the surrounding network.
[F24D 19/0273] Radiators fixed in order to prevent undesired detachment -- The classification F24D 19/0273 covers measures for securing radiators within domestic or space-heating installations so they cannot become unintentionally detached. It includes designs, fixing devices and mounting methods that ensure the radiator remains firmly attached during operation and maintenance. This entry falls under the broader class of mechanical engineering - heating - domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0276] Radiators fixed on the bottom -- This subgroup deals with floor-mounted heating radiators, i.e. radiators installed on the bottom surface of a room or appliance, used in domestic or space-heating installations. It is part of CPC class F24D for heating apparatus, specifically the sub-class 19/0276 dealing with bottom-fixed radiators.
[F24D 19/0279] Radiators fixed on the sides -- F24D 19/0279 covers side-mounted radiators used in domestic or space heating installations. The subclass includes designs where the heat-emitting unit is fixed to the vertical or horizontal side walls of a room or enclosure, addressing mounting methods, pipe connections and surface treatment to improve heat distribution and aesthetic integration.
[F24D 19/0283] Radiators fixed on the top -- Radiators installed on the ceiling or upper portion of a room, designed to emit heat downward to warm domestic or space-heating environments. The classification covers the arrangement, mounting methods and thermal performance of top-fixed heating panels within centralized heating systems.
[F24D 19/0286] Radiators fixed using a spring -- This subclass covers heating radiators that are mounted by means of a spring, using the spring as the primary fixing element to attach the radiator to its support. The spring provides flexible, resilient attachment, facilitating easy installation, vibration damping and thermal expansion accommodation in domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/0289] Radiators fixed using a flexible clip -- This subclass concerns radiators that are mounted to a heating system or supporting structure by means of a flexible clip, allowing quick and detachable installation while accommodating thermal expansion. The clip may be spring-loaded, elastic or otherwise resilient, providing a secure yet adaptable fixing method without permanent fasteners.
[F24D 19/0293] Radiators rotating without being demounted -- This subclass covers designs where a radiator can be rotated for cleaning or servicing while remaining installed in a central heating system. It includes mechanisms that allow pivoting or swiveling of the heat-exchange unit without removal. The feature is used in domestic or space heating applications.
[F24D 19/04] in skirtings -- F24D 19/04 covers heating installations that are placed in skirting zones, such as baseboard or edge-mounted radiators and electric heating elements integrated into the lower part of walls or floors. It relates to the distribution of heat through these peripheral surfaces in domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/06] Casings, cover lids or ornamental panels, for radiators -- The entry F24D 19/06 covers protective and decorative casings, cover lids or ornamental panels that are fitted to radiators in domestic or space-heating systems. These components shield the radiator, contain its contents and can improve its aesthetic appearance.
[F24D 19/061] Radiator shelves -- F24D 19/061 refers to radiator shelves, which are auxiliary components mounted on or above domestic heating radiators to increase convective heat transfer, provide a safe platform for placing objects, or improve aesthetic integration. The classification covers designs, mounting methods, and functional features of such shelves within central heating systems.
[F24D 19/062] Heat reflecting or insulating shields -- The CPC entry F24D 19/062 pertains to heat-reflecting or insulating shields used in domestic or space heating installations, such as central heating systems. These shields are designed to minimise heat loss or protect components by reflecting thermal radiation or providing thermal insulation.
[F24D 19/064] Coverings not directly attached to a radiator, e.g. box-like coverings -- This CPC subclass (F24D 19/064) falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic- or space-heating systems and covers protective or decorative enclosures that are not mounted directly on a radiator, such as box-like covers that surround the heating element.
[F24D 19/065] Grids attached to the radiator and covering its top -- F24D 19/065 relates to grids that are attached to a radiator and used to cover its top surface, often to support heat exchangers, protect the radiator, or guide airflow. The grids may be made of metal or plastic and are fixed directly to the radiator body.
[F24D 19/067] Front coverings attached to the radiator -- The subclass F24D 19/067 relates to radiators equipped with a front panel that is permanently attached to the heating unit. The covering serves to protect the radiator surface, improve appearance, or direct heat flow, and is integral rather than a separate, removable element.
[F24D 19/068] Side coverings attached to the radiator -- Side coverings attached to the radiator are protective or decorative panels fixed to the sides of a heating radiator to prevent accidental contact, improve safety and enhance appearance, and may also reduce heat loss. They are typically made of metal, plastic or fabric and are considered part of domestic heating system components in the mechanical-engineering classification.
[F24D 19/08] Arrangements for drainage, venting or aerating -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Valves for venting or aerating -> F16K24/00
[F24D 19/081] for steam heating systems -- F24D 19/081 covers steam heating installations used for domestic or space heating, including the generation, distribution and control of steam within heating systems. It addresses boiler design, pipe networks, regulators, vents and related components that deliver and manage steam heat in residential or commercial buildings.
[F24D 19/082] for water heating systems -- F24D 19/082 classifies inventions that deal with water heating in domestic or space-heating installations such as central heating systems. It includes boilers, heat exchangers and related control or distribution devices designed to raise water temperature for space or domestic use.
[F24D 19/083] Venting arrangements -- F24D 19/083 covers the design and configuration of venting systems for domestic or space-heating installations, including the layout of flues, chimneys, exhaust ducts and related components that discharge combustion gases or waste heat from heating apparatuses. It addresses how these passages are arranged to ensure safe, efficient removal of gases and compliance with building and safety standards.
[F24D 19/085] Arrangement of venting valves for central heating radiators -- F24D 19/085 covers designs that specify how venting valves are positioned and integrated on radiators within central heating installations, focusing on methods that facilitate air release and flow control to improve system efficiency and reliability. This includes arrangements of individual or multiple valves, their orientation, and connection to piping or radiator components.
[F24D 19/086] hand-operated -- F24D 19/86 covers heating apparatus for domestic or space-heating systems that are manually operated by hand, including hand-cranked thermostats, manual fuel-feeding devices, portable hand-operated heaters and other components where the user directly controls the heating function without electric or automatic mechanisms.
[F24D 19/087] automatic -- F24D 19/087 relates to automatic control features for domestic or space-heating systems, including central heating installations. It covers devices and methods that start, stop, or adjust heating based on thermostats, sensors, or programmed schedules without manual intervention.
[F24D 19/088] Draining arrangements -- F24D 19/088 classifies the components and methods used to remove liquid or condensate from domestic or space-heating installations, such as central-heating circuits, radiators or heat exchangers. It covers drainage pipes, valves, pumps and automatic devices that ensure safe, efficient discharge of water, condensate or air from heating systems.
[F24D 19/10] Arrangement or mounting of control or safety devices -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements where only the heater is controlled -> F24H9/20
[F24D 19/1003] for steam heating systems -- F24D 19/1003 covers inventions related to steam heating within domestic or space-heating installations, such as central heating systems. It includes the design, arrangement, distribution and control of steam-based heating apparatus for buildings, encompassing boilers, pipelines, radiators and associated control devices.
[F24D 19/1006] for water heating systems -- F24D 19/1006 covers inventions relating to water heating systems employed in domestic or space-heating installations, such as boilers, heat exchangers, and control devices that produce hot water for radiators, underfloor heating or domestic hot-water supply. The subgroup includes any technical features that improve the generation, distribution or regulation of heated water within central heating networks.
[F24D 19/1009] for central heating -- F24D 19/1009 designates inventions concerning central heating systems, including the apparatus, distribution networks and control methods used to provide heat throughout a building. It is classified under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/1012] by regulating the speed of a pump -- This CPC subgroup covers heating systems where the flow of heat-transfer fluid is controlled by varying the speed of the circulating pump. Modulating pump speed adjusts water flow and temperature output, providing efficient regulation of domestic or space heating.
[F24D 19/1015] using a valve or valves -- This CPC subclass concerns domestic or space-heating systems such as central heating installations where one or more valves are used to control fluid flow, pressure or temperature. It covers designs for valve types, their actuation mechanisms and integration within boiler, radiator or pipe networks.
[F24D 19/1018] Radiator valves -- F24D 19/1018 covers radiator valves, the components that regulate the flow of hot water or steam to heating radiators in domestic or space-heating systems. These valves may be manual, thermostatic or motor-operated and are designed to adjust heat output, improve energy efficiency, and protect the system from excess pressure.
[F24D 19/1021] a by pass valve -- F24D 19/1021 covers bypass valves incorporated in domestic or space-heating installations such as central heating circuits. The valve provides an alternate flow path to control pressure, balance water distribution or enable maintenance without interrupting system operation, contributing to efficient temperature regulation and energy use.
[F24D 19/1024] a multiple way valve -- This entry covers a multiple-way valve that directs heating fluid among several circuits in domestic or space-heating installations such as central heating systems. The valve enables switching or balancing flow between different zones or sources, facilitating control of temperature distribution.
[F24D 19/1027] hand operated -- The code refers to heating installations or devices that are operated manually by hand, for example manually controlled central heating systems or portable hand-operated heaters used in domestic or space heating.
[F24D 19/103] bimetal operated -- F24D 19/103 covers heating installations that are controlled by bimetallic actuators, where a temperature-sensitive bimetal strip bends to switch the system on or off. It includes thermostatic devices for domestic or space heating that use the differential expansion of two metals to regulate temperature.
[F24D 19/1033] motor operated -- F24D 19/1033 covers heating installations where a motor drives a component such as a fan, pump or circulator in domestic or space-heating systems. It includes motor-operated boilers, fan-assisted radiators and thermostatically controlled circulation devices used to distribute heat. The code is applied to designs that integrate electrical drive mechanisms with heating equipment.
[F24D 19/1036] Having differential pressure measurement facilities -- This subgroup covers central or domestic heating installations equipped with devices that measure differential pressure across the system, allowing flow monitoring, radiator balancing, and control of pump operation.
[F24D 19/1039] the system uses a heat pump -- F24D 19/1039 classifies heating or domestic-space-heating installations that employ a heat-pump cycle to provide warmth, typically integrating the pump with radiators, underfloor heating or air-handling units. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating systems, specifying the use of heat-pump technology in central or localized heating applications.
[F24D 19/1042] the system uses solar energy -- This subclass covers domestic or space heating installations that incorporate solar energy as a primary or supplementary power source. It includes systems such as solar-assisted central heating, solar collectors integrated with radiators, and other solar-driven heating configurations within the broader mechanical engineering heating category.
[F24D 19/1045] the system uses a heat pump and solar energy -- F24D 19/1045 covers heating systems that combine a heat pump with solar energy, used in domestic or space heating applications. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 19/1048] Counting of energy consumption -- F24D 19/1048 covers methods and devices for measuring or counting the energy consumption of domestic or space-heating installations, such as central heating systems. It addresses monitoring, recording and reporting of heat-related energy use within the broader mechanical-engineering heating field.
[F24D 19/1051] for domestic hot water -- F24D 19/1051 covers features of heating installations that provide hot water for household use, including the generation, storage, and distribution of domestic-hot-water within central heating or space-heating systems. It addresses the integration of water-heating units, heat-exchanger arrangements and control means that ensure a ready supply of hot water for domestic purposes.
[F24D 19/1054] the system uses a heat pump -- F24D 19/1054 covers domestic or space-heating installations that incorporate a heat pump to generate or transfer thermal energy. It includes central heating schemes where the heat pump is the primary source, often combined with auxiliary components for climate control.
[F24D 19/1057] the system uses solar energy -- This subclass deals with domestic or space-heating systems that obtain heat from solar energy, such as solar collectors linked to a central heating circuit. It includes designs where solar thermal energy is collected, stored and transferred to the building's heating distribution network.
[F24D 19/106] the system uses a heat pump and solar energy -- F24D 19/106 covers heating installations that integrate a heat pump with solar energy collectors to supply domestic or space heating. The classification applies to systems where solar thermal or photovoltaic energy assists the heat pump in providing warmth for homes or buildings.
[F24D 19/1063] counting of energy consumption -- F24D 19/1063 covers methods and devices for measuring or tallying the energy used by domestic or space-heating installations, such as central heating systems. It is situated in the Mechanical Engineering hierarchy under Heating and specifically Domestic-or Space-Heating Systems, focusing on the quantification of fuel or electricity consumption for monitoring efficiency and billing.
[F24D 19/1066] for the combination of central heating and domestic hot water -- This CPC subclass (F24D 19/1066) falls under mechanical engineering-heating and covers devices or methods that combine central heating with domestic hot-water supply, providing both space heating and hot water from a single system or source.
[F24D 19/1069] regulation in function of the temperature of the domestic hot water -- F24D 19/1069 covers devices or methods that control domestic hot-water temperature, such as thermostatic valves, sensors and control units used in central heating or hot-water systems. The classification is part of the CPC group for heating equipment within mechanical engineering, focusing on temperature-based regulation of water supplied for domestic use.
[F24D 19/1072] the system uses a heat pump -- F24D 19/1072 covers heating installations in which a heat-pump serves as the primary source of thermal energy for domestic or space-heating systems, including the associated distribution network, controls and auxiliary components required to deliver heat to a building.
[F24D 19/1075] the system uses solar energy -- The subgroup F24D 19/1075 relates to domestic or space-heating installations that employ solar energy, either as the main heat source or to supplement conventional fuel-based systems, and includes solar collectors and their integration with central heating distribution networks.
[F24D 19/1078] the system uses a heat pump and solar energy -- F24D 19/1078 covers domestic or space-heating systems that integrate a heat pump with solar energy collectors to provide heating. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and specifically central heating systems.
[F24D 19/1081] counting of energy consumption -- F24D 19/1081 concerns methods and devices for measuring, recording and calculating the energy used by domestic or space-heating installations such as central heating systems, enabling monitoring, control or billing of consumption. It addresses how the amount of heat energy delivered is counted within mechanical-engineering heating applications.
[F24D 19/1084] for air heating systems -- F24D 19/1084 covers heating installations that are intended to raise the temperature of air for domestic or space-heating purposes, such as central heating systems that circulate warm air through ducts or vents. The class includes designs, components and methods used to generate, control and distribute heated air within residential or similar environments.
[F24D 19/1087] system using a heat pump -- F24D 19/1087 covers installations that employ a heat pump to provide domestic or space heating. The code is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification under mechanical engineering, specifically heating systems such as central heating. It includes designs where the heat pump extracts heat from ambient sources and distributes it within a building.
[F24D 19/109] system using solar energy -- A solar-energy system for domestic or space heating combines solar collectors, heat transfer fluids, and storage or distribution components to provide thermal energy for buildings. It falls under mechanical engineering heating technologies and addresses central heating applications that rely on solar power to generate or supplement heat.
[F24D 19/1093] system using a heat pump and solar energy -- F24D 19/1093 designates a domestic or space-heating system that integrates a heat pump with solar energy collection, allowing solar thermal input to assist or replace conventional heat-pump operation in central heating installations. The configuration may include solar collectors, storage, and controls that coordinate the two sources for efficient temperature regulation.
[F24D 19/1096] for electric heating systems -- F24D 19/1096 refers to heating arrangements that employ electricity as the energy source, covering electric central-heating installations and other domestic or space-heating systems powered by electric elements, heat pumps, or similar electric devices.

[F24D 2101/10] Gas turbines -- F24D 2101/10 covers gas turbines used in heating applications, particularly for domestic or space-heating systems such as central heating. The subclass includes turbine-driven units that generate high-temperature gas flow to provide heat or combined heat-and-power for buildings.
[F24D 2101/20] Wind turbines -- F24D 2101/20 designates wind turbines employed to generate energy for domestic or space-heating installations. The entry is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F24D 2101/30] Fuel cells -- F24D 2101/30 covers fuel-cell technologies used for providing heat in domestic or space-heating installations, such as central heating systems. The code falls under the mechanical engineering section dealing with heating, specifically domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2101/40] Photovoltaic [PV] modules -- Photovoltaic modules are assemblies of solar cells that convert sunlight into electrical power, which can be used to supply energy for domestic or space-heating installations such as electric boilers, heat pumps or building-integrated heating systems. The CPC entry classifies these modules within the heating category because they serve as a renewable energy source for heating applications.
[F24D 2101/50] Thermophotovoltaic [TPV] modules -- This CPC subgroup F24D2101/50 pertains to thermophotovoltaic modules that generate electricity from heat for use in domestic or space-heating installations such as central heating systems. It classifies technologies that convert thermal radiation directly into electric power within heating equipment.
[F24D 2101/60] Thermoelectric generators, e.g. Peltier or Seebeck elements -- F24D 2101/60 covers thermoelectric generators used in heating systems, including Peltier and Seebeck elements that convert temperature differences into electrical power or provide direct heating-cooling. The subclass groups devices employing the thermoelectric effect for domestic or space-heating applications, such as supplemental power sources for central heating installations.
[F24D 2101/70] Electric generators driven by internal combustion engines [ICE] -- This class covers systems in which an internal-combustion engine is coupled to an electric generator to produce electricity, typically for auxiliary power in heating installations or standalone power units. It includes the engine-generator unit itself and its integration with heating equipment, but not purely mechanical or purely electrical generators without an ICE.
[F24D 2101/80] Electric generators driven by external combustion engines, e.g. Stirling engines -- The entry covers electric generators that are powered by external-combustion engines such as Stirling engines, linking the conversion of heat from domestic or space-heating systems into electrical energy. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under heating and specifically addresses combined heating-generator installations.
[F24D 2103/00] Thermal aspects of small-scale CHP systems -- The classification covers the thermal design and operation of small-scale combined heat and power installations used for domestic or space heating. It includes heat recovery from electricity generation, temperature regulation, distribution to heating networks, and measures to improve overall energy efficiency in compact CHP units.
[F24D 2103/10] Small-scale CHP systems characterised by their heat recovery units -- Small-scale combined heat-and-power installations whose defining element is the heat-recovery unit, used to provide domestic heating or hot water while generating electricity for a single building or modest complex. The code addresses thermal performance, integration and control of these compact CHP systems.
