[F24D 2103/13] characterised by their heat exchangers -- This subclass deals with the thermal aspects of small-scale combined heat-and-power (CHP) installations where the distinguishing feature is the heat-exchanger used, covering its design, operation and efficiency within domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2103/17] Storage tanks -- F24D 2103/17 denotes storage tanks used in small-scale combined heat and power installations for domestic or space heating, concentrating on their thermal characteristics within such heating systems.
[F24D 2103/20] Additional heat sources for supporting thermal peak loads -- This subclass covers technologies that add supplementary heat sources to small-scale combined heat and power installations to meet peak heating demand, such as auxiliary boilers, heat pumps, or burners integrated with domestic heating networks. It focuses on thermal management and control strategies for peak load support.
[F24D 2105/00] Constructional aspects of small-scale CHP systems -- The code F24D 2105/00 classifies documents that address the design and construction of small-scale combined heat and power (CHP) installations used for domestic or space heating. It covers technical aspects of integrating electricity generation with heating functions in compact systems, such as those employed in homes or small commercial buildings.
[F24D 2105/10] Sound insulation -- F24D 2105/10 relates to sound insulation measures applied in the construction of small-scale combined heat and power (CHP) systems that are part of domestic or space heating installations. The code covers design features that reduce acoustic transmission through heating equipment, ducts and enclosures.
[F24D 2200/00] Heat sources -- F24D 2200/00 designates heat sources used in domestic or space heating systems such as central heating. It covers devices that generate thermal energy including boilers, furnaces, heat pumps and other primary heating equipment. The code falls under the mechanical engineering heating category in the CPC.
[F24D 2200/02] Photovoltaic energy -- F24D 2200/02 covers heat-source installations that derive their energy from photovoltaic systems, i.e., the conversion of solar radiation into electricity used to generate or supply heat for domestic or space-heating applications. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2200/04] Gas or oil fired boiler -- The entry pertains to boilers that burn gas or oil to produce steam or hot water for domestic or space-heating applications, encompassing designs, components and methods that enhance efficiency, control and safety. It is classified under heat sources within central heating systems in the mechanical-engineering heating hierarchy.
[F24D 2200/043] More than one gas or oil fired boiler -- F24D 2200/043 covers heating installations that use more than one boiler powered by gas or oil, typically in domestic or space-heating systems. The class groups arrangements where multiple fuel-fired boilers operate together to supply heat.
[F24D 2200/046] Condensing boilers -- Condensing boilers are heating devices that recover the latent heat from water vapor in exhaust gases, achieving higher efficiency than conventional boilers. By condensing the combustion gases, they transfer additional thermal energy to the heating circuit, reducing fuel consumption in domestic or space-heating systems. This technology is commonly applied in gas-fired central heating installations.
[F24D 2200/06] Solid fuel fired boiler -- The code F24D 2200/06 refers to boilers that generate heat by combusting solid fuels such as coal, wood or biomass for domestic or space-heating applications. It includes the design, operation and associated components of solid-fuel fired heating systems used in central heating installations.
[F24D 2200/062] Coal fired boilers -- F24D 2200/062 designates coal-fired boilers employed in domestic or space-heating installations. The code classifies stationary heating equipment that combusts coal to produce steam or hot water for central heating of residential or similar buildings.
[F24D 2200/065] Wood fired boilers -- F24D 2200/065 designates wood-fired boilers that serve as heat sources in domestic or space-heating systems. The entry resides in the mechanical engineering hierarchy under heating and central heating installations. It covers boilers that burn wood to produce thermal energy for residential or similar applications.
[F24D 2200/067] Pellet fired boilers -- The CPC entry F24D 2200/067 covers pellet-fired boilers, which are heat-source devices that combust wood pellets to produce hot water or steam for domestic or space-heating systems such as central heating. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Domestic- or Space-Heating Systems → Heat sources.
[F24D 2200/07] Solid fuel burners -- F24D 2200/07 covers burners that use solid fuels such as wood, coal, peat or biomass to generate heat for domestic or space-heating installations, including central heating systems. The classification includes the design, operation and control of these combustion devices and their integration into heating circuits.
[F24D 2200/08] Electric heater -- F24D 2200/08 covers electric heaters used in domestic or space heating systems. It includes any heating device that converts electricity into heat for central heating applications. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic or space heating systems, heat sources.
[F24D 2200/10] Fire place -- The F24D 2200/10 entry covers fireplaces as heat-source devices used for domestic or space heating. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Domestic- or Space-Heating Systems → Heat sources, describing the structure, operation and thermal output of fire-places that provide primary or supplemental warmth in residential or similar settings.
[F24D 2200/11] Geothermal energy -- F24D 2200/11 classifies technologies that harness geothermal energy as a heat source for domestic or space-heating systems, such as central heating installations. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating, covering methods that extract and distribute heat from the earth's interior. This code is used in both the EPC and USPTO patent classification schemes.
[F24D 2200/115] Involving mains water supply -- The subclass F24D 2200/115 refers to heating installations that obtain water directly from a public mains supply for use as the heat source in domestic or space-heating systems, typically employing the mains water itself or circulating it through a heat exchanger to provide thermal energy.
[F24D 2200/12] Heat pump -- F24D 2200/12 denotes heat pumps, devices that move thermal energy from a low-temperature source to a higher-temperature space for domestic or space heating, typically employing a refrigeration cycle powered by electricity.
[F24D 2200/123] Compression type heat pumps -- F24D 2200/123 is a CPC subclass covering compression-type heat pumps used in domestic or space-heating systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and the heat-source group of central heating technologies, specifying devices that transfer heat by compressing a refrigerant.
[F24D 2200/126] Absorption type heat pumps -- F24D 2200/126 covers absorption heat pumps, which use a heat source to drive a refrigeration cycle without mechanical compressors. These devices typically employ a refrigerant-absorbent pair such as water-lithium bromide and are used in domestic or space-heating systems to provide efficient heat generation.
[F24D 2200/13] Heat from a district heating network -- F24D 2200/13 pertains to heat supplied via a district-heating network, where a central plant generates thermal energy that is distributed through a pipe system to multiple buildings for domestic or space heating. It is classified under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic- or space-heating systems, and heat sources in the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F24D 2200/14] Solar energy -- F24D 2200/14 designates solar energy used as a heat source for domestic or space-heating systems, such as central heating installations. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of heating, specifically the category of heating sources for residential applications.
[F24D 2200/15] Wind energy -- The subclass F24D 2200/15 groups inventions that employ wind energy as a heat source for domestic or space-heating systems, including central heating installations, wind-driven turbines, wind-powered heat pumps, and related methods of converting wind power into usable heating energy.
[F24D 2200/16] Waste heat -- The entry classifies waste heat as a heat source used in domestic or space-heating systems, such as central heating, covering the capture, recovery or utilization of otherwise discarded thermal energy within mechanical-engineering heating applications.
[F24D 2200/18] Flue gas recuperation -- The F24D 2200/18 subclass covers systems that recover heat from flue gases in domestic or space-heating installations, such as central heating. It includes heat-exchanger or recuperator arrangements that capture exhaust heat and reuse it to improve overall heating efficiency.
[F24D 2200/19] Fuel cells -- F24D 2200/19 covers fuel cells used as heat sources for domestic or space-heating systems, including central heating installations that generate heat through electrochemical conversion of fuel. The entry groups any apparatus where the thermal output of a fuel cell is employed for heating rather than solely for electricity generation.
[F24D 2200/20] Sewage water -- F24D 2200/20 covers the use of sewage water as a heat source in domestic or space-heating systems, describing methods or apparatuses that recover thermal energy from wastewater for central heating or similar applications.
[F24D 2200/22] Ventilation air -- F24D 2200/22 relates to heating systems that use ventilation air as the heat source, covering devices and methods that supply warmed fresh or recirculated air for domestic or space-heating applications such as central heating furnaces or air-handling units.
[F24D 2200/24] Refrigeration -- F24D 2200/24 refers to refrigeration as a heat source within domestic or space-heating systems. The subclass includes devices and methods that use cooling cycles to supply heat for central heating, heat pumps, and related applications. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating.
[F24D 2200/26] Internal combustion engine -- The CPC entry F24D 2200/26 classifies internal-combustion engines employed as heat sources for domestic or space-heating installations, such as central heating systems. It covers designs where the engine's waste heat or dedicated combustion is used to generate warm air or water for building heating. This falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2200/28] Biological processes -- F24D 2200/28 pertains to heat sources that employ biological processes, including the use of biomass, microbial activity, or biochemical reactions to generate thermal energy for domestic or space heating systems. It covers technologies that convert biological material into heat.
[F24D 2200/29] Electrical devices, e.g. computers, servers -- F24D 2200/29 covers heat-generating sources that are electrical devices such as computers, servers or similar equipment used within domestic or space-heating installations. The code applies when the primary purpose of the device is to provide thermal energy for heating rather than its usual computing function.
[F24D 2200/30] Friction -- F24D 2200/30 - Friction refers to heating methods where mechanical friction generates heat for domestic or space heating, such as friction-heating panels, friction-driven boilers, or devices that convert kinetic energy into thermal energy for central heating applications.
[F24D 2200/31] Air conditioning systems -- F24D 2200/31 covers air-conditioning systems that treat indoor air for heating, cooling, humidity control or ventilation in residential or building environments. These installations typically include components such as compressors, evaporators, condensers and fans, and are designed to maintain comfort conditions within a space. The class groups them under heat sources within mechanical engineering for domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2200/32] involving multiple heat sources in combination or as alternative heat sources -- This subclass covers heating installations that employ two or more heat generators either simultaneously or as interchangeable options, such as combined boiler-heat-pump units or systems that can switch between gas, electric, or solar sources. It is used to classify patents describing the integration, control, or selection of multiple heat sources for domestic or space heating.
[F24D 2220/00] Central Heating Systems -- A central heating system distributes heat from a primary source, such as a boiler or furnace, through a network of pipes or ducts to warm multiple rooms or an entire building, providing consistent domestic or space heating.
[F24D 2220/003] Generic central heating systems -- Generic central heating systems refer to the basic arrangements that generate heat in a building and distribute it through a network of pipes, ducts or radiators to provide space heating. They typically involve a heat source such as a boiler, furnace or heat pump, a distribution manifold, and terminal emitters, without specifying particular control strategies or specialized components.
[F24D 2220/006] Parts of a building integrally forming part of heating systems, e.g. a wall as a heat storing mass -- F24D 2220/006 covers building elements that themselves act as components of heating installations, such as walls or floors that store and release heat, effectively becoming part of the system's thermal mass. These integrally constructed parts serve both structural and heating functions within central heating setups.
[F24D 2220/02] Fluid distribution means -- This subclass covers the components and arrangements used to convey heated fluid throughout a central heating system, including pipes, pumps, manifolds and distribution networks that deliver heat to radiators or terminals. It also includes design features that regulate flow, balance pressure and ensure efficient heat transfer within domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 2220/0207] Pumps -- F24D 2220/0207 designates pumps employed in central heating installations, such as circulating, boiler-feed and condensate pumps that move water or other heating media through the system. It is part of the mechanical-engineering heating subclass for domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2220/0214] Inlets or outlets -- The CPC subclass F24D 2220/0214 covers the inlet and outlet elements of central heating installations, including supply, return and distribution connections for water, steam or other heat-transfer media. It pertains to fittings, valves, pipe sections and associated devices that introduce or discharge the heating fluid within domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2220/0221] Mixing cylinders -- Mixing cylinders are components of central heating systems that blend hot water from the boiler with cold water to deliver a stable supply temperature, typically using a thermostatic valve and a storage chamber. They improve temperature control, reduce thermal shock, and enhance system efficiency and safety.
[F24D 2220/0228] Branched distribution conduits -- It covers the design and use of branched distribution conduits that carry heated fluid from a central heating plant to multiple radiators or zones within a building, including pipe layouts, manifolds and branching fittings. These components form part of the plumbing network that distributes heat throughout domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 2220/0235] Three-way-valves -- The CPC entry F24D 2220/0235 covers three-way valves used in central heating installations. These devices direct the flow of hot water or steam among three circuit branches, enabling control of temperature distribution and system balancing. They fall under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2220/0242] Multiple way valves -- Multiple-way valves are components in central heating circuits that can direct fluid flow among three or more pipe branches, enabling zone control, temperature balancing and system routing. They are used to isolate, bypass or regulate sections of domestic or space-heating systems for efficient operation.
[F24D 2220/025] Check valves -- Check valves are one-way devices installed in central heating pipework to permit flow of hot water or steam in the intended direction while automatically preventing reverse flow. They protect pumps and boilers from back-pressure, reduce energy loss, and help maintain system pressure stability.
[F24D 2220/0257] Thermostatic valves -- Thermostatic valves are components used in central heating systems to automatically regulate the flow of hot water or steam based on temperature settings, maintaining desired indoor temperatures while improving energy efficiency and comfort. They adjust opening and closing positions in response to temperature changes.
[F24D 2220/0264] Hydraulic balancing valves -- Hydraulic balancing valves are components used in central heating pipe networks to regulate water flow and equalise pressure, ensuring uniform heat distribution among radiators or circuit loops. By fine-tuning flow rates they improve system efficiency and prevent pressure imbalances.
[F24D 2220/0271] Valves -- The class F24D 2220/0271 covers valves employed in central heating installations, including those that regulate flow, pressure, temperature or mixture of heating fluid within domestic or space-heating systems. It encompasses a range of valve types such as thermostatic, pressure-reducing, balancing and control valves designed for use in boilers, radiators and pipe networks.
[F24D 2220/0278] Expansion vessels -- The code F24D 2220/0278 refers to expansion vessels used in central heating installations, which absorb the volume changes of water as temperature varies to prevent pressure spikes and protect pipes and equipment. These devices are usually steel tanks containing a pre-charged gas bladder and form a standard component of domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2220/0285] Pipe sections -- F24D 2220/0285 covers pipe sections used in central heating installations, including straight, bent or curved lengths of metal, plastic or composite tubing designed to convey heated fluid throughout residential or commercial heating networks.
[F24D 2220/0292] Fluid distribution networks -- F24D 2220/0292 covers the design, layout and operation of networks that convey heating fluids such as water or steam from a central source to multiple heat emitters within a building, including pipe sizing, routing, pumps and control devices used in domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 2220/04] Sensors -- This CPC group covers sensors employed in central heating systems, such as temperature, pressure, flow and combustion monitoring devices that detect operating conditions. They enable automatic control, improve energy efficiency and enhance safety of domestic or building heating installations.
[F24D 2220/042] Temperature sensors -- F24D 2220/042 designates temperature sensors employed in central heating installations, covering devices that detect and transmit fluid or ambient heat levels for regulation of domestic or space-heating systems. The code includes thermostats, thermocouples, resistance temperature detectors and related control components used to maintain desired temperatures.
[F24D 2220/044] Flow sensors -- F24D 2220/044 covers flow sensors used in central heating installations, including devices that detect, measure or regulate the movement of water or other heating fluids within domestic or space-heating systems. The entry is applied to sensors such as turbine, ultrasonic or thermal-mass flow meters that provide data for controlling temperature, pressure or system efficiency. It pertains specifically to the elements that monitor fluid flow in central heating circuits.
[F24D 2220/046] Pressure sensors -- This CPC subclass covers pressure sensors incorporated in central heating installations for residential or commercial buildings. The devices detect and measure fluid pressure within boilers, radiators or pipe networks, enabling control systems to maintain safe and efficient operation of domestic space-heating systems.
[F24D 2220/048] Level sensors, e.g. water level sensors -- Level sensors detect the height of fluids, such as water, within a central heating system and provide an electrical signal to control pumps, valves, or alarms, ensuring safe operation and preventing overflow or empty-tank conditions. They are commonly installed in boilers, expansion tanks, and distribution manifolds.
[F24D 2220/06] Heat exchangers -- Heat exchangers are devices used in central heating systems to transfer thermal energy between separate fluid streams without mixing them, enhancing overall efficiency by recovering waste heat. The classification covers various designs, configurations and applications of these components within domestic heating installations.
[F24D 2220/07] Heat pipes -- Heat pipes are sealed, evacuated tubes or channels that transfer thermal energy by the continuous condensation and evaporation of a working fluid, allowing rapid, passive heat distribution with minimal temperature loss. In central heating systems they move heat from a source such as a boiler or solar collector to radiators or storage units, improving efficiency and uniformity of indoor heating.
[F24D 2220/08] Storage tanks -- The classification F24D 2220/08 covers storage tanks used in central heating installations, such as reservoirs that hold hot water or steam for distribution throughout a building. These tanks may be integrated with boilers, heat exchangers, or renewable energy systems to balance supply and demand and ensure continuous heating.
[F24D 2220/10] Heat storage materials, e.g. phase change materials or static water enclosed in a space -- This subclass concerns substances that store thermal energy for use in central heating or space-heating installations, including phase-change compounds and water contained in a sealed chamber. It covers the selection, design and integration of such heat-storage media to improve efficiency and load-balancing of heating systems.
[F24D 2220/20] Heat consumers -- Heat consumers are the devices or elements that receive thermal energy from a central heating system, such as radiators, convectors, underfloor heating panels or heat-exchanging units, and convert it into useful warmth for a building or space. This subclass (F24D 2220/20) groups the various types of receivers that are supplied by the heating source in domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 2220/2009] Radiators -- Radiators, classified under F24D 2220/2009, are heat-emitting components of central heating systems that transfer thermal energy from hot water or steam to indoor spaces. Typically made of metal panels, columns or finned tubes, they circulate heated fluid to provide space heating in residential or commercial buildings. This entry falls within the mechanical engineering hierarchy for domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2220/2018] Column radiators having vertically extending tubes -- Column radiators with vertically extending tubes are a type of central heating heat emitter in which a series of narrow vertical tubes are arranged within a vertical column housing. The design allows water or steam to flow upward through the tubes, providing efficient heat distribution for domestic or space-heating applications.
[F24D 2220/2027] Convectors (radiators wherein heat transfer mainly takes place by convection) -- Convectors are radiators designed for central heating systems in which heat is transferred primarily through the movement of air. They rely on natural or forced convection to circulate warm air throughout a space, providing efficient domestic heating.
[F24D 2220/2036] Electric radiators -- The CPC subclass F24D 2220/2036 Electric radiators covers devices that convert electrical energy into heat and release it by convection and radiation for use in central heating installations. These units are typically wall-mounted or floor-standing electric radiators employed in domestic or space-heating systems.
[F24D 2220/2045] Radiators having horizontally extending tubes -- This CPC subclass covers radiators whose heat-transfer tubes are arranged to extend horizontally rather than vertically or radially, within a central heating system. The design emphasizes improved heat distribution and compact installation for domestic or space heating applications.
[F24D 2220/2054] Panel radiators with or without extended convection surfaces -- This CPC entry covers panel radiators used in central heating systems that may incorporate extended convection surfaces such as fins to increase heat-transfer efficiency. The devices are typically wall-mounted and provide space heating by circulating hot water or steam through a metal panel.
[F24D 2220/2063] Central heating radiators having heat storage material incorporated -- The entry covers radiators used in central heating systems that incorporate a heat-storage material to retain and release thermal energy, enhancing temperature stability and overall efficiency. These radiators are intended for domestic or space-heating applications where stored heat can be drawn from the radiator body when needed.
[F24D 2220/2072] Radiators being skirting boards between floor and wall or ledges between wall and ceiling -- This CPC subclass covers radiators that are built into or mounted as skirting boards along the floor-wall joint or as ledges at the wall-ceiling junction, forming part of a central heating system.
[F24D 2220/2081] Floor or wall heating panels -- The F24D 2220/2081 entry covers heating panels that are built into floors or walls to provide radiant heat as part of a central heating system. These panels distribute warmth evenly across a space, often using hot water or electric elements, and are used in both domestic and commercial buildings for efficient space heating.
[F24D 2220/209] Sanitary water taps -- The F24D 2220/209 entry classifies sanitary water taps that dispense hot water as part of domestic central-heating systems. It covers the design, construction, and operation of taps used to deliver heated water for bathing, kitchen, or other household uses, and their integration with heating distribution networks.
[F24D 2240/00] Characterizing positions, e.g. of sensors, inlets, outlets -- This subclass covers methods and designs that define or describe the placement of elements such as sensors, inlets and outlets within domestic or space-heating installations, including central heating systems. It addresses how the positions are characterized to improve monitoring, control or overall system performance.
[F24D 2240/10] Placed within or inside of -- F24D 2240/10 covers heating system elements that are situated inside the equipment, such as sensors, inlets, outlets or other components placed within a boiler, heat exchanger, pipe network or radiators. It is used to classify patents describing the internal positioning of these parts in domestic or space-heating installations.
[F24D 2240/12] Placed outside of -- The CPC subclass F24D 2240/12 refers to domestic or space-heating installations where the positions of sensors, inlets, outlets or similar elements are specified as being placed outside of the heating system or its enclosure. It is used to classify patents that describe external mounting or locating of such components in central heating arrangements.
[F24D 2240/20] Placed at top position -- The subclass F24D 2240/20 covers heating systems where elements such as sensors, inlets or outlets are positioned at the top of the installation, for example in domestic or space-heating circuits including central heating. It specifies the top-position arrangement of these components within the system.
[F24D 2240/22] Placed at bottom position -- F24D 2240/22 refers to heating installations, particularly domestic or space-heating systems, in which sensors, inlets, outlets or similar components are located in a bottom position within the apparatus. This subclass is used to classify designs where the lowest point of the system is deliberately used for positioning such elements.
[F24D 2240/24] Placed at centre position -- F24D 2240/24 specifies the location of components such as sensors, inlets or outlets within domestic or space-heating systems when they are positioned at the centre of the apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and domestic-or-space-heating systems, focusing on the geometric characterisation of central placement.
[F24D 2240/243] Vertically centred -- The CPC subclass F24D 2240/243 pertains to heating installations, such as central or domestic heating systems, where sensors, inlets, outlets or similar components are positioned at a vertically centered location within the apparatus, defining a specific layout for efficient heat distribution.
[F24D 2240/246] Horizontally centred -- The entry classifies heating system components whose placement is horizontally centred, such as sensors, inlets or outlets, within domestic or space-heating installations. It is used to denote the relative position of these elements in the overall layout of a heating system.
[F24D 2240/26] Vertically distributed at fixed positions, e.g. multiple sensors distributed over the height of a tank, or a vertical inlet distribution pipe having a plurality of orifices -- F24D 2240/26 refers to heating system designs that locate sensors, inlets or outlets at fixed vertical positions, for example multiple sensors spaced along a tank's height or a vertical inlet pipe equipped with a series of orifices.
[F24D 2240/28] Horizontally distributed at fixed positions -- The CPC subclass F24D 2240/28 pertains to domestic or space-heating systems in which sensors, inlets, outlets or similar components are positioned at predetermined fixed points distributed horizontally across the installation, such as in central heating networks. It classifies designs that specify these fixed horizontal locations.
[F24D 2240/30] At vertical variable positions, e.g. a movable inlet pipe within a tank -- F24D 2240/30 relates to heating installations, particularly domestic or space heating, where the position of a pipe, inlet, or sensor inside a storage tank can be vertically adjusted. The classification covers systems that allow the inlet pipe to be moved up or down to control flow or temperature within the tank.
[F24D 2240/32] At horizontal variable positions -- F24D 2240/32 covers the description or characterization of horizontally variable positions of elements such as sensors, inlets or outlets in domestic or space-heating systems, for example central heating installations. It relates to the layout and adjustable placement of these components to optimise system performance.
[F24D 3/00] Hot-water central heating systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hot-water central heating systems:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - District heating systems -> F24D10/00 - Central heating systems using heat accumulated in storage masses -> F24D11/00
[F24D 3/005] combined with solar energy  solar heat collectors  per se   F24S -- The subclass F24D 3/005 covers hot-water central heating installations that are combined with solar energy, specifically those that incorporate solar heat collectors as an integral part of the heating system. It pertains to the use of solar thermal collectors to supplement or replace conventional heat sources in domestic or space-heating circuits.
[F24D 3/02] with forced circulation, e.g. by pumps -- F24D 3/02 designates hot-water central heating systems that use forced circulation, usually achieved with pumps to move water through the network. It is classified under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic- or space-heating systems.
[F24D 3/04] with the water under high pressure -- It covers hot-water central heating systems in which the water circulates under high pressure, such as pressurized boiler installations for domestic or space heating. The classification includes designs, components and control methods for high-pressure hot water distribution.
[F24D 3/06] Arrangements or devices for maintaining high pressure -- This subclass covers components and configurations used in hot-water central heating installations to keep the water at a pressure above normal operating levels, such as pressure-maintaining vessels, pumps, expansion tanks and control devices. It pertains to mechanical engineering heating, domestic or space heating systems.
[F24D 3/08] in combination with systems for domestic hot-water supply -- The subgroup relates to central heating installations that are combined with domestic hot-water supply systems, covering arrangements where space heating and DHW are provided by the same boiler or circuit. It includes configurations that jointly control temperature, distribution and energy use for both heating and hot water.
[F24D 3/082] Hot water storage tanks specially adapted therefor -- The entry covers hot-water storage tanks that are specifically designed for use in domestic or space heating systems, such as central heating installations. It includes tanks built with features like insulation, heat-exchange surfaces and control mechanisms to efficiently store and supply hot water within a heating circuit.
[F24D 3/085] Double-walled tanks -- Double-walled tanks are storage units for hot water in central heating systems that consist of an inner vessel encased by a second wall with air or insulating material, minimizing heat loss and protecting surrounding structures from high temperatures.
[F24D 3/087] Tap water heat exchangers specially adapted therefore -- F24D 3/087 covers tap water heat exchangers that are specially adapted for use in domestic central heating systems, enabling the transfer of heat from tap water to the heating circuit. These devices are designed to operate within hot-water central heating installations, providing efficient heat exchange.
[F24D 3/10] Feed-line arrangements, e.g. providing for heat-accumulator tanks, expansion tanks -- F24D 3/10 covers the design and arrangement of feed lines in hot-water central heating systems, including provisions for components such as heat-accumulator tanks and expansion tanks that regulate system pressure and store thermal energy. This subclass deals with how these ancillary elements are integrated into the primary circulation loop to ensure stable operation and efficient heat distribution.
[F24D 3/1008] expansion tanks -- F24D 3/1008 covers expansion tanks used in hot-water central heating systems. These devices absorb volume changes in the water due to temperature fluctuations, maintaining system pressure and preventing damage. They are integral components of domestic or space heating installations.
[F24D 3/1016] Tanks having a bladder -- Tanks having a bladder are storage vessels used in hot-water central heating systems where a flexible bladder separates water from a gas or vacuum space, allowing expansion, pressure regulation and preventing waterlogging of the system.
[F24D 3/1025] Compressor controlled pressure heads -- A compressor-controlled pressure head is a pump whose output pressure is regulated by a compressor mechanism, maintaining a constant water pressure in hot-water central heating circuits. By adjusting the pressure automatically, it optimises flow, improves system efficiency and ensures consistent heating performance.
[F24D 3/1033] with compressed gas cylinder -- F24D 3/1033 pertains to hot-water central heating systems that obtain thermal energy from a compressed gas cylinder, such as LPG or propane, which powers a boiler or heat-exchanger for domestic space heating.
[F24D 3/1041] Flow-through -- F24D 3/1041 pertains to flow-through hot-water central heating systems in which heated water is continuously circulated from a boiler through a network of pipes and radiators to supply space heating, without storage tanks.
[F24D 3/105] pumps combined with multiple way valves -- F24D 3/105 relates to hot-water central heating systems and covers devices where a pump is integrated with a multi-way valve, allowing control of water flow to different circuit branches. The combined unit simplifies installation and improves regulation of temperature distribution.
[F24D 3/1058] disposition of pipes and pipe connections -- This subclass covers the arrangement and layout of the pipe network and its connections in hot-water central heating installations, including pipe routing, spacing, supports, and joint configurations to ensure efficient flow and maintenance access. It addresses design aspects for domestic or space heating.
[F24D 3/1066] Distributors for heating liquids -- This subgroup covers devices that distribute heated water or other liquids within central heating installations, such as pumps, circulators, manifolds and distribution networks that convey thermal energy from boiler to radiators or terminals. It includes control valves and flow regulators designed for domestic or space heating systems.
[F24D 3/1075] Built up from modules -- F24D 3/1075 covers hot-water central heating systems that are built from pre-fabricated modules, enabling rapid on-site assembly and flexible layout. The modular design promotes standardisation, simplifies installation, and eases maintenance for residential or building heating applications.
[F24D 3/1083] Filling valves or arrangements for filling -- A filling valve or arrangement for filling is a component or set of components that introduces water into a hot-water central heating system, enabling the system to be charged or replenished. It includes valves, pumps, and associated fittings designed for safe, controlled filling of the circuit.
[F24D 3/1091] Mixing cylinders -- Mixing cylinders are components used in hot-water central heating systems to blend water at different temperatures, providing a stable output temperature and improving overall system efficiency. They consist of a cylindrical vessel with inlet and outlet connections and often contain internal baffles or agitators to ensure thorough mixing.
[F24D 3/12] Tube and panel arrangements for ceiling, wall, or underfloor heating -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electric underfloor heating -> F24D13/02
[F24D 3/122] Details
[F24D 3/125] Hydraulic pipe connections -- F24D 3/125 relates to hydraulic pipe connections employed in hot-water central heating systems, covering fittings, joints and coupling methods that link pipes for distributing heated water in residential or space-heating installations.
[F24D 3/127] Mechanical connections between panels
[F24D 3/14] incorporated in a ceiling, wall or floor -- F24D 3/14 covers hot-water central heating installations in which the heating elements are built into the structure of a building, such as radiant panels or pipes embedded in ceilings, walls or floors. These systems provide space heating by delivering hot water through fixed integral components rather than freestanding radiators.
[F24D 3/141] Tube mountings specially adapted therefor -- F24D 3/141 pertains to tube mountings specially adapted for hot-water central heating systems. It includes fittings, brackets, supports and other devices designed to attach, secure or connect heating tubes within boilers, radiators or heat exchangers, ensuring reliable operation and ease of installation in domestic heating installations.
[F24D 3/142] integrated in prefab construction elements -- F24D 3/142 classifies hot-water central heating systems that are built into prefabricated construction elements, such as panels or modules used in residential or commercial buildings. The entry covers integration of boilers, heat exchangers and distribution components within factory-produced structures, enabling compact, ready-to-install heating solutions.
[F24D 3/143] Tube clips with barbed anchors -- F24D 3/143 covers tube clips equipped with barbed anchors that secure heating pipes in hot-water central heating installations. These devices are used in domestic or space-heating systems to hold tubes in place, providing stability and preventing displacement.
[F24D 3/144] Clips for fastening heating tubes on a reinforcement net or mesh, e.g. mesh for concrete reinforcement -- The subclass F24D 3/144 covers clips designed to fasten hot-water heating tubes to a reinforcement net or mesh, such as the steel mesh used in concrete construction. These fittings secure the tubes in domestic or space heating systems, ensuring stable placement within the surrounding structure.
[F24D 3/145] Convecting elements concealed in wall or floor -- It pertains to hot-water central heating where the convecting heat-exchange elements are hidden within walls or floors, delivering heat by natural convection. These concealed radiators are part of the building fabric and feed heated water through piping.
[F24D 3/146] Tubes specially adapted for underfloor heating -- The classification concerns tubes that are specifically engineered for use in underfloor heating installations, including designs that facilitate flexible routing, integrated connections, or enhanced thermal conductivity to improve the distribution of hot water beneath floors.
[F24D 3/147] arranged in facades -- F24D 3/147 covers hot-water central heating installations where the heating components, such as pipework or radiators, are integrated into the building's façade, allowing the system to be concealed within or mounted on external walls. This arrangement is used to save interior space and provide aesthetic or architectural benefits.
[F24D 3/148] with heat spreading plates -- This subgroup covers hot-water central heating installations that incorporate heat spreading plates to distribute heat more evenly across radiators or panels. It includes designs where plates are attached to the heating element to improve surface area and temperature uniformity.
[F24D 3/149] Tube-laying devices -- F24D 3/149 covers devices that assist in laying the tubing that forms the circulation network of hot-water central heating installations. Such tube-laying equipment may include guides, rollers, automated feeders or manipulators that position, bend or join pipes during system assembly. The code falls under mechanical engineering, heating, domestic-or space-heating systems.
[F24D 3/16] mounted on, or adjacent to, a ceiling, wall or floor -- F24D 3/16 covers hot-water central heating installations where the heating unit or its associated equipment is mounted on, or located next to, a ceiling, wall or floor, typical of space-heating systems integrated into building structures.
[F24D 3/165] Suspended radiant heating ceiling -- F24D 3/165 covers suspended radiant heating ceilings, where ceiling panels containing hot water pipes are mounted below the ceiling surface to radiate heat upward into a room, typically as part of a hot-water central heating system for domestic or space heating.
[F24D 3/18] using heat pumps -- This subclass covers hot-water central heating systems that use heat-pump technology to transfer thermal energy from a low-temperature source to the domestic water circuit. It includes configurations where a heat pump serves as the primary source of heat for producing hot water in residential or building heating installations.
[F24D 5/00] Hot-air central heating systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hot-air central heating systems:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - District heating systems -> F24D10/00 - Central heating systems using heat accumulated in storage masses -> F24D11/00 - Air conditioning systems able for cooling and heating -> F24F
[F24D 5/005] combined with solar energy -- The subclass F24D 5/005 relates to hot-air central heating systems that are combined with solar energy. It covers arrangements where solar collectors supply heat to the air-distribution network, either as the primary source or as an auxiliary supplement to conventional fuel-based heaters.
[F24D 5/02] operating with discharge of hot air into the space or area to be heated -- F24D 5/02 covers hot-air central heating installations that distribute heated air directly into the rooms or zones being conditioned, typically using ducts, vents or diffusers to supply warmth throughout the space.
[F24D 5/04] with return of the air or the air-heater -- This subgroup covers hot-air central heating systems that incorporate a return duct for the heated air or include an integral air-heater, allowing the air to be recirculated to the furnace and improving energy use in domestic or space-heating applications.
[F24D 5/06] operating without discharge of hot air into the space or area to be heated -- F24D 5/06 concerns hot-air central heating installations that supply heat without blowing hot air directly into the conditioned room. The system typically uses heat exchangers, radiant panels or concealed ducts to transfer thermal energy, keeping the heating medium separate from the living space.
[F24D 5/08] with hot air led through radiators -- This subclass of F24D deals with hot-air central heating systems that convey heated air through radiators. It applies to domestic or space-heating installations where hot air is routed via radiator units to provide room heating.
[F24D 5/10] with hot air led through heat-exchange ducts in the walls, floor or ceiling -- The F24D 5/10 subclass covers hot-air central heating systems that deliver heated air through ductwork integrated in walls, floors or ceilings, allowing the air to exchange heat directly with these structural surfaces for space heating. It falls within mechanical-engineering heating technologies for domestic or space-heating applications.
[F24D 5/12] using heat pumps -- F24D 5/12 covers hot-air central heating systems that use heat pumps to generate and circulate warm air within a building. The classification includes designs where the pump extracts heat from ambient sources and delivers it through ducts or vents to provide domestic space heating.
[F24D 7/00] Central heating systems employing heat-transfer fluids not covered by groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Central heating systems employing heat-transfer fluids other than hot-water or hot-air, e.g. oil, salt, gas.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - District heating systems -> F24D10/00 - Central heating systems using heat accumulated in storage masses -> F24D11/00
[F24D 9/00] Central heating systems employing combinations of heat transfer fluids covered by two or more of groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Central heating systems employing combinations of heat transfer fluids covered by two or more of the prior groups.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - District heating systems -> F24D10/00 - Central heating systems using heat accumulated in storage masses -> F24D11/00
[F24D 9/02] Hot water and steam systems -- F24D 9/02 covers central heating installations that use both hot water and steam as heat-transfer media, typically in combined or hybrid systems for domestic or space heating. These arrangements integrate hot-water circulation with steam generation to provide efficient temperature control in buildings.
[F24F] AIR-CONDITIONING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and apparatus for air-conditioning, i.e. the supply of air to or the treatment of air in rooms or spaces by means which provide for the treatment of the air in the following ways:  Methods and apparatus for humidification of air for rooms or spaces.  Methods and apparatus for supplying air or extracting air from rooms or spaces, either by forced movement (for example by fans) or by natural effects (for example convection).  Methods and apparatus for circulating air within rooms or spaces, either by forced movement (for example by fans) or by natural effects (for example convection).  Methods and apparatus for use of air currents for screening, for example of entrances of buildings or of areas where particular operations are performed.  Use of energy recovery systems in air conditioning, ventilation or screening.  Details, for example control arrangements, safety arrangements, ducting arrangements or air flow control members, which are specially adapted for the methods or apparatus specified above.  Treatment, e.g. purification, of air supplied to human living or working spaces in air conditioning systems or in room units otherwise than by heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.  Self-contained air purifiers.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing dirt or fumes from areas where they are produced -> B08B15/00 - Vertical ducts for carrying away waste gases from buildings -> E04F17/02 - Tops for ventilating shafts; Terminals for flues -> F23L17/02  **Glossary:** - Air-conditioning: Supply of air to or the treatment of air in rooms or spaces by means of cooling or a combination of cooling and a further kind of air treatment, e.g. humidification, heating or air purification. - Ventilation: Supply of air to rooms or spaces, extraction of air from rooms or spaces, or circulation of air within rooms or spaces.
[F24F 1/00] Room units for air-conditioning, e.g. separate or self-contained units or units receiving primary air from a central station -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Usually, these room units comprise a refrigerant cycle.  **Glossary:** - Split unit: Air conditioning unit in split configuration, often with the compressor and condenser located in a separate external outdoor unit and the evaporator located in an indoor unit, the two units being connected via refrigerant pipes - Self-contained unit: Air conditioning unit with all apparatus for air treatment installed in a common casing, e.g. compressor, condenser and evaporator
[F24F 1/0003] characterised by a split arrangement, wherein parts of the air-conditioning system, e.g. evaporator and condenser, are in separately located units -- F24F 1/0003 covers split-type air-conditioning systems in which principal components such as the evaporator and condenser are located in separate indoor and outdoor units, allowing the equipment to be installed as self-contained or as part of a larger plant. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, specifically room-unit installations.
[F24F 1/0007] Indoor units, e.g. fan coil units -- F24F 1/0007 designates indoor air-conditioning equipment such as fan-coil units that are installed inside a room or building. These devices may be separate, self-contained, or receive primary air from a central plant, and are used to regulate temperature and airflow within individual spaces.
[F24F 1/00073] comprising a compressor in the indoor unit housing -- This subclass defines indoor air-conditioning room units in which the compressor is installed inside the indoor housing, typically for self-contained or split systems where the indoor component handles both compression and heat exchange. It applies to units serving a single space or receiving primary air from a central plant.
[F24F 1/00075] receiving air from a central station -- F24F 1/00075 covers room air-conditioning units that obtain their supply air from a central plant rather than generating it on site. It includes separate or self-contained units connected to a duct network delivering primary air from a central station for cooling or heating.
[F24F 1/00077] receiving heat exchange fluid entering and leaving the unit as a liquid -- This subclass covers room-air-conditioning units that take in and discharge the heat-exchange fluid entirely in liquid form. It applies to self-contained or split units where the fluid circulates as a liquid through the evaporator and condenser without vaporisation within the indoor unit.
[F24F 1/0011] characterised by air outlets -- F24F 1/0011 covers room air-conditioning units, such as separate or self-contained systems or those fed by a central plant, where the design is defined by the type or arrangement of the air outlets.
[F24F 1/0014] having two or more outlet openings -- The CPC subclass F24F 1/0014 pertains to room-air-conditioning units, either separate or self-contained, or those receiving primary air from a central system, that are equipped with two or more outlet openings for distributing conditioned air within the space. It defines designs where multiple diffusers or vents are provided to improve airflow distribution or serve different zones in a room.
[F24F 1/0018] characterised by fans -- This sub-group covers air-conditioning room units that are characterised by the use of fans, including separate or self-contained units and those receiving primary air from a central station, where the fan provides the air flow.
[F24F 1/0022] Centrifugal or radial fans -- The CPC subclass F24F 1/0022 covers centrifugal or radial fans employed in room-air-conditioning units, including self-contained systems and those supplied with primary air from a central plant. It specifies fan designs that move air by radial or axial flow generated through rotating impellers, serving heating and cooling functions in mechanical engineering applications.
[F24F 1/0025] Cross-flow or tangential fans -- Cross-flow or tangential fans are low-pressure, high-capacity rotary devices that move air parallel to the fan axis, often employed in room-air-conditioning units and other heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems to provide uniform airflow across a wide area. They are distinguished from axial and centrifugal fans by their blade arrangement and flow direction.
[F24F 1/0029] Axial fans -- F24F 1/0029 designates axial fans, rotary devices that move air parallel to the axis of rotation, employed in room-air-conditioning units such as separate, self-contained, or centrally supplied systems. This subclass groups fan configurations and features specific to heating and ventilation applications within mechanical engineering.
[F24F 1/0033] having two or more fans -- This subclass covers self-contained or separate room-air-conditioning units that incorporate two or more fans to move air through the system, allowing higher flow rates or improved distribution. Such configurations are used where a single fan cannot meet the required cooling or heating load.
[F24F 1/0035] characterised by introduction of outside air to the room -- This subclass covers room-air-conditioning units that incorporate fresh outside air into the conditioned space, either by mixing it with recirculated air or by supplying it directly. It applies to separate or self-contained units and those receiving primary air from a central plant.
[F24F 1/0038] in combination with simultaneous exhaustion of inside air -- F24F 1/0038 defines room-air-conditioning units that feature simultaneous indoor-air exhaust while providing conditioned air, applicable to separate, self-contained systems or those receiving primary air from a central plant. The classification falls under the mechanical engineering heating and air-conditioning subgroup.
[F24F 1/0041] characterised by exhaustion of inside air from the room -- This subclass covers room air-conditioning units that discharge indoor air to the outside. It applies to separate or self-contained units, as well as those supplied with primary air from a central plant, where the system intentionally exhausts the room's air.
[F24F 1/0043] characterised by mounting arrangements -- The subclass F24F 1/0043 covers room air-conditioning units that are distinguished principally by their mounting arrangements, such as wall-mounted, floor-standing, ceiling-suspended or combined installations, and includes the structural features that enable these mounting methods.
[F24F 1/0047] mounted in the ceiling or at the ceiling -- F24F 1/0047 covers room air-conditioning units that are installed in or on the ceiling, including self-contained or split units that receive primary air from a central system. The classification applies to ceiling-mounted equipment used for heating, cooling or ventilation of individual spaces.
[F24F 1/005] mounted on the floor -- F24F 1/005 defines air-conditioning room units that are installed on the floor, including self-contained or split systems positioned directly on the floor rather than on walls or ceilings.
[F24F 1/0053] mounted at least partially below the floor -- This subgroup covers air-conditioning room units whose installation includes at least part of the equipment positioned below the floor level, such as floor-concealed split units or underfloor installations that receive primary air from a central system.
[F24F 1/0057] mounted in or on a wall -- The subclass F24F 1/0057 covers air-conditioning room units that are installed on or within a wall, such as wall-mounted split units, self-contained wall cabinets, or devices that receive primary air from a central plant and are fixed to the wall structure. It pertains to heating, ventilating and air-conditioning equipment specifically designed for wall integration.
[F24F 1/0059] characterised by heat exchangers -- F24F 1/0059 covers room air-conditioning units that achieve cooling or heating by means of a heat exchanger, which transfers thermal energy between the indoor air and a secondary fluid or refrigerant. Any form of heat exchanger, such as plate, finned-tube or coil, may be used and is integral to the unit's operation.
[F24F 1/0063] by the mounting or arrangement of the heat exchangers -- This subgroup covers room air-conditioning units such as split, window or self-contained systems classified according to how their heat exchangers are mounted or arranged within the device. It includes configurations where the evaporator and condenser are positioned side by side, stacked, or integrated in other specific layouts.
[F24F 1/0067] by the shape of the heat exchangers or of parts thereof, e.g. of their fins -- This subclass groups room-air-conditioning units that are characterized by the geometric design of their heat-exchanger components, such as the shape of fins or other parts of the exchanger. It is applied when the fin configuration or overall exchanger form is a defining technical feature of the device.
[F24F 1/0068] characterised by the arrangement of refrigerant piping outside the heat exchanger within the unit casing -- This CPC subgroup refers to room-type air-conditioning units, such as separate or self-contained systems, in which the refrigerant piping is positioned outside the heat-exchanger but remains within the unit's outer casing, defining a specific internal layout.
[F24F 1/0071] with means for purifying supplied air -- This subgroup covers self-contained or split room-air-conditioning units that incorporate devices for cleaning the air they deliver, such as filters, ionizers or UV lamps. The purification means are integral to the unit that supplies conditioned air.
[F24F 1/0073] characterised by the mounting or arrangement of filters -- This CPC subgroup pertains to room-type air-conditioning units, including split or self-contained systems, where the invention is defined by the way air filters are mounted, arranged or oriented within the device to influence airflow, accessibility, or cleaning.
[F24F 1/0076] by electric means, e.g. ionisers or electrostatic separators -- The subclass F24F 1/0076 identifies room air-conditioning units that employ electric devices such as ionisers or electrostatic separators to treat the air. It pertains to self-contained or separate units, or those receiving primary air from a central system, which use electrical means for cleaning, ionising or separating particles within the conditioned airflow.
[F24F 1/008] with perfuming or deodorising means
[F24F 1/0083] with dehumidification means -- The subclass F24F 1/0083 pertains to room-air-conditioning units, either separate, self-contained or receiving primary air from a central plant, that incorporate dehumidification means to lower moisture content in the conditioned air. It addresses devices where the dehumidifying function is integrated with the cooling system for space heating or cooling applications.
[F24F 1/0087] with humidification means -- This subclass covers self-contained or split air-conditioning units that incorporate a humidifier to add moisture to the conditioned air. It applies to devices that combine cooling or heating with humidification functions, typically for residential or commercial indoor spaces.
[F24F 1/009] characterised by heating arrangements -- F24F 1/009 covers room-air-conditioning units that incorporate heating functionality, such as electric or water-based heaters, integrated into a self-contained or split system delivering conditioned air to a single space. The classification applies to designs where the heating arrangement is combined with the cooling circuit, allowing the unit to provide both warming and cooling from the same air-handling apparatus.
[F24F 1/0093] with additional radiant heat-discharging elements, e.g. electric heaters -- This subclass covers air-conditioning room units that incorporate supplemental radiant heating devices such as electric heaters to provide combined cooling and radiant heat. It applies to self-contained or split units that receive primary air from a central station and include additional heat-discharging elements.
[F24F 1/0097] using thermoelectric or thermomagnetic means, e.g. Peltier elements -- The subclass covers room-type air-conditioning units that use thermoelectric or thermomagnetic devices, such as Peltier elements, to achieve cooling or heating. It falls under mechanical engineering → heating → air-conditioning → room units.
[F24F 1/01] in which secondary air is induced by injector action of the primary air -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Room units in which secondary air is induced by injector action of the primary air.
[F24F 1/02] Self-contained room units for air-conditioning, i.e. with all apparatus for treatment installed in a common casing -- This subclass covers self-contained air-conditioning units designed for individual rooms, where all components for cooling, heating, and ventilation are housed in a single casing that can be installed directly in the space or connected to a central system. These units may be split or monoblock types and are intended for standalone operation.
[F24F 1/022] comprising a compressor cycle -- This subclass refers to room-type air-conditioning units, such as split or self-contained systems, that incorporate a compressor-driven refrigeration cycle to provide cooling or heating. The compressor cycle typically operates on a vapor-compression principle with refrigerant circulation between evaporator and condenser, and is used in units that receive primary air from a central plant or treat air locally.
[F24F 1/027] mounted in wall openings, e.g. in windows -- The code F24F 1/027 covers room-air-conditioning units that are installed directly in wall openings, such as windows, rather than being freestanding or built into a ceiling. These self-contained or separate units receive primary air from a central station or operate independently while fitting into the wall aperture.
[F24F 1/028] characterised by air supply means, e.g. fan casings, internal dampers or ducts -- F24F 1/028 refers to air-conditioning room units that are distinguished by their air-supply components, such as fan housings, internal dampers or ductwork, which direct primary air to the conditioned space.
[F24F 1/0284] with horizontally arranged fan axis -- The CPC subclass F24F 1/0284 covers room-air-conditioning units in which the fan shaft is positioned horizontally, such as split or wall-mounted systems where air is drawn across a horizontally oriented rotary fan to provide cooling or heating within a space.
[F24F 1/0287] with vertically arranged fan axis -- The subclass F24F 1/0287 covers room-air-conditioning units in which the fan is mounted with a vertical axis, such as split or self-contained models that direct airflow upward or downward through the cabinet. This arrangement can simplify ducting, improve space utilization, or affect how conditioned air is distributed within the treated space.
[F24F 1/029] characterised by the layout or mutual arrangement of components, e.g. of compressors or fans
[F24F 1/03] characterised by mounting arrangements -- F24F 1/03 designates room air-conditioning units, such as split, self-contained or centrally fed units, whose distinguishing feature is the mounting arrangement. It covers installations where the unit is wall-mounted, ceiling-suspended, floor-standing, integrated into furniture or otherwise fixed to a structure, emphasising how the device is attached rather than its internal components.
[F24F 1/031] penetrating a wall or window -- The subgroup concerns the installation of room-air-conditioning units by creating openings through walls or windows to route refrigerant, power, or control lines. It includes methods for cutting, sealing and structurally adapting the building envelope to accommodate self-contained or centrally supplied units.
[F24F 1/0314] mounted on a wall -- Wall-mounted room units for air-conditioning, including self-contained or units that receive primary air from a central station, designed for heating, cooling or ventilation of individual spaces. This classification covers devices installed on walls rather than freestanding or ceiling-mounted systems.
[F24F 1/0317] suspended from the ceiling -- This subclass covers air-conditioning room units that are mounted from the ceiling, including self-contained or split units designed to hang rather than sit on a floor or wall. The classification is used for ceiling-suspended installations in heating, ventilating and air-conditioning equipment.
[F24F 1/032] characterised by heat exchangers -- F24F 1/032 covers room-air-conditioning units-either separate, self-contained systems or units that receive primary air from a central station-where the core function is provided by heat exchangers that transfer thermal energy between the indoor air and a refrigerant or heating medium.
[F24F 1/0323] by the mounting or arrangement of the heat exchangers -- This subclass groups room-air-conditioning units according to the way their heat exchangers are mounted or arranged inside the system. It covers configurations such as vertical versus horizontal placement, stacked or side-by-side layouts, and other structural positions that affect airflow and heat-transfer performance.
[F24F 1/0325] by the shape of the heat exchangers or of parts thereof, e.g. of their fins -- F24F 1/0325 covers room-air-conditioning units that are distinguished according to the geometry of their heat-exchanger components, such as the shape of fins or other parts of the exchanger. It is situated in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under heating and air-conditioning.
[F24F 1/0326] characterised by the arrangement of refrigerant piping outside the heat exchanger within the unit casing -- This subclass of room-air-conditioning units covers designs in which the refrigerant piping is positioned on the outside of the heat-exchanger within the unit casing rather than routed through it. The external arrangement simplifies maintenance and can enhance heat dissipation.
[F24F 1/0328] with means for purifying supplied air -- Room air-conditioning units, whether separate, self-contained or fed from a central plant, that incorporate devices for cleaning the incoming air. The purification means may include filters, ionisers, UV lamps or other technologies to remove contaminants before distribution.
[F24F 1/035] characterised by the mounting or arrangement of filters -- In the CPC, code F24F 1/035 falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, specifically room units for air-conditioning. It covers inventions where the primary interest is how the filters are mounted or arranged within the unit, including self-contained or split systems that receive air from a central plant.
[F24F 1/0353] by electric means, e.g. ionisers or electrostatic separators -- This subclass concerns room air-conditioning units-separate, self-contained, or fed by primary air from a central station-that employ electric means such as ionisers or electrostatic separators to treat the airflow. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and air-conditioning categories.
[F24F 1/0355] with perfuming or deodorising means -- F24F 1/0355 pertains to room-air-conditioning units that include a built-in system for adding fragrance or removing unpleasant odours. It covers both self-contained units and those supplied with primary air from a central plant, where perfuming or deodorising means are integrated into the airflow.
[F24F 1/0358] with dehumidification means -- F24F 1/0358 classifies room air-conditioning units that incorporate dehumidification equipment. The code applies to self-contained or separate units as well as those receiving primary air from a central plant, where moisture removal is an integral part of the climate-control function.
[F24F 1/037] with humidification means -- This subclass covers air-conditioning room units that incorporate an integrated humidifier to add moisture to the supplied air. It applies to self-contained or split units and to systems that draw primary air from a central plant where the humidification function is built into the unit.
[F24F 1/0373] characterised by heating arrangements -- The CPC subclass F24F 1/0373 pertains to room-type air-conditioning units-such as split or self-contained systems or those receiving primary air from a central plant-that incorporate heating arrangements, including electric heaters, heat-pump cycles or other methods of supplying warm air within the same appliance. It distinguishes these combined heating-and-cooling units from purely cooling devices.
[F24F 1/0375] with additional radiant heat-discharging elements, e.g. electric heaters -- F24F 1/0375 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Air-conditioning → Room units for air-conditioning, such as separate or self-contained units or those supplied with primary air from a central station. It designates air-conditioning units that incorporate additional radiant heat-discharging elements, for example electric heaters, to provide supplemental heating within the same apparatus.
[F24F 1/0378] using thermoelectric or thermomagnetic means, e.g. Peltier elements -- F24F 1/0378 covers room-air-conditioning units that achieve heating or cooling by thermoelectric or thermomagnetic means, such as Peltier elements, rather than conventional compressors or refrigerants. It is applied to separate, self-contained or centrally supplied units that use solid-state temperature-change devices for climate control.
[F24F 1/039] using water to enhance cooling, e.g. spraying onto condensers -- F24F 1/039 covers room-type air-conditioning units that improve condenser cooling by applying water, such as sprays, mists or direct water flow onto the heat-exchange surfaces. The water acts as a heat-transfer medium to increase cooling efficiency, and the subclass includes various distribution arrangements, nozzles and water-recycling systems.
[F24F 1/04] Arrangements for portability -- F24F 1/04 covers designs that make room-type air-conditioning units portable, such as self-contained or separate units that can be moved between locations. It includes features like wheels, handles, quick-connect ducts or power links, and modular structures that enable easy relocation without permanent installation.
[F24F 1/06] Separate outdoor units, e.g. outdoor unit to be linked to a separate room comprising a compressor and a heat exchanger -- This subclass covers air-conditioning systems in which the outdoor portion is a distinct unit containing a compressor and a heat exchanger, and is connected to a separate indoor room unit. The configuration is typical of split-type or self-contained packages where the heat-transfer components are located outside the conditioned space.
[F24F 1/08] Compressors specially adapted for separate outdoor units -- This CPC subclass covers compressors specially adapted for separate outdoor air-conditioning units, such as split or self-contained room units, where the compressor is positioned outside the conditioned space. It includes features for outdoor mounting, weather protection, and integration with the unit's refrigerant circuit.
[F24F 1/10] Arrangement or mounting thereof -- F24F 1/10 relates to how separate or self-contained room air-conditioning units are positioned and fixed within a space, covering wall, floor, ceiling or panel mounts, brackets, support frames and integration with ducts or outlets. It addresses the spatial arrangement, orientation and attachment methods used to install these units in residential, commercial or industrial interiors.
[F24F 11/00] Control or safety arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of HVAC devices or systems ( F24F11/00 ).  Elements or components of control device, e.g. flap or damper control, ( F24F11/89 ).
[F24F 11/0001] for ventilation  F24F11/30  takes precedence
[F24F 11/0008] for air-humidification  F24F11/30  takes precedence -- F24F 11/0008 covers air-humidification functions in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems, specifically the control or safety arrangements that regulate moisture addition to the airstream. It falls under Mechanical Engineering - Heating - Air-Conditioning - Control or safety arrangements. The broader subclass F24F 11/30, which includes general humidification devices, takes precedence over this more specific entry.
[F24F 1/12] Vibration or noise prevention thereof -- F24F 1/12 covers techniques for limiting vibration and noise generated by room-air-conditioning units, including isolation mounts, sound-absorbing enclosures, resilient supports and other anti-vibration or acoustic treatments applied to self-contained or centrally fed units.
[F24F 11/30] for purposes related to the operation of the system, e.g. for safety or monitoring -- F24F 11/30 covers components and methods that support the operation of heating or air-conditioning systems by providing safety or monitoring functions. It includes sensors, controllers, alarms and related circuitry that detect faults, regulate performance or signal hazards to protect equipment and occupants. This sub-class falls under control or safety arrangements for HVAC equipment.
[F24F 11/32] Responding to malfunctions or emergencies -- F24F 11/32 covers control or safety systems in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning equipment that detect and react to faults, failures or hazardous conditions, initiating protective actions such as shutdown, alarms or corrective adjustments to prevent damage or danger.
[F24F 11/33] to fire, excessive heat or smoke -- F24F 11/33 covers safety or control systems for heating and air-conditioning units that detect or react to fire, excessive heat or smoke. It includes devices such as fire detectors, heat sensors, automatic shut-off valves and alarm or suppression mechanisms that protect the equipment from combustion hazards.
[F24F 11/34] by opening air passages -- The CPC subclass F24F 11/34 pertains to control or safety arrangements for heating and air-conditioning systems that operate by opening air passages. It includes dampers, shutters, adjustable vents and similar mechanisms that regulate airflow to achieve temperature or ventilation control.
[F24F 11/35] by closing air passages -- A control or safety arrangement for heating or air-conditioning systems that regulates temperature or airflow by blocking or closing ducts, vents, or other air passages. This method is used to modulate the climate within a space by limiting the movement of air through the system.
[F24F 11/36] to leakage of heat-exchange fluid -- F24F 11/36 covers devices or methods that detect, limit, or otherwise manage the unintended escape of fluid from heat-exchange equipment. It pertains to safety or control features that address fluid loss in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems, helping maintain efficiency and prevent damage from leaks.
[F24F 11/37] Resuming operation, e.g. after power outages -- F24F 11/37 relates to control or safety arrangements for heating, ventilation and air-conditioning equipment that automatically restart the system after a power failure, enabling safe and reliable resumption of operation. It includes methods, sensors and devices that detect loss of power and initiate the restarting sequence without manual intervention.
[F24F 11/38] Failure diagnosis -- F24F 11/38 covers techniques for detecting and diagnosing faults in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems, including monitoring of operating parameters, fault-tree analysis and diagnostic algorithms that support control or safety functions.
[F24F 11/39] Monitoring filter performance -- This subclass covers methods, devices or systems that assess the condition or efficiency of air-filter elements in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning installations, usually by measuring pressure drop, flow rate, particle loading or sensor signals to trigger maintenance or safety actions.
[F24F 1/14] Heat exchangers specially adapted for separate outdoor units -- This code covers heat exchangers that are designed specifically for the outdoor component of split or self-contained air-conditioning units. It includes devices that transfer heat between the refrigerant and ambient air in the external unit of a room-air-conditioning system.
[F24F 11/41] Defrosting -- F24F 11/41 relates to control or safety arrangements for defrosting in heating and air-conditioning equipment. It covers devices and methods that automatically remove frost from heat exchangers, evaporators or other components, often by heating, reversing flow, or using timed cycles, to maintain system efficiency and prevent ice buildup.
[F24F 11/42] of outdoor units -- F24F 11/42 addresses control and safety measures for the outdoor components of air-conditioning systems, covering devices and methods that monitor, regulate, protect or shut down compressors, condensers and related equipment in response to operating conditions or faults.
[F24F 11/43] of indoor units -- This subclass pertains to control or safety arrangements for indoor units of air-conditioning systems, covering thermostats, sensors, control panels and other devices that regulate temperature, airflow and protect the unit.
[F24F 11/46] Improving electric energy efficiency or saving -- F24F 11/46 relates to measures that increase the electric energy efficiency or reduce consumption of air-conditioning systems through control or safety arrangements. It includes designs, algorithms, sensors, and circuit techniques that optimise compressor operation, fan speed, temperature set-points or shutdown logic to minimise electricity use.
[F24F 11/47] Responding to energy costs -- This subgroup deals with control strategies and devices that modify the operation of heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems in reaction to changes in energy prices. It includes automated set-point adjustment, load-shedding, demand-response scheduling and other measures that optimise energy consumption while maintaining comfort or safety.
