[F24F 11/48] prior to normal operation, e.g. pre-heating or pre-cooling -- This subclass relates to control or safety arrangements that operate before the regular heating or cooling phase, for example systems that pre-heat or pre-cool equipment to improve efficiency, reduce start-up stress, or prepare the environment for normal operation.
[F24F 11/49] ensuring correct operation, e.g. by trial operation or configuration checks -- The code F24F 11/49 covers measures for verifying that heating or air-conditioning equipment operates correctly, such as trial runs, functional tests and configuration checks. It belongs to the control or safety arrangements subgroup of mechanical engineering heating and air-conditioning. These provisions aim to detect faults and confirm proper setup before normal use.
[F24F 11/50] characterised by user interfaces or communication -- F24F 11/50 covers control or safety arrangements for heating, ventilation or air-conditioning equipment that are distinguished by the presence of user interfaces or communication means, such as displays, buttons, remote control links, network connections or data-exchange functions for monitoring, setting or diagnosing system operation.
[F24F 11/52] Indication arrangements, e.g. displays -- F24F 11/52 covers devices that provide visual or other indications of the operating status of heating, ventilation or air-conditioning control systems. This includes displays, indicator lights and panels that inform users about temperature settings, system faults or safety conditions.
[F24F 11/523] for displaying temperature data -- This subclass covers devices or systems that present temperature information to users, such as digital or analog displays integrated in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning equipment for monitoring and controlling thermal conditions. It includes display panels, indicator lights and read-outs that convey measured temperature values within control or safety arrangements of HVAC systems.
[F24F 11/526] giving audible indications -- F24F 11/526 covers features in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems that provide audible signals to indicate operational status, faults or safety conditions, such as beeps, alarms or spoken prompts used for control or warning purposes.
[F24F 11/54] using one central controller connected to several sub-controllers -- A central controller linked to multiple sub-controllers coordinates the operation of heating or air-conditioning components, allowing a single supervisory unit to manage distributed nodes, synchronize functions, and isolate faults within the system.
[F24F 11/56] Remote control -- In the CPC, F24F covers control or safety arrangements for heating and air-conditioning systems, and subclass 11/56 refers specifically to remote control devices that operate such equipment from a distance, enabling users to adjust temperature or system settings without direct contact with the unit.
[F24F 11/57] using telephone networks -- F24F 11/57 covers heating, ventilating and air-conditioning systems that are monitored, adjusted or protected through telephone or telecommunications networks, allowing remote control, status reporting or safety signalling via the telephone infrastructure. This subclass falls under control or safety arrangements for HVAC equipment.
[F24F 11/58] using Internet communication -- F24F 11/58 covers control or safety arrangements for heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems that employ Internet communication. The classification includes remote monitoring, web-based command of temperature or airflow, and IoT-enabled safety functions that transmit data or receive instructions via online networks. It applies to any device or method that integrates Internet protocols into HVAC control logic.
[F24F 11/59] for presetting -- F24F 11/59 relates to control or safety arrangements in heating and air-conditioning equipment that enable presetting of operating parameters, such as temperature, airflow or mode, so the system can start automatically at a predetermined setting.
[F24F 1/16] Arrangement or mounting thereof -- This subclass concerns the structural arrangement and mounting methods for room-air-conditioning units, whether separate, self-contained or fed primary air from a central source. It includes positioning, support frames, ceiling, wall or floor installations and integration with building structures or ducts.
[F24F 11/61] using timers -- F24F 11/61 covers air-conditioning and heating systems that incorporate timers to control operation, sequencing, or shutdown of components, enhancing energy efficiency, safety or user convenience. It includes any timing devices that automatically start, stop, or adjust HVAC functions based on preset intervals.
[F24F 11/62] characterised by the type of control or by internal processing, e.g. using fuzzy logic, adaptive control or estimation of values -- F24F 11/62 pertains to heating or air-conditioning installations whose operation is defined by the type of control or internal processing, such as fuzzy logic, adaptive control or value-estimation techniques. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning and control or safety arrangements.
[F24F 11/63] Electronic processing -- This subclass covers electronic methods for controlling heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems, including sensors, microprocessors, controllers and safety functions. It includes digital signal processing, programmable logic devices and electronic regulation of temperature, pressure and airflow.
[F24F 11/64] using pre-stored data -- The subclass covers control or safety arrangements for heating and air-conditioning systems that function by referencing data stored beforehand, such as programmed set points, performance tables, or historical measurements, allowing automatic regulation without immediate external input.
[F24F 11/65] for selecting an operating mode -- F24F 11/65 covers control or safety arrangements in heating and air-conditioning systems that select an operating mode. It includes mode selectors, programmable controllers and thermostatic devices that switch between heating, cooling, ventilation or fan-only functions based on user input or sensor data.
[F24F 11/66] Sleep mode -- F24F 11/66 classifies air-conditioning units that incorporate a sleep-mode function, automatically reducing power or shutting down when the system is idle to conserve energy and enhance safety. This falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, and control or safety arrangements.
[F24F 11/67] Switching between heating and cooling modes -- F24F 11/67 pertains to control or safety arrangements that automatically switch a heating system to a cooling mode, or vice versa, in air-conditioning and heating equipment. It includes mechanisms that detect temperature or other operating conditions and trigger the appropriate mode change to maintain desired indoor climate.
[F24F 11/70] Control systems characterised by their outputs -- F24F 11/70 covers control systems for heating, ventilation or air-conditioning installations that are defined primarily by the type of output they produce, such as temperature, humidity, pressure or airflow signals. The classification includes devices that regulate these parameters directly, rather than those classified by their internal mechanisms or control logic.
[F24F 11/72] for controlling the supply of treated air, e.g. its pressure -- F24F 11/72 refers to arrangements that regulate the delivery of conditioned air in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems, specifically by monitoring and adjusting the pressure of the supplied treated air to ensure proper flow and performance.
[F24F 11/74] for controlling air flow rate or air velocity -- F24F 11/74 covers devices and methods that regulate the flow rate or velocity of air in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems. This includes adjustable dampers, variable-speed fans, airflow sensors and control algorithms designed to maintain desired indoor conditions or improve energy efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning and control or safety arrangements.
[F24F 11/745] the air flow rate increasing with an increase of air-current or wind pressure -- This subclass covers devices or methods in which the airflow rate rises as the air-current or wind pressure increases, typically applied in HVAC control or safety arrangements to automatically adjust ventilation.
[F24F 11/75] for maintaining constant air flow rate or air velocity -- F24F 11/75 covers control or safety arrangements in heating and air-conditioning systems that maintain a constant air-flow rate or velocity, ensuring steady ventilation performance despite changes in pressure, temperature or system demand. It includes devices such as flow-rate regulators, variable-speed fans and feedback controllers designed to keep airflow uniform. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, and control or safety arrangements.
[F24F 11/755] for cyclical variation of air flow rate or air velocity -- F24F 11/755 covers heating and air-conditioning control or safety arrangements that provide a cyclical variation of the air-flow rate or air velocity, such as periodic adjustments of fan speed, damper position or blower output. It is applied to HVAC components that intentionally modulate airflow in a repeating pattern to achieve temperature regulation, energy saving or comfort control.
[F24F 11/76] by means responsive to temperature, e.g. bimetal springs -- F24F 11/76 covers control or safety devices for heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems that operate through temperature-responsive elements such as bimetallic strips, thermostatic switches or similar components that move or actuate when a set temperature is reached, enabling automatic regulation or shutdown.
[F24F 11/77] by controlling the speed of ventilators -- F24F 11/77 covers heating, ventilation and air-conditioning arrangements in which the airflow is regulated by varying the speed of ventilators or fans. It includes control devices, motor-drive variations and speed-adjustment mechanisms that modulate air flow for comfort, efficiency or safety purposes.
[F24F 11/79] for controlling the direction of the supplied air -- F24F 11/79 concerns mechanisms that direct the flow of supplied air in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems. It includes devices such as adjustable diffusers, louvers or motor-driven vanes that steer conditioned air toward specific zones, enhancing comfort, efficiency or safety in climate-control installations.
[F24F 1/18] characterised by their shape -- F24F 1/18 covers air-conditioning room units, including separate, self-contained, or centrally supplied devices, in which the invention is primarily defined by the geometric configuration of the unit. The classification applies to features relating to overall shape, dimensions or contour that influence installation, integration or performance.
[F24F 11/80] for controlling the temperature of the supplied air -- F24F 11/80 covers devices or methods that regulate the temperature of air delivered by a heating, ventilation or air-conditioning system. It includes thermostats, temperature sensors and control circuits that adjust heating or cooling output to maintain a set point. The classification is used for patents dealing with temperature control of supplied air in HVAC installations.
[F24F 11/81] by controlling the air supply to heat-exchangers or bypass channels -- This subcategory covers inventions that regulate airflow to heat exchangers or bypass passages to control temperature or safety in heating and air-conditioning systems. It includes mechanisms that adjust supply ducts, valves, or fans to modify heat-transfer performance.
[F24F 11/83] by controlling the supply of heat-exchange fluids to heat-exchangers -- F24F 11/83 relates to control of the supply of heat-exchange fluids to heat-exchangers in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. It includes mechanisms, sensors or control strategies that adjust fluid flow to achieve desired temperature or safety outcomes. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning and control or safety arrangements.
[F24F 11/84] using valves -- F24F 11/84 covers control or safety arrangements in heating and air-conditioning systems that employ valves to regulate flow, pressure, temperature or to isolate circuit sections. It includes valve-driven actuation, throttling, shut-off and safety-relief functions within such installations.
[F24F 11/85] using variable-flow pumps -- The code covers air-conditioning and heating systems that employ variable-flow pumps to modulate fluid circulation, enabling adaptive control of temperature and flow rates for improved efficiency and safety. It includes methods and devices that adjust pump output according to demand, providing precise climate regulation.
[F24F 11/86] by controlling compressors within refrigeration or heat pump circuits -- F24F 11/86 covers methods and devices for controlling compressors in refrigeration or heat-pump circuits, including techniques that start, stop, modulate speed or adjust load to optimise temperature, pressure or energy use and to maintain safe operation.
[F24F 11/87] by controlling absorption or discharge of heat in outdoor units -- F24F 11/87 addresses control strategies that adjust how heat is absorbed or released in the outdoor component of air-conditioning or heating units. It includes mechanisms such as variable-speed compressors, thermostatic valves, and fan speed regulation to optimise heat exchange with the environment.
[F24F 11/871] by controlling outdoor fans -- F24F 11/871 classifies devices that regulate the operation of outdoor fans in heating or air-conditioning systems, covering control mechanisms, safety features and automatic adjustments to fan speed or activation based on temperature, pressure or other environmental parameters.
[F24F 11/873] by controlling refrigerant heaters -- F24F 11/873 covers inventions that regulate the operation of refrigerant heaters in air-conditioning or heating systems, typically to prevent overheating, improve efficiency or ensure safe start-up and shutdown. It includes control circuits, sensors and safety mechanisms that adjust heater power based on temperature, pressure or system status.
[F24F 11/875] by controlling heat-storage apparatus -- The code F24F 11/875 covers control or safety arrangements that regulate heating-air-conditioning systems by managing heat-storage apparatus, such as thermal storage tanks or phase-change devices. It includes methods and devices that adjust operation of the storage element to maintain desired temperature set-points or to optimise energy use.
[F24F 11/88] Electrical aspects, e.g. circuits -- F24F 11/88 covers the electrical elements of heating, ventilation and air-conditioning control or safety systems, such as wiring, power supplies, sensors, actuators and circuit designs that manage temperature, airflow or safety functions. It addresses how electrical circuitry integrates with mechanical HVAC equipment.
[F24F 11/89] Arrangement or mounting of control or safety devices -- This subclass addresses the way control or safety components are positioned and secured within heating or air-conditioning systems. It includes designs for mounting thermostats, pressure switches, relays and similar protective devices to ensure proper operation, accessibility and mechanical stability.
[F24F 1/20] Electric components for separate outdoor units -- The classification covers electrical components that are installed in the outdoor part of separate or self-contained air-conditioning units, including compressors, fan motors, relays, power supplies and control electronics that drive the condenser and fan assembly.
[F24F 12/00] Use of energy recovery systems in air conditioning, ventilation or screening -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus and systems with heat-exchange between supplied and exhausted air, e.g. using an air-to-air heat exchanger or using an intermediate fluid, e.g. heat pump.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Both heat and humidity transfer between supplied and exhausted air -> F24F3/147
[F24F 12/001] with heat-exchange between supplied and exhausted air -- Systems that recover energy by exchanging heat between the incoming fresh air and the outgoing exhaust air in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning installations, thereby improving overall efficiency.
[F24F 12/002] using an intermediate heat-transfer fluid -- The entry covers air-conditioning, ventilation or screening systems that employ a secondary fluid to transport heat between the source and the primary circuit, enabling energy recovery and improved efficiency. It includes designs where the intermediate fluid absorbs heat from one stream and releases it to another, reducing the overall energy demand of the HVAC process.
[F24F 12/003] using a heat pump -- The entry F24F 12/003 covers the use of heat-pump technology to recover energy within air-conditioning, ventilation or screening systems, improving overall efficiency by transferring heat from one part of the system to another. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of heating and air-conditioning and specifies how recovered heat is reused to reduce primary energy consumption.
[F24F 12/006] using an air-to-air heat exchanger  F24F12/002  takes precedence -- F24F 12/006 covers air-conditioning or ventilation systems that incorporate an air-to-air heat exchanger to recover energy from exhaust or return air. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and air-conditioning, specifically the use of energy-recovery devices. This subclass is subordinate to F24F 12/002, which defines the broader category of energy-recovery systems in such installations.
[F24F 1/22] Arrangement or mounting thereof -- F24F 1/22 relates to the specific ways room-type air-conditioning units are positioned or attached, such as wall, ceiling, floor or integrated mounts, detailing the mechanical arrangements that secure the unit while ensuring proper airflow and service access.
[F24F 1/24] Cooling of electric components -- F24F 1/24 covers the cooling of electric components, such as circuit boards, power electronics and other devices that generate heat, using air, liquid or phase-change methods integrated with or separate from room-air conditioning units. The classification includes heat sinks, fans, liquid-cooling loops and thermal management systems designed to keep component temperatures within safe limits.
[F24F 1/26] Refrigerant piping -- F24F 1/26 relates to the pipework that carries refrigerant between the components of a room air-conditioning system, such as the connections, manifolds and distribution lines linking indoor and outdoor units. It includes the design, arrangement and materials of the tubing used to form the refrigerant circuit in separate or self-contained units.
[F24F 1/28] for connecting several separate outdoor units -- F24F 1/28 concerns arrangements for linking multiple independent outdoor units of a room air-conditioning system, enabling coordinated operation or shared control. It pertains to self-contained or separate units that receive primary air from a central station.
[F24F 1/30] for use inside the separate outdoor units -- This CPC subclass (F24F 1/30) pertains to components or features designed to be installed inside the separate outdoor unit of room-air-conditioning systems, such as split or self-contained units, covering elements like coils, filters, control devices or other accessories that function within the outdoor housing.
[F24F 13/00] Details common to, or for air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or use of air currents for screening -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details concerning ducting arrangements ( F24F13/02 ); Air outlets ( F24F13/06 ); Air-flow control members ( F24F13/08 ); Casing or covers ( F24F13/20 ).  Means for preventing or evacuating condensate ( F24F13/22 ).  Means for preventing or suppressing noise ( F24F13/24 ).  Arrangement for air-circulation by means of air-induction ( F24F13/26 ).  Arrangement for mounting of filter ( F24F13/28 ), of heat exchanger ( F24F13/30 ).  Mounting means for HVAC devices ( F24F13/32 ).
[F24F 13/02] Ducting arrangements -- This subclass covers the design, configuration and installation of ducts that convey air for heating, air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or airflow-based screening, detailing how ducts are arranged, connected and routed within a system.
[F24F 13/0209] characterised by their connecting means, e.g. flanges -- F24F 13/0209 covers air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or screening systems that are distinguished by the way their components are joined, for example by flanged connections. The subclass groups designs where the connecting means such as flanges, bolts or clips are an essential feature of the equipment.
[F24F 13/0218] Flexible soft ducts, e.g. ducts made of permeable textiles -- The entry covers flexible soft ducts used in air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or air-current screening, specifically ducts made from permeable textile materials. These ducts are lightweight, collapsible or bendable, allowing airflow while permitting moisture transfer through the fabric, and are employed in heating, cooling and air-handling systems.
[F24F 13/0227] using parts of the building, e.g. air ducts inside the floor, walls or ceiling of a building -- The subclass F24F 13/0227 deals with using structural parts of a building-such as floors, walls or ceilings-as air ducts or distribution channels for heating, air-conditioning, ventilation or humidification, integrating these functions directly into the building's fabric.
[F24F 13/0236] with ducts including air distributors, e.g. air collecting boxes with at least three openings -- This subclass covers air-conditioning installations that use ductwork equipped with air distributors, such as collecting boxes that provide at least three separate openings for directing airflow. It relates to systems for heating, cooling, humidifying or ventilating spaces by employing multiple outlet paths within the duct network.
[F24F 13/0245] Manufacturing or assembly of air ducts; Methods therefor -- F24F 13/0245 relates to the manufacturing and assembly of air ducts used in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. It includes processes such as shaping, joining, sealing, insulating and installing duct components, as well as the methods employed to produce complete ductwork assemblies.
[F24F 13/0254] characterised by their mounting means, e.g. supports -- The subclass concerns air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or airflow screening equipment whose defining feature is the type of mounting arrangement used, such as brackets, frames, clamps or other support structures that attach the unit to a building, vehicle or other substrate.
[F24F 13/0263] Insulation for air ducts -- F24F 13/0263 covers the use of insulating materials or structures applied to air ducts in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. The purpose is to minimise thermal losses, prevent condensation, reduce noise and improve energy efficiency of ductwork.
[F24F 13/0272] Modules for easy installation or transport -- The entry F24F 13/0272 covers modular components intended for rapid installation or transport in air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or air-screening systems, allowing these HVAC elements to be easily assembled, relocated or integrated into larger equipment.
[F24F 13/0281] Multilayer duct -- The F24F 13/0281 entry covers ducts built from two or more superimposed layers, designed to transport heated, cooled, or humidified air while providing thermal insulation, moisture barriers, or enhanced mechanical rigidity. Such multilayer ducts are used in HVAC installations to improve energy efficiency and protect the airflow from external conditions.
[F24F 13/029] Duct comprising an opening for inspection, e.g. manhole -- The entry covers ducts in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems that are provided with an access opening, such as a manhole, to permit inspection, cleaning or maintenance of the interior.
[F24F 13/04] Air-mixing units -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Outlets for directing or distributing air into rooms or spaces, e.g. ceiling air diffuser -> F24F13/06
[F24F 13/06] Outlets for directing or distributing air into rooms or spaces, e.g. ceiling air diffuser -- The entry covers devices that direct or distribute conditioned air into rooms or other spaces, such as ceiling air diffusers, louvers, or similar outlets that spread airflow for heating, cooling, humidification or ventilation. It pertains to the air-conditioning segment of mechanical engineering, focusing on the means of delivering air to interior environments.
[F24F 13/0604] integrated in or forming part of furniture -- This subgroup covers air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or airflow devices that are incorporated into or constitute a component of furniture, such as chairs, desks or cabinets, providing climate control directly through the furniture itself. It includes designs where ducts, vents or small fans are built into the furniture structure.
[F24F 13/062] having one or more bowls or cones diverging in the flow direction -- This subclass covers air-conditioning or ventilation devices that incorporate one or more bowl-shaped or conical elements that expand the airflow as it moves through the system. The divergent geometry is used to distribute or slow the air, enhancing humidification, cooling, or screening functions.
[F24F 13/065] formed as cylindrical or spherical bodies which are rotatable -- The classification covers air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or screening systems that employ rotatable cylindrical or spherical bodies, such as fans, rotors or rotating beads, to generate airflow or condition the air.
[F24F 13/068] formed as perforated walls, ceilings or floors -- This subclass covers air-handling elements formed as perforated walls, ceilings or floors that direct airflow for air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or screening purposes, and it is classified under CPC code F24F 13/068.
[F24F 13/072] of elongated shape, e.g. between ceiling panels -- The F24F 13/072 entry covers devices of an elongated shape that are installed between ceiling panels to direct or control air flow for air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or screening purposes. It pertains to mechanical engineering solutions that manage air currents within indoor environments.
[F24F 13/075] having parallel rods or lamellae directing the outflow, e.g. the rods or lamellae being individually adjustable -- This subclass covers air-conditioning, humidification or ventilation systems that employ parallel rods or lamellae to shape the outgoing airflow, with each rod or lamella capable of individual adjustment.
[F24F 13/078] combined with lighting fixtures -- This subclass covers air-conditioning or ventilation installations that are combined with lighting fixtures, such as ceiling diffusers that incorporate lamps, allowing simultaneous climate control and illumination within a space.
[F24F 13/08] Air-flow control members, e.g. louvres, grilles, flaps or guide plates -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ventilation with forced flowusing wall or window fans, displacing air through the wall or window -> F24F7/013 - Outlets for directing or distributing air into rooms or spaces, e.g. ceiling air diffuser -> F24F13/06
[F24F 13/081] for guiding air around a curve -- The CPC subclass F24F 13/081 covers features that guide air flow around a curved path in air-conditioning, heating or ventilation systems, such as ducts, diffusers or baffles designed to direct the airstream along a bend. It pertains to mechanical engineering solutions for managing airflow curvature in HVAC installations.
[F24F 13/082] Grilles, registers or guards -- Grilles, registers or guards are components that regulate, protect, or direct airflow in heating, air-conditioning, ventilation or air-humidification systems, often forming the interface between ducts and occupied spaces. The classification covers designs, mounting methods and functional features of such devices used for screening or controlling air currents.
[F24F 13/084] with mounting arrangements, e.g. snap fasteners for mounting to the wall or duct -- This subclass covers mounting arrangements for air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or air-screening devices, such as snap fasteners used to attach the equipment to walls or ducts.
[F24F 13/085] including an air filter -- The F24F 13/085 entry covers air-conditioning or ventilation arrangements that incorporate an air filter, including systems for heating, cooling, humidification or airflow screening where filtration of the circulated air is integral to the device.
[F24F 13/10] movable, e.g. dampers -- The subclass F24F 13/10 covers movable components such as dampers that are used in air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or air-current screening systems. It lies within the mechanical-engineering heating and air-conditioning hierarchy.
[F24F 13/105] composed of diaphragms or segments -- This subclass covers air-conditioning, humidifying or ventilation systems that incorporate one or more diaphragms or segmented members to adjust or direct the flow of air. The diaphragms or segments may be flexible or movable parts that vary the opening or shape of ducts, vents or screens for temperature, humidity or screening control.
[F24F 13/12] built up of sliding members -- The entry concerns constructions that incorporate sliding members in air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or related air-current devices, such as dampers, louvers or adjustable ducts. It relates to mechanical engineering heating systems where movement is achieved by components that slide relative to one another.
[F24F 13/14] built up of tilting members, e.g. louvre -- This subclass covers air-conditioning or ventilation devices built from a series of adjustable slats or louvers that can be tilted to regulate airflow, shading, or screening. It includes constructions where the individual members are assembled together to form a multiplexed panel or screen.
[F24F 13/1406] characterised by sealing means -- This subclass covers features of heating, ventilating and air-conditioning systems that involve sealing devices to prevent air leakage, such as gaskets, seals or airtight enclosures, used in air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or airflow screening.
[F24F 13/1413] using more than one tilting member, e.g. with several pivoting blades  F24F13/15  takes precedence -- The class F24F 13/1413 relates to air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or airflow screening devices that employ more than one tilting element, such as several pivoting blades, to control the movement of air. It falls under the broader mechanical-engineering group for heating and air-conditioning, specifically under F24F 13/15, which governs the use of movable aerodynamic members.
[F24F 13/142] using pivoting blades with intersecting axles -- This subclass covers air-conditioning or ventilation systems that employ blades which can pivot and are mounted on intersecting shafts to direct airflow. The arrangement allows the blades to change angle relative to the air stream, improving control of cooling, heating or humidification. It is used in devices such as louvers, dampers or variable-geometry diffusers.
[F24F 13/1426] characterised by actuating means -- This subclass covers air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or airflow-screening installations where the movement of air is controlled by actuating means such as valves, dampers, motor-driven fans or other devices that adjust flow rate or direction. The focus is on the mechanical actuation mechanism rather than the thermal or filtration function.
[F24F 13/1486] characterised by bearings, pivots or hinges -- F24F 13/1486 covers air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or screening devices that incorporate mechanical elements such as bearings, pivots or hinges to allow movable or adjustable parts. The classification applies to systems where these components are integral to the operation or adjustment of airflow-related equipment.
[F24F 13/15] with parallel simultaneously tiltable lamellae -- A device for air-conditioning, humidifying, or ventilating spaces that employs a set of parallel lamellae-flat slats or louvers-that can be tilted all at once to regulate airflow direction and volume. The simultaneous adjustment of the parallel blades allows fine control of air currents for screening, cooling or heating purposes.
[F24F 13/16] built up of parallelly-movable plates -- A built-up arrangement of plates that can move parallel to each other, forming an adjustable air path for heating, cooling, humidification, ventilation or screening. The plates are positioned so airflow passes between them and their relative motion controls flow rate or heat exchange. This concept is classified under F24F 13/16.
[F24F 13/18] specially adapted for insertion in flat panels, e.g. in door or window-pane -- The F24F 13/18 subclass covers air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or air-screening devices that are specially designed to be installed directly into flat panels such as doors or window panes, allowing the unit to sit flush with the surface. This includes compact units or modules that integrate into building elements for discreet climate control.
[F24F 1/32] for connecting the separate outdoor units to indoor units -- F24F 1/32 covers the means for linking separate outdoor units to indoor units in room air-conditioning systems, such as self-contained or split units that receive primary air from a central source. It relates to the piping, ducts, refrigerant lines or other connectors used to transfer conditioned air or refrigerant between the two parts.
[F24F 13/20] Casings or covers -- This subgroup covers protective casings, housings and covers designed for air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or other air-flow devices, focusing on the structural elements that enclose or shield the functional components. It addresses the form, mounting and material aspects of such enclosures.
[F24F 13/22] Means for preventing condensation or evacuating condensate -- F24F 13/22 covers devices incorporated in air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or related systems that stop moisture from forming on surfaces or that remove collected condensate, such as drip trays, drainage pipes, sump pumps or surface-heating elements designed to keep components dry.
[F24F 13/222] for evacuating condensate -- The subclass deals with means for evacuating condensate in air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or air-screening installations, such as drainage, pumping or condensate-removal channels that keep the system dry and functional.
[F24F 13/224] in a window-type room air conditioner -- This subgroup covers features and components designed specifically for window-mounted room air-conditioners, including the integration of cooling, heating, humidification and ventilation functions within the window-type enclosure. It pertains to mechanical engineering of heating and air-conditioning systems.
[F24F 13/24] Means for preventing or suppressing noise -- F24F 13/24 covers devices or methods integrated into heating, air-conditioning, ventilation or air-humidification systems that reduce or eliminate unwanted sound. It includes silencers, acoustic liners, dampers and other noise-control components designed to improve acoustic comfort in buildings.
[F24F 13/26] Arrangements for air-circulation by means of induction, e.g. by fluid coupling or thermal effect -- The F24F 13/26 group covers air-circulation arrangements that rely on induction rather than conventional fans, using mechanisms such as fluid coupling or thermal-induced convection to move air. These systems are employed in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning equipment to distribute conditioned air or create airflow for screening, humidification or ventilation purposes.
[F24F 13/28] Arrangement or mounting of filters -- F24F 13/28 relates to the arrangement or mounting of filters in air-conditioning, air-humidification, ventilation or airflow-screening systems. It covers how filters are positioned, supported or integrated within ducts, units or devices to provide effective air treatment.
[F24F 13/30] Arrangement or mounting of heat-exchangers -- The class concerns the design and placement of heat-exchangers used in air-conditioning, humidification, ventilation or other air-flow systems, covering how the exchangers are positioned, supported and integrated with equipment. It includes mounting methods, orientation and spatial arrangement intended to optimise heat-transfer performance.
[F24F 13/32] Supports for air-conditioning, air-humidification or ventilation units -- The entry defines devices that provide structural support for air-conditioning, air-humidification or ventilation units, including brackets, frames, mounts or other holders that secure the equipment in place. It is classified under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning.
[F24F 1/34] Protection means thereof, e.g. covers for refrigerant pipes -- This subclass covers devices that protect refrigerant piping in room air-conditioning units, including separate or self-contained systems, by providing covers, shields or enclosures that guard the pipes against damage, leakage or external influences.
[F24F 1/36] Drip trays for outdoor units -- F24F 1/36 covers drip trays that are installed beneath outdoor air-conditioning units to collect condensate and protect the surrounding area from water runoff. These components are part of self-contained or split systems that receive primary air from a central station. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, specifically room units for air-conditioning.
[F24F 1/38] Fan details of outdoor units, e.g. bell-mouth shaped inlets or fan mountings -- This CPC subclass F24F 1/38 concerns fan details of outdoor air-conditioning units, including bell-mouth shaped inlets and fan mounting arrangements. It applies to heating and air-conditioning room units that are separate, self-contained, or receive primary air from a central station.
[F24F 1/40] Vibration or noise prevention at outdoor units -- F24F 1/40 relates to techniques for limiting vibration and noise produced by outdoor air-conditioning units, including anti-vibration mounts, acoustic enclosures, sound-absorbing barriers and other measures that improve comfort and meet noise regulations.
[F24F 1/42] characterised by the use of the condensate, e.g. for enhanced cooling -- A subclass for room air-conditioning units that deliberately use the condensate generated during cooling, for example by feeding it to heat exchangers, evaporative coolers, or other secondary cooling elements to boost overall cooling performance and efficiency.
[F24F 1/44] characterised by the use of internal combustion engines -- Room air-conditioning units, including separate or self-contained systems or those supplied with primary air from a central station, that are powered by internal combustion engines. The entry covers designs where the engine directly provides the energy for cooling or heating.
[F24F 1/46] Component arrangements in separate outdoor units -- The subclass addresses the layout and organization of components such as compressors, condensers, fans, heat exchangers and controls within the outdoor portion of split or packaged air-conditioning units. It covers how these parts are positioned, mounted and interconnected to optimise performance, serviceability and space efficiency.
[F24F 1/48] characterised by air airflow, e.g. inlet or outlet airflow -- The subclass F24F 1/48 covers room air-conditioning units whose defining feature is the manner in which air is moved through the device, including the design of inlets, outlets and internal flow paths. It applies to both self-contained and split units where airflow configuration distinguishes the invention.
[F24F 1/50] with outlet air in upward direction -- F24F 1/50 covers room-air-conditioning units, including separate or self-contained systems and those fed from a central station, where the outlet directs the conditioned air upward, typically toward the ceiling to promote mixing and even temperature distribution.
[F24F 1/52] with inlet and outlet arranged on the same side, e.g. for mounting in a wall opening -- The subclass F24F 1/52 covers room air-conditioning units in which both the air inlet and outlet are placed on the same side of the device, a configuration suitable for mounting in a wall opening. It applies to separate or self-contained units that may receive primary air from a central station.
[F24F 1/54] Inlet and outlet arranged on opposite sides -- F24F 1/54 pertains to room-air-conditioning units, such as separate or self-contained systems or those fed primary air from a central source, in which the air inlet and the outlet are placed on opposite sides of the device to promote a straight-through airflow path. This arrangement is common in split-type and portable units designed for efficient heating or cooling of indoor spaces.
[F24F 1/56] Casing or covers of separate outdoor units, e.g. fan guards -- F24F 1/56 relates to the protective casings or covers for standalone outdoor air-conditioning units, including components such as fan guards. The classification applies to equipment that forms the external housing of separate or self-contained units receiving primary air from a central station.
[F24F 1/58] Separate protective covers for outdoor units, e.g. solar guards, snow shields or camouflage -- F24F 1/58 relates to accessories that protect outdoor air-conditioning units, such as separate covers, solar guards, snow shields or camouflage panels. These protective elements are designed for room-type units that are either self-contained or receive primary air from a central plant, shielding the equipment from weather, solar radiation or visual detection.
[F24F 1/60] Arrangement or mounting of the outdoor unit -- This subclass deals with how the outdoor component of a room-air-conditioning system is positioned and secured, including brackets, stands, wall or roof mounts, and the methods used to connect the external unit to the indoor unit.
[F24F 1/62] Wall-mounted -- F24F 1/62 covers wall-mounted room-air-conditioning units that are either separate or self-contained, or that receive primary air from a central plant. The classification applies to installations where the cooling or heating apparatus is fixed directly to a wall, serving a single room or space.
[F24F 1/64] Ceiling-mounted, e.g. below a balcony -- F24F 1/64 covers ceiling-mounted room-air-conditioning units, such as self-contained or split systems installed in a ceiling space, including installations positioned beneath a balcony.
[F24F 1/66] under the floor level -- F24F 1/66 covers air-conditioning room units that are installed at floor level, with the main equipment or air distribution positioned beneath the floor surface. The classification applies to self-contained or separate units that deliver conditioned air from a floor-mounted source, often using under-floor diffusers or ducts to supply the space.
[F24F 1/68] Arrangement of multiple separate outdoor units -- The classification covers the layout and connection of several independent outdoor units that serve a single or multiple indoor room-air-conditioning systems. It includes the positioning, piping, electrical wiring and control schemes used when the outdoor condensers are installed separately rather than as a combined unit.
[F24F 2003/003] with primary air treatment in the central station and subsequent secondary air treatment in air treatment units located in or near the rooms -- The classification covers ventilation or air-conditioning systems where the primary air treatment such as filtration, heating, cooling or humidity control is carried out in a central station, and the air then receives secondary treatment in smaller units located in or near individual rooms.
[F24F 2003/005] with a single air duct for transporting treated primary air from the central station to air treatment units located in or near the rooms -- This CPC subgroup pertains to air-conditioning installations that use a single duct to convey the treated primary air from a central plant to individual treatment units situated in or near the occupied spaces. It falls under class F24F for air-conditioning, ventilation and heating systems.
[F24F 2003/006] with two air ducts for separately transporting treated hot and cold primary air from the central station to air treatment units located in or near the rooms -- The classification refers to heating, ventilating or air-conditioning installations that use two separate ducts to convey treated hot and cold primary air from a central plant to individual air-treatment units placed in or near the rooms. It covers systems where the dual-duct arrangement enables independent distribution of temperature-controlled airflow to each space.
[F24F 2003/008] Supplying highly filtered air to a room or to a limited area within a room -- F24F 2003/008 covers systems that deliver air that has been cleaned to a high degree, such as through HEPA or ULPA filtration, directly to a room or a confined zone within a room. It includes devices and methods for generating, moving, and distributing this highly filtered airflow to maintain clean-room conditions, protect occupants from contaminants, or provide localized air purification. The focus is on the supply of the filtered air rather than general ventilation.
[F24F 2003/0446] with a single air duct for transporting treated air from the central station to the rooms -- This subclass covers heating, ventilation or air-conditioning installations that use one duct to convey the treated air generated at a central plant to the individual rooms or zones, rather than multiple separate ducts for each space. It pertains to the distribution network design where a single conduit supplies conditioned airflow throughout the building.
[F24F 2003/0448] with two air ducts for separately transporting treated hot and cold air from the central station to the rooms -- A heating, ventilation or air-conditioning system that employs a pair of separate ducts to convey treated hot air and treated cold air from a central unit to individual rooms, allowing independent distribution of each temperature stream. This configuration is used to provide precise climate control in each space.
[F24F 2003/1435] comprising semi-permeable membrane -- F24F 2003/1435 defines heat-exchange apparatus that includes a semi-permeable membrane as a key component, enabling selective passage of fluids or gases while maintaining separation of phases. Such devices are used in membrane condensers, evaporators and similar thermal processes.
[F24F 2003/144] by dehumidification only -- This subclass covers air-conditioning or climate-control installations that achieve cooling exclusively by reducing the humidity of the air, i.e., by dehumidification only, without using direct sensible cooling or heat-exchange cycles. It includes systems that employ condensation, desiccants or adsorption to lower moisture content for comfort or process purposes.
[F24F 2003/1446] by condensing -- The entry covers methods and apparatuses that heat a liquid by utilizing the latent heat released when a vapor condenses, such as steam-condensing heat exchangers used for water or other fluid heating.
[F24F 2003/1452] heat extracted from the humid air for condensing is returned to the dried air -- The entry covers dehumidification systems that reclaim the heat released during condensation of moisture in humid air by directing it back to the already dried air stream, thereby improving overall energy efficiency of the HVAC process.
[F24F 2003/1458] using regenerators -- F24F 2003/1458 covers heat-exchange or HVAC systems that incorporate regenerators, devices that store thermal energy during one part of a cycle and release it in another to improve heating, cooling or air-conditioning efficiency. It includes designs where the regenerator is used for heat recovery, thermal storage or temperature regulation within the overall air-handling process.
[F24F 2003/1464] using rotating regenerators -- F24F 2003/1464 falls under CPC section F24F, which covers heating, ventilating and air-conditioning systems that employ regenerative heat exchangers. This subclass specifically relates to the use of rotating regenerator units that transfer heat between fluid streams as the regenerator elements turn or revolve.
[F24F 2005/0025] using heat exchange fluid storage tanks -- F24F 2005/0025 covers inventions that employ storage tanks containing a heat-exchange fluid to retain thermal energy for later use. It includes systems that charge the fluid with heat (or cold) from sources such as solar collectors, waste heat or chillers, and discharge it to provide heating, cooling or power when needed.
[F24F 2005/0028] using hydridable metals as energy storage media -- The classification covers technologies that store energy by reversibly reacting hydrogen with metals to form metal hydrides, using these hydridable metals as the primary energy-storage medium. It includes methods for hydrogen absorption and release, system designs for charging and discharging, and related control or safety features, often applied in batteries, fuel-cell backup, or load-leveling applications.
[F24F 2005/0032] Systems storing energy during the night -- F24F 2005/0032 pertains to heating, ventilating or air-conditioning systems that store energy generated or collected during daylight hours for release during the night, such as night-storage heaters, thermal-mass storage, or phase-change material accumulators. The classification covers the design, control and integration of such storage mechanisms within building climate systems.
[F24F 2005/0039] using a cryogen, e.g. CO 2  liquid or N 2  liquid -- The code covers devices or processes that employ a cryogenic liquid such as liquid carbon-dioxide or liquid nitrogen to achieve cooling, freezing or temperature control, often in refrigeration, food preservation or industrial thermal-management applications.
[F24F 2005/0053] receiving heat-exchange fluid from a well -- This group covers devices and methods for receiving the heat-exchange fluid brought up from a geothermal well, including reception chambers, connectors, separators and flow-control elements that guide the fluid from the wellhead to a plant or heat-exchanger.
[F24F 2005/0057] receiving heat-exchange fluid from a closed circuit in the ground -- This subclass covers apparatus or methods that receive a heat-exchange fluid returning from a closed underground circuit, typically in geothermal heat-pump systems, where the fluid has transferred thermal energy with the ground before being delivered to a heat-exchanger for further use.
[F24F 2005/006] receiving heat-exchange fluid from the drinking or sanitary water supply circuit -- The class concerns devices that draw a heat-exchange fluid directly from a drinking-water or sanitary water supply circuit for heating or cooling purposes. It includes arrangements where the domestic water network itself serves as the source of the fluid that passes through a heat-exchanger, rather than a separate closed loop.
[F24F 2005/0064] using solar energy -- The CPC class F24F covers heating, ventilating, air-conditioning and hot-water supply installations, specifically systems that generate thermal energy for space heating, water heating or air treatment. Sub-entry 2005/0064 designates those installations that derive their energy from solar radiation, including solar collectors, thermal storage and related control equipment.
[F24F 2005/0067] with photovoltaic panels -- F24F 2005/0067 covers heating, ventilating, air-conditioning or similar installations that incorporate photovoltaic panels, typically to generate electricity alongside thermal functions. The classification applies to systems where solar cells are integrated into the equipment or its surrounding structures to supplement power for the plant's operation. It includes combined solar-thermal and PV solutions for residential, commercial or industrial use.
[F24F 2005/0078] Double windows -- Double windows refers to window assemblies composed of two glazed units separated by a sealed cavity, providing improved thermal insulation, sound reduction, and sometimes safety. They may be installed in walls or roofs and can include movable or fixed sections.
[F24F 2005/0082] Facades -- F24F 2005/0082 covers the external envelope of buildings, specifically the design, construction and attachment of facades, cladding panels and outer wall systems that provide weather protection, thermal insulation or aesthetic covering for structures.
[F24F 2006/001] using a water curtain -- This CPC subgroup covers ventilation or air-conditioning installations that incorporate a water curtain as a means of cooling, humidifying, or creating a protective barrier in the airflow. The water curtain may be used to lower temperature, reduce dust, or improve air quality in industrial, commercial, or residential settings.
[F24F 2006/003] using a decorative fountain -- The CPC entry F24F 2006/003 covers the use of decorative fountains for circulating water as an ornamental or environmental feature. It includes designs, installations and operation of ornamental water jets, cascades or sprays that provide visual display, mild cooling or humidification, and any inventions that employ a fountain to enhance the appearance of a space or improve indoor climate.
[F24F 2006/005] using plants -- The CPC entry F24F 2006/005 covers inventions that integrate living plants into heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems, such as using plant transpiration for cooling, phytoremediation for air cleaning, or plant biomass for heat generation. It includes designs of green walls, roof gardens, bio-filters and other plant-based components that contribute to climate control in buildings or industrial processes.
[F24F 2006/006] with water treatment -- F24F 2006/006 refers to fire-fighting or fire-protection installations that incorporate a water-treatment step, such as filtration, demineralisation, chemical dosing or other conditioning of the water before it is used for extinguishing. The subclass groups systems where water quality is specifically managed to improve fire-suppression performance.
[F24F 2006/008] Air-humidifier with water reservoir -- F24F 2006/008 covers air humidifiers that incorporate a water reservoir as part of the device, where the reservoir stores water that is vaporized or dispersed into the air to increase humidity. The classification applies to machines where the tank is integral and includes mechanisms for water supply, distribution and control.
[F24F 2006/046] with a water pump -- F24F 2006/046 covers heating or hot-water installations that incorporate a water pump for circulating the heated fluid, such as hydronic radiators, water-based heating systems and combined heating-cooling units that rely on pumped water to distribute heat.
[F24F 2006/065] using slowly rotating discs for evaporation -- The CPC subclass F24F 2006/065 covers methods that employ slowly rotating discs to spread a liquid into a thin film, thereby increasing the surface area and promoting evaporation for drying, concentration or heat-recovery purposes, often used in chemical, food-processing or waste-treatment applications.
[F24F 2006/143] using pressurised air for spraying -- A spray apparatus that employs a stream of compressed air to atomize and discharge a fluid, where the pressurised air serves as the motive force for the spray. This includes devices such as air-powered paint sprayers, agricultural sprayers and other dispensers that generate a fine mist by mixing liquid with high-pressure air. The classification applies when the air pressure, rather than a pump or other mechanical means, creates the spray.
[F24F 2006/146] using pressurised water for spraying -- The CPC subclass F24F 2006/146 covers systems and methods that generate a spray by discharging water that has been pressurised, without the addition of chemicals or foam. It includes devices such as pressure-water sprayers used for cooling, cleaning, fire-extinguishing or similar applications where a fine water jet or mist is required.
[F24F 2007/001] with exhausting air ducts -- The CPC group F24F 2007/001 classifies heating, ventilation or air-conditioning apparatus that includes dedicated exhaust air ducts for removing indoor air to the outside. It covers designs where the exhaust ducts are integrated with the unit and may incorporate flow-control, back-pressure prevention or filtration features to improve indoor air quality.
[F24F 2007/002] Junction box, e.g. for ducts from kitchen, toilet or bathroom -- The entry F24F 2007/002 covers junction boxes used to connect ducts or pipework in residential or commercial buildings, particularly those serving kitchens, toilets or bathrooms. It includes devices that join, split or transition ventilation, waste-water or drainage lines, providing a sealed enclosure for the connections. This classification is used for products that facilitate the installation and maintenance of plumbing or air-handling systems in wet areas.
[F24F 2007/0025] using vent ports in a wall -- F24F 2007/0025 pertains to ventilation systems that employ vent ports installed in a wall to allow air exchange between interior and exterior spaces, covering the design, placement and sealing of such openings and their integration with heating, cooling or exhaust equipment.
[F24F 2007/004] Natural ventilation using convection -- The CPC subclass F24F 2007/004 relates to natural ventilation systems that rely on convection currents generated by temperature differences to move air through a structure. It covers designs where heated indoor air rises and cooler outdoor air is drawn in without mechanical fans, typically applied to buildings, tunnels, or other enclosed spaces.
[F24F 2007/005] Cyclic ventilation, e.g. alternating air supply volume or reversing flow direction -- This class covers ventilation systems that operate in a cyclic manner, where the airflow or supplied volume is periodically varied or the direction of flow is reversed. Such arrangements are used to improve air quality, reduce energy consumption, or control temperature and humidity in buildings, vehicles or equipment.
[F24F 2009/002] Room dividers -- F24F 2009/002 covers room-divider devices that create temporary or permanent partitions between interior spaces. The classification includes movable walls, folding panels, sliding screens and other structures designed to separate, conceal or reconfigure areas within a building, often for privacy, acoustics or functional layout changes. It pertains to mechanisms that enable installation, adjustment or removal without permanent alteration of the surrounding architecture.
[F24F 2009/005] combined with a door -- F24F 2009/005 covers heating or climate control devices that are integrated with a door, such as doors that include built-in heaters, temperature-controlled seals, or ventilation components to prevent condensation or improve energy efficiency.
[F24F 2009/007] using more than one jet or band in the air curtain -- This CPC subclass relates to air-curtain installations that use two or more jets or bands of airflow, arranged side-by-side or in series, to enhance the stability, uniformity or coverage of the protective air barrier.
[F24F 2011/0002] for admittance of outside air -- The code F24F 2011/0002 refers to elements that admit outside air into a structure, such as intake openings, louvers or controlled vents, used in ventilation or air-conditioning systems to provide fresh airflow.
[F24F 2011/0004] to create overpressure in a room -- F24F 2011/0004 covers methods or apparatus that produce a higher air pressure inside a room than in adjacent spaces, usually to prevent ingress of smoke, dust, contaminants or to maintain a controlled environment. It is applied in ventilation, fire protection, clean-room and other systems that need over-pressurisation for safety or quality purposes.
[F24F 2011/0005] to create underpressure in a room, keeping contamination inside -- F24F 2011/0005 covers systems and methods that generate a negative pressure in an enclosed space to prevent the escape of contaminants, typically used in laboratories, hospitals, or cleanrooms. The classification includes ventilation arrangements, fans, ducts and control mechanisms designed to keep hazardous or infectious agents confined within the room.
[F24F 2011/0006] using low temperature external supply air to assist cooling -- The CPC group F24F 2011/0006 covers climate-control systems that employ low-temperature external supply air to assist cooling, such as economizer or mixed-air strategies that introduce cool outdoor air to reduce or replace mechanical refrigeration, often combined with heat-exchange or recovery devices for improved energy efficiency.
[F24F 2012/005] using heat pipes -- This subgroup covers inventions that incorporate heat pipes for the transfer of thermal energy within heating, ventilation, air-conditioning or sanitary installations, including radiators, heat exchangers and other climate-control components. It includes methods and apparatuses that use the capillary-driven, passive flow of vapor inside sealed tubes to collect, transport and release heat in building or equipment thermal-management systems.
[F24F 2012/007] using a by-pass for bypassing the heat-exchanger -- F24F 2012/007 covers methods and apparatus that employ a bypass circuit to route fluid around a heat exchanger. The bypass is used to control temperature, allow continuous operation during maintenance, or improve system efficiency by temporarily avoiding heat exchange.
[F24F 2012/008] cyclic routing supply and exhaust air -- The CPC subclass F24F 2012/008 relates to ventilation or air-conditioning installations that employ a cyclic flow of air, where the same airstream is used alternately as supply and exhaust. It covers systems that regulate indoor climate by repeatedly routing the air through heating, cooling or filtering stages before re-introducing it into the space.
[F24F 2013/0608] Perforated ducts -- This group includes ductwork that has built-in perforations or slots to provide uniform air distribution, used in ventilation, heating or air-conditioning systems. It covers designs, manufacturing methods and modifications of perforated duct sections, liners or panels.
[F24F 2013/0612] Induction nozzles without swirl means -- This CPC subgroup covers induction nozzles that deliver airflow without employing swirl-generating devices, such as straight-through or jet nozzles that create a directed jet of air or gas. It includes designs where the flow is accelerated or diffused without an internal vane or swirl chamber, typically used in ventilation, HVAC, or industrial process equipment.
[F24F 2013/0616] Outlets that have intake openings -- F24F 2013/0616 covers outlets that incorporate intake openings for drawing air or fluid into a system, such as grilles, diffusers or registers used in ventilation, heating or cooling devices. The classification applies to designs where the outlet includes one or more openings that admit external air.
[F24F 2013/087] using inflatable bellows -- The CPC subclass F24F 2013/087 covers devices that employ inflatable bellows as a functional element, typically for adjusting or controlling the flow of fluids or gases in heating, ventilation, air-conditioning, or related systems. It includes designs where the bellows are expanded or contracted by pressurised air or other fluids to modulate openings, dampen vibrations, or provide sealing.
[F24F 2013/088] Air-flow straightener -- Air-flow straighteners are devices that align and smooth the direction of a gas stream, typically using vanes, plates, or diffusers placed in ducts, vents, or exhaust systems to reduce turbulence, improve distribution and enhance the efficiency of heating, ventilation or air-conditioning equipment. They are employed in both industrial and building applications where uniform airflow is required.
[F24F 2013/1433] with electric motors -- This CPC subgroup includes heating or hot-air generating devices that incorporate an electric motor to drive components such as fans, blowers, agitators or pumps. The motor provides the mechanical energy needed for air circulation, material movement or heat distribution within the apparatus. It applies to furnaces, ovens, dryers and similar installations where the motor is an essential part of the heating system.
[F24F 2013/144] with thermoactuators -- The CPC subclass F24F 2013/144 relates to heating or cooling devices that incorporate thermoactuators, which are components that change shape or position in response to temperature variations to control fluid flow or heat exchange. It covers systems where temperature-driven actuation is used to modulate operation, such as thermostatically controlled valves, shutters or pumps within heating, ventilation, air-conditioning, or refrigeration apparatus.
[F24F 2013/1446] with gearings -- F24F 2013/1446 covers heating-of-liquids apparatus that incorporates gear mechanisms, such as gear-driven pumps, gear-linked shafts or gear trains used to transmit power, adjust flow or control temperature within the heating system. It pertains to devices where the gearing is an integral part of the heating function, enabling mechanical drive of circulating fluids or heat-exchange components.
[F24F 2013/1453] with cables, e.g. bowden cables -- F24F 2013/1453 refers to washing-machine or dryer designs that use flexible cables, such as bowden cables, to transmit mechanical force or control signals between parts. These cables are employed for functions like door locking, drum drive, or valve operation, providing a compact, protected linkage within the appliance.
[F24F 2013/146] with springs -- F24F 2013/146 covers air-conditioning and air-heating installations that incorporate springs, such as spring-loaded dampers, shutters, filter housings or other components where spring action provides movement, sealing or pressure regulation within the heating, ventilation or air-conditioning system.
[F24F 2013/1466] with pneumatic means -- F24F 2013/1466 covers heating, ventilation or air-conditioning installations that incorporate pneumatic means, such as air-driven actuators, valves or control devices, to regulate flow, pressure or temperature. The subclass groups inventions where pneumatic components are essential to the operation of the system.
[F24F 2013/1473] with cams or levers -- This subgroup concerns heating, ventilation or cooling devices for buildings that are operated by mechanical cams or levers, such as thermostats, dampers, or valve mechanisms where the control motion is transferred directly through a camshaft or lever arm.
[F24F 2013/148] with magnets -- F24F 2013/148 covers air-conditioning or heating installations that incorporate magnetic components, such as magnetic refrigerants, magnetocaloric elements, or magnetic heat-pump devices. The classification is applied to inventions that use magnets to achieve temperature regulation, heat transfer or fluid movement.
[F24F 2013/1493] using an elastic membrane -- This CPC subclass concerns ventilation or air-conditioning systems that employ an elastic membrane to regulate airflow, seal ducts, or adapt to structural movement. The membrane may be stretched, inflated, or otherwise deformed to control flow or provide a flexible connection within the ductwork.
[F24F 2013/202] Mounting a compressor unit therein -- This CPC subgroup covers the arrangement or installation of a compressor unit inside a larger apparatus such as a refrigeration or air-conditioning system, specifying how the compressor is mounted within the enclosure or housing.
[F24F 2013/205] Mounting a ventilator fan therein -- F24F 2013/205 covers the arrangement of a ventilator fan within a duct, casing or other enclosure, focusing on the mounting configuration that secures the fan in place for air-handling systems. The subclass details methods, brackets or supports that integrate the fan into the surrounding structure.
[F24F 2013/207] with control knobs;  Mounting controlling members or control units therein -- The CPC subgroup F24F 2013/207 classifies heating or cooking appliances that incorporate control knobs and specify the mounting of the controlling members or control units within the equipment, such as ovens, stoves or water heaters where the user interface is integrated into the housing.
[F24F 2013/221] to avoid the formation of condensate, e.g. dew -- The CPC subgroup F24F 2013/221 covers solutions that prevent condensate or dew from forming on surfaces, such as walls, windows, or equipment, by using temperature regulation, airflow management, dehumidification, or surface heating. It includes devices, control methods, and coatings designed to keep moisture from accumulating in indoor or industrial environments.
[F24F 2013/225] by evaporating the condensate in the cooling medium, e.g. in air flow from the condenser -- This subclass covers refrigeration or air-conditioning devices that eliminate condensate by causing it to evaporate directly in the cooling medium, for example by passing the condensate into the air stream leaving the condenser. The evaporated water is reclaimed as part of the airflow, reducing drip or drainage requirements.
[F24F 2013/227] Condensate pipe for drainage of condensate from the evaporator -- F24F 2013/227 covers condensate pipes used to remove liquid condensate that forms in an evaporator, typically in refrigeration, air-conditioning or heat-pump systems. The pipe is designed to collect and discharge the condensate safely away from the evaporator and surrounding equipment. This sub-group falls under the broader class for devices involving fluid handling and drainage in climate-control installations.
[F24F 2013/228] Treatment of condensate, e.g. sterilising -- The code refers to technologies for handling and conditioning condensate, especially by sterilising or otherwise disinfecting it, typically in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning installations to prevent microbial growth and maintain system hygiene.
[F24F 2013/242] Sound-absorbing material -- F24F 2013/242 relates to sound-absorbing material employed in building interiors to diminish airborne noise. It includes porous, fibrous, foam or composite substances applied to walls, ceilings or other surfaces to reduce reverberation and improve acoustic comfort.
[F24F 2013/245] using resonance -- The CPC subclass F24F 2013/245 covers HVAC and fluid-treatment devices that exploit resonant phenomena-such as acoustic, electromagnetic or mechanical resonance-to enhance heat exchange, mixing, or flow control. It includes systems that generate or use resonance to improve energy efficiency or performance of heating, cooling, ventilation or related processes.
[F24F 2013/247] Active noise-suppression -- F24F 2013/247 covers active noise-suppression systems that generate sound waves or control mechanisms to cancel unwanted acoustic disturbances, typically in heating, ventilation, air-conditioning or similar installations. It includes sensors, signal-processing units and actuators designed to reduce noise levels in enclosed spaces.
[F24F 2110/00] Indexing scheme associated with group -- F24F 2110/00 in the CPC denotes the indexing scheme used to categorize groups of air-conditioning or heating equipment within the broader class of mechanical engineering. The entry defines how particular device groups are arranged and referenced in patent documents, facilitating systematic searching and retrieval of related inventions.
[F24F 2110/10] Temperature -- F24F 2110/10 covers the control, measurement and regulation of temperature in air-conditioning and heating systems within mechanical engineering. It includes devices, methods and circuits that sense or set temperature levels to maintain indoor climate. This subclass falls under the broader categories of heating and air-conditioning.
[F24F 2110/12] of the outside air -- F24F 2110/12 covers air-conditioning systems that involve the use of outside air, including its intake, mixing, or treatment for heating, cooling or ventilation purposes.
[F24F 2110/20] Humidity -- The F24F 2110/20 class relates to humidity control in air-conditioning systems, encompassing methods for generating, measuring, removing, or distributing moisture in indoor environments. It is situated in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under heating and then air-conditioning.
[F24F 2110/22] of the outside air -- The CPC subgroup F24F 2110/22 deals with heating, ventilation or air-conditioning arrangements that utilize outside air, such as intake of ambient air for temperature regulation of buildings or equipment. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of heating and air-conditioning.
[F24F 2110/30] Velocity -- The CPC entry F24F 2110/30 covers features and methods concerned with the velocity of air or other gases in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. It includes devices that measure, indicate, or control air flow speed, such as velocity sensors, anemometers and flow-rate regulators, and relates to design aspects that affect airflow rates in ducts and diffusers.
[F24F 2110/32] of the outside air -- F24F 2110/32 covers aspects of air-conditioning systems that handle the outside air, including intake, pre-treatment, mixing or heat exchange with ambient air. It encompasses devices that regulate, filter or condition fresh air before it enters the conditioned space.
[F24F 2110/40] Pressure, e.g. wind pressure -- F24F 2110/40 relates to the treatment of pressure effects, such as wind pressure, in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning equipment. It covers devices, methods or controls that measure, compensate for or protect against such pressures within HVAC systems.
[F24F 2110/50] Air quality properties -- F24F 2110/50 concerns the characteristics of indoor air quality addressed by heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems, including filtration, humidity regulation, removal of contaminants, and control of temperature, odor and particulate levels to maintain a healthy, comfortable environment.
[F24F 2110/52] of the outside air -- The CPC entry F24F 2110/52 covers devices or methods that utilize outside air in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems, such as controls that regulate the intake of outdoor air for temperature or air-quality management. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Air-Conditioning and pertains to the handling of external air to influence indoor climate.
[F24F 2110/60] Odour -- F24F 2110/60 - Odour relates to features of air-conditioning or heating installations that generate, neutralise, mask, or detect unpleasant smells, including odor-absorbing filters, deodorising additives, and scent-dispensing devices.
[F24F 2110/62] Tobacco smoke -- F24F 2110/62 covers systems and components for the removal, extraction or treatment of tobacco smoke within heating, ventilation and air-conditioning installations. It includes smoke-purging ducts, filters and control devices designed to improve indoor air quality in spaces where smoking occurs.
[F24F 2110/64] Airborne particle content -- The CPC subclass F24F 2110/64 covers technical features dealing with the amount or type of particles present in air handled by heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems, including filtration, cleaning, monitoring and control of airborne contaminants. It is applied to inventions that address removal, reduction, measurement or management of dust, pollen, smoke or other particulate matter in indoor air streams.
[F24F 2110/65] Concentration of specific substances or contaminants -- The CPC subclass F24F 2110/65 falls under mechanical engineering, heating and air-conditioning and addresses methods and apparatus for concentrating specific substances or removing contaminants within HVAC systems. It includes technologies such as selective filtration, adsorption, membrane separation and other techniques that concentrate or eliminate pollutants from indoor air streams.
[F24F 2110/66] Volatile organic compounds [VOC] -- F24F 2110/66 covers the use, control and mitigation of volatile organic compounds (VOC) in air-conditioning and heating systems, including emission measurement, filtration and treatment technologies. It is classified under mechanical engineering, heating and air-conditioning.
[F24F 2110/68] Radon -- This classification covers devices, methods and systems incorporated in heating or air-conditioning installations for detecting, diluting or removing radon gas from indoor spaces. It includes ventilation strategies, radon traps, sensors and control arrangements aimed at limiting radon exposure in buildings.
[F24F 2110/70] Carbon dioxide -- F24F 2110/70 covers the use of carbon dioxide as a working fluid in air-conditioning and heating systems. It includes devices and methods that employ CO2 as a refrigerant or heat-transfer medium, such as transcritical cycles and related components.
[F24F 2110/72] Carbon monoxide -- The CPC entry F24F 2110/72 covers carbon monoxide in heating and air-conditioning equipment, including its generation, detection, control, removal and safety measures such as sensors, alarms and ventilation systems designed to protect occupants from harmful exposure. This falls under mechanical engineering, heating and air-conditioning technologies.
[F24F 2110/74] Ozone -- F24F 2110/74 covers air-conditioning or heating apparatus that utilizes ozone, for example ozone generators integrated into ventilation systems for disinfection or ozone-based refrigerants. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning.
[F24F 2110/76] Oxygen -- F24F 2110/76 is a CPC subclass covering the use of oxygen, such as oxygen enrichment or injection, within heating and air-conditioning systems. It applies to devices and methods that employ oxygen to improve combustion, heat transfer, or indoor air quality.
[F24F 2110/80] Electric charge -- F24F 2110/80 covers devices and methods that employ electric charge to generate or control heat in air-conditioning and heating systems. It includes electric heating elements, electrostatic heating, and related control technologies used in mechanical engineering for climate control.
[F24F 2120/00] Control inputs relating to users or occupants -- F24F2120/00 pertains to control inputs that come from users or occupants in heating or air-conditioning equipment, such as thermostats, switches, occupancy sensors or other interfaces that let people adjust temperature, airflow or related settings.
[F24F 2120/10] Occupancy -- The entry F24F 2120/10 covers control inputs that detect or respond to the presence of users or occupants in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems. It pertains to devices such as occupancy sensors, motion detectors or seat-presence switches that adjust climate parameters based on whether a space is occupied.
