This classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, and specifically the sub-category of user-related control inputs.
[F24F 2120/12] Position of occupants -- The subclass F24F 2120/12 covers heating, ventilating or air-conditioning control inputs that are based on the position of occupants within a space. It includes sensors, detection methods and control strategies that adjust climate parameters according to where people are seated, standing or moving, to optimise comfort and energy use.
[F24F 2120/14] Activity of occupants -- F24F 2120/14 covers control inputs that adjust heating or air-conditioning based on the activity of occupants, such as movement, presence, metabolic heat generation or behavioural patterns, to improve comfort and energy efficiency. It includes sensors, algorithms or interfaces that detect or interpret these activities and modify climate settings accordingly.
[F24F 2120/20] Feedback from users -- F24F 2120/20 pertains to air-conditioning systems that receive and use feedback from users or occupants to control operation, such as adjusting temperature, airflow or ventilation based on occupant commands, sensor data or physiological signals. It includes control interfaces, sensors and logic that modify heating, cooling or airflow in response to this user input.
[F24F 2130/00] Control inputs relating to environmental factors not covered by group -- The F24F 2130/00 subclass covers control inputs that adjust environmental parameters such as humidity, air quality, or temperature distribution when those factors are not specifically addressed by other groups within the air-conditioning domain. It includes sensors, user commands or automated signals that influence the operation of heating, ventilation or cooling systems to maintain desired indoor conditions.
[F24F 2130/10] Weather information or forecasts -- The CPC entry F24F 2130/10 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Air-conditioning and covers control inputs that use weather information or forecasts when such environmental factors are not addressed by other groups. It pertains to HVAC systems that adjust operation based on external weather data.
[F24F 2130/20] Sunlight -- In the CPC, code F24F 2130/20 falls under mechanical engineering → heating → air-conditioning and covers control inputs that adjust environmental conditions based on sunlight. It addresses systems that modulate ventilation, shading, or temperature in response to solar radiation, distinct from other environmental control groups.
[F24F 2130/30] Artificial light -- F24F 2130/30 covers control inputs that manage artificial lighting as part of heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems, where the lighting function influences the indoor environment but is not addressed by other groups. It includes sensors, switches or control algorithms that adjust artificial light levels to maintain comfort or energy efficiency.
[F24F 2130/40] Noise -- F24F 2130/40 pertains to control inputs that manage noise in air-conditioning systems, covering aspects not addressed by other groups. It is classified under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, specifically for environmental factor controls beyond temperature or humidity.
[F24F 2140/00] Control inputs relating to system states -- F24F 2140/00 covers control inputs that are based on the present state of an air-conditioning system, such as measured temperature, pressure, humidity or flow rate. It includes devices or methods that adjust heating, cooling or ventilation actions in response to those state variables to achieve the desired indoor conditions.
[F24F 2140/10] Pressure -- The entry F24F 2140/10 pertains to control inputs that monitor or regulate pressure within heating and air-conditioning systems. It covers devices or methods that use pressure signals to determine system states, such as activating thermostats, adjusting compressors, or managing flow in HVAC installations.
[F24F 2140/12] Heat-exchange fluid pressure
[F24F 2140/20] Heat-exchange fluid temperature -- This subclass covers control inputs that monitor or regulate the temperature of the fluid circulating in heat-exchangers for heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. It includes sensors, set-points, feedback loops and other devices that sense or command fluid temperature to achieve desired system states.
[F24F 2140/30] Condensation of water from cooled air -- The subclass covers methods and devices that control system parameters to cause moisture to condense from air that has been cooled, typically within heating, ventilation and air-conditioning or refrigeration units. It includes control inputs that adjust temperature, humidity, or airflow to manage the condensation process.
[F24F 2140/40] Damper positions, e.g. open or closed -- The classification F24F 2140/40 covers the control inputs that set the position of air-flow dampers in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems, such as commands to open, close or partially open the damper. It relates to how system states are managed through these damper-position signals.
[F24F 2140/50] Load -- F24F 2140/50 designates load-related control inputs that affect the operating state of heating, ventilation or air-conditioning equipment. It covers methods and devices for adjusting system performance based on measured or commanded loads, such as temperature, pressure or flow set-points. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating and air-conditioning, focusing on how input signals represent system states.
[F24F 2140/60] Energy consumption -- The entry covers control inputs that specify or regulate the amount of energy used by heating and air-conditioning equipment, linking system-state signals to the measurement, monitoring or management of the unit's power consumption.
[F24F 2203/00] Treatment of air -- The entry F24F 2203/00 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers technologies for treating air within heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. It includes methods and apparatuses for conditioning, filtering, humidifying, dehumidifying or otherwise improving the quality of air circulated in buildings or industrial environments.
[F24F 2203/02] System or Device comprising a heat pump as a subsystem, e.g. combined with humidification/dehumidification, heating, natural energy or with hybrid system -- The entry F24F 2203/02 covers HVAC systems that incorporate a heat pump as a component, often together with humidification or dehumidification, heating or cooling functions, and may use natural-energy sources or operate as part of a hybrid arrangement.
[F24F 2203/021] Compression cycle -- The classification F24F 2203/021 defines the compression stage of a refrigeration or heat-pump cycle, covering the design, operation and control of compressors and associated motor-driven mechanisms that raise the pressure of refrigerant. It includes features that affect the thermodynamic performance of the cycle and the integration of the compressor within air-conditioning and heating systems.
[F24F 2203/023] with turbine used for expansion -- The subclass F24F 2203/023 covers air-conditioning or heating installations in which a turbine is employed to expand the working fluid, usually to drive a compressor or fan and improve energy efficiency. It is part of the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Air-Conditioning → Treatment of air.
[F24F 2203/025] with turbine for compression -- The F24F 2203/025 classification covers air-conditioning or ventilation systems where a turbine-typically a centrifugal or axial-flow impeller-is used to compress the air. It applies to devices that generate a pressure rise for circulating or treating air in heating, cooling or ventilation installations. This includes compressors integrated into HVAC units for forced-air distribution.
[F24F 2203/026] Absorption - desorption cycle -- The entry covers air-conditioning systems that use an absorption-desorption cycle, where a refrigerant is absorbed by a liquid absorbent at low temperature and pressure, then released (desorbed) by heating to generate cooling or heating effect. It includes the components, working fluids, and thermodynamic steps involved in such cycles for treating air.
[F24F 2203/028] using a solid absorbing medium -- This CPC subclass concerns air-treatment systems that incorporate a solid absorbing material to capture moisture, pollutants or odors from airstreams. It includes designs in ventilation, heating, cooling or dehumidification where the solid sorbent is a primary means of conditioning the air.
[F24F 2203/10] Rotary wheel -- F24F 2203/10 covers rotary wheels used in air-treatment systems such as heat-exchange or dehumidification units. The wheel rotates to transfer heat or moisture between air streams, improving energy efficiency in heating, cooling or ventilation applications. It is a component of mechanical engineering devices for conditioning air.
[F24F 2203/1004] Bearings or driving means -- F24F 2203/1004 concerns bearings and driving mechanisms used in devices for treating air, such as fans, compressors and other rotating components of heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. It addresses the design, selection and integration of shafts, bearings, couplings and motor drives that ensure reliable motion and power transmission in air-conditioning equipment.
[F24F 2203/1008] comprising a by-pass channel -- This subclass pertains to air-treatment installations that incorporate a bypass channel for part of the airflow. The bypass may be used to route air around a heating, cooling or filtering element, enabling selective treatment or energy saving. It is applied to HVAC systems where control of the air path is required.
[F24F 2203/1012] Details of the casing or cover -- The subclass F24F 2203/1012 covers design aspects of the enclosure or protective cover of air-treatment apparatus such as heating, ventilation or air-conditioning units. It includes features of the casing material, shape, sealing, mounting and integration that protect internal components and influence airflow or system performance.
[F24F 2203/1016] combined with another type of cooling principle, e.g. compression cycle -- This CPC subclass covers air-treatment installations where a cooling method such as a compression cycle is employed together with another cooling principle. It relates to hybrid HVAC systems that combine, for example, a refrigerant-compression cycle with evaporative, absorption or other secondary cooling techniques.
[F24F 2203/102] combined with a heat pipe -- This subclass covers air-treatment devices that incorporate a heat pipe as part of the heating or cooling system. It falls under mechanical engineering → heating → air-conditioning → treatment of air and pertains to technologies that move, condition, or regulate air while using a heat pipe for efficient heat transfer.
[F24F 2203/1024] combined with a humidifier -- This subclass covers air-conditioning devices that incorporate a humidifier, providing combined heating, cooling or air-treatment together with moisture addition. It pertains to systems that treat air while simultaneously controlling humidity.
[F24F 2203/1028] combined with a spraying device -- The subclass F24F 2203/1028 covers air-conditioning or ventilation systems that include a spraying device for humidifying, disinfecting or otherwise treating the air. It relates to the integration of nozzles, atomizers or mist generators within the climate-control unit to disperse liquids into the airflow.
[F24F 2203/1032] Desiccant wheel -- A desiccant wheel is a rotating heat-exchange element coated with moisture-absorbing material that dehumidifies air in HVAC systems. Air flowing through one sector of the wheel releases moisture onto the desiccant, while the opposite sector is heated to regenerate the material by driving the captured water out.
[F24F 2203/1036] Details -- This subclass concerns the specific details of air-conditioning systems that treat air, covering particular configurations, control mechanisms, component arrangements and related design features.
[F24F 2203/104] Heat exchanger wheel -- F24F 2203/104 covers heat-exchanger wheels used in air-conditioning systems to transfer thermal energy between air streams. These rotating or stationary matrix devices facilitate sensible or latent heat exchange, improving energy efficiency in heating, cooling or ventilating equipment.
[F24F 2203/1044] performing other movements, e.g. sliding -- The classification covers air-conditioning or heating devices that create airflow by mechanisms other than rotating fans, such as sliding or reciprocating plates, vanes or ducts that move air through linear motion. It includes systems where the airflow is generated or controlled by sliding elements to distribute conditioned air within a space. This is used for specialized ventilation, drafts or directed air flow in mechanical heating and cooling installations.
[F24F 2203/1048] Geometric details -- This subclass covers the shape, dimensions and arrangement of components used in air-conditioning and heating equipment, such as ducts, vents, diffusers and other elements that affect airflow. It includes specifications of cross-section, curvature, spacing and other geometric parameters that influence performance.
[F24F 2203/1052] comprising a non-axial air flow -- The subclass F24F 2203/1052 covers heating or air-conditioning devices in which the air moves along a non-axial path, such as radial, tangential or cross-flow configurations. It includes fans, diffusers, heat exchangers or ventilation elements that direct airflow sideways or circumferentially rather than straight through the system.
[F24F 2203/1056] comprising a reheater -- The CPC subclass F24F 2203/1056 falls under mechanical engineering → heating → air-conditioning → treatment of air and covers air-conditioning systems that incorporate a reheater, i.e., a device that raises the temperature of the circulated air after it has been cooled or de-humidified, typically to improve comfort, prevent condensation or meet specific temperature set-points.
[F24F 2203/106] Electrical reheater -- F24F 2203/106 designates electrical reheaters employed in air-treatment systems, such as air-conditioning or heating installations. It covers devices that raise the temperature of circulated air by means of electric heating elements, including resistive coils, plates or panels, and related control or safety components integrated into the air-handling equipment.
[F24F 2203/1064] Gas fired reheater -- F24F 2203/1064 covers gas-fired reheaters that raise the temperature of air after it has been cooled or de-humidified in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems. The classification pertains to devices that use combustion of gaseous fuels to add heat to the processed air before distribution.
[F24F 2203/1068] comprising one rotor -- The entry F24F 2203/1068 covers air-conditioning or air-treatment devices that incorporate a single rotor, such as a lone fan or rotary heat-exchange element, to move or condition the airflow. It pertains to mechanical engineering solutions for heating and cooling where the rotating component alone performs the primary air-treatment function.
[F24F 2203/1072] comprising two rotors -- F24F 2203/1072 covers air-treatment apparatus that includes two rotors, such as dual-rotor fans, blowers or compressors, where the rotors work together to move or condition air. The classification is used for devices that employ this dual-rotor configuration within heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems.
[F24F 2203/1076] comprising three rotors -- F24F 2203/1076 designates air-treatment units that incorporate three rotors, such as multi-rotor fans or rotary compressors used in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems. The three-rotor configuration is intended to improve airflow or pressure generation within the device.
[F24F 2203/108] comprising rotor parts shaped in sector form -- F24F 2203/108 relates to air-conditioning or heating devices in which the rotor's blades or vanes are formed as sector-shaped sections, a design used to move or treat air more efficiently within the system. This subclass falls under treatment of air in mechanical engineering, specifically heating and air-conditioning technologies.
[F24F 2203/1084] comprising two flow rotor segments -- The CPC subclass F24F 2203/1084 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Air-conditioning → Treatment of air and designates air-conditioning systems that incorporate two flow rotor segments to direct and circulate air within the device. This arrangement is used to improve airflow control and efficiency in heating or cooling applications.
[F24F 2203/1088] comprising three flow rotor segments -- F24F 2203/1088 refers to air-conditioning or heating devices that use a rotor divided into three flow segments, each segment directing airflow through the system. This arrangement is applied in mechanical engineering for treating air, such as distributing conditioned air more evenly within a space.
[F24F 2203/1092] comprising four flow rotor segments -- The CPC subclass F24F 2203/1092 is part of mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, and treatment of air, and it pertains to air-conditioning devices that incorporate four flow rotor segments in their airflow or heat-exchange system.
[F24F 2203/1096] comprising sealing means -- F24F 2203/1096 classifies air-treatment apparatus that incorporates sealing means to prevent undesired air leakage, covering designs such as ducts, vents, and components of heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems where seals are integral to performance.
[F24F 2203/12] Dehumidifying or humidifying belt type -- The entry F24F 2203/12 covers belt-type devices that add moisture to or remove moisture from a moving airstream, typically using porous or wetted belts that pass through the air stream to effect humidification or dehumidification. It is applied to air-conditioning systems where moisture control is achieved by contact between the belt surface and the air.
[F24F 2221/00] Air-conditioning -- F24F 2221/00 covers inventions relating to air-conditioning, the control of temperature, humidity and air quality in enclosed spaces. It includes systems that cool, heat, dehumidify or ventilate using refrigerant cycles, heat-pump technology, chilled-water loops and associated control mechanisms.
[F24F 2221/02] combined with lighting fixtures -- The code covers air-conditioning units that incorporate lighting fixtures, such as ceiling diffusers with built-in lights, providing both temperature control and illumination in a single assembly. It is used to classify integrated HVAC-lighting solutions in patents.
[F24F 2221/08] Installation or apparatus for use in sport halls, e.g. swimming pools, ice rings -- The code F24F 2221/08 covers air-conditioning installations or equipment specifically designed for sport facilities such as swimming pools, ice rinks and other large halls. It includes heating, cooling and ventilation systems adapted to the unique size, humidity and temperature requirements of these venues.
[F24F 2221/10] combined with, or integrated in, furniture -- This subcategory refers to air-conditioning units that are built into or attached to furniture, such as chairs, tables, or cabinets, providing localized cooling or heating within the piece. It includes integrated fans, vents, or refrigerant components that form part of the furniture structure.
[F24F 2221/12] transportable -- The CPC subclass F24F2221/12 covers transportable air-conditioning equipment, meaning self-contained cooling systems that can be moved or installed temporarily, often mounted on vehicles, trailers or other mobile platforms. It includes units designed to provide conditioned air without permanent installation.
[F24F 2221/125] mounted on wheels -- This subgroup covers air-conditioning units that are designed to be mobile, i.e., installed on a wheeled chassis or frame, allowing the system to be moved easily for deployment or maintenance. It includes portable or trailer-mounted cooling equipment used in buildings, vehicles or temporary sites.
[F24F 2221/14] mounted on the ceiling -- The code F24F 2221/14 designates air-conditioning equipment that is installed on the ceiling, such as ceiling-mounted indoor units of split systems. It covers devices whose primary mounting location is the ceiling and which deliver conditioned air into a space from that position. This classification is used for mechanical-engineering patents related to ceiling-mounted cooling, heating or ventilation units.
[F24F 2221/16] mounted on the roof -- This subclass relates to roof-mounted air-conditioning equipment, such as rooftop condensers, fans or split-type units installed on the top surface of a building. It covers arrangements where the whole system or major components are fixed on the roof for space-saving or distribution reasons.
[F24F 2221/17] mounted in a wall -- The code designates air-conditioning equipment that is installed directly in a wall, such as a wall-mounted indoor unit of a split system, which delivers conditioned air into a space.
[F24F 2221/18] combined with domestic apparatus -- F24F 2221/18 covers air-conditioning systems that are integrated with or incorporated into domestic appliances, such as refrigerators, washing machines, or ovens, where the cooling or heating function serves both the appliance and the surrounding space. It addresses designs that combine climate control with the primary function of household equipment.
[F24F 2221/183] combined with a hot-water boiler -- This classification covers air-conditioning installations that are integrated with a hot-water boiler, where the same system provides both cooling and domestic or space heating using the boiler's hot water. It applies to combined HVAC units that share a boiler for heat recovery or simultaneous heating and cooling.
[F24F 2221/186] combined with a fireplace -- A subclass for air-conditioning and heating installations that incorporate a fireplace, covering systems where the fireplace provides supplemental heat or is integrated with the air-distribution network to warm or condition indoor spaces.
[F24F 2221/20] mounted in or close to a window -- The classification F24F 2221/20 refers to air-conditioning devices that are installed in or directly adjacent to a window. It covers window-mounted or window-side units designed to cool or heat a room by using the window opening for mounting, ventilation or discharge.
[F24F 2221/22] Cleaning ducts or apparatus -- The subgroup F24F 2221/22 defines cleaning of air-conditioning ducts, ventilation or heat-exchange passages and the equipment used for that purpose. It includes methods such as mechanical brushing, high-pressure air or fluid jets, solvent application and related apparatus designed to remove dust, debris or contaminants from duct surfaces.
[F24F 2221/225] using a liquid -- This subclass covers air-conditioning equipment that uses a liquid as the working medium for heating or cooling, including systems where water, glycol, refrigerant or similar fluids are circulated through heat exchangers, piped, sprayed or sprayed onto surfaces, and where flow and temperature control are integral to the unit's operation.
[F24F 2221/26] improving the aesthetic appearance -- F24F 2221/26 covers air-conditioning equipment whose design is intended to enhance visual appeal, including shape, colour, surface finish and integration with interior décor. It applies to decorative features of fans, diffusers, vents or whole units that improve aesthetic appearance without altering the core functional performance.
[F24F 2221/28] using the Coanda effect -- This subclass covers air-conditioning devices that employ the Coanda effect, whereby a high-velocity air jet adheres to a nearby surface and entrains surrounding air to enhance distribution, mixing, or cooling within a space. It includes designs that use this fluid-dynamic principle to direct conditioned airflow without mechanical fans.
[F24F 2221/30] comprising fireproof material -- F24F 2221/30 designates air-conditioning units that incorporate fire-proof material, covering components or whole systems built to resist fire damage. It falls within the Cooperative Patent Classification hierarchy of mechanical engineering, heating, and air-conditioning.
[F24F 2221/32] preventing human errors during the installation, use or maintenance, e.g. goofy proof -- This subclass covers measures that reduce or eliminate operator mistakes when installing, operating or servicing air-conditioning equipment, such as design features that make incorrect assembly impossible or provide clear guidance. It includes error-proofing, fool-proof and self-checking mechanisms aimed at improving safety and reliability.
[F24F 2221/34] Heater, e.g. gas burner, electric air heater -- The subclass F24F 2221/34 covers devices that heat air, including gas burners and electric air heaters, intended for use in HVAC or ventilation systems. It pertains to arrangements that raise the temperature of an airstream before it is delivered to a space.
[F24F 2221/36] Modules, e.g. for an easy mounting or transport -- The CPC code F24F 2221/36 refers to modular air-conditioning components that are designed for simple mounting or transport, such as pre-assembled units, plug-in modules, transportable packs and mounting accessories that facilitate quick installation or relocation of heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems.
[F24F 2221/38] Personalised air distribution -- The CPC subclass F24F 2221/38 covers air-conditioning systems that provide personalised air distribution, such as individual nozzles, vents or diffusers that deliver conditioned air directly to specific occupants or zones, allowing separate temperature or airflow control.
[F24F 2221/40] HVAC with raised floors -- F24F 2221/40 covers heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning systems that incorporate raised floor installations, where the floor acts as a distribution duct for conditioned air. The classification applies to designs that deliver air through perforated or vented floor panels in commercial or industrial spaces.
[F24F 2221/42] Mobile autonomous air conditioner, e.g. robots -- A mobile autonomous air-conditioner is a self-propelled or robot-mounted system that provides cooling (or heating) while independently navigating its environment. It combines climate-control components with on-board power and control to operate without fixed installation.
[F24F 2221/44] Protection from terrorism or theft -- The subclass relates to air-conditioning installations that incorporate features to deter or limit terrorism or theft, such as reinforced enclosures, access control, intrusion detection, or fire- and blast-resistant components.
[F24F 2221/46] Air flow forming a vortex -- The CPC subclass F24F 2221/46 covers air-conditioning systems in which the supplied air is made to swirl, forming a vortex that enhances mixing, heat transfer or distribution within a room or duct. It includes devices such as vortex generators, swirl diffusers and related arrangements that deliberately create rotational airflow for improved climate control.
[F24F 2221/48] HVAC for a wine cellar -- F24F 2221/48 covers heating, ventilation and air-conditioning installations specifically designed for wine cellars, addressing temperature and humidity control to preserve wine quality. It falls under the mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning hierarchy.
[F24F 2221/50] HVAC for high buildings, e.g. thermal or pressure differences -- The CPC subclass F24F 2221/50 covers heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems intended for high-rise buildings, focusing on methods that exploit or counteract thermal or pressure differences caused by building height. It includes designs that manage stack effect, pressure balance, and vertical air distribution to provide comfortable indoor climates.
[F24F 2221/52] Weather protecting means, e.g. against wind, rain or snow -- This CPC subclass F24F 2221/52 covers devices incorporated in air-conditioning systems that protect the interior environment from external weather elements such as wind, rain, or snow. It relates to mechanical-engineering solutions for heating and climate control that include shielding components.
[F24F 2221/54] Heating and cooling, simultaneously or alternatively -- This CPC subclass deals with devices or methods that provide heating and cooling at the same time or allow them to be selected alternatively, typically in air-conditioning systems. It covers integrated temperature-control units that combine a heating element and a cooling cycle in a single apparatus.
[F24F 2221/56] Cooling being a secondary aspect -- F24F 2221/56 covers air-conditioning systems where cooling is provided only as an ancillary function rather than the primary purpose. It applies to devices whose main role is heating, ventilation or other services, with cooling incorporated as a secondary aspect.
[F24F 3/00] Air-conditioning systems in which conditioned primary air is supplied from one or more central stations to distributing units in the rooms or spaces where it may receive secondary treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air-conditioning systems in which conditioned primary air is supplied from one or more central stations to distributing units in the rooms or spaces where it may receive secondary treatment; Apparatus specially designed for such systems.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Room units -> F24F1/00
[F24F 3/001] in which the air treatment in the central station takes place by means of a heat-pump or by means of a reversible cycle  reversible cycle for humidifying and drying air  F24F3/147 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Reversible cycle for humidifying and drying air -> F24F3/147
[F24F 3/02] characterised by the pressure or velocity of the primary air -- This subclass refers to air-conditioning systems that provide conditioned primary air from one or more central stations to room-side distributors and are distinguished by the pressure or velocity of the primary air supplied.
[F24F 3/04] operating with high pressure or high velocity -- F24F 3/04 relates to air-conditioning systems that convey centrally generated primary air to room-side units and use high-pressure or high-velocity flow in the ductwork to distribute the air efficiently before any secondary treatment occurs.
[F24F 3/044] Systems in which all treatment is given in the central station, i.e. all-air systems -- All-air air-conditioning systems in which the primary air is fully conditioned-heated, cooled, humidified or dehumidified-at a central station and then distributed to rooms or spaces without any secondary treatment at the local units. This arrangement relies on a single central plant to provide the complete air-treatment process.
[F24F 3/0442] with volume control at a constant temperature -- F24F 3/0442 covers air-conditioning installations that keep the supplied air at a set temperature while varying the airflow volume to each space. The control adjusts fan speed or damper opening so the temperature remains constant despite changes in room demand, fitting central-station systems that may add secondary treatment on-site.
[F24F 3/0444] in which two airstreams are conducted from the central station via independent conduits to the space to be treated, of which one has a constant volume and a season-adapted temperature, while the other one is always cold and varies in volume -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems in which two airstreams are conducted from the central station via independent conduits to the space to be treated, of which one has a constant volume and a season adapted temperature to compensate for the fluctuating heat transfer losses of the building, while the other varies in volume and is always cold in order to compensate for the interior fluctuations and variable solar heating effects. These so-called "Dual Conduit Systems" are similar to high-pressure air-water systems.
[F24F 3/048] with temperature control at constant rate of air-flow -- Air-conditioning systems that keep the airflow rate unchanged while adjusting the temperature of the conditioned air. The control maintains a constant volume of primary air supplied from central stations, allowing temperature changes without varying the flow speed.
[F24F 3/052] Multiple duct systems, e.g. systems in which hot and cold air are supplied by separate circuits from the central station to mixing chambers in the spaces to be conditioned -- F24F 3/052 refers to multiple-duct air-conditioning systems in which hot and cold air are supplied from a central station through separate circuits to mixing chambers in the rooms or spaces to be conditioned, allowing secondary treatment of the air before distribution.
[F24F 3/0522] in which warm or cold air from the central station is delivered via individual pipes to mixing chambers in the space to be treated, the cold air/warm air ratio being controlled by a thermostat in the space concerned, i.e. so-called Dual-duct System -- The entry covers air-conditioning systems that supply warm and cold air from a central station through separate distribution pipes to individual mixing chambers located in each treated space. The temperature is regulated by a thermostat that adjusts the warm-to-cold air ratio within the chamber, constituting a dual-duct system.
[F24F 3/0525] in which the air treated in the central station is reheated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reheating may take place near the central station upon arrival, in the space to be treated, in a branch pipe to zone in a multi-zone system or in the warm pipe in a system having separate supply conduits for warm and cold air.
[F24F 3/0527] in which treated air having differing temperatures is conducted through independent conduits from the central station to various spaces to be treated, i.e. so-called "multi-Zone" systems  F24F3/0525  takes precedence -- This classification covers air-conditioning systems that deliver conditioned primary air from a central plant to separate distribution units, each receiving air at a different temperature through its own ductwork. Such multi-zone installations allow independent temperature control for individual rooms.
[F24F 3/056] the air at least partially flowing over lighting fixtures, the heat of which is dissipated or used -- F24F 3/056 relates to air-conditioning systems that route conditioned air over lighting fixtures, allowing the fixtures' heat to be dissipated into the airstream or to be harvested for secondary heating purposes. It applies to central air distribution where such combined air-lighting treatment occurs.
[F24F 3/06] characterised by the arrangements for the supply of heat-exchange fluid for the subsequent treatment of primary air in the room units
[F24F 3/065] with a plurality of evaporators or condensers -- F24F 3/065 covers air-conditioning installations that deliver primary conditioned air from one or more central plant units to room-side devices and incorporate several evaporators or several condensers within the system. The classification applies to multi-evaporator or multi-condenser configurations used for secondary treatment of the supplied air.
[F24F 3/08] with separate supply and return lines for hot and cold heat-exchange fluids -- A system in which the central air-conditioning plant delivers conditioned primary air to rooms via separate supply and return ducts for hot and cold heat-exchange fluids, allowing independent circulation of heating and cooling media throughout the building. This arrangement keeps the hot and cold fluid circuits distinct, improving temperature control and energy efficiency.
[F24F 3/10] with separate supply lines and common return line for hot and cold heat-exchange fluids -- This subclass covers air-conditioning installations where the primary conditioned air is delivered from central plants to room-side units via distinct supply lines for heating and cooling fluids, while the return flow of both fluids is combined in a single pipe. It applies to systems that use separate hot- and cold-fluid supply lines but share a common return line.
[F24F 3/12] characterised by the treatment of the air otherwise than by heating and cooling -- F24F 3/12 covers air-conditioning systems that deliver primary air from one or more central stations to room-side units which then perform secondary processes other than heating or cooling, such as humidification, dehumidification, ventilation, filtration or odor removal. The subclass distinguishes these installations from those whose sole function is temperature control.
[F24F 3/14] by humidification -- This subgroup covers air-conditioning systems where, after primary air is delivered from a central plant to local units, moisture is added to the air. It includes methods and devices for humidifying the distributed air as a secondary treatment within the space.
[F24F 3/1405] in which the humidity of the air is exclusively affected by contact with the evaporator of a closed-circuit cooling system or heat pump circuit -- This subclass covers air-conditioning systems that deliver conditioned primary air from central stations to room units, where the only way the air's humidity is altered is through contact with the evaporator of a closed-circuit cooling system or heat-pump circuit.
[F24F 3/1411] by absorbing or adsorbing water, e.g. using an hygroscopic desiccant -- This subclass deals with air-conditioning systems that perform secondary treatment by removing moisture from the primary air supplied from central stations using water-absorbing or adsorbing materials such as hygroscopic desiccants. The method is applied in the room-side units to condition the air further.
[F24F 3/1417] with liquid hygroscopic desiccants -- This subclass covers central air-conditioning installations that incorporate liquid hygroscopic desiccants to remove moisture from the primary air stream. The desiccant is circulated as a liquid, allowing regeneration and reuse while the conditioned air is distributed to room-side units.
[F24F 3/1423] with a moving bed of solid desiccants, e.g. a rotary wheel supporting solid desiccants -- This subclass covers air-conditioning installations that use a moving bed of solid desiccant material, such as a rotary wheel, to dehumidify or condition the primary air as it passes through the system. The desiccant bed travels continuously, allowing regeneration and reuse of the sorbent while maintaining a central supply of conditioned air to room units.
[F24F 3/1429] alternatively operating a heat exchanger in an absorbing/adsorbing mode and a heat exchanger in a regeneration mode -- F24F 3/1429 relates to air-conditioning systems that use two heat exchangers, one operating in an absorbing or adsorbing mode while the other runs in a regeneration mode, with the modes switched alternately to provide cooling or heating.
[F24F 3/147] with both heat and humidity transfer between supplied and exhausted air -- The F24F 3/147 subclass covers air-conditioning systems that transfer both heat and humidity between the supplied primary air and the exhausted air, typically in setups where conditioned air is delivered from a central plant to room units and may undergo secondary treatment through this heat-and-moisture exchange.
[F24F 3/153] with subsequent heating, i.e. with the air, given the required humidity in the central station, passing a heating element to achieve the required temperature -- This subgroup covers air-conditioning installations that first adjust humidity of the primary air at a central station and then pass the humidified stream through a heating element to raise it to the required temperature before distribution to rooms.
[F24F 3/16] by purification, e.g. by filtering -- F24F 3/16 covers air-conditioning systems that distribute primary air from one or more central stations to room units and then apply secondary treatment, specifically purification methods such as filtering, to clean the air before it reaches the occupied space.
[F24F 3/163] Clean air work stations, i.e. selected areas within a space which filtered air is passed -- This subclass defines air-conditioning systems that deliver filtered primary air from one or more central stations to specific work-stations or zones within a space, where the air may receive additional local treatment. It covers arrangements that create clean-air work areas by routing centrally supplied conditioned air directly to selected locations.
[F24F 3/167] Clean rooms, i.e. enclosed spaces in which a uniform flow of filtered air is distributed -- F24F 3/167 covers clean rooms, which are enclosed spaces where filtered air is supplied uniformly to maintain controlled contamination levels. The classification relates to air-conditioning systems that deliver conditioned primary air from central stations to these spaces.
[F24F 5/00] Air-conditioning systems or apparatus not covered by -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F24F 5/0003] Exclusively-fluid systems -- F24F 5/0003 refers to air-conditioning installations that operate solely with a fluid medium, such as water, steam, or refrigerant, and do not employ additional mechanical elements like fans or blowers, covering systems that provide cooling or heating through the circulation of the fluid alone.
[F24F 5/0007] cooling apparatus specially adapted for use in air-conditioning  F24F5/0046  takes precedence -- F24F 5/0007 designates cooling apparatus specially adapted for use in air-conditioning, covering devices such as chillers, condensers or evaporators that are integrated into HVAC systems but not otherwise classified. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, air-conditioning systems or apparatus not covered by other groups. The more specific subclass F24F 5/0046 has precedence over this entry.
[F24F 5/001] Compression cycle type -- F24F 5/001 pertains to air-conditioning systems that use a compression refrigeration cycle, where a refrigerant is compressed, condensed, expanded and evaporated to provide cooling or heating. The code covers machines such as chillers, heat pumps and split-unit air conditioners that employ this cycle and are not classified elsewhere.
[F24F 5/0014] using absorption or desorption -- This subcategory deals with air-conditioning systems that use absorption or desorption cycles to provide cooling or heating, typically employing a refrigerant-absorbent pair driven by a heat source such as waste heat or solar energy.
[F24F 5/0017] using cold storage bodies, e.g. ice -- F24F 5/0017 covers air-conditioning systems that employ cold-storage bodies such as ice or frozen water to provide cooling. The entry includes apparatus that uses these cold reservoirs as heat sinks for regulating indoor temperature, rather than conventional mechanical refrigeration cycles. It pertains to heating-related mechanical engineering applications.
[F24F 5/0021] using phase change material [PCM] for storage -- This subgroup covers air-conditioning systems or apparatus that incorporate phase-change material for thermal energy storage, allowing heat to be stored or released during operation. It applies to designs that use PCM-based storage not addressed by other more specific entries.
[F24F 5/0035] using evaporation -- F24F 5/0035 refers to air-conditioning systems that achieve cooling or humidity control by using evaporation, where a liquid such as water evaporates to absorb heat from the surrounding air.
[F24F 5/0042] characterised by the application of thermo-electric units or the Peltier effect -- This subclass covers air-conditioning systems that employ thermoelectric modules or the Peltier effect to provide cooling or heating, replacing traditional compressors and refrigerants. It includes designs where solid-state heat-pump elements are integrated for temperature regulation.
[F24F 5/0046] using natural energy, e.g. solar energy, energy from the ground -- F24F 5/0046 covers air-conditioning installations that obtain their energy from natural sources such as sunlight or the earth's heat, using solar collectors, geothermal heat pumps or similar technologies to provide cooling or heating.
[F24F 5/005] using energy from the ground by air circulation, e.g. "Canadian well" -- This subclass covers air-conditioning systems that obtain thermal energy from the ground by circulating air through underground ducts or wells, exemplified by the Canadian well method. It pertains to heating or cooling arrangements that use geothermal air flow rather than conventional ground-heat exchangers.
[F24F 5/0071] adapted for use in covered swimming pools -- Air-conditioning systems specially designed or modified for indoor swimming pools, focusing on temperature regulation, humidity control and resistance to moisture-induced corrosion. The entry covers installations adapted to the unique environmental conditions of covered pool facilities.
[F24F 5/0075] Systems using thermal walls, e.g. double window -- The F24F 5/0075 subclass covers air-conditioning and heating installations that incorporate thermal walls to reduce energy loss, such as double-glazed windows or other insulated panel structures. These systems use a separating barrier with low thermal conductivity to limit heat transfer between interior and exterior environments.
[F24F 5/0085] Systems using a compressed air circuit -- F24F 5/0085 covers air-conditioning systems that use a compressed-air circuit as the primary means of heat transfer, employing pressurised air rather than conventional refrigerants to provide cooling or heating. It includes arrangements where air is compressed, stored and expanded through heat exchangers to condition indoor spaces.
[F24F 5/0089] Systems using radiation from walls or panels -- F24F 5/0089 refers to air-conditioning systems that achieve heating or cooling by radiating energy from surfaces such as walls or panels instead of using convective airflow. The classification includes radiant panels, heated or cooled walls, and similar apparatus that exchange heat directly with occupants and objects in a space.
[F24F 5/0092] ceilings, e.g. cool ceilings -- The CPC subclass F24F 5/0092 covers air-conditioning devices integrated into building ceilings, such as cool ceilings that supply chilled air or provide radiant cooling from the ceiling surface. It falls under mechanical engineering heating, specifically air-conditioning systems not classified elsewhere.
[F24F 5/0096] combined with domestic apparatus -- This CPC subgroup covers air-conditioning systems that are integrated with domestic equipment, such as kitchen appliances, water heaters or other household devices, where the climate-control function shares components or is combined with the primary domestic function. It is applied to patents describing combined HVAC and residential-appliance solutions.
[F24F 6/00] Air-humidification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Local unit for room humidification by evaporation of water in the air, or by water dispersion or by injection of steam in the air.
[F24F 6/02] by evaporation of water in the air -- This subclass covers air-humidification systems that add moisture by evaporating water directly into the airstream, including evaporative humidifiers, spray nozzles, wetted-media devices and similar mechanisms that increase humidity by converting liquid water to vapor as the air passes through or over the water source.
[F24F 6/025] using electrical heating means  F24F6/105  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F24F 6/025 covers air-humidification techniques that employ electrical heating means to generate moisture, such as resistive heating of water sprays or steam generators powered by electricity. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning and air-humidification, and is superseded by the broader subclass F24F 6/105.
[F24F 6/04] using stationary unheated wet elements -- This group covers humidification devices that employ stationary wet materials such as pads, filters, or porous plates that are not heated, relying on the moisture from the wet element and the airflow to add moisture. It includes systems where the wet element remains at ambient temperature and humidification occurs by evaporation.
[F24F 6/043] with self-sucking action, e.g. wicks -- This subgroup includes humidifiers that generate moisture by a self-sucking or capillary action, such as wick-based systems that draw water up through a porous material without external pumps. The design is typically used in small or passive air-conditioning units to add humidity to the airflow.
[F24F 6/06] using moving unheated wet elements -- F24F 6/06 pertains to air-humidification techniques that use moving unheated wet elements to add moisture to airflow. It describes systems where a rotating, oscillating or otherwise mobile wet surface evaporates water as air passes over it, providing humidification without the application of external heat, often improving energy efficiency in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning installations.
[F24F 6/08] using heated wet elements -- F24F 6/08 refers to air-humidification techniques that employ heated wet elements such as steam generators, heated water sprays or evaporative pads to add moisture to airstreams, typically used in climate control or ventilation systems.
[F24F 6/10] heated electrically -- F24F 6/10 covers devices that add heat to an air-humidification system using electrical energy. It includes electrically heated humidifiers, steam generators and other components where electric elements raise the temperature of the moisture to improve humidification performance. This subclass falls under air-conditioning within the heating section of mechanical engineering.
[F24F 6/105] using the heat of lamps -- F24F 6/105 pertains to air-humidification techniques that exploit the heat generated by lamps to evaporate water, thereby adding moisture to the air in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems. This approach is used where a lamp source is already present, such as in lighting fixtures, to improve energy efficiency and simplify humidifier integration.
[F24F 6/12] by forming water dispersions in the air -- This subclass covers humidification techniques that generate a dispersion of water particles in the surrounding air, such as atomization, ultrasonic nebulization, or spray nozzles, to increase humidity. It includes devices that form fine water droplets or mist and deliver them into the air for climate control.
[F24F 6/14] using nozzles -- F24F 6/14 covers air-humidification systems that use nozzles to atomize or disperse water into the airflow, a technique employed in heating, ventilation and air-conditioning equipment to control indoor humidity.
[F24F 6/16] using rotating elements -- The F24F 6/16 subclass covers air-humidification methods that employ rotating elements such as disks, drums, rollers or impellers to disperse moisture into the airstream. It includes mechanisms where the motion of the rotating part atomizes, sprays or spreads water, often in conjunction with fans or ducts, to increase relative humidity in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems.
[F24F 6/18] by injection of steam into the air -- F24F 6/18 covers devices and methods that humidify indoor air by injecting steam directly into the air stream. It includes systems that generate, transport, and disperse steam to increase moisture content for comfort, health, or process needs, and falls under the air-conditioning branch of mechanical engineering heating.
[F24F 7/00] Ventilation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems or apparatus for supplying air to rooms or exhausting air from rooms or circulating air within the rooms, e.g. by means of wall-ducts or systems using window or roof apertures.  **Glossary:** - Natural circulation: Air circulation without active fan, e.g. chimney effect, using solar energy or using wind energy
[F24F 7/003] in combination with air cleaning -- The CPC subclass F24F 7/003 designates ventilation arrangements that are combined with air-cleaning features, such as filters, ionizers or other purification elements built into the airflow path, and covers devices that supply or exhaust air while simultaneously removing contaminants.
[F24F 7/007] with forced flow -- F24F 7/007 pertains to ventilation arrangements that use mechanically generated airflow, such as fans, blowers, or other driven devices, to move air through a space or ductwork for heating, cooling, or air-quality purposes. The classification includes systems where the flow is actively induced rather than relying on natural convection.
[F24F 7/013] using wall or window fans, displacing air through the wall or window -- The F24F 7/013 subclass covers ventilation systems that employ wall-mounted or window-mounted fans to transport air through the building envelope. It includes designs where the fan displaces indoor air to the outside or draws external air in, facilitating passive or assisted airflow for heating, cooling or air quality control.
[F24F 7/02] Roof ventilation -- F24F 7/02 pertains to roof ventilation systems used to introduce or exhaust air through building roofs. It includes designs of vents, hatches, louvers and associated ducts that control airflow for temperature regulation, moisture removal or pollutant evacuation. The code also covers related sealing and weatherproofing features.
[F24F 7/025] with forced air circulation by means of a built-in ventilator -- F24F 7/025 covers air-conditioning systems that circulate air by means of an integral fan or built-in ventilator. The subclass includes devices where forced air flow is generated internally to distribute conditioned air throughout a space.
[F24F 7/04] with ducting systems -- F24F 7/04 designates ventilation equipment that incorporates integrated ducting systems for conveying air from the source to distribution points, covering fans, blowers and related components where ducts form an essential part of the device.
[F24F 7/06] with forced air circulation, e.g. by fan -- This subclass deals with heating or cooling apparatus that distributes air by means of a mechanically driven fan or blower, providing forced-air circulation for temperature control. It includes systems where air is propelled by a fan to deliver conditioned air throughout a space.
[F24F 7/065] fan combined with single duct; mounting arrangements of a fan in a duct -- The subclass addresses fans built into a single duct and the specific mounting configurations for securing the fan within that duct, typically used in ventilation and air-conditioning applications. It includes designs where the fan and duct form a combined unit.
[F24F 7/08] with separate ducts for supplied and exhausted air -- This subclass covers ventilation installations that employ distinct ducts for delivering fresh air and removing exhaust air, keeping supply and outflow pathways separate. It applies to mechanical-engineering heating and air-conditioning designs that isolate intake and exhaust to improve indoor air quality and system efficiency.
[F24F 7/10] with air supply, or exhaust, through perforated wall, floor or ceiling -- F24F 7/10 classifies ventilation systems that deliver fresh air or remove stale air by means of perforated structural elements such as walls, floors or ceilings. The code covers designs where the perforations themselves serve as the air-supply or exhaust paths, integrating the ducting directly into the building fabric. It is used for mechanical engineering solutions that combine heating, cooling and airflow distribution without separate ducts.
[F24F 8/00] Treatment, e.g. purification, of air supplied to human living or working spaces otherwise than by heating, cooling, humidifying or drying -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  Air purifier  Air purifier device with coupling mechanism  **Glossary:** - Self-contained air purifier: Apparatus with all its air purifying elements, e.g. filters or fans, arranged in a common casing.
[F24F 8/10] by separation, e.g. by filtering -- This subgroup covers air-treatment systems that improve indoor air quality by separating contaminants, typically using filters or similar media, without providing heating, cooling, humidifying or drying. It includes designs for ducts, filter housings and related components that capture particles, gases or odors.
[F24F 8/108] using dry filter elements -- This subgroup covers air-treatment installations that employ dry filter elements to remove particulates, dust or other contaminants from the air supplied to human living or working spaces in heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems.
[F24F 8/117] using wet filtering -- The F24F 8/117 subclass covers air-conditioning apparatus that treats supplied air by means of wet filtering, using moisture to capture particles, contaminants or odors. It applies to ventilation or HVAC systems where air purification is performed without heating, cooling, humidifying or drying the airflow.
[F24F 8/125] using wet filter elements -- F24F 8/125 covers the use of wet filter elements in air-conditioning or ventilation systems to treat supplied air, such as by removing contaminants, odors or pollutants, without providing heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/133] by direct contact with liquid, e.g. with sprayed liquid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removal of particles from the treated air by direct contact with a liquid, e.g. by spraying liquid into a stream of air or bubbling gas through a liquid.
[F24F 8/142] Treatment of used liquid, e.g. cleaning for recycling -- F24F 8/142 covers devices or processes that treat used liquid, for example by cleaning it for reuse or recycling, within air-conditioning or heating installations. The classification pertains to systems that recover, purify or recondition fluids that have circulated in the equipment, improving efficiency and reducing waste.
[F24F 8/15] by chemical means -- F24F 8/15 pertains to air-conditioning installations that treat supplied air by chemical means, such as adsorption, absorption or reactive processes to eliminate pollutants, odors or microorganisms, without involving heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/158] using active carbon -- This subgroup covers air-treatment devices for indoor spaces that employ activated carbon to remove contaminants, odors, or pollutants from supplied air, without providing heating, cooling, humidifying or drying functions. It is used in ventilation, filtration or air-purification systems where adsorption on activated carbon is the primary method of cleaning the airflow.
[F24F 8/167] using catalytic reactions -- This subgroup concerns air-conditioning installations that improve the quality of air delivered to occupied spaces by employing catalytic chemical reactions to remove or transform pollutants, odors or harmful substances, without changing temperature, humidity or moisture content.
[F24F 8/175] using biological materials, plants or microorganisms -- F24F 8/175 relates to air-conditioning methods that purify indoor air using biological agents such as plants, bacteria or other microorganisms to eliminate contaminants, odors or pathogens, rather than relying on conventional heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/183] by centrifugal separation, e.g. using vortices -- The subclass F24F 8/183 concerns air-treatment methods for heating, ventilation or air-conditioning systems that purify supplied air by means of centrifugal separation, for example using vortex generators to spin air and separate particles or droplets, thereby cleaning the airstream without reliance on heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/192] by electrical means, e.g. by applying electrostatic fields or high voltages -- F24F 8/192 covers air-treatment methods that use electrical techniques such as electrostatic fields or high-voltage discharges to purify the air supplied to indoor environments, without involving heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/194] by filtering using high voltage -- This subgroup covers air-conditioning systems that purify supplied air by passing it through a high-voltage filter or ioniser, where an electric field charges particles so they are captured on electrodes. It applies to ventilation air cleaning, not to heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/20] by sterilisation -- The classification F24F 8/20 covers air-conditioning systems that treat supplied air by sterilisation, eliminating microorganisms to improve indoor air quality. It falls under mechanical engineering heating for purification of air in human living or working spaces, excluding heating, cooling, humidifying or drying functions.
[F24F 8/22] using UV light -- F24F 8/22 covers air-conditioning systems that use ultraviolet radiation to disinfect or purify the air supplied to indoor spaces. It includes devices and methods that integrate UV lamps within ducts, filters or diffusers to inactivate microorganisms, remove odors or break down pollutants, without providing heating, cooling, humidifying, or drying functions.
[F24F 8/24] using sterilising media -- F24F 8/24 covers methods and apparatus for treating indoor ventilation air by introducing sterilising media such as chemical disinfectants, UV radiation or antimicrobial agents to eliminate pathogens without changing temperature, humidity or moisture content. It falls under the CPC subclass for air-conditioning treatment beyond heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/26] using ozone -- F24F 8/26 relates to air-conditioning systems that treat supplied air by employing ozone for purification. It covers methods or devices that generate or inject ozone to remove contaminants, odors or microorganisms from indoor air without altering temperature or humidity.
[F24F 8/28] specially adapted for combatting or avoiding Legionella bacteria -- F24F 8/28 covers air-conditioning or ventilation installations that incorporate measures specifically intended to prevent the growth or spread of Legionella bacteria, such as water treatment, filtration, or temperature control beyond ordinary heating, cooling, humidifying or drying. The class applies to systems for human-occupied spaces where air quality must be safeguarded against this pathogen.
[F24F 8/30] by ionisation -- The CPC entry F24F 8/30 falls under mechanical engineering, heating and air-conditioning, and covers methods for treating air supplied to human living or working spaces that are not heating, cooling, humidifying or drying, specifically using ionisation to purify the air.
[F24F 8/40] by ozonisation -- The code refers to air-conditioning systems that purify supplied air by generating ozone to oxidise odours, microbes and other pollutants. Ozonisation is employed as a secondary treatment after heating, cooling, humidifying or drying to improve indoor air quality in residential, commercial or industrial environments.
[F24F 8/50] by odorisation -- F24F 8/50 covers methods and devices that introduce odorants into air supplied to indoor spaces so that leaks, contamination or hazardous conditions can be detected by scent, typically by adding fragrance or chemical odorants to ventilation, exhaust or distribution systems.
[F24F 8/60] by adding oxygen
[F24F 8/70] by removing radon -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reducing of radon concentration in living or working space for humans or spaces associated therewith, e.g. sub-basements.
[F24F 8/80] Self-contained air purifiers -- The code F24F 8/80 designates self-contained air-purifying devices that treat indoor air in residential or occupational environments. It covers equipment that cleans, filters, or otherwise improves air quality without providing heating, cooling, humidifying or drying functions.
[F24F 8/90] Cleaning of purification apparatus -- F24F 8/90 concerns methods and devices for cleaning equipment that purifies air in indoor spaces, such as filters, adsorbers or sterilisation units, without providing heating, cooling, humidifying or drying. It falls under the air-conditioning subclass that deals with treatment of supplied air for human occupancy.
[F24F 8/95] specially adapted for specific purposes -- F24F 8/95 in the CPC covers air-treatment installations that are specially adapted for particular purposes, such as targeted removal of contaminants, odors or pathogens, in spaces where heating, cooling, humidifying or drying are not the primary function. It includes devices and methods designed for specific applications in residential, commercial or industrial environments, extending basic air-conditioning technology to meet specialized purification needs.
[F24F 8/96] for removing pollen -- F24F 8/96 refers to air-conditioning devices that treat indoor air by removing pollen, i.e., filtration or cleaning methods designed to eliminate pollen particles from the supplied air in buildings. This falls under mechanical engineering, heating, air-conditioning, and the treatment of air for human spaces, excluding heating, cooling, humidifying or drying.
[F24F 8/97] for removing tobacco smoke -- The F24F 8/97 entry covers air-conditioning or ventilation systems that incorporate means to eliminate tobacco smoke from indoor air, such as filters, adsorbents, electrostatic precipitators or chemical scrubbers. It is applied to devices that purify supplied air in homes, workplaces or other occupied spaces.
[F24F 8/98] for removing ozone -- F24F 8/98 relates to air-conditioning systems that treat supplied air by removing ozone. The classification covers methods or devices designed to eliminate ozone from indoor air in residential, commercial or industrial environments, distinct from heating, cooling, humidifying or drying functions.
[F24F 8/99] for treating air sourced from urban areas, e.g. from streets -- This CPC subclass covers systems that treat air drawn from urban sources such as streets before it is supplied to indoor living or working spaces, focusing on purification rather than heating, cooling, humidifying or drying. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Air-conditioning → Treatment of air supplied to human spaces.
[F24F 9/00] Use of air currents for screening, e.g. air curtains -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F24H] FLUID HEATERS, e.g. WATER OR AIR HEATERS, HAVING HEAT-GENERATING MEANS, e.g. HEAT PUMPS, IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Self-contained heaters comprising heat generating means for heating fluids:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Steam generation -> F22  **Glossary:** - Water includes other liquids;air includes other gases or gas mixtures: "water" and "air" always mean respectively, the liquid and gas to be heated - Furnace tubes: means tubes inside the heater wherein combustion is performed - Fire tubes: means tubes inside the heater wherein combustion is performed - Water tubes: means the tubes inside the heater through which water to be heated flows - Heater: means apparatus including both heat generating means and means for transferring the generated heat to water or air
[F24H 1/00] Water heaters, e.g. boilers, continuous-flow heaters or water-storage heaters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Water heaters having generating means of the following kind:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Steam boilers -> F22B
[F24H 1/0009] of the reduced pressure or vacuum steam type -- The entry F24H 1/0009 covers water-heating devices that generate steam at sub-atmospheric or vacuum pressure, such as low-pressure or vacuum boilers and similar heaters that use reduced-pressure steam to transfer heat to water. It falls under mechanical-engineering heating technologies for fluid heaters.
[F24H 1/0018] using electric energy supply -- The F24H 1/0018 subgroup covers water-heating apparatus that obtain their power from an electric energy supply, including electric boilers, continuous-flow heaters and storage water heaters, and falls under the broader class of fluid heaters in mechanical engineering.
[F24H 1/0027] using fluid fuel -- F24H 1/0027 covers water-heating installations that obtain their heat from a fluid fuel such as oil, diesel or gas. It includes boilers, continuous-flow heaters and storage water heaters where the fuel is burned or otherwise used to generate thermal energy for heating water.
[F24H 1/0036] of the sealed type -- The CPC subclass F24H 1/0036 covers sealed-type water heating devices, such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters, where the heating element and water are contained within an impermeable enclosure. This design prevents direct contact between the heat-generating components and the water, enhancing safety and reducing corrosion. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters.
[F24H 1/0045] with catalytic combustion -- F24H 1/0045 designates water heating apparatuses-boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters-that generate heat by catalytic combustion, a method in which a catalyst promotes fuel oxidation at lower temperatures than direct flame combustion. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/0054] Gas- or oil-fired immersion heaters for open containers or ponds -- The F24H 1/0054 subclass covers immersion heaters that burn gas or oil and are placed directly in open water bodies such as tanks, ponds or pools to generate heat. These devices heat a metal element immersed in the fluid, allowing direct transfer of thermal energy for industrial, agricultural or municipal water-heating applications.
[F24H 1/0063] using solid fuel -- F24H 1/0063 covers fluid-heating devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage water heaters that generate heat by combusting solid fuels, including coal, wood, pellets or other solid-fuel sources, and transferring the released energy to the water through a heat-exchanger.
[F24H 1/0072] Special adaptations -- F24H 1/0072 covers special adaptations of fluid heaters, such as water-storage, continuous-flow, or boiler type water heaters. It includes modifications, auxiliary devices or control arrangements that improve performance, safety, efficiency or integration of the heating system. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Heating → Fluid heaters having heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/009] for vehicle systems -- This subclass covers water-heating apparatus installed in vehicles, such as on-board boilers, continuous-flow or storage water heaters that supply hot water for cabin heating, engine cooling or domestic use. The systems may employ heat pumps, waste-heat recovery or other heat-generating means integrated with the vehicle's power source.
[F24H 1/06] Portable or mobile, e.g. collapsible -- F24H 1/06 covers portable or mobile water heating units, such as collapsible or transportable boilers, continuous-flow heaters and water-storage heaters that can be moved to different locations. The code falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for heating fluid heaters that use heat-generating means like heat pumps.
[F24H 1/08] Packaged or self-contained boilers, i.e. water heaters with control devices and pump in a single unit -- F24H 1/08 covers packaged or self-contained boilers, where the water-heating unit includes its own control circuitry and pump integrated into a single enclosure. These compact water heaters are ready-to-install units used for domestic or commercial heating applications.
[F24H 1/10] Continuous-flow heaters, i.e. heaters in which heat is generated only while the water is flowing, e.g. with direct contact of the water with the heating medium -- F24H 1/10 covers continuous-flow water heaters in which heat is produced only while water moves through the unit, typically by direct contact with a heating medium such as a heat pump or combustion gas. These devices include boilers and on-demand heaters that have no storage tank.
[F24H 1/101] using electric energy supply -- F24H 1/101 covers fluid heaters such as water boilers, continuous-flow units or storage tanks whose heat-generating means are powered by an electric energy supply, including electrically driven heat pumps and similar devices. It pertains to the use of electricity as the energy source for heating water in mechanical-engineering applications.
[F24H 1/102] with resistance -- F24H 1/102 covers fluid heating apparatus, such as water boilers or continuous-flow water heaters, that generate heat using electrical resistance elements. The classification applies when the primary heating means are resistive coils or similar resistive components.
[F24H 1/103] with bare resistances in direct contact with the fluid -- F24H 1/103 covers water-heating appliances such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that employ bare heating elements placed directly in the fluid without protective sheaths, allowing the resistance material to be in direct contact with the water or other heating medium.
[F24H 1/105] formed by the tube through which the fluid flows -- F24H 1/105 covers water heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters in which the heating element is formed by the tube that carries the water or other fluid. The tube itself acts as the heat-generating member, typically a heat pump or similar source, defining the fluid's flow path within the device.
[F24H 1/106] with electrodes -- F24H 1/106 covers fluid-heating devices, especially water heaters, that generate heat using electrodes immersed in the fluid, typically by electric resistance or electro-chemical heating processes.
[F24H 1/107] using fluid fuel -- F24H 1/107 covers water heating devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that are powered by fluid fuels like gasoline, diesel or heating oil. It lies within the mechanical engineering hierarchy under heating and fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/108] using solid fuel -- F24H 1/108 defines water-heating apparatus-such as boilers, continuous-flow units or storage heaters-where the heat source is solid fuel, including coal, wood, charcoal, biomass or similar combustible materials. It covers designs that generate heat by directly burning the solid fuel to raise the temperature of water for domestic, industrial or commercial use.
[F24H 1/12] in which the water is kept separate from the heating medium -- This subclass covers water-heating installations in which the water to be heated is not mixed with the heating medium but is separated by a heat-exchange surface. Typical examples include indirect boilers, heat-pump water heaters and storage tanks that use a coil or a shell-and-tube exchanger. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters, in general.
[F24H 1/121] using electric energy supply -- This subclass covers water heating devices that are powered by electricity, including electric boilers, instant electric water heaters, and storage heaters that use electric elements to raise water temperature. It pertains to fluid heaters where the heat source is an electric energy supply.
[F24H 1/122] combined with storage tank -- F24H 1/122 covers water heating apparatus that incorporates a storage tank, such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters, where the heating element and the tank are integrated into a single unit.
[F24H 1/124] using fluid fuel -- The subclass F24H 1/124 relates to water-heater systems that obtain their energy from fluid fuels such as liquid petroleum, natural gas, LPG or other combustible liquids or gases. It covers the arrangement of burners, combustion chambers and control devices that use these fuels to heat water in boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters.
[F24H 1/125] combined with storage tank -- F24H 1/125 pertains to water heating systems that incorporate a storage tank together with the heating unit, such as boilers, continuous-flow heaters or other devices that store heated water for later use.
[F24H 1/127] using solid fuel -- F24H 1/127 covers water-heating installations such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that obtain heat from solid fuels like wood, coal, or biomass pellets, detailing the specific arrangements and components used for solid-fuel combustion.
[F24H 1/128] combined with storage tank -- The subclass F24H 1/128 covers water-heater installations in which the heat-generating unit, such as a boiler, heat pump or continuous-flow heater, is integrated with a storage tank, allowing heated water to be retained for later use. It applies to fluid heating devices within the mechanical-engineering heating sector that combine the heating source and the storage component in a single assembly.
[F24H 1/14] by tubes, e.g. bent in serpentine form -- The F24H 1/14 subclass concerns water-heating installations that employ tubular heat exchangers, such as bent or serpentine tubes, to transfer heat to the water. It includes designs where the tube configuration creates a long flow path, enhancing heat transfer efficiency in boilers, continuous-flow heaters, or storage heaters.
[F24H 1/142] using electric energy supply -- This subclass covers water heating devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters where the heating is powered by electricity rather than gas, fuel, or other sources. It includes electric resistance heating, heat pumps, and other electric-driven methods applied to water heating systems.
[F24H 1/145] using fluid fuel -- F24H 1/145 refers to fluid-fuel-powered water heaters such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that generate heat by burning a liquid or gaseous fuel. It covers heating devices where the primary heat-generating means is a combustible fluid, including oil, gas, or other fluid fuels used to heat water or produce steam.
[F24H 1/147] using solid fuel -- F24H 1/147 designates water-heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that are powered by solid fuels. The subclass addresses systems where solid fuel combustion provides the heat for heating water or other fluids.
[F24H 1/16] helically or spirally coiled -- This subgroup covers water-heating devices that use a helix or spiral coil as the heat-exchange element, a design often applied in boilers or continuous-flow heaters to increase surface area and enhance heat transfer.
[F24H 1/162] using electrical energy supply -- This subgroup covers water heating devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that are powered by electricity rather than fossil fuels or other energy sources. It includes systems where the heat-generating means are electric elements, heat pumps or resistive heating.
[F24H 1/165] using fluid fuel -- This CPC subclass covers water-heating devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that are powered by a fluid fuel. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/167] using solid fuel -- The subclass F24H 1/167 covers water-heating apparatus-boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters-that generate heat by burning solid fuels such as coal, wood, peat or biomass. It includes any design where the solid-fuel combustion system provides the primary energy for heating water.
[F24H 1/18] Water-storage heaters -- F24H 1/18 covers water-storage heaters, such as boilers and tanks that store heated water for later use. The subclass includes devices that generate heat by electric, gas, or other means and retain it in a storage vessel for distribution to heating systems or domestic consumption.
