[F24H 1/181] Construction of the tank -- F24H 1/181 relates to the tank used in water-heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow heaters or storage heaters. It details the tank's shape, material, insulation, supporting structure and assembly methods that affect safety, durability and heat-retention performance.
[F24H 1/182] Insulation -- F24H 1/182 covers insulation applied to water heating equipment such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters to minimise heat loss and improve energy efficiency.
[F24H 1/183] Inner linings -- The F24H 1/183 subclass pertains to the inner linings of fluid-heater devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage water heaters. It covers protective or insulating layers applied to the interior surfaces of the heating vessel, including the materials, coatings or constructions employed to prevent corrosion, improve heat transfer or contain the heated fluid.
[F24H 1/185] using electric energy supply  F24H1/201  takes precedence
[F24H 1/186] using fluid fuel -- F24H 1/186 concerns water-heating apparatus-including boilers, continuous-flow and storage heaters-whose heat-generating means are powered by a fluid fuel such as oil, gasoline or other liquid hydrocarbons. It is a subcategory of mechanical-engineering heating devices that use fluid fuels to raise water temperature.
[F24H 1/187] using solid fuel -- F24H 1/187 covers water-heating apparatus, such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters, that generate heat by burning solid fuels like coal, wood, pellets or other biomass. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering heating, specifically fluid heaters that use heat-generating means such as heat pumps or direct combustion. It addresses designs and methods for efficiently transferring heat from solid-fuel combustion to water.
[F24H 1/188] with means for compensating water expansion -- F24H 1/188 refers to water heating apparatus such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters that include a device to compensate for the expansion of water when heated. The expansion-compensation means may be an expansion tank, bellows or a similar component that prevents pressure buildup. This subgroup is part of the broader class for fluid heaters in mechanical engineering.
[F24H 1/20] with immersed heating elements, e.g. electric elements or furnace tubes -- F24H 1/20 covers water heating devices that employ an immersed heating element such as an electric coil or furnace tube to raise the temperature of the water, typical of boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters. The element is placed directly in the water and transfers heat by direct conduction.
[F24H 1/201] using electric energy supply -- This subclass concerns water-heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that obtain their heating power from an electric energy supply. It includes electric resistive elements, heat-pump driven units and other electrically powered heating means used to raise water temperature.
[F24H 1/202] with resistances -- F24H 1/202 covers water-heater devices such as boilers, continuous-flow heaters or storage heaters that generate heat by electrical resistance elements, rather than by combustion or heat-pump cycles. The subclass is part of the broader mechanical-engineering group for fluid heaters and specifies heating systems that rely on resistive heating to raise water temperature.
[F24H 1/203] with electrodes -- The subclass F24H 1/203 refers to water-heating apparatus-such as boilers, continuous-flow units or storage heaters-that employ electrodes as the heat-generating element, typically using electric resistance or electro-thermal methods. It is situated under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters in the CPC hierarchy.
[F24H 1/205] with furnace tubes -- F24H 1/205 covers fluid heating devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage water heaters that incorporate furnace tubes as the heat-generating element. The tubes form a combustion chamber where fuel is burned and heat is transferred directly to the fluid.
[F24H 1/206] with submerged combustion chamber -- F24H 1/206 covers water-heating devices such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters that incorporate a combustion chamber situated directly within the water volume; the fuel is burned under the water surface, allowing heat to be transferred by direct contact. This subclass is used for water heaters where the heat-generating means are submerged in the medium being heated.
[F24H 1/207] with water tubes -- This subclass refers to water-heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that use water tubes as the principal heat-exchange element.
[F24H 1/208] with tubes filled with heat transfer fluid -- The classification F24H 1/208 covers water-heating equipment such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that employ tubes filled with a heat-transfer fluid to convey thermal energy from a heat-generating source to the water. It falls under the broader group of mechanical-engineering heating devices that use fluid heat exchangers.
[F24H 1/22] Water heaters other than continuous-flow or water-storage heaters, e.g. water heaters for central heating -- F24H 1/22 designates water-heating apparatus that are not continuous-flow or storage type, such as units that heat water for distribution through a central heating system. These devices typically incorporate a heat-generating source like a boiler or heat pump and serve to provide thermal energy to radiators or floor-tube networks.
[F24H 1/225] electrical central heating boilers -- F24H 1/225 covers electrical central-heating boilers, which are water-heating devices powered by electricity and used to supply hot water for building heating systems. The class falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters such as water or air heaters with heat-generating means, specifically addressing electrically driven boilers for central heating applications.
[F24H 1/24] with water mantle surrounding the combustion chamber or chambers -- This subclass covers water-heating apparatus in which a water-filled mantle encloses the combustion chamber or chambers, allowing heat generated by combustion to be transferred directly to the water. The mantle surrounds one or more chambers and serves as the primary heat-transfer medium.
[F24H 1/26] the water mantle forming an integral body -- The entry covers water-heating devices in which the water-filled mantle is formed as a single, integral component of the heater's structure, such as boilers or continuous-flow units where the heating chamber and water container are one piece.
[F24H 1/263] with a dry-wall combustion chamber -- F24H 1/263 covers water-heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that incorporate a dry-wall combustion chamber, meaning the combustion gases are confined in a separate, non-wet enclosure apart from the water circuit. This subclass refines the broader category of fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/28] including one or more furnace or fire tubes -- The subclass F24H 1/28 relates to water-heating devices that incorporate one or more furnace or fire tubes, where combustion gases travel through tubes immersed in water to transfer heat, as found in boilers and continuous-flow water heaters.
[F24H 1/282] with flue gas passages built-up by coaxial water mantles -- F24H 1/282 covers water heaters-such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage units-where the flue-gas passages are formed by coaxial water mantles that surround the combustion chamber, allowing heat to be transferred directly through the water-filled mantle surrounding the gas flow. This design integrates the heating and exhaust paths within a single, water-filled structure.
[F24H 1/285] with the fire tubes arranged alongside the combustion chamber -- This subgroup covers water-heater devices, such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters, in which the fire tubes are positioned alongside the combustion chamber rather than within it, allowing heat to be transferred from the flame space directly to the water through tubes that run parallel to the chamber walls.
[F24H 1/287] with the fire tubes arranged in line with the combustion chamber -- F24H 1/287 covers water-heating devices such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters in which the fire tubes are installed longitudinally, directly in line with the combustion chamber, so that heat from the flame is transferred straight into the water circuit. This arrangement improves heat transfer efficiency and simplifies the boiler's construction.
[F24H 1/30] the water mantle being built up from sections -- The subclass F24H 1/30 covers water-heating devices in which the heating mantle is constructed from multiple sections that are assembled together around the water path, allowing modular construction or replacement of parts in boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters.
[F24H 1/32] with vertical sections arranged side by side -- This group covers water-heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that are built with multiple vertical heating sections arranged side by side, allowing modular or staged heating within a single unit.
[F24H 1/34] with water chamber arranged adjacent to the combustion chamber or chambers, e.g. above or at side -- F24H 1/34 designates water-heating appliances where the water chamber is positioned directly adjacent to the combustion chamber or chambers, such as mounted above or alongside the burner, allowing heat to be transferred from the flame to the water. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters that use heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/36] the water chamber including one or more fire tubes -- F24H 1/36 refers to water-heater constructions where the water chamber contains one or more fire tubes that carry the heating medium through the water, allowing heat transfer directly from the tube surfaces to the stored water. This subgroup applies to boilers, continuous-flow and storage heaters that employ such tubular arrangements.
[F24H 1/38] with water contained in separate elements, e.g. radiator-type element -- This subcategory of fluid heaters covers water-heating systems where the water is held in separate heat-exchange elements such as radiators rather than a single vessel. It includes boilers or continuous-flow units that circulate water through distinct tubes or panels to transfer heat.
[F24H 1/40] with water tube or tubes -- This subclass refers to water-heating installations such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters that employ one or more water tubes as part of the heat-exchange or heating element. The tubes carry the water through a zone heated by combustion, electric elements or a heat pump, allowing efficient transfer of thermal energy to the fluid.
[F24H 1/403] the water tubes being arranged in one or more circles around the burner -- This CPC subclass covers water-heater designs where the water tubes are arranged in one or more circles around the burner. It applies to boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that use this tubular geometry to enhance heat transfer. The entry is placed under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/406] the tubes forming a membrane wall -- F24H 1/406 covers water-heater designs in which a wall of the heating chamber is formed by a bundle of tubes that act as a membrane, allowing heat to be transferred between the fluid inside the tubes and the water being heated. This configuration is typical in boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters where the tube array provides both structural support and efficient heat exchange.
[F24H 1/41] in serpentine form -- F24H 1/41 covers water heaters in which the heating element or heat-exchange path is arranged in a serpentine shape, causing the fluid to follow a winding course that improves heat-transfer efficiency and allows a compact design. This configuration is applied to boilers, continuous-flow heaters and water-storage heaters.
[F24H 1/43] helically or spirally coiled -- The entry covers water-heating apparatus such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that employ a helically or spirally coiled heat-transfer element. The coil serves as the heat-generating means, allowing fluid to pass through the spiral for efficient heat exchange in the heating system.
[F24H 1/44] with combinations of two or more of the types covered by groups -- F24H 1/44 covers water-heating devices such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that incorporate two or more of the basic types defined in the preceding groups, for example a system that combines a heat pump with a conventional boiler. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/445] with integrated flue gas condenser -- A water-heating device, such as a boiler or continuous-flow heater, that incorporates a flue-gas condenser to recover latent heat from exhaust gases, thereby increasing overall thermal efficiency and reducing fuel consumption.
[F24H 1/46] Water heaters having plural combustion chambers -- F24H 1/46 covers water heating apparatus such as boilers or continuous-flow units that incorporate two or more separate combustion chambers for generating heat. The multiple chambers may be arranged in parallel or series to improve efficiency, capacity control, or safety. This subclass falls under the hierarchy mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 1/48] Water heaters for central heating incorporating heaters for domestic water -- F24H 1/48 covers water-heater units that supply heat for a central heating system while simultaneously providing domestic hot water. The subclass includes combined boilers, continuous-flow heaters or storage tanks that incorporate a domestic-water heating element, enabling integrated heating for both space-heating circuits and household water supply.
[F24H 1/50] incorporating domestic water tanks -- The subclass F24H 1/50 covers water-heating installations that incorporate a domestic storage tank, such as boilers or continuous-flow heaters with an integrated tank for supplying hot water. It includes designs where the heating element and the water-storage container form a single unit, enabling on-demand or stored-water heating for residential use.
[F24H 15/00] Control of fluid heaters -- F24H 15/00 concerns the regulation and monitoring of fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters and heat-pump units. It includes thermostats, sensor-based feedback, control algorithms, safety shut-off, flow-rate or temperature set-point adjustment, and electronic, pneumatic or hydraulic actuation of the heating element.
[F24H 15/10] characterised by the purpose of the control -- F24H 15/10 covers control arrangements for fluid-heating installations such as water or air heaters where the control is defined principally by its intended purpose, for example maintaining a set temperature, preventing overheating, or optimizing energy use. The subclass groups devices and methods whose control strategy is described in terms of the function it is meant to achieve rather than the specific hardware employed.
[F24H 15/104] Inspection -- F24H 15/104 covers inspection of fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters, including the devices, sensors, and procedures used to monitor and verify system performance, safety, and compliance during operation or maintenance. It pertains to control systems that incorporate diagnostic or checking functions for heat-generating equipment like heat pumps and other fluid heaters.
[F24H 15/108] Resuming operation, e.g. after power outages -- This subgroup covers methods and devices that restart fluid-heater systems after an interruption such as a power outage, providing automatic and safe re-energising of pumps, burners or control circuits. It includes control logic for detecting the loss of power, preserving operating parameters and resuming normal operation.
[F24H 15/112] Preventing or detecting blocked flues -- F24H 15/112 relates to methods and devices that prevent or detect a blocked flue in fluid heating systems such as water or air heaters, including heat-pump installations. The entry focuses on control mechanisms that monitor flue flow and trigger alerts or shutdowns to avoid unsafe operating conditions.
[F24H 15/116] Disabling the heating means in response thereto -- F24H 15/116 covers control arrangements that deactivate the heating element of fluid heaters such as water or air systems, typically in response to safety signals, temperature thresholds, or user commands. The entry includes methods and devices that shut off the heat-generating means to prevent overheating or to stop operation on demand.
[F24H 15/12] Preventing or detecting fluid leakage -- F24H 15/12 covers technologies that detect or prevent leakage in fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters, including sensors, monitoring circuits, automatic shut-off valves and related control methods. The code is applied to systems that integrate leak-detection and safety functions within the broader class of fluid-heater control.
[F24H 15/124] Preventing or detecting electric faults, e.g. electric leakage -- F24H 15/124 covers measures for preventing or detecting electric faults, such as leakage or short-circuit conditions, in fluid-heater control systems. It includes monitoring, protective, and diagnostic devices that guard heating installations against electrical failures. This subclass falls under the broader category of fluid-heater control within mechanical-engineering heating technology.
[F24H 15/128] Preventing overheating -- F24H 15/128 covers control measures that prevent overheating in fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters, including heat-pumps. It relates to devices, sensors, thermostats, cut-off switches or control algorithms that monitor temperature and automatically reduce or stop heat generation to avoid unsafe temperature rise.
[F24H 15/132] Preventing the operation of water heaters with low water levels, e.g. dry-firing -- The subclass relates to devices or control systems that halt a water heater's operation when the water level drops too low, preventing dry-firing and possible damage. It includes sensors, switches or logic that monitor water level and interrupt power or fuel supply.
[F24H 15/136] Defrosting or de-icing -- This subclass covers control techniques and devices that melt or prevent ice buildup on fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters and heat pumps. It includes defrost cycles, sensors, and automated de-icing systems that regulate temperature or flow to clear frost during operation.
[F24H 15/14] Cleaning -- F24H 15/14 pertains to the control of fluid heaters, such as water or air heating systems that incorporate a heat-generating means like a heat pump, specifically addressing methods and apparatus for cleaning those heaters. It includes techniques for removing deposits, scale or fouling from heat exchangers and related components to maintain efficient operation.
[F24H 15/144] Measuring or calculating energy consumption -- F24H 15/144 pertains to methods and devices for determining the energy used by fluid-heater systems such as water or air heaters, including heat-pump arrangements; it covers sensors, metering instruments and calculation techniques that monitor or compute consumption while the heater operates, forming part of the control of fluid heaters subclass.
[F24H 15/148] Assessing the current energy consumption -- F24H 15/148 covers techniques for measuring and analysing the instantaneous energy consumption of fluid heating installations, including water and air heaters, to provide feedback for performance monitoring or control. It includes sensors, data acquisition, calculation methods and software tools that assess current power draw and efficiency during operation.
[F24H 15/152] Forecasting future energy consumption -- The F24H 15/152 subclass deals with methods and systems that predict the amount of energy a fluid-heater-such as a water- or air-heater or heat pump-will require in upcoming periods. It includes algorithms, data-analysis techniques and modeling approaches used to forecast consumption for better control and optimization of heating installations.
[F24H 15/156] Reducing the quantity of energy consumed -- F24H 15/156 covers methods and devices for reducing the energy consumed by fluid heating systems such as water or air heaters. It includes control strategies, efficient operation and energy-saving measures applied to heating units, often using heat pumps or other heat-generating means. The aim is to lower overall power demand while maintaining the required temperature output.
[F24H 15/16] Reducing cost using the price of energy, e.g. choosing or switching between different energy sources -- The F24H 15/16 subclass covers strategies for lowering operating expense of fluid heating systems by selecting or switching among different energy sources, such as electricity, gas, or renewable fuels, and managing the associated cost implications.
[F24H 15/164] where the price of the electric supply changes with time -- F24H 15/164 covers control methods for fluid heating systems that adjust operation in response to time-varying electricity prices, such as shifting heating periods or modulating output when tariffs are low. It includes strategies for demand-responsive scheduling, price-signal monitoring and set-point adaptation to minimise energy cost while maintaining required heating performance.
[F24H 15/168] Reducing the electric power demand peak -- The entry deals with methods, devices or control strategies for fluid heating systems that are designed to lower the peak electricity demand, such as load-shedding, demand-side management or modulation of heater operation to smooth power consumption.
[F24H 15/172] Scheduling based on user demand, e.g. determining starting point of heating -- F24H 15/172 covers methods for scheduling the operation of fluid heaters, such as water or air heating systems, according to user demand, including determining the optimal start time for heating. It is part of the mechanical-engineering heating subclass dealing with control of fluid heaters that use heat-generating means like heat pumps.
[F24H 15/174] Supplying heated water with desired temperature or desired range of temperature -- This subclass deals with methods or devices that regulate the temperature of water delivered from a heating system to meet a set target or stay within a specified range. It includes control strategies, sensors, actuators and feedback loops that adjust heat output or flow to achieve the desired water temperature.
[F24H 15/175] where the difference between the measured temperature and a set temperature is kept under a predetermined value -- F24H 15/175 pertains to control arrangements for fluid heaters that keep the difference between the measured temperature and the set temperature within a predetermined limit, ensuring the heating output stays within a defined tolerance of the desired value.
[F24H 15/176] Improving or maintaining comfort of users -- F24H 15/176 covers control strategies and devices that enhance or preserve user comfort in fluid-heater systems, such as water or air heating installations. It includes methods for regulating temperature, flow rate, distribution patterns, and feedback mechanisms that adapt heating output to occupants' needs and environmental conditions.
[F24H 15/18] Preventing sudden or unintentional change of fluid temperature -- F24H 15/18 concerns control techniques that stop a fluid-heater from experiencing a rapid or accidental shift in temperature, covering sensors, regulation methods and protective devices that keep the heating medium, such as water or air, at a stable temperature during operation.
[F24H 15/184] Preventing harm to users from exposure to heated water, e.g. scalding -- F24H 15/184 covers safety features that protect users from scalding when they encounter heated water in fluid-heater systems such as water or air heaters and heat pumps. The entry includes controls, sensors, mixing valves and other devices that limit temperature, detect excessive heat or otherwise prevent harmful exposure.
[F24H 15/196] Automatically filling bathtubs or pools -- F24H 15/196 covers systems that automatically fill bathtubs, pools, or similar receptacles with water, typically integrated with heating control to ensure the desired temperature. The invention may include sensors, timers, or electronic controllers that regulate water flow and temperature during the filling process.
[F24H 1/52] incorporating heat exchangers for domestic water -- F24H 1/52 covers heating devices that incorporate a heat exchanger for supplying domestic water, such as boilers, continuous-flow or storage heaters that transfer thermal energy from a heat-generating source like a heat pump to water within an integrated exchanger. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → heating → fluid heaters having heat-generating means.
[F24H 15/20] characterised by control inputs -- F24H 15/20 relates to fluid heating devices, such as water or air heaters, whose operation is governed by control inputs like thermostats, user commands or sensor signals that adjust the heating output, timing or mode. This subclass captures systems where the heat-generating means is modulated by external or internal control signals to achieve desired temperature or performance.
[F24H 15/204] Temperature of the air before heating -- The entry deals with control of the temperature of the incoming air before it is heated in fluid heating systems such as water or air heaters, including heat-pump installations. It covers methods and devices for regulating pre-heat air temperature.
[F24H 15/208] Temperature of the air after heating -- F24H 15/208 relates to the measurement or regulation of the temperature of the air leaving a fluid heater, such as a water- or air-type heater or heat pump. It covers devices, sensors and control methods that monitor and adjust the outlet air temperature to achieve desired heating performance.
[F24H 15/212] Temperature of the water -- F24H 15/212 covers methods and devices for controlling the temperature of water used in fluid heating systems such as water-based or air heaters, including heat-pump installations. It specifies control strategies that adjust heating output to maintain a desired water temperature, ensuring efficient operation and user comfort.
[F24H 15/215] before heating -- F24H 15/215 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification dealing with control of fluid heaters before heating. It addresses methods, devices, or strategies for regulating or adjusting a heating system-such as a water or air heater or heat pump-prior to the start of the heating cycle, ensuring efficient operation and safety.
[F24H 15/219] after heating -- The subclass covers control techniques applied after a fluid heater has generated heat, such as thermostats or controllers that monitor and adjust temperature downstream, regulate flow or cut off energy once the desired temperature is reached, and similar post-heating regulation in water or air heating systems.
[F24H 15/223] in the water storage tank -- F24H 15/223 concerns control methods and devices for fluid heating systems that store heated water, such as regulation of temperature, pressure, or flow within the storage tank. It includes sensors, thermostats and control circuits that manage the operation of water heaters, heat pumps or similar equipment.

[F24H 1/523] Heat exchangers for sanitary water directly heated by the burner -- F24H 1/523 classifies heat exchangers that heat sanitary water directly from a burner, transferring combustion heat to the water without an intermediate fluid. It addresses control strategies, sensors, and actuators that operate directly on the evaporator to achieve desired heating performance.
[F24H 15/232] at the condenser -- The entry covers control functions applied to the condenser of fluid-heater systems such as water or air heat pumps, including regulation of temperature, flow or pressure to optimise heat-transfer performance and system efficiency.
[F24H 15/235] Temperature of exhaust gases -- The F24H 15/235 subclass covers technologies for measuring, monitoring or regulating the temperature of exhaust gases in fluid-heater systems such as heat pumps, water or air heaters. [F24H 15/238] Flow rate -- F24H 15/238 relates to the regulation of fluid flow in heating installations, covering methods and devices that control the rate at which water, air or other heating media are circulated through heaters, including heat-pump systems, to achieve desired temperature or efficiency.
[F24H 15/242] Pressure -- The subclass F24H 15/242 covers control methods and devices that regulate pressure in fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters and heat-pump systems, ensuring safe operation and optimal heat transfer. It addresses mechanisms for maintaining desired pressure levels within the heating circuit.
[F24H 15/246] Water level -- The entry covers devices and methods for detecting, monitoring and regulating the water level in fluid-heater systems such as boilers, heat pumps or water-based radiators. [F24H 15/248] of water storage tanks

[F24H 15/258] Outdoor temperature -- Outdoor temperature is the ambient external temperature used as an input for controlling fluid-heater systems such as water or air heaters and heat pumps. By measuring this parameter, the heating system can adjust its output and efficiency to match changing outdoor conditions.
[F24H 1/526] Pipes in pipe heat exchangers for sanitary water -- F24H 1/526 covers the design and arrangement of pipes that form the internal circuitry of pipe-type heat exchangers used to heat sanitary water, such as in boilers, continuous-flow units or storage heaters. [F24H 15/262] Weather information or forecast -- F24H 15/262 covers control systems for fluid heaters that incorporate weather information or forecasts, adjusting heating operation according to predicted external conditions to improve efficiency or comfort. [F24H 15/265] Occupancy -- The F24H 15/265 entry pertains to occupancy-based control of fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters, including heat-pump systems. [F24H 15/269] Time, e.g. hour or date
[F24H 15/273] Address or location -- F24H 15/273 covers control schemes for fluid heaters that use an address or location identifier to select heating parameters, enabling the system to adjust temperature, flow or timing based on the specific point in a network, such as a room, zone or pipe segment.

[F24H 15/296] Information from neighbouring devices -- F24H 15/296 covers methods and systems that acquire information from adjacent devices-such as sensors, other appliances, or building-automation components-to monitor and control fluid heating installations. The data is used to adjust temperature, flow rate or energy consumption of water or air heaters.
[F24H 15/30] characterised by control outputs -- This subclass concerns fluid heating systems, such as water or air heaters, that are characterised by the nature of their control outputs, for example valve positions, pump speeds or on-off signals used to regulate temperature or flow.
[F24H 15/305] Control of valves -- F24H 15/305 deals with methods and devices that regulate valves in fluid heating systems such as water or air heaters, including those driven by heat-pumps. It covers control of flow, temperature or pressure to optimise the operation of the heater.
[F24H 15/31] of valves having only one inlet port and one outlet port, e.g. flow rate regulating valves -- This subclass covers valves that have a single inlet and a single outlet, designed specifically for regulating the flow rate in fluid heating systems such as water or air heaters, including those incorporating heat-pumps or other heat-generating devices.
[F24H 15/315] of mixing valves -- F24H 15/315 covers mixing valves that regulate the temperature of fluid-heater systems by blending hot and cold streams, such as in water or air heating installations. These devices are part of the control mechanisms for fluid heaters within the mechanical-engineering heating field.
[F24H 15/32] of switching valves -- F24H 15/32 concerns the control of fluid heaters-such as water or air heating systems that use heat pumps or other heat-generating means-by means of switching valves. The subclass deals with valve types and operating arrangements that regulate the flow of heating fluid to modulate temperature or system performance. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters.
[F24H 15/325] of by-pass valves -- F24H 15/325 pertains to control of fluid heaters through bypass valves, which divert heating medium around the heating element to adjust temperature, maintain desired output, or prevent overheating. The subclass includes designs and mechanisms for integrating such valves in water or air heating systems.
[F24H 15/33] Control of dampers -- F24H 15/33 addresses the control of dampers in fluid-heater systems such as water or air heaters, including those employing heat pumps. It covers mechanisms that regulate airflow or fluid flow to adjust heating performance.
[F24H 15/335] Control of pumps, e.g. on-off control -- F24H 15/335 addresses the control of pumps used in fluid heating systems such as water or air heaters, including on-off control schemes. It is applied to pump management within heat-generating equipment like heat pumps, and is classified under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heater categories.
[F24H 15/34] Control of the speed of pumps -- The entry concerns methods and devices for regulating the speed of pumps used in fluid-heating installations such as water or air heaters. It includes control strategies, sensors and drive arrangements-typically variable-frequency drives or feedback loops-that adjust pump performance to meet heating demand or improve efficiency.
[F24H 15/345] Control of fans, e.g. on-off control -- F24H 15/345 relates to the control of fans in fluid heating installations, such as water or air heaters, and includes simple on-off switching or basic control schemes that regulate fan operation to manage temperature or airflow.
[F24H 15/35] Control of the speed of fans -- F24H 15/35 covers methods and devices for regulating the rotational speed of fans used in fluid heating installations such as water or air heaters, including heat-pumps. The classification addresses control strategies, sensors, and actuators that adjust fan output to maintain the desired temperature or efficiency.
[F24H 15/355] Control of heat-generating means in heaters -- The F24H 15/355 subclass concerns devices and methods that regulate the heat-producing components of fluid heaters, such as water or air heating units and heat-pump systems. It covers control strategies for adjusting temperature, power output, or operating cycles of the heating element.
[F24H 15/36] of burners -- The F24H 15/36 subclass covers control arrangements and methods specifically for burners in fluid-heater systems, such as water or air heaters. It includes devices that regulate flame ignition, fuel flow, combustion temperature and safety shut-off to ensure efficient and reliable operation of the heating unit.
[F24H 15/365] of two or more burners, e.g. an array of burners -- F24H 15/365 covers control arrangements for fluid heaters that use two or more burners, for example burner arrays, allowing coordinated regulation of heat output and operation of the heating system.
[F24H 15/37] of electric heaters
[F24H 15/375] Control of heat pumps -- F24H 15/375 deals with the regulation of heat pumps used in fluid heating systems. It includes devices, sensors and control strategies that adjust temperature, pressure or flow to maintain desired heating performance.
[F24H 15/38] Control of compressors of heat pumps -- The CPC subclass F24H 15/38 includes methods and devices for controlling the compressor in heat-pump fluid heaters. It addresses regulation of compressor speed, capacity, start-stop sequencing and efficiency to optimize heating or cooling performance. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → heating → fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 15/385] Control of expansion valves of heat pumps -- F24H 15/385 covers the control of expansion valves in heat-pump systems, focusing on methods and devices that regulate the valve opening to maintain desired temperature and pressure levels. It falls under fluid-heater control within mechanical engineering heating technologies.
[F24H 15/39] Control of valves for distributing refrigerant to different evaporators or condensers in heat pumps -- This code covers control devices that operate valves to route refrigerant to different evaporators or condensers in heat-pump systems, managing distribution for heating or cooling. It is part of the fluid-heater subclass within mechanical engineering.
[F24H 15/395] Information to users, e.g. alarms -- This subclass covers provisions that convey information to the operator of fluid heaters, such as visual or audible alarms, status displays, and warning signals indicating temperature, malfunction, or safety conditions. It includes integrated control panels, indicator lights, buzzers and other devices that alert users to the heater's performance or faults.
[F24H 1/54] Water heaters for bathtubs or pools -- F24H 1/54 covers water-heating devices intended for bathing or swimming-pool use, such as integrated heaters that supply hot water directly to bathtubs, whirlpools or pool circulation systems. The classification includes units that combine a heat source, pump and control elements to maintain the desired temperature for recreational or sanitary water applications.
[F24H 15/40] characterised by the type of controllers -- F24H 15/40 covers control systems for fluid heating apparatus such as water or air heaters that use heat-generating units like heat pumps, with the subclass defined by the particular type of controller used.
[F24H 15/407] using electrical switching, e.g. TRIAC -- F24H 15/407 relates to the control of fluid heating systems, such as water or air heaters, by employing electronic switching devices like triacs to regulate the power supplied.
[F24H 15/414] using electronic processing, e.g. computer-based -- This subclass concerns the electronic or computer-based control of fluid heating devices such as water or air heaters and heat pumps. It includes methods and apparatus that use digital processing to monitor and adjust temperature, flow or energy use for optimal operation.
[F24H 15/421] using pre-stored data -- F24H 15/421 covers control systems for fluid-heating installations, such as water or air heaters, that operate on pre-stored data. The classification includes methods and devices that use memory-based parameters, lookup tables, or previously recorded information to regulate temperature, flow or energy consumption, rather than relying solely on real-time sensing. It falls within the broader group of heating apparatuses and their control mechanisms.
[F24H 15/429] for selecting operation modes -- F24H 15/429 relates to control arrangements for fluid heaters-such as water or air heating units with heat-generating means-allowing the user or system to select among different operating modes, for example heating, standby or auxiliary functions; it falls within the mechanical-engineering heating subclass for fluid heaters.
[F24H 15/436] for selecting sleeping modes
[F24H 15/443] using a central controller connected to several sub-controllers -- The subclass pertains to fluid-heater control systems in which a primary controller is linked to several secondary controllers, allowing coordinated regulation of temperature, flow or operation across multiple heating units.
[F24H 15/45] remotely accessible -- F24H 15/45 relates to control systems for fluid heating devices that can be accessed and operated from a distance, using remote interfaces such as networks, wireless links, or telecommunication channels, allowing users to monitor and adjust temperature, flow or operational modes without being on site.
[F24H 15/457] using telephone networks or Internet communication -- This subclass deals with methods and systems for controlling fluid heating devices such as water or air heaters through telephone networks or Internet communication, enabling remote adjustment of temperature settings, scheduling, and monitoring. It covers both hardware interfaces and software protocols that facilitate remote operation.
[F24H 15/464] using local wireless communication -- F24H 15/464 concerns control of fluid heaters such as water or air heating systems by employing local wireless communication links. The subclass addresses ways to transmit commands or sensor data over short-range radio, infrared or other wireless media to adjust heating parameters, monitor status, or coordinate multiple units. It is used for patents describing wireless thermostats, zone controllers, and smart heating networks.
[F24H 15/479] for programming the system
[F24H 15/486] using timers -- F24H 15/486 covers methods and devices that control fluid heating systems, such as water or air heaters, by employing timers to schedule heating operations. Timed control can improve energy efficiency and allow automatic start-stop sequences based on predetermined intervals.
[F24H 15/493] specially adapted for enabling recognition of parts newly installed in the fluid heating system, e.g. for retrofitting or for repairing by replacing parts -- This subclass deals with control arrangements for fluid-heating systems that are specially adapted to recognise newly installed or replaced components, enabling easy retrofitting or repair of water or air heaters, including those with heat-pumps. It ensures the system can identify and integrate the added parts automatically.
[F24H 2210/00] Indexing codes related to fluid heaters having heat-generating means -- F24H 2210/00 is a CPC subclass that covers indexing codes used to categorize fluid-heater devices equipped with built-in heat-generating mechanisms such as heat pumps, ranging from water to air heating systems. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Heating - Fluid heaters having heat-generating means.
[F24H 2220/00] Measures for environmentally correct disposal -- F24H 2220/00 covers methods and devices for the environmentally responsible disposal of fluid heating systems, including removal, recycling or safe decommissioning of heat pumps, water or air heaters and related components. It addresses waste management, treatment and recovery processes to minimise environmental impact.
[F24H 2230/00] Solid fuel fired boiler -- F24H 2230/00 designates boilers that use solid fuel - such as coal, wood or biomass - to generate heat for heating water or air. The entry includes systems where the solid fuel combustion provides the primary heat source for a fluid heater, often integrated with heat-pump or other heat-generating means.
[F24H 2230/02] Solid and fluid fuel fired boilers -- F24H 2230/02 designates boilers that burn solid or fluid fuels to generate heat, including water or air heating systems such as those using heat pumps. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 2240/00] Fluid heaters having electrical generators -- F24H 2240/00 covers fluid heating devices that incorporate an electrical generator as the heat-producing element, such as heat pumps or electrically driven water or air heaters. The class is in the mechanical engineering heating sector for fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 2240/01] Batteries, electrical energy storage device -- The code F24H 2240/01 classifies batteries and other electrical energy-storage devices that supply power to fluid-heating installations such as water or air heaters, including those employing heat-pump technology. It falls under mechanical-engineering heating, specifically fluid heaters having electrical generators.
[F24H 2240/02] with combustion engines -- It covers fluid-heating apparatuses that use a combustion engine as the heat-generating source, such as an internal-combustion motor driving a water or air heater. These devices may also incorporate an electrical generator driven by the engine.
[F24H 2240/04] External combustion engines -- F24H 2240/04 covers external combustion engines, which generate heat by burning fuel outside the working fluid and transfer that heat to a fluid such as water or air, often incorporated in fluid heaters that include an electrical generator.
[F24H 2240/06] Internal combustion engines
[F24H 2240/08] with peltier elements -- Fluid heaters that employ thermoelectric Peltier modules to generate or transfer heat to the fluid, integrating the solid-state cooling/heating elements within the heating system. The classification covers devices such as water or air heaters where the Peltier effect provides the primary heat-generating mechanism.
[F24H 2240/09] with photovoltaic cells -- F24H 2240/09 refers to fluid heating devices such as water or air heaters that incorporate photovoltaic cells to generate electricity for the system. The subclass groups heaters with built-in solar panels or similar photovoltaic generators that supply power to the heat-generating components.
[F24H 2240/10] with fuel cells -- This subclass covers fluid heating devices such as water or air heaters that incorporate fuel-cell technology to generate electricity for the heating process. It includes systems where the fuel cell supplies power to drive a heat pump or other heat-generating components.
[F24H 2240/12] with thermodynamic cycle for converting thermal energy to mechanical power to produce electrical energy -- F24H 2240/12 covers fluid heating installations that incorporate a thermodynamic cycle to transform the supplied thermal energy into mechanical work, which is then used to generate electricity. Such systems typically integrate heat exchangers, a working-fluid loop and a power conversion unit like a turbine or Stirling engine to produce electrical power while providing heating.
[F24H 2240/122] Stirling cycles -- The entry covers fluid-heater devices that employ a Stirling thermodynamic cycle to generate heat, typically using an electrical generator as the heat-producing element. It applies to heating systems such as water or air heaters in which the Stirling cycle drives the transfer of thermal energy, often incorporated in heat-pump or other electrically powered heating arrangements.
[F24H 2240/125] Carnot cycles -- The F24H 2240/125 entry defines patents concerning the use of Carnot cycles in fluid heating devices that incorporate electrical generators, such as heat-pump systems that convert electrical energy into heat for water or air.
[F24H 2240/127] Rankine cycles, e.g. steam heat engines -- F24H 2240/127 classifies Rankine-cycle devices such as steam heat engines, where a liquid is heated, evaporated and expanded to produce mechanical work or heat, often coupled with electrical generators or heat-pump components. The entry falls under mechanical engineering → heating → fluid heaters with heat-generating means.
[F24H 2250/00] Electrical heat generating means -- F24H 2250/00 refers to fluid-heater devices such as water or air heaters that use electrical elements to produce heat, including resistive coils, electric heat-pump units or other electrically powered heat-generating means. It is a narrow subclass within mechanical-engineering heating for fluid heaters equipped with electrical heat sources.
[F24H 2250/02] Resistances -- The F24H 2250/02 code covers fluid-heater designs that employ resistive heating elements as the electrical means of generating heat, such as electric coils or plates used in water or air heaters and heat-pump-type systems. It specifies the use of these resistance-type devices to convert electrical energy into heat for circulating or stationary fluids.
[F24H 2250/04] Positive temperature coefficients [PTC] -- The F24H 2250/04 subclass covers fluid heating devices that employ electrical heating elements with a positive temperature coefficient, meaning the element's resistance rises as it warms, limiting current flow and providing self-regulating temperature control. It applies to water, air or other fluid heaters that use this PTC principle.
[F24H 2250/06] Peltier -- F24H 2250/06 covers fluid heating devices that employ electrical thermoelectric elements operating on the Peltier effect, whereby an electric current creates a temperature gradient across a semiconductor junction to heat or cool a fluid. The classification groups such solid-state heat-generating means under electrical heat-generating devices for water or air heaters.
[F24H 2250/08] Induction -- F24H 2250/08 refers to fluid heating devices such as water or air heaters that generate heat by induction. The classification covers heaters in which an alternating magnetic field induces electric currents in the fluid or a metal element, producing heat without direct resistance elements. It falls under electrical heat-generating means within the broader heating and mechanical engineering categories.
[F24H 2250/10] Electrodes -- F24H 2250/10 refers to electrodes that act as the electrical heating elements in fluid heaters such as water or air heaters, including systems using heat pumps. The classification covers the design, material and arrangement of these electrically powered heat-generating components within the heating apparatus.
[F24H 2250/12] Microwaves -- F24H 2250/12 refers to fluid heating installations that generate heat by microwave energy, using electrical microwave generators to heat water, air or other fluids directly instead of conventional resistive or combustion elements. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering heating, specifically fluid heaters with electrically driven heat-generating means.
[F24H 2250/14] Lamps -- F24H 2250/14 - Lamps covers fluid-heating devices, such as water or air heaters and heat-pump systems, that employ lamp-type electrical heating elements as the primary source of heat. The subclass includes designs where the lamp generates thermal energy directly transferred to the fluid, rather than using conventional resistive or infrared heating elements.
[F24H 3/00] Air heaters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air heaters sorted by the type of heat source, e.g. using electric energy supply or using fluid combustible or using solid combustibles.  Air heaters with forced circulation of air.  Air heaters with a main heat source and additional heating arrangements.  Air heaters where the air is heated at the same location where the heat is generated.  **Glossary:** - Heating medium: means the resistive element, PTC/NTC element, induction element, microwave generator, Peltier element or lamp - Direct contact: means that the resistive element, electrodes and fins are bond together without additional elements (e.g. tubes or plates) in between
[F24H 3/002] using electric energy supply -- F24H 3/002 pertains to air-heating devices that derive their heat from an electric power supply, including resistance heating elements or electrically driven heat-pump systems, as opposed to combustion or other energy sources.
[F24H 3/004] with a closed circuit for a heat transfer liquid -- This group covers air-heating installations in which the heat-generating device is linked to a closed-circuit system that circulates a heat-transfer liquid to the air stream. The liquid absorbs heat from a source such as a heat pump and releases it to the air within the heater.
[F24H 3/006] using fluid fuel -- Air heaters that obtain heat from the combustion of a fluid fuel such as liquid petroleum, gaseous petroleum, or other liquid or gaseous hydrocarbons are covered. The classification includes burners, nozzles and related components that supply the fluid fuel to the heating element, as well as control systems for regulating the fuel flow and combustion. It applies to devices where the fluid fuel is the primary source of thermal energy for warming air.
[F24H 3/008] using solid fuel -- F24H 3/008 refers to air-heating devices that obtain heat by burning solid fuels such as wood, coal, charcoal or biomass. The subclass covers arrangements in which the solid-fuel combustion chamber is combined with the air-flow system to heat the circulated air directly, including various burner and heat-exchange configurations.
[F24H 3/02] with forced circulation -- Air heaters that use a fan or pump to move air through the heating element, ensuring uniform temperature distribution and rapid heat delivery, typically employed in residential, commercial, or industrial heating systems. The forced-circulation can be achieved by mechanical blowers, fans or ducted airflow to improve efficiency.
[F24H 3/022] using electric energy supply -- F24H 3/022 covers air-heating devices that are powered directly by electricity. It falls under the class for fluid heaters such as water or air heaters with heat-generating means like heat pumps, specifically the subgroup dealing with electric energy supply as the source of heat.
[F24H 3/025] using fluid fuel -- The subclass F24H 3/025 covers air-heating appliances that obtain their heat from a fluid fuel, such as gasoline, diesel, liquid propane or other liquid hydrocarbons. It includes designs where the fluid is burned or otherwise used to generate heat that is transferred to the moving air stream. This falls under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters and air heaters.
[F24H 3/027] using solid fuel -- F24H 3/027 pertains to air-heating installations that derive their thermal energy from solid fuels such as coal, wood, or biomass. The classification covers devices where the combustion of these solids directly heats air circulated for space heating, including furnaces, stoves and related combustion systems.
[F24H 3/04] the air being in direct contact with the heating medium, e.g. electric heating element -- F24H 3/04 refers to air-heating devices where the air is heated by direct contact with the heating medium, for example an electric heating element, instead of being warmed indirectly via a separate heat-exchanger.
[F24H 3/0405] using electric energy supply, e.g. the heating medium being a resistive element; Heating by direct contact, i.e. with resistive elements, electrodes and fins being bonded together without additional element in-between  F24H3/06 ,  F24H3/08 ,  F24H3/10  take precedence -- F24H 3/0405 covers air heaters that use electric energy, with the heating medium being a resistive element whose heat is transferred by direct contact with the air, the resistive element, electrodes and fins being bonded together without any additional component in between. The code is situated under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters and air heaters.
[F24H 3/0411] for domestic or space-heating systems -- F24H 3/0411 covers air heating devices that are used in domestic or space-heating installations. It relates to fluid heaters employing air as the heating medium, typically integrated in heating systems for homes or buildings.
[F24H 3/0417] portable or mobile -- The F24H 3/0417 entry covers air-heater devices that are designed to be portable or mobile, allowing them to be moved or transported for temporary heating applications. It includes compact, self-contained units such as portable heat-pump or electric air-heater systems intended for use in vehicles, camps, or other mobile environments.
[F24H 3/0423] hand-held air guns -- Hand-held air guns are portable devices that generate and direct heated air onto a limited area for personal comfort, drying, or surface heating. They combine a compact heating element with a blower, allowing users to apply a focused stream of warm air without the need for stationary equipment.
[F24H 3/0429] For vehicles -- F24H 3/0429 covers air-heating devices intended for use in vehicles, including cabin heaters that warm interior air by means such as heat pumps, combustion or other heat-generating systems. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters.
[F24H 3/0435] Structures comprising heat spreading elements in the form of fins -- F24H 3/0435 covers air-heater constructions that incorporate heat-spreading elements formed as fins. The classification pertains to mechanical-engineering fluid heaters where the fins are integrated into the heating device to enhance the distribution of heat to the moving air stream. This entry falls under the broader categories of heating, fluid heaters and air heaters.
[F24H 3/0441] Interfaces between the electrodes of a resistive heating element and the power supply means -- F24H 3/0441 covers the connections that link the electrodes of a resistive heating element to the power-supply system, including terminals, connectors and wiring arrangements used in air-heater devices. It is a subcategory of fluid heaters for air within the mechanical-engineering heating hierarchy.
[F24H 3/0447] Forms of the electrode terminals, e.g. tongues or clips -- F24H 3/0447 covers the specific designs of electrode terminals used in air-heater systems, such as conductive tongues, clips or other attachment forms that connect the heating element to the power supply. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters, focusing on the physical configuration of the terminals that facilitate electrical connection and heat generation.
[F24H 3/0452] Frame constructions -- The entry refers to structural frameworks used in air heating devices, covering supports, housings, and mounting frames that hold heating elements, fans, or ducts. It includes designs of metal or plastic frames that provide stability and facilitate installation in residential or commercial air heater units.
[F24H 3/0458] One-piece frames -- The entry designates air-heater devices whose supporting structure, housing and airflow elements are formed as a single, integral frame, typically by casting, molding or extrusion. Such one-piece frames provide built-in rigidity, reduce part count and simplify assembly of the heating unit.
[F24H 3/0464] Two-piece frames, e.g. two-shell frames, also including frames as a central body with two covers -- F24H 3/0464 designates two-piece frames, including two-shell constructions or a central body fitted with two covers, employed in air-heater units that belong to the fluid-heater class of heating apparatuses within mechanical engineering.
[F24H 3/047] Multiple-piece frames assembled on their four or more edges -- The entry F24H 3/047 designates air-heater constructions whose housing is built from several separate members that are joined together along all four (or more) edges, forming a complete frame that encloses the heating elements and airflow channels. This type of multi-piece frame is used in fluid-heater appliances such as heat-pump or electric air heaters.
[F24H 3/0476] Means for putting the electric heaters in the frame under strain, e.g. with springs -- This subgroup covers devices that apply a pre-load to electric heating elements mounted in an air-heater frame, usually by using springs or similar elastic components. The arrangement keeps the heater under strain to maintain reliable electrical contact, improve heat distribution and prevent movement during operation.
[F24H 3/0482] Frames with integrated fan -- This subclass covers air-heating devices where the structural frame of the heater incorporates a fan that circulates heated air, eliminating a separate fan housing. The fan is built into or attached to the frame that supports the heating element, improving compactness and airflow efficiency.
[F24H 3/0488] using fluid fuel
[F24H 3/0494] using solid fuel -- This subgroup covers air-heating installations that generate heat by burning solid fuels such as wood, coal, biomass or charcoal. It includes burners, combustion chambers and heat-exchange arrangements specifically adapted for solid-fuel combustion to supply heated air for space heating or ventilation.
[F24H 3/06] the air being kept separate from the heating medium, e.g. using forced circulation of air over radiators -- The F24H 3/06 subgroup covers air-heater designs in which the heated air never mixes with the heating medium; instead, air is moved past radiators or similar heat-exchange surfaces by forced circulation, keeping the two fluids separate. This arrangement is typical of systems such as fan-assisted convectors or radiant panels that use a liquid or refrigerant loop to generate heat while the airflow remains distinct.

[F24H 3/08] by tubes -- F24H 3/08 covers air-heating apparatus in which the heat-generating medium is carried in tubes that transfer thermal energy to the passing air; the tubes may contain water, refrigerant or another fluid and are arranged to maximise surface exposure to the airflow, as in heat-pump or water-filled radiator systems.
[F24H 3/081] using electric energy supply -- F24H 3/081 covers air-heating devices that obtain their heat from an electric energy supply, such as resistive elements or electric heat-pump systems, as a subcategory of fluid heaters.
[F24H 3/082] The tubes being an electrical isolator containing the heater -- F24H 3/082 covers air-heater designs in which the heating element is enclosed within tubes that function as electrical isolators, separating the heater from surrounding components while allowing heat transfer to the airflow. This subclass addresses the combined structural and insulating role of the tubes within fluid-based heating systems.
[F24H 3/084] The tubes being an electrode for the heater -- The subclass F24H 3/084 covers air-heating devices in which the heating tubes themselves act as electrodes, so that electrical current passes through the tubes to generate heat directly. This arrangement is used in electric air heaters or heat-pump systems where the tube serves both as a conduit for fluid and as the heating element.
[F24H 3/085] The tubes containing an electrically heated intermediate fluid, e.g. water -- F24H 3/085 refers to air-heater designs that use tubes filled with an electrically heated intermediate fluid, such as water, to transfer heat to the passing air. The fluid is heated by electric elements within the tubes, which act as the primary heat-generating medium for the device.
[F24H 3/087] using fluid fuel -- F24H 3/087 concerns air-heating devices that obtain heat from a fluid fuel such as gasoline, diesel or other liquid hydrocarbons. It applies to mechanical-engineering heating systems where the fluid fuel is burned or otherwise used to generate hot air for space heating.
[F24H 3/088] using solid fuel -- It covers air heating devices in which the heat-generating means is a solid fuel such as wood, coal, pellets or other combustible solid material. The classification applies to designs where the solid fuel is burned directly to heat air, including stoves, furnaces and portable heaters.
[F24H 3/10] by plates -- Air-heater designs that generate heat using plate elements, where the plates act as heat-transfer surfaces to warm passing air. The plates may be heated directly or through a fluid circuit, providing uniform heating in compact units.
[F24H 3/102] using electric energy supply -- The subclass F24H 3/102 addresses air heating devices that are powered by an electric energy supply, covering designs in which electricity is the primary source for generating heat and distributing it to airstreams. It includes various electric-driven air-heater configurations such as resistive elements, electric heat pumps and solid-state heating systems used in residential, commercial or industrial ventilation applications.
[F24H 3/105] using fluid fuel -- F24H 3/105 pertains to air-heating apparatus in which the heat-generating means are powered by a fluid fuel, such as liquid or gaseous petroleum, natural gas, or other combustible fluids, and the heated air is discharged directly to the space to be warmed.
[F24H 3/107] using solid fuel -- The F24H 3/107 group covers air-heating installations that obtain their heat by combustion of solid fuels such as wood, coal, peat or biomass pellets. It includes devices where the solid-fuel burner forms the primary heat-generating element for the air heater.
[F24H 3/12] with additional heating arrangements -- It relates to air heating units that include supplementary heating means in addition to the main heat-generating component, such as secondary electric or fuel burners, auxiliary heat exchangers or combined-source arrangements, aimed at boosting temperature output or performance.
[F24H 4/00] Fluid heaters characterised by the use of heat pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liquid or gas heaters using heat pumps as heat source.
[F24H 4/02] Water heaters -- F24H 4/02 covers water heating devices that employ heat-pump technology to raise water temperature. These systems belong to the fluid heaters subclass of heating apparatuses, where the heat-generating means is a heat pump rather than direct combustion or electric resistance.
[F24H 4/04] Storage heaters -- F24H 4/04 covers storage heaters, which are fluid heating devices that store thermal energy during periods of low demand or cheap electricity, typically using a heat-pump or similar source, and release the heat later to space or water.
[F24H 4/06] Air heaters -- F24H 4/06 pertains to air heaters that employ heat-pump technology to raise the temperature of air for space heating or ventilation. The classification includes devices in which the heating medium is air and the heat is generated by a heat pump rather than direct combustion or electric resistance.
[F24H 6/00] Combined water and air heaters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combined water and air heaters.
[F24H 7/00] Storage heaters, i.e. heaters in which the energy is stored as heat in masses for subsequent release -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Storage heaters ranked by the kind of the heat source, in which the heat storing material stays in the heater and wherein the released heat is either conveyed to a transfer fluid, e.g. air or water, or the released heat is radiated.
[F24H 7/002] using electrical energy supply -- F24H 7/002 covers storage heaters, where heat is stored in a mass for later release, that are supplied with electrical energy. The subclass falls under fluid heaters and heating in mechanical engineering, and includes devices such as electric storage water or air heaters using heat-generating means like heat pumps.
[F24H 7/005] using fluid fuel -- F24H 7/005 covers storage heating devices whose heat-generating source is a fluid fuel, such as oil, gas or other combustible liquids. It applies to systems that store thermal energy in a mass and later release it, falling under the hierarchy mechanical engineering → heating → fluid heaters → storage heaters. This subclass addresses the use of fluid fuel to charge the stored-heat reservoir for subsequent heating.
[F24H 7/007] using solid fuel -- F24H 7/007 covers fluid heating devices, such as water or air heaters, that obtain their heat from solid fuels like coal, wood or biomass. It applies to storage-type heaters where the combustion of a solid fuel supplies thermal energy that is stored in a mass for later release.
[F24H 7/02] the released heat being conveyed to a transfer fluid -- F24H 7/02 covers storage heaters in which the heat stored in a solid mass is released and conveyed to a transfer fluid such as water or air for distribution.
[F24H 7/0208] using electrical energy supply -- This subclass covers storage heaters that use an electrical energy supply to charge a thermal mass, storing heat for later release. It falls under mechanical-engineering heating, specifically fluid heaters with heat-generating means such as heat pumps, and pertains to devices that store heat in masses before distribution.
[F24H 7/0216] the transfer fluid being air -- The code refers to storage heating devices that retain thermal energy in a mass and employ air as the heat-transfer fluid. It is situated under mechanical-engineering heating, specifically fluid heaters with heat-generating means such as heat pumps.
[F24H 7/0225] with supplementary heating means -- F24H 7/0225 covers storage heaters that incorporate an auxiliary or supplementary heating element to provide extra heat when the stored thermal energy is insufficient, such as electric coils or secondary burners used alongside the primary heat-storing mass. This subclass falls under fluid heaters with heat-generating means in mechanical engineering.
[F24H 7/0233] the transfer fluid being water -- The subclass F24H 7/0233 covers storage heaters that use water as the heat-transfer fluid. It falls under mechanical-engineering heating, specifically fluid heaters with heat-generating means, and addresses designs where thermal energy is stored in a mass for later release.
[F24H 7/0241] with supplementary heating means -- This subgroup covers storage heaters that include an additional heating element to raise the temperature output when required. It applies to devices where the primary heat-storage medium is complemented by a secondary source such as an electric resistor, gas burner or similar supplementary heating means.
[F24H 7/025] using fluid fuel -- F24H 7/025 covers storage heating devices that generate heat by burning a fluid fuel, such as oil, gasoline or other liquid hydrocarbons, to store thermal energy in a mass for later release. This subclass falls under fluid heaters with heat-generating means and specifically addresses the use of fluid-based combustion in storage-type heaters.
[F24H 7/0258] the transfer fluid being air -- F24H 7/0258 covers storage heating systems that use air as the heat-transfer medium, meaning the stored thermal energy is moved and distributed by circulating air rather than a liquid. It includes designs where the heating apparatus, such as a heat pump or electric element, directly heats air that then passes through the storage mass for later release.
[F24H 7/0266] the transfer fluid being water -- F24H 7/0266 refers to fluid-heater designs in which water is used as the heat-transfer medium. It covers storage heating devices that circulate water to store and later release thermal energy.
[F24H 7/0275] using solid fuel -- F24H 7/0275 pertains to storage heating systems that employ solid fuel as the energy source. It falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters that store heat in a mass for later release. The subclass addresses designs and methods where solid fuels such as coal, wood, or pellets generate heat stored for subsequent distribution.
[F24H 7/0283] the transfer fluid being air -- F24H 7/0283 covers storage heaters that store thermal energy in a mass and use air as the heat-transfer fluid. The class is part of mechanical engineering heating, specifically fluid heaters such as water or air heaters with heat-generating means like heat pumps.
[F24H 7/0291] the transfer fluid being water -- This subclass covers storage-type fluid heaters in which water serves as the heat-transfer medium, specifying designs where the heating energy is stored in a mass and later released to the water that circulates to provide space heating.
[F24H 7/04] with forced circulation of the transfer fluid -- This subclass covers storage heaters that employ a forced circulation system to move the heat-transfer fluid, such as water or air, through the storage mass. The forced flow may be provided by pumps or fans, allowing controlled release of the stored thermal energy. Classified as F24H 7/04.
[F24H 7/0408] using electrical energy supply -- This subclass covers storage heaters that are powered by electricity, where the heater stores thermal energy in a mass and releases it later. It falls under fluid heaters in mechanical-engineering heating, specifically the category for heaters that use a heat-generating means such as heat pumps, focusing on electric energy supply for storage-type devices.
[F24H 7/0416] the transfer fluid being air -- F24H 7/0416 covers storage heaters that use air as the heat-transfer medium, meaning the thermal energy is stored in a mass and transferred to the surrounding space by circulating air. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters, and specifically storage heaters.
[F24H 7/0425] with supplementary heating means -- F24H 7/0425 pertains to storage heaters that include an extra heating element to provide supplementary heat, such as an electric or gas source, augmenting the primary heat stored in the mass for later release.
[F24H 7/0433] the transfer medium being water -- This subclass of CPC F24H 7/0433 pertains to storage heaters that use water as the heat-transfer medium, whereby heat stored in a mass is conveyed to the space via circulating water. It includes systems such as water-filled radiators or hydronic storage units often combined with heat-pump or other heat-generating sources.
[F24H 7/0441] with supplementary heating means -- F24H 7/0441 covers storage heaters-devices that store thermal energy in a mass for later release-that incorporate an additional or auxiliary heating element. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, heating, and fluid heaters with heat-generating means.

This falls under mechanical engineering, heating, fluid heaters and specifically storage heaters that employ air as the transfer fluid.

It pertains to systems that rely on water to convey and store thermal energy.

It applies to fluid heating systems where the heat-generating device uses solid fuel to charge the thermal store for later release.

[F24H 7/062] with electrical energy supply -- F24H 7/062 covers storage heaters that are supplied with electrical energy, charge a heat-storing mass and later release the stored heat for space heating.

[F24H 8/00] Fluid heaters characterised by means for extracting latent heat from flue gases by means of condensation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fluid heaters arranged in a self-contained manner:
[F24H 8/003] having means for moistening the combustion air with condensate from the combustion gases -- F24H 8/003 covers fluid heating systems such as water or air heaters that include a device for adding condensate from the combustion gases to the incoming combustion air. By moistening the air, the system extracts additional latent heat from the condensate, improving overall thermal efficiency.
[F24H 8/006] Means for removing condensate from the heater -- This subclass covers devices or arrangements that collect and discharge condensation formed inside fluid heaters that use latent-heat recovery from flue gases. It includes drainage, pumps, traps or overflow structures designed to prevent water accumulation and maintain efficient operation.

[F24H 9/0036] Dispositions against condensation of combustion products -- F24H 9/0036 covers design features in fluid heaters-such as water or air heating units with heat-generating means-that prevent condensation of combustion gases within the system, typically by separating exhaust streams, using drainage or temperature-control measures to keep the combustion products dry.

[F24H 9/0084] Combustion air preheating -- F24H 9/0084 covers equipment or methods that warm the air fed to a combustion chamber in fluid-heater systems such as water or air heaters. [F24H 9/0089] by double wall boiler mantle -- This subclass covers fluid heating devices that incorporate a double-wall boiler mantle as the heat-generating element, where the mantle forms an inner and outer enclosure to improve heat transfer and safety. [F24H 9/1827] Positive temperature coefficient [PTC] resistor -- F24H 9/1827 refers to fluid heating apparatus-water or air heaters, including heat-pump types-that employ a positive temperature coefficient (PTC) resistor as the heat-generating element. The PTC resistor automatically reduces current as its temperature rises, providing self-regulating temperature control and improved safety in heating systems.

[F24H 9/40] Arrangements for preventing corrosion -- F24H 9/40 designates arrangements integrated into fluid-heater systems, including water, steam or air heaters and heat pumps, that are intended to prevent or mitigate corrosion of the heating components. [F24S] SOLAR HEAT COLLECTORS -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy -> F03G6/00  **Glossary:** - Solar heat collector modules: whole solar heat collectors; part of solar heat collectors, e.g. reflectors, lens or heat storages - Absorbing elements: elements for absorbing solar-ray and converting it into heat - Solar heat systems: systems having solar heat collectors as their components and using the collected heat
[F24S 10/00] Solar heat collectors using working fluids -- F24S 10/00 covers solar heat collectors that employ a working fluid to absorb and transfer solar energy, including flat-plate, evacuated-tube, and other fluid-based designs where the fluid circulates to a heat-exchange system.
[F24S 10/10] the working fluids forming pools or ponds -- F24S 10/10 pertains to solar heat collector systems that employ a standing body of fluid, such as a pool or pond, as the working medium for heat absorption and transfer. These collectors rely on natural convection or direct solar heating of the fluid rather than circulating loops, and are used primarily for low-temperature heating or thermal storage.
[F24S 10/13] Salt-gradient ponds -- F24S 10/13 covers salt-gradient ponds, which are solar thermal collectors that store heat in a pond containing a stratified solution of sodium nitrate or similar salts, creating a temperature gradient that allows efficient heat extraction for industrial or power-generation processes. The system uses solar radiation to heat the upper layer, while the lower layers retain heat for later use.
[F24S 10/17] using covers or floating solar absorbing elements -- Solar heat collectors that use working fluids and incorporate protective covers or floating absorbing elements to enhance absorption and protect the fluid from the environment. The covers may be transparent or selective glazing, while floating absorbers are positioned on the fluid surface to capture solar radiation efficiently.
[F24S 10/20] having circuits for two or more working fluids -- The subclass F24S 10/20 in the Cooperative Patent Classification concerns solar heat collectors that incorporate separate circuits for two or more working fluids, enabling simultaneous circulation of different liquids or gases to transfer heat, often to improve overall efficiency or provide distinct temperature zones.
[F24S 10/25] having two or more passages for the same working fluid layered in direction of solar-rays, e.g. having upper circulation channels connected with lower circulation channels -- F24S 10/25 covers solar heat collectors that use a working fluid flowing through two or more passages arranged in layers in the direction of solar rays, with upper circulation channels linked to lower ones. This layered channel configuration improves heat capture and fluid circulation within the collector.
[F24S 10/30] with means for exchanging heat between two or more working fluids -- F24S 10/30 covers solar heat collectors that incorporate a system for transferring heat between two or more working fluids, such as a heat-transfer fluid and a secondary fluid for storage or distribution.
