[F24S 40/44] Draining rainwater or condensation -- F24S 40/44 covers systems or methods for draining rainwater or condensation from solar heat collectors, preventing fluid accumulation that could impair the collector's performance or cause safety hazards.
[F24S 40/46] Maintaining vacuum, e.g. by using getters -- This subclass covers techniques for preserving the vacuum inside solar heat collectors, such as employing getter materials that absorb residual gases to maintain low pressure. It includes designs and methods that ensure the collector's sealed environment remains intact during operation, improving efficiency and longevity.
[F24S 40/48] Deaerating or degassing the working fluid -- This subclass deals with methods and devices that remove dissolved gases from the fluid circulating in solar-thermal collectors, preventing corrosion, loss of efficiency and safety problems. It includes vacuum deaerators, venting systems, degassing chambers and related equipment used to purge air or non-condensable gases from the working fluid of solar heat collectors.
[F24S 40/50] Preventing overheating or overpressure -- The F24S 40/50 entry concerns safety or protection arrangements for solar heat collectors that prevent overheating or excess pressure. It covers design features, control systems and devices that monitor temperature and pressure, and mechanisms that automatically relieve or limit build-up to protect the collector and its surroundings.
[F24S 40/52] by modifying the heat collection, e.g. by defocusing or by changing the position of heat-receiving elements -- This safety or protection arrangement for solar heat collectors modifies the way heat is gathered, for example by defocusing the collector optics or relocating the heat-receiving elements, thereby limiting temperature rise and preventing overheating.
[F24S 40/53] by venting solar heat collector enclosures -- The entry covers safety or protection arrangements for solar thermal collectors that rely on venting the collector enclosure. It includes designs, systems and methods that vent heat, gases or pressure to prevent overheating, fire hazard or damage to the collector and surrounding equipment.
[F24S 40/55] Arrangements for cooling, e.g. by using external heat dissipating means or internal cooling circuits -- F24S 40/55 covers cooling measures for solar heat collectors, including external heat-dissipating devices or internal cooling circuits, designed to remove excess heat and maintain safe operation of the collector system.
[F24S 40/57] Preventing overpressure in solar collector enclosures -- The subclass F24S 40/57 covers safety arrangements that protect solar heat collector enclosures from excessive internal pressure. It includes devices such as pressure-relief valves, burst discs or venting systems designed to prevent damage or failure due to overpressure. These measures are part of the broader category of heating and solar heat collectors.
[F24S 40/58] Preventing overpressure in working fluid circuits -- This classification covers safety measures that stop excess pressure from building up in the fluid circuits of solar heat collectors. It includes devices such as pressure relief valves, burst discs and control systems designed to protect the collector and its components from over-pressure damage.
[F24S 40/60] Arrangements for draining the working fluid -- This CPC subclass covers protective features for solar thermal systems that enable safe removal of the heat-transfer fluid, such as drain valves, overflow channels, or automatic emptying mechanisms designed to prevent damage or hazards when the collector is shut down or exposed to extreme conditions.
[F24S 40/70] Preventing freezing -- F24S 40/70 covers safety or protection measures that prevent freezing in solar heat collectors. It includes designs, devices or methods that protect collector fluid from freezing damage, such as heat exchangers, drainback systems, antifreeze circuits, or insulation strategies.
[F24S 40/80] Accommodating differential expansion of solar collector elements -- The CPC entry F24S 40/80 covers safety or protection measures for solar heat collectors that address differential expansion of collector components, ensuring structural integrity and preventing damage or failure caused by temperature-induced dimensional changes. It pertains to designs that allow collector elements to expand or contract without compromising the system's performance or safety.
[F24S 40/85] Arrangements for protecting solar collectors against adverse weather conditions  F24S40/10  takes precedence -- This classification covers design features that shield solar thermal collectors from rain, wind, snow and hail, ensuring reliable operation and longevity. It includes protective housings, covers, gaskets and drainage systems that prevent moisture ingress and physical damage. The class is subordinate to F24S 40/10, which deals with general safety or protection arrangements for solar heat collectors.
[F24S 40/90] Arrangements for testing solar heat collectors -- This classification covers the methods, apparatus and procedures used to evaluate the performance, efficiency, durability and safety of solar heat collectors, including test rigs, measurement techniques and standardised test conditions.
[F24S 50/00] Arrangements for controlling solar heat collectors -- F24S 50/00 covers devices, methods and control systems that regulate the operation of solar heat collectors, such as temperature-control loops, flow-rate regulators, tracking mechanisms and safety shut-off arrangements, enabling efficient and safe conversion of solar energy into usable heat.
[F24S 50/20] for tracking -- This subclass deals with devices and systems that orient solar heat collectors to follow the sun's apparent motion, thereby increasing thermal gain. It includes mechanical, electromechanical or passive tracking mechanisms that adjust the collector's position relative to solar irradiation.
[F24S 50/40] responsive to temperature -- This subclass covers devices and methods that adjust the operation of solar heat collectors in response to temperature, such as thermostatic controls, automatic valve actuators, and feedback systems that regulate fluid flow or collector orientation.
[F24S 50/60] responsive to wind -- F24S 50/60 covers devices and methods that modify the performance of solar heat collectors in response to wind, such as automatic shutters, vents or tracking mechanisms that open, close or re-orient the collector to reduce losses or enhance gain under windy conditions.
[F24S 50/80] for controlling collection or absorption of solar radiation -- The subclass covers arrangements that regulate the capture or absorption of solar radiation in solar heat collectors, such as tracking, shading, concentration or other control mechanisms that adjust the collector's performance.
[F24S 60/00] Arrangements for storing heat collected by solar heat collectors -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements for storing heat collected by solar heat collectors in the working fluids forming pools or ponds -> F24S10/10
[F24S 60/10] using latent heat -- This subclass covers technologies that store solar thermal energy by converting it to latent heat in phase-change materials or fluids, enabling the heat to be retained and released later. It is applied in solar heating systems where the collected heat is stored in a latent-heat medium for subsequent use.
[F24S 60/20] using chemical reactions, e.g. thermochemical reactions or isomerisation reactions -- F24S 60/20 covers solar-heat collector systems that store the collected thermal energy by means of reversible chemical reactions, such as thermochemical or isomerisation processes, enabling high-temperature storage and later heat release. It is a subcategory of mechanical-engineering heating arrangements for storing heat gathered by solar collectors.
[F24S 60/30] storing heat in liquids -- The F24S 60/30 classification covers arrangements that store thermal energy collected by solar heat collectors using liquid media. It includes systems such as water-based tanks, thermal oil reservoirs or phase-change liquid storage that retain heat for later use in heating or power applications. This falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, heating, and solar heat collectors.
[F24S 70/00] Details of absorbing elements -- In the CPC, code F24S 70/00 concerns the design and construction of the absorber component of solar thermal collectors. It includes specifications of the material, surface treatment, geometry and mounting of the absorbing elements that capture solar radiation for heat generation.
[F24S 70/10] characterised by the absorbing material -- F24S 70/10 concerns solar heat collectors in which the performance is defined by the properties of the absorber material. It covers designs that specify the type of selective coating, metallic or ceramic layers, surface texture or other material characteristics that enhance solar absorption and reduce thermal losses. The subclass is used for patents describing how the absorber composition or treatment improves collector efficiency.
[F24S 70/12] made of metallic material -- The code F24S 70/12 covers solar-heat collectors in which the absorber element is constructed from metallic material, focusing on the design and performance of such metal absorbers within heating systems.
[F24S 70/14] made of plastics -- The code F24S 70/14 refers to solar-heat collectors and specifically to the design and construction details of the absorbing elements when they are made of plastics, covering material selection, shaping, and integration within the collector system.
[F24S 70/16] made of ceramic -- This subclass covers the material characteristics of absorbing components in solar thermal collectors when the absorbing surface is formed from ceramic. It includes specifications on composition, structure and thermal properties of ceramic absorbers used to capture solar radiation.
[F24S 70/20] characterised by absorbing coatings -- F24S 70/20 pertains to solar heat collectors whose absorbing elements are provided with specialised coatings that increase solar radiation capture while reducing thermal losses. Such coatings may be selective, anti-reflective, anti-oxidative or nanostructured and are applied to metal, glass or polymer surfaces to improve collector efficiency.
[F24S 70/225] for spectrally selective absorption -- F24S 70/225 covers solar heat collectors whose absorbing surface is engineered to preferentially absorb radiation of specific wavelengths while reflecting others, enhancing conversion efficiency. This spectrally selective absorption reduces radiative heat losses, and the class addresses the design details of the absorbing elements in solar heating systems.
[F24S 70/25] Coatings made of metallic material -- F24S 70/25 covers metallic material coatings applied to the absorber components of solar heat collectors. These coatings are used to improve solar radiation absorption, protect against corrosion and enhance the thermal efficiency of the collector's absorbing elements.
[F24S 70/275] Coatings made of plastics -- F24S 70/275 covers coatings made of plastics that are applied to the absorbing elements of solar heat collectors. The classification is used for inventions relating to protective or functional plastic layers that improve durability, corrosion resistance, or heat absorption efficiency in solar thermal systems.
[F24S 70/30] Auxiliary coatings, e.g. anti-reflective coatings -- F24S 70/30 covers auxiliary coatings applied to the absorbing elements of solar heat collectors, such as anti-reflective layers that enhance light capture and reduce thermal losses, improving overall collector efficiency.
[F24S 70/60] characterised by the structure or construction -- The subclass F24S 70/60 groups solar-heat collector absorber elements whose novelty lies in the way they are built or arranged. It covers inventions defined by particular structural features, construction methods, material choices, geometry or mounting of the absorbing component within a solar collector.
[F24S 70/65] Combinations of two or more absorbing elements -- This subclass concerns solar heat collector designs that integrate two or more distinct absorbing components to improve energy capture. It covers arrangements where different absorber types are combined within a single collector, such as mixing flat plates with evacuated tubes.
[F24S 80/00] Details, accessories or component parts of solar heat collectors not provided for in groups -- This class covers miscellaneous parts, accessories and detailed components for solar heat collectors that are not otherwise listed in the specific sub-groups. It includes items such as mounting hardware, control devices, connectors and protective fittings used in solar thermal systems.
[F24S 80/10] Materials for heat-exchange conduits -- F24S 80/10 pertains to the specific materials employed for the fluid-carrying conduits in solar heat-collector systems. It includes metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics and composites chosen for their thermal conductivity, durability and resistance to corrosion, and covers any conduit material not classified in other groups.
[F24S 80/20] Working fluids specially adapted for solar heat collectors -- It covers fluids specifically formulated for use in solar thermal collectors, including heat-transfer liquids, oils, molten salts and nanofluids that possess properties such as low freezing point, high thermal stability and optimal viscosity to improve energy capture and storage in solar heating systems.
[F24S 80/30] Arrangements for connecting the fluid circuits of solar collectors with each other or with other components, e.g. pipe connections -- F24S 80/30 covers the design and arrangement of fluid-circuit connections for solar heat collectors, including pipework and other means of linking collector loops to each other or to auxiliary components such as storage tanks, pumps or heat exchangers, when not covered by more specific groups.
[F24S 80/40] Casings -- The subclass F24S 80/40 designates casings that serve as details, accessories or component parts of solar heat collectors and are not otherwise listed in related groups. It pertains to mechanical-engineering heating technologies, specifically to the structural enclosures that protect or support solar-collector systems.
[F24S 80/45] characterised by the material -- F24S 80/45 refers to accessories, details or component parts of solar heat collectors that are defined chiefly by the material from which they are made, rather than by their function or shape. It includes items such as frames, tubes, covers or mounting elements whose specific material composition distinguishes them from other collector parts not covered in the main groups.
[F24S 80/453] made of metallic material -- This CPC subclass covers metallic details, accessories or component parts of solar heat collectors that are not listed in other groups. It is situated under mechanical engineering, heating, solar heat collectors and pertains specifically to items made of metal.
[F24S 80/457] made of plastics -- The entry covers plastic parts, accessories or detail components of solar heat collectors that are not listed elsewhere, such as plastic housings, fittings, covers or insulating elements used in solar thermal systems.
[F24S 80/50] Elements for transmitting incoming solar rays and preventing outgoing heat radiation -- The code F24S 80/50 covers components of solar thermal collectors that channel incoming sunlight into the absorber while limiting heat loss, such as transparent covers, mirrors, selective coatings or reflective shields. These elements are not listed in other sub-groups and are essential for improving collector efficiency.
[F24S 80/52] characterised by the material -- The F24S 80/52 subclass groups accessories or component parts of solar heat collectors that are distinguished primarily by the material they are made of, such as metal frames, polymer tubes, or glass glazing, and which are not covered in other, more specific groups within the solar-collector section.
[F24S 80/525] made of plastics -- This subclass covers plastic components, accessories or detail parts used in solar heat-collector systems that are not listed in any other groups within the solar-collector category of mechanical engineering. It applies specifically to items made of plastics that form part of a solar heating collector assembly.
[F24S 80/54] using evacuated elements -- F24S 80/54 covers accessories or component parts for solar-heat collectors that employ evacuated elements such as vacuum-filled tubes, chambers or housings designed to improve thermal insulation. It includes items not specifically listed in other solar-collector groups.
[F24S 80/56] characterised by means for preventing heat loss -- F24S 80/56 relates to components or accessories for solar heat collectors that are specifically designed to reduce heat loss, such as insulation layers, reflective shields, glazing or sealing elements. It covers items not classified elsewhere in the solar-collector groups.
[F24S 80/58] characterised by their mountings or fixing means -- F24S 80/58 covers accessories and component parts of solar heat collectors that are defined by their mounting or fixing means, such as brackets, clamps, frames or other support structures used to attach the collector to a surface or building, and are not covered in other groups.
[F24S 80/60] Thermal insulation -- F24S 80/60 refers to thermal-insulation items that serve as details, accessories or component parts of solar heat collectors and are not covered by other groups. It includes any material or device used to minimise heat loss in solar thermal systems, fitting within the broader hierarchy of mechanical engineering, heating and solar heat collectors.
[F24S 80/65] characterised by the material -- The CPC subclass F24S 80/65 covers details, accessories or component parts of solar heat collectors that are distinguished primarily by the material from which they are made, such as frames, glazing, absorbers or heat-transfer elements of specific metals, polymers, glass or composites. It applies to parts not otherwise allocated to more specific groups within the solar-heat-collector category.
[F24S 80/70] Sealing means -- Sealing means are accessories or component parts that provide watertight or airtight closure for solar heat collectors, preventing fluid or gas leakage at joints, gaps or openings. They are used in solar thermal systems where specific sealing solutions are not covered by other classification groups.
[F24S 90/00] Solar heat systems not otherwise provided for -- F24S 90/00 covers solar heat systems that do not fall under any more specific solar-collector categories, including combined or auxiliary installations, control devices, and overall system arrangements not otherwise classified. It pertains to the design, integration, and operation of solar heating equipment that is not specifically described in other subclasses of the solar collector group.
[F24S 90/10] using thermosiphonic circulation -- F24S 90/10 pertains to solar heat systems that rely on thermosiphonic circulation, i.e., passive movement of the heat-transfer fluid driven by temperature-induced density differences, without mechanical pumps. The classification falls under mechanical engineering - heating - solar heat collectors - solar heat systems not otherwise provided for.
[F24T] GEOTHERMAL COLLECTORS -- F24T covers geothermal collectors, devices that capture heat from the Earth's subsurface for heating or power generation. These systems typically include ground heat exchangers such as horizontal or vertical loops and are used in residential, commercial and industrial applications to provide space heating, hot water or to feed heat-pump installations.
[F24T 10/00] Geothermal collectors -- F24T 10/00 covers geothermal collectors, devices that extract thermal energy from the ground for heating, cooling, or power generation. It includes ground-coupled heat exchangers, bore-hole loops, and flat-plate or trench collectors that transfer earth heat to a working fluid. The class addresses the structural, material, and fluid-flow features of such systems.
[F24T 10/10] with circulation of working fluids through underground channels, the working fluids not coming into direct contact with the ground -- A geothermal collector in which a closed-loop network of underground channels carries a working fluid to extract heat from the earth, the fluid never coming into direct contact with the ground, and the heat is transferred to the heating system via a heat-exchange process.
[F24T 10/13] using tube assemblies suitable for insertion into boreholes in the ground, e.g. geothermal probes -- F24T 10/13 covers methods that employ tube assemblies designed for placement in underground boreholes, such as geothermal probes, to extract or transfer heat from the earth's subsurface for heating or energy-generation applications.
[F24T 10/15] using bent tubes -- F24T 10/15 classifies inventions that employ bent tubes as components of geothermal heat-collecting systems. It covers designs where curved tubing enhances fluid flow or heat exchange within solar-thermal or ground-source collectors, a subcategory of heating technologies in mechanical engineering.
[F24T 10/17] using tubes closed at one end, i.e. return-type tubes -- The sub-group F24T 10/17 concerns geothermal heating collectors that use tubes closed at one end, forming a return-type flow path for the heat-transfer fluid. This design improves heat exchange by directing the fluid back through the same tube, and falls under mechanical-engineering heating systems for geothermal energy collection.
[F24T 10/20] using underground water as working fluid -- F24T 10/20 covers geothermal collector systems that employ natural underground water as the heat-transfer medium. The classification applies to installations where water extracted from aquifers or subsoil is circulated through heat exchangers to supply heating or cooling.
[F24T 10/30] using underground reservoirs for accumulating working fluids or intermediate fluids -- F24T 10/30 pertains to geothermal collector systems that store the working or intermediate fluid in underground reservoirs such as aquifers or rock formations, using the subsurface as a thermal storage medium. It covers methods for injecting, retaining, and extracting the fluid to accumulate heat and supply it to surface heating circuits.
[F24T 10/40] operated without external energy sources, e.g. using thermosiphonic circulation or heat pipes
[F24T 2010/50] Component parts, details or accessories -- F24T 2010/50 covers the component parts, details or accessories of air-conditioning systems, including fans, filters, ducts, control units, thermostats and other subsidiary elements that form or support the main equipment. It is used to classify inventions that improve or modify these ancillary items.
[F24T 2010/53] Methods for installation -- This subclass covers the procedures and techniques employed to install heating, ventilation or air-conditioning equipment or components, including site preparation, mounting, connection, sealing, testing and commissioning steps required to bring the system into operational condition.
[F24T 2010/56] Control arrangements -- The CPC subclass F24T 2010/56 falls under the broader F24T group for heating, ventilating, air-conditioning and refrigeration installations and specifically covers control arrangements. It includes devices, circuits and methods that regulate temperature, humidity, airflow or related parameters in such systems.
[F24T 2201/00] Prediction -- F24T 2201/00 covers methods and models for predicting the performance, efficiency, heat output, or behavior of geothermal heating collectors. It includes analytical calculations, numerical simulations, and data-based forecasting techniques used to assess system design, sizing, and operation under varying environmental and load conditions. This subclass is part of the broader groups dealing with heating and geothermal collector technologies.
[F24T 50/00] Geothermal systems -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for producing mechanical power from geothermal energy -> F03G4/00
[F24V] COLLECTION, PRODUCTION OR USE OF HEAT NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- The F24V subclass groups inventions that involve the collection, generation, storage, transfer or utilization of heat that is not specifically covered by other heating categories. It includes unconventional or hybrid heating methods, heat-recovery processes, and systems for managing thermal energy in ways that do not fit existing more detailed CPC groups.
[F24V 30/00] Apparatus or devices using heat produced by exothermal chemical reactions other than combustion -- This subclass covers devices that generate and apply heat from chemical reactions that release energy without burning, such as certain fuel cells or polymerization reactors. It includes apparatus that harness exothermic reactions for heating, drying, or temperature control, excluding conventional combustion systems.
[F24V 40/00] Production or use of heat resulting from internal friction of moving fluids or from friction between fluids and moving bodies -- F24V 40/00 covers technologies that generate or exploit heat created by the internal friction of flowing liquids or gases, or by the friction between those fluids and moving components such as pumps, turbines, mixers or conveyors. It includes methods for capturing, transferring or using this friction-induced thermal energy.
[F24V 40/10] the fluid passing through restriction means -- This subgroup deals with heating produced when a fluid is forced through a restriction such as an orifice, valve, nozzle or porous plug, where the pressure drop generates heat by internal friction. It includes devices or methods that intentionally use this effect for heating or energy recovery.
[F24V 50/00] Use of heat from natural sources, e.g. from the sea -- F24V 50/00 covers technologies that capture and utilize heat from natural sources such as seawater, geothermal reservoirs, or solar-heated bodies of water for heating, cooling, or power generation. It includes systems that directly transfer this ambient thermal energy to buildings, industrial processes, or marine applications without additional artificial heating.
[F24V 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other main groups of this subclass -- F24V 99/00 pertains to heating technologies whose subject matter does not fall within any other main group of subclass F24V. It is used for devices, methods or systems for collecting, producing or using heat that are not specifically addressed elsewhere in the mechanical-engineering heating class.
[F25] REFRIGERATION OR COOLING -- F25 is the CPC subclass for refrigeration or cooling technologies, covering inventions that produce, maintain, or transfer low temperatures. It includes patents on systems, components and methods such as chillers, freezers, heat pumps and related heat-removal processes. The class is applied to mechanical engineering solutions for cooling spaces, substances or equipment.
[F25B] REFRIGERATION MACHINES, PLANTS OR SYSTEMS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refrigeration machines, plants or systems; combined heating and refrigeration systems; heat-pump systems.  Cold production at temperatures below ambient, not transportation of heat.
[F25B 1/00] Compression machines, plants or systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compression machines, plants or systems with non-reversible cycle.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Self-contained rotary compression machines, i.e. with compressor, condenser, and evaporator rotating as a single unit -> F25B3/00 - Compression machines, plants or systems, with several evaporator circuits, e.g. for varying refrigerating capacity -> F25B5/00 - Compression machines, plants or systems, with several condenser circuits -> F25B6/00 - Compression machines, plants or systems, with cascade operation, i.e. with two or more circuits, the heat from the condenser of one circuit being absorbed by the evaporator of the next circuit -> F25B7/00 - Compression machines, plants or systems, in which the refrigerant is air or other gas of low boiling point -> F25B9/00
[F25B 1/005] of the single unit type  F25B1/10  takes precedence
[F25B 1/02] with compressor of reciprocating-piston type -- This subclass concerns refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that employ a compressor of the reciprocating-piston type, where refrigerant is compressed by pistons moving back and forth within cylinders, a common arrangement in many domestic and industrial chillers.
[F25B 1/04] with compressor of rotary type -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of the single unit type -> F25B1/005 - With multi-stage compression -> F25B1/10
[F25B 1/047] of screw type -- F25B 1/047 covers screw-type compressors used in refrigeration or cooling systems, including the design and operation of screw rotors that compress refrigerant gas. It pertains to mechanical engineering of refrigeration machinery and plant components that employ the screw principle for compression.
[F25B 1/053] of turbine type -- Turbine-type compression machines for refrigeration or cooling systems use a rotating turbine or impeller to raise the pressure of the refrigerant, converting kinetic energy directly into compression rather than employing pistons, scrolls or other mechanisms. The subclass covers designs in which the working fluid expands across turbine blades and is discharged at higher pressure for use in chillers, heat pumps or air-conditioning plants.
[F25B 1/06] with compressor of jet type, e.g. using liquid under pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ejectors operating as compressor.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of the single unit type -> F25B1/005 - With multi-stage compression -> F25B1/10
[F25B 1/08] using vapour under pressure -- The F25B 1/08 subclass covers refrigeration or cooling systems that employ vapour under pressure as the working medium to produce compression. It includes designs where pressurised steam or other vapour drives the compressor, either directly or through intermediate mechanisms, to achieve cooling in machines, plants or system installations.
[F25B 1/10] with multi-stage compression -- F25B 1/10 covers refrigeration or cooling machines that employ more than one compression stage to raise the refrigerant pressure. Multi-stage compression improves efficiency and enables higher pressure ratios, and is commonly used in large-capacity chillers and heat-pump systems.
[F25B 11/00] Compression machines, plants or systems, using turbines, e.g. gas turbines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compression machines, plant, or systems, using turbines, e.g. gas turbines.
[F25B 11/02] as expanders -- The code F25B 11/02 designates turbine-driven expanders used in refrigeration or cooling systems, where the turbine acts as a compression machine to expand high-pressure fluid and produce mechanical work or a cooling effect. It is classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, refrigeration machines, plants or systems, and compression machines that employ turbines such as gas turbines.
[F25B 11/04] centrifugal type -- F25B 11/04 covers centrifugal compressors used in refrigeration or cooling systems, where the compression of refrigerant is achieved by a rotating impeller that imparts kinetic energy to the gas, which is then converted to pressure. The classification applies to machines, plants or complete systems that employ this centrifugal type for gas compression, typically in large-scale or industrial refrigeration installations.
[F25B 13/00] Compression machines, plants or systems, with reversible cycle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compression machines, plant, or systems with reversible cycle: both condenser and evaporator change function when reversing the reversing valve.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Defrosting cycles: -> F25B47/02
[F25B 15/00] Sorption machines, plants or systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sorption machines, plants or systems, operating continuously, e.g. absorption type.  vaporization of the refrigerant; absorption of the vapour by an absorbing liquid; extraction of the refrigerant vapour by heating this liquid; condensation of the refrigerant; expansion of the refrigerant.
[F25B 15/002] using the endothermic solution of salt -- The CPC subclass F25B 15/002 pertains to sorption refrigeration or cooling systems that generate cooling by using an endothermic salt solution. The process involves dissolving a salt in a solvent to absorb heat, which drives the refrigeration cycle in absorption or adsorption machines. This approach is employed in chillers and cooling plants where the salt solution acts as the refrigerant-absorbent medium.
[F25B 15/004] of rotary type -- F25B 15/004 classifies sorption refrigeration or cooling machines that employ a rotary configuration, where the working solid or liquid moves in a rotating chamber to facilitate the absorption, adsorption or desorption processes. It is a narrow subdivision of sorption machines, plants or systems within the mechanical engineering and refrigeration categories.
[F25B 15/006] with cascade operation -- F25B 15/006 covers sorption refrigeration machines, plants or systems that incorporate cascade operation, where two or more refrigeration cycles are linked in series so that the condenser of the higher-temperature cycle serves as the evaporator of the lower-temperature cycle, improving overall efficiency and enabling lower temperature levels.
[F25B 15/008] with multi-stage operation  F25B15/006  takes precedence -- This CPC subgroup refers to sorption refrigeration machines, plants or systems that employ more than one stage in the absorption or adsorption cycle to improve performance, such as using successive sorbent beds or multiple heat exchangers. It refines the broader category F25B 15/006 which covers sorption devices in general.
[F25B 15/02] without inert gas -- F25B 15/02 relates to sorption refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that operate without an inert gas, where the refrigerant is directly absorbed or adsorbed by a sorbent material in the cycle.
[F25B 15/025] Liquid transfer means -- F25B 15/025 refers to the liquid-transfer devices used in sorption refrigeration machines, plants or systems, such as pumps, valves, capillary tubes or other means that move refrigerant, absorbent or other working fluids between circuit components. The code situates the feature within mechanical-engineering refrigerating technology, specifically under sorption-type cooling equipment.
[F25B 15/04] the refrigerant being ammonia evaporated from aqueous solution -- This subclass deals with refrigeration systems that employ an aqueous ammonia solution as the refrigerant, where ammonia is evaporated from the liquid mixture to provide cooling. It includes designs and methods for extracting the refrigerant from water-based solutions in sorption or absorption machines.
[F25B 15/06] the refrigerant being water vapour evaporated from a salt solution, e.g. lithium bromide -- This subclass covers sorption refrigeration systems that use water vapour as the refrigerant, generated by evaporating it from a salt solution such as lithium bromide. It applies to machines, plants or systems where the absorption-desorption cycle provides cooling.
[F25B 15/08] the refrigerant being sulfuric acid -- F25B 15/08 covers sorption refrigeration machines, plants or systems that employ sulfuric acid as the refrigerant, including the design, operation and components of such installations. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under refrigeration, specifically the subgroup for sorption-type cooling equipment.
[F25B 15/09] the refrigerant being hydrogen desorbed from a hydride -- This sub-group covers refrigeration systems that employ hydrogen released from metal hydrides as the refrigerant, including methods and apparatus for desorbing hydrogen from a hydride to provide cooling in sorption cycles.
[F25B 15/10] with inert gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Absorption systems which, in addition to refrigerant and absorbent, also employs an inert medium (such as hydrogen) in order to balance pressure in the various parts of the refrigerating circuit  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of rotary type -> F25B15/004 - With cascade operation -> F25B15/006 - With multi-stage operation -> F25B15/008 - With resorber -> F25B15/12 - Using osmosis -> F25B15/14 - Using desorption cycle -> F25B15/16
[F25B 15/12] with resorber -- F25B 15/12 pertains to sorption refrigeration machines, plants or systems that incorporate a resorber, a component that absorbs the refrigerant after it is released from the sorbent to enable regeneration of the cycle. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the refrigeration and cooling domain that uses heat-driven sorption processes.
[F25B 15/14] using osmosis -- F25B 15/14 relates to refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that employ osmosis as the sorption mechanism. It includes devices in which a solvent passes through a semipermeable membrane, creating a pressure or concentration gradient that drives heat absorption for cooling.
[F25B 15/16] using desorption cycle -- F25B 15/16 relates to refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that use a sorption process where heat drives the desorption of a refrigerant from an adsorbent or absorbent, providing cooling without a conventional compressor.
[F25B 17/00] Sorption machines, plants or systems, operating intermittently, e.g. absorption or adsorption type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sorption machines, plants or systems with alternating periods of vaporization and condensation.
[F25B 17/02] the absorbent or adsorbent being a liquid, e.g. brine -- In intermittent sorption refrigeration machines and plants, this subclass deals with configurations where the absorbent or adsorbent is a liquid, for example a brine solution. It is part of the hierarchy covering mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and sorption machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 17/04] with two or more boilers operating alternately -- F25B 17/04 covers sorption refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that run intermittently and use two or more boilers operating alternately to supply the needed heat. The alternating boiler arrangement improves efficiency by allowing one boiler to heat while the other cools.
[F25B 17/06] with the boiler and evaporator built-up as a unit in a tiltable or revolving arrangement -- This subclass covers sorption refrigeration or cooling systems where the boiler and evaporator are integrated into a single unit that can be tilted or rotated, enabling gravity-assisted fluid flow or convenient positioning of the heat-exchange components within the machine.
[F25B 17/08] the absorbent or adsorbent being a solid, e.g. salt -- The code F25B 17/08 classifies refrigeration or cooling machines that work by sorption, where the absorbent or adsorbent is a solid material such as a salt and the system operates intermittently in absorption or adsorption cycles.
[F25B 17/083] with two or more boiler-sorbers operating alternately -- This classification covers sorption refrigeration plants that employ two or more boiler-sorber units which are operated in an alternating cycle to provide continuous cooling. The alternating operation allows one unit to generate heat while another absorbs it, enhancing overall system performance.
[F25B 17/086] with two or more boiler-sorber/evaporator units -- F25B 17/086 covers sorption refrigeration or cooling machines that operate in a batch or intermittent mode and incorporate two or more combined boiler-sorber and evaporator units. The configuration is typical of absorption or adsorption systems where each unit alternately provides heating for desorption and cooling for evaporation, enabling continuous output despite the cyclic process.
[F25B 17/10] using the endothermic solution of salt -- F25B 17/10 covers intermittent sorption refrigeration or cooling systems that employ an endothermic salt solution as the working fluid, such as absorption or adsorption cycles where the salt dissolves or reacts with water or another medium to create a cooling effect. This subclass is part of the broader mechanical-engineering field for refrigeration machines and plants that use sorption processes rather than conventional compressors.
[F25B 17/12] using desorption of hydrogen from a hydride -- F25B 17/12 pertains to sorption refrigeration or cooling systems that operate intermittently by releasing hydrogen from a metal-hydride material. The classification covers methods and apparatus that use the desorption of hydrogen from a hydride as the driving thermodynamic step in absorption- or adsorption-type refrigeration cycles.
[F25B 19/00] Machines, plants or systems, with a single mode of operation, not covered by groups -- F25B 19/00 relates to refrigeration machines, plants or systems that carry out a single mode of operation-such as only cooling, only heating, or another isolated function-and are not otherwise classified in the more specific groups of the refrigeration subgroup.
[F25B 19/005] the refrigerant being a liquefied gas -- F25B 19/005 relates to refrigeration machines, plants or systems that employ a liquefied gas as the refrigerant. It covers designs where the refrigerant is introduced and circulated in a liquid state under pressure, distinguishing them from systems using vapor-compression or other cooling cycles.
[F25B 19/02] using fluid jet, e.g. of steam -- F25B 19/02 covers refrigeration or cooling apparatus that employs a fluid jet, such as steam, to create a cooling effect, enhance heat transfer, or drive a single-mode refrigeration cycle. The jet may be used for direct cooling of a space or to energize a secondary cooling medium.
[F25B 19/04] using liquid jet, e.g. of water -- F25B 19/04 covers refrigeration or cooling machines that employ a liquid jet, for example water, as a means of heat transfer or fluid circulation, primarily in single-mode installations not classified elsewhere.
[F25B 21/00] Machines, plants or systems, using electric or magnetic effects -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines, plants or systems, using electric or magnetic effects.
[F25B 21/02] using Peltier effect -- F25B 21/02 covers refrigeration machines, plants or systems that achieve cooling or heating by employing the Peltier effect, a thermoelectric phenomenon where an electric current creates a temperature difference across junctions of dissimilar materials. It is a subclass within mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and devices that use electric or magnetic effects.
[F25B 21/04] reversible -- The F25B 21/04 subclass covers reversible refrigeration, heat-pump or air-conditioning machines and systems that operate using electric or magnetic effects, allowing the direction of heat flow to be switched. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 23/00] Machines, plants or systems, with a single mode of operation not covered by groups -- F25B 23/00 classifies refrigeration machines, plants or systems that operate in a single mode and that do not fall into any more specific sub-groups defined elsewhere in the refrigeration section. It is used for devices whose primary function is cooling or heat-pumping but whose design or operation is not covered by the detailed groups below it. This entry captures miscellaneous single-mode refrigeration equipment within mechanical engineering.
[F25B 2300/00] Indexing scheme associated with groups -- F25B 2300/00 designates the indexing scheme that defines the hierarchical groupings for patents in the refrigeration machines, plants or systems subclass. It is used to organise and navigate the detailed technical categories within this mechanical-engineering field, facilitating consistent citation and searching of related inventions.
[F25B 23/003] using selective radiation effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling by radiation to the night sky, e.g. by selective coating on a (night sky) radiator.
[F25B 23/006] boiling cooling systems -- F25B 23/006 covers refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that use a boiling process to absorb heat, where a liquid refrigerant vaporises to produce the cooling effect. It includes designs in which boiling is the primary mode of operation and is not covered by more specific groups.
[F25B 2309/00] Gas cycle refrigeration machines -- F25B 2309/00 refers to refrigeration machines that operate on a gas-cycle principle, such as vapour-compression or absorption systems. The code covers devices where a working fluid circulates through components like compressors, condensers, evaporators and expansion devices to produce cooling.
[F25B 2309/001] with a linear configuration or a linear motor -- The CPC subclass F25B 2309/001 refers to gas-cycle refrigeration machines that employ a linear arrangement or a linear motor to drive the compressor or other moving parts. It falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigeration machines, plants or systems → gas-cycle refrigeration machines.
[F25B 2309/002] with parallel working cold producing expansion devices in one circuit -- This subclass covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines that employ two or more expansion devices operating in parallel within a single circuit to produce the cold effect. The arrangement allows simultaneous expansion processes, improving capacity or efficiency while sharing a common refrigerant loop.
[F25B 2309/003] characterised by construction or composition of the regenerator -- F25B 2309/003 covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines where the regenerator is defined by its particular construction or material makeup, such as the arrangement of heat-exchange surfaces or the use of specific alloys or composites. The classification applies to designs that modify the regenerator to improve efficiency, durability, or manufacturing.
[F25B 2309/004] using a compressor of the rotary type -- F25B 2309/004 relates to gas-cycle refrigeration machines that employ a rotary-type compressor. It covers the design and operation of refrigeration systems where the compressor's rotating mechanism provides the pressure increase needed for the refrigeration cycle, fitting within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2309/005] using an expander of the rotary type -- F25B 2309/005 covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines that incorporate a rotary-type expander to reduce pressure of the refrigerant. The classification is used for systems where a rotating expander replaces conventional throttling devices, improving efficiency in cooling or refrigeration cycles. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration, and specific refrigeration machine categories.
[F25B 2309/006] using a distributing valve of the rotary type -- This CPC subclass F25B 2309/006 covers the use of a rotary-type distributing valve in gas-cycle refrigeration machines such as compressors, condensers, or evaporators. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems. The classification pertains to designs that employ a rotary valve to control refrigerant flow within the cycle.
[F25B 2309/02] using the Joule-Thompson effect -- The subclass concerns gas-cycle refrigeration machines that achieve cooling by expanding a high-pressure gas through a throttling or valve portion, exploiting the Joule-Thompson effect. It includes equipment where this thermodynamic principle is used to lower temperature in the refrigeration cycle.
[F25B 2309/021] with a cryosurgical probe tip having a specific construction -- The CPC subclass F25B 2309/021 covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines that integrate a cryosurgical probe tip designed with a particular construction. It applies to cooling systems where the refrigerant circuit directly powers a surgical probe for low-temperature tissue treatment, specifying the tip's structural features that enable precise cryogenic operation.
[F25B 2309/022] characterised by the expansion element -- This CPC subclass denotes gas-compression refrigeration or heat-pump machines whose defining feature is the expansion element, such as an expansion valve, orifice or capillary tube that reduces the refrigerant pressure after compression. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration and gas-cycle machines.
[F25B 2309/023] with two stage expansion -- Gas cycle refrigeration machines that employ a two-stage expansion of the refrigerant, where the pressure drop occurs in two successive steps to improve thermodynamic efficiency and control of the cooling process.
[F25B 2309/06] Compression machines, plants or systems characterised by the refrigerant being carbon dioxide -- F25B 2309/06 covers compression-type refrigeration machines, plants or systems that use carbon dioxide as the working refrigerant. It pertains to mechanical engineering of cooling equipment where CO₂ serves as the fluid in the compression cycle, distinguishing these installations from those using other refrigerants.
[F25B 2309/061] with cycle highest pressure above the supercritical pressure -- This subclass covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines whose operating cycle reaches a pressure higher than the supercritical pressure of the refrigerant, so the fluid stays in a supercritical state during the high-pressure portion of the cycle. It is typical of transcritical carbon-dioxide systems and similar high-pressure cooling technologies.
[F25B 2309/14] Compression machines, plants or systems characterised by the cycle used -- This subclass covers refrigeration compressors, plant installations and complete systems that are defined primarily by the thermodynamic cycle they employ, such as vapor-compression, absorption or mixed cycles, rather than by specific mechanical features or applications. It groups together devices where the cycle type is the distinguishing factor within gas-cycle refrigeration machines.
[F25B 2309/1401] Ericsson or Ericcson cycles -- The F25B 2309/1401 entry covers refrigeration machines that employ Ericsson (or Ericcson) cycles, a thermodynamic gas-cycle process where heat is added and rejected at constant pressure using regeneration and isothermal compression and expansion. It applies to mechanical engineering refrigeration systems that use this external-heat-addition cycle.
[F25B 2309/1402] Pulse-tube cycles with acoustic driver -- The F25B 2309/1402 subclass covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines that employ pulse-tube cycles driven by an acoustic driver, where compression and expansion are created by oscillating pressure waves in a sealed tube rather than by mechanical pistons. This technology is used in specialized cooling systems for high-efficiency or low-vibration applications.
[F25B 2309/1403] Pulse-tube cycles with heat input into acoustic driver -- A pulse-tube refrigeration cycle that generates cooling by feeding heat into the acoustic driver, which creates pressure oscillations that drive the refrigerant through the system. This method belongs to gas-cycle refrigeration machines and is classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration, and specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2309/1404] Pulse-tube cycles with loudspeaker driven acoustic driver -- The F25B 2309/1404 entry covers pulse-tube refrigeration cycles that employ a loudspeaker-driven acoustic driver to generate the pressure oscillations needed for cooling. It is a subcategory of gas-cycle refrigeration machines within the broader field of refrigeration and cooling systems.
[F25B 2309/1405] Pulse-tube cycles with travelling waves -- Pulse-tube cycles with travelling waves are a type of gas-cycle refrigeration machine that uses a sealed tube where pressure oscillations travel as acoustic waves to produce cooling. The travelling-wave design enables continuous operation with high efficiency and few moving parts, making it well suited for cryogenic applications such as space instruments and scientific equipment.
[F25B 2309/1406] Pulse-tube cycles with pulse tube in co-axial or concentric geometrical arrangements -- F25B 2309/1406 covers refrigeration systems that use a pulse-tube thermodynamic cycle in which the pulse tube is positioned either inside the same axis as the compressor or surrounding it in a concentric layout. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically gas-cycle refrigeration machines.
[F25B 2309/1407] Pulse-tube cycles with pulse tube having in-line geometrical arrangements -- The CPC entry F25B 2309/1407 covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines that employ pulse-tube cycles in which the pulse tube is arranged in an in-line geometrical configuration. It relates to the design and operation of refrigeration systems that use this specific linear pulse-tube layout.
[F25B 2309/1408] Pulse-tube cycles with pulse tube having U-turn or L-turn type geometrical arrangements -- A pulse-tube refrigeration cycle in which the pulse tube is arranged in a U-turn or L-turn configuration, providing a compact path for gas oscillation within gas-cycle refrigeration machines. This geometric arrangement improves heat-exchange efficiency and allows a more streamlined system design.
[F25B 2309/1409] Pulse-tube cycles with pulse tube having special type of geometrical arrangements not being a coaxial, in-line or U-turn type -- F25B 2309/1409 covers pulse-tube refrigeration cycles where the pulse tube is configured in a non-coaxial, non-in-line, and non-U-turn geometry, employing alternative shapes or arrangements to achieve the thermodynamic cycle.
[F25B 2309/1411] Pulse-tube cycles characterised by control details, e.g. tuning, phase shifting or general control -- F25B 2309/1411 deals with pulse-tube refrigeration cycles in gas-cycle refrigeration machines where the performance is modulated by control techniques such as tuning, phase-shifting or other general control measures. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2309/1412] Pulse-tube cycles characterised by heat exchanger details -- A pulse-tube refrigeration cycle in which the performance or operation is defined by particular heat-exchanger configurations, such as the geometry, material or arrangement of the exchangers, is covered by this subclass. It pertains to gas-cycle cooling systems that employ a pulse-tube device and focus on the detailed design features of the heat-exchange components.
[F25B 2309/1413] Pulse-tube cycles characterised by performance, geometry or theory -- The CPC subclass F25B 2309/1413 concerns pulse-tube refrigeration cycles that are distinguished by their performance characteristics, geometric configuration, or theoretical description. It sits within gas-cycle refrigeration machines, itself a sub-category of refrigeration systems in mechanical engineering.
[F25B 2309/1414] Pulse-tube cycles characterised by pulse tube details -- F25B 2309/1414 covers pulse-tube refrigeration cycles where the design or geometry of the pulse tube itself is the distinguishing feature. These cycles belong to gas-cycle refrigeration machines and are used in cryogenic cooling systems. The classification groups technologies that improve efficiency or performance through specific pulse-tube characteristics.
[F25B 2309/1415] Pulse-tube cycles characterised by regenerator details -- This subclass concerns gas-cycle refrigeration machines that employ a pulse-tube cycle, specifically where the design, structure or operating details of the regenerator are defining features of the system. It is used to classify inventions related to refrigeration or cooling equipment that use this particular thermodynamic cycle.
[F25B 2309/1416] Pulse-tube cycles characterised by regenerator stack details -- This CPC subclass covers pulse-tube refrigeration cycles in which the design or arrangement of the regenerator stack is specified. It includes machines that use a gas-cycle and a pulse-tube to produce cooling, with particular emphasis on the geometry, material, or packing of the regenerator. The classification is used for patents involving detailed regenerator configurations in such systems.
[F25B 2309/1417] Pulse-tube cycles without any valves in gas supply and return lines -- Pulse-tube refrigeration cycles that operate without any valves in the gas supply and return lines, using a continuous gas flow to produce cooling through pressure-induced acoustic oscillations. The design eliminates moving valve mechanisms, simplifying the system and reducing mechanical wear.
[F25B 2309/1418] Pulse-tube cycles with valves in gas supply and return lines -- The entry covers pulse-tube refrigeration cycles that use valves placed in the gas supply and return lines to control the flow of refrigerant. It falls under gas-cycle refrigeration machines, describing a specific method for improving efficiency and performance in pulse-tube cooling systems.
[F25B 2309/14181] the valves being of the rotary type -- The subclass F25B 2309/14181 pertains to rotary-type valves employed in gas-cycle refrigeration machines, covering their design and function within refrigeration systems.
[F25B 2309/1419] Pulse-tube cycles with pulse tube having a basic pulse tube refrigerator [PTR], i.e. comprising a tube with basic schematic -- The subclass F25B 2309/1419 covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines that employ pulse-tube cycles, specifically where the pulse tube functions as a basic pulse-tube refrigerator. It relates to systems that generate cooling by oscillating gas flow through a tube without moving displacers, providing a compact, vibration-free refrigeration method.
[F25B 2309/1421] Pulse-tube cycles characterised by details not otherwise provided for -- F25B 2309/1421 covers pulse-tube refrigeration cycles, a type of gas-cycle refrigeration where cooling is produced by oscillating gas flow in a sealed tube without moving pistons. This subclass collects designs that have specific features or variations not captured by other more detailed entries, such as unique resonator shapes, timing arrangements, or auxiliary components. It is part of the broader category of refrigeration machines and systems.
[F25B 2309/1422] Pulse tubes with basic schematic including a counter flow heat exchanger instead of a regenerative heat exchanger -- This subclass covers pulse-tube refrigeration devices that employ a basic schematic where a counter-flow heat exchanger replaces the conventional regenerative heat exchanger, used in gas-cycle cooling systems for cryogenic applications.
[F25B 2309/1423] Pulse tubes with basic schematic including an inertance tube -- This CPC subclass covers pulse-tube refrigeration devices that incorporate an inertance tube in the basic schematic. Pulse tubes are gas-cycle cooling machines that produce low temperatures without moving pistons, and the inertance tube improves phase-shifting between pressure and mass flow. The entry falls under F25B 2309/14, which deals with gas-cycle refrigeration machines.
[F25B 2309/1424] Pulse tubes with basic schematic including an orifice and a reservoir -- Pulse tube refrigeration devices that use a simple layout comprising an orifice and a reservoir for controlling gas flow and thermodynamic oscillations. This subclass covers designs where the basic pulse-tube configuration is employed for cooling without moving pistons, typically in cryogenic or low-temperature applications.
[F25B 2309/14241] Pulse tubes with basic schematic including an orifice reservoir multiple inlet pulse tube -- F25B 2309/14241 covers pulse-tube refrigeration units that incorporate a basic schematic featuring an orifice reservoir and multiple inlet ports, enabling efficient gas-cycle cooling without moving pistons. The design is used in compact cryogenic systems.
[F25B 2309/1425] Pulse tubes with basic schematic including several pulse tubes -- F25B 2309/1425 covers refrigeration systems that employ pulse-tube technology, a cryogenic cooling method that uses a series of pulse tubes without moving parts to transfer heat. The entry includes basic schematic representations of configurations containing multiple pulse tubes within a gas-cycle refrigeration machine.
[F25B 2309/1426] Pulse tubes with basic schematic including at the pulse tube warm end a so called warm end expander -- F25B 2309/1426 covers gas-cycle refrigeration machines of the pulse-tube type in which the basic schematic includes an expander at the warm end of the tube. The classification falls under mechanical engineering - refrigeration or cooling - refrigeration machines, plants or systems - gas cycle refrigeration machines. It addresses designs that add a warm-end expander to improve efficiency or control of the pulse-tube cycle.
[F25B 2309/1427] Control of a pulse tube -- This subclass covers methods and devices that regulate the operation of a pulse-tube refrigerator, a gas-cycle cooling system without a moving displacer, by adjusting pressure, temperature, phase or flow parameters to optimise performance and stability.
[F25B 2309/1428] Control of a Stirling refrigeration machine -- F25B 2309/1428 pertains to methods and devices for controlling Stirling refrigeration machines, a type of gas-cycle refrigeration system. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems. The class addresses regulation of temperature, pressure, and cycle timing to optimise performance and efficiency.
[F25B 2313/00] Compression machines, plants or systems with reversible cycle not otherwise provided for -- The CPC entry F25B 2313/00 covers compression equipment, plants or systems that operate on a reversible cycle and are not covered by other more specific headings. It includes machines that can both compress and expand a refrigerant, such as reversible heat-pump or cryogenic compressors, where the cycle direction can be switched without falling under separate classifications.
[F25B 2313/001] with two or more accumulators -- This CPC subclass covers refrigeration or cooling compression systems that operate on a reversible thermodynamic cycle and include two or more accumulators for storing refrigerant, typically to improve performance, stability, or capacity. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration machines, and specifically compression machines not otherwise specified.
[F25B 2313/002] geothermal -- F25B 2313/002 refers to refrigeration or cooling systems that employ geothermal energy as the heat source or sink in a reversible compression cycle not otherwise specified. It covers compression machines, plants or systems designed to use ground-derived heat for heating, cooling or heat-pump applications within the broader mechanical-engineering category.
[F25B 2313/003] Indoor unit with water as a heat sink or heat source -- The entry covers indoor units of reversible refrigeration or heat-pump systems that use water as the heat-exchange medium, serving either as a heat sink or heat source. It applies to devices where water circulates through coils or plates within the indoor segment of the plant, enabling cooling or heating functions not covered by other specific categories.
[F25B 2313/004] Outdoor unit with water as a heat sink or heat source -- This classification covers reversible refrigeration or heat-pump outdoor units that use water as the heat-exchange medium, either rejecting or absorbing heat. It applies to systems where water serves as the sink or source in the outdoor component of a compression cycle not otherwise specified.
[F25B 2313/005] Outdoor unit expansion valves -- F25B 2313/005 designates expansion valves that are installed in the outdoor segment of a refrigeration or air-conditioning system, typically a heat pump or chiller, where they control refrigerant flow into the compressor's suction line. These devices are part of reversible-cycle compression plants not covered elsewhere.
[F25B 2313/006] two pipes connecting the outdoor side to the indoor side with multiple indoor units -- A reversible refrigeration system using a compressor where two pipes convey refrigerant between a single outdoor unit and multiple indoor units, allowing heating and cooling across the connected space. This configuration is classified under compression machines with a reversible cycle not otherwise provided for, specifically for systems employing separate supply and return lines.
[F25B 2313/007] three pipes connecting the outdoor side to the indoor side with multiple indoor units -- A CPC subclass for mechanical refrigeration systems that use three distinct pipes linking an outdoor unit to multiple indoor units, typically in reversible-cycle compressors not covered elsewhere. The configuration enables simultaneous supply and return of refrigerant and condensate between the outdoor side and each indoor terminal.
[F25B 2313/008] Refrigerant heaters -- F25B 2313/008 covers devices that add heat to refrigerant flowing in a reversible compression cycle, typically to improve start-up, defrosting or low-temperature performance of refrigeration machines. The heaters are integrated into compressors, condensers, evaporators or associated piping and are designed for systems not covered by more specific categories. This sub-class falls under the broader group for refrigeration machines, plants or systems in mechanical engineering.
[F25B 2313/009] indoor unit in circulation with outdoor unit in first operation mode, indoor unit in circulation with an other heat exchanger in second operation mode or outdoor unit in circulation with an other heat exchanger in third operation mode
[F25B 2313/021] Indoor unit or outdoor unit with auxiliary heat exchanger not forming part of the indoor or outdoor unit -- F25B 2313/021 covers HVAC systems where either the indoor or the outdoor unit incorporates an auxiliary heat exchanger that is separate from the main heat-exchanger assembly of that unit. The auxiliary exchanger provides additional heating or cooling capacity but is not built into the primary unit housing. This subclass falls under compression machines, plants or systems with reversible cycles in the refrigeration field.
[F25B 2313/0211] the auxiliary heat exchanger being only used during defrosting -- This CPC entry covers refrigeration systems where an auxiliary heat exchanger operates solely for defrosting, providing heat to melt frost without contributing to the normal cooling cycle. It applies to reversible compression machines and plants that include such a dedicated defrost heat exchanger.
[F25B 2313/0212] the auxiliary heat exchanger being only used during dehumidifying -- The entry F25B 2313/0212 concerns refrigeration or cooling machines that operate on a reversible compression cycle, specifically focusing on the auxiliary heat exchanger that is engaged solely during the dehumidification phase of operation.
[F25B 2313/0213] the auxiliary heat exchanger being only used during heating -- F25B 2313/0213 covers auxiliary heat exchangers that operate solely during the heating mode of reversible-cycle compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems. The provision applies when the exchanger is not used in cooling, defining a specific subcategory of refrigeration equipment.
[F25B 2313/0214] the auxiliary heat exchanger being used parallel to the indoor unit during heating operation -- This CPC subclass pertains to reversible refrigeration or heat-pump systems where an auxiliary heat exchanger is positioned in parallel with the indoor unit during heating operation, enhancing heat transfer or capacity. It falls under mechanical engineering refrigeration machines, reversible compression cycles not otherwise specified.
[F25B 2313/0215] the auxiliary heat exchanger being used parallel to the outdoor heat exchanger during heating operation -- The entry covers systems where an auxiliary heat exchanger operates in parallel with the outdoor heat exchanger during the heating mode of a reversible compression refrigeration cycle, improving heat transfer efficiency or capacity. It applies to mechanical-engineering refrigeration machines or plants not otherwise classified.
[F25B 2313/023] using multiple indoor units -- F25B 2313/023 covers refrigeration or cooling systems that employ multiple indoor units in a reversible cycle, such as heat pumps or air-conditioners with several indoor modules connected to a single outdoor unit. The classification applies to compression machines and plants where the indoor units operate in parallel or series to condition different spaces.
[F25B 2313/0231] with simultaneous cooling and heating -- A refrigeration or heat-pump system that compresses a working fluid and, within the same reversible cycle, delivers cooling to one side while providing heating to the other side at the same time, such as a conventional heat-pump used for space heating and cooling.
[F25B 2313/0232] with bypasses -- The CPC subclass F25B 2313/0232 designates refrigeration or compression machines that incorporate bypasses-auxiliary flow paths allowing refrigerant or working fluid to circumvent components such as compressors, condensers, or expansion devices. Bypasses are used to modulate capacity, control pressure, or facilitate defrosting and other operational functions.
[F25B 2313/02321] during cooling -- The CPC entry F25B 2313/02321 covers features of reversible compression refrigeration cycles that are applied during the cooling phase of operation. It relates to methods, controls or components used while the system removes heat in a refrigeration machine, plant or system not otherwise specified in higher-level subclasses.
[F25B 2313/02322] during defrosting -- The F25B 2313/02322 subclass concerns reversible compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems and the specific actions, control methods or components employed during the defrosting phase to remove ice or frost accumulation. It addresses techniques for initiating, timing or managing defrost cycles in such equipment.
[F25B 2313/02323] during heating -- The subclass deals with reversible compression refrigeration or heat-pump systems during the heating phase of the cycle, covering methods, components or control strategies that supply heat to the refrigerant when it is being condensed or used for space heating. It applies to compression machines, plants or systems that are not covered by other more specific categories.
[F25B 2313/0233] in parallel arrangements -- The CPC entry F25B 2313/0233 relates to refrigeration or cooling compression machines, plants or systems that employ a reversible cycle and are arranged in parallel. It covers configurations where two or more compressors or compressor groups operate simultaneously to share load, increase capacity, or provide redundancy, such as parallel compressor banks in chillers or heat-pump installations.
[F25B 2313/02331] during cooling -- The subclass F25B 2313/02331 deals with reversible compression refrigeration or heat-pump systems specifically while they are operating in the cooling mode. It includes provisions for control, regulation, monitoring or performance features that are applied during the cooling phase of the cycle.
[F25B 2313/02332] during defrosting -- This CPC subclass covers methods or apparatus for carrying out the defrosting phase in reversible-cycle compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems that are not otherwise specified. It addresses the removal of ice or frost from heat-exchange components during operation.
[F25B 2313/02333] during dehumidification -- The code F25B 2313/02333 pertains to compression refrigeration machines or plants that include a reversible cycle and address the stage where the system removes moisture from the air. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems, and captures features of dehumidification within the overall cycle.
[F25B 2313/02334] during heating -- The subgroup F25B 2313/02334 pertains to reversible compression or refrigeration machines that are operated in the heating mode of their cycle, such as heat-pump or heat-recovery applications. It covers the design, components and control methods specifically related to the heating phase of the reversible cycle.
[F25B 2313/0234] in series arrangements -- F25B 2313/0234 designates refrigeration compression machines, plants or systems using reversible cycles that are connected in series, where the discharge of one unit feeds the suction of the next to raise pressure or temperature in stages. The classification applies to series arrangements not otherwise specified in other subclasses.
[F25B 2313/02341] during cooling -- The CPC entry F25B 2313/02341 relates to refrigeration or heat-pump machines, plants or systems that employ a reversible compression cycle and specifically addresses the cooling phase of that cycle, such as heat-exchange or other processes that remove heat from the refrigerant during operation. It is used for patents covering the cooling step that is not otherwise classified elsewhere.
[F25B 2313/02342] during defrosting -- This CPC subclass covers features, methods, or components that operate specifically during the defrosting phase of reversible compression refrigeration cycles, including control strategies, timing, and equipment adjustments used to melt frost on heat exchangers or evaporators.
[F25B 2313/02343] during dehumidification -- The subclass relates to reversible-cycle compression refrigeration machines or systems in which dehumidification of air or gas is carried out during operation, such as when the unit functions as a heat pump or refrigerator and removes moisture from the circulating fluid.
[F25B 2313/02344] during heating -- This subclass covers reversible refrigeration or heat-pump machines that include features or actions taken while the system is in the heating part of its cycle, such as heating of the compressor, heat exchangers, or other components during the heating mode.
[F25B 2313/025] using multiple outdoor units -- This CPC subclass (F25B 2313/025) covers refrigeration or cooling systems that employ multiple outdoor units, such as multi-split or multi-condensing-unit arrangements, where each outdoor unit serves one or more indoor units within the same reversible cycle. It pertains to the design, control, or operation of such distributed outdoor components in mechanical engineering refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2313/0251] being defrosted alternately -- The CPC subclass F25B 2313/0251 refers to refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that use a reversible compression cycle and are defrosted alternately, meaning defrosting is performed periodically rather than continuously, often by reversing the cycle or applying intermittent heating.
[F25B 2313/0252] with bypasses -- F25B 2313/0252 covers refrigeration or cooling compression machines, plants or systems that operate on a reversible cycle and incorporate bypasses to divert refrigerant flow, allowing partial load operation or defrosting. The bypasses may be valves, lines or ducts that connect different circuit points, improving efficiency or control in systems not covered by other subclasses.
[F25B 2313/02521] during cooling -- The code F25B 2313/02521 designates features, methods or control measures that are applied specifically during the cooling phase of a reversible-cycle compression refrigeration system not otherwise classified. It includes any adjustments, regulation or auxiliary operations carried out while the machine is providing cooling.
[F25B 2313/02522] during defrosting -- The subclass F25B 2313/02522 addresses the defrosting phase of reversible-cycle compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems that are not otherwise covered. It includes methods, controls or components designed to remove ice or frost during operation to maintain efficiency and prevent damage.
[F25B 2313/02523] during heating -- This subclass addresses reversible compression refrigeration or heat-pump systems where a specific functional element operates during the heating phase of the cycle, such as heat-exchanging components, controls or auxiliary devices that are engaged while the system delivers heat.
[F25B 2313/0253] in parallel arrangements -- F25B 2313/0253 covers refrigeration or cooling compressors that are connected in parallel, allowing multiple compression units to operate simultaneously to share load, increase capacity, or provide redundancy within a reversible cycle system.
[F25B 2313/02531] during cooling -- F25B 2313/02531 covers aspects of reversible compression refrigeration systems that are implemented during the cooling stage of the cycle, such as control methods, heat exchangers, or energy-recovery measures applied while the refrigerant is being condensed or the load is being cooled.
[F25B 2313/02532] during defrosting -- This CPC subclass covers features of reversible compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems that are applied during the defrosting stage, including control methods, heat-exchange arrangements or cycle adjustments used to remove ice buildup.
[F25B 2313/02533] during heating -- The subclass F25B 2313/02533 covers reversible compression-cycle machines or systems in which heating is performed as part of the cycle, such as heat-pump or waste-heat recovery operations. It applies to equipment not otherwise classified elsewhere within the broader group of refrigeration machines, plants or systems that use a reversible cycle.
[F25B 2313/0254] in series arrangements -- This subclass covers refrigeration or cooling compression systems that employ a reversible cycle and are configured with multiple compressors arranged in series, where the discharge of one unit feeds the suction of the next. Such series arrangements are used to achieve higher pressure ratios or efficiency in plant designs not covered by other subclasses.
[F25B 2313/02541] during cooling -- F25B 2313/02541 designates aspects of reversible compression refrigeration systems that occur during the cooling stage. It covers methods, devices or control actions applied while the cycle extracts heat, such as heat-exchanger operation, flow regulation or energy recovery during the cooling phase.
[F25B 2313/02542] during defrosting -- This CPC subclass relates to mechanical-engineered refrigeration machines that use a reversible compression cycle, specifically addressing the defrosting phase of such systems. It covers methods, controls or apparatus for initiating or managing defrosting while the machine operates.
[F25B 2313/02543] during heating -- The CPC subclass F25B 2313/02543 refers to refrigeration or cooling compression machines, plants or systems that operate on a reversible thermodynamic cycle and addresses the heating stage of the cycle. It covers features, control methods or components specifically related to the heating portion of such systems.
[F25B 2313/027] characterised by the reversing means -- F25B 2313/027 covers refrigeration or cooling machines that use a reversible compression cycle and are distinguished by a reversing means.
