The reversing means enables the direction of refrigerant flow or compressor operation to be changed, allowing the system to function alternately as a chiller and a heat pump. It applies to compression machines, plants or systems not otherwise provided for.
[F25B 2313/0271] the compressor allows rotation in reverse direction -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration machines and specifically addresses compression machines with a reversible cycle that are not otherwise covered. It includes compressors engineered to permit rotor rotation in the opposite direction, allowing the unit to operate as both a compressor and an expander in reversible refrigeration or heat-pump systems.
[F25B 2313/0272] using bridge circuits of one-way valves -- The entry covers reversible compression refrigeration machines that incorporate bridge circuits built from one-way valves to control refrigerant flow. It concerns valve arrangements forming a bridge network, enabling directional flow control in systems that operate with a reversible thermodynamic cycle.
[F25B 2313/02731] using one three-way valve -- The entry refers to reversible refrigeration or heat-pump systems that employ a single three-way valve to direct refrigerant flow between the compressor, condenser and evaporator. It covers designs where one valve accomplishes the switching needed for heating, cooling or heat-recovery cycles in a compression plant.
[F25B 2313/02732] using two three-way valves -- This CPC subclass concerns refrigeration or cooling systems that employ compression machines with a reversible thermodynamic cycle, specifically those that use two three-way valves to control the direction of refrigerant flow and to manage suction and discharge ports. The configuration allows the compressor to operate in both heating and cooling modes by switching valve positions.

It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration and reversible compression technology.
[F25B 2313/02742] using two four-way valves -- A refrigeration system that uses two four-way valves to direct refrigerant flow, enabling reversible operation of a compression cycle not specifically covered elsewhere. The configuration allows separate control of the high- and low-pressure circuits, facilitating both cooling and heat-pump functions in compact machines or plants.
[F25B 2313/02743] using three four-way valves -- A refrigeration or cooling system that employs three four-way valves within a reversible compression cycle not covered by other subclasses. The configuration controls fluid flow and direction, enabling efficient heat exchange and temperature regulation in machines, plants or systems that use multiple valve stages.
[F25B 2313/0276] using six-way valves -- This subclass covers refrigeration compressors and related reversible-cycle machines that incorporate six-way valves to control fluid flow, enhance efficiency or enable reverse operation. It includes designs, arrangements and methods for using such valves in compression systems not classified elsewhere.
[F25B 2313/0279] using nine-way valves -- This CPC subclass covers refrigeration or cooling compression machines, plants or systems that employ nine-way valves to control refrigerant flow in a reversible cycle not otherwise specified. It pertains to designs where a single valve provides multiple flow paths for charging, discharging and pressure equalisation within the system.
[F25B 2313/02791] using shut-off valves -- This CPC subclass covers the use of shut-off valves in reversible compression equipment such as refrigeration machines or plant systems where the specific application is not otherwise listed. It pertains to mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling systems employing reversible cycles.
[F25B 2313/02792] using reversing valve changing the refrigerant flow direction due to pressure differences of the refrigerant and not by external actuation -- A reversible refrigeration system that changes the direction of refrigerant flow using a reversing valve that operates solely from the refrigerant's pressure differences, without any external actuation. This design falls within mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically compression machines, plants or systems with a reversible cycle not otherwise specified.
[F25B 2313/029] Control issues -- F25B 2313/029 pertains to control aspects of reversible compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems that are not otherwise classified. It addresses the design, regulation and monitoring of operating parameters such as pressure, temperature and flow to optimise efficiency, performance and safety.
[F25B 2313/0291] related to the pressure of the indoor unit -- F25B 2313/0291 is a Cooperative Patent Classification for technologies in mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling systems, dealing with compression machines, plants or systems that use a reversible cycle not otherwise specified. It applies to inventions that address the pressure of the indoor unit in such reversible refrigeration installations. [F25B 2313/0293] related to the indoor fan, e.g. controlling speed -- The entry covers reversible refrigeration or cooling compression systems in which the speed of the indoor fan is controlled, including variable-speed drives, control circuits or methods for adjusting fan rotation to regulate airflow and temperature within the system.
[F25B 2313/0294] related to the outdoor fan, e.g. controlling speed -- F25B 2313/0294 covers features of compression-machine systems that involve an outdoor fan, specifically arrangements for controlling the fan's speed, such as variable-speed drives, speed-control mechanisms or sensors that adjust airflow in reversible-cycle refrigeration or cooling plants.

It includes arrangements for measuring temperature, pressure, flow or other operating parameters to control the cycle.
[F25B 2313/0311] Pressure sensors near the expansion valve -- This subclass concerns pressure sensors placed adjacent to the expansion valve in reversible-cycle refrigeration or cooling machines, used to monitor refrigerant pressure for control and safety. These sensors enable precise regulation of valve operation, improving system efficiency and protecting components.
[F25B 2313/0312] Pressure sensors near the indoor heat exchanger -- The classification pertains to pressure sensors placed near the indoor heat-exchanger of a reversible refrigeration or cooling system, used to monitor refrigerant pressure and assist in controlling the operation of compression machines and heat-transfer cycles.
[F25B 2313/0313] Pressure sensors near the outdoor heat exchanger -- The entry covers pressure-sensing devices that are positioned adjacent to the outdoor heat-exchanger of a reversible refrigeration or cooling system. [F25B 2313/0315] Temperature sensors near the outdoor heat exchanger -- This subclass deals with temperature-sensing devices positioned at or near the outdoor heat-exchanger of a compression-type refrigeration machine or system, used to monitor refrigerant or ambient conditions for control, efficiency or safety purposes.

The sensors monitor the heater's temperature to control heating, improve efficiency and protect system components.
[F25B 2315/00] Sorption refrigeration cycles or details thereof -- The CPC subclass F25B 2315/00 covers sorption refrigeration cycles, including absorption and adsorption cooling processes, their components and operating methods within refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2315/001] Crystallization prevention -- This CPC subclass covers measures in sorption refrigeration cycles to prevent crystallization of the refrigerant or sorbent, including system designs, temperature regulation, additives and component arrangements that inhibit crystal formation and maintain efficient operation.
[F25B 2315/002] Generator absorber heat exchanger [GAX] -- The entry describes a generator-absorber heat exchanger (GAX) used in sorption refrigeration cycles, in which the generator and absorber are combined into a single heat-exchange unit to transfer heat directly between them. This integration improves thermal efficiency and compactness of refrigeration machines, plants or systems that employ sorption technology.
[F25B 2315/003] Hydrates for sorption cycles -- F25B 2315/003 covers the use of hydrate compounds as working media in sorption refrigeration cycles, describing their role in absorption or adsorption processes to generate cooling. The classification addresses the selection, preparation, and performance of hydrates within refrigeration plants and systems.
[F25B 2315/004] Inert heat-exchangers -- Inert heat-exchangers are devices used in sorption refrigeration cycles to transfer heat between the sorbent and an external fluid without chemically interacting with the refrigerant. They typically consist of metal plates or tubes that conduct heat while remaining inert, enabling efficient heat recovery and reducing temperature gradients in the cycle.
[F25B 2315/005] Regeneration -- F25B 2315/005 Regeneration covers techniques for restoring the sorbent in sorption refrigeration cycles, including heating, pressure changes or desorption steps that release the previously absorbed refrigerant. The entry addresses equipment and process details that enable the sorbent to be reused efficiently in cooling systems.
[F25B 2315/006] Reversible sorption cycles -- F25B 2315/006 refers to reversible sorption cycles used in refrigeration systems, where a solid or liquid adsorbent repeatedly absorbs and releases a refrigerant to produce cooling or heating. The classification covers cycle designs, control methods and component arrangements that allow the sorption process to be cycled back-and-forth without external heat input beyond the regeneration step.
[F25B 2315/007] Parallel systems therefor -- This subclass covers the use of parallel arrangements of components or subsystems within sorption refrigeration cycles, such as multiple adsorbers, condensers or evaporators operating simultaneously to increase capacity or efficiency. It includes designs that connect these units in parallel to share heat or mass flow, improving performance of refrigeration machines and plants.
[F25B 2321/00] Details of machines, plants or systems, using electric or magnetic effects -- This subclass covers the design, components and operation of refrigeration machines, plants or systems that employ electric or magnetic principles, such as electrically driven compressors, magnetic cooling cycles, control circuits and related energy-transfer devices. It details how these effects are applied within the overall refrigeration equipment.
[F25B 2321/001] by using electro-caloric effects -- This subgroup classifies refrigeration machines, plants or systems that generate cooling by applying an electric field to a material that exhibits the electro-caloric effect, causing a reversible temperature change. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration, and the use of electric or magnetic phenomena for cooling.
[F25B 2321/002] by using magneto-caloric effects -- This CPC subclass covers refrigeration or cooling equipment that exploits the magneto-caloric effect, i.e., temperature changes induced by varying magnetic fields, for the operation of machines, plants or systems. It falls under mechanical engineering and relates to details of machines using electric or magnetic effects.

The moving magnet drives a thermodynamic cycle that transfers heat within the apparatus.

Includes methods of varying field strength, direction or configuration and devices that enhance an existing magnetic field for better performance.
[F25B 2321/003] by using thermionic electron cooling effects -- The code F25B 2321/003 covers refrigeration machines, plants or systems that achieve cooling through thermionic electron-cooling effects, where heat is removed by the emission of electrons from a hot cathode to a cooler electrode and the associated electric or magnetic fields drive the process. It applies to designs that use these electron-based mechanisms for refrigeration in mechanical-engineering applications.

It classifies devices that employ this effect to provide heating or cooling functions in mechanical engineering applications.

[F25B 2321/0251] by a gas -- F25B 2321/0251 classifies refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that are powered by a gas, where the cooling effect is produced through electric or magnetic means. It is a detailed subcategory within mechanical engineering for gas-driven refrigeration equipment.

This indirect heating method is used to regenerate the sorbent during cooling cycles, improving energy utilization in refrigeration plants.

[F25B 2339/0446] characterised by the refrigerant tubes connecting the header of the condenser to the receiver -- The entry concerns refrigeration systems where the refrigerant tubes are designed to link the header of the condenser directly to the receiver. [F25B 2339/045] Condensers made by assembling a tube on a plate-like element or between plate-like elements -- F25B 2339/045 pertains to condensers constructed by attaching a tube to a plate-like element or by placing the tube between two plate-like elements, a design used in refrigeration and cooling systems to improve heat exchange efficiency.
[F25B 2339/046] Condensers with refrigerant heat exchange tubes positioned inside or around a vessel containing water or pcm to cool the refrigerant gas -- The CPC subclass F25B 2339/046 relates to refrigeration condensers in which the refrigerant heat-exchange tubes are installed inside or around a vessel that contains water or a phase-change material, allowing the water or PCM to absorb heat and cool the refrigerant gas.
[F25B 2339/047] Water-cooled condensers -- Water-cooled condensers are heat-exchange devices in refrigeration systems where water circulates to remove heat from the refrigerant vapor, causing it to condense. The classification covers designs such as tube-bundle, finned-tube, and spray-type condensers, their arrangement, water-flow arrangements, and integration with plant water circuits for efficient thermal management.
[F25B 2341/00] Details of ejectors not being used as compression device -- The code F25B 2341/00 classifies information on ejectors that are used in refrigeration or cooling systems but are not functioning as compression devices, covering their design, operation and performance characteristics. It applies to mechanical-engineering aspects of refrigeration machines, plants or systems where ejectors serve purposes such as expansion, mixing or pressure reduction rather than compression.
[F25B 2341/001] Ejectors not being used as compression device -- The code F25B 2341/001 refers to ejectors used in refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems for purposes other than compressing the refrigerant, such as pressure reduction, flow control or fluid mixing.
[F25B 2341/0011] Ejectors with the cooled primary flow at reduced or low pressure -- The subclass covers ejectors used in refrigeration or cooling systems where the primary fluid is precooled and the ejector operates at a reduced or low pressure, distinguishing these devices from ejectors employed as compression units.
[F25B 2341/0012] Ejectors with the cooled primary flow at high pressure -- F25B 2341/0012 covers ejectors in refrigeration systems where the primary fluid is cooled and supplied at a pressure higher than the secondary stream, and the ejector is not used as a compressor. It pertains to the design and operation of these high-pressure, cooled-primary-flow ejectors within refrigeration machines and plant.
[F25B 2341/0013] Ejector control arrangements -- The F25B 2341/0013 entry relates to control arrangements for ejectors used in refrigeration or cooling systems when the ejector does not function as a compression device. It covers devices such as valves, regulators, sensors and feedback mechanisms that adjust flow, pressure or speed to optimize the ejector's performance within the plant.
[F25B 2341/0014] Ejectors with a high pressure hot primary flow from a compressor discharge -- The CPC code F25B 2341/0014 classifies ejector devices that handle a high-pressure hot primary flow taken directly from a compressor discharge, where the ejector is not used as a compression element. It pertains to refrigeration or cooling systems within mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2341/0015] using two or more ejectors -- A subclass of refrigeration machines that covers the use of two or more ejectors arranged together, where the ejectors function solely for fluid expansion or entrainment rather than as a compression device, improving system performance in cooling or condensation cycles.
[F25B 2341/0016] Ejectors for creating an oil recirculation -- The entry F25B 2341/0016 covers ejectors that are employed to generate an oil-circulation flow within refrigeration machines, plants or systems, distinguishing them from ejectors used as compression devices. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for refrigeration or cooling equipment.
[F25B 2341/06] Details of flow restrictors or expansion valves -- F25B 2341/06 covers the design and functional details of flow restrictors and expansion valves used in refrigeration machines, plants or systems to regulate refrigerant flow, where the ejector is not employed as a compression device. The entry pertains to mechanical-engineering aspects of cooling technology.
[F25B 2341/062] Capillary expansion valves -- F25B 2341/062 covers capillary expansion valves, a subtype of flow-control devices used in refrigeration and cooling systems to expand refrigerant from high to low pressure via a capillary tube. These valves regulate temperature and pressure in machines or plants without employing ejectors as compression devices.
[F25B 2341/063] Feed forward expansion valves -- A feed-forward expansion valve is a control device in refrigeration systems that modulates refrigerant flow based on upstream temperature or pressure signals rather than relying on downstream feedback. By anticipating load changes, it improves system responsiveness and efficiency, and it is used in machines where ejectors are not employed as compression devices.
[F25B 2341/064] Superheater expansion valves -- F25B 2341/064, titled Superheater expansion valves, covers devices that control the flow of superheated refrigerant from a high-pressure side to a lower-pressure side, using an expansion valve rather than an ejector for compression. It falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigeration machines, plants or systems, and relates to details where ejectors are not employed as a compression device.
[F25B 2341/067] Expansion valves having a pilot valve -- This classification covers expansion valves that incorporate a pilot valve to control refrigerant flow in refrigeration systems, improving pressure regulation and efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2341/068] Expansion valves combined with a sensor -- F25B 2341/068 relates to expansion valves that incorporate a sensor for monitoring and controlling refrigerant flow in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. The classification covers combined valve-sensor devices that enhance performance and safety, distinct from ejectors used as compression devices.
[F25B 2341/0681] the sensor is heated -- The subclass F25B 2341/0681 pertains to refrigeration or cooling machines that incorporate an ejector which is not used as a compression device, and in which a sensor within the system is deliberately heated, typically to prevent frost formation or ensure reliable temperature measurement.
[F25B 2341/0682] the sensor contains sorbent materials -- CPC code F25B 2341/0682 covers sensors that incorporate sorbent materials within refrigeration or cooling equipment, focusing on ejector components used for functions other than compression. The classification is applied to patents describing how such sensors aid heat exchange or moisture absorption in refrigeration machines and plants.
[F25B 2341/0683] the sensor is disposed in the suction line and influenced by the temperature or the pressure of the suction gas -- F25B 2341/0683 covers refrigeration systems where a sensor is installed in the suction line to monitor the temperature or pressure of the suction gas, providing feedback for control without acting as a compression element.
[F25B 2345/00] Details for charging or discharging refrigerants -- F25B 2345/00 relates to the processes and equipment used to charge or discharge refrigerants in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, encompassing methods for adding, recovering, evacuating and removing refrigerant as well as monitoring quantities and pressures during these operations.
[F25B 2345/001] Charging refrigerant to a cycle -- The subclass F25B 2345/001 covers methods and apparatus for charging a refrigerant into a refrigeration cycle, including the introduction of refrigerant, control of its quantity or pressure, and related steps for filling or topping up a refrigeration system. It is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy under refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2345/002] Collecting refrigerant from a cycle -- The F25B 2345/002 subclass covers devices or processes that collect refrigerant from a refrigeration cycle, such as during charging, recovery, or discharge. It relates to systems that capture the working fluid for reuse, reclamation or safe disposal, fitting within mechanical engineering and refrigeration machine classifications.
[F25B 2345/003] Control issues for charging or collecting refrigerant to or from a cycle -- This subclass covers control techniques and devices for adding or removing refrigerant in a refrigeration cycle, including valves, sensors and control algorithms used during charging, recovery or collection operations to regulate pressure, temperature or flow.
[F25B 2345/004] with several tanks to collect or charge a cycle -- The subclass F25B 2345/004 covers refrigeration machines that use several tanks to collect refrigerant or to charge it during a cooling cycle. It relates to the handling of refrigerants in systems where multiple storage containers are employed for charging or discharge operations.
[F25B 2345/005] Service stations therefor -- Service stations for refrigerant handling are facilities where refrigerants are added, recovered, reclaimed or recycled for refrigeration machines, plants or systems. The entry covers the equipment, infrastructure and procedures used to charge or discharge refrigerants at these stations.
[F25B 2345/0051] having a carrying handle -- This CPC subgroup pertains to refrigeration or cooling apparatus where the component for charging or discharging refrigerant is equipped with a carrying handle, facilitating manual transport of the unit or its refrigerant containers. Such designs are common in portable refrigeration units, portable condensers, or service cans meant to be moved by a user during maintenance.
[F25B 2345/0052] having wheels -- F25B 2345/0052 covers refrigeration or cooling equipment for charging or discharging refrigerants that is fitted with wheels, enabling the unit to be moved easily for operation, transport, or maintenance.
[F25B 2345/006] characterised by charging or discharging valves -- The subgroup F25B 2345/006 relates to refrigeration machines, plants or systems that include valves specifically designed for charging or discharging refrigerant, such as service, recovery or control valves that introduce or remove refrigerant from the circuit during operation or maintenance.
[F25B 2345/007] characterised by the weighing of refrigerant or oil -- This sub-group relates to refrigeration equipment where the process of charging or discharging the system is monitored by measuring the mass of refrigerant or oil. It covers methods and devices that use weighing to determine the quantity of fluid added or removed, ensuring accurate charge levels in compressors, heat exchangers or complete plant installations.
[F25B 2347/00] Details for preventing or removing deposits or corrosion -- The CPC code F25B 2347/00 falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, refrigeration machines, plants or systems and covers technologies for preventing or removing deposits or corrosion in refrigeration equipment, such as anti-scaling and anti-corrosion treatments, cleaning methods, and material selections.
[F25B 2347/02] Details of defrosting cycles -- F25B 2347/02 relates to the design and operation of defrosting cycles in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, describing methods, timing, control and power strategies used to melt ice or frost on evaporators and thereby prevent deposit buildup and maintain cooling efficiency.
[F25B 2347/021] Alternate defrosting -- F25B 2347/021 - Alternate defrosting covers methods and devices for removing ice or frost from refrigeration equipment using techniques other than standard heating, aiming to prevent deposit buildup or corrosion in cooling systems.
[F25B 2347/022] Cool gas defrosting -- F25B 2347/022 concerns the use of a cool gas to defrost refrigeration machines, plants or systems, focusing on methods that prevent or remove deposits and corrosion.
[F25B 2347/023] Set point defrosting -- Set-point defrosting is a control technique for refrigeration equipment in which a predetermined temperature or time threshold triggers the defrost cycle, melting accumulated ice and reducing corrosion risk. Sensors detect when the evaporator reaches the set point, and the system automatically initiates heating or flow reversal to remove deposits without manual intervention.
[F25B 2400/00] General features or devices for refrigeration machines, plants or systems, combined heating and refrigeration systems or heat-pump systems, i.e. not limited to a particular subgroup of -- F25B 2400/00 covers general features or devices used in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including combined heating-refrigeration and heat-pump arrangements, without being confined to a specific subgroup.
[F25B 2400/01] Heaters -- F25B 2400/01 refers to heaters that are part of refrigeration, cooling or heat-pump installations. It covers general heating elements used to raise temperature within refrigeration machines, plants or combined heating-refrigeration systems, without being limited to a specific subgroup.
[F25B 2400/02] Centrifugal separation of gas, liquid or oil -- The CPC subclass F25B 2400/02 covers devices that use centrifugal forces to separate gas, liquid or oil within refrigeration, heating-refrigeration or heat-pump systems. It applies to any general feature or arrangement that performs this separation, irrespective of the specific type of refrigerating machine.
[F25B 2400/03] Suction accumulators with deflectors -- Suction accumulators equipped with deflectors are components in refrigeration and heat-pump circuits that store excess refrigerant during low-load periods and redirect the flow to prevent liquid carry-over into the compressor. The deflectors guide the refrigerant toward the vapor side, improving system stability and protecting the compressor from damage.
[F25B 2400/04] Refrigeration circuit bypassing means -- The entry covers devices that provide a bypass path in a refrigeration, heating-refrigeration or heat-pump circuit, allowing refrigerant to circulate around selected components such as compressors, condensers or evaporators for control, maintenance or efficiency purposes. It encompasses any means-valves, ports, or other arrangements-used to temporarily divert the flow without disrupting overall system operation.
[F25B 2400/0401] for the compressor -- This CPC subclass covers compressors employed in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including combined heating-refrigeration and heat-pump arrangements, and relates specifically to the compressor component without restriction to any narrower sub-group.
[F25B 2400/0403] for the condenser -- The code F25B 2400/0403 covers condenser elements used in refrigeration, cooling or heat-pump systems, specifying designs that facilitate heat rejection from the refrigerant to a secondary fluid or air. It includes general condenser configurations such as air-cooled, water-cooled, and combined heating-refrigeration arrangements, without restricting to a particular sub-type. This classification groups devices that form the essential heat-exchange stage of a refrigeration cycle.
[F25B 2400/0405] for the desuperheater -- This subclass covers desuperheaters, devices that reduce the temperature of superheated refrigerant leaving a compressor in refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration systems. They are used to improve efficiency and protect downstream components by lowering the refrigerant's superheat before expansion.
[F25B 2400/0407] for the ejector -- This CPC subclass covers ejectors employed in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including combined heating-refrigeration and heat-pump installations, without restricting to any particular type of ejector or cycle. It focuses on the ejector's function to expand or compress refrigerant within the overall system.
[F25B 2400/0409] for the evaporator -- The code F25B 2400/0409 covers general features or devices that are applied to the evaporator of refrigeration machines, plants or combined heating-refrigeration systems, including heat-pump installations. It includes any design, arrangement or adaptation of the evaporator that is not confined to a more specific subclass, such as improvements in heat-exchange surfaces, flow distribution or integration with other system components.
[F25B 2400/0411] for the expansion valve or capillary tube -- This subclass covers the expansion valve or capillary tube, the device that throttles refrigerant flow in a refrigeration or heat-pump system to produce the desired pressure drop and temperature reduction. It includes any arrangement, control method or improvement of such throttling elements used in combined heating-refrigeration cycles.
[F25B 2400/0413] for the filter or drier -- This subgroup covers filters or driers incorporated in refrigeration machines, plants or combined heating-refrigeration and heat-pump systems. It includes any general arrangement, construction or mounting of moisture-removing or contaminant-trapping devices that are not limited to a more specific category within the refrigeration field.
[F25B 2400/0415] for the receiver -- This subclass refers to components or arrangements designed for the receiver in refrigeration, combined heating-refrigeration, or heat-pump systems, covering any form of heat-absorbing element without limiting to a specific type of receiver.
[F25B 2400/0417] for the subcooler -- This classification concerns subcooler devices used in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including combined heating-refrigeration and heat-pump installations. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and general features of refrigeration equipment, and is not limited to a specific subgroup.
[F25B 2400/0419] for the superheater -- The F25B 2400/0419 subclass groups general elements of refrigeration, heating-cooling, or heat-pump systems that incorporate a superheater, covering devices that raise the temperature of a fluid after it has been condensed, without being confined to any more specific subcategory.
[F25B 2400/05] Compression system with heat exchange between particular parts of the system -- This subclass covers refrigeration or heat-pump machines where the compressor's heat is transferred to other components of the same system, such as intercoolers, economisers or the condenser, without limiting the type of equipment. It applies to any arrangement where heat exchange occurs between distinct parts of the compression cycle, including combined heating-refrigeration installations.
[F25B 2400/051] between the accumulator and another part of the cycle -- F25B 2400/051 covers devices, arrangements or connections that link the accumulator with other elements of a refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration cycle, such as compressors, evaporators or expansion valves. It defines the functional relationship and placement of the accumulator within the overall system.
[F25B 2400/052] between the capillary tube and another part of the refrigeration cycle -- This subclass concerns any component or arrangement located between the capillary tube and another element of a refrigeration, heating-refrigeration or heat-pump system, such as connections, fittings, accumulators or sensors that control flow or pressure.
[F25B 2400/053] between the storage receiver and another part of the system -- This subgroup covers components that connect the storage receiver to other parts of a refrigeration, heating or heat-pump system, such as piping, valves or heat exchangers. It includes mechanisms for transferring refrigerant or heat between the receiver and elements like compressors, condensers, evaporators or auxiliary devices.
[F25B 2400/054] between the suction tube of the compressor and another part of the cycle -- This subclass covers devices, arrangements or passages that connect the compressor's suction tube to another element of the refrigeration or heat-pump cycle, such as valves, filters, exchangers, accumulators or piping, enabling the flow of low-pressure refrigerant from the evaporator to the compressor.
[F25B 2400/06] Several compression cycles arranged in parallel -- F25B 2400/06 denotes refrigeration or heat-pump systems that employ two or more compression cycles operating side by side. The parallel arrangement allows the cycles to share load, improve efficiency, or provide redundancy, and it applies to general features of such machines rather than to a specific sub-type. This code falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, refrigeration, and refrigeration plants or systems.
[F25B 2400/061] the capacity of the first system being different from the second -- This subclass covers refrigeration, heating or heat-pump installations that comprise two linked systems whose capacities are not equal, such as combined heating-refrigeration or heat-pump arrangements where the first circuit's capacity differs from the second. It addresses design features that manage the disparity in performance between the paired subsystems.
[F25B 2400/07] Details of compressors or related parts -- This subclass addresses the specific design and construction details of compressors and their associated components used in refrigeration, heating or heat-pump systems. It includes descriptions of pistons, rotors, valves, drive mechanisms and other parts that form the compressor unit, without limiting to any particular type of compressor.
[F25B 2400/071] Compressor mounted in a housing in which a condenser is integrated -- A compressor that is installed inside a single housing which also contains an integrated condenser, forming a compact unit for refrigeration, combined heating-refrigeration, or heat-pump applications. The design consolidates the two major components in one enclosure to save space and simplify system assembly.
[F25B 2400/072] Intercoolers therefor -- F25B 2400/072 refers to intercoolers used in refrigeration, heating or heat-pump systems. These devices cool the refrigerant between compression stages to improve cycle efficiency and are not limited to any particular subgroup of refrigeration machines.
[F25B 2400/073] Linear compressors -- Linear compressors are a type of compressor in which the piston or moving element travels back and forth along a straight line to compress refrigerant, eliminating the crankshaft and rotating components of conventional compressors. This design reduces vibration, improves efficiency and is used in refrigeration machines, heating-refrigeration or heat-pump systems.
[F25B 2400/074] with multiple cylinders -- The code refers to refrigeration machines, plants or systems that employ more than one compression cylinder. These multiple-cylinder configurations are used in combined heating-refrigeration or heat-pump installations to increase capacity, improve efficiency or provide staged operation, without being limited to a specific sub-type of equipment.
[F25B 2400/075] with parallel compressors -- This subgroup concerns refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration systems that use two or more compressors operating in parallel, sharing load to improve efficiency, capacity control or reliability, without being limited to a more specific compressor arrangement.
[F25B 2400/0751] the compressors having different capacities -- This subgroup concerns refrigeration compressors designed with multiple or adjustable capacity levels, such as variable-displacement, multi-stage or modular units, enabling the machine to match output to varying load conditions and improve overall efficiency in cooling, heating or heat-pump systems.
[F25B 2400/076] having multiple cylinders driven by a rotating swash plate -- It covers refrigeration or heat-pump compressors that employ multiple cylinders driven by a rotating swash plate, which converts rotary motion into reciprocating piston motion. The feature is applicable to a wide range of refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2400/077] Compressor control units, e.g. terminal boxes, mounted on the compressor casing wall containing for example starter, protection switches or connector contacts -- It covers integrated control modules attached to the exterior of a refrigeration compressor, providing functions such as starting, protective switching and electrical connections. These units are typically mounted on the compressor housing and include terminal boxes, starter contacts, protection devices and connector interfaces.
[F25B 2400/08] Refrigeration machines, plants and systems having means for detecting the concentration of a refrigerant -- The subclass covers refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration machines, plants and systems that incorporate devices for detecting the concentration of the refrigerant. It applies to any arrangement where refrigerant-level or composition monitoring is integral to operation, such as leak detection or performance control.
[F25B 2400/09] Refrigeration machines, plants and systems having means for detecting the concentration of a sorbent solution -- The code covers refrigeration equipment or systems that incorporate sensors or analytical devices to monitor the concentration of a sorbent solution used in the cycle. Such detection enables control of performance, efficiency, or safety by adjusting operation based on solution strength. It applies to any refrigeration, heat-pump, or combined heating-cooling installations with this capability.
[F25B 2400/11] Drop catchers -- Drop catchers are devices fitted to refrigeration, heating-refrigeration or heat-pump systems to collect condensate droplets that fall from evaporators, condensers or other components, preventing water buildup and corrosion. They usually comprise a tray or funnel that channels the liquid to a drain or recovery line, improving system reliability.
[F25B 2400/12] Inflammable refrigerants
[F25B 2400/121] using R1234 -- F25B 2400/121 classifies refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration systems that employ the refrigerant R1234. It covers general features or devices for such machines or plants without restricting to a more specific subgroup within the broader mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F25B 2400/13] Economisers -- Economisers are components in refrigeration, heating-and-cooling or heat-pump installations that recover waste heat from the condenser to raise the temperature of the refrigerant before it expands, thereby reducing energy consumption and improving overall system efficiency.
[F25B 2400/14] Power generation using energy from the expansion of the refrigerant -- This classification covers systems where the expansion of a refrigerant in a refrigeration cycle is harnessed to produce mechanical or electrical power, such as in combined heat-pump or power-recovery units. It includes devices that extract work from the pressure drop of the refrigerant rather than solely for cooling.
[F25B 2400/141] the extracted power is not recycled back in the refrigerant circuit -- This CPC subclass covers refrigeration, cooling or heat-pump systems in which the power extracted from the cycle-such as waste heat, mechanical or electrical energy-is not returned to the refrigerant circuit for recompression or circulation, but is instead discharged or used elsewhere.
[F25B 2400/15] Microelectro-mechanical devices -- F25B 2400/15 pertains to micro-electro-mechanical devices used in refrigeration, combined heating-refrigeration, or heat-pump systems. It includes miniature actuators, sensors, switches or control elements that integrate electrical and mechanical functions within the overall plant, without being limited to any specific sub-group.
[F25B 2400/16] Receivers -- Receivers are components in refrigeration, combined heating-refrigeration or heat-pump systems that temporarily store refrigerant, providing a buffer between compressor and evaporator and allowing separation of liquid and vapor phases. They help smooth operation, control capacity and accommodate excess refrigerant in the cycle.
[F25B 2400/161] arranged in parallel -- F25B 2400/161 covers refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration systems in which two or more units or components are installed side-by-side and operate in parallel, sharing the load or providing redundancy. This arrangement allows the parallel units to run simultaneously or independently, enhancing capacity, efficiency or reliability.
[F25B 2400/162] characterised by the plug or stop -- The code F25B 2400/162 designates refrigeration, heating or heat-pump machines whose construction includes a plug or a stop as a distinguishing element, covering general-purpose devices rather than a specific sub-type. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigeration machines, plants or systems, and is used in both EPO and USPTO classifications.
[F25B 2400/17] Re-condensers -- Re-condensers are components in refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration systems that condense the refrigerant vapor after it has expanded, returning it to a liquid state before it re-enters the compressor. They are not limited to a specific sub-type of system and may be integrated with various plant configurations for efficient heat recovery and temperature control.
[F25B 2400/18] Refrigerant conversion -- F25B 2400/18 covers methods and apparatus for converting one refrigerant into another within refrigeration, cooling or heat-pump systems, such as retrofitting existing equipment to use a different working fluid or recovering and re-charging refrigerant mixtures. It includes devices that modify, replace or recycle refrigerants to meet performance, safety or environmental requirements.
[F25B 2400/19] Pumping down refrigerant from one part of the cycle to another part of the cycle, e.g. when the cycle is changed from cooling to heating, or before a defrost cycle is started -- F25B 2400/19 covers methods and devices that move refrigerant from one part of a refrigeration cycle to another, for example when switching a system from cooling to heating or before a defrost cycle begins. It relates to general features of refrigeration machines, plants or systems rather than a specific subgroup.
[F25B 2400/21] Modules for refrigeration systems -- F25B 2400/21 relates to modules for refrigeration systems, covering general-purpose components or assemblies - such as compact units that integrate compressors, heat exchangers, valves or controls - used in refrigeration machines, combined heating-refrigeration setups or heat-pump installations without being limited to a specific sub-category.
[F25B 2400/22] Refrigeration systems for supermarkets -- This subclass relates to refrigeration systems designed for supermarkets, including the integrated cooling of display cases, walk-in chillers and air-handling equipment needed to maintain low temperatures throughout the store. It covers overall system configurations, control strategies and combined heating-refrigeration arrangements specific to retail food environments.
[F25B 2400/23] Separators -- Separators are components inserted in refrigeration, heat-pump or combined heating-refrigeration circuits to remove solid particles, liquid droplets or other contaminants from the refrigerant flow, thereby protecting downstream equipment such as compressors and improving overall system efficiency. They are applicable across a wide range of refrigeration machines, plants and systems.
[F25B 2400/24] Storage receiver heat -- General devices and arrangements for storing heat in a receiver within refrigeration, cooling or heat-pump systems, enabling temporary thermal energy retention for later use or for combined heating-refrigeration operation. This includes receivers that accumulate heat from the refrigerant cycle.
[F25B 25/00] Machines, plants or systems, using a combination of modes of operation covered by two or more of the groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines, plants or systems, using a combination of modes of operation covered by two or more of the groups F25B1/00 - F25B23/00 .
[F25B 2500/00] Problems to be solved -- The subclass F25B 2500/00 groups the technical problems that inventions in refrigeration machines, plants or systems aim to overcome, such as improving energy efficiency, reducing noise, enhancing reliability, minimizing environmental impact or simplifying control. It serves as a reference point for identifying the specific challenges addressed by a patent application in this mechanical-engineering area.
[F25B 2500/01] Geometry problems, e.g. for reducing size -- F25B 2500/01 relates to geometry problems in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, focusing on design modifications that reduce overall dimensions, improve compactness, or optimise the spatial arrangement of components. It includes methods and devices for reshaping, scaling or rearranging parts to achieve size reduction while maintaining performance.
[F25B 2500/02] Increasing the heating capacity of a reversible cycle during cold outdoor conditions -- This CPC subgroup deals with techniques for boosting the heating performance of reversible refrigeration cycles, such as heat-pump systems, when operating in cold outdoor environments. It covers modifications to cycle refrigerant flow, compression, or heat-exchanger design that raise the delivered heat output under low ambient temperatures.
[F25B 2500/03] Cavitations -- The CPC code F25B 2500/03 covers cavitation issues that arise in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, particularly in compressors where vapor bubbles form and collapse in the fluid. It addresses problems such as performance loss, noise generation, increased wear and possible damage caused by these bubble dynamics, and includes methods for detecting, preventing or mitigating cavitation effects.
[F25B 2500/04] Clogging -- F25B 2500/04 relates to clogging in refrigeration or cooling machines, plants and systems, addressing blockages that impair the flow of refrigerant, coolant or air through components such as condensers, evaporators, pipelines or filters, and the measures needed to prevent or clear such obstructions.
[F25B 2500/05] Cost reduction -- The F25B 2500/05 subclass pertains to measures that lower the overall expense of refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including design, material or operational choices that reduce manufacturing, installation or running costs. It covers approaches such as simplifying construction, using cheaper components, improving energy efficiency, and streamlining maintenance to achieve cost-effective refrigeration solutions.
[F25B 2500/06] Damage -- The code F25B 2500/06 relates to problems of damage occurring in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It covers the identification, analysis, prevention, and mitigation of failures or impairments affecting components such as compressors, condensers, evaporators, and control units within cooling equipment. This classification is used to index inventions that address the causes, effects, or repair methods for such damage.
[F25B 2500/07] Exceeding a certain pressure value in a refrigeration component or cycle -- F25B 2500/07 covers methods and devices for detecting when the pressure in a refrigeration component or cycle surpasses a predetermined threshold and for initiating corrective actions such as venting, shutdown, or pressure relief. It is applied to refrigeration machines, plants, and systems to protect against over-pressure damage and maintain safe operation.
[F25B 2500/08] Exceeding a certain temperature value in a refrigeration component or cycle -- The code F25B 2500/08 covers technologies that intentionally allow a refrigeration component or cycle to rise above a predefined temperature limit, such as during defrosting, heat-recovery, or safety shut-down procedures. It includes control strategies, sensors and system designs that detect and manage that temperature exceedance.
[F25B 2500/09] Improving heat transfers -- F25B 2500/09 covers any technique or device that increases the efficiency of heat exchange in refrigeration or cooling equipment, such as modified surfaces, flow arrangements, or enhanced thermal-conductive materials. The aim is to improve the rate of heat transfer while minimizing energy consumption and system size.
[F25B 2500/11] Reducing heat transfers -- F25B 2500/11 deals with techniques that limit or minimize heat flow in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including insulating components, optimizing heat-exchanger designs, and controlling temperature gradients to improve energy efficiency.
[F25B 2500/12] Sound -- F25B 2500/12 covers aspects of sound generated by refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including noise reduction, acoustic insulation, mufflers and methods for measuring or controlling acoustic emissions. It addresses problems of unwanted noise in mechanical cooling equipment.
[F25B 2500/13] Vibrations -- This CPC group covers technical measures and design aspects aimed at reducing or controlling vibratory phenomena in refrigeration equipment such as compressors, condensers and related plant components. It includes analysis of vibration sources, isolation, damping, balancing and mounting methods to improve performance, durability and noise.
[F25B 2500/14] the presence of moisture in a refrigeration component or cycle -- F25B 2500/14 covers measures for dealing with moisture that may be present in any part of a refrigeration system or cycle. It includes methods for removing, detecting or mitigating water vapor, condensate or ice that can impair performance, cause corrosion or reduce efficiency. The classification is used for inventions that address moisture-related problems in refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2500/15] Hunting, i.e. oscillation of controlled refrigeration variables reaching undesirable values -- The entry covers the problem of hunting, where control-loop variables in refrigeration machines such as temperature, pressure or flow rate oscillate beyond acceptable limits, causing instability and reduced efficiency. It addresses detection, analysis and corrective measures to prevent or damp these undesirable oscillations.
[F25B 2500/16] Lubrication -- The F25B 2500/16 subclass covers lubrication in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, describing methods, devices and arrangements for supplying lubricant to compressors, condensers, expanders or related components to reduce friction, wear and heat, improve efficiency and ensure reliable operation of cooling equipment.
[F25B 2500/17] Size reduction -- F25B 2500/17 covers techniques and devices for reducing the size of components or working fluids in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, such as compressors, condensers, evaporators or related equipment. It includes methods that achieve a more compact design while maintaining performance and efficiency.
[F25B 2500/18] Optimization, e.g. high integration of refrigeration components -- The CPC class F25B 2500/18 covers methods and designs that optimise refrigeration systems by tightly integrating components such as compressors, condensers, evaporators and controls. It includes high-density layouts, combined functions and other approaches that improve efficiency, compactness or performance of mechanical refrigeration machines and plant installations.
[F25B 2500/19] Calculation of parameters -- F25B 2500/19 pertains to methods for determining the operating characteristics of refrigeration equipment. It covers calculations of thermodynamic parameters such as capacity, coefficient of performance, power consumption and efficiency for compressors, condensers, evaporators and complete cooling systems.
[F25B 2500/21] Reduction of parts -- The F25B 2500/21 subclass concerns refrigeration machines, plants or systems in which the number of components is deliberately reduced to simplify construction, lower weight, size or cost, or improve reliability. It covers design approaches that combine functions, eliminate auxiliary parts, or replace multiple elements with a single integrated unit.
[F25B 2500/22] Preventing, detecting or repairing leaks of refrigeration fluids -- The CPC subclass F25B 2500/22 covers technologies for preventing, detecting or repairing leaks of refrigeration fluids in mechanical engineering cooling systems, including sensors, sealing methods, diagnostic tools and repair techniques used in refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2500/221] Preventing leaks from developing -- F25B 2500/221 addresses methods and apparatus for preventing the development of leaks in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It includes designs that seal joints, use leak-detecting sensors, maintain pressure differentials or employ barrier materials to stop refrigerant escape, thereby enhancing safety and efficiency.
[F25B 2500/222] Detecting refrigerant leaks -- F25B 2500/222 covers techniques and apparatus for detecting refrigerant leaks in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, including sensors, monitoring devices, and diagnostic methods that identify, locate or quantify escaped refrigerant during operation or testing.
[F25B 2500/23] High amount of refrigerant in the system -- F25B 2500/23 covers refrigeration or cooling systems that operate with an unusually large quantity of refrigerant. It pertains to designs where the refrigerant charge is increased to improve heat transfer, capacity, or to meet specific thermodynamic requirements, often in large-scale or high-performance installations.
[F25B 2500/24] Low amount of refrigerant in the system -- F25B 2500/24 covers refrigerating equipment that operates with a reduced refrigerant charge. It includes designs, components and control strategies that minimise the amount of fluid needed while maintaining cooling performance, often to improve safety, reduce emissions or comply with regulations. The subclass addresses solutions for low-refrigerant systems in mechanical engineering.
[F25B 2500/25] Standardisation of apparatus or parts -- Standardisation of apparatus or parts refers to the development and application of uniform specifications, dimensions, performance criteria or interface requirements for refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It enables interchangeable components, facilitates compliance with industry norms and supports consistent quality and safety across mechanical-engineering cooling technologies.
[F25B 2500/26] characterised by the startup of the refrigeration cycle -- This subclass of mechanical engineering deals with refrigeration machines, plants or systems that are specifically characterised by the startup of the refrigeration cycle, covering devices, control methods or sequences that initiate and stabilise the cycle when the system is turned on.
[F25B 2500/27] characterised by the stop of the refrigeration cycle -- F25B 2500/27 covers refrigeration machines, plants or systems in which the refrigeration cycle is intentionally halted, for example during defrosting, standby operation, or maintenance. The subclass addresses designs and control methods that stop the compressor or other cycle components to interrupt cooling.
[F25B 2500/28] Means for preventing liquid refrigerant entering into the compressor -- This subclass covers devices that stop liquid refrigerant from reaching the compressor, protecting it from damage caused by liquid slugging. It includes accumulators, suction line filters, suction dryers, and other control valves that separate or hold liquid before the compressor inlet.
[F25B 2500/29] High ambient temperatures -- The F25B 2500/29 subclass deals with refrigeration machines, plants or systems that must operate efficiently in high ambient temperatures, addressing challenges such as reduced cooling capacity, increased energy demand and stress on components. It includes design features, control strategies and material choices that enable reliable performance when the surrounding air temperature is unusually hot.
[F25B 2500/31] Low ambient temperatures -- The code F25B 2500/31 designates solutions where refrigeration equipment must operate efficiently in low ambient temperature conditions. It covers methods, devices, or system designs that maintain performance despite cold surroundings, addressing challenges such as reduced heat exchange and increased energy consumption.
[F25B 2500/32] Weight -- F25B 2500/32 concerns inventions related to the weight of refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It covers aspects such as reducing overall mass, balancing components, or handling load requirements to improve performance, installation or transport.
[F25B 25/005] using primary and secondary systems -- F25B 25/005 refers to refrigeration or cooling machines that use both a primary refrigeration system and an additional secondary system, such as a cascade or heat-recovery loop, to combine different modes of operation. The arrangement improves overall efficiency by linking two cycles within one plant.
[F25B 25/02] Compression-sorption machines, plants, or systems -- Compression-sorption machines, plants or systems combine a mechanical compressor with a sorption (absorption or adsorption) cycle to produce refrigeration, heating or heat-pump effects, integrating the two processes in a single unit or plant. This subclass covers the integrated equipment, its components and overall arrangements.
[F25B 2600/00] Control issues -- The CPC subclass F25B 2600/00 covers control aspects of refrigeration machines, plants and systems, including the design, implementation and optimization of control strategies, regulation, automation, safety mechanisms and performance monitoring. It addresses issues such as temperature regulation, load management and fault detection in cooling equipment.
[F25B 2600/01] Timing -- The F25B 2600/01 timing entry addresses control methods that govern the timing and sequencing of refrigeration and cooling equipment, such as the start-stop cycles of compressors, valves, and heat exchangers, to optimize performance and safety. It includes devices and algorithms that coordinate operational phases within refrigeration plants.
[F25B 2600/02] Compressor control -- F25B 2600/02 covers the control of compressors used in refrigeration and cooling systems. It includes methods and devices that regulate compressor speed, pressure, temperature or capacity to maintain desired cooling performance and protect equipment. The class is part of the CPC subgroup for mechanical engineering refrigeration control issues.
[F25B 2600/021] Inverters therefor -- The F25B 2600/021 entry concerns inverters employed in refrigeration or cooling equipment, specifically devices that convert electrical power to control the operation of compressors, fans or other mechanical components in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It addresses the electronic control methods used to regulate speed and performance.
[F25B 2600/022] for multi-stage operation -- F25B 2600/022 refers to control techniques and devices used in refrigeration or cooling machines that operate in more than one stage, such as sequential compressors, staged expansion valves or multi-stage temperature regulation. The entry covers methods for coordinating the stages to optimise efficiency, capacity and temperature stability.
[F25B 2600/023] controlling swash plate angles -- This subclass covers methods and devices for regulating the tilt or angular position of a swash plate in refrigeration compressors or related machinery, typically to adjust displacement, capacity or efficiency. It includes control mechanisms, sensors and actuators that manage the plate's angle during operation.
[F25B 2600/024] by controlling the electric parameters, e.g. current or voltage -- This subclass pertains to control strategies for refrigeration or cooling systems that manage the machine's operation by varying electrical quantities such as voltage, current, or power. Adjusting these parameters can regulate compressor speed, temperature set-points, or energy consumption, and is used in electronic controllers for chillers and domestic refrigerators.
[F25B 2600/025] by controlling speed -- F25B 2600/025 pertains to refrigeration machines or systems in which the operating speed of components such as compressors, fans or pumps is regulated to achieve desired performance. It includes methods, sensors, controllers or drive arrangements that adjust speed in response to load, temperature or efficiency criteria.
[F25B 2600/0251] with on-off operation -- This classification covers refrigeration or cooling machines where the control system operates in a simple on-off mode, without intermediate modulation. It applies to devices such as compressors or chillers that are started or stopped directly based on a binary control signal.
[F25B 2600/0252] with two speeds -- The subclass F25B 2600/0252 pertains to control aspects of refrigeration machines or systems that operate at two distinct speeds, such as dual-speed compressors or fan drives, enabling variable capacity or efficiency in cooling applications.
[F25B 2600/0253] with variable speed -- This classification covers refrigeration or cooling equipment where the operating speed of a component, such as a compressor, can be varied to regulate performance. It pertains to control strategies that adjust speed to match load or improve efficiency, including variable-speed drives and related control mechanisms.
[F25B 2600/026] by controlling unloaders -- The class covers control techniques for unloaders in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, focusing on how the unloading of compressors or other components is regulated to adjust capacity, improve efficiency and maintain desired operating conditions.
[F25B 2600/0261] external to the compressor -- The CPC subclass F25B 2600/0261 relates to control functions that are located outside the compressor of a refrigeration or cooling system. It covers external control devices, sensors and circuitry that regulate compressor operation, pressure or temperature.
[F25B 2600/0262] internal to the compressor -- The entry covers control functions that are built into the compressor itself, such as regulation of pressure, temperature, speed or internal valve operation to optimise performance and safety of refrigeration machines and systems. It includes methods and devices that monitor and adjust these parameters from within the compressor housing.
[F25B 2600/027] by controlling pressure -- The F25B 2600/027 classification covers techniques for regulating pressure within refrigeration or cooling installations, including pressure-sensing, valve actuation, and control algorithms that maintain desired pressure levels to optimise performance and safety of refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2600/0271] the discharge pressure -- F25B 2600/0271 classifies control issues that relate to the discharge pressure of refrigeration machines, plants or systems, covering methods and devices for monitoring, regulating or adjusting the pressure at the compressor outlet to ensure safe and efficient operation. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically the subsection on refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2600/0272] the suction pressure -- F25B 2600/0272 is a Cooperative Patent Classification code for control issues relating to suction pressure in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It covers methods, devices or regulation techniques that monitor or adjust the suction pressure to optimise the operation of cooling equipment.
[F25B 2600/05] Refrigerant levels -- F25B 2600/05 addresses the monitoring and regulation of refrigerant quantity in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, focusing on methods and devices that maintain proper refrigerant levels for efficient operation. It relates to control strategies that adjust and supervise refrigerant amounts during normal use.
[F25B 2600/07] Remote controls -- F25B 2600/07 addresses remote-control devices for refrigeration systems, covering apparatus that enables starting, stopping, or adjusting the operation of compressors, condensers, or related plant components from a distance. It includes handheld, wireless or wired controllers designed to influence cooling performance without direct manual interaction with the machinery.
[F25B 2600/11] Fan speed control -- The code F25B 2600/11 belongs to mechanical engineering, under refrigeration or cooling, and deals with control issues for fan speed in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It covers methods and devices that regulate fan rotational speed to optimise cooling performance and energy consumption.
[F25B 2600/111] of condenser fans -- The classification F25B 2600/111 pertains to the control of condenser fans in refrigeration or cooling systems, addressing operational, speed-regulation and monitoring functions. It is located under mechanical engineering - refrigeration or cooling - refrigeration machines, plants or systems - control issues.
[F25B 2600/112] of evaporator fans -- This classification covers control issues related to evaporator fans in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It includes the design, regulation, monitoring and protection of fan speed and operation to ensure efficient heat exchange and overall system reliability.
[F25B 2600/13] Pump speed control -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and devices for regulating the speed of pumps used in refrigeration and cooling systems, including variable-frequency drives, throttling mechanisms and electronic control strategies that adjust flow and pressure. It covers both mechanical and electronic techniques applied to compressors or circulating pumps in refrigeration plants.
[F25B 2600/15] during shut down -- F25B 2600/15 addresses the control aspects of refrigeration machines, plants or systems during shutdown. It includes the design, operation and regulation of devices and procedures that manage safe power-off sequencing, temperature maintenance, pressure relief and prevention of refrigerant leakage when the system is turned off. This code is used to classify inventions that improve shutdown control for cooling installations.
[F25B 2600/17] by controlling the pressure of the condenser -- The entry F25B 2600/17 covers control techniques that adjust the pressure of a refrigeration condenser, such as valves, regulators or electronic controllers, to maintain optimal operation and efficiency of the cooling cycle. It relates to mechanical-engineering solutions for refrigeration machines and systems, focusing on how pressure control improves performance, safety and energy use.
[F25B 2600/19] Refrigerant outlet condenser temperature -- This subclass concerns the temperature of refrigerant as it leaves the condenser in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, encompassing methods for measuring, monitoring or controlling that outlet temperature to optimise performance and energy efficiency. It addresses design and operational features where precise condenser outlet temperature is critical for cycle regulation.
[F25B 2600/21] Refrigerant outlet evaporator temperature -- This CPC subclass relates to control measures for the temperature of refrigerant leaving the evaporator in refrigeration machines or systems, covering methods, devices or strategies that monitor or regulate that outlet temperature to ensure efficient and safe operation.
[F25B 2600/23] Time delays -- F25B 2600/23 covers time-delay features used in the control of refrigeration machines, plants or systems, such as delayed start, stop or cycling to improve efficiency, protect components or manage load. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically addresses control-related aspects of these devices.
[F25B 2600/25] Control of valves -- The CPC class F25B covers refrigeration machines, plants and systems and its subgroup 2600/25 relates to the control of valves within these installations. It includes valve actuation, regulation and monitoring techniques that manage refrigerant flow and system performance.
[F25B 2600/2501] Bypass valves -- Bypass valves are control devices used in refrigeration machines and plant systems to divert refrigerant around components such as compressors or heat exchangers, enabling regulation of pressure and temperature during start-up, shutdown or load changes. They help maintain stable operation and protect equipment.
[F25B 2600/2503] Condenser exit valves -- Condenser exit valves refer to the devices positioned at the outlet of a refrigeration or cooling condenser that regulate the flow of condensed refrigerant into the subsequent part of the system. They control pressure, flow rate, and direction to ensure proper operation, protect downstream components, and enable efficient heat-exchange performance.
[F25B 2600/2505] Fixed-differential control valves -- Fixed-differential control valves are devices used in refrigeration systems to regulate fluid flow so that a predetermined pressure difference is maintained across the valve. They ensure stable operation of compressors, condensers or evaporators by automatically adjusting opening based on the measured pressure differential.
[F25B 2600/2507] Flow-diverting valves -- The entry F25B 2600/2507 concerns flow-diverting valves used in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. These valves are designed to redirect or split refrigerant or coolant streams for control, balancing, or safety purposes, and are applied in various mechanical-engineering cooling installations.
[F25B 2600/2509] Economiser valves -- F25B 2600/2509 covers economiser valves, which are control components in refrigeration or cooling equipment that regulate the flow of refrigerant to enhance heat-exchange efficiency and reduce energy consumption. These valves are used in systems such as chillers and heat pumps to manage temperature differentials and improve overall plant performance.
[F25B 2600/2511] Evaporator distribution valves -- The F25B 2600/2511 subclass covers valves that distribute refrigerant from a common line to one or more evaporators, allowing balanced flow and precise temperature control in refrigeration or cooling systems. It is situated under Mechanical Engineering → Refrigeration or Cooling → Refrigeration machines, plants or systems → Control issues.
[F25B 2600/2513] Expansion valves -- Expansion valves are flow-regulating components in refrigeration cycles that control the rate at which liquid refrigerant expands to low pressure, thereby managing temperature and pressure drop in the evaporator. They are integral to the control of refrigeration machines and plant performance, ensuring efficient cooling and system stability.
[F25B 2600/2515] Flow valves -- The F25B 2600/2515 subclass concerns flow valves used in refrigeration or cooling installations. It covers devices that regulate the movement of refrigerant or coolant through a system, such as expansion, thermostatic, solenoid or shut-off valves, and includes their design, operation and integration with the surrounding plant. This entry falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigeration machines, plants or systems → control issues.
[F25B 2600/2517] Head-pressure valves -- The CPC subclass F25B 2600/2517 pertains to head-pressure valves, devices used in refrigeration and cooling machines to regulate the high-pressure side of the refrigerant circuit, ensuring proper flow control, system stability and safe operation.
[F25B 2600/2519] On-off valves -- On-off valves are control components used in refrigeration machines and plant systems to start or stop the flow of refrigerant, enabling precise regulation of cooling cycles and system safety. They are classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically address control issues in the CPC hierarchy.
[F25B 2600/2521] On-off valves controlled by pulse signals -- The code F25B 2600/2521 refers to refrigeration-system control devices that use pulse-signal actuation to open or close on-off valves. It classifies valves whose operation is governed by short electrical pulses, a common method for regulating flow in chillers, compressors and other cooling plant components.
[F25B 2600/2523] Receiver valves -- Receiver valves are components in refrigeration systems that collect and store excess refrigerant from the compressor, regulating its flow back into the circuit to maintain pressure and temperature stability. They serve as a control point ensuring safe and efficient operation of cooling plants and systems.
[F25B 2600/2525] Pressure relief valves -- It covers devices that protect refrigeration or cooling systems by automatically releasing excess pressure to prevent overload or damage. These pressure relief valves are used in compressors, condensers, and related plant components to maintain safe operation.
[F25B 27/00] Machines, plants or systems, using particular sources of energy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines, plants or systems, using particular sources of energy should be read as driven by particular sources of energy.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Heat pumps characterised by the source of low potential heat -> F25B30/06
[F25B 2700/00] Sensing or detecting of parameters -- The F25B 2700/00 class covers any sensor or detector that measures operating variables such as temperature, pressure, flow, level or power in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, providing the data needed for control, safety or performance monitoring.

[F25B 2700/03] Oil level -- The code covers sensors or detection means for monitoring oil level in refrigeration machines or related plant components, ensuring proper lubrication and safe operation. It includes devices that measure, indicate or control the quantity of oil present in the system.
[F25B 2700/04] Refrigerant level -- F25B 2700/04 covers methods and devices for measuring or monitoring the amount of refrigerant within a refrigeration system, such as sensors, gauges or electronic detectors used in compressors, condensers and other plant components. It relates to the sensing or detecting of refrigerant level in refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 2700/05] Load shedding of a compressor -- F25B 2700/05 relates to load-shedding methods for compressors in refrigeration systems, where sensors monitor operating parameters and trigger partial or full power reduction to prevent overload, conserve energy, or maintain desired temperature conditions.
[F25B 2700/06] Piston positions of a compressor -- F25B 2700/06 relates to the sensing or detection of piston positions in compressors used in refrigeration or cooling machines. It includes methods and devices that monitor the location of each piston during the compression cycle to control performance, safety and efficiency.
[F25B 2700/11] Sensor to detect if defrost is necessary -- The classification F25B 2700/11 relates to sensors used in refrigeration equipment that monitor parameters indicating frost formation and determine when a defrost cycle should be initiated. These devices typically measure temperature, humidity, ice thickness or heat flow to trigger automatic defrosting in cooling machines.
[F25B 2700/111] using an emitter and receiver, e.g. sensing by emitting light or other radiation and receiving reflection by a sensor -- F25B 2700/111 covers refrigeration or cooling equipment that incorporates sensors which emit radiation such as light or other electromagnetic waves and detect the reflected signal with a receiver. The technique is used to monitor parameters like temperature, pressure or flow within the system by analysing the returned radiation.
[F25B 2700/13] Mass flow of refrigerants -- The entry F25B 2700/13 refers to methods and devices for measuring the mass flow rate of refrigerants within refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the refrigeration or cooling area, and addresses the sensing or detection of this key operating parameter.
[F25B 2700/131] at the outlet of a subcooler -- The code designates the detection or measurement of operating parameters, such as temperature or pressure, at the outlet of a sub-cooler in a refrigeration system, used to monitor and control the performance of the cooling cycle.

[F25B 2700/2117] of an evaporator
