[F25B 2700/21172] at the inlet -- F25B 2700/21172 covers devices or methods for sensing or detecting operating parameters at the inlet of refrigeration machines, plants or systems, such as temperature, pressure or flow rate, to monitor and control the cooling process.
[F25B 2700/21173] at the outlet -- This CPC subclass refers to devices or methods that sense or detect parameters at the outlet of refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It includes temperature, pressure, flow or other condition monitoring components positioned at the discharge side of compressors, condensers, evaporators or related piping.
[F25B 2700/21174] of the refrigerant at the inlet of the evaporator -- This CPC subclass covers methods or devices for sensing or detecting the refrigerant's properties or amount at the inlet of the evaporator in refrigeration or cooling systems. It is positioned under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigeration machines, plants or systems → sensing or detecting of parameters.
[F25B 2700/21175] of the refrigerant at the outlet of the evaporator -- The CPC entry F25B 2700/21175 covers devices or methods that sense or detect the refrigerant's condition at the outlet of an evaporator in a refrigeration system. It pertains to monitoring parameters such as temperature, pressure, or flow to enable control and optimisation of the cooling cycle. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration machines and the detection of operating parameters.
[F25B 27/005] in compression type systems -- F25B 27/005 covers compression-type refrigeration or cooling machines that use a particular source of energy, such as electricity, gas or waste heat, to drive the compressor. It includes the configuration of compressors, condensers and evaporators within systems that rely on compression to move heat.
[F25B 27/007] in sorption type systems -- F25B 27/007 covers refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that operate on sorption principles, such as absorption or adsorption cycles, using heat from sources like gas, solar or waste energy to drive the refrigeration process.
[F25B 27/02] using waste heat, e.g. from internal-combustion engines -- The subclass F25B 27/02 covers refrigeration or cooling installations that recover waste heat, for example the exhaust or coolant of an internal-combustion engine, to provide the energy needed for the refrigeration cycle. It belongs to the group of machines, plants or systems that use specific energy sources within mechanical engineering.
[F25B 29/00] Combined heating and refrigeration systems, e.g. operating alternately or simultaneously -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combined heating and refrigeration systems, e.g. operating alternately or simultaneously should be read as operating simultaneously.
[F25B 29/003] of the compression type system -- A combined heating and refrigeration system of the compression type integrates a compressor-driven cycle that can supply cooling and, in the same plant, provide heating either by alternating operation or by simultaneous heat recovery. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems, and is classified as F25B 29/003 in the CPC.
[F25B 29/006] of the sorption type system -- This subclass pertains to combined heating and refrigeration installations that operate by sorption, using a material to absorb or adsorb a refrigerant and release it through heat input to provide cooling or heating, often alternating or simultaneous.
[F25B 3/00] Self-contained rotary compression machines, i.e. with compressor, condenser and evaporator rotating as a single unit -- Self-contained rotary compression machines are refrigeration units where the compressor, condenser and evaporator are integrated and rotate together as a single assembly, providing a compact, modular cooling solution for applications such as portable fridges, automotive air-conditioning and small-scale chillers. This design eliminates separate piping and supports efficient heat exchange within a unified rotating body.
[F25B 30/00] Heat pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refrigeration systems employed for heating, by using the heat given off by the condenser.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compression machines, plants or systems with non-reversible cycle; Machines, plants or systems, using a combination of modes of operation covered by two or more of the groups -> F25B1/00 - F25B25/00 - Combined heating and refrigeration systems, e.g. operating alternately or simultaneously -> F25B29/00
[F25B 30/02] of the compression type -- F25B 30/02 is a CPC subclass for heat-pump devices that operate by a compression cycle. It covers apparatus and methods where a refrigerant is compressed to raise its temperature for heating or cooling, including related components such as compressors, condensers and expansion devices.
[F25B 30/04] of the sorption type -- This group covers heat pumps that operate on sorption principles, where a refrigerant is absorbed or adsorbed by a solid or liquid sorbent to provide heating or cooling without mechanical compression.
[F25B 30/06] characterised by the source of low potential heat -- The class F25B 30/06 covers heat-pump installations whose design or operation is defined by the origin of the low-potential heat they extract. It includes systems that draw heat from sources such as waste heat, ambient air, solar thermal collectors, ground or water bodies, where the source itself is a distinguishing feature of the pump's cycle.
[F25B 31/00] Component parts or details -- F25B 31/00 covers the individual components and detailed parts used in refrigeration machines, plants or systems, such as compressors, heat exchangers, valves, and control devices. It pertains to the design, construction and functional features of these elements within the broader field of mechanical engineering for cooling and refrigeration.
[F25B 31/002] Lubrication -- F25B 31/002 addresses the provision of lubrication for the moving parts of refrigeration machines and related systems. It includes methods, devices and arrangements for delivering oil or other lubricants to compressors, valves, evaporators and similar components to reduce wear, control temperature and improve efficiency.
[F25B 31/004] oil recirculating arrangements -- F25B 31/004 covers oil recirculation arrangements in refrigeration machines or systems, such as pumps, channels, and valves that return oil to the compressor or other components to ensure lubrication and heat removal.
[F25B 31/006] Cooling of compressor or motor -- F25B 31/006 relates to techniques for cooling the compressor or motor in refrigeration machines and systems. It includes designs such as water-cooled, oil-cooled, or air-cooled arrangements that remove heat from the drive unit to improve efficiency and prevent overheating. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, component parts or details.
[F25B 31/008] by injecting a liquid -- F25B 31/008 classifies refrigeration or cooling apparatus that employs a liquid-injection method, such as injecting a refrigerant liquid into the compressor, heat exchanger, or circuit to enhance cooling performance, control pressure, or improve efficiency. The entry covers component parts and detailed implementations of this liquid-injection technique in refrigeration machines and systems.
[F25B 31/02] of motor-compressor units -- F25B 31/02 covers the component parts and details of motor-compressor units employed in refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It includes the design, construction, mounting, coupling and sealing of the motor and compressor as an integrated assembly within the broader field of mechanical engineering for cooling applications.
[F25B 31/023] with compressor of reciprocating-piston type -- This subgroup covers refrigeration or cooling machines that incorporate a compressor of the reciprocating-piston type, focusing on the design and arrangement of that component within the system. It belongs to CPC class F25B, which deals with component parts and details of refrigeration equipment.
[F25B 31/026] with compressor of rotary type -- The subclass F25B 31/026 refers to refrigeration or heat-pump systems whose compressor is of a rotary type, such as screw, scroll or rotary-vane compressors. It covers component-level features and arrangements specific to these rotary compressors within the overall refrigeration machine or plant.
[F25B 33/00] Boilers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Boilers/generators: that part of an absorption machine in which the refrigerant vapour is driven off by heat.  Analysers: a small fractionating column, in an absorption machine between generator and rectifier or condenser.  Rectifiers: that part of an absorption machine in which entrained absorbent is removed from the refrigerant vapour before condensation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Boiler-absorbers -> F25B35/00
[F25B 35/00] Boiler-absorbers, i.e. boilers usable for absorption or adsorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Boiler-absorbers, i.e. boilers usable for absorption or adsorption
[F25B 35/02] using a liquid as sorbent, e.g. brine -- The classification covers refrigeration or cooling systems that include boiler-absorbers where a liquid, such as brine, is employed as the sorbent to absorb or adsorb refrigerant. It pertains to the design and operation of such boiler-absorbers within refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 35/04] using a solid as sorbent -- The F25B 35/04 subclass deals with refrigeration or cooling systems that employ a solid material as the sorbent in boiler-absorber units, where the solid absorbs or adsorbs a refrigerant to drive the cooling cycle. It covers designs, methods and arrangements that use solids rather than liquids for the absorption or adsorption process in such boilers.
[F25B 37/00] Absorbers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Boiler-absorbers -> F25B35/00
[F25B 39/00] Evaporators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Evaporators: heat exchangers in which liquid refrigerant is vaporized to produce refrigeration;  Condensers: heat exchangers in which refrigerant vapour is liquefied by removal of heat.
[F25B 39/02] Evaporators -- F25B 39/02 designates evaporators, the components in refrigeration or cooling systems that absorb heat from a space or process by turning liquid refrigerant into vapor. It includes all configurations such as shell-and-tube, plate, finned-tube, and spray-type units used in machines, plants or system installations for cooling or freezing applications. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Refrigeration or Cooling → Refrigeration Machines, Plants or Systems → Evaporators.
[F25B 39/022] with plate-like or laminated elements -- F25B 39/022 covers evaporators that employ plate-like or laminated elements to increase heat-transfer area in refrigeration or cooling systems. The design provides compact, efficient heat exchange and is used in various mechanical engineering applications for improved performance.
[F25B 39/024] with elements constructed in the shape of a hollow panel -- This subclass pertains to refrigeration evaporators whose heat-exchange elements are made as hollow panels, integrating fluid passages within a rigid, panel-like structure. The design enhances heat transfer efficiency and allows a more compact, self-supporting evaporator construction.
[F25B 39/026] specially adapted for sorption type systems -- F25B 39/026 designates evaporators that are specially adapted for sorption-type refrigeration systems, where cooling is driven by heat-induced adsorption or absorption rather than mechanical compression. These evaporators incorporate features to manage the distinct heat and mass transfer characteristics of sorption cycles in cooling plants.
[F25B 39/028] having distributing means -- This subclass covers evaporators that incorporate a distribution system for the refrigerant or cooling fluid, such as manifolds, spray nozzles, or pipe networks, to spread the medium evenly over the heat-exchange surface and enhance uniform heat transfer.
[F25B 39/04] Condensers -- F25B 39/04 covers condensers used in refrigeration and cooling systems, where vapor from the compressor is cooled to become liquid. It includes designs for heat-exchange surfaces, tube-and-shell arrangements, and air-cooled or water-cooled configurations that improve heat-removal efficiency and compactness.
[F25B 40/00] Subcoolers, desuperheaters or superheaters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Subcoolers: Heat exchangers, after the condenser, for subcooling the condensed refrigerant. Sometimes also called supercooler.  Desuperheaters: Heat exchangers, preceding the condenser for removing all or part of the superheat.  Superheaters: Heat exchanger in which wet vapour leaving an evaporator is dried and superheated.
[F25B 40/02] Subcoolers -- Subcoolers are components in refrigeration or cooling systems that lower the temperature of the refrigerant below its condensation point after it leaves the condenser. By extracting additional heat, they increase the refrigerant's liquid fraction, improve system efficiency, and reduce the load on downstream expansion devices.
[F25B 40/04] Desuperheaters -- F25B 40/04 covers desuperheaters, which are heat-exchangers that remove superheat from high-pressure refrigerant vapor after compression, lowering its temperature before it enters the condenser. The entry includes designs, operating principles and arrangements of such devices used in refrigeration cycles, often in conjunction with subcoolers.
[F25B 40/06] Superheaters -- F25B 40/06 covers superheaters used in refrigeration or cooling systems, focusing on devices that raise the temperature of vapour above its saturation point to improve efficiency and control in refrigeration machines, plants or related equipment. This subclass falls under the broader group for subcoolers, desuperheaters or superheaters within mechanical engineering.
[F25B 41/00] Fluid-circulation arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fluid-circulation arrangements, e.g. for transferring liquid from evaporator to boiler.  Ejector refrigeration cycle with the ejector used as expansion device.  Free or natural cooling.
[F25B 41/006] optical fluid control arrangements -- A fluid-circulation arrangement for refrigeration or cooling machines that uses optical means, such as light-based sensors or light-controlled valves, to monitor or regulate the flow of refrigerant. The optical system can detect flow characteristics or actuate components without direct contact, improving control precision and reducing mechanical wear. This falls under the mechanical-engineering subclass for refrigeration-machine fluid-circulation arrangements.
[F25B 41/10] using electro-osmosis -- F25B 41/10 covers refrigeration or cooling installations that use electro-osmosis to circulate the working fluid. It includes arrangements where an electric field drives fluid through porous or micro-structured passages, replacing or assisting mechanical pumps to improve flow control, efficiency or compactness of the system.
[F25B 41/20] Disposition of valves, e.g. of on-off valves or flow control valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The configuration of valves in refrigeration circuits. For example:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Expansion valves -> F25B41/31
[F25B 41/22] between evaporator and compressor -- F25B 41/22 covers the fluid-circulation components situated between the evaporator and the compressor in refrigeration or cooling machines. It includes the suction line, associated piping, and any intermediate devices such as filters, accumulators, or control valves that manage refrigerant flow from the evaporator back to the compressor.
[F25B 41/24] Arrangement of shut-off valves for disconnecting a part of the refrigerant cycle, e.g. an outdoor part -- F25B 41/24 concerns the configuration of shut-off valves that enable a section of a refrigerant circuit-typically an outdoor component-to be isolated from the rest of the system. It falls under mechanical engineering for refrigeration machines, plants or systems, specifically fluid-circulation arrangements.
[F25B 41/26] of fluid flow reversing valves -- F25B 41/26 refers to valves that reverse the flow of refrigerant within fluid-circulation arrangements of refrigeration machines or plants. These devices control the direction of fluid movement to enable heating or cooling cycles and are classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and fluid-circulation arrangements.
[F25B 41/28] specially adapted for sorption cycles -- F25B 41/28 covers refrigeration or cooling systems whose fluid-circulation arrangements are specifically designed for sorption cycles, including absorption, adsorption or desorption processes that drive heat-based refrigeration. The code applies to machines, plants or system components that use a working fluid such as water, ammonia or refrigerant vapor in conjunction with a sorbent to transfer heat without a conventional compressor.
[F25B 41/30] Expansion means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dispositions of means in a refrigeration circuit that provides expansion for the refrigerant fluid. For example:
[F25B 41/31] Expansion valves -- F25B 41/31 pertains to expansion valves that regulate the flow of refrigerant from the high-pressure side to the low-pressure side in refrigeration cycles. By controlling pressure and temperature, these valves are essential components of fluid-circulation arrangements, enabling efficient heat exchange and stable operation of cooling systems.
[F25B 41/315] actuated by floats -- F25B 41/315 pertains to fluid-circulation arrangements in refrigeration or cooling machines that are controlled by floats. The float detects the level of a liquid and actuates components such as valves, pumps or compressors to regulate refrigerant flow. This subclass is used for designs where level-dependent actuation ensures proper system performance.
[F25B 41/32] having flow rate limiting means other than the valve member, e.g. having bypass orifices in the valve body -- This subclass covers refrigeration machines and systems in which the refrigerant flow is limited by devices other than the main valve, such as bypass orifices or additional flow-restricting passages incorporated in the valve body, to control the circulation rate.
[F25B 41/325] having two or more valve members -- This subgroup covers refrigeration or cooling systems that incorporate fluid-circulation circuits with two or more valve members, such as additional expansion, check, or control valves, enabling multiple flow paths, staged regulation, or redundancy within the plant.
[F25B 41/33] with the valve member being actuated by the fluid pressure, e.g. by the pressure of the refrigerant -- This subclass covers refrigeration or cooling systems where the valve that regulates fluid flow is driven directly by the pressure of the circulating refrigerant or other working fluid, without external mechanical or electronic controls. The actuation relies solely on the fluid's own pressure to open or close the valve.
[F25B 41/335] via diaphragms -- F25B 41/335 covers refrigeration or cooling systems where fluid is directed through diaphragm components, using flexible membranes to separate and convey refrigerant between circuit sections. It applies to arrangements that employ diaphragms for controlling flow or pressure in the fluid-circulation network of refrigeration machines.
[F25B 41/34] with the valve member being actuated by electric means, e.g. by piezoelectric actuators -- This CPC subclass F25B 41/34 covers refrigeration or cooling systems in which the valve that controls fluid flow is driven by electrical energy, such as piezoelectric actuators, rather than mechanical linkage. It falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigeration machines, plants or systems → fluid-circulation arrangements.
[F25B 41/345] by solenoids -- This subclass addresses refrigeration or cooling machines whose fluid-circulation system is controlled by solenoid devices, such as solenoid-actuated expansion valves, pumps or switches that direct refrigerant flow. It includes arrangements where solenoids operate valves or other flow-control components within the refrigeration circuit.
[F25B 41/347] with the valve member being opened and closed cyclically, e.g. with pulse width modulation -- This subgroup deals with refrigeration or cooling systems that regulate fluid flow by cyclically opening and closing a valve, typically employing pulse-width modulation to control the refrigerant. It falls under fluid-circulation arrangements in mechanical-engineering refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 41/35] by rotary motors, e.g. by stepping motors -- F25B 41/35 covers refrigeration or cooling systems in which the fluid-circulation pump is driven by a rotary motor, including stepper or other electrically powered rotors. It pertains to machines, plants or system designs where motor-driven pumps circulate refrigerant through the cycle.
[F25B 41/355] by electric heating of bimetal elements, shape memory elements or heat expanding elements -- The subclass F25B 41/355 deals with refrigeration or cooling machines that employ electric heating of bimetal, shape-memory or heat-expanding components to control fluid-circulation functions, such as opening valves, actuating expanders or adjusting flow paths within the system.
[F25B 41/36] with the valve member being actuated by bimetal elements or shape-memory elements influenced by fluids, e.g. by the refrigerant -- This subclass covers refrigeration fluid-circulation arrangements in which the valve member is driven by temperature-sensitive bimetal elements or shape-memory alloys that react to the refrigerant or other working fluid. The actuation occurs via the fluid's thermal influence, causing the element to deform and open or close the valve without external power. It falls under refrigeration machines, plants or systems in mechanical engineering.
[F25B 41/37] Capillary tubes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flow restrictors with fixed openings.
[F25B 41/375] characterised by a variable restriction, e.g. restrictors made of shape memory alloy -- This subclass covers fluid-circulation arrangements in refrigeration machines that incorporate a variable restriction element, such as a restrictor made from shape-memory alloy, allowing the flow resistance to change in response to temperature or magnetic field. It is used to adjust cooling performance dynamically.
[F25B 41/38] specially adapted for reversible cycles, e.g. bidirectional expansion restrictors -- The subgroup identifies fluid-circulation components used in refrigeration or cooling machines that are specially designed for reversible cycles, such as bidirectional expansion restrictors that control flow during both heating and cooling phases of the system.
[F25B 41/385] Dispositions with two or more expansion means arranged in parallel on a refrigerant line leading to the same evaporator -- F25B 41/385 covers refrigeration arrangements where two or more expansion devices, such as valves or capillary tubes, are installed in parallel on a single refrigerant line that supplies the same evaporator, enabling independent flow control, redundancy or capacity modulation.
[F25B 41/39] Dispositions with two or more expansion means arranged in series, i.e. multi-stage expansion, on a refrigerant line leading to the same evaporator -- This CPC subclass concerns refrigeration or cooling systems that employ two or more expansion devices arranged in series on the refrigerant line, providing multi-stage pressure reduction before the fluid reaches a single evaporator. It covers configurations where staged expansion is used to improve temperature control, efficiency, or capacity of the system.
[F25B 41/40] Fluid line arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pipe structures: connections, fittings, joints.
[F25B 41/42] Arrangements for diverging or converging flows, e.g. branch lines or junctions -- This group covers designs of piping or ductwork that split or combine fluid streams within refrigeration systems, such as branch lines, manifolds, tees, and junctions that manage flow distribution or collection. It addresses how the fluid circulation network is arranged to achieve divergent or convergent flow patterns in cooling machines and plants.
[F25B 41/45] for flow control on the upstream side of the diverging point, e.g. with spiral structure for generating turbulence -- F25B 41/45 concerns fluid-circulation arrangements in refrigeration machines where flow is regulated on the upstream side of a diverging junction, often by spiral or similar structures that generate turbulence to improve distribution and performance.
[F25B 41/48] for flow path resistance control on the downstream side of the diverging point, e.g. by an orifice -- The subgroup deals with mechanisms that control the resistance of a fluid flow on the downstream side of a branching point, typically by inserting an orifice or similar restrictor to balance pressures and improve performance of refrigeration circuits.
[F25B 43/00] Arrangements for separating or purifying gases or liquids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for separating or purifying gases or liquids  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements for separating or purifying gases or liquids in analysers or rectifiers -> F25B33/00 - Subcoolers, desuperheaters or superheaters -> F25B40/00
[F25B 43/003] Filters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Filters and/or driers.
[F25B 43/006] Accumulators -- Accumulators are pressurised vessels in refrigeration systems that temporarily store refrigerant liquid, allowing excess fluid to be absorbed and preventing gas entrainment. They smooth flow, buffer pressure fluctuations, and help separate liquid from vapor during transients, improving system reliability and efficiency.
[F25B 43/02] for separating lubricants from the refrigerant -- This CPC subclass relates to refrigeration equipment and describes arrangements that separate lubricating oil from the refrigerant, using devices such as separators, filters or coalescers to keep oil out of the cooling circuit and maintain system efficiency.
[F25B 43/04] for withdrawing non-condensible gases -- F25B 43/04 covers arrangements in refrigeration or cooling machines, plants or systems that remove non-condensable gases from the refrigerant circuit, improving performance and preventing loss of efficiency.
[F25B 43/043] for compression type systems -- F25B 43/043 covers arrangements in compression-type refrigeration or cooling systems that are designed to separate or purify gases or liquids within the cycle, such as condensers, separators, or scrubbers integrated into the plant. The classification applies to mechanical engineering solutions where the refrigerant or product stream is cleaned, de-moistured, or divided into distinct phases as part of the overall system.
[F25B 43/046] for sorption type systems -- F25B 43/046 relates to refrigeration or cooling installations that employ sorption-type cycles, using heat-driven adsorption or absorption of a refrigerant to produce cooling instead of mechanical compression. The arrangement typically includes a solid or liquid sorbent that captures refrigerant vapor, enabling heat-exchange processes for separating or purifying gases or liquids.
[F25B 45/00] Arrangements for charging or discharging refrigerant -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Charging/discharging of refrigerant to/from a cycle from an external source.  Balancing of refrigerant within a cycle.
[F25B 47/00] Arrangements for preventing or removing deposits or corrosion, not provided for in another subclass -- F25B 47/00 covers any arrangement in refrigeration or cooling equipment that is designed to prevent or eliminate deposits, scaling, fouling or corrosion when no more specific subclass applies, such as generalized cleaning, anti-corrosion coatings, chemical dosing or mechanical cleaning systems integrated into the machine or plant.
[F25B 47/003] for preventing corrosion -- F25B 47/003 covers arrangements in refrigeration machines, plants or systems that are designed to prevent or remove corrosion or deposits when a specific solution is not covered elsewhere. It includes protective coatings, material selections, and de-contamination techniques applied to components of the refrigerant circuit.
[F25B 47/006] for preventing frost -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preventing frost/condensation.
[F25B 47/02] Defrosting cycles -- F25B 47/02 covers defrosting cycles in refrigeration machines, describing methods and timing for removing ice or frost buildup on heat exchangers. The classification pertains to arrangements that prevent deposits or corrosion not covered elsewhere in the refrigeration subclass.
[F25B 47/022] hot gas defrosting -- Hot-gas defrosting refers to methods that circulate heated refrigerant gas from the compressor or condenser through the evaporator or other cold surfaces to melt frost and ice deposits, eliminating the need for separate heating elements and improving defrost efficiency in refrigeration machines.
[F25B 47/025] by reversing the cycle -- This subclass covers refrigeration or cooling installations where the thermodynamic cycle is reversed to provide heating or cooling, such as heat pumps that switch between refrigeration and heat-pumping modes, and related control arrangements not covered elsewhere.
[F25B 47/027] for defrosting sorption type systems -- This subclass covers arrangements for defrosting sorption-type refrigeration machines, plants or systems, providing means to remove ice or frost buildup that are not covered in other subclasses. It falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 49/00] Arrangement or mounting of control or safety devices -- F25B 49/00 pertains to refrigeration machines, plants or systems and specifically covers the design and positioning of control or safety devices. It includes methods for mounting thermostats, pressure relief valves, sensors and other protective components within compressors, condensers and related equipment to ensure reliable operation and compliance with safety standards.
[F25B 49/005] of safety devices  F25B49/02  and  F25B49/04  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Also classification of monitoring devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For compression type machines, plants or systems -> F25B49/02 - For sorption type machines, plants or systems -> F25B49/04
[F25B 49/02] for compression type machines, plants or systems -- This subclass covers the arrangement or mounting of control and safety devices specifically for compression-type refrigeration machines, plants or systems. It includes the positioning, installation and integration of sensors, switches, relief valves and related components that manage pressure, temperature or operational safety in these cooling units.
[F25B 49/022] Compressor control arrangements -- This class covers how control and safety devices are arranged or mounted on compressors in refrigeration or cooling systems, including the placement of control panels, pressure switches, overload relays and other protective components to ensure proper operation and prevent hazards.
[F25B 49/025] Motor control arrangements -- F25B 49/025 pertains to the layout and mounting of control or safety devices for motor-driven refrigeration equipment, covering how these components are arranged within refrigeration machines, plants or systems to ensure proper operation and protection.
[F25B 49/027] Condenser control arrangements -- The entry refers to the way control or safety devices are arranged or mounted to regulate a condenser in refrigeration machines and systems. It covers the layout, positioning and integration of components that manage condenser operation, ensuring proper temperature control and protection against faults.
[F25B 49/04] for sorption type machines, plants or systems -- F25B 49/04 relates to the way control or safety devices are arranged or mounted on sorption-type refrigeration machines, plants or systems, covering the positioning of components that regulate operation, protect against faults and ensure safe performance within these cooling installations.
[F25B 49/043] Operating continuously -- F25B 49/043 covers refrigeration machines, plants or systems in which control or safety devices are arranged so that the equipment can operate continuously, without interruption for start-stop cycles, often using automatic or self-regulating mechanisms. It is a sub-category of the mounting and arrangement of such devices within the broader field of mechanical engineering refrigeration.
[F25B 49/046] Operating intermittently -- F25B 49/046 concerns refrigeration machines, plants or systems where control or safety devices are arranged or mounted to operate intermittently, meaning they are activated only at set intervals rather than continuously. This classification covers the design and placement of such periodic control mechanisms to regulate temperature or protect equipment.
[F25B 5/00] Compression machines, plants or systems, with several evaporator circuits, e.g. for varying refrigerating capacity -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With cascade operation -> F25B7/00
[F25B 5/02] arranged in parallel -- F25B 5/02 designates compression machines, plants or systems that use several evaporator circuits arranged in parallel to provide variable refrigerating capacity, allowing multiple circuits to operate simultaneously for greater control and efficiency. This falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically refrigeration machines, plants or systems.
[F25B 5/04] arranged in series -- A refrigeration or cooling system that employs multiple evaporator circuits to vary capacity, where the circuits are connected one after another in series. The series arrangement allows staged expansion or sequential heat exchange, improving control of the cooling output.
[F25B 6/00] Compression machines, plants or systems, with several condenser circuits -- The F25B 6/00 class covers refrigeration compression machines, plants or systems that incorporate two or more separate condenser circuits, a configuration used to improve energy efficiency, handle varying loads, or provide staged cooling in mechanical engineering refrigeration and cooling applications.
[F25B 6/02] arranged in parallel -- F25B 6/02 covers compression machines, plants or systems that have several condenser circuits arranged in parallel, meaning multiple compressors or circuit branches operate simultaneously side-by-side to share the cooling load or provide redundancy.
[F25B 6/04] arranged in series -- F25B 6/04 covers compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems that employ two or more condenser circuits connected one after another, so the discharge gas passes through each condenser in turn. The series arrangement allows staged heat removal and improved efficiency.
[F25B 7/00] Compression machines, plants or systems, with cascade operation, i.e. with two or more circuits, the heat from the condenser of one circuit being absorbed by the evaporator of the next circuit -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compression machines, plants or systems, in which the refrigerant is air or other gas of low boiling point -> F25B9/00
[F25B 9/00] Compression machines, plants or systems, in which the refrigerant is air or other gas of low boiling point -- F25B 9/00 covers compression machines, plants or systems that use air or other gases with a low boiling point as the refrigerant, typically in refrigeration or cooling applications where the working fluid remains gaseous rather than liquid.
[F25B 9/002] characterised by the refrigerant -- The CPC class F25B 9/002 covers compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems that are distinguished by the type of refrigerant used, specifically those employing air or other gases with low boiling points as the working fluid. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and pertains to systems where the refrigerant's properties are a defining feature.
[F25B 9/004] the refrigerant being air -- F25B 9/004 classifies compression refrigeration machines, plants or systems that operate with air or other low-boiling-point gases as the refrigerant, covering air-cycle chillers and similar gas-compression cooling installations in the broader field of mechanical engineering refrigeration.
[F25B 9/006] the refrigerant containing more than one component  F25B9/004  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The refrigerant being air -> F25B9/004
[F25B 9/008] the refrigerant being carbon dioxide -- F25B 9/008 covers compression machines, plants or systems that employ carbon dioxide as the refrigerant. It pertains to the design, operation and control of such equipment within refrigeration and cooling technology, including component selection and performance criteria specific to CO₂'s thermodynamic properties.
[F25B 9/02] using Joule-Thompson effect -- F25B 9/02 relates to refrigeration compression machines, plants or systems that use the Joule-Thompson effect to produce cooling, typically with air or other gases of low boiling point as the refrigerant. The classification covers designs that exploit the temperature drop from throttling as part of the compression cycle.
[F25B 9/04] using vortex effect -- F25B 9/04 relates to refrigeration or cooling machines that use a vortex flow to enhance the compression or expansion of low-boiling gases such as air, improving heat-transfer efficiency or pressure rise within the system.
[F25B 9/06] using expanders -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With several cooling stages -> F25B9/10
[F25B 9/065] using pressurised gas jets -- This subgroup concerns refrigeration compressors that create cooling by discharging a pressurised gas jet onto a surface or into a chamber, using the jet's expansion to lower temperature. It applies to machines where the refrigerant is air or other low-boiling-point gases and the jet action forms the main compression or cooling mechanism.
[F25B 9/08] using ejectors -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With several cooling stages -> F25B9/10
[F25B 9/10] with several cooling stages -- This subclass covers refrigeration or cooling systems that use air or another low-boiling-point gas as the refrigerant and employ two or more distinct cooling stages in the compression cycle. The multi-stage arrangement is intended to increase overall efficiency or reach lower temperatures than a single-stage system.
[F25B 9/12] using
[F25B 9/14] characterised by the cycle used, e.g. Stirling cycle -- The CPC subclass F25B 9/14 covers refrigeration or cooling systems that use a Stirling or similar cycle, where the refrigerant is a low-boiling-point gas such as air. It pertains to compression machines, plants or systems that are characterised by the thermodynamic cycle employed.
[F25B 9/145] pulse-tube cycle -- The pulse-tube cycle is a thermodynamic refrigeration method that uses a sealed tube where periodic pressure pulses generate a temperature gradient without any moving parts, typically powered by a compressor. It is suited for cryogenic and low-temperature cooling applications.
[F25C] PRODUCING, WORKING OR HANDLING ICE -- **Glossary:** - Ice: Any frozen liquid and also covers frozen semi-liquids or pasty substances
[F25C 1/00] Producing ice -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Freezers for pelletizing/granulating liquids.  Production of ice slurries.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods or apparatus specially adapted for producing ice or snow for winter sports or similar recreational purposes, e.g. for sporting installations; Producing artificial snow -> F25C3/00
[F25C 1/02] Producing natural ice, i.e. without refrigeration -- F25C 1/02 classifies technologies for producing natural ice without using mechanical refrigeration, covering methods and apparatus that harvest or form ice directly from water bodies such as lakes, ponds or rivers by exploiting ambient cold conditions. It includes systems that facilitate ice formation, collection, storage and handling for later use in cooling or other applications.
[F25C 1/04] by using stationary moulds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Automatic ice producing units using moulds which are not rotated or moved during freezing of the water.  Drive units to harvest ice of automatic ice makers with stationary mould during freezing.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/045] with the open end pointing downwards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Automatic ice producing units using moulds which are not rotated or moved during freezing of the water, the mould has an open end pointing downwards.  Drive units to harvest ice for automatic ice makers with stationary mould during freezing, the mould with the open end pointing downwards.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/06] open or openable at both ends -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Automatic ice producing units using moulds which are not rotated or moved during freezing of the water, the mould is open or openable at both ends.  Production of bar-ice.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/08] by immersing freezing chambers or plates into water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by immersing cooling fingers, rods, conduits, pipes, chambers or plates into water.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/10] by using rotating or otherwise moving moulds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by using rotating or otherwise moving moulds during freezing of water.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Producing ice by immersing freezing chambers or plates into water -> F25C1/08
[F25C 1/12] by freezing water on cooled surfaces, e.g. to form slabs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by freezing water on cooled surfaces.  Ice slabs.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/125] on flexible surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by freezing water on flexible surfaces.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/14] to form thin sheets which are removed by scraping or wedging, e.g. in the form of flakes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by freezing water on cooled flat surfaces to form thin sheets which are removed by scraping or wedging, e.g. in the form of flakes.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/142] from the outer walls of cooled bodies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by freezing water on cooled surfaces to form thin sheets which are removed by scraping or wedging from the outer walls of cooled bodies.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/145] from the inner walls of cooled bodies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by freezing water on cooled surfaces to form thin sheets which are removed by scraping or wedging from the inner walls of cooled bodies.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/147] by using augers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing ice by freezing water to form thin sheets which are removed from the inner walls of cooled bodies by using augers.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/16] by partially evaporating water in a vacuum -- F25C 1/16 covers ice production methods that use partial evaporation of water under vacuum conditions. The technique creates a low-pressure environment causing water to evaporate, which removes heat and leads to ice formation. It falls within the refrigeration and cooling section of mechanical engineering.
[F25C 1/18] of a particular transparency or translucency, e.g. by injecting air -- A subgroup of ice-making methods that control the optical appearance of the ice, achieving a specific level of transparency or translucency, for example by introducing air bubbles into the ice during formation. It addresses techniques for producing clear or semi-clear ice rather than merely frozen water.
[F25C 1/20] by agitation -- The F25C 1/20 subgroup covers methods for producing ice in which the water is mechanically agitated, such as by stirring, shaking or rotating, to promote nucleation and crystal growth during the freezing process. It applies to refrigeration or cooling apparatus that generate ice by using motion rather than static freezing.
[F25C 1/22] Construction of moulds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Moulds for non-automatic ice production.  Constructional details of moulds of automatic ice production.
[F25C 1/24] for refrigerators, e.g. freezing trays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Construction of moulds to be filled by tap water manually and placed inside refrigerators manually.
[F25C 1/243] Moulds made of plastics e.g. silicone -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Construction of moulds to be filled by water manually and placed inside refrigerators manually, the moulds made of plastics, e.g. silicone.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/246] Moulds with separate grid structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Construction of moulds to be filled manually with water and placed manually inside a refrigerator, the mould has a separate grid structure.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F25C 1/25] Filling devices for moulds -- It covers equipment that introduces water or other liquid into moulds that are later frozen to form ice, such as mechanisms that dispense water into ice-cube trays or industrial ice moulds. These filling devices are classified under CPC F25C 1/25 within mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → producing, working or handling ice.
[F25C 2300/00] Special arrangements or features for producing, working or handling ice -- The CPC subclass F25C 2300/00 covers special arrangements or features relating to the production, processing or handling of ice within refrigeration and cooling systems. It includes design elements such as ice-making mechanisms, storage configurations, transport devices and control systems that improve ice quality, efficiency or safety.
[F25C 2301/00] Special arrangements or features for producing ice -- F25C 2301/00 covers special arrangements or features incorporated into ice-making equipment, such as mechanisms, controls or structural modifications that enhance the production of ice. It is classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and producing, working or handling ice.
[F25C 2301/002] Producing ice slurries -- F25C 2301/002 addresses methods and apparatus for producing ice slurries, which are mixtures of fine ice particles suspended in a liquid for cooling or process uses. It is classified under special arrangements or features for producing ice within refrigeration and mechanical engineering.
[F25C 2303/00] Special arrangements or features for producing ice or snow for winter sports or similar recreational purposes, e.g. for sporting installations -- F25C 2303/00 covers special arrangements or features for generating ice or snow for winter sports or similar recreational uses, such as ice rinks or ski slopes. It belongs to the mechanical engineering branch, under refrigeration or cooling and the subcategory of producing, working or handling ice.
[F25C 2303/042] Snow making by using solid ice, e.g. ice crushing -- The code refers to a method for making artificial snow or ice for winter-sports or recreational installations by crushing solid ice and distributing the particles, a specific technique within refrigeration and ice-handling processes.
[F25C 2303/044] Snow making using additional features, e.g. additives, liquid gas -- This subclass covers devices or processes for generating snow or ice for winter-sport installations where the water stream is treated with additives, liquid gases or other agents to modify crystal formation, improve efficiency, or alter texture.
[F25C 2303/046] Snow making by using low pressure air ventilators, e.g. fan type snow canons -- The entry covers methods for producing snow or ice for recreational or sporting installations by employing low-pressure air ventilators, such as fan-type snow cannons. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and the subcategory for special ice-producing arrangements.
[F25C 2303/048] Snow making by using means for spraying water -- The code refers to methods and apparatus for creating snow by spraying water, typically used in ski slopes or other recreational winter-sport installations. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration and ice production, focusing on special arrangements for snow generation.
[F25C 2303/0481] with the use of compressed air -- This subclass covers devices for producing ice or snow for winter sports or recreation that use compressed air as the energy source. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and the subcategory of producing, working or handling ice. The CPC code is F25C 2303/0481.
[F25C 2305/00] Special arrangements or features for working or handling ice -- F25C 2305/00 pertains to special arrangements or features in equipment that work with or handle ice, such as mechanisms for moving, storing or processing ice within refrigeration systems. It is classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically the subcategory of producing, working or handling ice.
[F25C 2305/022] Harvesting ice including rotating or tilting or pivoting of a mould or tray -- F25C 2305/022 covers methods and apparatus for harvesting ice in which a mould or tray is rotated, tilted or pivoted to release the ice. It is classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and the subfield of producing, working or handling ice, specifically special arrangements for handling ice.
[F25C 2305/0221] rotating ice mould -- A rotating ice mould is a mechanical component used in refrigeration systems to form ice by continuously turning a shaped cavity, allowing uniform ice formation and easy release. The rotation improves heat transfer and prevents sticking, making it suitable for ice makers and cooling equipment.
[F25C 2305/024] Rotating rake -- The classification F25C 2305/024 refers to a rotating rake used as a special arrangement for handling or working ice in refrigeration and cooling systems. It describes a device that continuously scrapes, lifts, or conveys ice from a surface or container, typically found in ice-production equipment.
[F25C 2400/00] Auxiliary features or devices for producing, working or handling ice -- F25C 2400/00 pertains to auxiliary features or devices that aid in the production, processing or handling of ice in refrigeration systems. It includes equipment such as ice dispensers, storage trays, transport mechanisms and other components that support ice making or distribution.
[F25C 2400/02] Freezing surface state -- The subclass F25C 2400/02 refers to auxiliary features or devices that create or maintain a frozen surface for the production, working or handling of ice. It includes mechanisms that generate a solidified layer on a substrate, such as cooling plates or frozen-surface generators used in ice-rinks, food processing or other applications requiring a stable ice sheet.
[F25C 2400/04] Ice guide, e.g. for guiding ice blocks to storage tank -- An ice guide is a device that directs ice blocks from a production or handling system toward a storage tank, ensuring proper flow and placement. It belongs to the auxiliary features category for producing, working or handling ice within refrigeration and cooling technology. The classification code for this element is F25C 2400/04.
[F25C 2400/06] Multiple ice moulds or trays therefor -- This subclass covers devices that incorporate two or more ice moulds or trays as part of an ice-making system, enabling simultaneous production of several ice portions. It includes auxiliary arrangements that support the operation or handling of the multiple moulds within refrigeration or cooling equipment.
[F25C 2400/08] for different type of ice -- F25C 2400/08 designates auxiliary features or devices used in the production, working or handling of ice that are adapted for different types of ice. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling, and concerns equipment that supports varied ice forms such as cubes, flakes or blocks.
[F25C 2400/10] Refrigerator units -- F25C 2400/10 relates to refrigerator units, the complete appliance that provides cooling for food or other items, typically comprising a compressor, evaporator, and insulated compartment. The classification includes auxiliary features or devices that support the production, handling or storage of ice within such units.
[F25C 2400/12] Means for sanitation -- F25C 2400/12 designates sanitation equipment or methods integrated into auxiliary devices for producing, working or handling ice, such as cleaning, disinfecting or sterilising mechanisms that keep ice-making or ice-handling systems hygienic. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically the subcategory of ice-production auxiliary features.
[F25C 2400/14] Water supply -- The F25C 2400/14 water supply class covers devices and arrangements that provide water to ice-making or ice-handling equipment, including inlet pipes, distribution manifolds, water level control and related auxiliary components used in refrigeration systems for producing, working or handling ice.
[F25C 2500/00] Problems to be solved -- The CPC subclass F25C 2500/00 covers inventions that address difficulties encountered in the production, treatment, storage, or distribution of ice. It includes solutions aimed at improving efficiency, safety, quality control, energy consumption, or handling techniques for ice-related processes and equipment.
[F25C 2500/02] Geometry problems
[F25C 2500/04] Calculation of parameters -- The F25C 2500/04 subclass covers the calculation of thermodynamic, fluid-dynamic and performance parameters for processes that produce, work with or handle ice in refrigeration or cooling systems. It includes methods for determining heat loads, energy consumption, capacity, efficiency and related design variables.
[F25C 2500/06] Spillage or flooding of water -- The F25C 2500/06 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification concerns devices or methods for managing water that spills or floods during ice-producing, working or handling operations. It covers systems that detect, collect, drain, or otherwise control unwanted water in refrigeration or cooling equipment that generates or processes ice.
[F25C 2500/08] Sticking or clogging of ice -- The code F25C 2500/08 falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling processes involving the production, working or handling of ice. It designates inventions that address the problem of ice sticking together or clogging equipment, which can impair the flow and performance of ice-handling systems. This entry is used by both the EPO and USPTO to classify such technical solutions.
[F25C 2600/00] Control issues -- F25C2600/00 covers the control aspects of ice-producing and handling equipment, including sensors, feedback mechanisms and regulation strategies that manage temperature, timing and flow to ensure efficient and safe ice generation and processing.
[F25C 2600/02] Timing -- F25C 2600/02 covers timing devices and control methods used in refrigeration or cooling systems that produce, work on, or handle ice. It pertains to the regulation of cycle periods, start-stop sequences, and synchronization of ice-making equipment to ensure proper operation and efficiency.
[F25C 2600/04] Control means -- F25C 2600/04 pertains to control means for apparatus that produces, works or handles ice. It includes devices and systems that regulate temperature, flow, timing, safety or other operating parameters of ice-making or ice-handling equipment. The code is used to classify patents related to control mechanisms within refrigeration and cooling technology.
[F25C 2700/00] Sensing or detecting of parameters -- The CPC subclass F25C 2700/00 covers devices or methods for sensing or detecting parameters in systems that produce, work with, or handle ice within refrigeration or cooling technology. It includes sensors for temperature, pressure, humidity, level, or other operating variables that affect ice-related processes.
[F25C 2700/02] Level of ice -- F25C 2700/02 (Level of ice) concerns methods and devices for sensing or detecting the amount or height of ice in systems that produce, work with, or store ice, such as ice makers or storage units. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and focuses on parameter-monitoring technology.
[F25C 2700/04] Level of water -- This classification covers devices or methods that measure or indicate the water level in systems related to ice-production, handling, or cooling. It includes sensors, gauges and control mechanisms used in refrigeration equipment to monitor water quantity.
[F25C 2700/06] Rotation angle of the ejector ejecting ice from a stationary mould -- The entry covers the angular position or rotation angle of an ejector that removes ice formed in a stationary mould, defining how far the ejector is turned to release the ice. It applies to refrigeration or cooling systems where ice production, working or handling involves a controlled ejector movement.
[F25C 2700/08] Power to drive the auger motor of an auger type ice making machine -- The entry covers the means of supplying power to the auger motor that drives the screw in an auger-type ice-making machine, including the electrical or mechanical power source and its control for moving and shaping ice. It relates to the drive system that enables the auger to transport water and form ice.
[F25C 2700/10] Rotating speed of the auger motor of an auger type ice making machine -- The code F25C 2700/10 relates to the control of the rotational speed of the auger motor in an auger-type ice-making machine. [F25C 5/142] extrusion of ice crystals -- F25C 5/142 covers the process of forming ice by forcing molten or semi-molten ice crystals through a die or nozzle to create continuous shapes, such as bars, rods or filaments. This extrusion technique is used in industrial ice production for food processing, cooling systems and decorative ice applications.
[F25C 5/18] Storing ice -- F25C 5/18 relates to technologies for storing ice, covering apparatus, containers, and methods that keep ice at low temperatures after it is produced. It includes insulated ice bins, refrigerated storage units, and control systems that maintain ice quality and prevent melting, falling within the broader fields of mechanical engineering and refrigeration.
[F25C 5/182] Ice bins therefor -- F25C 5/182 pertains to ice bins used for storing and handling ice in refrigeration or cooling equipment. [F25C 5/185] with freezing trays -- The CPC subclass F25C 5/185 covers devices for producing ice that employ freezing trays, where water is poured into removable molds that are cooled until solidified. It pertains to the handling of ice in refrigeration and cooling systems.
[F25C 5/187] with ice level sensing means -- F25C 5/187 relates to ice-working or handling equipment that incorporates ice-level sensing means, enabling the system to monitor and control the amount of ice present during storage, transport or dispensing and to prevent overflow or shortage.
[F25C 5/20] Distributing ice -- F25C 5/20 relates to equipment and methods for distributing ice generated in refrigeration or cooling systems, encompassing the transport, dispensing and delivery of ice to storage or end-use points.
[F25C 5/22] particularly adapted for household refrigerators -- This subclass covers features, components or procedures that are specifically designed for domestic refrigerators, such as shelving arrangements, door hinges, temperature-control mechanisms and other adaptations that make the appliance suitable for household use.
