[F25D] REFRIGERATORS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refrigerators; cold rooms; ice-boxes; cooling or freezing apparatus not covered by any other subclass.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Refrigerated show cases -> A47F3/04 - Thermally-insulated vessels for domestic use -> A47J41/00 - Refrigerated vehicles -> B60 - B64 - Containers with thermal insulation in general -> B65D81/38 - Heat-transfer, heat-exchange or heat-storage materials, e.g. refrigerants, or material for the production of heat or cold by chemical reactions other than by combustion -> C09K5/00 - Cooling of engines or pumps -> F04B , F04C , F04D - Thermally-insulated vessels for liquefied or solidified gases -> F17C - Air-conditioning or air-humidification -> F24F - Refrigeration machines, plants or systems -> F25B - Production, working, storing or distribution of ice -> F25C - Cooling of instruments and comparable apparatus without refrigeration -> G12B  **Glossary:** - Device: means an enclosed space to be cooled; such devices being associated either with refrigerating machinery, e.g. in a refrigerator, or with other cold sources, e.g. in an ice-box.
[F25D 1/00] Devices not associated with refrigerating machinery -- F25D 1/00 covers components and accessories used in refrigeration or cooling installations that are not part of the primary refrigerating machine, such as control devices, safety valves, condensate collectors, and other auxiliary equipment.
[F25D 1/02] using naturally cold water, e.g. household tap water -- This subclass covers refrigeration devices that obtain cooling by circulating naturally cold water such as tap water, rather than using a compressor-driven refrigerant cycle. It includes systems where the water is the primary heat sink for the refrigerating apparatus.
[F25D 11/00] Devices associated with refrigerating machinery -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - By forced circulation -> F25D17/06 - Purses, travelling bags or baskets, suitcases -> A45C - Hand carts -> B62B - Containers for storage or transport of articles or materials -> B65D
[F25D 11/003] Transport containers -- F25D 11/003 relates to transport containers that incorporate refrigerating or cooling equipment for moving temperature-sensitive goods. It includes portable refrigerated boxes, insulated shipping containers and mobile cooling units used in trucks, trains or other vehicles.
[F25D 11/006] with cold storage accumulators -- F25D 11/006 relates to refrigeration or cooling devices that incorporate a cold-storage accumulator, a component that temporarily stores chilled fluid or refrigerant to smooth temperature fluctuations and reduce load on the primary system. This subclass covers designs where the accumulator is integrated into the refrigerator or similar apparatus for improved efficiency and temperature control.
[F25D 11/02] with cooling compartments at different temperatures -- F25D 11/02 pertains to refrigerators that incorporate separate cooling compartments maintained at different temperatures. The classification covers designs where distinct zones provide tailored refrigeration levels within a single-appliance, enabling simultaneous storage of items requiring varied temperature conditions.
[F25D 11/022] with two or more evaporators -- F25D 11/022 classifies refrigerating apparatus that incorporate two or more evaporators, such as multi-zone refrigerators or systems designed to provide separate cooling zones. The code falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically refrigerators.
[F25D 11/025] using primary and secondary refrigeration systems -- F25D 11/025 covers methods and apparatus that combine a primary refrigeration cycle with one or more secondary cycles to improve efficiency or achieve lower temperatures. It includes arrangements where the secondary system uses the primary system's coolant or heat exchange to provide additional cooling for the refrigerator.
[F25D 11/027] of the sorption cycle type -- The entry F25D 11/027 covers refrigerating devices that use a sorption-cycle principle, such as absorption or adsorption chillers, where heat input drives the refrigeration process instead of a mechanical compressor. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling, and relates to appliances associated with refrigerating machinery.
[F25D 11/04] specially adapted for storing deep-frozen articles -- F25D 11/04 covers refrigerator units that are specifically designed for the storage of deep-frozen goods. The classification includes appliances with enhanced insulation, low-temperature control systems, and structures that maintain temperatures well below the freezing point of water. These devices are used in commercial or industrial settings where long-term preservation of frozen foods is required.
[F25D 13/00] Stationary devices, e.g. cold-rooms -- F25D 13/00 covers stationary refrigeration equipment such as cold-rooms and similar fixed installations used for preserving or cooling goods. The classification includes the structural and functional aspects of these devices, encompassing their construction, insulation, and integration into larger mechanical engineering systems for cooling and temperature control.
[F25D 13/02] with several cooling compartments, e.g. refrigerated locker systems -- F25D 13/02 covers stationary refrigeration apparatus that provides two or more independent cooling compartments, such as locker-style units where each section can be set to a different temperature for varied storage needs.
[F25D 13/04] the compartments being at different temperatures -- F25D 13/04 relates to refrigeration or cooling apparatus in which separate compartments are designed to operate at different temperature levels, allowing distinct zones within a single stationary device such as a cold-room or commercial refrigerator. This classification covers the arrangement, control and insulation methods that maintain these varied temperatures simultaneously.
[F25D 13/06] with conveyors carrying articles to be cooled through the cooling space -- This subclass covers stationary refrigeration units such as cold-rooms that include a conveyor system moving products through the cooled interior. The conveyor transports items into, across, or out of the cooling space for continuous cooling. It is used in industrial or commercial settings where bulk cooling of packaged goods is required.
[F25D 13/062] with refrigerated conveyors -- The code refers to stationary refrigeration units such as cold-rooms that include a refrigerated conveyor for transporting items while keeping them at low temperature. It covers mechanical-engineering solutions integrating cooling and conveyance within a single system.
[F25D 13/065] Articles being submerged in liquid coolant -- This CPC subclass covers refrigeration systems for stationary units such as cold rooms where the items to be cooled are placed directly in a liquid coolant. It includes methods and apparatuses that immerse products in liquids like water, glycol or other refrigerants to achieve temperature control. The classification is used for patents involving submersion cooling of stored articles.
[F25D 13/067] with circulation of gaseous cooling fluid -- A stationary refrigeration device such as a cold-room in which the cooling fluid is circulated in the gaseous phase. The system moves the refrigerant gas through evaporators, condensers and piping to absorb heat and maintain low temperatures inside the enclosure.
[F25D 15/00] Devices not covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Products cooled by a cooling system at a remote place;  Devices for freezing of pipes for forming a frozen ice plug in a section of a pipe to be repaired.  Movable devices implies also refrigerators.
[F25D 16/00] Devices using a combination of a cooling mode associated with refrigerating machinery with a cooling mode not associated with refrigerating machinery -- F25D 16/00 covers appliances that integrate a conventional refrigerating cycle with an additional cooling method that does not rely on compression, such as evaporative, thermoelectric or air-side heat-exchanger cooling, allowing the unit to switch between or combine the two modes for energy efficiency.
[F25D 17/00] Details or features of the devices covered by groups -- F25D 17/00 pertains to the specific details or features of refrigeration devices, such as construction, arrangement, components or operating principles, within the broader class of refrigerators in mechanical engineering. It is used to classify patents that describe particular aspects of how a refrigerator is built or functions.
[F25D 17/005] in cold rooms -- F25D 17/005 covers refrigeration or cooling apparatus that is installed inside a cold room, such as evaporators, condensers, or air-handling units forming part of the room's internal climate-control system. The code applies to equipment designed to maintain low temperatures within the enclosed space rather than to external or stand-alone units.
[F25D 17/02] for circulating liquids, e.g. brine -- F25D 17/02 relates to refrigerating or cooling apparatus that employ a secondary liquid, such as brine, which is circulated through the system to absorb or release heat. It covers devices where the liquid is pumped or otherwise moved to provide thermal transfer within the refrigerator or cooling unit.
[F25D 17/04] for circulating air, e.g. by convection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circulation of air by natural convection.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Forced circulation of air is classified in -> F25D17/06
[F25D 17/042] Air treating means within refrigerated spaces  air conditioning in general  F24F -- F25D 17/042 covers air-treating equipment installed inside refrigerated compartments, including fans, ducts, heating or de-humidifying devices and other means for conditioning temperature, humidity or air quality within the space, as part of the overall refrigeration or cooling system.
[F25D 17/045] Air flow control arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air dampers; Air valves.
[F25D 17/047] Pressure equalising devices -- F25D 17/047 covers devices that equalise pressure inside refrigeration appliances, typically by allowing gas to flow between the sealed compartment and the external environment to balance internal pressure changes during operation or door opening. These mechanisms help maintain efficient cooling, prevent oil migration, and protect seals, and are used in household and commercial refrigerators.
[F25D 17/06] by forced circulation -- This subgroup covers refrigerator designs that use a mechanically driven system-such as pumps, fans or compressors-to force the circulation of refrigerant or cooling fluid through the appliance, rather than relying on natural convection. It includes arrangements where the induced flow enhances heat-transfer efficiency and temperature regulation.
[F25D 17/062] in household refrigerators -- F25D 17/062 covers household refrigerators, i.e., domestic cooling appliances that preserve food using an electrically driven compressor and a single insulated compartment. The entry applies to typical kitchen fridge units and related features within the mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, refrigerators hierarchy.
[F25D 17/065] with compartments at different temperatures -- It refers to refrigerators that contain multiple compartments each maintained at a distinct temperature, such as freezer and fresh-food sections or variable zones for different food types. The classification covers designs, controls and insulation methods that enable separate temperature regulation within a single appliance.
[F25D 17/067] Evaporator fan units -- The F25D 17/067 class covers fan assemblies that are mounted on or integrated with evaporators in refrigeration systems. These units provide forced air flow over the evaporator coil to enhance heat transfer and may include the motor, blades, housing and any associated control devices.
[F25D 17/08] using ducts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ducts for cooling by radiation, i.e. indirect cooling.
[F25D 19/00] Arrangement or mounting of refrigeration units with respect to devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mounting of complete refrigeration units.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mounting of components of refrigerating machinery -> F25D23/006
[F25D 19/003] with respect to movable containers -- The subclass F25D 19/003 covers the arrangement or mounting of refrigeration units specifically in relation to movable containers such as transport trucks, shipping containers or portable coolers. It pertains to how the cooling apparatus is positioned, secured or integrated with these mobile vessels to ensure effective temperature control while allowing the container to be moved or relocated.
[F25D 19/006] Thermal coupling structure or interface -- The entry covers structures that provide a thermal link between a refrigeration unit and the device it serves, such as plates, heat-exchangers or other interfaces that conduct heat to or from the unit while it is mounted or arranged with the equipment.
[F25D 19/02] plug-in type -- F25D 19/02 classifies refrigeration units that are mounted in a plug-in configuration, allowing the unit to be inserted into or removed from a refrigerator or other device as a single module. Such designs facilitate easy installation, maintenance, or replacement without disassembling the surrounding equipment.
[F25D 19/04] with more than one refrigeration unit -- This CPC subclass covers refrigerators that incorporate more than one refrigeration unit and specifies how the units are arranged or mounted relative to each other or to other device components. It lies within the mechanical engineering hierarchy under refrigeration or cooling, focusing on multi-unit configurations.
[F25D 21/00] Defrosting -- F25D21/00 covers methods and devices for removing ice or frost from the interior of a refrigerator. It includes automatic or manual defrosting cycles, heating elements and control mechanisms that melt accumulated frost to maintain cooling efficiency. The classification is used for patents relating to refrigeration systems that incorporate defrost functionality.
[F25D 21/002] Defroster control -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** This place does not cover: Classification in F25B47/02 when frost is removed by heat of the refrigeration cycle.
[F25D 21/004] Control mechanisms  F25D21/006  takes precedence -- The code F25D 21/004 covers control mechanisms for the defrost system of refrigerators, such as thermostats, timers, sensors and electronic units that initiate, regulate and terminate the defrost cycle. It is used to classify inventions that manage when and how ice removal is performed to maintain cooling efficiency.
[F25D 21/006] with electronic control circuits -- This subclass covers refrigerator defrosting systems that use electronic control circuits to regulate the removal of frost, including sensors, timers, microprocessors or solid-state devices that control heating elements or compressor operation.
[F25D 21/008] by timer -- F25D 21/008 covers refrigerating appliances that employ a timer to control the defrosting cycle. The timer initiates periodic interruption of cooling, allowing ice buildup to melt automatically without manual intervention. This subclass refines the broader categories of mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and refrigerators, focusing on timed defrost mechanisms.
[F25D 21/02] Detecting the presence of frost or condensate -- This classification covers methods or devices that sense frost or condensate on refrigerator components to initiate a defrost cycle, including optical, thermal or humidity sensors used in cooling appliances.
[F25D 21/025] using air pressure differential detectors -- F25D 21/025 covers defrosting methods for refrigerators that employ air-pressure-differential detectors to sense frost accumulation or temperature gradients and trigger automatic defrost cycles. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling, and relates to devices that use pressure changes in the air flow to control ice removal.
[F25D 21/04] Preventing the formation of frost or condensate -- F25D 21/04 covers features that stop frost or water from building up on refrigerator evaporators. It includes automatic-defrost cycles, heated elements, surface coatings and airflow designs that keep moisture from condensing and freezing. The aim is to maintain efficient cooling and avoid ice accumulation.
[F25D 21/06] Removing frost -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** This place does not cover: Classification in F25B47/02 when frost is removed by heat of the refrigeration cycle.
[F25D 21/065] by mechanical means -- This CPC subclass covers systems and methods for defrosting refrigerators by mechanical means such as rotating fans, vibrating elements or moving surfaces that physically remove ice. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering, Refrigeration or Cooling, Refrigerators, Defrosting, code F25D 21/065.
[F25D 21/08] by electric heating -- F25D 21/08 relates to the defrosting of refrigerators by employing electric heating. It covers devices, systems or methods that use electric heating elements to melt accumulated frost or ice in cooling equipment. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically the refrigerator defrosting category.
[F25D 21/10] by spraying with fluid -- F25D 21/10 relates to defrosting of refrigerators by spraying a fluid onto the cold surface, typically the evaporator. The method uses water or a glycol-based liquid to melt ice and restore efficient heat transfer, often as part of an automatic defrost cycle.
[F25D 21/12] by hot-fluid circulating system separate from the refrigerant system -- The CPC subclass F25D 21/12 pertains to refrigeration appliances where a hot fluid circulation system, distinct from the refrigerant circuit, is used for defrosting. It covers methods and apparatus that employ a separate heating fluid loop to melt frost on evaporator surfaces, improving efficiency.
[F25D 21/125] the hot fluid being ambient air -- F25D 21/125 relates to defrosting arrangements for refrigerators in which ambient air from the surrounding environment is used as the hot fluid to melt frost on the evaporator. This classification covers systems that circulate or direct external air for the purpose of clearing ice buildup during operation.
[F25D 21/14] Collecting or removing condensed and defrost water -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Passenger or goods spaces characterized by condensed liquid drainage means -> B60H1/3233 - Removing condensate in air-conditioning -> F24F13/22 - Collecting and removing condensate for heat-exchangers -> F28B9/08
[F25D 2201/00] Insulation -- F25D 2201/00 covers insulation for refrigerators, describing the materials, structures and methods used to reduce heat transfer into the storage compartment, such as foams, vacuum panels, reflective films and multi-layer barriers.
[F25D 2201/10] with respect to heat
[F25D 2201/12] using an insulating packing material -- F25D 2201/12 classifies the use of a packing material that provides thermal insulation for refrigerators. It covers methods or designs where an insulating layer is applied around or between components to reduce heat transfer and improve energy efficiency in refrigeration appliances. This subclass falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Refrigeration or Cooling → Refrigerators → Insulation.
[F25D 2201/122] of loose fill type -- A refrigeration appliance insulated with a loose-fill material, such as mineral wool, cellulose fibre or foam beads, where the filler is placed loosely around the walls or between panels rather than being bonded or sprayed. This type of insulation improves thermal resistance while accommodating shrinkage and expansion during operation.
[F25D 2201/124] of fibrous type -- This CPC subclass covers insulation for refrigerators that is made from fibrous materials, such as mineral wool or foam reinforced with fibers. It pertains to thermal management in refrigeration units, detailing the use of fiber-based insulating structures to improve energy efficiency and temperature control.
[F25D 2201/126] of cellular type -- The entry refers to refrigerators that use cellular-type insulation, where the thermal barrier is formed by a network of closed cells such as foamed polymers or honeycomb structures that trap gas to limit heat flow. This construction improves energy efficiency and helps maintain the interior temperature of the appliance.
[F25D 2201/1262] with open cells -- The code designates insulation for refrigerators that employs open-cell materials, such as foams whose individual cells remain unsealed, allowing gas or air to circulate within the structure. This type of insulation improves thermal performance while providing a lightweight, flexible barrier inside cooling units.
[F25D 2201/128] of foil type -- This subclass covers refrigerators whose interior is insulated with foil-type material, such as thin metal or metal-foil laminates that act as a radiant barrier and provide low thermal conductivity. It applies to any refrigeration unit that uses this kind of foil insulation to improve energy efficiency and temperature control.
[F25D 2201/1282] with reflective foils -- This subclass covers refrigeration units whose thermal insulation incorporates reflective foil layers to reduce heat transfer. The foils are applied within the walls, doors or panels of the refrigerator to improve energy efficiency and maintain temperature stability.
[F25D 2201/14] using subatmospheric pressure -- The code F25D 2201/14 refers to refrigeration or cooling equipment, specifically refrigerators, that employ subatmospheric (vacuum) pressure as an insulating method. It covers designs that create evacuated spaces or panels to reduce heat flow and improve energy efficiency.
[F25D 2201/30] with respect to sound -- F25D 2201/30 is a CPC subclass under mechanical engineering for refrigeration, focusing on the insulation of refrigerators where the feature concerns sound. It covers designs, materials or methods that improve acoustic performance of the insulated envelope, such as noise-absorbing panels, dampening layers or acoustic sealing. The classification is used to index patents addressing how insulation contributes to reduced sound emission in cooling appliances.
[F25D 23/00] General constructional features -- The subclass F25D 23/00 deals with the overall structural design of refrigerators, including the configuration of the cabinet, compartments, door assemblies, and mounting or supporting elements. It addresses how these components are arranged and integrated to form the basic refrigerator unit, without focusing on particular refrigeration cycles or detailed functional mechanisms.
[F25D 2300/00] Special arrangements or features for refrigerators -- The subclass covers additional components or structural modifications incorporated into a refrigerator to enhance performance, convenience or efficiency. It includes features such as adjustable shelves, door compartments, interior lighting, automatic defrost, temperature regulation systems, ice makers and energy-saving controls.

[F25D 23/021] Sliding doors -- F25D 23/021 relates to the construction of sliding doors for refrigerators, describing the structural layout, sealing arrangements and actuation means that allow the doors to move laterally across the appliance front.
[F25D 23/023] Air curtain closures -- F25D 23/023 pertains to air-curtain closures for refrigerators, where a directed flow of air creates a barrier across an open door or panel to reduce heat transfer and moisture loss. The feature typically involves jets or slots that project air across the opening, enhancing energy efficiency and preserving interior temperature.
[F25D 23/025] Secondary closures -- The entry concerns secondary closures in refrigeration appliances, i.e., additional sealing devices such as inner doors, flaps, pads or latch mechanisms that work with the primary door to improve insulation, safety or access. It falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigerators → general constructional features.
[F25D 23/026] for open-top cabinets -- F25D 23/026 covers refrigerators with open-top cabinet designs, where the cooling compartment is accessed from the top rather than through a side door. [F25D 2303/00] Details of devices using other cold materials -- The subclass F25D 2303/00 covers technical information on refrigerator and cooler designs that employ cold sources other than conventional refrigerants, such as ice, dry ice, liquid nitrogen, evaporative media or phase-change materials. It includes the structure, operation and integration of these alternative cooling agents within refrigeration equipment.
[F25D 2303/08] Devices using cold storage material, i.e. ice or other freezable liquid -- The entry covers appliances that employ ice or other freezable liquids as the cooling medium, such as ice-boxes, early portable refrigerators and devices where a stored cold material is used to lower the temperature of a compartment. It is classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, refrigerators.
[F25D 2303/081] using ice cubes or crushed ice -- This subclass covers refrigerators that achieve cooling by incorporating ice cubes or crushed ice as the cooling medium, such as portable coolers, ice-boxes and similar devices. It applies to mechanical-engineering refrigeration systems that use other cold materials rather than a built-in refrigerant cycle.
[F25D 2303/082] disposed in a cold storage element not forming part of a container for products to be cooled, e.g. ice pack or gel accumulator -- This subclass covers refrigeration devices that employ a separate cold-storage element, such as an ice pack or gel accumulator, which is placed in the system but does not form part of the product-holding container, providing cooling through a secondary cold material.
[F25D 2303/0821] the element placed in a compartment which can be opened without the need of opening the container itself -- This subclass covers internal components of a refrigerator that are housed in a separate compartment and can be accessed or removed without opening the main refrigeration chamber. Typical examples include ice bins, water reservoirs or drawers that have their own hinged or sliding openings, allowing service or refilling while the freezer or fridge door remains closed.
[F25D 2303/0822] Details of the element -- The CPC subclass F25D 2303/0822 covers technical details of the element in a refrigerator that utilizes alternative cold media instead of conventional refrigerants. It includes design, construction and functional features of components that employ substances such as ice, dry ice, chilled water or other non-standard cooling agents. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Refrigeration or Cooling → Refrigerators.
[F25D 2303/08221] Fasteners or fixing means for the element -- This CPC group concerns refrigerators that employ additional cold-material components such as ice packs or phase-change elements, and specifically covers the fasteners, brackets, clamps or other fixing means used to attach those cold elements to the appliance. It includes designs of mounting hardware that secure secondary cooling media within the refrigeration unit.
[F25D 2303/08222] Shape of the element -- This CPC subgroup covers the geometric shape of components in refrigeration equipment that employ alternative cold sources such as ice, dry ice or other phase-change materials. It addresses design features of elements like evaporators, cold plates or storage chambers where the shape influences heat transfer efficiency. The classification is used for patents describing novel shapes of such elements within refrigerators.
[F25D 2303/08223] having the shape of an ice cube -- F25D 2303/08223 covers refrigerating appliances that use alternative cold substances and are shaped like an ice cube. The entry is classified under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and refrigerators.
[F25D 2303/083] using cold storage material disposed in closed wall forming part of a container for products to be cooled -- The CPC entry F25D 2303/083 refers to refrigeration devices that incorporate a cold storage material placed within a sealed wall that forms part of the container holding the product to be cooled. It covers systems where the refrigerant is a solid or phase-change material integrated into the container structure, providing indirect cooling without a separate refrigerant circuit.
[F25D 2303/0831] the liquid is disposed in the space between the walls of the container -- This subclass concerns refrigerator constructions in which a liquid coolant is positioned in the cavity between the container's inner and outer walls, providing cooling by direct contact with the stored items. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically addresses devices that employ alternative cold materials for enhanced temperature control.
[F25D 2303/0832] the liquid is disposed in an accumulator pack locked in a closable wall forming part of the container -- This subclass covers refrigerator designs in which a liquid refrigerant is placed in an accumulator pack that is locked into a closable wall forming part of the container, allowing the liquid to be retained within the appliance structure. It pertains to devices that use alternative cold materials and focus on built-in accumulator configurations.
[F25D 2303/084] Position of the cold storage material in relationship to a product to be cooled -- F25D 2303/084 addresses how the cold storage material is positioned relative to the product being cooled in refrigeration devices. It includes configurations where the refrigerant is placed around, beneath, within, or adjacent to the item, such as ice packs or gel packs in refrigerated containers.
[F25D 2303/0841] external to the container for a beverage, e.g. a bottle, can, drinking glass or pitcher -- F25D 2303/0841 relates to refrigerating equipment that cools a beverage by acting on the outside of the container, such as bottles, cans, glasses or pitchers. It includes external chillers, cooling sleeves or racks that transfer cold to the container surface rather than placing the liquid inside a built-in cooling chamber.
[F25D 2303/0842] inside the beverage contained in a bottle, can, drinking glass, pitcher or dispenser
[F25D 2303/0843] on the side of the product -- F25D 2303/0843 covers refrigerators where the cooling element or other cold material is positioned on the side of the product rather than inside the main chamber, including side-mounted cooling plates, panels or compartments that directly chill the stored item, often used in specialty or portable cooling devices.
[F25D 2303/0844] above the product -- The entry covers refrigerator designs that incorporate auxiliary cold media placed above the stored goods, such as ice compartments, cooling packs or other secondary refrigerants that provide additional cooling to the product area. [F25D 23/04] with special compartments, e.g. butter conditioners -- The classification F25D 23/04 covers refrigerators that incorporate special internal compartments such as butter conditioners, providing dedicated spaces for particular food items. [F25D 23/06] Walls -- F25D 23/06 pertains to the structural walls of refrigerators, encompassing inner and outer panels, insulating layers and supporting frames that form the cabinet. It addresses general constructional features of refrigeration units.
[F25D 23/061] with conduit means -- The subclass F25D 23/061 covers refrigerators that incorporate conduit means such as pipes, channels or ducts to transport refrigerant, condensate or other fluids within the appliance. It details the arrangement, connection and integration of these conduits as part of the overall construction.
[F25D 23/062] defining a cabinet -- F25D 23/062 covers features that define the overall cabinet of a refrigerator, including its shape, dimensions, mounting, and external enclosure elements that house the cooling system and internal compartments. It pertains to the structural design of the refrigerator's outer shell, such as walls, doors, and frames, rather than internal components.
[F25D 23/063] formed by an assembly of panels -- F25D 23/063 pertains to refrigerators whose overall structure is built from a set of panels assembled together, describing the construction method where individual flat components are joined to form the cabinet or housing.
[F25D 23/064] formed by moulding, e.g. moulding  in situ -- This CPC subclass covers refrigerators whose housings or structural parts are produced by molding techniques, including forming the body directly in place (in-situ molding). It relates to constructional features of refrigeration appliances where the enclosure or internal components are shaped by casting, injection, or similar molding processes.
[F25D 23/065] Details -- F25D 23/065 covers specific details of refrigerator construction, such as the design, arrangement and mounting of internal components, sealing elements and other structural particulars that influence the appliance's performance or assembly.
[F25D 23/066] Liners -- The CPC subclass F25D 23/066 covers liners used in refrigerator units, including inner containers, protective coatings or insulating sheets that line the refrigerating compartment or shelves. It addresses the material, shape and mounting of such liners that isolate the interior, improve durability or aid cleaning.
[F25D 23/067] Supporting elements -- F25D 23/067 covers the supporting elements used in refrigerators, such as shelves, brackets, frames and other structural members that hold or position internal components. These features are designed to provide stability, maintain alignment of cooling parts and facilitate access for loading or maintenance.
[F25D 23/068] Arrangements for circulating fluids through the insulating material -- This subclass deals with refrigerator constructions that route a fluid-often a refrigerant or heat-transfer liquid-through the insulating material surrounding the cooling compartment. It includes designs using channels, ducts or porous media within the insulation to enhance heat distribution, reduce thermal gradients, or improve overall efficiency.
[F25D 23/069] Cooling space dividing partitions -- F25D 23/069 relates to internal partitions that separate the cooling space of a refrigerator or freezer into distinct compartments. It covers the design, placement and construction of walls, shelves or barriers that isolate sections to maintain different temperatures. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and the general constructional features of refrigerators.
[F25D 23/08] Parts formed wholly or mainly of plastics materials -- The subclass F25D 23/08 covers components of refrigerators that are constructed mainly or entirely from plastic materials, such as doors, shelves, bins, or insulating panels. It includes any design or manufacturing features specific to using plastics in these parts, reflecting the trend toward lightweight, corrosion-resistant refrigeration elements.
[F25D 23/082] Strips -- The CPC subclass F25D 23/082 relates to strips used in the construction of refrigerators. It covers general constructional features of strip-type components such as sealing or insulating strips within refrigeration appliances.
[F25D 23/085] Breaking strips -- In refrigerator construction, breaking strips are sacrificial elements installed in the refrigerant circuit that fracture at a predetermined pressure to release refrigerant safely and prevent damage to the compressor or surrounding components. They are typically thin metal or polymer pieces positioned in the capillary or liquid line and act as pressure-relief devices during over-pressure or overheating events.
[F25D 23/087] Sealing strips -- F25D 23/087 covers sealing strips employed in refrigeration units, defining their construction, attachment and material characteristics that ensure an airtight closure of doors or compartments and contribute to energy-saving performance.
[F25D 23/10] Arrangements for mounting in particular locations, e.g. for built-in type, for corner type -- F25D 23/10 classifies inventions that specify how a refrigerator is mounted in a specific position, such as built-in installations or corner placements. It covers structural arrangements that enable the appliance to fit within a defined space, integrate with surrounding cabinetry, or accommodate unique site constraints. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and the broader category of refrigerators.
[F25D 23/12] Arrangements of compartments additional to cooling compartments -- This subclass deals with the design and layout of extra compartments within a refrigerator that are not intended for cooling, such as door shelves, vegetable bins, or snack drawers, describing how they are positioned relative to the main cooling chamber. It covers structural configurations that separate these auxiliary spaces from the refrigerated volume.
[F25D 23/123] Butter compartment -- The F25D 23/123 entry covers the design and integration of butter compartments in household or commercial refrigerators, including their location, shape, sealing methods and any specialized insulation or temperature control features that distinguish them from other freezer or fresh-food sections. It addresses structural aspects such as drawers, trays, lids and mounting arrangements that facilitate easy access and maintain appropriate conditions for butter storage.
[F25D 23/126] Water cooler -- F25D 23/126 designates water coolers, a subcategory of refrigerators, focusing on their general constructional features such as cabinet layout, internal compartments, and dispensing mechanisms. The code is used in the cooperative patent classification for mechanical engineering refrigeration technologies.
[F25D 2317/00] Details or arrangements for circulating cooling fluids -- F25D 2317/00 covers the design and configuration of components that move refrigerant or other cooling fluids through a refrigerator. It includes pumps, channels, manifolds, piping layouts and control mechanisms that ensure efficient fluid circulation within the cooling system.
[F25D 2317/04] Treating air flowing to refrigeration compartments -- The F25D 2317/04 class covers methods and devices that treat the air supplied to refrigeration compartments, including heating, cooling, dehumidifying, cleaning, or otherwise conditioning the airflow before it enters the refrigerated space. It is used for improving air quality, temperature uniformity, or preventing frost buildup in refrigerators.
[F25D 2317/041] by purification -- The code F25D 2317/041 classifies refrigerator designs that incorporate a purification step for the circulating cooling fluid, detailing how the fluid is cleaned or filtered within the system to maintain efficiency and prevent contamination.
[F25D 2317/0411] by dehumidification -- This CPC subclass concerns refrigerators that incorporate dehumidification functions. It covers specific details or arrangements for circulating cooling fluids to remove moisture from the interior air. The code F25D 2317/0411 identifies such dehumidifying features in refrigeration equipment.
[F25D 2317/04111] Control means therefor -- F25D 2317/04111 covers control devices for circulating cooling fluids within refrigerator systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling, and relates to arrangements that manage the flow of coolant.
[F25D 2317/0413] by humidification -- The subclass F25D 2317/0413 covers refrigerator configurations that employ humidification as part of the method for circulating cooling fluids, using moisture addition to enhance heat-transfer efficiency or control condensation within the cooling loop. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and refrigerators.
[F25D 2317/04131] Control means therefor -- A control device for the circulation of cooling fluids in a refrigerator, covering mechanisms that regulate flow, pressure, or temperature of the refrigerant within the system. This falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigerators, detailing fluid-circulating arrangements.
[F25D 2317/0415] by deodorizing -- F25D 2317/0415 covers refrigeration systems in which the circulating cooling fluid is treated to remove odors, typically by incorporating deodorizing agents or processes as part of the fluid circulation loop. This sub-class falls under details or arrangements for circulating cooling fluids in refrigerators.
[F25D 2317/0416] using an ozone generator -- The code F25D 2317/0416 covers refrigerator or cooling-system designs that incorporate an ozone generator to treat the circulating cooling fluid, for purposes such as sterilisation, fouling reduction or oxidative cleaning within the refrigerant loop.
[F25D 2317/0417] using an UV-lamp -- The entry concerns the integration of an ultraviolet lamp into systems that circulate cooling fluids inside refrigerators, detailing how UV irradiation is used to treat or disinfect the fluid as it moves through the apparatus. This falls under the mechanical engineering subclass for refrigeration and cooling fluid circulation.
[F25D 2317/043] by creating a vacuum in a storage compartment -- This subclass addresses refrigerator designs that use a vacuum created inside the storage compartment as a means of circulating the cooling fluid. The vacuum reduces internal pressure to draw the refrigerant through the system, improving heat removal without mechanical pumps. It applies to appliances where fluid movement relies on pressure-differentials generated by the vacuum.
[F25D 2317/06] with forced air circulation -- F25D 2317/06 covers refrigerators that use forced air to move the cooling fluid through the appliance. It pertains to design features that create a directed airflow, such as fans or blowers, to enhance heat transfer within the refrigerated compartment.
[F25D 2317/061] through special compartments -- The CPC subclass F25D 2317/061 covers refrigerators that incorporate special compartments for circulating cooling fluids, detailing arrangements that direct refrigerant through dedicated sections within the appliance. These designs improve temperature control and energy efficiency by isolating fluid pathways.
[F25D 2317/062] along the inside of doors -- This subclass covers features of refrigerating appliances where the coolant circulation path runs along the interior surfaces of the doors, such as ducts, tubes or channels integrated into the door structure to distribute refrigerant. It relates to arrangements for circulating cooling fluids in refrigerator door designs.

It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically details or arrangements for circulating cooling fluids in refrigerators.

It specifies arrangements that integrate the air return as a characteristic feature of the fluid circulation circuit.

Code F25D 2317/0653 covers such fluid passages integrated into the mullion structure.

[F25D 2321/00] Details or arrangements for defrosting -- F25D 2321/00 relates to the design and operation of defrosting mechanisms in refrigerators. It includes heating elements, timed or humidity-controlled cycles, sensors and control circuits that automatically remove frost buildup from evaporators, ensuring efficient cooling performance.
[F25D 2321/14] Collecting condense or defrost water -- This subclass covers devices or arrangements in refrigerators that gather water produced during condensation or defrost cycles and direct it to a drain or collection system, preventing accumulation inside the cabinet. It concerns defrost water management within refrigeration units.
[F25D 2321/141] Removal by evaporation -- F25D 2321/141 concerns methods for removing frost from refrigerators by evaporating the ice. The classification covers designs where defrost water is evaporated, typically using heated air or a heating element, to prevent ice buildup.

It falls under the mechanical-engineering branch for refrigeration or cooling, specifically within the refrigerators category and the details or arrangements for defrosting.
[F25D 2321/1412] using condenser heat or heat of desuperheaters -- The CPC subclass F25D 2321/1412, within mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigerators → details or arrangements for defrosting, pertains to methods that use the heat from a condenser or from desuperheaters to accomplish the defrosting process.

[F25D 2321/142] characterised by droplet guides -- The CPC class F25D 2321/142 belongs to mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and the subcategory of refrigerators, covering details or arrangements for defrosting that are characterised by droplet guides. These guides direct condensate droplets away from the evaporator during a defrost cycle, improving moisture management and preventing ice buildup.

[F25D 2400/16] Convertible refrigerators -- F25D 2400/16 covers convertible refrigerators, which can be switched between refrigeration and freezing modes, allowing a single unit to serve as a regular fridge or a freezer. The subclass includes general features of such dual-purpose devices that are not assigned to any more specific category.
[F25D 2400/18] Aesthetic features -- This subclass addresses the aesthetic aspects of refrigerators, cold rooms, ice-boxes and related cooling equipment, covering visual design elements such as shapes, colors, surface finishes and decorative features that are not covered by other subclasses. It focuses on appearance rather than functional cooling performance.
[F25D 2400/20] Carts specially adapted for transporting objects to be cooled -- Carts specially adapted for transporting objects to be cooled are mobile units used inside refrigerators, cold rooms or other cooling equipment to move goods while maintaining low temperatures. They typically feature insulated platforms, wheels or rollers and may include mechanisms for securing loads during transport.
[F25D 2400/22] Cleaning means for refrigerating devices -- The subclass addresses devices, systems or methods that clean refrigerators, cold rooms, ice-boxes or other cooling or freezing equipment, encompassing mechanical, chemical or combined cleaning means that are not classified in any other subclass of refrigeration devices.
[F25D 2400/24] Protection against refrigerant explosions -- The subclass covers safety features and devices that prevent or mitigate explosions of refrigerants in refrigerators, cold rooms, ice-boxes and other cooling or freezing equipment. It includes pressure-relief valves, rupture disks, explosion-proof housings, gas-detector controls and other protective measures designed to limit over-pressure or ignition of refrigerant gases.
[F25D 2400/26] Refrigerating devices for cooling wearing apparel, e.g. garments, hats, shoes or gloves -- F25D 2400/26 relates to refrigerating devices intended to cool wearing apparel such as garments, hats, shoes or gloves. These systems provide personal-wear cooling rather than general refrigeration of food or other items.
[F25D 2400/28] Quick cooling -- This subclass covers rapid-cooling methods or apparatus integrated in refrigerators, cold rooms, or ice-boxes that accelerate temperature drop beyond standard cycles. It includes features such as blast-chill chambers, high-speed fans, or heat-exchange designs intended to quickly lower product temperature.
[F25D 2400/30] Quick freezing -- Quick freezing covers equipment or techniques that rapidly lower the temperature of products, such as food, by employing high-capacity refrigeration cycles, blast-freezer chambers, or immersion methods to achieve rapid ice formation. It is classified under mechanical engineering for refrigeration devices that are not covered by any more specific subclass.
[F25D 2400/32] Removal, transportation or shipping of refrigerating devices from one location to another -- F25D 2400/32 covers the removal, transport and shipping of refrigeration equipment such as refrigerators, cold rooms or ice-boxes from one site to another, including related handling methods and packaging.
[F25D 2400/34] Temperature balancing devices -- F25D 2400/34 covers devices that equalise temperature within a refrigerator, cold room or similar cooling apparatus, such as heat exchangers, thermostatic valves or internal circulation systems designed to maintain uniform temperature distribution and improve energy efficiency. The class applies to features not classified elsewhere in the refrigerator subclass.
[F25D 2400/36] Visual displays -- This subclass covers visual-display devices integrated in refrigerators, cold rooms, ice-boxes or other cooling and freezing equipment, such as digital read-outs, LED panels or indicator lights that present temperature, status or other operating information, when the display is not classified elsewhere.
[F25D 2400/361] Interactive visual displays -- F25D 2400/361 is a CPC subclass for interactive visual displays incorporated into refrigeration appliances such as refrigerators, cold rooms or ice-boxes. It covers any display or touchscreen used to present information, provide user interfaces or control functions that are not otherwise classified in more specific subclasses.
[F25D 2400/38] Refrigerating devices characterised by wheels -- This subclass covers refrigerators, cold rooms, ice-boxes and other cooling or freezing apparatus that are equipped with wheels, allowing them to be moved easily. It includes any general features or devices for such mobile refrigerating equipment not classified elsewhere.
[F25D 2400/40] Refrigerating devices characterised by electrical wiring -- This subclass covers refrigerating appliances such as refrigerators, freezers, cold rooms or ice-boxes whose distinguishing feature is the presence or arrangement of electrical wiring, including power distribution, control circuits or safety wiring integrated into the cooling system.
[F25D 25/00] Charging, supporting, and discharging the articles to be cooled -- F25D 25/00 covers the processes and devices for introducing refrigerant into a cooling system, supporting the items being cooled, and removing the cooled articles from the unit. It includes methods for charging the system, structural arrangements that hold products during refrigeration, and mechanisms for unloading or discharging the cooled items.
[F25D 2500/00] Problems to be solved
[F25D 2500/02] Geometry problems -- F25D 2500/02 covers geometry problems in refrigerators, addressing the shape, dimensions, and spatial arrangement of components such as cabinets, doors, shelves, or internal compartments that affect capacity, performance, or manufacturability. It includes design issues related to optimizing volume, heat distribution, or structural layout within cooling appliances.
[F25D 2500/04] Calculation of parameters -- F25D 2500/04 refers to inventions that provide methods or computer models for calculating key performance parameters of refrigerators, such as cooling capacity, power consumption, coefficient of performance, or thermodynamic properties. The classification is used for patents addressing analytical, numerical or simulation techniques to predict or optimise refrigerator behaviour.
[F25D 2500/06] Stock management -- The CPC entry F25D 2500/06 pertains to problems concerning stock management for refrigerators. It covers inventions that improve the handling, tracking, replenishment or distribution of refrigeration units, including systems, methods or devices that optimise inventory control, storage layouts or supply-chain processes for these appliances.
[F25D 25/005] using containers -- The subclass F25D 25/005 covers methods and apparatus that use containers to charge, support or discharge items being cooled in refrigeration or freezer units. It includes removable or integral containers for loading, transporting or removing the cooled articles and for facilitating heat exchange during operation.
[F25D 25/02] by shelves
[F25D 25/021] combined with trays -- This CPC group covers refrigerating appliances that incorporate trays as part of the system for loading, supporting, or unloading the items to be cooled. The trays are integrated with the charging or discharging mechanisms, allowing the goods to be placed on or removed from the trays while the refrigeration cycle operates. It applies to devices where the trays are a functional element of the cooling unit.
[F25D 25/022] Baskets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wire baskets.
[F25D 25/024] Slidable shelves -- F25D 25/024 covers slidable shelves used in refrigerators to hold, charge, support, and discharge items being cooled. The shelves are designed to move horizontally or vertically within the compartment, facilitating easy access to stored goods. This subclass falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and refrigerators.
[F25D 25/025] Drawers -- This classification covers the design and arrangement of drawers used inside refrigerators to hold and organize cooled items, including their mechanisms for loading, supporting, and unloading contents. It pertains to refrigeration equipment under mechanical engineering, focusing on internal compartments that facilitate charging, supporting, and discharging of stored goods.
[F25D 25/027] Rotatable shelves -- Rotatable shelves are adjustable platforms inside a refrigerator that can be turned or pivoted to change orientation, enabling users to reposition items for efficient loading, unloading and organization of cooled articles. They belong to the CPC subclass covering charging, supporting and discharging of refrigerated goods.
[F25D 25/028] Cooled supporting means -- The code F25D 25/028 covers refrigerated support components used inside cooling appliances, such as chilled shelves, trays or racks that both hold items and convey cooling to the support itself. These means help maintain a uniform low temperature for the stored articles while providing structural support during charging, supporting and discharging operations.
[F25D 25/04] by conveyors -- F25D 25/04 covers refrigerating appliances in which the loading, support and removal of goods are performed by conveyor mechanisms. It pertains to systems that move items through the cooling chamber for efficient charging, supporting, and discharging.
[F25D 2600/00] Control issues -- F25D 2600/00 covers control issues for refrigerating appliances, including devices and methods that regulate temperature, manage defrost cycles, or adjust operating parameters of refrigerators. It falls under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigerators in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F25D 2600/02] Timing -- The CPC subclass F25D 2600/02 relates to timing mechanisms that control the operation of refrigeration appliances, encompassing devices that schedule cooling cycles, defrost periods and other functional phases of a refrigerator. It addresses control issues specific to the timing of these processes.
[F25D 2600/04] Controlling heat transfer -- F25D 2600/04 covers inventions that regulate the transfer of heat within a refrigerator, including devices, methods or control strategies for managing heat-exchange processes, such as thermostatic regulation, variable-speed compressors, flow-control valves or electronic monitoring that optimise cooling performance and energy use.
[F25D 2600/06] Controlling according to a predetermined profile -- This CPC subgroup covers methods and devices for controlling a refrigerator based on a preset temperature or operational profile, including programmable thermostats and cycle-adjustment schemes that follow a predefined schedule. The focus is on implementing control strategies that follow a predetermined setpoint curve rather than reactive control.
[F25D 27/00] Lighting arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lighting arrangements for lighting cooling enclosures, both inside and outside the enclosure.
[F25D 2700/00] Means for sensing or measuring -- F25D 2700/00 covers devices incorporated in refrigerators that detect or measure operating conditions such as temperature, pressure, humidity or ice formation. These sensing or measuring means are used to control the cooling cycle, improve energy efficiency or provide user feedback in household or commercial refrigeration units.
[F25D 2700/02] Sensors detecting door opening -- F25D 2700/02 covers sensor devices that detect when a refrigerator door is opened, typically using mechanical, magnetic, optical or electronic methods to trigger alarms, control circuits or energy-saving functions. These sensors are integral to refrigerator designs to monitor access, improve safety and optimise power consumption.
[F25D 2700/04] Sensors detecting the presence of a person -- In CPC class F25D 2700/04, under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigerators → means for sensing or measuring, the entry covers sensors that detect the presence of a person, used in refrigerator systems to control operation, improve energy efficiency and enhance safety.
[F25D 2700/06] Sensors detecting the presence of a product -- This subclass of mechanical engineering for refrigeration covers devices that sense whether an item is inside a refrigerator, typically using optical, capacitive or weight sensors to trigger control actions. It is used in automatic door control, defrost cycles, and inventory monitoring.
[F25D 2700/08] Sensors using Radio Frequency Identification [RFID] -- F25D 2700/08 is a CPC class in the Mechanical Engineering section, under Refrigeration or Cooling → Refrigerators → Means for sensing or measuring. It designates sensors that use Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) technology to detect, monitor or control parameters in refrigeration equipment.
[F25D 2700/10] Sensors measuring the temperature of the evaporator -- The classification F25D 2700/10 covers devices that sense or measure the temperature of the evaporator in refrigeration or cooling systems, typically integrated into refrigerators. These sensors provide feedback for controlling cooling cycles and improving efficiency.
[F25D 2700/12] Sensors measuring the inside temperature -- F25D 2700/12 designates temperature-sensing devices incorporated in refrigerators to monitor the interior climate. The code covers any sensor used for measuring inside temperature, whether electronic, mechanical or combined, and supports control, safety and efficiency functions in cooling appliances.
[F25D 2700/121] of particular compartments -- This subclass covers sensors or measuring devices that are installed in particular compartments of a refrigerator, such as the freezer, fresh-food drawer or vegetable bin, to monitor parameters like temperature, humidity or pressure and support compartment-specific control and safety functions.
[F25D 2700/122] of freezer compartments -- This CPC subclass covers devices or methods for detecting or measuring parameters within freezer compartments of refrigeration units. It includes temperature, humidity or pressure sensors integrated into the freezer space to monitor conditions for safety or performance.
[F25D 2700/123] more than one sensor measuring the inside temperature in a compartment -- This classification covers refrigerators that use two or more sensors to measure the temperature inside a compartment, allowing more precise monitoring and control of the cooling performance.
[F25D 2700/14] Sensors measuring the temperature outside the refrigerator or freezer -- F25D 2700/14 refers to sensors that measure the temperature of the environment surrounding a refrigerator or freezer, providing data for control, safety or display functions. These devices belong to the refrigeration subgroup of mechanical engineering and are classified under means for sensing or measuring.
[F25D 2700/16] Sensors measuring the temperature of products -- The subclass F25D 2700/16 concerns sensors that measure the temperature of products placed in refrigeration units. It includes devices such as thermocouples, resistance temperature detectors, infrared or contact sensors used to monitor and control cooling performance.
[F25D 27/005] combined with control means -- F25D 27/005 covers refrigerators that incorporate lighting arrangements together with control means, such as switches, sensors or timers that activate or modulate interior illumination. It includes systems where the light operation is linked to door opening, temperature regulation or user settings.
[F25D 29/00] Arrangement or mounting of control or safety devices -- F25D 29/00 covers the way control or safety devices such as thermostats, pressure relief valves, or electronic sensors are positioned or fixed within refrigeration equipment. It includes designs for mounting brackets, housings, or integration methods that ensure reliable operation and compliance with safety standards. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically refrigeration or cooling systems for refrigerators.
[F25D 29/001] for cryogenic fluid systems -- F25D 29/001 designates the arrangement or mounting of control or safety devices in refrigerating appliances that handle cryogenic fluids. It applies to mechanical engineering solutions for safely managing ultra-low temperature systems such as liquid nitrogen or helium refrigeration units.
[F25D 29/003] for movable devices -- F25D 29/003 covers the arrangement or mounting of control and safety devices on refrigerating appliances that are designed to be moved, such as portable or mobile refrigerators and freezers. The classification addresses the placement of switches, thermostats, pressure release valves and other protective components in units that can be relocated during use or transport.
[F25D 29/005] Mounting of control devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mounting of control devices; Mounting of sensors to refrigeration devices
[F25D 29/006] Safety devices -- F25D 29/006 pertains to safety devices incorporated in refrigerators, describing how control or protective components are arranged or mounted to guard against hazards such as over-pressure, over-temperature, electrical faults, or other unsafe operating conditions.
[F25D 29/008] Alarm devices -- This subclass covers alarm devices incorporated in refrigerating appliances, such as systems that detect temperature deviations, door openings, or component failures and generate audible or visual warnings to alert the user.
[F25D 3/00] Devices using other cold materials -- The code F25D 3/00 covers refrigeration or cooling devices that generate a low temperature by employing cold substances other than standard refrigerants, such as ice, dry ice, chilled brine, liquid nitrogen or other cryogenic media. It includes appliances, portable units and systems that use these alternative cold materials to achieve cooling.
[F25D 3/005] combined with heat exchangers -- The entry refers to refrigeration units that employ cold substances other than conventional refrigerants and incorporate heat exchangers, allowing heat to be transferred between the cooling medium and another fluid within the same device. This integration is used to improve overall thermal efficiency and enable combined cooling-heating functions.
[F25D 3/02] using ice, e.g. ice-boxes -- F25D 3/02 covers refrigeration devices that employ ice or other solid cold substances as the cooling medium, such as traditional ice-boxes. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, and specifically refrigerators using other cold materials.
[F25D 3/04] Stationary cabinets -- Stationary cabinets are fixed refrigeration units that employ alternative cold sources such as ice, dry ice, or phase-change materials rather than conventional compressors, used for storage or display of temperature-sensitive goods.
[F25D 3/045] Details
[F25D 3/06] Movable containers -- F25D 3/06 covers movable containers that incorporate a cold material other than conventional refrigerants to provide cooling. These include portable coolers, insulated boxes, transport containers and similar devices in which the cold source is a solid or liquid material carried within the container. The classification applies to refrigeration systems where the container itself is the cooling element.
[F25D 3/08] portable, i.e. adapted to be carried personally -- Portable refrigeration devices that use alternative cold materials such as ice, phase-change substances, or other non-refrigerant cooling agents, rather than conventional refrigerants, and are designed to be carried personally. This subclass covers handheld or transportable units within the broader category of refrigerators.
[F25D 3/10] using liquefied gases, e.g. liquid air -- This subclass covers refrigerators that achieve cooling by employing liquefied gases such as liquid air, where the gas expands or vaporizes to absorb heat. It includes systems that store the cryogenic fluid and use its phase change to provide refrigeration rather than conventional compressors or absorption cycles.
[F25D 31/00] Other cooling or freezing apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling by creating a vacuum.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Storing of products in a refrigerated enclosure under vacuum: -> F25D2317/043
[F25D 31/001] Plate freezers -- F25D 31/001 refers to plate freezers, which are devices that employ a series of thin, flat plates to freeze or chill products by direct contact with a refrigerant. These units are used in industrial refrigeration for rapid freezing of food or other materials and fall under the mechanical engineering category of refrigeration apparatus.
[F25D 31/002] Liquid coolers, e.g. beverage cooler  receptacle coolers  F25D31/006 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Repectacle coolers -> F25D31/006
[F25D 31/003] with immersed cooling element -- A refrigeration device in which the cooling element, such as an evaporator coil, is placed directly inside the space or medium to be cooled, allowing heat to be transferred by immersion rather than through separate heat-exchange surfaces. This arrangement is used in appliances like immersion chillers, cold-storage bins, or other apparatus where the refrigerant contacts the product directly.
[F25D 31/005] Combined cooling and heating devices -- F25D 31/005 designates apparatus that integrates cooling and heating functions in a single system, such as heat pumps or combined refrigerator-heater units. These devices employ a shared refrigeration cycle to provide temperature control for both heating and cooling purposes and are classified under other cooling or freezing apparatus.
[F25D 31/006] specially adapted for cooling receptacles, e.g. tanks -- F25D 31/006 covers devices that are specifically designed to cool containers such as tanks, incorporating features that adapt the refrigeration system to the shape, size or material of the receptacle. It falls within mechanical engineering, under refrigeration or cooling, and pertains to other cooling or freezing apparatus for refrigerated tanks.
[F25D 31/007] Bottles or cans
[F25D 31/008] Drinking glasses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pre-chilling of glasses.
[F25D 3/102] Stationary cabinets -- F25D 3/102 covers stationary refrigeration cabinets that use cold substances other than conventional refrigerants, such as ice, dry ice or chilled water, to keep their interior at low temperatures. These cabinets are fixed in place and are intended for storage or display of perishable items.
[F25D 3/105] Movable containers -- F25D 3/105 concerns refrigeration systems that employ movable containers filled with alternative cold substances such as ice, gel packs, or phase-change materials. The classification covers devices where the cold source is carried in a container that can be placed into or removed from the refrigerator, enabling transport or temporary cooling without a built-in compressor.
[F25D 3/107] portable, i.e. adapted to be carried personally -- F25D 3/107 covers portable refrigeration apparatus that employ alternative cold substances rather than conventional refrigerants. These devices are designed to be carried personally for tasks such as food or medication cooling. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration, and refrigerators using other cold materials.
[F25D 3/11] with conveyors carrying articles to be cooled through the cooling space -- The subclass F25D 3/11 covers refrigeration devices that employ a conveyor system to move articles through the cooling space, using cold materials other than conventional refrigerants such as ice, dry ice, or other chilled substances, to achieve the desired temperature reduction.
[F25D 3/12] using solidified gases, e.g. carbon-dioxide snow -- F25D 3/12 covers refrigeration devices that employ solidified gases, such as carbon-dioxide snow, as the cooling medium. The class includes systems where the frozen gas is generated and used to absorb heat, providing refrigeration for various applications.
[F25D 3/122] Stationary cabinets -- F25D 3/122 covers stationary cabinets that provide cooling by employing cold materials other than conventional refrigerants, such as ice, dry ice, gel packs or phase-change substances. These units are fixed in place and designed for temporary or long-term storage of temperature-sensitive goods.
[F25D 3/125] Movable containers -- This subclass covers refrigerating devices that employ portable or transportable containers designed to keep goods at low temperature, typically using ice, cold packs or other removable cooling media. It includes insulated boxes, transport tanks and similar movable units used in refrigeration systems.
[F25D 3/127] Stationary devices with conveyors carrying articles to be cooled through the cooling space -- Stationary refrigeration units that include conveyors to transport articles through a cooling zone for cooling are covered by this subclass. It addresses devices where the cold material-often a refrigerant or ice-remains fixed while items move on a belt or chain within the refrigerated space. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration, and refrigerators that use other cold materials.
[F25D 3/14] portable, i.e. adapted to be carried personally -- F25D 3/14 designates portable refrigeration units that employ alternative cold-material media instead of conventional refrigerants. These devices are intended to be carried by a user and are classified under mechanical engineering → refrigeration or cooling → refrigerators → devices using other cold materials.
[F25D 5/00] Devices using endothermic chemical reactions, e.g. using frigorific mixtures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling by endothermic chemical reaction between substances triggered by for example rupturing/piercing a membrane.
[F25D 5/02] portable, i.e. adapted to be carried personally -- Portable refrigeration devices that use endothermic chemical reactions, such as frigorific mixtures, to produce cooling and are intended to be carried personally. These units belong to the mechanical engineering subclass for refrigerators.
[F25D 7/00] Devices using evaporation effects without recovery of the vapour -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cooling effect is derived from the evaporation of water, for which a supply of heat is required.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vacuum coolers -> F25D31/00 - Butter or cheese dishes with cooling devices -> A47G19/26 - Evaporative coolers -> F24F5/0035 , F28D5/00
[F25D 9/00] Devices not associated with refrigerating machinery and not covered by groups -- F25D 9/00 classifies devices that are used with refrigerators but are not part of the refrigerating plant itself and do not fall within any other defined subgroup. This includes interior fittings, dispensing mechanisms, defrost heaters and other ancillary equipment that serves the refrigerator without directly handling the refrigerant.
[F25D 9/005] using fluorinated halogenous hydrocarbons -- The subclass F25D 9/005 covers refrigeration devices that employ fluorinated halogenated hydrocarbons-such as CFCs, HCFCs or HFCs-as working fluids, but are not part of the main refrigerating machinery groups. It includes ancillary or specialized cooling equipment that uses these gases for heat-transfer purposes.
[F25J] LIQUEFACTION, SOLIDIFICATION OR SEPARATION OF GASES OR GASEOUS MIXTURES BY PRESSURE AND COLD TREATMENT -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or systems for liquefying or solidifying gases or gaseous mixtures and for separating the constituents of gaseous or liquid mixtures involving the use of liquefaction or solidification by rectification or partial condensation, the processes or systems use internal and/or external refrigeration to reach very low temperatures, i.e. so-called cryogenic temperatures, in general well below -50°C;  Arrangements of cold exchangers or cold accumulators in cryogenic separation or liquefaction plants.
[F25J 1/00] Processes or apparatus for liquefying or solidifying gases or gaseous mixtures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or systems wherein a gaseous feed or feed mixture is liquefied or solidified using internal and/or external refrigeration and withdrawn as liquefied or solidified product or product mixture. The gases or gaseous mixtures comprises so-called "permanent gases", e.g. noble gases, hydrogen and primary air constituents, and gases or main components of gaseous mixtures having boiling points equal to or lower than -78°C, i.e. the boiling point of carbon dioxide, e.g. natural gas, carbon monoxide.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recovering volatile solvents by condensation -> B01D5/00 - Vapour recovery systems combined with filling nozzles -> B67D7/54 - Solidification of carbonic acid -> C01B32/55 - Production of ammonia -> C01C1/00 - Cryogenic pumps -> F04B37/08 - Pipe-line systems -> F17D1/00  **Glossary:** - Refrigeration cascade: The feed stream is cooled by two or more subsequent refrigeration means - Natural gas: Substitute natural gas, gas obtained from a crude oil well, i.e. associated or petroleum gas, or from a gas well, i.e. non-associated gas, gas recovered from a tertiary oil recovery process, mine or coal-bed-seam gas, landfill gas - BOG: Boil-off gases i.e. evaporated gases from cryogenic liquid storage vessels - MCR: Multi-component refrigerant - SCR: Single-component refrigerant
[F25J 1/0002] characterised by the fluid to be liquefied -- F25J 1/0002 covers processes and apparatus for liquefying gases that are distinguished by the particular fluid being treated, such as air, nitrogen, hydrogen or carbon dioxide, and includes design features or operating conditions tailored to the specific physical properties of that gas.
[F25J 1/0005] Light or noble gases  F25J1/0012  takes precedence -- F25J 1/0005 covers processes or apparatus for liquefying or solidifying light or noble gases such as helium, neon, argon, krypton, or xenon. It is nested under mechanical engineering refrigeration and cooling, specifically the segment dealing with pressure and cold treatment of gases. The more specific subclass F25J 1/0012 takes precedence over this entry.
[F25J 1/0007] Helium -- The code F25J 1/0007 covers processes and equipment for liquefying or solidifying helium, a noble gas with an extremely low boiling point of 4.2 K. It includes cryogenic systems that compress, cool, and expand helium to achieve its liquid or solid state for applications such as superconducting magnets and low-temperature research.
[F25J 1/001] Hydrogen -- F25J 1/001 relates to technologies for liquefying or solidifying hydrogen, covering processes that use high-pressure and low-temperature conditions to convert hydrogen gas into liquid or solid form and the associated equipment for its production, storage and handling.
[F25J 1/0012] Primary atmospheric gases, e.g. air -- F25J 1/0012 pertains to processes and apparatus for liquefying or solidifying primary atmospheric gases, such as air, by employing pressure and low-temperature treatment in order to separate or store the gas mixture.
[F25J 1/0015] Nitrogen -- This subclass covers processes and apparatus for liquefying or solidifying nitrogen, using pressure and low-temperature treatment to convert the gas into liquid or solid form for industrial, laboratory or energy-storage applications. It resides within mechanical engineering, under refrigeration or cooling, and specifically the group for liquefaction, solidification or separation of gases by pressure and cold treatment.
[F25J 1/0017] Oxygen -- F25J 1/0017 covers technologies for the liquefaction or solidification of oxygen, including methods that use pressure and low-temperature conditions to transform the gas into liquid or solid form. It also includes equipment designed for handling, storing, or processing oxygen in these states.
[F25J 1/002] Argon -- F25J 1/002 covers technologies for the liquefaction or solidification of argon, including apparatus and processes that employ pressure and low temperature to convert argon gas into liquid or solid form. It falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration or cooling, specifically the sub-category dealing with gas separation by pressure and cold treatment.
[F25J 1/0022] Hydrocarbons, e.g. natural gas -- This subclass covers processes or apparatus that liquefy or solidify hydrocarbon gases such as natural gas by applying pressure and low temperature. It includes cycles, methods and equipment designed to achieve the phase change of the gas for storage or transport.
[F25J 1/0025] Boil-off gases "BOG" from storages -- The classification covers processes and apparatus for handling boil-off gas that escapes from stored liquefied gases, such as LNG, including its recovery, recompression, reliquefaction or use as fuel, and falls under mechanical engineering, refrigeration and cooling, specifically the treatment of gases by pressure and low temperature.
[F25J 1/0027] Oxides of carbon, e.g. CO 2 -- F25J 1/0027 covers technologies for the liquefaction or solidification of carbon oxides, especially carbon dioxide, in refrigeration or cooling systems. It includes processes and apparatus that employ pressure and low-temperature conditions to separate or store CO₂ in liquid or solid form.
[F25J 1/003] characterised by the kind of cold generation within the liquefaction unit for compensating heat leaks and liquid production -- F25J 1/003 relates to liquefaction or solidification of gases where the cooling needed to offset heat leaks and generate the liquid product is produced inside the liquefaction unit itself, rather than by an external refrigeration source. This subclass covers apparatus and processes that integrate cold generation within the main liquefaction equipment to improve efficiency.
[F25J 1/0032] using the feed stream itself or separated fractions from it, i.e. "internal refrigeration" -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus that use the incoming gas stream or its separated fractions as the refrigerant for cooling, liquefying or solidifying gases, known as internal refrigeration. It includes systems where the feed itself provides the cooling effect instead of an external refrigerant.
