[F26B 23/00] Heating arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heating arrangements providing a heat source for drying machines, located outside or within the drying enclosure.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements for supplying or controlling heated air or other gases for drying solid material or objects -> F26B21/00
[F26B 23/001] using waste heat -- The classification F26B 23/001 covers heating arrangements for drying solid materials that make use of waste heat, i.e., heat recovered from other industrial processes, to remove moisture. It falls under mechanical engineering, drying solid objects by removing liquid.
[F26B 23/002] recovered from dryer exhaust gases  F26B23/022  takes precedence -- The CPC code F26B 23/002 covers arrangements for recovering heat, moisture or other useful products from the exhaust gases of dryers used to remove liquid from solid materials. It includes heat exchangers, condensers or scrubbers that capture thermal energy or condensate as the gas leaves the dryer, improving overall energy efficiency of the drying process. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering → drying → drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid → heating arrangements.
[F26B 23/004] by compressing and condensing vapour in exhaust gases, i.e. using an open cycle heat pump system -- F26B 23/004 covers drying of solid materials by heating arrangements that compress and condense vapour in exhaust gases, employing an open-cycle heat-pump system to remove liquid from the product. This method recovers heat from the vapour stream, improving energy efficiency during the drying process.
[F26B 23/005] using a closed cycle heat pump system  with recycling of drying medium  F26B21/333 ; using a heat pipe system -- This subclass covers drying solid objects where the heat is supplied by a closed-cycle heat pump that recirculates the drying medium, optionally using heat-pipe technology. It pertains to heating arrangements that recycle the drying gas or liquid within the process.
[F26B 23/007] recovered from the dried product  burning the product  F26B23/028
[F26B 23/008] using a heat pump cycle -- The F26B 23/008 subclass pertains to heating arrangements employed in drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid, specifically where the drying process is driven by a heat-pump cycle. It captures technologies that use the reversible compression and expansion of a refrigerant to recycle heat and improve energy efficiency during solid-material drying.
[F26B 23/02] using combustion heating -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion for generating IR radiation, either through flame radiation or combustion heated tubes -> F26B3/305 - Heating rollers for drying materials of long length -> F26B13/186 - Air generating units for drying solid materials or objects -> F26B21/001
[F26B 23/022] incinerating volatiles in the dryer exhaust gases, the produced hot gases being wholly, partly or not recycled into the drying enclosure -- F26B 23/022 covers drying systems where the exhaust gases are treated by burning off volatile compounds, and the resulting hot gases may be fed back into the dryer, partially recirculated, or discarded. This technique improves energy efficiency and removes harmful volatiles during the drying of solid materials.
[F26B 23/024] by means of catalytic oxidation -- F26B 23/024 covers heating arrangements used to dry solid materials or objects by removing liquid through catalytic oxidation. The method employs a catalyst to accelerate oxidation of moisture, providing efficient heat generation for the drying process.
[F26B 23/026] with pulse combustion, e.g. pulse jet combustion drying of particulate materials -- F26B 23/026 concerns drying solid or particulate materials by using pulse-combustion heating, such as pulse-jet combustion. The method creates rapid bursts of high-temperature gas that contact the material, improving heat transfer and moisture removal efficiency compared with conventional steady-state heating. It is applied in mechanical-engineering processes where fast, energy-dense drying is required.
[F26B 23/028] using solid fuel; burning the dried product -- F26B 23/028 covers heating arrangements for drying solid materials that employ solid fuel, specifically where the dried product itself is burnt to supply the required heat. The class addresses methods that use the material's own combustion as the drying energy source.
[F26B 23/04] using electric heating -- F26B 23/04 covers heating arrangements used to dry solid materials or objects by removing liquid, where the heat source is electric. It falls under the mechanical-engineering domain of drying solid substances, specifying electric heating as the means to achieve moisture removal.
[F26B 23/06] resistance heating -- F26B 23/06 designates resistance heating as a method of supplying heat for drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid, typically using electrical elements that generate heat through electrical resistance. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically drying solid materials, and is classified as a heating arrangement.
[F26B 23/10] using tubes or passages containing heated fluids -- F26B 23/10 covers drying methods that employ tubes or passages filled with heated fluids to transfer heat to solid materials, facilitating the removal of liquid. It is used in mechanical-engineering drying systems to dry solids by heating.
[F26B 25/00] Details of general application not covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of machines or processes other than arrangements of drying air or gas supply or heating arrangements such as:  Cleaning arrangements, e.g. for drying chambers.  Loading, unloading and other handling of objects or materials to be dried or dried.  Treatment of dryer exhaust gases.  Seals, locks and air curtains for drying enclosures.  Alarm, safety and fire protection systems.  Driving mechanisms specific for drying machines.  Agitating, stirring or scraping devices.  Drying chambers and parts thereof.  Rollers, e.g. for conveying specific to drying machines and their requirements.  Controlling the drying process depending on the liquid content of dried material or wet material being charged.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Driving mechanisms for rotary drums, e.g. kiln -> F26B11/022 - Arrangements for gas sealing drum dryers, e.g. kilns -> F26B11/024 - Loading and unloading materials in rotary drums, e.g. kilns -> F26B11/026 - Seals, locks for web drying enclosures -> F26B13/005 - Rollers and driving mechanisms for rollers for drying material of long length -> F26B13/14 - Treatment of dryer exhaust gas through incineration of volatiles -> F26B13/22 - Loading and unloading of wicket conveyor -> F26B15/085 - Material feeding means for pneumatic, flash or vortex dryers -> F26B17/103 - Safety and fire protection system using inert gases -> F26B21/40 - Driving mechanisms covered by another subclass, e.g. regarding centrifuges -> B04B9/00
[F26B 25/001] Handling, e.g. loading or unloading arrangements -- The CPC entry F26B 25/001 covers handling arrangements used in drying processes for solid materials, specifically equipment or mechanisms for loading and unloading the objects being dried. It includes fixtures, conveyors, or other devices that move items into, through, or out of dryers.
[F26B 25/002] for bulk goods -- This CPC subgroup covers methods and apparatus for drying bulk goods-such as grains, powders or other solid aggregates-where large quantities are processed and moisture is removed by convection, conduction, contact or similar drying techniques. It falls under mechanical engineering, drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid.
[F26B 25/003] for articles -- F26B 25/003 covers drying processes applied to articles, i.e., the removal of liquid from solid objects or bulk materials using mechanical engineering methods. The class addresses techniques where the material itself is the object being dried, rather than a continuous stream or surface coating.

[F26B 25/006] Separating volatiles, e.g. recovering solvents from dryer exhaust gases -- The CPC subclass F26B 25/006 covers methods and apparatus for separating volatile substances from the exhaust gases of dryers, such as recovering solvents or other vapours that have been removed from solid materials during drying. It includes techniques for condensing, adsorbing, or otherwise extracting these volatiles to reduce emissions or reuse the recovered liquids.
[F26B 25/007] Dust filtering; Exhaust dust filters -- F26B 25/007 addresses dust-filtering technology used in exhaust systems for drying processes, covering the design, construction and operation of filters that remove solid particles from gas streams. It includes filter media, housing arrangements, cleaning or regeneration methods, and adaptations for handling particulate-laden exhaust air in industrial drying equipment.

It includes any novel or auxiliary power transmission, motor, gear or actuator arrangement applied to remove liquid from solid materials. The code is part of the CPC group for drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid.
[F26B 25/04] Agitating, stirring, or scraping devices -- F26B 25/04 covers devices that use agitation, stirring or scraping to aid the drying of solid objects by removing liquid, such as mixers, tumbling devices or scraper mechanisms integrated in drying chambers. These tools create mechanical motion to increase liquid removal efficiency from the material being dried.
[F26B 25/06] Chambers, containers, or receptacles -- F26B 25/06 designates chambers, containers or receptacles employed in drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid, as part of mechanical-engineering drying technologies. The classification applies where the drying apparatus includes dedicated vessels or enclosures that hold the material during the removal process, covering designs not covered by more specific sub-groups.
[F26B 25/063] Movable containers or receptacles, e.g. carts, trolleys, pallet-boxes  for use in freeze-drying apparatus  F26B5/06 ; in general  B62D -- The CPC subclass F26B 25/063 covers movable containers or receptacles such as carts, trolleys or pallet-boxes designed for use in freeze-drying equipment, focusing on handling solid materials while removing liquid. It falls under F26B5/06 within the broader B62D class and pertains to mechanical-engineering drying of solids.
[F26B 25/066] Movable chambers, e.g. collapsible, demountable  self-contained mobile devices, e.g. for agricultural produce  F26B19/005
[F26B 25/08] Parts thereof -- F26B 25/08 classifies the components of drying equipment used to remove liquid from solid materials or objects. It covers structural, mechanical or auxiliary parts of dryers such as trays, belts, rotors and heating elements that are integral to the drying process. The code is nested under mechanical engineering for drying solid substances.
[F26B 25/10] Floors, roofs, or bottoms -- This subgroup concerns drying of solid objects where the surface to be dried forms a floor, roof or bottom of a container or structure. It includes methods that apply heat, airflow, vacuum or other means to remove moisture from such horizontal or inclined surfaces, especially in industrial or building contexts.
[F26B 25/12] Walls or sides -- The CPC code F26B 25/12 refers to drying equipment for solid materials where the specification concerns the walls or sides of the drying chamber. It covers features such as the construction, arrangement, insulation, or heating of the chamber's vertical surfaces that influence heat distribution, moisture removal efficiency, or sealing during the drying process.
[F26B 25/14] Chambers, containers, receptacles of simple construction -- This subclass covers simple chambers, containers or receptacles used for drying solid materials by removing liquid from them. It includes basic, uncomplicated designs without specialized features such as heating elements, agitation mechanisms or elaborate seals.
[F26B 25/16] mainly closed, e.g. drum -- F26B 25/16 covers dryers that are essentially enclosed, such as drum dryers, where solid material is placed inside a closed vessel and heated to evaporate moisture. The design relies on indirect heat transfer within the sealed chamber, allowing controlled drying of bulk solids.
[F26B 25/18] mainly open, e.g. dish, tray, pan -- F26B 25/18 relates to drying apparatus for solid objects that are principally open, such as dishes, trays or pans. It falls under mechanical engineering methods for removing liquid from solid materials, typically using heated air or similar convection techniques to achieve moisture removal.
[F26B 25/185] Spacers; Elements for supporting the goods to be dried, i.e. positioned in-between the goods to build a ventilated stack  separators for articles packaged in stacks  B65D57/00 ; manipulating spacers for stacking purposes  B65G57/005 -- F26B 25/185 relates to devices that hold or separate objects during drying by inserting spacers between them to form a ventilated stack, improving airflow and moisture removal. It also covers any apparatus for placing, adjusting or handling such spacers in stacked batches of goods.
[F26B 25/20] Rollers -- Rollers are cylindrical components used in drying operations to convey, support or press solid materials while liquid is removed, often incorporating heating or surface treatment to accelerate moisture evaporation. They are employed in dryers, ovens or continuous processing lines where the material passes over or between rotating cylinders that promote contact with hot surfaces and improve drying efficiency.
[F26B 25/22] Controlling the drying process in dependence on liquid content of solid materials or objects -- The subclass F26B 25/22 covers methods and apparatuses that adjust drying parameters according to the liquid content of the solid material or object being dried, using sensors, feedback loops or control algorithms to optimise energy consumption, drying time and product quality. It is applied to drying equipment where moisture level monitoring directly influences heat, airflow or other drying actions.
[F26B 25/225] by repeated or continuous weighing of the material or a sample thereof -- The subclass F26B 25/225 covers drying of solid materials or objects by removing liquid in which the process is monitored and controlled by repeatedly or continuously weighing the material or a sample thereof. It is applied when weight measurements are used to determine moisture content and to adjust drying parameters, ensuring efficient removal of the liquid from the solid.
[F26B 3/00] Drying solid materials or objects by processes involving the application of heat -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drying solid materials or objects by processes applying heat as primary drying principle.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processes involving direct heating, e.g. radiation, or indirect heating, e.g. convection, of the drying good merely as a measure to compensate cooling effects through evaporation, e.g. in a vacuum dryer -> F26B5/00 - Heat generated by friction through ultrasonic vibrations -> F26B5/02 - Condensing vapour other than steam onto the surface of the materials or objects to be dried -> F26B21/471
[F26B 3/005] by applying a combustible liquid onto the materials, the liquid being burnt off subsequently -- This CPC subclass F26B 3/005 describes drying solid materials by first applying a combustible liquid to the material and then burning it, using the heat from combustion to evaporate moisture. It is a subcategory of drying solid materials or objects by heat within mechanical engineering.
[F26B 3/02] by convection, i.e. heat being conveyed from a heat source to the materials or objects to be dried by a gas or vapour, e.g. air -- F26B 3/02 covers drying of solid materials or objects by convection, where heat from a source is transferred to the material through a moving gas or vapour, typically air, to remove moisture. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, drying, and specifically drying solid materials by applying heat.
[F26B 3/04] the gas or vapour circulating over or surrounding the materials or objects to be dried -- F26B 3/04 pertains to drying solid materials by heating in which a gas or vapor is directed to flow over or surround the items that are being dried. The classification covers processes that employ circulating hot air, steam or other vapors to remove moisture from solids.
[F26B 3/06] the gas or vapour flowing through the materials or objects to be dried -- F26B 3/06 covers drying of solid materials or objects where the process relies on a gas or vapour that is passed through the material to remove moisture. It includes systems that use forced air, steam or other drying gases to enhance heat transfer and accelerate moisture evaporation. This sub-class is part of the broader mechanical engineering field of drying solid materials by heat application.
[F26B 3/08] so as to loosen them, e.g. to form a fluidised bed -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Centrifugal fluidised beds -> F26B7/007
[F26B 3/082] arrangements of devices for distributing fluidising gas, e.g. grids, nozzles  F26B3/0926  takes precedence; such devices  per se   B01J8/44 -- The code F26B 3/082 covers the layout and integration of components that spread fluidising gas, such as grids, nozzles or similar distributors, within equipment used for heat-based drying of solid materials. It deals with how these gas-distribution elements are arranged rather than the individual devices themselves, which are classified under other headings.
[F26B 3/084] with heat exchange taking place in the fluidised bed -- F26B 3/084 covers drying of solid materials or objects by applying heat where the heat-exchange occurs within a fluidised bed, allowing efficient removal of moisture. The fluidised bed provides uniform temperature distribution and promotes rapid drying.
[F26B 3/088] using inert thermally-stabilised particles -- This subclass concerns drying solid materials or objects by applying heat while employing inert, thermally-stabilised particles that absorb and release heat to aid moisture removal. The particles remain chemically inactive during the process, providing a controlled thermal environment that improves drying efficiency and protects heat-sensitive materials.
[F26B 3/092] agitating the fluidised bed, e.g. by vibrating or pulsating -- This sub-class concerns drying of solid materials by heat where the fluidised-bed dryer is actively agitated, for example through vibration or pulsation, to improve particle mixing, heat transfer and overall drying efficiency.
[F26B 3/0923] by mechanical means, e.g. vibrated plate, stirrer -- F26B 3/0923 covers drying of solid materials where heat is applied together with mechanical actions such as vibrating plates or stirrers to enhance moisture removal. The classification is used for devices and processes that combine heating with agitation to improve drying efficiency.
[F26B 3/0926] by pneumatic means, e.g. spouted beds -- F26B 3/0926 covers drying solid materials or objects by heat using pneumatic methods such as spouted beds. The code falls under mechanical engineering, drying solid materials by removing liquid, focusing on heat-assisted processes that employ airflow or gas to enhance evaporation.
[F26B 3/097] using a magnetic field to stabilise the fluidised bed -- This subclass relates to drying solid materials with heat while using a magnetic field to stabilise the fluidised bed, improving heat transfer uniformity and preventing particle agglomeration.
[F26B 3/10] the gas or vapour carrying the materials or objects to be dried with it -- This subclass covers the use of a carrier gas or vapour to convey solid materials or objects through a drying zone where heat removes moisture. It includes the selection, flow control and handling of the gas or vapour that transports the items during the drying process.
[F26B 3/12] in the form of a spray -- F26B 3/12 covers drying of solid materials or objects using heat when the material is applied as a spray. The classification includes processes that atomize the material and dry the droplets in-flight or on a surface.
[F26B 3/14] the materials or objects to be dried being moved by gravity -- F26B 3/14 deals with drying solid materials or objects that are transported through the dryer by gravity rather than by mechanical means. It applies to heat-based drying processes where items move on inclined surfaces, fall through a chamber, or slide under their own weight, distinguishing these arrangements from conveyors or other active handling systems.
[F26B 3/16] in a counter-flow of the gas or vapour -- F26B 3/16 relates to drying solid materials or objects with heat where the drying gas or vapour moves in a counter-flow relative to the material being dried, allowing the fresh hot gas to contact the wettest part of the product and the cooler, saturated gas to encounter the drier portion, thereby improving heat and mass transfer efficiency.
[F26B 3/18] by conduction, i.e. the heat is conveyed from the heat source, e.g. gas flame, to the materials or objects to be dried by direct contact -- The subgroup F26B 3/18 covers drying of solid materials or objects where heat is transferred by direct contact from a heat source, such as a gas flame, to the items being dried, i.e. by conduction. It applies to processes that remove liquid by heating the material itself through conductive heat flow.
[F26B 3/20] the heat source being a heated surface -- F26B 3/20 covers drying of solid objects where heat is supplied directly by a heated surface that contacts or is in close proximity to the material, such as conveyor belts, plates, drums or rollers that transfer thermal energy to remove moisture. Typical applications include food, paper and mineral processing where moisture is removed by conduction from the heated surface.
[F26B 3/205] the materials to be dried covering or being mixed with heated inert particles which may be recycled -- A method for drying solid materials in which the material to be dried is covered by or mixed with heated inert particles that transfer heat, and the particles may be recovered and reused. This approach enhances heat-transfer efficiency and can lower overall energy consumption.
[F26B 3/22] the heat source and the materials or objects to be dried being in relative motion, e.g. of vibration -- F26B 3/22 relates to drying solid materials or objects by heat while the heat source and the items are in relative motion, for example through vibration, rotation or oscillation. The motion improves heat transfer and can increase drying efficiency.
[F26B 3/225] the materials or objects to be dried being immersed in a fluidised bed of heated particles  for webs  F26B13/106 -- F26B 3/225 relates to drying solid materials or objects by immersing them in a fluidised bed of heated particles, a method commonly applied to continuous webs such as textiles, paper or similar substrates.
[F26B 3/24] the movement being rotation -- This subclass concerns drying solid materials by heating while the material is moved through a rotating motion, such as in rotating drums, cylinders or other rotary devices that convey the workpiece and promote uniform heat exposure and moisture removal.
[F26B 3/26] the movement being performed by gravity -- F26B 3/26 pertains to drying solid materials or objects by heat where the motion of the material is provided by gravity, for example letting objects fall through a heated chamber. The code falls under mechanical engineering, drying, and drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid.
[F26B 3/28] by radiation, e.g. from the sun -- F26B 3/28 designates drying of solid materials or objects by applying heat in the form of radiation, such as sunlight, rather than by direct contact or convection. The process relies on electromagnetic energy to evaporate moisture from the material.
[F26B 3/283] in combination with convection -- The subclass F26B 3/283 covers drying of solid materials or objects by heat where the process is combined with convection, meaning that heated air or gases are circulated over the material to enhance moisture removal. It applies to equipment such as ovens, kilns or dryers that employ forced or natural airflow together with thermal energy.
[F26B 3/286] by solar radiation -- The F26B 3/286 subclass covers drying of solid materials or objects by applying heat supplied from solar radiation. It falls under the broader category of mechanical-engineering drying processes that remove liquid from solids using thermal energy. This entry specifies that the heat source is sunlight rather than conventional furnace or other artificial means.
[F26B 3/30] from infrared-emitting elements -- F26B 3/30 covers drying of solid materials using heat applied through infrared-emitting elements, where the infrared radiation directly transfers energy to the material to evaporate moisture. This subclass falls within the broader category of drying solid materials by heat.
[F26B 3/305] the infrared radiation being generated by combustion or combustion gases -- The CPC subclass F26B 3/305 belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for drying solid materials or objects by heat. It covers drying processes that use infrared radiation produced directly by combustion or by the gases released during combustion. This method applies the radiated heat to remove liquid from the material being dried.
[F26B 3/32] by development of heat within the materials or objects to be dried -- F26B 3/32 covers drying solid materials or objects through the generation of heat within the material itself, rather than applying external heat sources. This internal heating can be achieved by chemical reactions, electrical resistance, or other means that produce heat directly inside the material to remove moisture. It is a subcategory of drying by heat in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F26B 3/34] by using electrical effects -- F26B 3/34 covers drying solid materials or objects by heat where the heat is generated through electrical means, such as resistive heating, induction or electric arcs. It includes processes that use electrical energy to raise temperature and remove moisture.
[F26B 3/343] in combination with convection -- F26B 3/343 relates to drying solid materials or objects by applying heat together with convection currents to enhance liquid removal. It falls under the mechanical-engineering branch for drying processes that use heat-assisted convection.
[F26B 3/347] Electromagnetic heating, e.g. induction heating or heating using microwave energy -- This subclass concerns drying solid materials by means of electromagnetic energy, specifically techniques that generate heat through induction or microwave radiation. The methods apply heat directly to the workpiece or its immediate environment, enabling efficient removal of moisture.
[F26B 3/353] Resistance heating -- F26B 3/353 concerns resistance heating, a process in which electrical current flows through a resistive element to produce heat that is applied to solid materials or objects for drying. The method is employed in ovens, kilns and similar equipment to remove moisture by controlled thermal energy.
[F26B 3/36] by using mechanical effects, e.g. by friction -- F26B 3/36 pertains to drying solid materials or objects by applying mechanical effects, such as friction, to aid the removal of liquid while heat is also used. It is a sub-category of heat-based drying of solids within the mechanical engineering field.
[F26B 5/00] Drying solid materials or objects by processes not involving the application of heat -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drying solid materials or objects by processes applying a primary drying principle not involving heat.  Direct heating, e.g. radiation, or indirect heating, e.g. convection, of the drying good as measure to compensate cooling effects through evaporation are not considered to be a primary drying principle.
[F26B 5/005] by dipping them into or mixing them with a chemical liquid, e.g. organic; chemical, e.g. organic, dewatering aids  F26B3/005  takes precedence; using chemical vapours or gases  F26B21/40 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  by dipping them into or mixing them with a chemical liquid, e.g. organic; chemical, e.g. organic, dewatering aids  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Applying a combustible liquid onto the material, the liquid being burnt off subsequently -> F26B3/005 - Condensing vapour other than steam onto the surface of the material to be dried -> F26B21/471
[F26B 5/02] by using ultrasonic vibrations -- F26B 5/02 covers methods for drying solid materials without applying heat by employing ultrasonic vibrations. The technique uses high-frequency sound waves to create cavitation and micro-streaming that expel liquid from the material, enabling efficient moisture removal in mechanical engineering applications.
[F26B 5/04] by evaporation or sublimation of moisture under reduced pressure, e.g. in a vacuum -- This subclass covers methods for drying solid materials that use evaporation or sublimation of moisture under reduced pressure, such as vacuum drying. It falls under mechanical engineering, drying solid materials without applying heat.
[F26B 5/041] for drying flowable materials, e.g. suspensions, bulk goods, in a continuous operation, e.g. with locks or other air tight arrangements for charging/discharging -- The CPC subclass F26B 5/041 covers apparatus or methods for drying flowable solid materials such as suspensions or bulk goods in a continuous operation, using airtight charging and discharging zones like locks rather than applying heat. It is placed under mechanical engineering, drying solid materials or objects by non-thermal processes.
[F26B 5/042] for drying articles or discrete batches of material in a continuous or semi-continuous operation, e.g. with locks or other air tight arrangements for charging/discharging -- F26B 5/042 concerns drying solid articles or discrete batches in a continuous or semi-continuous system using sealed or lockable chambers for loading and unloading, without applying heat. The arrangement enables airtight charging and discharging to remove moisture by air flow or reduced pressure rather than thermal energy.
[F26B 5/044] for drying materials in a batch operation in an enclosure having a plurality of shelves which may be heated  F26B5/045  takes precedence -- F26B 5/044 covers batch drying of solid materials or objects inside an enclosure that contains multiple shelves, where the shelves can be heated to aid moisture removal. The method does not rely on direct heat application to the material itself, and the later subclass F26B 5/045 supersedes it.
[F26B 5/045] for drying thin, flat articles in a batch operation, e.g. leather, rugs, gels -- F26B 5/045 covers batch drying of thin, flat articles-such as leather, rugs or gels-by techniques that do not rely on direct heating, typically using airflow, vacuum, or other mechanical means to remove moisture from the material's surface. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering for drying solid materials without applying heat.
[F26B 5/047] for continuous drying of material in long length, e.g. webs -- F26B 5/047 covers continuous drying of solid material presented in long lengths, such as webs, using processes that remove liquid without applying heat. It is classified under mechanical engineering for drying solid items by non-thermal methods.
[F26B 5/048] in combination with heat developed by electro-magnetic means, e.g. microwave energy -- F26B 5/048 covers drying of solid materials where the primary method excludes direct heating, but includes supplemental heat generated by electromagnetic sources such as microwave energy, typically applied in combination with other drying mechanisms.
[F26B 5/06] the process involving freezing -- This subgroup covers drying solid objects by freezing, where moisture is removed by converting it to ice and then sublimating or melting under reduced pressure rather than applying heat. It falls under mechanical engineering, drying solid materials by non-thermal means.
[F26B 5/065] the product to be freeze-dried being sprayed, dispersed or pulverised -- This subclass deals with freeze-drying processes in which the material to be dried is first sprayed, dispersed or pulverised into small particles before sublimation removes moisture without direct heating. It falls under mechanical-engineering drying of solid objects by non-thermal methods.
[F26B 5/08] by centrifugal treatment -- F26B 5/08 covers drying solid materials or objects by centrifugal treatment, using rotational or centrifugal forces to extract liquid without applying heat. The technique is applied in mechanical engineering for processes such as spinning, centrifugation, or rotary drums to achieve moisture removal.
[F26B 5/10] the process involving freezing -- F26B 5/10 covers drying solid materials or objects by freezing, where water is turned into ice and then removed, typically by sublimation or melting, without applying external heat. This non-thermal method is used for heat-sensitive items, preserving structure and properties while extracting moisture.
[F26B 5/12] by suction -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for drying material of long length with progressive movement by applying suction -> F26B13/30 or F26B13/16
[F26B 5/14] by applying pressure, e.g. wringing -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for drying material of long length with progressive movement by applying pressure, brushing or wiping -> F26B13/28
[F26B 5/16] by contact with sorbent bodies, e.g. absorbent mould -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for drying material of long length with progressive movement by employing sorbent surfaces -> F26B13/26
[F26B 7/00] Drying solid materials or objects by processes using a combination of processes not covered by a single one of groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drying solid materials or objects by processes using a synergy creating combination, opposed to a simple agglomeration, of a heat applying "hot" process and a "cold" process.
[F26B 7/002] using an electric field and heat -- F26B 7/002 relates to drying solid materials or objects by applying heat together with an electric field, a combined method that does not belong to any single process group. The approach uses the electric field to enhance moisture removal while heat supplies thermal energy, improving drying efficiency for solid products.
[F26B 7/005] using admixture with sorbent materials and heat, e.g. generated by the mixture -- The subclass F26B 7/005 covers drying of solid materials or objects by employing a mixture that contains sorbent additives which absorb moisture while the mixture itself generates heat, thereby combining sorption and thermal drying in a single process not covered by any individual group.
[F26B 7/007] centrifugal fluidised beds -- F26B 7/007 designates centrifugal fluidised-bed apparatuses used to dry solid materials by employing a combination of drying actions, such as heat, air flow, or vacuum, applied simultaneously within a rotating drum that creates a fluidised layer of the product. This technology falls under mechanical engineering of drying solid objects by multiple-process methods.
[F26B 9/00] Machines or apparatus for drying -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or apparatus for drying "large" objects at rest.  Machines or apparatus for drying solid materials or "small" objects, e.g. bulk materials at rest or with local agitation, e.g. fixed or moving "small" stirrer in a "large" silo.
[F26B 9/003] Small self-contained devices, e.g. portable -- F26B 9/003 covers compact, self-contained drying equipment such as portable units that can be moved to the point of use. The classification applies to machines that dry solid objects by removing liquid without needing external infrastructure.
[F26B 9/006] Removable covering devices, e.g. pliable or flexible -- F26B 9/006 covers removable covering devices, such as pliable or flexible sheets, used in drying machines or apparatus that remove liquid from solid materials or objects. These coverings can be attached, detached, or moved to protect, contain, or facilitate the drying process.
[F26B 9/02] in buildings -- F26B 9/02 relates to drying machines or apparatus installed in buildings for removing moisture from solid materials or objects, including building ventilation systems, drying rooms, and dehumidifiers used in construction, maintenance or indoor processing.
[F26B 9/04] in presses or clamping devices -- F26B 9/04 covers drying apparatus that are built into presses or clamping devices, allowing moisture to be removed from solid workpieces while they are being compressed or held. The subclass includes heating elements, hot-air, steam or other drying means integrated with the press or clamp to accelerate drying during the machining or forming operation.
[F26B 9/06] in stationary drums or chambers -- F26B 9/06 covers drying machines in which solid material is placed in a fixed drum or chamber that remains stationary while heat, airflow or other drying action is applied. The subclass includes equipment that removes moisture from bulk solids without moving the container itself, such as static drum or cabinet dryers.
[F26B 9/063] for drying granular material in bulk, e.g. grain bins or silos with false floor  shallow layer rotary sweep drying bins  F26B9/10 ; perforated wall silos with drying air channels in the stack  F26B9/103 -- F26B 9/063 covers machines or apparatus for drying granular bulk materials such as grain in bins or silos, using a false floor with a shallow rotary sweep or perforated walls that direct drying air through the material stack.
[F26B 9/066] the products to be dried being disposed on one or more containers, which may have at least partly gas-previous walls, e.g. trays or shelves in a stack  F26B9/003  takes precedence; in combination with duct systems  F26B21/00 -- F26B 9/066 covers drying machines where the material to be dried is placed on containers such as trays or shelves that may have partially gas-tight walls, often arranged in stacked rows. It falls under the broader class for drying solid materials and follows the more general rule F26B 9/003, and may be combined with duct-system features defined in F26B 21/00.
[F26B 9/08] including agitating devices -- F26B 9/08 refers to machines or apparatus for drying solid materials that incorporate an agitating action, such as tumblers, rotary drums, vibratory units or similar devices that enhance heat transfer during drying. These devices are used in industrial processes to remove liquid from objects by moving them while heat is applied.
[F26B 9/082] mechanically agitating or recirculating the material being dried -- The code F26B 9/082 relates to machines or apparatus that dry solid materials or objects by mechanically agitating or recirculating the material during the drying process, ensuring more uniform moisture removal. It is situated within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for drying solid materials by removing liquid.
[F26B 9/085] moving the material in a substantially vertical sense using conveyors or agitators, e.g. screws or augers with vertical axis, which are positioned inside the drying enclosure -- The code F26B 9/085 covers machines that transport solid material vertically inside a drying enclosure using conveyors, agitators, screws or augers with a vertical axis. These devices move the material up or down within the chamber to enhance heat exposure and drying efficiency.
[F26B 9/087] the recirculation path being positioned outside the drying enclosure -- This subclass deals with machines or apparatus for drying solid materials in which the recirculation path for the drying medium is arranged outside the drying enclosure. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under mechanical engineering → drying → drying solid materials or objects by removing liquid therefrom → machines or apparatus for drying.
[F26B 9/10] in the open air -- F26B 9/10 covers drying machines that operate in the open air, using ambient conditions to remove moisture from solid objects without enclosed heating or forced airflow. It includes devices that rely on natural convection, wind or solar heating to dry materials.
[F26B 9/103] using fixed or removable drying air channels placed in the stack, e.g. horizontally or vertically -- F26B 9/103 relates to drying machines that use fixed or removable air channels positioned within the stack of material-such as horizontal or vertical ducts-to direct drying air through solid items for efficient moisture removal.
[F26B 9/106] the channels to be inserted into the stack, e.g. after its formation -- F26B 9/106 covers drying apparatus in which channels are added to a stack of drying media after the stack has been formed. The channels provide pathways for airflow or liquid removal, improving efficiency of solid material drying.
[F27] FURNACES -- F27 covers furnaces and related heating apparatus used in mechanical engineering for processing materials, including industrial furnaces, kilns, melting pots and heat-treatment ovens. The class includes designs for generating, controlling and distributing heat, as well as auxiliary systems such as burners, refractory linings and temperature-regulation devices.
[F27B] FURNACES, KILNS, OVENS OR RETORTS IN GENERAL -- F27B relates to general designs and principles of furnaces, kilns, ovens and retorts used in mechanical engineering. It addresses their construction, heating methods and functional features applicable across a range of industrial processes.
[F27B 1/00] Shaft or like vertical or substantially vertical furnaces -- F27B 1/00 covers furnaces that are essentially vertical shafts or similar configurations, where the workpiece moves up or down through a heated zone. These designs are used for heat-treatment, melting, sintering or other processing where gravity assists material flow, and they may include shaft-type kilns, retorts or tunnel-like ovens.
[F27B 1/005] wherein no smelting of the charge occurs, e.g. calcining or sintering furnaces -- F27B 1/005 designates shaft or essentially vertical furnaces that operate without smelting the charge, covering equipment used for calcining, sintering or similar heat-treatment processes. It falls within the CPC hierarchy of Mechanical Engineering → Furnaces → General furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts.
[F27B 1/02] with two or more shafts or chambers, e.g. multi-storey -- F27B 1/02 covers furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that contain two or more vertical shafts or chambers, typically arranged in a multi-storey configuration, allowing separate heating zones or stages within a single installation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the group for shaft-type vertical furnaces.
[F27B 1/025] with fore-hearth -- F27B 1/025 covers vertical or substantially vertical furnaces that incorporate a fore-hearth, a recessed chamber at the front bottom of the furnace used for loading, unloading or pre-heating material before it enters the main heating zone. This subcategory falls under the broader group of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts in general within mechanical engineering.
[F27B 1/04] Combinations or arrangements of shafts -- The subclass covers designs in which two or more vertical or substantially vertical furnace shafts are combined or arranged together, such as paired, intersecting, or grouped shaft configurations. It includes structural or functional layouts that integrate multiple shafts within a single furnace to improve heat distribution, material flow or processing efficiency.
[F27B 1/06] of other than up-draught type -- F27B 1/06 designates vertical or substantially vertical furnaces, kilns or ovens that operate with a flow of gases other than the up-draught arrangement, such as down-draught or cross-draught configurations. These devices are used for heating, melting, sintering or similar processes where the combustion gases do not rise naturally through the vessel.
[F27B 1/08] heated otherwise than by solid fuel mixed with charge -- This subclass covers shaft or vertical furnaces where the heating source is not solid fuel mixed with the material being processed. It includes furnaces using gases, liquids, electricity, radiation or other non-solid fuel methods to raise temperature, regardless of furnace shape. It is part of the mechanical engineering sequence for furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts.
[F27B 1/10] Details, accessories or equipment specially adapted for furnaces of these types -- The subclass covers specialised components, fittings and auxiliary devices that are designed specifically for vertical or essentially vertical furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It includes loading mechanisms, temperature-control units, refractory linings, support structures and other accessories that enhance the operation or safety of these types of furnaces.
[F27B 11/00] Bell-type furnaces -- F27B 11/00 covers bell-type furnaces, a class of heat-treating equipment where the workpiece is placed on a stationary refractory floor and a hinged, bell-shaped lid is lowered to enclose the chamber during heating. These furnaces are used for processes such as annealing, tempering, or sintering where precise temperature control and a sealed atmosphere are required.
[F27B 1/12] Shells or casings -- The F27B 1/12 subgroup relates to the shells or casings that form the outer enclosure of shaft-type or other substantially vertical furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It covers the design, construction, material selection and supporting structures of the outer vessel that contains the heating chamber and protects the internal working space.
[F27B 1/14] Arrangements of linings -- The F27B 1/14 subclass concerns the design and layout of internal linings for shaft-type or essentially vertical furnaces, kilns, ovens and retorts. It covers the positioning, segmentation, and configuration of refractory or protective layers that line the furnace chamber, including partitions, buckets, and other arrangements intended to control heat flow, protect the furnace structure and facilitate maintenance.
[F27B 1/16] Arrangements of tuyeres -- F27B 1/16 covers the design and positioning of tuyeres - air or fuel nozzles - within vertical shaft furnaces, kilns or ovens. It includes configurations that control the flow of combustion gases, improve heat distribution, or facilitate material handling in such furnaces.
[F27B 1/18] Arrangements of dust collectors -- F27B 1/18 covers the configuration and placement of dust-collector systems within shaft-type or otherwise vertical furnaces. It addresses how cyclones, bag filters, electrostatic precipitators or similar particulate-removal equipment are integrated into the furnace structure to capture emissions.
[F27B 1/20] Arrangements of devices for charging -- F27B 1/20 defines the design and layout of charging devices for vertical or substantially vertical furnaces, kilns, ovens and retorts. It covers the arrangement of feed hoppers, conveyors, loading arms and related mechanisms that introduce material into the furnace chamber.
[F27B 1/21] Arrangements of devices for discharging -- F27B 1/21 covers the layout and integration of components that remove material or products from shaft-type or comparable vertical furnaces. It includes designs of doors, hatches, conveyors, tilting mechanisms and related control systems that enable safe and efficient discharge. The code focuses on how these devices are arranged relative to each other and to the furnace structure.
[F27B 1/22] Arrangements of heat-exchange apparatus -- The F27B 1/22 subclass covers the design and layout of heat-exchange equipment within vertical or substantially vertical furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It includes any configuration of tubes, plates, coils or other exchangers that are integrated into the furnace structure to transfer heat between the combustion zone and the workpiece or process material.
[F27B 1/24] Cooling arrangements -- F27B 1/24 relates to the cooling arrangements employed in vertically oriented furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It covers devices, systems and methods for removing heat from the furnace chamber, such as cooling jackets, air or water circulation, and other means to lower temperature after the heating phase. The classification is used for patents describing how the furnace is cooled to protect materials or improve efficiency.
[F27B 1/26] Arrangements of controlling devices -- The code F27B 1/26 covers the layout and integration of control devices in vertical shaft or similar furnaces, including how temperature, pressure, atmosphere or feed are monitored and regulated. It addresses the positioning, interconnection and coordination of thermostats, sensors, actuators and control panels that manage the furnace's operating conditions.
[F27B 1/28] Arrangements of monitoring devices, of indicators, of alarm devices -- The code F27B 1/28 covers the design and layout of monitoring instruments, visual or audible indicators, and alarm systems used in shaft-type or other substantially vertical furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It addresses how these devices are positioned and integrated to detect temperature, pressure, gas composition or other process conditions and to signal abnormal operating states.
[F27B 13/00] Furnaces with both stationary charge and progression of heating, e.g. of ring type or of the type in which a segmental kiln moves over a stationary charge -- F27B 13/00 covers furnace designs in which the material to be heated remains stationary while the heating zone moves around it, such as ring-type furnaces or configurations where a segmental kiln travels over a fixed charge. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, specifically furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts in general. It addresses the mechanics of progressive heating with a stationary load.
[F27B 13/02] of multiple-chamber type with permanent partitions -- F27B 13/02 covers multiple-chamber furnaces that have permanent partitions separating each chamber, where the material to be processed remains stationary while heat moves through the chambers, typical of ring-type or segmental kilns that advance heating without moving the charge.
[F27B 13/04] of single-chamber type with temporary partitions -- F27B 13/04 denotes single-chamber furnaces that incorporate a temporary partition, allowing a stationary charge while the heating zone progresses; examples include ring-type or segmental kilns where a moving heating segment passes over a fixed material bed.
[F27B 13/06] Details, accessories or equipment specially adapted for furnaces of this type -- The subclass concerns components, accessories or specialized equipment designed for furnaces that combine a stationary charge with a moving heating zone, such as ring-type or segmental kilns where the kiln travels over a fixed material. It covers any detail or device specially adapted to this particular furnace configuration.
[F27B 13/08] Casings -- F27B 13/08 designates the casings that enclose furnaces or kilns in which a stationary charge is heated by a moving heating zone, such as ring-type kilns or segmental kilns that travel over a fixed charge. The subclass covers the outer shell, liner or protective housing that contains the process atmosphere and supports the thermal cycle.
[F27B 13/10] Arrangements of linings -- F27B 13/10 covers the design and configuration of interior linings in furnaces where the material remains stationary while the heating zone moves, such as ring-type kilns or segmental kilns that travel over a fixed charge. It includes methods for arranging refractory, insulating or protective layers to optimize heat transfer, durability and thermal stress management.
[F27B 13/12] Arrangements of heating devices -- F27B 13/12 covers the design and layout of heating devices used in furnaces that combine a fixed material charge with a moving heating zone, such as ring-type kilns or segmental kilns where a rotating segment passes over a stationary charge. The subclass addresses configurations of burners, radiant panels, induction coils or other heat sources within these particular furnace types.
[F27B 13/14] Arrangement of controlling, monitoring, alarm or like devices -- This class relates to the layout and integration of control, monitoring or alarm equipment in furnaces that have a fixed charge while the heating zone moves, such as ring furnaces or segmental kilns that travel over a stationary material bed. It covers how sensors, controllers, safety alarms and display devices are positioned and interconnected to manage temperature, flow and process parameters.
[F27B 14/00] Crucible or pot furnaces -- Crucible or pot furnaces are batch-type heating devices in which a removable container, the crucible or pot, holds the material to be processed, allowing direct contact with the heat source. They are used for melting, heat treating or sintering metals and other materials, typically operating at high temperatures and often removable for easy loading and unloading.
[F27B 14/02] with tilting or rocking arrangements -- Crucible or pot furnaces equipped with mechanisms that tilt or rock the furnace body to discharge molten metal, slag or other material. The arrangement allows the vessel to be moved from a horizontal to a sloped position, facilitating emptying and cleaning without dismantling the furnace. This design is used in steelmaking, foundry and heat-treatment operations.
[F27B 14/04] adapted for treating the charge in vacuum or special atmosphere -- F27B 14/04 covers crucible or pot furnaces that are specially adapted to treat the charge in a vacuum or in a controlled, non-air atmosphere, enabling processes such as melting, sintering or heat treatment where oxidation or contamination must be avoided. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, and addresses equipment designed for vacuum or inert-gas operation.
[F27B 14/06] heated electrically, e.g. induction crucible furnaces with or without any other source of heat -- F27B 14/06 refers to crucible or pot furnaces that are heated by electricity, typically using induction, and may be combined with other heat sources. These devices belong to the mechanical-engineering group for furnaces, kilns and ovens and are designed for precise thermal processing of materials in a contained crucible.
[F27B 14/061] Induction furnaces -- Induction furnaces are crucible or pot type heating devices that melt metal by generating alternating magnetic fields, which induce electric currents directly in the charge. The resulting rapid, contact-less heating provides precise temperature control and high energy efficiency, making them common in steelmaking, foundry and alloy production.
[F27B 14/063] Skull melting type -- Skull melting type furnaces are crucible or pot furnaces in which a solidified shell of the material being melted forms a protective "skull" around the liquid core, providing thermal insulation and preventing chemical interaction with the crucible walls, and are commonly used for high-temperature melting of reactive metals such as titanium and zirconium.
[F27B 14/065] Channel type -- The channel type refers to crucible or pot furnaces in which the heating zone is formed as a passage or channel through which the work-piece or material moves, allowing continuous heating and processing. Such furnaces are used for melting, heat-treating or sintering operations where a steady flow of material is required.
[F27B 14/08] Details specially adapted for crucible or pot furnaces -- F27B 14/08 covers technical details that are uniquely adapted for crucible or pot furnaces, including specialized supports, lids, loading mechanisms, heating elements and insulation arrangements that improve operation, safety or efficiency of these closed-vessel heating devices. It is a subcategory within mechanical engineering for furnace designs where the workpiece is contained in a removable crucible or pot.
[F27B 14/0806] Charging or discharging devices -- This CPC subclass covers devices that introduce material into or remove material from a crucible or pot furnace, such as feeders, ladles, hoppers, augers, valves or other mechanisms used for charging the furnace with charge or discharging molten or solid material.
[F27B 14/10] Crucibles -- F27B 14/10 designates crucibles - the containers or pots that hold material during heating in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It covers their construction, materials, shapes and features that enable high-temperature processing, including refractory lining and sealing arrangements. This subclass groups patents concerning the design and use of such heat-resistant vessels within mechanical engineering.
[F27B 14/12] Covers therefor -- The subclass F27B 14/12 relates to protective and sealing covers for crucible or pot furnaces, including removable lids, doors or shielding elements that close the furnace cavity during operation, maintenance or standby.
[F27B 14/14] Arrangements of heating devices -- F27B 14/14 refers to the specific layout and integration of heating devices within crucible or pot furnaces, covering the positioning, configuration and interconnection of burners, heaters, and related components to achieve uniform temperature control and efficient heat transfer in these types of industrial ovens.
[F27B 14/143] Heating of the crucible by convection of combustion gases -- The CPC subclass F27B 14/143 covers methods for heating a crucible or pot furnace by directing combustion gases past the crucible so that heat is transferred through convection. It relates to the design and operation of furnaces, kilns and ovens where the hot gases flow around the vessel to raise its temperature.
[F27B 14/20] Arrangement of controlling, monitoring, alarm or like devices -- F27B 14/20 covers the layout and integration of control, monitoring and alarm systems for crucible or pot-type furnaces, kilns and ovens. It includes the positioning of sensors, thermostats, safety interlocks and display devices that manage temperature, pressure or other operating parameters within such heating equipment.
[F27B 15/00] Fluidised-bed furnaces -- Fluidised-bed furnaces are industrial heating units that suspend solid particles in an upward flow of gas, creating a fluid-like medium that ensures uniform temperature distribution and rapid heat transfer. They are employed for processes such as calcination, pyrolysis, and waste treatment, offering efficient energy use and precise control of reaction conditions.
[F27B 15/003] Cyclones or chain of cyclones -- F27B 15/003 refers to the use of cyclones or a chain of cyclones within fluidised-bed furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It covers designs in which centrifugal separators are integrated into the furnace to remove particulate matter or to control gas flow, improving combustion efficiency and reducing emissions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the broader category of furnaces.
[F27B 15/006] Equipment for treating dispersed material falling under gravity with ascending gases -- The entry covers apparatus used in fluidised-bed furnaces where solid particles fall by gravity while an upward gas stream fluidises and transports them, including hoppers, distributors and related handling devices. It focuses on equipment that ensures uniform mixing and conveyance of dispersed material within the furnace.
[F27B 15/02] Details, accessories or equipment specially adapted for furnaces of these types -- This subclass pertains to the specific details, accessories and equipment that are specially adapted for fluidised-bed furnaces, covering components such as loading mechanisms, discharge devices, control and monitoring systems, sealing arrangements and other auxiliary parts that enhance the operation, safety or maintenance of these furnaces.
[F27B 15/04] Casings -- Casings are the structural enclosures that contain the fluidised-bed chamber of a furnace, supporting the bed material and ensuring containment of gases, heat and particulate flow while withstanding thermal stress and corrosion.
[F27B 15/06] Arrangements of linings -- F27B 15/06 covers the design and configuration of refractory linings in fluidised-bed furnaces, kilns, ovens and retorts. It includes the arrangement of removable or modular lining sections, insulated panels and protective inserts intended to shield the vessel and optimise heat distribution and material flow.
[F27B 15/08] Arrangements of devices for charging -- The subclass F27B 15/08 covers the arrangement of devices that charge material into fluidised-bed furnaces, describing how feeders, conveyors, hoppers and other charging mechanisms are positioned and coordinated within the furnace system. It is a detailed sub-category under mechanical engineering, furnaces, kilns, ovens and retorts, specifically focusing on fluidised-bed furnace charging layouts.
[F27B 15/09] Arrangements of devices for discharging -- This CPC subclass F27B 15/09 covers arrangements of devices for discharging material from fluidised-bed furnaces, including conveyors, valves, taps and other mechanisms that remove solids, liquids or gases after processing. It addresses the spatial layout, integration and control of these discharge systems within the furnace structure.
[F27B 15/10] Arrangements of air or gas supply devices -- F27B 15/10 concerns the design and layout of air or gas supply devices for fluid-bed furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, encompassing ducts, nozzles, distributors and other arrangements that introduce and control the flow of gases into the fluidised bed.
[F27B 15/12] Arrangements of dust collectors -- F27B 15/12 covers the design and layout of dust collection systems used in fluidised-bed furnaces, kilns and similar heating apparatus. It includes positioning, mounting and integration of filters, cyclones, hoods and other collector components to control particulate emissions.
[F27B 15/14] Arrangements of heating devices -- F27B 15/14 concerns the layout and integration of heating elements inside fluidised-bed furnaces, which suspend solid particles in a flowing gas to enhance heat transfer. The code covers configurations such as the placement of burners, heat exchangers and auxiliary heaters that support furnace operation and temperature control.
[F27B 15/16] Arrangements of cooling devices -- It covers the design and layout of cooling systems integrated into fluidised-bed furnaces, including placement of heat exchangers, water sprays and other devices to control the temperature of the furnace interior and exhaust gases. These arrangements aim to improve thermal efficiency and protect refractory components.
[F27B 15/18] Arrangements of controlling devices -- The CPC entry F27B 15/18 covers the arrangement of control devices for fluidised-bed furnaces, including the layout of sensors, actuators, controllers and associated circuitry that regulate temperature, pressure, material flow and combustion within the furnace. It addresses how these components are integrated to achieve efficient, safe and automated operation.
[F27B 15/20] Arrangements of monitoring devices, of indicators, of alarm devices -- F27B 15/20 relates to the design of fluidised-bed furnaces and specifies how monitoring instruments, visual indicators and alarm systems are arranged within the equipment. It covers the placement, interconnection and integration of sensors and warning devices to ensure safe and efficient operation of these high-temperature processes.
[F27B 17/00] Furnaces of a kind not covered by any of groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Types of furnaces not covered by any preceding group:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Structural combinations of furnaces -> F27B19/02
[F27B 17/0008] Open field furnace for burning bricks -- F27B 17/0008 designates an open-field furnace used for burning bricks, where the firing chamber is not enclosed but exposed to the outdoors or a large open space, allowing direct heating of the brick stack without a confined retort or kiln structure.
[F27B 17/0016] Chamber type furnaces -- F27B 17/0016 designates chamber-type furnaces, where the material to be heated is placed in a sealed chamber isolated from the combustion zone, allowing controlled temperature environments for processes such as heat treatment, drying or sintering. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the broader category of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts not covered by other groups.
[F27B 17/0025] specially adapted for treating semiconductor wafers -- This subgroup contains furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that are specifically designed or modified for processing semiconductor wafers, such as heating, annealing or other thermal treatments required in wafer fabrication. It addresses the structural and functional adaptations needed to treat delicate wafer materials within a controlled furnace environment.
[F27B 17/0033] the floor of the furnaces consisting of the support carrying the charge, e.g. car type furnaces -- The CPC subclass F27B 17/0033 covers the floor of furnaces that serves as the support carrying the charge, such as in car-type furnaces. It is situated within mechanical engineering, furnaces, and the broader group of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts.
[F27B 17/0041] specially adapted for burning bricks or pottery  F27B17/0033  takes precedence -- F27B 17/0041 designates furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that are specially adapted for the firing of bricks or pottery, covering constructions and features not included in any other sub-group. It is a narrower subclass of F27B 17/0033, which has priority, and sits within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under furnaces and general furnace types.
[F27B 17/005] with cylindrical chambers -- F27B 17/005 covers furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts whose heating chamber is cylindrical in shape. The classification applies to equipment where the work space is a round tube or barrel, such as rotary kilns, tube furnaces and cylindrical retorts used in metallurgy, ceramics or heat-treatment processes.
[F27B 17/0058] with superposed cylindrical chambers -- F27B 17/0058 relates to furnaces that contain two or more cylindrical chambers arranged one above the other, each chamber sharing a common axis. The design enables sequential heating zones or staged processing of materials such as ceramics, metals or chemical precursors. It falls under the general group for furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts not covered by other specific groups.
[F27B 17/0066] arrangement of the charge, e.g. bricks -- F27B 17/0066 covers the design and positioning of the material loaded into a furnace, such as the layout of bricks or other charge substances, to control heat flow, efficiency and structural stability. This sub-group addresses the internal arrangement of the charge in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts not covered by other groups.
[F27B 17/0075] Heating devices therefor -- F27B 17/0075 designates heating devices used in furnaces that do not fall into any more specific CPC groups. It covers auxiliary burners, electric or gas heating elements, and other means of supplying heat to such general-purpose furnaces within the mechanical-engineering field.
[F27B 17/0083] with means for circulating the atmosphere -- F27B 17/0083 covers furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that are not classified elsewhere and that incorporate devices for circulating the internal atmosphere, such as fans, blowers or exhaust systems, to achieve uniform temperature distribution or a controlled atmosphere.
[F27B 17/02] specially designed for laboratory use -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Furnaces especially designed for laboratory use.  Furnaces for dental work-pieces
[F27B 17/025] for dental workpieces -- F27B 17/025 covers furnaces specifically designed for processing dental workpieces, such as crowns, bridges and other prosthetic components, where precise heating is required for sintering, glazing or heat-treating dental materials.
[F27B 19/00] Combinations of different kinds of furnaces that are not all covered by any single one of main groups -- F27B 19/00 relates to combinations of different kinds of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that cannot be classified under any single main group, covering hybrid or multi-type heating apparatuses used in mechanical engineering processes.
[F27B 19/02] combined in one structure -- The code F27B 19/02 covers a single unit that incorporates two or more different types of furnaces, such as kilns, ovens or retorts, within one structure, enabling the combined operation of various heating processes in a unified apparatus. This classification applies to integrated furnace arrangements not covered by a single main group.
[F27B 19/04] arranged for associated working -- F27B 19/04 designates configurations that combine two or more different types of furnaces-such as kilns, ovens or retorts-in a single installation where they operate together or share functions, without fitting entirely within any single main-group definition. The subclass covers arrangements that enable associated working, including shared heating, material flow or control between the combined units.
[F27B 2003/125] Hearths -- F27B 2003/125 pertains to hearths, the fire-resistant floor or base of a fireplace, stove or similar heating appliance, covering their shape, materials, insulation and mounting methods. It addresses features that contain, support or protect the surrounding floor from heat and combustion by-products.
[F27B 2003/165] Roofs -- F27B 2003/165 covers roofs of ventilating, heating or air-conditioning apparatus, including protective or sealing structures that enclose equipment such as ducts, boilers, condensers or similar units. The classification pertains to the design, material and mounting of such roofs to ensure weather protection and functional integration.
[F27B 2005/062] Cooling elements -- F27B 2005/062 covers cooling elements used in refrigeration, air-conditioning and heat-exchange systems, including heat-transfer surfaces, fins, evaporators, condensers and other components that facilitate removal of heat from a working medium. The classification applies to designs, structures and arrangements of such elements that improve thermal efficiency or control fluid flow. It also includes integrated cooling modules that combine multiple functions within a single device.
[F27B 2005/064] disposed in the furnace, around the chamber, e.g. coils -- The CPC entry F27B 2005/064 pertains to arrangements where heating elements or other devices are disposed in the furnace around the chamber, such as coils that radiate heat onto the workpiece. It addresses configurations that place heat sources directly surrounding the interior of a furnace to improve temperature uniformity or heating efficiency.
[F27B 2005/066] disposed around the fan -- The CPC subclass F27B 2005/066 covers arrangements where auxiliary elements such as housings, shrouds, ducts or protective structures are positioned surrounding a fan. It includes designs that guide airflow, provide structural support, reduce noise or protect the fan by placing components around the rotating impeller.
[F27B 2005/068] for external cooling -- F27B 2005/068 covers devices and methods that provide cooling from outside the heated object or space, typically using external heat exchangers, cooling jackets, or other apparatus that remove heat to the ambient environment. It is used in building climate control, industrial equipment and transport refrigeration where the cooling source is located externally.
[F27B 2005/143] Heating rods disposed in the chamber -- The CPC entry F27B 2005/143 covers heating rods that are positioned inside a chamber to provide heat for processes such as drying, curing, melting or annealing. It includes arrangements where the rods are fixed, suspended or otherwise arranged to transfer thermal energy directly to the chamber interior. The classification applies to devices in furnaces, ovens, reactors or similar equipment where internal heating elements are used.
[F27B 2005/146] the heating rods being in the tubes which conduct the heating gases -- This CPC subgroup covers heating devices where the heating elements are rods placed inside tubes that convey heating gases. The arrangement allows the rods to be directly exposed to the hot gas stream, improving heat transfer efficiency for furnaces, dryers or similar thermal processing equipment.
[F27B 2005/161] Gas inflow or outflow -- The CPC entry F27B 2005/161 pertains to devices and arrangements that introduce gas into or discharge gas from a space, encompassing ducts, inlets, outlets, vents and associated control elements used in ventilation, heating, cooling and pneumatic systems.
[F27B 2005/162] through closable or non-closable openings of the chamber walls -- F27B 2005/162 covers fans, blowers and similar rotating machines whose housings include openings in the chamber walls that can be either permanently open or fitted with closable closures such as doors, shutters or valves, providing access, service or flow control through these openings.
[F27B 2005/163] Controlled openings, e.g. orientable -- F27B 2005/163 classifies devices that incorporate controllable openings, such as orientable louvers, shutters, vents, or adjustable ducts, which can be positioned to regulate airflow, light, or sound. The entry covers mechanisms that enable deliberate setting of the opening angle or area, including manual, motor-driven, or pneumatic actuation. It is used for inventions where the opening can be varied to meet functional requirements.
[F27B 2005/164] Air supply through a set of tubes with openings -- A system that delivers air by means of a group of parallel tubes each provided with one or more openings, enabling distribution of the airflow across a defined area. The arrangement is used in ventilation, cooling, drying or pressurisation applications where uniform air supply is required. It covers the geometry of the tube bundle, the positioning of the openings and the method of directing the supplied air.
[F27B 2005/165] Controlled tubes, e.g. orientable or with closable openings -- The F27B 2005/165 subclass pertains to tubes whose internal flow or pressure is managed by mechanisms such as rotating or pivoting sections, hinged portions, or shutters that can be opened or closed on demand, enabling adjustable passage or sealing.
[F27B 2005/166] Means to circulate the atmosphere -- F27B 2005/166 classifies devices that generate or direct airflow for atmospheric circulation, including fans, blowers, ventilators and related mechanisms used in heating, cooling, ventilation or air-conditioning systems. The subclass covers structural elements, drive arrangements and control components that enable the movement of air within a defined space.
[F27B 2005/167] the atmosphere being recirculated through the treatment chamber by a turbine -- The entry covers air-handling systems that recirculate the surrounding atmosphere through a treatment chamber using a turbine to drive the flow. It includes designs where the turbine creates a closed-loop airflow for filtration, heating, cooling or contaminant removal before the air is returned to the space.
[F27B 2005/168] by more than one turbine -- The CPC subclass F27B 2005/168 refers to fluid-power or propulsion installations that employ more than one turbine, such as multi-turbine pumps, compressors, or turbine-driven generators arranged to work together or in parallel. It covers the mechanical configuration, coupling, and control of multiple turbines within a single system.
[F27B 2005/169] the atmosphere being continuously renewed by exterior means -- This subclass concerns devices or methods that continuously replace interior air with fresh outside air. It includes ventilation, air exchange and renewal systems that draw external atmosphere into a space, either naturally or by mechanical means, to maintain a constant supply of fresh air.
[F27B 2007/005] for the treatment of slurries or wet materials -- F27B 2007/005 covers devices, systems and methods for treating slurries, suspensions or other wet bulk materials, including dewatering, filtration, agitation, separation or extraction of components in mixers, reactors, treatment vessels or similar equipment. It addresses processes that improve handling, reduce moisture or recover valuable substances from such mixtures.
[F27B 2007/022] the drum having a non-uniform section along its length -- F27B 2007/022 classifies drums whose diameter or shape changes along the axis, such as tapered or stepped drums used in conveyors, rollers or processing equipment. It addresses designs where the non-uniform section serves to modify speed, tension or load distribution.
[F27B 2007/025] with different chambers, e.g. treatment zones -- F27B 2007/025 covers pneumatic conveying installations that incorporate separate chambers or treatment zones within the transport line, allowing distinct processing steps such as cleaning, drying, coating or separation to be performed on the conveyed material. The classification applies to devices where the material passes through one or more dedicated zones arranged in series or parallel before reaching its final destination.
[F27B 2007/027] with more than one drum -- The CPC subgroup F27B 2007/027 pertains to centrifugal pumps that incorporate more than one drum in their design, typically to provide staged pumping or higher flow capacity. It covers configurations where the multiple drums are arranged to act in series or parallel within the same pump assembly.
[F27B 2007/041] Longitudinal tubes -- The classification covers devices that contain one or more tubes running lengthwise through a mixing, separating or cleaning apparatus, used to convey material, heat, or gas along the axis of the machine. It applies to longitudinal tubes integrated in mixers, separators, soot blowers or gas-cleaning equipment.
[F27B 2007/043] the partition being a cylinder, coaxial to the rotary drum, defining two chambers -- The entry concerns a rotary-drum apparatus used in solid-waste or material processing that includes an internal cylindrical partition coaxial with the drum. This partition divides the drum interior into two separate chambers, allowing distinct processing zones or handling of different streams within the same rotating container.
[F27B 2007/045] the charge going in one direction in one chamber, then after a turn coming back in the other direction in the other chamber -- CPC F27B 2007/045 covers pumps that move a fluid charge forward in one chamber, then after a change of direction cause the charge to return in the opposite direction through a second chamber, typical of double-acting or reciprocating pump designs.
[F27B 2007/046] Radial partitions -- Radial partitions are internal structural elements placed radially within pump casings, compressors or fluid-handling devices to divide the flow area, reinforce the housing, or create separate chambers for fluid routing or pressure control.
[F27B 2007/048] defining an helical chamber -- This CPC subgroup concerns rotary positive-displacement pumps that incorporate a helical chamber as the fluid-transfer cavity. It covers the geometry, formation and functional aspects of the helical passage used to move liquid through the pump.
