This subclass falls under details or accessories common to more than one kind of furnace within the mechanical engineering domain.
[F27D 3/0033] charging of particulate material -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices for introducing particulate material such as powders, granules or solid feedstock into a furnace, kiln, oven or retort. It includes charging mechanisms like hoppers, conveyors, rotary valves and pneumatic feeds used across various furnace types.
[F27D 3/0084] Charging; Manipulation of SC or SC wafers -- F27D 3/0084 covers the charging and handling of solid charge material, including the manipulation of solid-charge wafers, within furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It deals with methods and devices for loading, moving, distributing or positioning solid fuel or material in such heating equipment.
[F27D 3/02] Skids or tracks for heavy objects -- This classification covers devices such as skids or track systems used to move or support heavy loads during the charging process of furnaces, kilns, ovens, or retorts. It includes portable or fixed support structures that facilitate handling of bulk material or equipment within the furnace chamber and applies across multiple furnace types.
[F27D 3/022] Skids -- Skids are portable support frames or platforms used to mount and transport furnace components such as burners, refractory modules or heat-treatment units, enabling the equipment to be moved or positioned within a furnace system. They provide a stable base and facilitate installation, maintenance and alignment across different furnace types.
[F27D 3/024] Details of skids, e.g. riders -- The subclass covers skids, riders and similar support devices used to move, position or level furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, focusing on designs that apply to more than one type of furnace. It details their construction, attachment methods and functional features.
[F27D 3/026] transport or conveyor rolls for furnaces; roller rails -- F27D 3/026 relates to transport or conveyor rolls used in furnaces, covering both the rolling elements and the rails that guide them. These components facilitate the movement of material through different types of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts.
[F27D 3/028] Roller rails or succession of small sized rollers -- F27D 3/028 designates roller rails or a succession of small rollers that guide and support material during the charging of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. These components are accessories used to feed bulk or granular loads into the heating chamber, ensuring smooth and controlled entry of the charge.
[F27D 3/04] Ram or pusher apparatus -- F27D 3/04 covers ram or pusher devices for introducing material into furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. These mechanisms are used to load bulk charge through a dedicated chamber or opening and may include hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical drives. The class is part of the accessories of furnaces group in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[F27D 3/06] Charging or discharging machines on travelling carriages -- F27D 3/06 covers machines that load or unload material onto travelling carriages used with different furnace types. The classification includes mechanisms that transport the charge into or out of the furnace while the carriage moves, applicable to multiple furnace designs.
[F27D 3/08] Screw feeders -- F27D 3/08 relates to screw feeders used as charging devices for furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It covers the design and operation of screw mechanisms that convey solid material into the heating chamber, applicable to any furnace type where such feeders are employed.
[F27D 3/10] Charging directly from hoppers or shoots -- This subclass deals with methods for feeding material straight into a furnace, kiln, oven or retort from a hopper, chute or similar feed device, without intermediate handling. It covers direct charging arrangements that can be used in multiple types of furnaces.
[F27D 3/12] Travelling or movable supports or containers for the charge -- F27D 3/12 classifies travelling or movable supports or containers used to hold the material being charged into furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that are common to multiple furnace types. It falls under the subclass for details or accessories of furnaces within mechanical engineering.
[F27D 3/123] Furnace cars -- F27D 3/123 - Furnace cars are mobile devices designed to transport, charge or discharge molten metal, slag or other materials into or out of a furnace, kiln, oven or retort. They form part of the charging equipment and may include rail-mounted or wheeled carriers that move between processing stations.
[F27D 3/14] Charging or discharging liquid or molten material -- This CPC subclass concerns methods and equipment for introducing or removing liquid or molten material in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, covering charging devices and related accessories used across various furnace types.
[F27D 3/145] Runners therefor -- This subclass concerns the runner systems that convey charge material into furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, covering designs that are used in more than one type of furnace. It includes channels, tubes or other conduits that transport the charge to the heating zone.
[F27D 3/15] Tapping equipment -- Tapping equipment comprises devices and systems used to withdraw molten material from furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, such as taps, tap holes, tapping rods and associated control mechanisms. It falls under charging accessories that are applicable across multiple types of high-temperature vessels.
[F27D 3/1509] Tapping equipment -- F27D 3/1509 covers tapping equipment used to withdraw molten material from furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, such as ladles, spouts, valves and related mechanisms. The classification applies to accessories that are common to multiple furnace types and deals with the design, operation and integration of these discharge devices.
[F27D 3/1518] Tapholes -- F27D 3/1518 covers tapholes, which are openings or ports in the walls of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that allow the controlled removal of molten metal, slag or gases during operation. The classification applies to designs, sealing mechanisms and accessories associated with such taps across different furnace types.
[F27D 3/1527] Taphole forming equipment, e.g. boring machines, piercing tools -- The CPC subclass F27D 3/1527 pertains to taphole forming equipment used to create the opening through which material is introduced into a furnace, including boring machines and piercing tools. These devices are classified as accessories common to several types of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts.
[F27D 3/1536] Devices for plugging tap holes, e.g. plugs stoppers -- the devices fixed permanently to the melt container are classified in F27D3/1509
[F27D 3/1545] Equipment for removing or retaining slag -- F27D 3/1545 pertains to equipment designed to remove or retain slag in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, usable across different furnace types. It is classified under the CPC subgroup for details or accessories of such heating equipment within the mechanical engineering section.
[F27D 3/1554] for removing the slag from the surface of the melt -- The F27D 3/1554 entry covers equipment or techniques used in furnaces to skim or extract slag that forms on the surface of a molten metal bath, falling under the "charging" subgroup of accessories common to multiple furnace types.
[F27D 3/1563] by the use of scrapers -- F27D 3/1563 covers charging methods for furnaces that employ scrapers to move solid material into the heating chamber. It pertains to accessories used in a variety of furnace types, including kilns, ovens and retorts, where scraping devices facilitate the introduction of charge material.
[F27D 3/1572] by suction -- The F27D 3/1572 entry defines charging of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts by suction, describing devices and methods that employ vacuum or negative pressure to draw bulk material into the heating chamber. This falls under the CPC group for furnace accessories that are applicable to multiple furnace types.
[F27D 3/1581] by the use of blowers -- The CPC subclass F27D 3/1581 covers furnace charging methods that employ blowers to transport or inject material into the furnace. It falls under mechanical engineering, furnaces, and details-or-accessories of furnaces, specifically for processes where a blower supplies the charge.
[F27D 3/159] for retaining slag during the pouring of the metal or retaining metal during the pouring of the slag -- The code covers devices installed on furnaces, kilns or ovens that keep slag in place while molten metal is poured, or conversely retain metal while slag is removed. These accessories are used in multiple furnace types to improve separation and control during the pouring process.
[F27D 3/16] Introducing a fluid jet or current into the charge -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Charging particulate material using a fluid carrier -> F27D3/18
[F27D 3/18] Charging particulate material using a fluid carrier -- This subclass deals with methods for introducing solid particles into a furnace, kiln, or oven by conveying them with a gas or liquid stream. It covers devices and processes that use a fluid medium to transport and feed the material into the heating zone, applicable to various furnace types.
[F27D 5/00] Supports, screens or the like for the charge within the furnace -- F27D 5/00 pertains to supports, screens or similar devices placed inside furnaces to hold the charge and promote even heating. It includes grates, trays, cradles or removable screens that prevent sagging and improve heat distribution.
[F27D 5/0006] Composite supporting structures -- F27D 5/0006 covers composite structures that support or screen the material being processed inside furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, such as layered or mixed-material trays, grids and similar charge-holding devices. These accessories are used in various furnace types to hold, distribute or protect the charge during heating.
[F27D 5/0012] Modules of the sagger or setter type; Supports built up from them -- This CPC subclass covers modular charge supports of the sagger or setter type used in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It includes supports constructed from individual sagger or setter modules that can be assembled to form a support framework for the material being processed.
[F27D 5/0018] Separating elements -- F27D 5/0018 refers to separating elements installed inside furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts to support, screen or otherwise divide the charge. It includes grates, perforated plates, screens, and similar structures that hold or separate material during heating and are applicable to multiple furnace types.
[F27D 5/0025] assembled to present a three-point support -- F27D 5/0025 covers assembled three-point supports designed to hold the charge within a furnace, kiln, oven or retort. These devices form part of the details or accessories common to multiple furnace types, providing a simple, stable support structure for the material being processed.
[F27D 5/0031] Treatment baskets for ceramic articles -- F27D 5/0031 covers treatment baskets used for handling ceramic articles inside furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. These baskets act as supports or screens that hold the charge during heating and are applicable to several types of furnace equipment. The code falls under mechanical engineering, furnaces, and accessories common to multiple furnace designs.
[F27D 5/0037] Supports specially adapted for semi-conductors -- F27D 5/0037 covers specially designed supports used inside furnaces for holding semiconductor materials during processing. These supports are a type of accessory that secures the charge and may include screens or similar devices adapted to the delicate handling requirements of semiconductor production. The code falls under the mechanical engineering group for furnace details that are common to multiple furnace types.
[F27D 5/0043] Supports specially adapted for dental prosthesis -- F27D 5/0043 relates to accessories for furnaces that are designed to hold dental prostheses during heat-treatment operations. The entry covers specially adapted supports, trays or screens that secure crowns, bridges or other prosthetic workpieces inside the furnace and can be used in various furnace types.
[F27D 5/005] Supports specially adapted for holding elongated articles in an upright position, e.g. sparking plugs -- The subclass F27D 5/005 covers supports installed in furnaces that are specially shaped to hold elongated items, such as spark plugs, in an upright position while the furnace operates. These devices keep the articles properly aligned with the charge, often using rods, brackets or screen-like fixtures.
[F27D 5/0056] Hangers -- Hangers are support elements designed to hold or suspend the charge inside a furnace, kiln, oven or retort, providing access or positioning for processing. They serve as accessories common to various furnace types, enabling efficient loading, unloading, or distribution of material within the heating chamber.
[F27D 5/0062] Shields for the charge -- The CPC code F27D 5/0062 covers shields for the charge used in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, indicating accessories that protect or support the material being processed. These devices are applicable to several types of furnace constructions and are classified under details or accessories common to more than one furnace kind.
[F27D 5/0068] Containers -- F27D 5/0068 refers to containers used inside furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts to hold, support or screen the charge being processed, encompassing any such accessory that can be employed across multiple furnace types.
[F27D 7/00] Forming, maintaining or circulating atmospheres in heating chambers -- F27D 7/00 covers devices or methods that create, sustain or move the internal atmosphere of heating chambers such as furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It includes systems for introducing gases, controlling temperature or pressure, and circulating the environment, and is applicable to accessories used in more than one type of furnace.
[F27D 7/02] Supplying steam, vapour, gases or liquids -- The F27D 7/02 subclass concerns equipment or systems that supply steam, vapor, gases or liquids to heating chambers in order to create, maintain or circulate the process atmosphere inside furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It applies to any type of furnace where such atmosphere-control devices are used.
[F27D 7/04] Circulating atmospheres by mechanical means -- The subclass covers mechanical systems that move, mix or recirculate gases, air or combustion products within the heating chamber of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It includes fans, blowers, ducts, conveyors and related devices used to create, maintain or control the atmosphere for temperature uniformity, combustion efficiency or specific gas composition, and applies to any furnace type that employs such mechanical circulation.
[F27D 7/06] Forming or maintaining special atmospheres or vacuum within heating chambers -- F27D 7/06 covers methods and devices for creating, sustaining or controlling special gases, inert or reactive atmospheres, or vacuum inside furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It pertains to the generation, introduction, removal or regulation of gases to achieve desired processing conditions across multiple furnace types.
[F27D 9/00] Cooling of furnaces or of charges therein -- The F27D 9/00 subclass covers devices, arrangements and methods used to lower the temperature of a furnace or the material being processed inside it. It includes cooling systems such as water-cooled walls, spray nozzles, air-blast or heat-exchanger installations that remove heat from the furnace structure or the charge during or after operation.
[F27D 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details or accessories not covered by any preceding group
[F27D 99/0001] Heating elements or systems -- The subclass covers heating elements or complete heating systems employed in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts when the element design is applicable to more than one type of furnace and is not classified elsewhere. It includes details and accessories related to such heating components.
[F27D 99/0003] with particulate fuel, e.g. aspects relating to the feeding -- This subclass addresses components and mechanisms that introduce particulate fuel into furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that are used across several furnace types. It includes feeding devices, distribution systems and related accessories for solid fuels such as coal, biomass or waste, when such fuel-handling features are not covered by other groups.
[F27D 99/0005] Injecting liquid fuel -- F27D 99/0005 covers devices for injecting liquid fuel into furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, focusing on details or accessories that are applicable to more than one type of furnace. These fuel-injection systems are treated as generic furnace accessories when the technology is not otherwise classified in more specific groups.
[F27D 99/0006] Electric heating elements or system -- F27D 99/0006 covers electric heating elements or complete heating systems that are employed in multiple kinds of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. The entry includes any electrically powered component designed to generate heat for these appliances, regardless of the specific furnace type, and excludes items already classified in more specific groups. It falls under the subclass for details or accessories of furnaces that are common across different furnace designs.
[F27D 99/0033] using burners -- F27D 99/0033 covers the use of burners as components or processes applicable to multiple furnace types. It includes burner placement, fuel supply, ignition and flame-control arrangements that are not specific to a single furnace design. The class collects generic burner-related features used across furnaces, kilns, ovens and retorts.
[F27D 99/0035] Heating indirectly through a radiant surface -- A furnace component or accessory that provides heat by transferring energy through a radiating surface rather than direct flame or contact, such as a heated wall, panel or tube that emits infrared radiation to warm the workpiece. It is placed in the residual group for accessories common to multiple furnace types not covered elsewhere.
[F27D 99/007] Partitions -- The F27D 99/007 entry covers partitions that are installed inside furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts and are not otherwise specified in more detailed groups. It applies to any type of furnace where such internal dividing or separating elements are used, providing a catch-all category for partitions that serve structural, thermal or flow-control functions across multiple furnace designs.
[F27D 99/0073] Seals -- F27D 99/0073 covers seals used in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that are applicable to multiple furnace types. It includes any sealing element that prevents leakage of gases, liquids or solids during heating processes and is not otherwise classified in more specific groups.
[F27D 99/0075] Gas curtain seals -- Gas curtain seals are systems that create a continuous flow of gas across openings in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, forming a protective barrier that limits heat loss and prevents ingress of contaminants while permitting material passage.
[F27D 99/0076] Furnace car seals, i.e. seals used in continuous furnaces or kilns for preventing gas or heat exchange between heating chamber and the area comprising driving means -- F27D 99/0076 covers furnace-car seals, the devices fitted in continuous furnaces or kilns to block the passage of gases and heat between the heating chamber and the surrounding drive mechanisms. It falls under the subclass for details or accessories of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts that appear in more than one type of furnace.
[F27M] INDEXING SCHEME RELATING TO ASPECTS OF THE CHARGES OR FURNACES, KILNS, OVENS OR RETORTS -- F27M defines indexing systems for the properties and handling of charges and the operation of furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, covering aspects such as material composition, loading methods, temperature control and performance parameters.
[F27M 2001/00] Composition, conformation or state of the charge -- The class deals with the material makeup, arrangement and physical condition of the charge placed in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, including additives, packing, moisture content, grain size and other factors that influence heating performance. It covers how these compositional or conformational aspects affect the operation of thermal processing equipment.

[F27M 2002/01] in one layer -- F27M 2002/01 is a CPC subclass under Mechanical Engineering > Furnaces that deals with the indexing of charge disposition in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. [F27M 2002/013] in a push furnace type -- The entry F27M 2002/013 designates the disposition of the charge in a push-furnace type, covering processes where material is introduced and then advanced through the furnace by a pushing mechanism, typically for continuous heating and metallurgical treatment.
[F27M 2002/016] workpieces being separated by a separating element -- This subclass covers furnace operations in which the material being processed is divided or removed by a separating element, such as a partition, grate, or trap, to control the disposition of the charge within the furnace. It applies to mechanical-engineering processes involving kilns, ovens or retorts where workpieces are separated during heating or cooling.
[F27M 2002/03] as a batch of articles in a basket -- The CPC subclass F27M 2002/03 covers the disposition of the charge in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts when the material to be processed is arranged as a batch of articles placed in a basket. It pertains to methods of loading, arranging, or handling the charge as grouped items during heating operations. [F27M 2002/04] Palletised -- F27M 2002/04 (Palletised) covers techniques for placing a charge in a furnace, kiln, oven or retort on pallets, including the arrangement, handling and movement of palletised material during heating or processing. It addresses the disposition of the charge to improve loading efficiency, uniform heat distribution and ease of removal.
[F27M 2002/10] Brick disposition in stacks on the support -- The entry defines the arrangement of bricks placed in stacked form on a support as part of the charge within furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It covers how the brick layout is organized to influence heat distribution and structural stability during heating operations.
[F27M 2002/11] Continuous charging -- F27M 2002/11 - Continuous charging is a subclass in the Cooperative Patent Classification covering mechanical engineering furnaces and related indexing schemes for charges, kilns, ovens or retorts. It addresses methods and apparatuses for continuously feeding material into a furnace or similar heating vessel.
[F27M 2002/12] Discontinuous charging -- F27M 2002/12 Discontinuous charging covers techniques for loading furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts in a non-continuous manner, such as batch or periodic feeding of the charge. The entry is part of the CPC subgroup on mechanical engineering, specifically the indexing scheme that addresses how the charge is introduced and managed.
[F27M 2003/00] Type of treatment of the charge -- F27M 2003/00 classifies the various methods applied to the charge in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, including how the material is heated, cooled, mixed, stirred, fed or otherwise treated during processing.
[F27M 2003/01] Annealing -- F27M 2003/01 - Annealing covers the heat-treatment step in which a charge placed in a furnace, kiln, oven or retort is heated to a prescribed temperature and held to soften the material, relieve internal stresses and improve its ductility or grain structure.
[F27M 2003/02] Preheating, e.g. in a laminating line -- Preheating refers to the initial heating stage applied to a charge before the main heating operation, such as in a laminating line where the material is warmed to a required temperature prior to further processing. This treatment improves energy efficiency and material handling by reducing thermal gradients during the primary furnace or oven cycle.
[F27M 2003/025] Drying -- F27M 2003/025 designates the drying of a charge, meaning the removal of moisture from material placed in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Furnaces → Indexing scheme relating to aspects of the charges or furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts → Type of treatment of the charge, and is used to classify patents that disclose drying methods or equipment for such thermal processing.
[F27M 2003/03] Calcining -- F27M 2003/03 designates the type of treatment applied to a charge within furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, specifically the process of calcining, which involves heating a solid material to a high temperature in order to induce thermal decomposition, phase change or removal of volatile constituents. This entry falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy for furnaces and related equipment.
[F27M 2003/04] Sintering -- F27M 2003/04 - Sintering covers processes in which powdered or granulated material is heated below its melting point to cause particle bonding, typically within furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It falls under Mechanical Engineering, Furnaces, and addresses the type of treatment applied to the charge.
[F27M 2003/05] Brazing -- F27M 2003/05 designates the treatment of a furnace charge by brazing, a process in which metal parts are joined through localized heating and a filler metal without melting the base materials. It falls under mechanical engineering, furnaces and the indexing scheme for charge-related operations.
[F27M 2003/06] Enamelling -- F27M 2003/06 Enamelling refers to processes for applying enamel coatings to a charge within furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, covering the specific type of treatment applied during heating. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Furnaces → Indexing scheme relating to aspects of the charges or furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts → Type of treatment of the charge.
[F27M 2003/07] Carburising -- F27M 2003/07 designates the carburising treatment of a charge in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It covers processes that enrich the surface of metal workpieces with carbon by heating the charge in a carbon-rich atmosphere, a method used to harden and improve wear resistance of steel components. This entry falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for furnace operations.
[F27M 2003/08] Curing -- CPC class F27M 2003/08 falls under mechanical engineering and furnaces, covering methods for curing processes applied to charges in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It pertains to the type of treatment of the charge during heat treatment.
[F27M 2003/085] Curing a painted surface -- F27M 2003/085 classifies patents that disclose heating processes for curing painted surfaces, typically performed in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It falls under the mechanical-engineering group for furnaces, specifically the subclass dealing with the type of treatment applied to the charge. This entry is used to locate inventions that use thermal curing to harden or set paint coatings.
[F27M 2003/09] Expanding the charge, e.g. clay -- F27M 2003/09 classifies a type-of-treatment where the material placed in a furnace, kiln, oven or retort is expanded, for example by adding clay, to change its volume or porosity during heating. The entry falls under mechanical engineering and concerns the treatment of the charge in such thermal equipment.
[F27M 2003/10] Soaking -- F27M 2003/10 Soaking covers the stage in furnace, kiln or oven operations where the charge is held at a set temperature for a designated period to achieve thermal uniformity, relieve stresses or facilitate chemical reactions. It is classified under mechanical engineering, furnaces, and treatments of the charge.
[F27M 2003/11] Incineration -- The CPC subclass F27M 2003/11 "Incineration" pertains to mechanical-engineering furnace technologies and classifies methods and devices for treating a charge by incinerating it in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts, with emphasis on the combustion process that destroys material, reduces waste volume and may recover energy.
[F27M 2003/12] Reactivation of particles, e.g. carbon -- F27M 2003/12 covers methods for reactivating particles-most commonly carbonaceous materials such as coke-within furnace, kiln or oven charges. The entry addresses treatments that restore the chemical or physical activity of spent particles, enabling their reuse in subsequent heating or metallurgical processes.
[F27M 2003/13] Smelting -- F27M 2003/13 denotes smelting, a treatment applied to the charge in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts. It is classified under Mechanical Engineering - Furnaces - Indexing scheme relating to aspects of the charges or furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts - Type of treatment of the charge.
[F27M 2003/14] Pyrolising -- F27M 2003/14 "Pyrolising" classifies processes in which a solid or mixed charge is heated in the absence or limited presence of oxygen to decompose it into gases, vapours and solid residues, specifying the type of charge treatment used in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts.
[F27M 2003/15] Vaporisation -- This CPC subclass deals with processes where the material charged into a furnace, kiln, oven or retort is converted to vapor, i.e. vaporisation as a treatment step. It falls under mechanical engineering and furnaces and covers methods and apparatus for inducing vaporisation of the charge.
[F27M 2003/155] Condensation -- This subclass refers to processes in which the material being heated in a furnace, kiln, oven or retort undergoes condensation, usually to recover heat or moisture from the charge. It falls under CPC code F27M 2003/155, which groups treatment methods affecting the charge in thermal equipment.
[F27M 2003/16] Treatment involving a chemical reaction -- F27M 2003/16 covers processes applied to the charge in furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts in which a chemical reaction-such as combustion, oxidation, reduction or other transformation-is an essential part of the treatment, distinguishing it from purely physical heating methods.
[F27M 2003/162] between two or more components of the charge -- F27M 2003/162 classifies furnace, kiln, oven or retort operations where the charge is placed between two or more components, indicating a treatment in which the material is sandwiched or layered between other substances during heating.
[F27M 2003/165] Reduction -- F27M 2003/165 refers to methods that reduce a charge in a furnace, kiln, oven or retort. It covers treatments that lower the amount of material, remove volatiles or apply reducing agents to achieve a smaller or lighter charge during heating processes. This falls under the CPC hierarchy for type of charge treatment in mechanical-engineering furnaces.
[F27M 2003/167] Oxydation -- F27M 2003/167 - Oxidation classifies furnace processes in which the charge is deliberately exposed to oxygen or oxidizing agents to alter its chemical composition, such as converting metal ores to oxides or removing impurities. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering > Furnaces > Indexing scheme relating to aspects of the charge or furnaces, kilns, ovens or retorts > Type of treatment of the charge.
[F27M 2003/17] Coating -- This subclass covers patents describing the application of a coating to the charge in a furnace, kiln, oven or retort to modify its thermal, chemical or mechanical behavior during heating. It is classified under type of treatment of the charge in the indexing scheme for furnace-related processes within the Mechanical Engineering section.
[F27M 2003/18] Degasifying -- F27M 2003/18 degasifying refers to processes that remove dissolved or entrapped gases from the material being heated in a furnace, kiln, oven or retort, improving product quality or operational efficiency. It is classified under mechanical engineering, furnaces, and the indexing scheme for treatment of the charge.
[F27M 2003/19] Dipping in a salt bath -- F27M 2003/19 designates a furnace-related process in which the charge is treated by dipping it in a salt bath, using molten salts to alter surface or chemical properties. It resides under mechanical engineering → furnaces → indexing scheme for aspects of charges, kilns, ovens or retorts, specifically the type of treatment of the charge.
[F28] HEAT EXCHANGE IN GENERAL -- F28 covers the design, operation and performance of devices and systems that transfer thermal energy, such as heat exchangers, condensers, boilers, radiators and related equipment. It includes methods for enhancing heat transfer, controlling temperature differentials and integrating heat-exchange components into broader mechanical processes.
[F28B] STEAM OR VAPOUR CONDENSERS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  As specified in the head notes of the field F28 , an apparatus using heat exchange for specific purposes is classified either in subclass F28B or in the appropriate subclasses of, for example, classes F22 , F24 , F25 , F26 , or F27 ; or in F28C , F28D .  The subclass F28B covers heat exchangers for converting steam or vapour from its gaseous to its liquid state: typically, a steam condenser has a function to condense exhausted steam from a steam turbine for reuse in the cycle. Steam condensers are widely used in steam turbine power plants. Usually, steam condensers are operated at a pressure below atmospheric pressure, to increase the plant efficiency.  However, F28B does not cover condensers used for separation, nor condensers for refrigeration systems.  In this subclass, the condenser is not characterised by a mixture of vapours to be separated, nor by a separation process, nor by a specific cooperation with steam engine plants or refrigeration systems:  Constructional features related to the heat transfer are additionally classified in F28D / F28F .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Condensation of vapours; recovering volatile solvents by condensation -> B01D5/00 - Separation of gases or vapours -> B01D53/00 - Drying gases or vapours -> B01D53/26 - Condensation during pre-treatment of gases prior to electrostatic precipitation of dispersed particles -> B03C3/014 - Purifying combustible gases containing carbon monoxide by cooling to condense non-gaseous materials -> C10K1/04 - Condensing arrangements in domestic laundry driers -> D06F58/24 - Plants characterised by condensers arranged to co-operate with engines -> F01K9/00 - Plants characterised by engines being structurally combined with condensers -> F01K11/00 - Fluid heaters having heat-generating means specially adapted for extracting latent heat from flue gases by means of condensation -> F24H8/00 - Condensers for refrigeration systems -> F25B39/04 - Processes or apparatus for liquefying gases or gaseous mixtures -> F25J1/00 - Separating the constituents of gaseous mixtures involving the use of liquefaction by partial condensation -> F25J3/06
[F28B 1/00] Condensers in which the steam or vapour is separate from the cooling medium by walls, e.g. surface condenser -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Indirect contact condensers, also called surface condensers, e.g. using liquid as cooling medium or using air as cooling medium.  Liquid cooled condensers are classified in F28B1/02  Example of a water cooled condenser: US 6128901 A  Steam from a low pressure turbine enters a tube and shell condenser through the steam inlet (1) at the top of the condenser and is distributed throughout the shell side. Cooling water is pumped through water inlet (2) in the inlet water box (3) and is distributed throughout the tubes in the tube bundles  Air cooled condensers are classified in F28B1/06  Example of an air cooled condenser: US 2010263840 A  The condenser (20) includes a steam delivery manifold (22), first (24) and second (26) set of bundles of elongated tubes, which are supported on a tent-shaped or V-shaped frame structure (28). The condenser(20) further includes an axial fan (30) directing ambient air upwardly through the first and second sets of tubes (24 ,26).
[F28B 1/02] using water or other liquid as the cooling medium -- F28B 1/02 pertains to surface condensers that employ water or another liquid as the cooling medium, separating the steam or vapor from the coolant by a wall. It covers designs and methods for using liquids to condense vapour in heat-exchange equipment.
[F28B 1/04] employing moving walls -- The subclass covers condensers where the wall that separates steam or vapour from the cooling medium can move, such as rotating, oscillating or reciprocating surfaces, to improve heat transfer or facilitate cleaning. It applies to surface condensers with a non-fixed separating wall.
[F28B 1/06] using air or other gas as the cooling medium -- F28B 1/06 covers steam or vapour condensers where the cooling medium is air or another gas, rather than water, and the condensate is kept separate from the gas by solid walls, such as in surface-type condensers that use ambient air for heat removal.
[F28B 1/08] employing moving walls -- F28B 1/08 covers condensers in which the steam or vapour is separated from the cooling medium by walls that move relative to each other, such as rotating or sliding surface condensers, enabling heat transfer while keeping the two fluids physically apart.
[F28B 11/00] Controlling arrangements with features specially adapted for condensers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Condenser controlling means, e.g. methods for monitoring condenser performance.  Example: US 5005351 A  For monitoring condenser operation, the condenser is equipped with temperature sensors (30) and pressure sensors (32), as well as a chemical sensor (34) for permitting measurement of condensate water quality.
[F28B 2001/065] with secondary condenser, e.g. reflux condenser or dephlegmator -- The subclass covers apparatus that incorporates a secondary condenser, such as a reflux condenser or dephlegmator, to further condense vapors after the primary condensation stage. It is used in systems where additional cooling or reflux is required to improve separation or recycle condensate.
[F28B 3/00] Condensers in which the steam or vapour comes into direct contact with the cooling medium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Direct contact condensers  Direct contact condensers, in which a cooling liquid is injected into the steam are classified in F28B3/04  Example: US 3834133 A  Water introduced into the partition (20) via the pipes (14,16) is sprayed through the openings (22) outwardly across the housing (10) and in the path of the steam passing therethrough. Due to the resulting transfer of heat from the steam to the water, steam will be condensed, with the mixture of water and condensate falling into a well (24).  Direct contact condensers, in which steam is injected into the cooling liquid are classified in F28B3/06  Example: US 4304198 A  Steam is passed through a blow-off pipe (11) into a closed vessel (14) having a water space (12) and a gas space (13). A nest of heat exchanger tubes (15) extends through the water space (12) in a heat transfer relationship to cool the water space
[F28B 3/02] by providing a flowing coating of cooling liquid on the condensing surface -- This subclass covers condensers in which the condensing surface is continuously coated with a flowing film of cooling liquid, allowing direct contact between the steam or vapour and the cooling medium to improve heat-transfer efficiency.
[F28B 3/04] by injecting cooling liquid into the steam or vapour -- A condenser in which a cooling liquid is directly introduced into the steam or vapour stream, allowing the two phases to contact each other for rapid heat transfer and condensation. This includes spray-type condensers and similar devices where liquid is injected into the vapor flow.
[F28B 3/06] by injecting the steam or vapour into the cooling liquid -- This subclass covers direct-contact condensers where steam or vapour is introduced directly into the cooling liquid, allowing heat transfer by mixing and immediate condensation within the liquid phase. Such devices are common in absorption chillers, some power-plant condensers and industrial cooling where a simple, compact design is preferred.
[F28B 3/08] with rotatable members -- This subclass covers condensers where steam or vapour contacts the cooling medium directly and incorporates rotatable components such as drums, wheels or rotating surfaces to improve heat-transfer efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and steam or vapour condensers.
[F28B 5/00] Condensers employing a combination of the methods covered by main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Condensers with cooling by both indirect and direct contact with a cooling medium.  Example: US 6189871 B  In the cooling chamber (4) are nozzles (6) which inject cool condensate in the form of water drops into the chamber. At the end of the cooling chamber (4), the steam passes through the orifices of a perforated diaphragm (8) which brings about a regular distribution of the cooled steam over the cooling tubes in the tube bundles (10).
[F28B 7/00] Combinations of two or more condensers, e.g. provision of reserve condenser -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combination of condensers.  Example: US 4301861 A  The duct (10) has a first branch (12) leading to a surface type steam condenser (13) in which a portion of the steam is condensed by means of a medium cooled within a wet type cooling tower (16), and a second branch (14) leading to a dry type cooling tower (15) in which the remaining portion of the steam is condensed.
[F28B 9/00] Auxiliary systems, arrangements, or devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of condensers.  Means for feeding steam or vapours are classified in F28B9/02  Example: US 2005161094 A  The steam drainage line (5) has air-cooled condensation elements connection to a main steam drainage line (10) via individual branch lines (6), with the cross-section of the main steam drainage line reduced in stages, for reducing pressure losses by allowing gradual reduction in length of branch lines.  Means for feeding cooling liquid are classified in F28B9/04  Example: US 2003061814 A  The steam surface condenser system (15) includes a make-up water header (19) which introduces make-up water into a condenser steam dome (17). The make-up water header is connected to a make-up water heater bundle (11) and provided with self-adjusting spray nozzles (16). The nozzles spray the make-up water in the direction counter-current to the flow of turbine exhaust steam in the form of fine droplets.  Means for recooling the cooling liquid are classified in F28B9/06  Example: US 3851702 A  The steam of the turbine (1) flows into the mixing condenser (2) where it is condensed by means of injected condensate which is supplied through the pipe conduit (9). The condensed steam is transferred in the form of a condensate by the circulation pump (3) through the lower branch of the pipe conduit (9) to the air cooled surface heat exchangers (4). The warm condensate traverses the pipes of the latter and returns through the upper branch of the pipe conduit (9) into the mixing condenser 2 .  Means for collecting condensates are classified in F28B9/08  Example: US 6296049 B  Steam cooling tubes supported by a tube support plate (9) are bisected by a pair of envelopes (28) each of which has a bottom shroud (20B) divided into an inner space (23) and an outer space (24) by a top shroud (20A) which are connected by a circulation opening (8) at the junction. A drain opening (7) is formed at the junction between the tube support plate and the inner space of the bottom shroud.  Means for removing non-condensable gases are classified in F28B9/10  Example: US 6588499 B  The condenser (10) includes an air ejector system header (22) attached to the top of the D-section condensing tubes (15). Uncondensed steam and inert gas are drawn into and collected in the header at the top of the condensing tubes (15) because a vacuum pump (26) runs continuously thereby lowering the pressure in the air ejector (20) to a point below the pressure of the exhaust steam header (12).
[F28B 9/005] for protection against freezing -- F28B 9/005 covers auxiliary components and arrangements for steam or vapour condensers that protect the system from freezing, such as heating coils, insulation, or circulating warm fluids. It is used to classify inventions that prevent ice formation in condenser circuits, ensuring reliable operation in low-temperature environments.
[F28B 9/02] for feeding steam or vapour to condensers -- A subclass of the CPC dealing with auxiliary equipment that supplies steam or vapor to condensers, used in heat-exchange systems such as those in power plants or industrial processes. It covers arrangements and devices specifically designed to feed the working fluid into the condensate unit.
[F28B 9/04] for feeding, collecting, and storing cooling water or other cooling liquid -- F28B 9/04 covers auxiliary arrangements that feed, collect and store cooling water or other coolant for steam or vapour condensers. It includes devices such as feed pumps, collection tanks and storage reservoirs that supply the condenser with a continuous supply of cooling liquid.
[F28B 9/06] with provision for re-cooling the cooling water or other cooling liquid -- This CPC subclass pertains to steam or vapor condensers that incorporate an auxiliary system for re-cooling the cooling water or other cooling liquid, such as heat-exchange arrangements that lower the temperature of the cooling medium before it is recirculated to the condenser.
[F28B 9/08] for collecting and removing condensate -- F28B 9/08 covers devices and methods that collect and discharge condensate from steam or vapor condensers. It includes auxiliary components such as drains, traps and collection chambers designed to manage liquid water formed during heat exchange. The subclass is part of the mechanical engineering field dealing with heat exchangers.
[F28B 9/10] for extracting, cooling, and removing non-condensable gases -- This subclass covers auxiliary devices and arrangements for steam or vapour condensers that extract, cool and eliminate non-condensable gases from the condensate system, improving heat-transfer efficiency and preventing pressure buildup. It includes methods for gas removal, cooling of the extracted gases and related equipment such as venting or scrubbing units.
[F28C] HEAT-EXCHANGE APPARATUS, NOT PROVIDED FOR IN ANOTHER SUBCLASS, IN WHICH THE HEAT-EXCHANGE MEDIA COME INTO DIRECT CONTACT WITHOUT CHEMICAL INTERACTION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heat exchangers involving heat transfer between different media without separating wall, e.g. cooling towers.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mixers -> B01F - Chemical or physical processes -> B01J - Fluid heaters having heat generating means -> F24H - Regenerative heat exchangers -> F28D17/00 , F28D19/00 - Latent heat storage in direct contact with a heat exchange medium or another latent heat storage medium -> F28D20/02
[F28C 1/00] Direct-contact trickle coolers, e.g. cooling towers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heat exchangers in which one medium trickles freely over a material and is brought into direct contact with a cooling medium. e.g. ambient air.  Cooling towers are classified according to the type of flow: counter-current, cross-current or both.  This group covers also:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Building construction of cooling towers -> E04H5/12 - Enclosed spaces cooled by trickle -> F25 - Dry cooling towers -> F28B1/06 - Indirect contact heat exchangers in which one medium trickles freely over conduits -> F28D3/00 - Indirect evaporative cooler -> F28D5/00 - Component parts of trickle coolers -> F28F25/00
[F28C 1/003] comprising outlet ducts for exhaust gases -- F28C 1/003 covers heat-exchange apparatus in which the exhaust gases are directed through outlet ducts after passing through a direct-contact cooling zone, such as a trickle cooler or cooling tower. The ducts guide the gases away from the media while maintaining the direct contact cooling principle. This subclass refines the broader category of direct-contact heat-exchange devices that do not involve chemical interaction.
[F28C 1/02] with counter-current only -- The subclass F28C 1/02 covers direct-contact heat-exchange apparatus such as trickle coolers or cooling towers that operate with a counter-current flow of liquid and gas. In these devices the hot and cold streams meet directly without a separating wall, and the flow is arranged so the gas moves opposite to the liquid, improving heat-transfer efficiency.
[F28C 1/04] with cross-current only -- The entry designates direct-contact cooling towers that operate solely with cross-current flow, meaning the cooling water and air move opposite each other across the tower. It covers apparatus where the two media exchange heat by direct contact without any chemical interaction.
[F28C 1/06] with both counter-current and cross-current -- A direct-contact heat-exchange device, such as a trickle cooler, that combines opposite (counter-current) flow of the cooling and heated streams with transverse (cross-current) flow, allowing heat transfer in both directions within the same apparatus.
[F28C 1/08] Arrangements for recovering heat from exhaust steam -- This subclass covers arrangements that recover heat from exhaust steam by letting the steam directly contact a cooling medium, such as water sprays or trickle coolers, without any chemical interaction. It applies to devices like cooling-tower systems that transfer heat through direct-contact heat exchange.
[F28C 1/10] Arrangements for suppressing noise -- F28C 1/10 pertains to noise-reduction measures in direct-contact heat-exchange equipment such as trickle coolers and cooling towers. It includes baffling, acoustic liners, louvers or other structural features intended to lower the sound produced by water spray and airflow.
[F28C 1/12] Arrangements for preventing clogging by frost -- F28C 1/12 covers devices and methods that keep frost from blocking the flow path in direct-contact trickle coolers such as cooling towers. Typical solutions include heating elements, anti-freeze additives, controlled water distribution or airflow patterns that melt or prevent ice formation on the packing or spray surfaces.
[F28C 1/14] comprising also a non-direct contact heat exchange -- F28C 1/14 concerns direct-contact trickle coolers, such as cooling towers, that additionally incorporate a non-direct contact heat-exchange stage, where part of the heat transfer occurs through a separated fluid medium without the gases and liquids mixing.
[F28C 1/16] Arrangements for preventing condensation, precipitation or mist formation, outside the cooler -- This CPC subclass F28C 1/16 pertains to devices or methods that keep condensate, precipitated particles or mist from forming on the external side of direct-contact heat-exchange equipment such as trickle-cooler towers. It includes structural or operational features that drain, divert or otherwise inhibit liquid or aerosol buildup beyond the cooling surface.
[F28C 2001/006] Systems comprising cooling towers, e.g. for recooling a cooling medium -- The CPC group F28C 2001/006 concerns complete systems that incorporate cooling towers, typically used to lower the temperature of a circulating cooling medium for reuse. Such arrangements are common in power generation, industrial processes and HVAC installations where heated fluid is recirculated after being cooled by air-water contact within the tower.
[F28C 2001/145] with arrangements of adjacent wet and dry passages -- F28C 2001/145 covers pumps, compressors, fans or similar fluid-moving devices that incorporate adjoining wet and dry passages, allowing liquid and gas streams to flow side by side for purposes such as heat exchange, separation or lubrication.
[F28C 3/00] Other direct-contact heat-exchange apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Direct contact heat exchangers without trickling of one heat exchange medium, e.g. direct contact between two gases , between two liquids or between a liquid and a gas without or with change of state; one of the heat exchange medium being a fluent solid, e.g. direct contact fluidized bed.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of food by heating loose materials -> A23B2/40 - Evaporating with heated gases or vapours or liquids in contact with a liquid -> B01D1/14 - Distillation or related exchange processes in which liquids are contacted with gaseous media, e.g. stripping -> B01D3/00 - Mixing gases with liquids -> B01F23/20 - Devices using other cold materials -> F25D3/00 - Furnaces, ovens with cooling grates, endless belts, rotary drums -> F27D15/02 - Direct contact condensers -> F28B3/00 - Indirect contact fluidized bed -> F28D13/00
[F28C 3/005] one heat-exchange medium being a solid  F28C3/10  takes precedence -- F28C 3/005 covers direct-contact heat-exchange devices in which one of the media is a solid, such as a solid filler, phase-change material, or packed bed, that transfers heat directly to or from a liquid or gas without chemical reaction. It falls under the broader class for apparatus where the media meet directly, and its scope is limited by the higher-level entry F28C 3/10.
[F28C 3/02] the heat-exchange media both being gases or vapours -- F28C 3/02 covers heat-exchange devices in which both media are gases or vapours that come into direct contact without any chemical interaction. It includes regenerative, gas-to-gas and similar apparatus used for thermal energy transfer in mechanical engineering.
[F28C 3/04] the heat-exchange media both being liquids -- F28C 3/04 covers heat-exchange devices in which the two heat-transfer media are both liquids that flow in direct contact, allowing heat to be transferred without any chemical reaction between them. This subclass includes exchangers such as direct-mixing coils, liquid-liquid spray condensers, and similar equipment where the liquids intermingle for thermal exchange.
[F28C 3/06] the heat-exchange media being a liquid and a gas or vapour -- F28C 3/06 covers heat-exchange apparatus in which a liquid and a gas or vapour form the heat-exchange media and come into direct contact without any chemical reaction. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically heat exchange in general, and applies to devices not classified elsewhere that rely on direct-contact heat transfer between the two phases.
[F28C 3/08] with change of state, e.g. absorption, evaporation, condensation -- This subclass covers direct-contact heat-exchange devices in which the two media physically mix and undergo a phase change such as absorption, evaporation or condensation, allowing heat to be transferred without any chemical interaction.
[F28C 3/10] one heat-exchange medium at least being a fluent solid, e.g. a particulate material -- F28C 3/10 covers heat-exchange devices in which at least one of the media is a flowing solid such as a particulate or granular material that moves directly through the exchanger without chemical reaction. The solid carries heat by convection while staying in physical contact with the other medium.
[F28C 3/12] the heat-exchange medium being a particulate material and a gas, vapour, or liquid -- F28C 3/12 covers direct-contact heat-exchange devices that employ a solid particulate material together with a gas, vapour or liquid as the heat-transfer medium, allowing the two phases to mix and exchange heat without chemical reaction. This subclass focuses on apparatus where the particulate and fluid phases are intentionally combined for efficient thermal transfer.
[F28C 3/14] the particulate material moving by gravity, e.g. down a tube -- F28C 3/14 relates to direct-contact heat-exchange devices in which a particulate medium transfers heat while moving solely by gravity, for example particles flowing down a tube. The classification covers apparatus that use this gravitational descent of solids to achieve heat exchange without chemical interaction.
[F28C 3/16] the particulate material forming a bed, e.g. fluidised, on vibratory sieves -- F28C 3/16 covers heat-exchange devices where a particulate medium forms a moving bed, such as a fluidised or vibrated layer, used to transfer heat directly between the medium and a surface. The classification includes arrangements that employ vibratory sieves or similar mechanisms to keep the bed in motion for efficient heat transfer.
[F28C 3/18] the particulate material being contained in rotating drums -- F28C 3/18 covers direct-contact heat-exchange devices in which a particulate solid is retained inside one or more rotating drums, allowing the solid to exchange heat directly with another medium such as gas or liquid. The rotating motion promotes uniform temperature distribution and continuous material handling, typical of dryers, mixers and similar equipment. This subclass is used when the heat-exchange media contact the particles without any chemical reaction.
[F28D] HEAT-EXCHANGE APPARATUS, NOT PROVIDED FOR IN ANOTHER SUBCLASS, IN WHICH THE HEAT-EXCHANGE MEDIA DO NOT COME INTO DIRECT CONTACT -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heat exchangers having heat exchange media separated by a solid wall; Apparatus with heat accumulating medium.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Heated or cooled garments -> A41D13/00 - Heat exchanger for medical treatment -> A61F7/00 - Evaporators for separation -> B01D1/00 - Absorbing units for separation -> B01D53/18 - Apparatus for drying gases or vapours -> B01D53/26 - [F28D 1/00] Heat-exchange apparatus having stationary conduit assemblies for one heat-exchange medium only, the media being in contact with different sides of the conduit wall, in which the other heat-exchange medium is a large body of fluid, e.g. domestic or motor car radiators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Radiator-type heat exchangers, e.g. with plate-like conduits or tubular conduits, e.g. for domestic heating (a heating medium flows inside the heat exchanger, for heating the outside air) or for engine cooling systems (a hot fluid flows inside the heat exchanger and is cooled by the outside air) ; Tanks with integrated heat exchange conduits.  F28D1/02 relates to heat exchangers with conduits immersed in a large body of fluid,  e.g. heat exchangers immersed in a natural body of water  Example:  US 5931217 A :  Marine heat exchanger with low profile header construction  e.g. thermosiphon radiators  Example:  US 6009935 A:  Thermosyphon radiator comprising a sealed panel (1) formed into two discrete chambers A,B, each containing a reservoir of vaporising liquid (6) and a heating member (3).  e.g. convectors having air flow channels, with an air driving element  Example: US 6120372 A  Fan convector (1) with adjustable air deflector elements (6) for space heating - uses thermostat (8) to control operation of fan (3) and deflectors (6) in air outlet to control temperature  F28D1/03 relates to heat exchangers with plate-like or laminated conduits  e.g having conduits formed by paired plates touching each other  Example:  US2011079378 A  Fluid/air heat exchanger, e.g. air cooled oil cooler comprising a first sheet metal plate (2) folded with tight folds to form fins (3) and placed over a second sheet metal plate (6) in order to form channels (4) for the passage of the fluid.  F28D1/04 relates to heat exchangers with tubular conduits  e.g with bent conduits  Example:  US 4901791 A  Refrigeration fluid condenser assembly - has parallel flow passages (A, B) defining serpentine fluid paths with unequal length passages  e.g. with straight conduits  Example:  US 5348081 A  Condenser for automotive air conditioning system, comprising, a first and second condenser module including two parallel tanks, (10,12, 14,16) interconnected by a plurality of extruded aluminum tubes (18) with fins (20)  F28D1/06 relates to heat exchangers with conduits forming part or being attached to the tank containing the body of fluid  Example:  US5722487 A  Channelling members (6) on the outside surfaces of the ends of a large tank (2) for carrying heat transfer fluids and controlling the temperature of the tank contents, the channel member having straight sections (8) arranged in a spiral pattern and joined together by arcuately shaped fillets (10)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Evaporative cooler -> F28D5/00  **Glossary:** - Large body of fluid: either an unlimited body of heat-exchange medium, e.g. ambient air, surrounding the stationary conduits assemblies for the first heat-exchange medium, also with conduits for the large body of fluid and circulation by means of a fluid-driving element, or a limited amount of heat-exchange medium, e.g. water, in a container, without mass-transfer with the outside during the heat transfer process
[F28D 1/02] with heat-exchange conduits immersed in the body of fluid -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices where the tubes or channels that carry one fluid are immersed directly in a larger body of another fluid, allowing heat transfer through the tube walls without direct contact between the media. Typical examples include automotive and domestic radiators.
[F28D 1/0206] Heat exchangers immersed in a large body of liquid  apparatus using liquid heat storage material  F28D20/0034 -- Heat exchangers of this subclass use stationary tube bundles in which a circulating fluid carries heat on one side of the tube walls while the opposite side is immersed in a large body of liquid that serves as the heat-exchange medium, often for storage or cooling, such as radiators placed directly in a water tank.
[F28D 1/0213] for heating or cooling a liquid in a tank -- F28D 1/0213 covers heat-exchange apparatus in which a stationary conduit passes through a large body of fluid, the fluid contacts one side of the conduit wall while the liquid inside the conduit is heated or cooled, such as radiators used to condition liquid stored in a tank.
[F28D 1/022] for immersion in a natural body of water, e.g. marine radiators -- F28D 1/022 covers heat-exchange devices in which the working medium is placed directly in a natural body of water, such as seawater, to dissipate heat. Typical examples are marine radiators where the coolant circulates through stationary conduits and the surrounding water provides the cooling fluid without direct contact between separate media.
[F28D 1/0226] with an intermediate heat-transfer medium, e.g. thermosiphon radiators -- F28D 1/0226 defines heat-exchange devices that use stationary pipe assemblies for a single heat-transfer fluid, while an intermediate medium such as a thermosiphon circulates inside the pipes and transfers heat to a larger surrounding fluid body, for example the coolant in domestic or automotive radiators.
[F28D 1/0233] with air flow channels -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices in which a fixed tube bundle carries a liquid. The second medium is air that moves through designed flow channels on the opposite side of the tube walls, typical of radiators.
[F28D 1/024] with an air driving element -- This subclass includes heat-exchange devices that use an air-driving element such as a fan or blower to move air over the outer surface of stationary conduit assemblies. The apparatus transfers heat between a fluid flowing inside the conduits and a large body of fluid outside, without direct contact between the two media, as found in automotive radiators and domestic heating radiators.
[F28D 1/0246] heat-exchange elements having several adjacent conduits forming a whole, e.g. blocks -- F28D 1/0246 covers heat-exchange elements composed of several adjacent conduits that are integrally formed as a block, allowing one fluid to flow through the individual channels while a second fluid contacts the outer surfaces of the block. This arrangement is typical of radiators and similar devices where the two media are separated by the conduit walls.
[F28D 1/03] with plate-like or laminated conduits -- This subclass covers heat-exchange apparatus that employs stationary plate-like or laminated conduits, with one fluid flowing inside the conduit and a second bulk fluid contacting the opposite side of the conduit wall, such as the coolant in a radiator, without the two media mixing.
[F28D 1/0308] the conduits being formed by paired plates touching each other  F28D1/0358  takes precedence -- This subclass covers heat-exchange apparatus in which the fluid-conducting passages are created by two plates placed together so their faces form the conduit walls, each plate forming half of the channel and the plates being in direct contact. The design is used for single-side heat exchange with the opposite side exposed to a large volume of fluid such as radiator cooling water. The broader subclass F28D1/0358 supersedes this more specific grouping.
[F28D 1/0316] Assemblies of conduits in parallel  F28D1/0325 ,  F28D1/035  take precedence -- F28D 1/0316 covers heat-exchange apparatus with stationary conduit assemblies that run in parallel and handle a single contained medium on one side of the conduit wall while the opposite side contacts a large body of fluid such as radiator water. The classification is limited by the more specific subclasses F28D 1/0325 and F28D 1/035, which take precedence.
[F28D 1/0325] the plates having lateral openings therein for circulation of the heat-exchange medium from one conduit to another -- F28D 1/0325 defines heat-exchange apparatus whose plates contain lateral openings that allow the heat-exchange medium to pass from one conduit to another, enabling heat transfer between a stationary conduit circuit and an external fluid body such as a domestic or automotive radiator.
[F28D 1/0333] the plates having integrated connecting members -- F28D 1/0333 covers plate heat-exchangers whose individual plates incorporate the connecting or joining features, such as built-in tubes, manifolds or fastening elements, so the plates themselves form the fluid passages without separate connectors.
[F28D 1/0341] with U-flow or serpentine-flow inside the conduits -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices in which the heat-transfer tubes are fixed and a single fluid flows through them in a U-shaped or serpentine pattern, while the opposite side of the tube wall contacts a large body of another fluid such as the coolant in a radiator.
[F28D 1/035] with U-flow or serpentine-flow inside the conduits  F28D1/0341  takes precedence -- F28D 1/035 covers heat-exchange devices that use stationary tube assemblies in which a single fluid flows through the tubes in a U-flow or serpentine pattern while a second fluid surrounds the tubes as a bulk body, typical of radiators. The subclass is for arrangements not covered elsewhere and is superseded by F28D 1/0341.
[F28D 1/0358] the conduits being formed by bent plates -- F28D 1/0358 refers to heat-exchange devices in which the fluid-conducting passages are formed by bent metal plates that create stationary channels. The plates separate a single flowing medium from a larger body of fluid on the opposite side, as seen in typical radiators.
[F28D 1/0366] the conduits being formed by spaced plates with inserted elements  F28D1/0358  takes precedence -- Heat-exchange apparatus in which the conduit for one medium is created by a set of parallel plates containing inserted elements that form the flow passages, while the opposite medium circulates as a bulk fluid around the plates, as in radiators where the fluid contacts the outer sides of the conduit walls. This subclass specifies the plate-and-insert construction for stationary single-medium conduits.
[F28D 1/0375] the plates having lateral openings therein for circulation of the heat-exchange medium from one conduit to another -- F28D 1/0375 relates to heat-exchange apparatus with stationary conduit assemblies where the plates forming the heat-transfer wall have side openings that let the fluid move laterally from one channel to another, enabling circulation of the same heat-exchange medium through multiple conduits within the device.
[F28D 1/0383] with U-flow or serpentine-flow inside the conduits -- F28D 1/0383 applies to heat-exchange devices in which the internal fluid moves through stationary conduits that are shaped in a U-flow or serpentine pattern, allowing heat transfer to an external fluid that surrounds the conduit walls. The external medium is a large body of fluid such as radiator coolant or water.
[F28D 1/0391] a single plate being bent to form one or more conduits -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices where a single plate is shaped into one or more channels for a fluid, with the opposite side of the plate exposed to a large body of another fluid such as air. The design uses stationary conduit assemblies and the two heat-transfer media do not mix directly.
[F28D 1/04] with tubular conduits -- F28D 1/04 covers heat-exchange devices that use fixed tubular passages for one fluid while the opposite side of the tube wall is in contact with a second, bulk fluid, such as radiators for automobiles or homes. The design involves stationary conduit assemblies and does not include configurations where the two media directly mix.
[F28D 1/0408] Multi-circuit heat exchangers, e.g. integrating different heat exchange sections in the same unit or heat exchangers for more than two fluids  F28F9/0234  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers heat-exchange devices that contain several separate circuit sections within a single housing, allowing more than two fluids or different heat-exchange zones to operate together. The design uses stationary tubes for one medium while the opposite side contacts a bulk fluid such as coolant in radiators. It is superseded by subclass F28F9/0234.
[F28D 1/0417] with particular circuits for the same heat exchange medium, e.g. with the heat exchange medium flowing through sections having different heat exchange capacities or for heating/cooling the heat exchange medium at different temperatures -- A heat-exchange device, such as a radiator, in which the same fluid circulates through distinct circuit sections that have different heat-transfer capacities or are heated/cooled to different temperatures, while the opposite side of the conduit wall contacts a large body of fluid.
[F28D 1/0426] with units having particular arrangement relative to the large body of fluid, e.g. with interleaved units or with adjacent heat exchange units in common air flow or with units extending at an angle to each other or with units arranged around a central element -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices that use stationary tubes or channels for a single heat-transfer fluid while the opposite side exchanges heat with a bulk fluid such as coolant in a radiator. It specifically addresses configurations where the heat-exchange units are positioned in special patterns- interleaved, side-by-side in a common airflow, angled relative to each other, or arranged around a central core.
[F28D 1/0435] Combination of units extending one behind the other  F28D1/0452  takes precedence -- F28D 1/0435 covers heat-exchange devices in which a stationary conduit carries one fluid while the opposite side of the conduit wall contacts a large body of fluid, such as in domestic or automobile radiators, and the arrangement consists of multiple units placed one behind the other. This subclass is overridden by the more specific F28D 1/0452, which takes precedence.
[F28D 1/0443] Combination of units extending one beside or one above the other  F28D1/0452  takes precedence -- Heat-exchange apparatus with stationary conduit assemblies for a single fluid, where the fluid passes through a conduit wall that contacts a large body of fluid such as a radiator, and the device combines separate heat-exchange units arranged side-by-side or one above the other; the layout integrates the units into a single assembly, and the subject matter is superseded by subclass F28D 1/0452.
[F28D 1/0452] Combination of units extending one behind the other with units extending one beside or one above the other -- F28D 1/0452 covers heat-exchange devices that have stationary conduit assemblies handling one fluid while the opposite side of the conduit wall contacts a large body of fluid such as coolant, and that combine multiple heat-exchange units arranged in series with additional units placed laterally or vertically relative to the series.
[F28D 1/0461] Combination of different types of heat exchanger, e.g. radiator combined with tube-and-shell heat exchanger; Arrangement of conduits for heat exchange between at least two media and for heat exchange between at least one medium and the large body of fluid -- F28D 1/0461 covers heat-exchange apparatus that combines distinct exchanger types, such as a radiator integrated with a tube-and-shell unit, and provides conduit arrangements that enable heat transfer between two or more media as well as between one medium and a large body of fluid like coolant in a radiator.
[F28D 1/047] the conduits being bent, e.g. in a serpentine or zig-zag -- The entry refers to heat-exchange apparatus with stationary tubes that are formed in bent shapes such as serpentine or zig-zag, so the working fluid travels through a curved conduit while the opposite side of the tube wall contacts a large body of fluid.
[F28D 1/0471] the conduits having a non-circular cross-section  F28D1/0473 ,  F28D1/0476 ,  F28D1/0478  take precedence -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices in which the stationary conduits that carry a single heat-transfer fluid have a non-circular cross-section, the fluid contacting opposite walls while the second medium is a surrounding bulk fluid such as a radiator coolant. Related narrower subclasses (F28D 1/0473, 1/0476, 1/0478) take precedence over this definition.
[F28D 1/0472] the conduits being helically or spirally coiled -- F28D 1/0472 covers heat-exchange devices where the stationary tubes or conduits for one fluid are formed as helically or spirally coiled elements, the opposite side of the tube wall contacts a larger body of fluid such as coolant in radiators.
[F28D 1/0473] the conduits having a non-circular cross-section -- F28D 1/0473 covers heat-exchange devices in which the stationary tubing has a non-circular cross-section, allowing one fluid to flow inside the conduit while a second fluid circulates around the outside of the tube wall. The classification is used for radiators and similar exchangers where the two media are separated by the tube wall.
[F28D 1/0475] the conduits having a single U-bend -- F28D 1/0475 concerns heat-exchange apparatus with a stationary conduit that incorporates a single U-bend, allowing one heat-exchange medium to circulate through the curved section while the opposite side of the wall contacts a large fluid body such as a radiator coolant.
[F28D 1/0476] the conduits having a non-circular cross-section -- F28D 1/0476 relates to heat-exchange devices where the stationary tubes or channels that carry one fluid are formed with a non-circular cross-section, such as rectangular, oval or finned shapes. This subclass is used for radiators and similar apparatus in which the other heat-exchange medium is a large body of fluid.
[F28D 1/0477] the conduits being bent in a serpentine or zig-zag -- This subclass concerns heat-exchange apparatus in which the stationary conduits are formed in a serpentine or zig-zag pattern, allowing a single tube to transfer heat between an internal fluid and an external bulk fluid without direct contact. Such configurations are typical of radiators where the tube walls separate the two media.
[F28D 1/0478] the conduits having a non-circular cross-section -- This subclass covers heat-exchange apparatus with stationary conduits that have a non-circular cross-section, enabling enhanced heat transfer when the opposite side of the conduit wall contacts a large body of fluid such as in radiators.
[F28D 1/053] the conduits being straight -- F28D 1/053 covers heat-exchange devices that use stationary straight conduits, each conduit carrying one fluid while a separate fluid surrounds the conduit wall on the opposite side. The design is typical of radiators where the internal medium flows through straight tubes and the external medium, such as air, passes over the tube surfaces. This subclass excludes arrangements where the conduits are curved or where the two media come into direct contact.
[F28D 1/05308] Assemblies of conduits connected side by side or with individual headers, e.g. section type radiators  F28D1/05358  takes precedence -- This CPC subgroup defines heat-exchange assemblies in which a series of stationary conduits are arranged side-by-side or linked to separate headers, such as the section-type radiators used in automotive or domestic cooling systems. The design handles a single internal fluid while an external body of fluid contacts the opposite side of the conduit walls. The later group F28D 1/05358 overrides this entry.
[F28D 1/05316] Assemblies of conduits connected to common headers, e.g. core type radiators  F28D1/05366  takes precedence -- Heat-exchange apparatus with stationary conduit assemblies that carry a single heat-exchange medium while the opposite medium is a large body of fluid, the conduits being joined to common inlet and outlet headers in a core-type radiator configuration. This subclass covers radiators where the tubes are linked to shared headers, and it supersedes the related entry F28D 1/05366.
[F28D 1/05325] with particular pattern of flow, e.g. change of flow direction  F28D1/05341  takes precedence -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices with stationary tubes that convey a single fluid while the opposite side of the tube wall contacts a bulk fluid, and where the flowing medium follows a specific pattern such as a change of flow direction. The particular flow arrangement distinguishes the apparatus from other heat-exchange types. The broader subclass F28D1/05341 governs this feature and takes priority.
[F28D 1/05333] with multiple rows of conduits or with multi-channel conduits  F28D1/05341  takes precedence -- A heat-exchange apparatus comprising stationary conduit assemblies arranged in multiple rows or formed as multi-channel conduits, in which one fluid circulates inside the conduits while the second medium is a surrounding bulk fluid such as water in a radiator. The subclass F28D 1/05341 overrides this entry.
[F28D 1/05341] with multiple rows of conduits or with multi-channel conduits combined with a particular flow pattern, e.g. multi-row multi-stage radiators -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices where a stationary network of tubes carries one fluid while the opposite side of the tube wall is in contact with a bulk fluid such as coolant, and the tube arrangement includes several rows or multi-channel channels designed to produce a specific flow pattern, typical of multi-row or multi-stage radiators.
[F28D 1/0535] the conduits having a non-circular cross-section -- F28D 1/0535 covers heat-exchange devices in which the stationary conduits that carry one fluid have a non-circular cross-section, the opposite side of the conduit wall being exposed to a large body of fluid such as a radiator.
