[F28D 7/10] the conduits being arranged one within the other, e.g. concentrically -- This subclass pertains to stationary heat-exchange units where the two fluids travel in separate tubular passages that are arranged concentrically-one conduit placed inside the other-so the media exchange heat through the shared wall without direct contact.
[F28D 7/103] consisting of more than two coaxial conduits or modules of more than two coaxial conduits -- F28D 7/103 refers to heat-exchange apparatus where both fluids flow through stationary tubular assemblies and are separated by the conduit wall, and the device comprises more than two coaxial conduits or modules of such conduits.
[F28D 7/106] consisting of two coaxial conduits or modules of two coaxial conduits -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices in which the two heat-transfer streams flow through stationary concentric tubes, one conduit placed inside another, so the media are separated by the tube wall and exchange heat across it. The arrangement may be formed by a single pair of coaxial tubes or by modular groups of such pairs.
[F28D 7/12] the surrounding tube being closed at one end, e.g. return type -- F28D 7/12 covers heat exchangers in which one fluid flows through a stationary tube while a second fluid flows in a surrounding tube that is closed at one end, a return-type arrangement, allowing heat to be transferred across the tube wall without direct contact between the media.
[F28D 7/14] both tubes being bent -- This subclass pertains to heat-exchange devices where the two separate fluid streams move through stationary tubular conduits that are both curved or bent, allowing heat transfer across the tube walls while the tubes follow a non-straight path.
[F28D 7/16] the conduits being arranged in parallel spaced relation -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices in which two fluids move through stationary tubular conduits that run side-by-side in parallel, each fluid contacting opposite sides of the conduit wall without direct mixing. The tubes are arranged in spaced, parallel rows, as commonly found in shell-and-tube exchangers.
[F28D 7/1607] with particular pattern of flow of the heat exchange media, e.g. change of flow direction  F28D7/1623 ,  F28D7/1638 ,  F28D7/1661 ,  F28D7/1676 ,  F28D7/1692  take precedence -- A heat-exchange apparatus in which stationary tubular conduit assemblies carry both media separately on opposite sides of the conduit walls, and the flow of the media follows a specific pattern such as a change in direction. This subclass covers designs that specify particular flow arrangements, and the more detailed sub-entries F28D 7/1623, 7/1638, 7/1661, 7/1676 and 7/1692 take precedence over the broader definition.
[F28D 7/1615] the conduits being inside a casing and extending at an angle to the longitudinal axis of the casing; the conduits crossing the conduit for the other heat exchange medium -- This subclass covers heat-exchange units in which the stationary tubes for each fluid are housed within a common casing, with one group of tubes aligned at an angle to the casing's longitudinal axis and intersecting the tubes carrying the other medium, allowing indirect heat transfer without direct contact between the media.
[F28D 7/1623] with particular pattern of flow of the heat exchange media, e.g. change of flow direction -- The subclass covers heat-exchange devices where both fluids stay in fixed tubular assemblies, each fluid contacting opposite sides of the tube wall, and the flow follows a specific pattern such as a reversal or change of direction. It applies to stationary tube arrangements in which the heat-exchange media never come into direct contact.
[F28D 7/163] with conduit assemblies having a particular shape, e.g. square or annular; with assemblies of conduits having different geometrical features; with multiple groups of conduits connected in series or parallel and arranged inside common casing  F28D7/1615  takes precedence
[F28D 7/1638] with particular pattern of flow or the heat exchange medium flowing inside the conduits assemblies, e.g. change of flow direction from one conduit assembly to another one  F28D7/1661 ,  F28D7/1676  take precedence -- F28D 7/1638 covers heat-exchange devices that use fixed tubular bundles for both fluids, where the flow pattern of one or both media changes between conduit assemblies, such as a reversal of direction from one bundle to the next. This subclass is subordinate to the broader category for stationary tubular heat exchangers, and its more specific sub-subclasses F28D 7/1661 and F28D 7/1676 take precedence.
[F28D 7/1646] with particular pattern of flow of the heat exchange medium flowing outside the conduit assemblies, e.g. change of flow direction -- F28D 7/1646 covers heat-exchange equipment in which the fluid that flows around stationary tube bundles follows a defined flow pattern, such as a change of direction, rather than a simple unidirectional stream. The arrangement is used when the two heat-transfer media contact opposite sides of the tube walls and the external flow is shaped to improve heat transfer or pressure-drop characteristics.
[F28D 7/1653] the conduit assemblies having a square or rectangular shape -- The F28D 7/1653 entry covers heat-exchange apparatus in which stationary tubular conduit assemblies carry the two media on opposite sides of the conduit walls, and the conduits themselves are formed with a square or rectangular cross-section. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and apparatus where the media never mix directly.
[F28D 7/1661] with particular pattern of flow of the heat exchange media, e.g. change of flow direction
[F28D 7/1669] the conduit assemblies having an annular shape; the conduits being assembled around a central distribution tube -- This subclass relates to heat-exchange apparatus in which stationary tubular conduits are arranged in an annular configuration around a central distribution tube, allowing one fluid to flow inside the central tube and the second fluid to pass through the surrounding annular space, each contacting opposite sides of the conduit wall.
[F28D 7/1676] with particular pattern of flow of the heat exchange media, e.g. change of flow direction -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices in which two fluids flow on opposite sides of stationary tubular conduits and the flow pattern includes specific changes such as reversal of direction, cross-flow or other programmed variations to improve heat transfer. It applies to apparatus not provided for in any other heat-exchange subclass.
[F28D 7/1684] the conduits having a non-circular cross-section -- F28D 7/1684 pertains to heat-exchange apparatus with stationary tubular conduit assemblies in which the conduits have a non-circular cross-section, allowing the two heat-exchange media to flow on opposite sides of the tube wall without direct contact.
[F28D 7/1692] with particular pattern of flow of the heat exchange media, e.g. change of flow direction -- F28D 7/1692 covers heat-exchange devices that use stationary tubular passages for both fluids and employ a specific flow arrangement, such as reversing or alternating flow direction, to enhance heat transfer. The two media remain separated by the tube walls and the design is not covered by other subclasses.
[F28D 9/00] Heat-exchange apparatus having stationary plate-like or laminated conduit assemblies for both heat-exchange media, the media being in contact with different sides of a conduit wall -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Examples:  US 3734177 A  Heat exchanger (10) exchanging heat between a first fluid which may be engine lubricating oil and a second fluid which may be a liquid coolant such as water, comprising a baffle sheet (11) of heat conducting metal arranged in serpentine section separating the first fluid that is confined by a first casing (12) from the second fluid that is confined by a second casing (13), the baffle sheet (11) having spaced folds (14) .  US 4848450 A  Heat exchanger including a core structure formed by a plurality of square or rectangular plate members (10) in stacked arrangement, each plate member (10) including along two opposed sides a portion (18) bent at right angles to the basic plate and then bent inwardly (19) to wrap around the next adjacent plate member (10).  US 5222551 A  A laminate-type heat exchanger having a plurality of plates (1) brazed to each other, each plate having a flange (2) and projection formed along the circumference thereof, raised portions (4) and hollowed portions (5) formed at both ends of an area surrounded by the projection and which define together reservoirs, communication holes (6, 7) formed through the raised portions (4) and the hollowed portions (5).  US 2007107889 A  Heat exchanger assembly including a core assembly (12) having passages that are closed off on at least one side by a closure bar (36). A reinforcing bar (40) is received within a channel of the closure bar to create an interface with substantially no gaps that may accumulate moisture within the core assembly.  Heat exchangers having conduits formed by spirally-wound plates are classified in F28D9/04  Example:  US 2006124286 A  Heat exchanger having spiral shaped primary and secondary paths (10,12) separated by partition wall (14), inbetween end plates (16, 18) having individual inlet ports (22,30) and outlet ports (26,34) for respective paths  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Elements constructed for building-up into stacks, e.g. capable of being taken apart for cleaning -> F28F3/08 - Elements constructed in the shape of a hollow panel, e.g. with channels -> F28F3/12 - Hollow panels formed by separating portions of a pair of joined sheets to form channels, e.g. by inflation -> F28F3/14
[F28D 9/0006] the plate-like or laminated conduits being enclosed within a pressure vessel -- This subclass covers heat exchangers in which stationary plate or laminated channels for the two fluids are sealed inside a pressure vessel, keeping the media separated and allowing heat transfer across the conduit walls.
[F28D 9/0012] the apparatus having an annular form -- F28D 9/0012 covers heat-exchange devices where the stationary plate-like or laminated conduits are arranged in an annular configuration, allowing the two heat-transfer media to flow on opposite sides of the conduit walls without direct contact. The design provides compact, efficient heat transfer in applications requiring separation of fluids.
[F28D 9/0018] without any annular circulation of the heat exchange media -- F28D 9/0018 relates to heat-exchange apparatus in which stationary plate-like or laminated conduit assemblies carry the two heat-exchange media on opposite sides of a conduit wall, with no annular circulation of either medium.
[F28D 9/0025] the conduits being formed by zig-zag bend plates -- Heat-exchange apparatus in which the stationary plate-like or laminated conduits are formed from zig-zag bend plates, creating a series of alternating bends that define separate flow paths for the two media, each medium contacting opposite sides of the conduit walls.
[F28D 9/0031] the conduits for one heat-exchange medium being formed by paired plates touching each other  F28D9/0012 ,  F28D9/0025 ,  F28D9/0081 ,  F28D9/04  take precedence -- Heat-exchange apparatus in which the conduits for one fluid are created by two plates that are pressed together to form a plate-type channel, while the second fluid passes through separate channels; this sub-class is limited to configurations where the paired plates constitute the conduit and is subordinate to the broader groups F28D9/0012, F28D9/0025, F28D9/0081 and F28D9/04.
[F28D 9/0037] the conduits for the other heat-exchange medium also being formed by paired plates touching each other  F28D9/0043  takes precedence -- F28D 9/0037 pertains to heat-exchange devices that use fixed, plate-like or laminated channel assemblies for both fluids, each fluid flowing on opposite sides of a conduit wall, with the channels for the second fluid also formed by two plates pressed together; the more detailed subclass F28D 9/0043 takes precedence over this definition.
[F28D 9/0043] the plates having openings therein for circulation of at least one heat-exchange medium from one conduit to another -- F28D 9/0043 relates to stationary plate-type heat-exchange units in which the plates have openings that permit at least one heat-transfer fluid to flow from one conduit passage to another, allowing indirect heat exchange between separate media on opposite sides of the plate.
[F28D 9/005] the plates having openings therein for both heat-exchange media -- F28D 9/005 covers heat-exchange devices in which stationary plate-like or laminated shells form separate pathways for the two media, and the plates are pierced with openings that allow each fluid to flow through opposite sides of the plate wall, enabling indirect heat transfer without direct contact.
[F28D 9/0056] with U-flow or serpentine-flow inside conduits; with centrally arranged openings on the plates
[F28D 9/0062] the conduits for one heat-exchange medium being formed by spaced plates with inserted elements  F28D9/0012 ,  F28D9/0025 ,  F28D9/0081 ,  F28D9/04  take precedence -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices where the conduit for one fluid is formed by a series of spaced plates that contain inserted elements such as ribs, spacers or corrugations, creating a stationary laminated passage. The plates keep the two heat-exchange media on opposite sides of the wall, preventing direct contact while the inserted elements shape flow and enhance heat transfer.
[F28D 9/0068] with means for changing flow direction of one heat exchange medium, e.g. using deflecting zones -- Heat-exchange devices with fixed plate or laminated channels that keep the two fluids separated on opposite sides of a wall, equipped with structures such as deflecting zones that alter the flow direction of one of the media. This arrangement improves heat transfer efficiency while maintaining non-direct contact between the fluids.
[F28D 9/0075] the plates having openings therein for circulation of the heat-exchange medium from one conduit to another -- F28D 9/0075 covers heat-exchange devices that use stationary plate-like or laminated conduits where the two heat-transfer fluids flow on opposite sides of a plate wall, and the plates contain openings that let the medium move from one channel to another, improving fluid distribution and heat transfer.
[F28D 9/0081] the conduits for one heat-exchange medium being formed by a single plate-like element  F28D9/0012  takes precedence ; the conduits for one heat-exchange medium being integrated in one single plate-like element  F28D9/0012  takes precedence -- F28D 9/0081 covers heat-exchange equipment in which the channels for one of the fluids are created as a single plate-like element, the opposite side of the plate serving as the conduit wall for the other fluid. This arrangement is subordinate to the broader subclass F28D 9/0012, which has priority.
[F28D 9/0087] with flexible plates -- F28D 9/0087 covers heat-exchange devices that use stationary plate-like or laminated ducts to keep two media separated, each contacting opposite sides of a conduit wall, where the plates themselves are flexible, allowing the assembly to conform to shape or pressure variations.
[F28D 9/0093] Multi-circuit heat-exchangers, e.g. integrating different heat exchange sections in the same unit or heat-exchangers for more than two fluids -- F28D 9/0093 covers heat-exchange devices that combine several circuit sections within a single unit or that transfer heat among more than two fluids, using stationary plate-like or laminated conduit assemblies in which the two media flow on opposite sides of a solid wall.
[F28D 9/02] the heat-exchange media travelling at an angle to one another -- F28D 9/02 covers heat-exchange apparatus in which the two media flow through stationary plate-like or laminated channels and cross each other at an angle, each medium contacting opposite sides of the conduit wall without direct mixing.
[F28D 9/04] the conduits being formed by spirally-wound plates or laminae -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices in which the stationary channels for the two fluids are created by winding flat plates or thin laminae into a spiral, forming a conduit whose walls present one side to one medium and the opposite side to the other. The design provides compact, plate-type heat exchangers without direct contact between the media.
[F28F] DETAILS OF HEAT-EXCHANGE AND HEAT-TRANSFER APPARATUS, OF GENERAL APPLICATION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Particular elements of heat exchangers; details of heat transfer devices; means for modifying heat transfer; control arrangements for heat exchangers.
[F28F 1/00] Tubular elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tubular elements; assemblies of tubular elements,e.g. particular shape, construction, geometry of individual tubular element, e.g. multiple wall conduits, tubes with variable shape.  Tubular elements of cross-section which is non circular, e.g. flat tubes, flat multichannel tubes.  Tubular elements of cross-section which is polygonal, e.g. square, rectangular.  Tubular elements which are crimped or corrugated in cross-section.  Tubular elements which are crimped or corrugated in longitudinal section.  Means for increasing heat transfer area, e.g. fins, ribs.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Elements specially adapted for movement -> F28F5/00
[F28F 1/003] Multiple wall conduits, e.g. for leak detection  leak-detection in metal cooled nuclear reactor steam generators  F22B1/066
[F28F 1/006] with variable shape, e.g. with modified tube ends, with different geometrical features  F28F1/025 ,  F28F1/06 ,  F28F1/08 ,  F28F9/16 ,  F28F9/18  take precedence -- F28F 1/006 covers tubular heat-exchange elements whose shape can be altered, for example by modifying tube ends or incorporating varying geometric features. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 1/02] Tubular elements of cross-section which is non-circular -- F28F 1/02 relates to tubular heat-exchange elements whose cross-section is non-circular, covering shapes such as rectangular, oval, elliptical, or other polygonal tubes used in heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 1/022] with multiple channels -- This subclass covers tubular heat-exchange elements that incorporate more than one internal channel, enabling separate fluid streams to flow within a single tube and thereby improving heat-transfer performance in general-purpose heat exchangers.
[F28F 1/025] with variable shape, e.g. with modified tube ends, with different geometrical features  F28F1/06 ,  F28F1/08 ,  F28F9/16 ,  F28F9/18  take precedence
[F28F 1/04] polygonal, e.g. rectangular -- F28F 1/04 covers tubular heat-exchange elements that have a polygonal cross-section, such as rectangular or other multi-sided shapes. These shapes are used to improve flow distribution, surface area, or mechanical strength in heat exchangers.
[F28F 1/045] with assemblies of stacked elements -- The subclass F28F 1/045 relates to heat-exchange devices that employ tubular elements arranged in stacked assemblies. It includes designs where multiple tubes are packed or layered to form a compact heat-transfer core, typically used in shell-and-tube or plate-type exchangers. The focus is on the configuration and integration of these stacked tube assemblies within general-purpose heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 1/06] crimped or corrugated in cross-section -- F28F 1/06 covers tubular elements whose cross-section is intentionally crimped or corrugated to increase surface area and improve heat-transfer performance. These shapes are used in heat exchangers and other thermal-management devices where enhanced fluid turbulence or structural rigidity is needed. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heat-exchange general.
[F28F 1/08] Tubular elements crimped or corrugated in longitudinal section -- Tubular heat-exchange elements whose walls are formed with longitudinal crimping or corrugations, giving a wavy or folded profile along the length of the tube to increase surface area, enhance turbulence and improve heat-transfer efficiency in general-purpose heat-exchange equipment.
[F28F 1/10] Tubular elements and assemblies thereof with means for increasing heat-transfer area, e.g. with fins, with projections, with recesses -- It relates to tubes or tube bundles that incorporate surface-enhancing features such as fins, ribs, projections or recesses to enlarge the heat-transfer area. The classification covers the design and integration of these elements within heat-exchange equipment.
[F28F 11/00] Arrangements for sealing leaky tubes and conduits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices or processes for repairing damaged heat exchange conduits  F28F11/02 : using obturating elements, e.g. washers, plugs  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Means for stopping flow in pipes in general -> F16L55/10
[F28F 11/02] using obturating elements, e.g. washers, inserted and operated independently of each other -- The CPC subgroup F28F 11/02 covers arrangements for sealing leaky tubes or conduits that use separate obturating elements such as washers, each inserted and operated independently of the others, and is situated within mechanical engineering, heat-exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 11/04] using pairs of obturating elements, e.g. washers, mounted upon central operating rods -- F28F 11/04 concerns sealing arrangements for heat-exchange tubes in which two obturating elements such as washers are mounted on a central operating rod to close a leak. The classification applies to devices that use paired sealing elements to compress against the tube wall, providing a removable, adjustable seal in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment.
[F28F 1/105] the means being corrugated elements extending around the tubular elements -- F28F 1/105 relates to heat-exchanger devices that use corrugated elements wrapped around tubular members. The corrugations create additional turbulence and surface area, thereby enhancing heat-transfer performance. It applies to general-purpose tubular heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 11/06] using automatic tube obturating appliances -- The subclass F28F 11/06 covers methods and devices that automatically seal or block leaking tubes in heat-exchange equipment, using self-acting plugs, caps or similar obturating appliances. It addresses systems that detect a leak and promptly close the conduit without manual intervention.
[F28F 1/12] the means being only outside the tubular element -- The entry covers features that are attached to the outside of a tube in heat-exchange devices, such as external fins, jackets or supports that enhance heat transfer without being integral to the tube wall. It is used for general-application heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 1/122] and being formed of wires -- This subclass covers tubular heat-exchange elements that are constructed from wire, such as wire-mesh tubes or finned wire bundles used in heat exchangers. It is part of the broader group detailing general-application heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus within mechanical engineering.
[F28F 1/124] and being formed of pins -- F28F 1/124 classifies tubular heat-exchange elements that are constructed from pins. It applies to devices where the heat-transfer surface is formed by an array of pin-like members, typically used to enhance fluid turbulence and improve heat-transfer efficiency in general-purpose heat-exchange equipment.
[F28F 1/126] consisting of zig-zag shaped fins  F28F1/105  takes precedence -- F28F 1/126 covers heat-exchanger tubular elements that incorporate zig-zag shaped fins to increase surface area and improve heat-transfer efficiency. The design falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application. It is subordinate to F28F1/105, which takes precedence.
[F28F 1/128] Fins with openings, e.g. louvered fins -- This subgroup covers heat-exchange fins that incorporate openings such as louvers to enhance airflow or heat transfer. The openings may be formed by slots or perforations, allowing controlled fluid passage through the fin surface.
[F28F 1/14] and extending longitudinally -- This subclass covers tubular heat-exchange elements that incorporate longitudinal extensions such as fins, ribs or lugs running along the tube's length to increase surface area, enhance heat transfer, or provide support in general-purpose heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 1/16] the means being integral with the element, e.g. formed by extrusion -- A tubular heat-exchange element that incorporates integral features, such as fins or ribs, formed as part of the tube itself-typically by extrusion-so that the heat-transfer enhancement is built into the element rather than added later. This applies to general-purpose heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 1/18] the element being built-up from finned sections -- F28F 1/18 covers tubular heat-exchange elements that are assembled from finned sections. The classification includes tubes or pipes formed by joining multiple finned pieces to create a composite structure that improves heat-transfer performance, such as finned-segment heat exchangers used in mechanical engineering applications.
[F28F 1/20] the means being attachable to the element -- The code F28F 1/20 relates to the accessories that can be secured to tubular elements of heat-exchange or heat-transfer devices, such as brackets, clamps, sleeves or fittings designed to attach, support or connect the tubes within a heat-exchanger assembly.
[F28F 1/22] the means having portions engaging further tubular elements -- F28F 1/22 covers structures or devices that include portions designed to engage additional tubular elements in a heat-exchange apparatus, such as brackets, clips, or retaining means that connect, support, or hold tubes together to ensure proper heat transfer and mechanical stability.
[F28F 1/24] and extending transversely -- The F28F 1/24 subclass covers tubular heat-exchange elements that incorporate transverse extensions, such as ribs, fins or similar protrusions extending outward from the tube wall to enhance heat-transfer surface area and fluid turbulence in general-purpose heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 1/26] the means being integral with the element -- F28F 1/26 pertains to tubular heat-exchange elements that incorporate functional or supporting features as an integral part of the tube itself, such as built-in fins, baffles, or internal passages. The classification covers designs where these means are formed together with the tube rather than added as separate components.
[F28F 1/28] the element being built-up from finned sections -- F28F 1/28 classifies tubular heat-exchange elements that are constructed from one or more finned sections, where the fins are integral or attached to the tube walls to increase surface area for heat transfer. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus of general application.
[F28F 1/30] the means being attachable to the element -- The CPC code F28F 1/30 covers detachable or attachable components for tubular heat-exchange elements, such as end caps, supports, fittings or accessories that can be mounted on or removed from the tubes. These means are intended for assembly, adjustment, cleaning or replacement of the tubes in heat-exchange or heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 13/00] Arrangements for modifying heat-transfer, e.g. increasing, decreasing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for optimizing heat transfer (increasing heat transfer), other than fins; Heat insulating means (decreasing heat transfer); e.g. thermal joints, use of particular conductive materials, thermal barriers, use of permeable mass, perforated or porous materials.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Particular heat transfer materials -> C09K5/00 - Pulse tube cooler -> F25B9/14 - Cooling semiconductors with porous structures -> H10W40/25 - Cooling semiconductors by jet impingement -> H10W40/776
[F28F 13/003] by using permeable mass, perforated or porous materials  F28F13/18  takes precedence
[F28F 13/02] by influencing fluid boundary -- F28F 13/02 covers arrangements that modify heat-transfer performance by influencing the fluid boundary layer on a heat-exchange surface. It includes methods such as adding surface roughness, coatings, fins or other features that change flow characteristics to increase or decrease thermal transfer.
[F28F 13/04] by preventing the formation of continuous films of condensate on heat-exchange surfaces, e.g. by promoting droplet formation -- The F28F 13/04 subclass deals with methods that avoid the creation of a continuous condensate film on heat-exchange surfaces by encouraging the formation of discrete droplets. By breaking up the liquid layer, the approach improves heat-transfer efficiency in general-purpose exchangers.
[F28F 13/06] by affecting the pattern of flow of the heat-exchange media -- F28F 13/06 covers heat-exchange equipment whose performance is altered by shaping the flow pattern of the working fluid, for example through baffles, corrugated passages or flow-directing elements that increase or decrease heat transfer.
[F28F 13/08] by varying the cross-section of the flow channels -- The class covers heat-exchanger designs that modify heat transfer by changing the cross-sectional shape of the fluid channels along their length. Varying the channel geometry-such as tapering, stepped or contoured sections-alter flow velocity and surface area to increase or decrease heat-transfer efficiency.
[F28F 13/10] by imparting a pulsating motion to the flow, e.g. by sonic vibration -- F28F 13/10 covers heat-exchange arrangements that alter the fluid flow by adding a pulsating motion, such as sonic or acoustic vibrations, to increase or decrease heat transfer rates. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 13/12] by creating turbulence, e.g. by stirring, by increasing the force of circulation -- F28F 13/12 concerns arrangements that modify heat-transfer by creating turbulence, such as stirring or increasing the flow force in heat-exchange equipment. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 13/125] by stirring -- The F28F 13/125 subclass covers heat-exchange or heat-transfer devices that employ stirring to modify the rate of heat transfer, such as increasing mixing or flow turbulence within the exchanger. It pertains to general-application apparatus where mechanical agitation is used to enhance or reduce heat-exchange performance.
[F28F 13/14] by endowing the walls of conduits with zones of different degrees of conduction of heat -- This subclass covers heat-exchange arrangements in which the interior surfaces of pipes or ducts are designed with regions that have varying thermal conductivity, allowing selective increase or decrease of heat transfer along the conduit. It includes methods of embedding materials or treatments that create zones of high or low conduction to control temperature distribution.
[F28F 13/16] by applying an electrostatic field to the body of the heat-exchange medium -- F28F 13/16 classifies arrangements that modify heat-transfer performance by applying an electrostatic field to the heat-exchange medium itself. The field influences the physical properties of the fluid or solid, allowing the heat-transfer rate to be increased or decreased in general-purpose exchangers.
[F28F 13/18] by applying coatings, e.g. radiation-absorbing, radiation-reflecting -- F28F 13/18 covers heat-exchange arrangements that modify heat transfer by applying surface coatings, such as radiation-absorbing or radiation-reflecting layers, to improve or reduce heat flow. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 13/182] especially adapted for evaporator or condenser surfaces  F28F13/187  takes precedence -- F28F 13/182 relates to configurations of heat-exchange equipment specially designed to modify the performance of evaporator or condenser surfaces, for example by adding fins, coatings or surface geometries that increase or decrease heat transfer.
[F28F 13/185] Heat-exchange surfaces provided with microstructures or with porous coatings -- F28F 13/185 covers heat-exchange surfaces that incorporate micro-structured or porous coatings to enhance or control heat transfer. These features may increase surface area, promote turbulence, or provide insulation, and are applied to general-purpose heat-transfer devices.
[F28F 13/187] especially adapted for evaporator surfaces or condenser surfaces, e.g. with nucleation sites
[F28F 1/32] the means having portions engaging further tubular elements -- This subgroup covers components in heat-exchange equipment that provide portions designed to engage, support or connect additional tubular elements such as tubes, rods or pipes. It includes baffles, retainers, clips or similar structures that hold or align secondary tubes within a primary heat-exchanger bundle.
[F28F 1/325] Fins with openings -- The code F28F 1/325 classifies heat-exchange fins that incorporate openings, such as slots or perforations, to allow fluid to pass through while increasing the exposed surface area for heat transfer. These finned components are used in tubular heat-exchanger assemblies to improve thermal performance without adding excessive bulk.
[F28F 1/34] and extending obliquely -- The code covers heat-exchange tubes that are positioned or built to extend obliquely relative to the main flow or axis of the exchanger. Such angled tubular elements are used to enhance fluid turbulence, improve heat transfer efficiency, or accommodate space constraints in compact or serpentine designs.
[F28F 1/36] the means being helically wound fins or wire spirals -- F28F 1/36 covers tubular heat-exchange elements that use helically wound fins or wire spirals to increase surface area and improve heat-transfer efficiency.
[F28F 1/38] and being staggered to form tortuous fluid passages -- F28F 1/38 covers tubular heat-exchange elements whose tubes are arranged in a staggered pattern to create tortuous fluid passages. The configuration forces the fluid to follow a winding path, enhancing turbulence and heat-transfer efficiency while also affecting pressure drop. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 1/40] the means being only inside the tubular element -- F28F 1/40 covers features that are situated entirely within a tubular heat-exchange element, such as internal fins, turbulators, or baffles that improve fluid flow and heat transfer without extending outside the tube wall. This sub-class is part of the broader group on tubular elements used in heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 1/405] and being formed of wires -- This subgroup covers heat-exchanger tubes that are made from wires, such as wire-wrapped or wire-mesh bundles forming the tubular element. It includes constructions where the tube wall consists of a woven or twisted wire structure rather than solid material.
[F28F 1/42] the means being both outside and inside the tubular element -- F28F 1/42 covers features that contact both the inner and outer surfaces of a tubular heat-exchange element, such as fins, ribs or corrugations that protrude through the tube wall to improve heat transfer. The classification applies to designs where a single component serves as the means for heat exchange on both sides of the tube.
[F28F 1/422] with outside means integral with the tubular element and inside means integral with the tubular element  F28F1/424  takes precedence -- F28F 1/422 classifies tubular heat-exchange elements that incorporate integral features on both the outside and the inside of the tube. Such designs include built-in fins, corrugations or other structures that form part of the tube wall to enhance heat transfer without separate attachments. This subclass is subordinate to the general category for tubular heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 1/424] Means comprising outside portions integral with inside portions -- F28F 1/424 covers tubular heat-exchange elements whose outer portion is formed as a continuous, integral part of the inner passage, such as co-extruded or built-up tubes where the wall and any external fins are one piece.
[F28F 1/426] the outside portions and the inside portions forming parts of complementary shape, e.g. concave and convex -- This subclass covers tubular heat-exchange elements whose outer surface and inner bore are formed with matching complementary shapes, such as a concave outer wall and a convex inner channel, to improve fluid flow and heat-transfer efficiency.
[F28F 1/44] and being formed of wire mesh -- F28F 1/44 covers heat-exchange apparatus using tubular elements whose walls are formed of wire mesh, providing a permeable structure that enhances fluid flow and heat transfer. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 17/00] Removing ice or water from heat-exchange apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for draining water from heat exchangers, e.g. means for draining condensates from evaporators  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing condensates in steam condensers -> F28B9/08
[F28F 17/005] Means for draining condensates from heat exchangers, e.g. from evaporators  F28B9/08  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F28F 17/005 relates to means for draining condensates or melted ice from heat-exchange apparatus such as evaporators. It includes drainage passages, collection chambers, pumps or other devices that remove water or condensate to prevent fouling or blockage. This subclass supersedes the earlier F28B9/08.
[F28F 19/00] Preventing the formation of deposits or corrosion, e.g. by using filters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for preventing corrosion, abrasion, erosion, damages in heat exchangers, e.g. by using inserts or attachments, by using protective electric currents; preventing deposits of ice, means for preventing freezing of heat exchange media; by using scrapers  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Coatings for modifying heat transfer -> F28F13/18 - Removing ice from heat exchangers -> F28F17/00 - Preventing or removing incrustations in thermal cracking apparatus -> C10G9/16 - Preventing clogging by frost in cooling towers -> F28C1/12 - Cleaning heat exchangers -> F28G
[F28F 19/002] by using inserts or attachments -- The code relates to heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment that uses inserts, attachments or removable components to hinder scale, deposits or corrosion on the transfer surfaces. Such elements may be placed inside tubes, channels or on exterior surfaces to provide filtration, turbulence or protective barriers, and are applicable to general-purpose heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 19/004] by using protective electric currents, voltages, cathodes, anodes, electric short-circuits
[F28F 19/006] Preventing deposits of ice -- This group covers measures to stop ice from accumulating on heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment, such as using filters, surface coatings, heating elements or mechanical removal, to maintain performance and avoid corrosion.
[F28F 19/008] by using scrapers -- F28F 19/08 covers heat-exchange or heat-transfer devices that include scrapers to clean surfaces and prevent the build-up of deposits or corrosion. The scrapers are employed as a mechanical means of removing fouling, extending the efficient operation of the apparatus.
[F28F 19/01] by using means for separating solid materials from heat-exchange fluids, e.g. filters -- The entry concerns heat-exchange devices that incorporate means such as filters to remove solid particles from the working fluid, thereby preventing deposits and corrosion. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat-exchange and details of apparatus for general application.
[F28F 19/02] by using coatings, e.g. vitreous or enamel coatings -- F28F 19/02 covers methods of preventing scale, deposits or corrosion in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment by applying protective coatings such as vitreous or enamel layers.
[F28F 19/04] of rubber -- F28F 19/04 relates to rubber elements used in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment to prevent deposits, scaling or corrosion, for example as protective linings, seals or filter inserts. It is a subclass of mechanical-engineering heat-exchange devices of general application.
[F28F 19/06] of metal
[F28F 2001/027] with dimples -- F28F 2001/027 classifies metal sheet, strip or panel that has been provided with dimples, typically by stamping, punching or forming, to increase stiffness, strength, acoustic or aerodynamic performance. It covers the production and shaping processes that create the dimpled surface and the resulting dimpled metal articles.
[F28F 2001/428] Particular methods for manufacturing outside or inside fins -- F28F 2001/428 belongs to CPC class F28F, which addresses the manufacture of heat-exchange devices. This subgroup specifically covers processes for forming external or internal fins, such as extrusion, stamping, welding or additive-manufacturing techniques applied to tubes, plates or other surfaces.
[F28F 2009/004] Common frame elements for multiple cores -- F28F 2009/004 relates to devices that use a single structural frame or housing to support several electromagnetic cores, such as multi-pole transformers or inductors, enabling shared mechanical support and precise alignment of the cores within one combined enclosure.
[F28F 2009/0285] Other particular headers or end plates -- This subclass covers specially designed headers or end plates for piping systems that have distinctive shapes, materials or functions not covered elsewhere. It includes custom distribution headers, reinforced end caps or other unique fittings that connect, support or terminate pipes.
[F28F 2009/0287] having passages for different heat exchange media -- F28F 2009/0287 covers heat exchangers that provide separate passages for different heat-exchange media, allowing the fluids to flow in distinct channels while transferring thermal energy between them. The classification includes designs where the media are guided through tubes, plates, channels or similar conduits within a common housing to achieve efficient heat transfer.
[F28F 2009/029] with increasing or decreasing cross-section, e.g. having conical shape -- F28F 2009/029 covers devices or structural elements whose cross-section changes along their length, such as members that become wider or narrower, for example conical shapes. This subgroup of the F28F main group relates to components designed to have a variable profile for strength, flow or fitting purposes.
[F28F 2009/0292] with fins -- F28F 2009/0292 covers heat-exchange devices that incorporate fins to enlarge the heat-transfer surface, such as radiators, condensers, evaporators, or cooling plates. The fins may be attached, formed, or integral with the primary heat-conduction element and are intended to improve thermal efficiency in heating, cooling or condensation processes.
[F28F 2009/0295] comprising cooling circuits -- The entry defines apparatus for handling or processing solids that incorporates one or more cooling circuits, such as internal water, oil or refrigerant channels, to remove heat generated during mixing, grinding, or other operations. It applies to devices where the cooling system is an integral part of the machine.
[F28F 2009/0297] Side headers, e.g. for radiators having conduits laterally connected to common header -- F28F 2009/0297 covers side-header arrangements for radiators or similar heat-exchange units, where a common lateral header receives multiple conduits or tubes that are connected side-by-side. The classification includes designs in which the header runs parallel to the tubes and provides the main fluid distribution path.
[F28F 2009/222] Particular guide plates, baffles or deflectors, e.g. having particular orientation relative to an elongated casing or conduit -- This CPC subclass covers guide plates, baffles or deflectors that are specially oriented with respect to an elongated casing or conduit. It relates to devices that control or modify fluid flow by directing it along or across the pipe, often used in compressors, turbines or heat exchangers. The classification applies to designs where the orientation of the element is a defining feature.
[F28F 2009/224] Longitudinal partitions -- The CPC group F28F 2009/224 covers lifting or hoisting devices that incorporate longitudinal partitions, such as internal walls or guides running along the length of a drum, cage or guide to keep multiple ropes, cables or loads separated during winding or movement. It applies to patents describing these partition arrangements in elevators, winches and similar equipment.
[F28F 2009/226] Transversal partitions -- The CPC subclass F28F 2009/226 pertains to transverse partitions incorporated in mixers or processing chambers, i.e., walls or plates that extend across the vessel to divide it into separate zones for controlling material flow, residence time or temperature. These partitions are employed in equipment such as batch mixers, extruders or reactors to improve mixing efficiency or enable staged processing.
[F28F 2009/228] Oblique partitions -- F28F 2009/228 - Oblique partitions covers pump, compressor or fluid-handling components that incorporate internal or external partition walls placed at an angle to the main flow direction, designed to guide, separate or regulate the fluid stream and improve efficiency.
[F28F 2013/001] Particular heat conductive materials, e.g. superconductive elements -- The CPC entry F28F 2013/001 is part of class F28F, which concerns heating, steam generation and related apparatus, and it specifically addresses particular heat-conductive materials, including superconductive elements, that are employed to achieve highly efficient thermal transfer in various devices and systems.
[F28F 2013/005] Thermal joints -- F28F 2013/005 - Thermal joints covers devices and methods for joining components to conduct or dissipate heat, such as heat-transfer fittings, brazed or welded couplings, and other assemblies that create a thermally conductive path between parts.
[F28F 2013/006] Heat conductive materials -- The CPC subclass F28F 2013/006 covers heat-conductive materials such as metals, alloys, ceramics, composites and polymers that are engineered to transfer thermal energy efficiently. It includes substances used for thermal management in machinery, electronics, heating systems and other applications where rapid heat dissipation or distribution is required.
[F28F 2013/008] Variable conductance materials;  Thermal switches -- A variable conductance material or thermal switch is a component whose ability to transfer heat can be actively altered, allowing it to turn heat flow on, off, or regulate it in response to an external stimulus such as temperature, electric current, or mechanical stress. This classification covers such materials, devices, and methods for controlling thermal conductivity.
[F28F 2025/005] Liquid collection;  Liquid treatment;  Liquid recirculation;  Addition of make-up liquid -- The F28F 2025/005 subclass defines inventions relating to the gathering of liquids from a system, their subsequent treatment, and the recirculation of the processed fluid, together with the introduction of fresh make-up liquid to maintain operation. It covers apparatus, methods, and control arrangements for managing these fluid-handling steps in industrial or domestic settings.

[F28F 2210/00] Heat exchange conduits -- F28F 2210/00 covers the design, construction and arrangement of conduits that carry fluid through heat exchangers, including tubes, pipes, channels and other passageways that facilitate heat transfer between media. [F28F 2210/02] with particular branching, e.g. fractal conduit arrangements -- This subclass covers heat-exchange conduits that incorporate distinctive branching geometries, such as fractal-like arrangements, to increase surface area and promote more uniform fluid distribution for improved heat-transfer performance.
[F28F 2210/04] Arrangements of conduits common to different heat exchange sections, the conduits having channels for different circuits -- The code covers designs where a single heat-exchange conduit is shared by multiple sections, each section carrying its own fluid circuit through separate internal channels. [F28F 2210/06] having walls comprising obliquely extending corrugations, e.g. in the form of threads -- A heat-exchange conduit whose wall is formed with obliquely extending corrugations, such as thread-like profiles, to enhance fluid turbulence and heat-transfer efficiency. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 2210/08] Assemblies of conduits having different features -- Covers groups of heat-exchange conduits arranged together in which the individual pipes differ in dimensions, material, shape, internal structure or surface treatment. The classification applies to assemblies such as condensers, boilers or heat-recovery units where varied conduit types are combined to achieve specific thermal performance.
[F28F 2210/10] Particular layout, e.g. for uniform temperature distribution -- The CPC subclass F28F 2210/10 concerns the specific arrangement of heat-exchange conduits designed to achieve a uniform temperature distribution across the system. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and the details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 2215/00] Fins -- The F28F 2215/00 class concerns fins that are added to heat-exchange or heat-transfer devices to enlarge the surface area and improve thermal dissipation. It includes the design, shape, material, attachment and arrangement of fins used in mechanical engineering applications such as radiators, condensers, heat sinks and similar equipment.
[F28F 2215/02] Arrangements of fins common to different heat exchange sections, the fins being in contact with different heat exchange media -- The subclass F28F 2215/02 covers heat-exchanger designs in which a set of fins is shared by several sections of the device, each fin being in contact with a different heat-exchange medium. It addresses common fin arrangements that serve multiple flow paths within the same apparatus.
[F28F 2215/04] Assemblies of fins having different features, e.g. with different fin densities -- The subclass pertains to heat-exchange devices in which several fins are assembled together, each fin type differing in geometry, spacing or density to achieve specific thermal characteristics. It includes designs that combine rows of fins with varied pitches, profiles or fin-densities within a single unit.
[F28F 2215/06] Hollow fins -- Hollow fins are lightweight heat-transfer elements formed with internal cavities or channels that allow fluid flow through the fin body, increasing surface area and improving convection while reducing material use. They are used in general-purpose heat-exchange apparatus such as radiators, condensers and evaporators to enhance thermal performance without adding significant weight.
[F28F 2215/08] with openings, e.g. louvers -- The code F28F 2215/08 designates heat-exchange fins that include openings such as louvers, which modify fluid flow and improve thermal performance. These fins are used in general heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment within mechanical engineering.
[F28F 2215/10] Secondary fins, e.g. projections or recesses on main fins -- The subclass F28F 2215/10 covers secondary fin structures that are formed as projections or recesses on primary fins of heat-exchange devices. It includes designs where additional fin elements are integrated with the main fin to increase surface area, modify airflow, or enhance heat transfer performance.
[F28F 2215/12] with U-shaped slots for laterally inserting conduits -- F28F 2215/12 covers heat-exchange fins that incorporate U-shaped slots allowing conduits to be inserted laterally. The design facilitates fluid flow through the fin structure, improving heat transfer while maintaining structural integrity. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically general heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 2215/14] in the form of movable or loose fins -- This subclass covers heat-exchange apparatus equipped with fins that are not fixed in place but can be moved, detached, or loosely positioned. The movable or loose fins may be adjusted, replaced, or reoriented to modify surface area or flow characteristics in mechanical engineering heat-exchange systems.
[F28F 2220/00] Closure means, e.g. end caps on header boxes or plugs on conduits -- The subclass F28F 2220/00 covers closure devices for heat-exchange equipment, such as end caps for header boxes or plugs for conduit openings, designed to seal fluid paths and maintain the structural integrity of the apparatus.
[F28F 2225/00] Reinforcing means -- F28F 2225/00 covers reinforcing means incorporated in heat-exchange devices such as fins, tubes or panels to increase structural integrity and improve heat-transfer performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application.
[F28F 2225/02] for casings -- F28F 2225/02 covers reinforcing means applied to the casings of heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment, such as shells, tubesheets or outer shells, to increase their mechanical strength, resistance to pressure or thermal stress. The provision may include ribs, stays, braces, rings or similar structural elements designed for general-purpose heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 2225/04] for conduits -- This subgroup covers reinforcing structures incorporated into heat-exchange conduits, such as ribs, stiffeners or support members that strengthen tubes, pipes or channels against pressure, vibration or deformation. It applies to general-purpose heat-transfer apparatus where the conduit itself is reinforced.
[F28F 2225/06] for fins -- The subclass F28F 2225/06 addresses reinforcing means for fins in heat-exchange and heat-transfer devices, such as ribs, support structures or other features that strengthen or stabilize the fin elements used to enhance thermal performance.
[F28F 2225/08] for header boxes -- This subclass covers reinforcing means incorporated into header boxes of heat-exchange apparatus, such as ribs, gussets or other structural supports that increase strength against pressure or thermal stresses. It is part of the details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application within mechanical engineering.
[F28F 2230/00] Sealing means -- F28F 2230/00 covers sealing elements used in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment of general application. It includes gaskets, O-rings, diaphragms and other devices that prevent fluid leakage between components of boilers, condensers, radiators and similar mechanical systems. The classification is nested under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 2235/00] Means for filling gaps between elements, e.g. between conduits within casings -- This CPC subclass concerns devices that fill voids between components such as ducts inside housings, ensuring continuous flow or structural integrity. It covers methods and structures for sealing or bridging gaps in heat-exchange assemblies.
[F28F 2240/00] Spacing means -- The classification F28F 2240/00 covers spacing elements employed in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment. It includes devices such as spacers, plates, rods or supports that keep tubes, plates or other heat-transfer surfaces apart to ensure proper fluid flow and thermal performance. This applies to general-purpose heat-exchange apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F28F 2245/00] Coatings -- F28F 2245/00 classifies coatings intended for heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment of general use, including protective, anti-corrosion, insulating or otherwise performance-enhancing layers applied to tubes, plates, shells or related components. It covers the material composition, application methods and functional purposes of such surface treatments.
[F28F 2245/02] hydrophilic -- Hydrophilic refers to coatings that attract water and are used on heat-exchange or heat-transfer components to improve wetting, promote film formation and increase thermal efficiency. Such treatments are applied to surfaces of condensers, evaporators, radiators and similar mechanical engineering equipment to enhance heat transfer performance.
[F28F 2245/04] hydrophobic -- This subgroup covers heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment that uses hydrophobic surface treatments or coatings to repel water, reduce fouling and improve thermal efficiency. It is classified under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, details of heat-exchange apparatus, and coatings.
[F28F 2245/06] having particular radiating, reflecting or absorbing features, e.g. for improving heat transfer by radiation -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices that incorporate surfaces engineered with radiating, reflective or absorptive characteristics to boost thermal transfer by radiation. It includes coatings, textures or structures applied to plates, tubes or other elements that modify radiative heat flow for improved performance.
[F28F 2245/08] self-cleaning -- This subclass covers self-cleaning features applied to heat-exchange or heat-transfer apparatus, typically using coatings or surface treatments that reduce fouling without manual cleaning. It includes catalytic, hydrophobic, nanostructured or other surface technologies designed to maintain thermal efficiency of general-application heat-exchange equipment.
[F28F 2250/00] Arrangements for modifying the flow of the heat exchange media, e.g. flow guiding means -- This subclass covers devices or structural features that change the flow path of heat-exchange media within a heat-transfer apparatus, such as baffles, vanes, or other flow-guiding means, aimed at improving heat-transfer efficiency, controlling pressure drop, or reducing fouling.
[F28F 2250/02] Streamline-shaped elements -- The code F28F 2250/02 designates streamline-shaped elements incorporated in flow-guiding arrangements that modify the movement of heat-exchange media within general-purpose heat-transfer equipment. These elements are used to improve fluid dynamics, reduce pressure losses, and enhance heat-transfer efficiency in mechanical-engineering applications involving heat exchangers.
[F28F 2250/04] Communication passages between channels -- The F28F 2250/04 entry concerns passages that connect separate flow channels within a heat-exchange device, allowing the heat-transfer medium to move between them. Such communication means include ducts, manifolds or crossover pipes that modify the distribution of the fluid or gas streams to improve overall heat-exchange performance. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically general heat-exchange apparatus and flow-guiding arrangements.
[F28F 2250/06] Derivation channels, e.g. bypass -- This subclass covers derivation channels such as bypasses that divert or split the fluid stream within a heat-exchange system to alter flow distribution or pressure drop. It includes design features that guide the heat-exchange medium through subsidiary passages for performance optimisation.
[F28F 2250/08] Fluid driving means, e.g. pumps, fans -- The CPC subclass F28F 2250/08 covers fluid-driving devices such as pumps and fans that are employed to move the media within heat-exchange systems, enabling control of flow patterns and enhancing heat-transfer performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically heat-exchange apparatus and flow-guiding arrangements.
[F28F 2250/10] Particular pattern of flow of the heat exchange media -- F28F 2250/10 covers specific flow patterns of heat-exchange media, focusing on arrangements that modify how the fluid moves through a heat-exchange device. It includes designs of flow-guiding elements and other means that direct the media to improve heat transfer efficiency.
[F28F 2250/102] with change of flow direction -- This subclass covers heat-exchange devices that incorporate arrangements to alter the direction of the working fluid, such as flow-guiding elements, bends, or redirecting passages, enabling a change in flow direction within the heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 2250/104] with parallel flow -- It relates to heat-exchange apparatus where the fluids move in parallel paths. The arrangement directs the heating or cooling medium to flow alongside the other medium, maintaining the same direction throughout the exchanger. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 2250/106] with cross flow -- This subclass covers heat-exchange apparatus in which the media flow across each other, i.e. a cross-flow arrangement, typically achieved with baffles, plates or other flow-guiding elements to improve heat-transfer performance. Such configurations are used in general-purpose exchangers where the two fluid streams intersect at roughly right angles.
[F28F 2250/108] with combined cross flow and parallel flow -- This subclass covers heat-exchanger designs that direct the secondary fluid so that part of the flow passes across the primary stream while another part moves in the same direction, creating a combined cross-flow and parallel-flow pattern within a single unit.
[F28F 2255/00] Heat exchanger elements made of materials having special features or resulting from particular manufacturing processes -- This subclass covers heat-exchanger components that are constructed from materials possessing distinctive properties such as corrosion resistance, high-temperature stability or enhanced thermal conductivity, or that are produced by specialized manufacturing techniques like sintering, welding or additive manufacturing. The focus is on the material or process characteristics that give the elements unique performance in heat-transfer applications.
[F28F 2255/02] Flexible elements -- Flexible elements are heat-exchanger components designed to bend, roll or deform while maintaining thermal performance, typically produced from materials with special properties such as high elasticity, corrosion resistance or shape-memory behavior, or fabricated by processes like sintering or additive manufacturing that give them unique mechanical characteristics. These elements are used where space constraints, vibration or thermal expansion require adaptable, resilient heat-transfer surfaces.
[F28F 2255/04] comprising shape memory alloys or bimetallic elements -- The subclass F28F 2255/04 covers heat-exchanger components that incorporate shape-memory alloys or bimetallic elements. These materials are employed for their ability to deform or expand with temperature, allowing more efficient heat transfer or self-adjusting flow paths. The entry applies to designs and manufacturing methods that produce such specially featured elements.
[F28F 2255/06] composite, e.g. polymers with fillers or fibres -- This subclass covers heat-exchanger components that are made from composite materials, such as polymer matrices reinforced with filler particles or fibers, which provide improved mechanical or thermal performance. It includes designs where the composite structure is integral to the heat-transfer element, enhancing durability and efficiency.
[F28F 2255/08] pressed -- A pressed heat-exchanger element is produced by shaping a metal sheet or strip through a pressing, stamping or embossing operation, creating patterns such as ridges, corrugations or channels that improve fluid flow and heat transfer. The process yields components with enhanced surface area or structural rigidity while retaining the basic material properties. This includes plates, fins or other elements formed by mechanical deformation.
[F28F 2255/10] made by hydroforming -- The subgroup F28F 2255/10 covers heat-exchanger elements that are produced by hydroforming, a high-pressure fluid-forming technique that shapes metal tubes or sheets into complex, high-strength profiles. Such components are intended to provide improved heat-transfer performance and durability compared with conventionally formed parts.
[F28F 2255/12] expanded or perforated metal plate -- A heat-exchange element comprising an expanded or perforated metal plate, used to increase surface area and promote fluid flow within a heat-transfer device. The plate's geometry is formed by stretching, punching or other manufacturing processes that create a lattice or cellular structure, enhancing thermal performance.
[F28F 2255/14] molded -- F28F 2255/14 (molded) covers heat-exchanger elements that are produced by molding processes such as injection molding, compression molding, casting or sintering, where the manufacturing method or material gives the element special properties or shapes for heat-exchange applications.
[F28F 2255/143] injection molded -- The CPC subclass F28F 2255/143 covers heat-exchanger elements that are formed by injection molding, using materials with special characteristics or produced through particular manufacturing techniques. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus of general application. This category is used to classify inventions where the molding process and material properties are essential to the performance of the heat-exchanger component.
[F28F 2255/146] overmolded -- F28F 2255/146 covers heat-exchanger components that are overmolded, meaning a second material is molded around an existing element to impart special properties such as corrosion resistance or enhanced thermal performance. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus of general application.
[F28F 2255/16] extruded -- This subclass covers heat-exchanger components that are produced by extrusion, a process where material is forced through a die to form continuous shapes such as tubes, plates or fins. The resulting elements have special material characteristics or manufacturing benefits that improve heat-transfer efficiency or mechanical strength.
[F28F 2255/18] sintered -- The code F28F 2255/18 covers heat-exchanger components produced by sintering, a powder-metal process in which compacted particles are heated below their melting point so they bond together. This yields elements with porous or finely structured surfaces that improve heat transfer and corrosion resistance.
[F28F 2255/20] with nanostructures -- This subclass covers heat-exchanger components that incorporate nanostructured materials or surfaces, where the nanostructure is used to improve heat transfer, corrosion resistance, or mechanical strength. It is a subset of heat-exchange elements with special material features or specific manufacturing processes. The code is F28F 2255/20.
[F28F 2260/00] Heat exchangers or heat exchange elements having special size, e.g. microstructures -- The classification F28F 2260/00 covers heat exchangers or heat-exchange elements that are built with unusually small or specialised dimensions, such as microstructured devices. It is nested under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus of general application.
[F28F 2260/02] having microchannels -- This subclass covers heat exchangers that incorporate microchannels, such that the flow passages have dimensions in the micrometer range, enabling high surface-to-volume ratios and enhanced heat transfer. It applies to devices of any general type where the microstructured channels are the defining feature.
[F28F 2265/00] Safety or protection arrangements -- This subclass covers safety and protective features incorporated into heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment, such as guards, shut-off devices, pressure-relief mechanisms, temperature monitoring and other measures that prevent injury, damage or malfunction during operation. It resides under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus of general application.
[F28F 2265/02] in the form of screens or covers -- This CPC subclass F28F 2265/02 relates to heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment that includes safety or protective devices in the form of screens or covers, such as guards or protective panels designed to prevent accidental contact with moving parts, hot surfaces or hazardous fluid streams.
[F28F 2265/06] by using means for draining heat exchange media from heat exchangers -- This class pertains to safety or protection features that employ devices for draining the fluid medium out of a heat exchanger. It includes valves, drains, or other mechanisms designed to remove heat-exchange media during shutdown, maintenance, or emergency situations, thereby preventing damage or hazards.
[F28F 2265/10] for preventing overheating, e.g. heat shields -- This subclass covers safety features designed to avoid overheating in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment. It includes devices such as heat shields, thermal barriers, and other protective arrangements that limit temperature rise in the apparatus.
[F28F 2265/12] for preventing overpressure -- This CPC subclass covers safety or protection arrangements for heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment, specifically mechanisms that prevent overpressure in boilers, heat exchangers, and similar apparatus. It is nested under mechanical engineering → heat exchange in general → details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus, of general application.
[F28F 2265/14] for preventing damage by freezing, e.g. for accommodating volume expansion
[F28F 2265/16] for preventing leakage -- The CPC subclass F28F 2265/16 refers to safety or protection arrangements in heat-exchange equipment that are specifically designed to prevent leakage. It includes sealing devices, gaskets, protective covers, leak-detection systems and other measures that keep fluids or gases from escaping the heat-transfer apparatus.
[F28F 2265/18] for removing contaminants, e.g. for degassing -- F28F 2265/18 covers safety or protective features in heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment that remove contaminants from the fluid, such as degassing functions. The subclass includes designs that integrate filtration, venting or other contaminant-removal devices within the heat-exchanger system.
[F28F 2265/20] for preventing development of microorganisms -- The F28F 2265/20 subclass covers safety or protection arrangements in heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus that are designed to prevent the growth or spread of microorganisms. It includes measures such as antimicrobial materials, sterilisation systems, hygienic design features and cleaning procedures applied to heat-exchangers used in general industrial or process settings.
[F28F 2265/22] for draining -- F28F 2265/22 covers drainage provisions incorporated in heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment of general use, aimed at safely removing liquids that could impair operation or create hazards. The subclass addresses protective or safety-related arrangements that prevent fluid accumulation in boilers, condensers, radiators and similar devices.
[F28F 2265/24] for electrical insulation -- The subclass F28F 2265/24 covers safety or protective features in heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus that employ electrical insulation. It includes designs where insulating materials or barriers are used to prevent electrical hazards, such as short-circuits or electric shock, within the equipment. This protects both the device and personnel while maintaining the heat-exchange function.
[F28F 2265/26] for allowing differential expansion between elements -- This CPC subclass covers arrangements that permit components of a heat-exchange or heat-transfer device to expand at different rates, such as expansion joints, flexible bellows or other protective features that relieve thermal stress while maintaining safety.
[F28F 2265/28] for preventing noise -- F28F 2265/28 pertains to noise-prevention features incorporated in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment. It covers silencers, acoustic baffles, vibration dampers and other protective arrangements that reduce sound generated by the apparatus during operation.
[F28F 2265/30] for preventing vibrations -- The F28F 2265/30 subclass covers safety or protective features incorporated in heat-exchange and heat-transfer equipment to prevent or reduce vibrations. It applies to general-purpose apparatus where vibration control is required to protect the system's integrity, improve performance, or ensure operator safety.
[F28F 2265/32] for limiting movements, e.g. stops, locking means -- F28F 2265/32 belongs to the mechanical-engineering sub-group for heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus and covers safety or protection arrangements that limit movement, such as stops, locking means or other devices that restrain motion of components. It is used to classify inventions that provide mechanical limits to prevent unwanted displacement in heat-exchange equipment.
[F28F 2270/00] Thermal insulation -- F28F 2270/00 classifies inventions relating to thermal insulation used in heat-exchange and heat-transfer devices of general application, positioned under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus. The code covers insulating materials, structures and methods that reduce heat loss or gain in such equipment.
[F28F 2270/02] by using blind conduits -- This subclass covers thermal insulation for heat-exchange or heat-transfer apparatus that uses blind conduits, which are sealed pipe sections without fluid flow. It includes designs where the blind conduit acts as a barrier to reduce heat loss or gain, improving the device's overall efficiency.
[F28F 2275/00] Fastening -- The F28F 2275/00 subclass groups fastening devices and methods employed to join or secure parts of heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment. It includes clamps, brackets, screws, welds and other means for attaching tubes, plates, shells or similar components in general-application heat-exchange apparatus.
[F28F 2275/02] by using bonding materials -- This subclass deals with fastening methods for heat-exchange or heat-transfer devices that employ bonding materials such as adhesives, solder, brazing or welds. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general and details of heat-exchange apparatus, and covers ways to attach components using bonding agents.
[F28F 2275/025] by using adhesives -- The F28F 2275/025 subclass covers fastening of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus by means of adhesives. It pertains to general-application heat-exchange equipment where components are bonded rather than bolted, welded or mechanically clamped. This approach is used to secure parts of the device while allowing efficient thermal performance.
[F28F 2275/04] by brazing -- This CPC subclass concerns the fastening of heat-exchange or heat-transfer equipment by brazing. It falls under mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, details of heat-exchange and heat-transfer apparatus, of general application. The code F28F 2275/04 specifically covers the use of brazing as a method to join components of such apparatus.
[F28F 2275/045] with particular processing steps, e.g. by allowing displacement of parts during brazing or by using a reservoir for storing brazing material -- The subclass addresses fastening techniques in heat-exchange apparatus that involve distinctive brazing processes, for example allowing the joined components to shift relative to each other during brazing or incorporating a built-in reservoir that supplies the brazing filler material.
[F28F 2275/06] by welding -- The CPC subclass F28F 2275/06 (by welding) refers to fastening methods in which components of heat-exchange or heat-transfer apparatus are joined by welding. It covers the welding of tubes, plates, shells and other parts used in general-application heat-exchange equipment as a means of securing the assembly.
[F28F 2275/061] by diffusion bonding -- It refers to fastening of heat-exchange or heat-transfer apparatus by diffusion bonding, a solid-state joining technique that bonds surfaces through atomic diffusion under elevated temperature and pressure, typically used for metallic components where welds or bolts are undesirable.
[F28F 2275/062] by impact pressure or friction welding -- A subclass of mechanical-engineering heat-exchange detailing fastening methods that join components by impact pressure or friction welding, used for assembling heat-transfer apparatus without separate fasteners. This covers welding techniques that create a solid-state bond through localized pressure or friction, applied to general-purpose heat-exchange equipment.
[F28F 2275/064] by induction welding or by using microwaves -- This subclass covers fastenings for heat-exchange or heat-transfer devices that are joined by induction welding or microwave energy. It pertains to mechanical engineering applications where the fastening method uses electromagnetic heating rather than mechanical fasteners.
[F28F 2275/065] by ultrasonic or vibration welding -- This subclass covers fastening techniques applied to heat-exchange and heat-transfer devices that use ultrasonic or vibration welding to join components. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the details of heat-exchange apparatus and general fastening methods. The classification is used for patents involving ultrasonic or vibratory welds to secure heat-exchange elements.
[F28F 2275/067] by laser welding -- F28F 2275/067 covers the use of laser welding as a fastening technique in heat-exchange and heat-transfer devices. It applies to mechanical engineering contexts where components are joined by directing a high-energy laser beam to fuse materials, providing a precise, strong bond without additional fasteners.
[F28F 2275/068] by explosive welding -- The entry concerns fastening of heat-exchange or heat-transfer components by explosive welding, a process in which a controlled explosive charge drives one metal part against another at high velocity to create a solid-state metallurgical bond. This method joins tubes, plates or other heat-exchange elements without conventional bolts, rivets or adhesives.
[F28F 2275/08] by clamping or clipping -- F28F 2275/08 covers fastening methods that secure heat-exchange or heat-transfer components by clamping or clipping. It is situated within mechanical engineering, heat exchange in general, and details of heat-exchange apparatus of general application.
